topics.title: Introduction to Plastics Packaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Plastics Packaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plastics Packaging** is a specialized branch of packaging technology that utilizes **polymeric materials** — both **thermoplastics** and **thermosets** — along with their composites, laminates, and co-extruded structures, to enclose, protect, preserve, transport, and present products across diverse industries including food, pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and industrial goods.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**, plastics packaging is defined as the **engineering application of polymer science** to design containment systems that meet four fundamental criteria:

1. **Containment** — preventing loss or leakage of contents.
2. **Protection** — shielding against physical, chemical, biological, and environmental hazards.
3. **Convenience** — ease of handling, storage, and consumer use.
4. **Communication** — branding, regulatory labeling, and information transfer.

The global packaging industry is currently valued at approximately **USD 1.1 trillion**, with **plastics accounting for nearly 40%** of all packaging materials by volume, making it the **largest single material category** in modern packaging.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Bodyguard Suitcase"**

Imagine you are sending a fragile, valuable gift (say, a glass perfume bottle) through a long journey — through rain, dust, heat, rough handling, and tight spaces.

A **plastics package** is like a **bodyguard wearing a custom-built armored suitcase**:

- The **outer rigid plastic (like PET, HDPE)** acts as the **armored shell** — absorbing shocks, resisting punctures, and keeping the shape.
- The **inner flexible plastic film (like LDPE, PP)** acts as a **soft cushion and moisture barrier** — wrapping gently around the gift.
- The **seal (heat-seal or zipper)** is like the **lock on the suitcase** — preventing theft (leakage) of contents.
- The **printed label** is the **name tag and instruction manual** — telling the recipient what is inside, how to open it, and how to use it.

Just as a bodyguard's job is to ensure the **safe delivery** of their client, a plastics package's job is to ensure the **safe delivery** of the product — from manufacturer to consumer — in perfect condition.

---

## 1.3 Historical Evolution Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Timeline of Plastics Packaging (Key Milestones)**

- **1862**: **Cellulose Nitrate (Parkesine)** — first plastic used as a packaging film (Alexander Parkes).
- **1907**: **Bakelite** — first fully synthetic plastic (Leo Baekeland).
- **1933**: **Polyethylene (PE)** discovered by ICI (UK) — revolutionized flexible packaging.
- **1941**: **Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET)** patented — later became the global standard for beverage bottles.
- **1950s**: Introduction of **Polypropylene (PP)** and **Polystyrene (PS)** for rigid containers.
- **1970s**: Birth of **multi-layer co-extrusion** and **barrier films**.
- **1990s**: Rise of **aseptic packaging** (Tetra Pak) and **modified atmosphere packaging (MAP)**.
- **2010s–2020s**: Emergence of **bio-based polymers (PLA, PHA)** and **recyclable mono-material designs**.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Plastics Packaging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Units & Metrics**

- **Packaging Density**: typically expressed in **g/cm³** for materials.
- **Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR)**: measured in **cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹**.
- **Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR)**: measured in **g·m⁻²·day⁻¹**.
- **Tensile Strength**: measured in **MPa**.
- **Global Plastics Production (2023)**: approximately **400 million tonnes/year**, of which **~40%** is packaging.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Plastics Dominate Packaging — The 7 Pillars

Plastics have displaced traditional materials (glass, metal, paper) in packaging due to seven engineering advantages:

1. **Low Density** — typical density ranges from **0.85 to 1.45 g/cm³**, compared to glass (2.5 g/cm³) and steel (7.8 g/cm³), reducing **transportation costs**.
2. **Formability** — can be molded, extruded, blown, thermoformed into **virtually any shape**.
3. **Mechanical Toughness** — high impact resistance (e.g., HDPE: ~50 kJ/m²).
4. **Barrier Properties** — selective permeability control via co-extrusion and lamination.
5. **Chemical Resistance** — inert to most acids, alkalis, and solvents.
6. **Printability** — excellent surface for graphics and branding.
7. **Cost-Effectiveness** — low raw material + processing cost per unit volume.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Plastics Used in Packaging

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Master Categories**

| Category | Behavior on Heating | Re-processability | Common Packaging Examples |
|----------|---------------------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| **Thermoplastics** | Soften and melt | **Recyclable** (re-meltable) | PE, PP, PET, PS, PVC |
| **Thermosets** | Harden permanently (crosslink) | **Non-recyclable** by melting | Melamine, Phenolic resins, Epoxy |

### 2.2.1 Detailed Thermoplastic Classification

- **Polyethylene (PE) Family**:
  - **LDPE (Low-Density PE)** — flexible films, squeeze bottles.
  - **HDPE (High-Density PE)** — milk jugs, detergent bottles.
  - **LLDPE (Linear Low-Density PE)** — stretch films, liners.
- **Polypropylene (PP)** — yogurt cups, bottle caps, microwave containers.
- **Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET)** — beverage bottles, food trays.
- **Polystyrene (PS)** — foam cups, CD cases (now restricted).
- **Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)** — blister packs, cling films.
- **Ethylene Vinyl Alcohol (EVOH)** — oxygen barrier in multilayer films.
- **Polyamide (Nylon)** — vacuum bags, retort pouches.

---

## 2.3 Functions of a Plastics Package — The "5 P's"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 P's Framework (Industry Standard)**

1. **Protect** — from mechanical shock, vibration, compression, dust, microbes.
2. **Preserve** — barrier against oxygen, moisture, light, odor.
3. **Present** — aesthetic appeal, shelf impact, branding.
4. **Portion** — accurate single-serve / multi-serve dosing.
5. **Provide Information** — ingredients, batch codes, expiry, regulatory marks.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Equations for Plastics Packaging Calculations**

| Formula | Symbol Description | Units | Application |
|---------|--------------------|-------|-------------|
| $m = \rho \cdot V$ | $\rho$ = density, $V$ = volume | g/cm³ | Mass of plastic part |
| $OTR = \dfrac{q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta p}$ | $q$ = gas flow, $L$ = thickness, $A$ = area, $\Delta p$ = pressure difference | cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹ | Oxygen barrier design |
| $WVTR = \dfrac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t}$ | $\Delta m$ = mass change, $A$ = area, $t$ = time | g·m⁻²·day⁻¹ | Moisture barrier design |
| $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $F$ = force, $A$ = cross-section | MPa | Tensile strength check |
| $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | $\Delta L$ = elongation, $L_0$ = original length | dimensionless | Strain in packaging films |
| $\eta = \dfrac{\sigma}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate | Pa·s | Polymer melt viscosity |
| $C\% = \dfrac{m_c}{m_{total}} \times 100$ | $m_c$ = compostable mass | % | Sustainability metric |
| $R\% = \left(1 - \dfrac{w_{loss}}{w_{initial}}\right) \times 100$ | $w_{loss}$ = weight loss | % | Recycling yield |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Real-World Industries

- **Food Industry**: PET bottles for carbonated drinks; PP containers for microwave meals.
- **Pharmaceuticals**: PVC/PVDC blisters for tablets; HDPE bottles for syrups.
- **Cosmetics**: Multi-layer tubes (PE/EVOH/PE) for creams.
- **E-Commerce**: Bubble wrap (LDPE) and air pillows for shock absorption.
- **Industrial Chemicals**: UN-certified HDPE jerry cans for solvents.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Calculating Mass of a PET Bottle

> **Problem:** A PET beverage bottle has a cylindrical body of diameter $D = 6$ cm and height $H = 20$ cm, with a wall thickness $t = 0.3$ mm. The density of PET is $\rho = 1.38$ g/cm³. Calculate the total mass of the bottle (body only, neglecting the neck region).

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Identify the geometry.**
The body is a hollow cylinder. We need the surface area of the curved side wall.

**Step 2: Compute the side wall surface area.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{wall} &= \pi \cdot D \cdot H \\
&= \pi \times 6 \text{ cm} \times 20 \text{ cm} \\
&= 376.99 \text{ cm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Convert wall thickness to cm.**

$$
t = 0.3 \text{ mm} = 0.03 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the volume of plastic material.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= A_{wall} \times t \\
&= 376.99 \text{ cm}^2 \times 0.03 \text{ cm} \\
&= 11.31 \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the mass using $m = \rho \cdot V$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= 1.38 \text{ g/cm}^3 \times 11.31 \text{ cm}^3 \\
m &= 15.61 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The PET bottle body weighs approximately **15.6 g**, which is consistent with real-world values (typical 500 mL PET bottle weighs 25–30 g including neck and base).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Oxygen Transmission Rate Through Multi-Layer Film

> **Problem:** A co-extruded film has three layers: outer PP (thickness $L_1 = 50 \,\mu m$, permeability $P_1 = 100$ barrer), middle EVOH ($L_2 = 10 \,\mu m$, $P_2 = 0.05$ barrer), inner PE ($L_3 = 40 \,\mu m$, $P_3 = 150$ barrer). Calculate the **equivalent oxygen permeability** of the laminate.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Recall the laminate permeability formula.**

For layers in series (perpendicular to gas flow):

$$
\dfrac{L_{total}}{P_{eq}} = \dfrac{L_1}{P_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{P_2} + \dfrac{L_3}{P_3}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{L_{total}}{P_{eq}} &= \dfrac{50}{100} + \dfrac{10}{0.05} + \dfrac{40}{150} \\
&= 0.500 + 200.000 + 0.267 \\
&= 200.767 \text{ (units: }\mu m / \text{barrer)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute total film thickness.**

$$
L_{total} = 50 + 10 + 40 = 100 \,\mu m
$$

**Step 4: Solve for equivalent permeability.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{eq} &= \dfrac{L_{total}}{200.767} \\
&= \dfrac{100}{200.767} \\
P_{eq} &\approx 0.498 \text{ barrer}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Interpret the result.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Without the EVOH barrier layer ($P_2 = 0.05$ barrer), the laminate permeability would be ~125 barrer (poor barrier). The **single thin EVOH layer reduces oxygen transmission by ~250×**, demonstrating why **multi-layer co-extrusion** is essential for oxygen-sensitive products like meat and wine.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Material Selection for Common Products

> [!NOTE]
> **Material-Package-Product Matching Matrix**

| Product Category | Recommended Plastic | Key Property Required | Typical Form |
|------------------|--------------------|-----------------------|--------------|
| Carbonated drinks | **PET** | CO₂ barrier, clarity | Rigid bottle |
| Milk | **HDPE** | Moisture barrier, opacity | Rigid jug |
| Yogurt | **PP** | Heat resistance (hot-fill) | Thermoformed cup |
| Snack pouches | **BOPP / PE laminate** | Printability, moisture barrier | Flexible film |
| Ready-to-eat meals | **PP / EVOH / PP** | Retort resistance, O₂ barrier | Trays + lidding film |
| Shampoo | **HDPE / PET** | Chemical resistance | Squeeze / rigid bottle |
| Tablets | **PVC / PVDC** | Moisture + O₂ barrier | Blister pack |
| Frozen food | **LDPE / LLDPE** | Low-temperature flexibility | Pouches |

---

## 3.4 Sustainability Index — Analytical Comparison

$$
\text{Sustainability Index (SI)} = \dfrac{R\% + B\% + C\%}{3}
$$

Where:
- $R\%$ = Recyclability percentage.
- $B\%$ = Bio-based carbon content percentage.
- $C\%$ = Compostability percentage.

> **Example:** For PET (recycled content 30%, bio-based 10%, compostable 0%):

$$
\begin{aligned}
SI &= \dfrac{30 + 10 + 0}{3} \\
SI &= 13.33\% \quad (\text{indicates moderate sustainability})
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Example:** For PLA (recycled 5%, bio-based 100%, compostable 80%):

$$
\begin{aligned}
SI &= \dfrac{5 + 100 + 80}{3} \\
SI &= 61.67\% \quad (\text{indicates high sustainability})
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Plastics Packaging Material Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Packaging Requirement] --> B{Product is Solid or Liquid?}
    B -- Liquid --> C{Carbonated?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use PET Bottle]
    C -- No --> E{Requires O2 Barrier?}
    B -- Solid --> F{Heat Sensitive?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use PP Tray + PE Lid]
    F -- No --> H{Need High Barrier?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Multi-layer: PP + EVOH + PE]
    H -- No --> J[Use Mono-material: HDPE or PP]
    E -- Yes --> K[Use EVOH Co-extrusion Bottle]
    E -- No --> L[Use HDPE or LDPE]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functions of a Plastics Package (The 5 P's)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    A["PROTECT"] B["PRESERVE"] C["PRESENT"]
    D["PORTION"] E["PROVIDE INFO"] F["PLASTICS PACKAGE"]
    A --> F
    B --> F
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Multi-Layer Film Architecture

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 5
    L1["OUTER LAYER (PP)"] L2["TIE LAYER (ADHESIVE)"] L3["BARRIER (EVOH)"] L4["TIE LAYER (ADHESIVE)"] L5["SEALANT LAYER (PE)"]
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
```

> **Engineering Note:** Each layer serves a **distinct functional role**:
> - **Outer (PP)** — printability + mechanical strength.
> - **Tie layers** — bond dissimilar polymers.
> - **Barrier (EVOH)** — blocks oxygen ingress.
> - **Sealant (PE)** — heat-seals to form the pouch.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Lifecycle Diagram — Plastics Packaging Value Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Raw Polymer Resin] --> P[Processing: Extrusion / Blow Molding]
    P --> PK[Packaged Product]
    PK --> U[Consumer Use]
    U --> S[Sorting at End-of-Life]
    S -- Recyclable --> RC[Mechanical Recycling]
    S -- Non-Recyclable --> LF[Landfill / Incineration]
    S -- Compostable --> CM[Industrial Composting]
    RC --> R
```

---

## 4.5 Architecture-Level Functional Flow — Plastics Package Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Identify Product Requirements] --> N2[Define Shelf Life Target]
    N2 --> N3[Select Barrier Specification]
    N3 --> N4[Choose Polymer Family]
    N4 --> N5[Design Package Geometry]
    N5 --> N6[Prototype & Testing]
    N6 --> N7{Tests Passed?}
    N7 -- Yes --> N8[Mass Production]
    N7 -- No --> N5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define plastics packaging and list any four functions it performs.**

**Model Answer:**

> Plastics packaging is the use of **polymeric materials** (thermoplastics and thermosets) to **contain, protect, preserve, transport, and present** products across industries.

**Four functions (the 4 P's or 5 P's framework):**
1. **Protection** — from mechanical and environmental damage.
2. **Preservation** — barrier to oxygen, moisture, and light.
3. **Presentation** — aesthetic and branding value.
4. **Portion control** — accurate dosing of contents.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between thermoplastics and thermosets used in packaging.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> | Property | Thermoplastics | Thermosets |
> |----------|----------------|------------|
> | Heating behavior | Soften & melt | Harden permanently |
> | Crosslinking | No (linear chains) | Yes (3D network) |
> | Recyclability | High (re-meltable) | Negligible |
> | Examples | PE, PP, PET, PS | Melamine, Phenolic |
> | Packaging use | Bottles, films, trays | Specialty closures |

---

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with examples the classification of plastics used in packaging. Compare thermoplastic and thermoset behavior.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A multilayer co-extruded film consists of three layers: LDPE (thickness 60 μm, permeability 200 barrer), EVOH (thickness 15 μm, permeability 0.05 barrer), and PET (thickness 25 μm, permeability 50 barrer). Calculate the **equivalent permeability** of the laminate and comment on the role of EVOH.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A (a)

**Classification of Plastics Used in Packaging:**

Plastics are broadly classified into **two categories** based on their response to heat and thermal processing:

**1. Thermoplastics** — Linear or branched chain polymers that **soften on heating and harden on cooling**. They can be **re-melted and reprocessed multiple times**, making them **recyclable**.

**Sub-classification of Thermoplastics:**

- **Polyethylene (PE) Family**:
  - **LDPE (Low-Density PE)** — used in **squeeze bottles, cling films, and flexible packaging** due to its high flexibility and transparency.
  - **HDPE (High-Density PE)** — used in **milk jugs, detergent bottles, and rigid containers** due to its high strength and chemical resistance.
  - **LLDPE (Linear Low-Density PE)** — used in **stretch films and industrial liners** due to superior puncture resistance.

- **Polypropylene (PP)** — used in **yogurt cups, bottle caps, microwave containers** owing to its high melting point (~165°C) and good fatigue resistance.

- **Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET)** — used in **carbonated beverage bottles and food trays** owing to its excellent clarity, strength, and CO₂ barrier.

- **Polystyrene (PS)** — used in **foam cups and protective packaging**; however, restricted due to environmental concerns.

- **Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)** — used in **blister packs and cling films**; also restricted in food-contact applications.

- **Ethylene Vinyl Alcohol (EVOH)** — used as a **barrier layer in multi-layer films** for oxygen-sensitive foods.

**2. Thermosets** — Polymers that undergo **irreversible crosslinking** during curing. Once set, they **cannot be remelted or reprocessed**.

**Examples:** Melamine formaldehyde (used in dinnerware), phenolic resins (used in heat-resistant closures), epoxy (used in laminates and coatings).

**Comparison of Thermoplastics vs. Thermosets in Packaging:**

- **Thermoplastics** dominate the packaging industry (over **95%** of usage) due to recyclability and ease of processing.
- **Thermosets** are used only in niche applications requiring high heat resistance.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A (b)

**Step 1: Identify the layers and apply the series-resistance model for laminate permeability.**

$$
\dfrac{L_{total}}{P_{eq}} = \dfrac{L_1}{P_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{P_2} + \dfrac{L_3}{P_3}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{L_{total}}{P_{eq}} &= \dfrac{60}{200} + \dfrac{15}{0.05} + \dfrac{25}{50} \\
&= 0.300 + 300.000 + 0.500 \\
&= 300.800 \text{ (units: }\mu m / \text{barrer)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total thickness.**

$$
L_{total} = 60 + 15 + 25 = 100 \,\mu m
$$

**Step 4: Compute $P_{eq}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{eq} &= \dfrac{100}{300.800} \\
P_{eq} &\approx 0.3325 \text{ barrer}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Comment on EVOH's role.**

The EVOH layer (15 μm) contributes **300/300.8 ≈ 99.7%** of the total resistance to oxygen transmission. Without EVOH, the laminate permeability would be:

$$
P_{eq, \, \text{no EVOH}} \approx \dfrac{100}{(60/200 + 25/50)} = \dfrac{100}{0.8} = 125 \text{ barrer}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The thin EVOH layer **reduces oxygen permeability by a factor of ~376×**. This is why EVOH is **indispensable** in packaging oxygen-sensitive products such as **meat, cheese, and wine**.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the historical evolution of plastics packaging with key milestones. Explain the global significance of plastics in the modern packaging industry.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A PET bottle has a total volume of 500 mL. The bottle (with cap) weighs 25 g. After use, it is recycled. The recycling process recovers 18 g of usable PET. Calculate the **recycling yield** and **sustainability index** if the bio-based carbon content is 15% and compostability is 0%.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B (a)

**Historical Evolution:**

- **1862**: **Cellulose Nitrate (Parkesine)** — first semi-synthetic plastic used for packaging.
- **1907**: **Bakelite** — first fully synthetic plastic; used for rigid containers.
- **1933**: **Polyethylene (PE)** discovered by ICI — began the era of flexible plastic films.
- **1941**: **PET** patented — became the global standard for beverage bottles.
- **1950s**: **PP and PS** introduced — expanded rigid packaging applications.
- **1970s**: **Co-extrusion technology** developed — enabled multi-layer barrier films.
- **1990s**: **Aseptic packaging** (Tetra Pak) and **MAP** revolutionized food preservation.
- **2010s–2020s**: **Bio-polymers (PLA, PHA)** and **recyclable mono-material designs** emerged due to sustainability pressures.

**Global Significance:**

- Plastics account for **~40% of all packaging by volume** globally.
- Global packaging market size: **~USD 1.1 trillion** (2023).
- Plastics packaging enables **food waste reduction** by extending shelf life.
- Lightweighting reduces **transportation emissions** (PET bottle is ~25 g vs. glass bottle ~200 g).
- Drives innovation in **medical, pharmaceutical, and e-commerce** sectors.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B (b)

**Step 1: Compute recycling yield.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R\% &= \left(1 - \dfrac{w_{loss}}{w_{initial}}\right) \times 100 \\
&= \left(1 - \dfrac{25 - 18}{25}\right) \times 100 \\
&= \left(1 - \dfrac{7}{25}\right) \times 100 \\
&= 72\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Sustainability Index.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SI &= \dfrac{R\% + B\% + C\%}{3} \\
&= \dfrac{72 + 15 + 0}{3} \\
SI &= 29\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A 72% recycling yield is **good but not optimal** (industry target >90%). The sustainability index of 29% is **moderate**, primarily limited by the **non-compostable nature** of PET.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — Always convert mm to cm (or μm to m) before substituting in formulas. Students frequently mix units and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Wrong laminate permeability formula** — Use the **series-resistance model** $\sum (L_i / P_i)$ for layered films, **NOT the parallel model**.
> 3. **Confusing OTR and WVTR units** — OTR uses **cm³**, WVTR uses **grams** of water vapor.
> 4. **Missing units in final answers** — Always write units (e.g., g, barrer, %) in the final boxed answer.
> 5. **Writing "plastic" without specifying thermoplastic/thermoset** — Examiners deduct marks for lack of specificity.
> 6. **Skipping diagrams in long answers** — Even a simple hand-drawn Mermaid-style block diagram earns **1–2 extra marks**.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - Plastics packaging uses **polymers** (thermoplastics + thermosets).
> - **4–5 P's framework**: Protect, Preserve, Present, Portion, Provide Info.
> - **Thermoplastics** (PE, PP, PET, PS) are **recyclable**; **thermosets** are **not**.
> - **PET** dominates beverage bottles; **HDPE** dominates milk jugs; **PP** dominates microwave containers.
> - **EVOH** is the **gold standard oxygen barrier** in multi-layer films.
> - Key formulas: $m = \rho V$, OTR, WVTR, **series permeability model**.
> - **Sustainability Index** = average of (Recyclability + Bio-content + Compostability).
> - **Co-extrusion** allows combining multiple polymer properties in one film.
> - Plastics packaging reduces **food waste** and **transportation emissions** compared to glass/metal.
> - **Mono-material design** is the current industry trend for circular economy compliance.

---

*End of Module 1 Notes — Introduction to Plastics Packaging*
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | PEPOT748*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functions of Packaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Alignment):**
> *Packaging is defined as a coordinated system of preparing goods for transport, distribution, storage, retailing, and end-use, such that the product reaches the consumer in the intended condition, at optimum cost, and with maximum informational and protective efficiency.*

In the context of **Plastics Packaging Technology (PEPOT748)**, packaging is not merely a wrapper — it is an engineered barrier and information carrier that integrates **material science**, **mechanical design**, **food science**, and **marketing logistics** into a single deliverable artefact.

The **primary functions of packaging** are universally accepted (per *Soroka* and *Robertson*) as belonging to four core categories, often remembered by the mnemonic **PICS**:

| Letter | Function | Engineering Intent |
|--------|----------|--------------------|
| **P** | Protection | Resist physical, chemical, biological hazards |
| **I** | Information | Convey regulatory, nutritional, brand data |
| **C** | Containment | Hold the product without leakage or deformation |
| **S** | Convenience | Enable handling, opening, dispensing, reuse |

A fifth modern addition is **Sustainability**, demanded by the 2024 KTU syllabus on circular economy and EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) compliance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module Highlight:**
> The current syllabus explicitly links the *functions of packaging* to the **selection of plastics** (PET, HDPE, PP, PVC, PS, EVOH, PVDC, multilayer laminates). Each function dictates a different polymer property requirement.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are shipping a **delicate glass jar of honey** from Kerala to a customer in Rajasthan.

- The **glass jar** is your *product* — fragile, valuable, and chemically sensitive to moisture gain/loss.
- The **plastic shrink-wrap** around the lid prevents honey from leaking and tampering.
- The **PET bottle** around the jar protects against impact.
- The **cardboard box** (often with an inner LDPE liner) cushions against drops.
- The **printed label** tells the customer about weight, expiry, FSSAI number, barcode.
- The **plastic carry-hand** on the box lets the delivery agent lift it easily.
- The **recycling symbol (♳ – ♷)** reminds everyone that the PET is recyclable.

**Every single one of these is a function of packaging.** Now replace the glass with a pharmaceutical tablet, the honey with a powder, the customer with a hospital — and you instantly see why **functions of packaging drive the engineering of plastics selection**.

### 1.3 Classification of Packaging Functions

Packaging functions are typically grouped under **three hierarchical levels**:

1. **Primary Functions** (essential, non-negotiable):
   Containment + Protection + Preservation
2. **Secondary Functions** (regulatory & commercial):
   Communication + Information + Marketing
3. **Tertiary Functions** (logistics):
   Handling + Unitisation + Distribution efficiency

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:**
> *"The 4 S's — **S**afety, **S**ale, **S**upply, **S**ustainability"* — these are the four pillars on which any packaging function stands in the modern KTU 2024 outcome-based framework.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Packaging Engineering

The following **quantitative metrics** govern packaging function evaluation (must be memorised for KTU University Exams):

- **WVTR** — Water Vapour Transmission Rate ($\text{g/m}^2\cdot\text{day}$)
- **OTR** — Oxygen Transmission Rate ($\text{cm}^3/\text{m}^2\cdot\text{day}\cdot\text{atm}$)
- **MFI / MFR** — Melt Flow Index of the plastic ($\text{g/10 min}$)
- **BCT** — Box Compression Test strength ($\text{N}$ or $\text{kN}$)
- **Drop Test Height** ($\text{cm}$) — ASTM D4169
- **Cobb Value** — water absorption of paper ($\text{g/m}^2$)
- **Shelf Life** ($\text{days}$ or $\text{months}$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All these metrics appear frequently in KTU 2024 Part-A (3-mark) and Part-B (14-mark) questions. Memorising the units is non-negotiable.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Function-by-Function Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Containment Function

The **first and most fundamental** function. A package must hold its product from filling to final disposal without leakage.

- **Engineering Logic**: Requires mechanical integrity (seam strength, burst resistance) and chemical inertness of the plastic with the contents.
- **Common Plastics Used**: HDPE for chemicals, PET for beverages, PP for hot-fill, PVC for blister packs.
- **Failure Mode**: Stress cracking, seal failure, permeation-driven migration.

### 2.1.2 Protection Function

Protection is **multidimensional** and is broken down by the *type of hazard*:

| Hazard Type | Description | Relevant Plastic Property |
|-------------|-------------|---------------------------|
| **Mechanical** | Shock, vibration, compression | Tensile strength, impact resistance, modulus |
| **Climatic** | Heat, cold, humidity, UV | HDT, glass transition $T_g$, UV stabilisers |
| **Biological** | Microbes, insects, rodents | Hermetic seal, anti-microbial additives |
| **Chemical** | Oxygen, moisture, aroma loss | Barrier properties (WVTR, OTR) |
| **Electromagnetic** | Light-induced degradation | UV-blocking additives, opaque pigments |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:**
> No single plastic provides all protection — therefore **multilayer co-extrusion** (e.g., PET/EVOH/PE) is the industry standard. EVOH provides oxygen barrier, while PE provides moisture seal.

### 2.1.3 Preservation Function

Preservation extends the **shelf life** of the product by controlling the internal atmosphere of the package.

- **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)**: Replaces $\text{O}_2$ with $\text{N}_2$ or $\text{CO}_2$.
- **Vacuum Packaging**: Removes air entirely; uses EVOH/PA-based multilayer plastics.
- **Active Packaging**: Oxygen scavengers, moisture absorbers, ethanol emitters.
- **Intelligent Packaging**: Time-temperature indicators (TTI) — usually printed on plastic film.

### 2.1.4 Communication / Information Function

Plastics packages communicate via **printed surfaces, labels, embossing, QR codes, and tamper-evident bands**.

- Mandatory information includes: MRP, weight, batch number, manufacturing/ expiry date, FSSAI, ISI mark, recycling code.
- The printability of a plastic depends on **surface energy** (must be $> 38 \text{ dyne/cm}$ for ink adhesion — usually achieved by **corona treatment** of PE/PP films).

### 2.1.5 Convenience Function

Consumer-driven functions including:

- **Easy-open** (laser score on PET bottles, peelable lidding films).
- **Reclosability** (zip-lock pouches, screw caps on HDPE).
- **Microwaveability** (CPET trays).
- **Portability** (stand-up pouches with spouts).

### 2.1.6 Sustainability Function (KTU 2024 Mandate)

The newest and most exam-relevant function under the **NEP 2020 / KTU 2024** framework:

- **Reduce** — lightweighting of PET bottles.
- **Reuse** — refillable containers.
- **Recycle** — mono-material designs (all-PE or all-PET).
- **Recover** — energy recovery via incineration of non-recyclable plastics.
- **Redesign** — bio-based polymers (PLA, PHA).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Where Used |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------|------------|
| 1 | Permeation flux | $J = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}$ | $\text{cm}^3\cdot\text{cm}/\text{cm}^2\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{cm Hg}$ | Barrier design |
| 2 | Shelf life (moisture-sensitive) | $\theta_s = \dfrac{W_c \cdot m_p}{P_{H_2O}\cdot A\cdot \Delta RH}$ | days | Desiccant / food packs |
| 3 | Shelf life (oxygen-sensitive) | $\theta_s = \dfrac{m_{O_2}^{\text{crit}}}{R_{O_2}\cdot A}$ | days | MAP, vacuum packs |
| 4 | Compression strength (McKee formula) | $\text{BCT} = k_1 \cdot \text{ECT}^0.5 \cdot \text{ stiffness}^0.5$ | $\text{N}$ | Transport packaging |
| 5 | Cobb value | $C = \dfrac{m_{wet} - m_{dry}}{A}$ | $\text{g/m}^2$ | Paper-based laminates |
| 6 | Headspace oxygen gain | $V_{O_2} = \dfrac{R_{O_2}\cdot A \cdot t}{V_{hs}}$ | fraction | MAP design |
| 7 | Cost-per-unit | $C_{pu} = \dfrac{C_{pkg}}{N_{units}}$ | ₹/unit | Optimisation |
| 8 | Light transmission | $T_{\lambda} = \dfrac{I_t}{I_0}$ | dimensionless | UV-barrier films |
| 9 | Tensile stress in a pouch | $\sigma = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{t}$ | $\text{MPa}$ | Blow-moulded bottles |
| 10 | Crimp / seal strength | $F_s = W \cdot \sigma_s$ | $\text{N}$ | Heat-sealed pouches |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In LaTeX rendering, the absolute magnitude (e.g., $T_g$ value or modulus) should be written using $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ rather than ASCII pipes — this preserves both LaTeX rendering and KTU markdown formatting.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The functions framework is the **starting point of every packaging design brief** in industry:

1. A **pharmaceutical company** will prioritise *protection + information* (barrier against moisture + tamper evidence + batch tracking).
2. A **beverage brand** will prioritise *containment + convenience* (PET bottle + screw cap + shrink sleeve).
3. A **frozen food exporter** will prioritise *preservation + logistics* (multilayer PA/PE pouch + vacuum + corrugated shipper).
4. A **cosmetics brand** will prioritise *communication + convenience* (printed HDPE jar + pump dispenser).

Every function directly translates into a **material, a process, a cost line, and a regulatory check** — which is why KTU examiners test this topic extensively.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Shelf Life of a Moisture-Sensitive Product

> **Problem Setup:**
> A pharmaceutical tablet weighing $m_p = 5\,\text{g}$ is packed in an HDPE bottle of wall thickness $L = 1.2\,\text{mm}$. The water-vapour permeability of the HDPE is $P_{H_2O} = 1.2\times 10^{-13}\,\text{cm}^3\cdot\text{cm}/\text{cm}^2\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{cm Hg}$. The internal surface area is $A = 120\,\text{cm}^2$. The critical moisture content is $W_c = 2\%$ of tablet weight, and the relative humidity difference $\Delta RH = 60\%$. Calculate the shelf life.

### Step 1: State the governing equation

The shelf life for moisture-sensitive goods is derived from **Fick's first law of diffusion** through the package wall:

$$\theta_s = \frac{W_c \cdot m_p}{P_{H_2O}\cdot A \cdot \Delta RH}$$

where $\Delta RH$ is expressed as a partial-pressure ratio (i.e., $60\% = 0.60$ in consistent units).

### Step 2: Substitute numerical values (with explicit unit conversion)

Given:
- $W_c = 0.02$ (dimensionless fraction)
- $m_p = 5\,\text{g}$
- $P_{H_2O} = 1.2\times 10^{-13}\,\text{cm}^3\cdot\text{cm}/\text{cm}^2\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{cm Hg}$
- $A = 120\,\text{cm}^2$
- $\Delta RH = 0.60$ (dimensionless fraction, equivalent to $0.60\,\text{cm Hg}$ normalised)

### Step 3: Numerator evaluation

The numerator is:

$$W_c \cdot m_p = 0.02 \times 5 = 0.10\,\text{g of water}$$

### Step 4: Denominator evaluation

The denominator is:

$$P_{H_2O}\cdot A \cdot \Delta RH = 1.2\times 10^{-13} \times 120 \times 0.60$$

Computing step-by-step:

$$\begin{aligned}
1.2\times 10^{-13} \times 120 &= 1.44\times 10^{-11} \\
1.44\times 10^{-11} \times 0.60 &= 8.64\times 10^{-12}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Final division

$$\theta_s = \frac{0.10}{8.64\times 10^{-12}} \approx 1.157\times 10^{10}\,\text{seconds}$$

Converting to days:

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_s \,\text{[days]} &= \frac{1.157\times 10^{10}}{86400} \\
\theta_s &\approx 133{,}912\,\text{days} \approx 367\,\text{years}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU Exam:** The very long shelf life confirms that the chosen HDPE wall thickness is *over-engineered* for this moisture-sensitive product — a valid design review observation. If $\theta_s$ had come out *below the labelled shelf life*, the engineer would have to switch to **PP, multilayer with EVOH, or add a desiccant**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Compression Strength of a Corrugated Shipper (McKee Equation)

The Box Compression Test (BCT) value is derived empirically:

$$\text{BCT} = k_1 \cdot \text{ECT}^a \cdot \text{D}^{b}$$

Simplified form commonly tested:

$$\text{BCT} = k_1 \cdot \text{ECT}^{0.5}\cdot \text{Stiffness}^{0.5}$$

with $k_1 \approx 0.59$ for regular slotted containers.

**Numerical substitution:**

Given: $\text{ECT} = 6\,\text{kN/m}$, $\text{Stiffness} = 250\,\text{N\cdot cm}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{BCT} &= 0.59 \times \sqrt{6} \times \sqrt{250} \\
&= 0.59 \times 2.449 \times 15.811 \\
&\approx 22.85\,\text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

This BCT is the **tertiary-protection function** quantified — used by KTU examiners to test if a student understands the *logistics-function link*.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Function vs. Plastic Selection

| Plastic | Containment | Protection | Preservation | Communication | Convenience | Sustainability |
|---------|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| **PET** | High | Medium | Medium | Excellent (print) | High (clear) | High (recyclable ♳) |
| **HDPE** | High | High | Medium | Fair | Medium | High (♴) |
| **PVC** | High | Low | Low | Excellent | Low | Low (♶ issue) |
| **LDPE** | Medium | Medium | Low | Fair | High | Medium (♴) |
| **PP** | High | High | Medium-High | Fair | High (microwave) | High (♵) |
| **PS** | High | Low | Low | Good | Medium | Low (♸) |
| **EVOH** | – | – | Very High (O₂) | – | – | – |
| **PA (Nylon)** | – | Very High (puncture) | High | – | – | – |
| **PLA** | Medium | Low | Low | Good | Medium | Excellent (bio) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:**
> Always relate the *function* to a *property* to a *polymer*. Examiners award full marks only when the chain is complete. Example: *Protection from oxygen → high O₂ barrier → EVOH layer in PET/EVOH/PE laminate.*

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Tree for Function-Based Selection

1. **Is the product moisture-sensitive?**
   - Yes → prioritise low **WVTR** → use **PP, HDPE, or metallised PET**.
2. **Is the product oxygen-sensitive?**
   - Yes → prioritise low **OTR** → use **EVOH, PVDC, or metallised films**.
3. **Is light-induced degradation an issue?**
   - Yes → use **opaque / pigmented / UV-blocking additives**.
4. **Does it need a long shelf life (>12 months)?**
   - Yes → adopt **multilayer co-extrusion** or **aluminium foil laminate**.
5. **Is the product fragile?**
   - Yes → use **cushioning plastics (EPE foam, EPS, bubble wrap)**.

---


> [!NOTE]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
*List and briefly explain the four primary functions of packaging as per Soroka's classification.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four primary functions of packaging, known as the **PICS framework**, are:

1. **Protection** — Guarding the product from mechanical, climatic, chemical, and biological hazards during storage and transit.
2. **Information** — Communicating product details such as ingredients, batch number, expiry, weight, and regulatory marks (FSSAI, ISI) to the consumer and authorities.
3. **Containment** — Holding the product securely without leakage or deformation from filling to consumption.
4. **Convenience / Containment-Aid** — Facilitating handling, opening, dispensing, and reuse by the consumer.

*(Mark split: 1 mark per function × 3 = 3 marks; one extra point for naming the framework.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
*Why is EVOH commonly used as an inner layer in multilayer plastic packaging?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**EVOH (Ethylene Vinyl Alcohol)** is used as an inner oxygen-barrier layer in multilayer structures (e.g., PET/EVOH/PE) because:

1. It exhibits an exceptionally **low Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR)** of $\approx 0.5\,\text{cm}^3/\text{m}^2\cdot\text{day}\cdot\text{atm}$, far superior to PE or PP.
2. It is **transparent**, allowing the consumer to see the product.
3. However, EVOH is **moisture-sensitive** — hence it must be sandwiched between moisture-barrier plastics like PE or PET.

*(Mark split: Property explanation 2 marks + reason for sandwiching 1 mark.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often write **functions in generic language** without linking each one to a *polymer property*. Always end each function with the material science implication.
> 2. Students forget the **units** of WVTR and OTR — losing at least 1 mark per question.
> 3. In derivation problems, skipping the **unit conversion step** leads to a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. Avoid confusing **protection** with **preservation** — *protection* is from external hazards; *preservation* is from internal degradation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** *(7 Marks)*
Explain in detail the **protection function** of plastic packaging. Discuss the different types of hazards a package must guard against, and the specific plastic materials used for each.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The protection function ensures the product is shielded from external environmental and mechanical hazards that may degrade quality, safety, or aesthetics during distribution and shelf life.

**Classification of Hazards (4 marks):**

| Hazard Type | Description | Plastic Solution |
|-------------|-------------|------------------|
| **Mechanical** | Drop, vibration, compression | HDPE crates, EPS foam, EPE cushioning |
| **Climatic** | Humidity, temperature, UV | PP with UV stabiliser, metallised PET |
| **Chemical** | $\text{O}_2$, moisture ingress | EVOH, PVDC, multilayer |
| **Biological** | Microbes, rodents | Hermetic seals, anti-microbial masterbatch |
| **Electromagnetic** | Visible/UV light | Amber PET, titanium dioxide pigmented PE |

**Material-Property Linkage (2 marks):**
For each hazard, the engineer must map the *required property* → *plastic grade*. Example: $\text{O}_2$ barrier requirement → EVOH co-extruded with PET and PE.

---

**(b)** *(7 Marks)*
A food product packed in a plastic pouch has the following data:
- Pouch area $A = 400\,\text{cm}^2$
- Wall thickness $L = 0.1\,\text{mm}$
- $\text{O}_2$ permeability $P_{O_2} = 5\times 10^{-13}\,\text{cm}^3\cdot\text{cm}/\text{cm}^2\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{cm Hg}$
- Partial-pressure difference $\Delta p = 0.21\,\text{atm} \approx 16\,\text{cm Hg}$
- Critical $\text{O}_2$ absorbed $= 5\,\text{cm}^3$ at STP

Calculate the **shelf life** of the product.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Governing equation for oxygen-sensitive shelf life:
$$\theta_s = \frac{V_{O_2}^{crit}}{R_{O_2}\cdot A}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Oxygen transmission rate calculation:
$$R_{O_2} = \frac{P_{O_2}\cdot \Delta p}{L}$$

$$R_{O_2} = \frac{5\times 10^{-13} \times 16}{0.01}$$

$$R_{O_2} = 8\times 10^{-10}\,\text{cm}^3/\text{cm}^2\cdot\text{s}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Total flux through pouch:
$$Q_{O_2} = R_{O_2}\cdot A = 8\times 10^{-10}\times 400 = 3.2\times 10^{-7}\,\text{cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Shelf life calculation:
$$\theta_s = \frac{5}{3.2\times 10^{-7}} = 1.5625\times 10^{7}\,\text{seconds}$$

Convert to days:
$$\theta_s = \frac{1.5625\times 10^{7}}{86400} \approx 180.8\,\text{days} \approx 6\,\text{months}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The shelf life of approximately **6 months** is acceptable for most ambient-shelf food products; however, switching to a metallised laminate would extend it to 12–18 months.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]

**(a)** *(7 Marks)*
Discuss the **preservation function** of packaging. Explain MAP, vacuum packaging, active packaging, and intelligent packaging with relevant plastic materials.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Preservation extends shelf life by controlling the internal package environment through atmosphere modification, scavenging, or real-time monitoring.

**MAP — Modified Atmosphere Packaging (2 marks):**
- Replaces headspace $\text{O}_2$ with $\text{N}_2$ or $\text{CO}_2$.
- Plastics used: **PA/PE multilayer, EVOH-based co-extruded films**.
- Application: fresh meat, cheese, salads.

**Vacuum Packaging (1 mark):**
- Removes air; uses high-barrier **PA/EVOH/PE** structures.
- Application: coffee, processed meat, dried fruits.

**Active Packaging (2 marks):**
- Incorporates scavengers (oxygen, ethylene) and emitters (ethanol, $\text{CO}_2$).
- Plastics used: **LDPE/PP films with embedded sachets or masterbatch additives**.

**Intelligent Packaging (1 mark):**
- Time-Temperature Indicators (TTI), freshness sensors printed on **PET or PP labels**.
- Communicates remaining shelf life via colour change.

---

**(b)** *(7 Marks)*
Explain the **sustainability function** of packaging under the KTU 2024 framework. Discuss the 5R approach with one plastic example for each.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The sustainability function ensures packaging minimises environmental burden across its life cycle — from raw material to end-of-life — in line with India's **Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2022** and KTU 2024 syllabus.

**5R Framework (5 marks — 1 mark each):**

| R-Principle | Engineering Action | Plastic Example |
|-------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **Reduce** | Lightweighting | PET bottle: 32 g → 22 g |
| **Reuse** | Refill systems | HDPE milk crate returnable loop |
| **Recycle** | Mono-material design | All-PE stand-up pouch |
| **Recover** | Energy from waste | Incineration of mixed plastics |
| **Redesign** | Bio-based polymers | PLA from corn-starch |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Under the KTU 2024 outcome-based framework, every packaging function must include a **sustainability audit** — failure to comply leads to EPR penalties under Indian law.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Functions of Packaging:**

- ⭐ The **four primary functions** are: Protection, Information, Containment, Convenience — known as **PICS**.
- ⭐ A modern fifth function is **Sustainability** (mandatory under KTU 2024 / NEP 2020).
- ⭐ Packaging levels: **Primary** (direct contact), **Secondary** (grouping), **Tertiary** (logistics).
- ⭐ **WVTR** unit = $\text{g/m}^2\cdot\text{day}$; **OTR** unit = $\text{cm}^3/\text{m}^2\cdot\text{day}\cdot\text{atm}$.
- ⭐ **EVOH** = oxygen barrier; **PE/PP** = moisture barrier; **PET** = clarity + mechanical; **HDPE** = chemical resistance; **PA (Nylon)** = puncture resistance.
- ⭐ **McKee equation** links Edge Crush Test (ECT) and stiffness to Box Compression Test (BCT).
- ⭐ Shelf-life formulas must be quoted in both **seconds and days** in derivations.
- ⭐ Always end a function discussion with the **material property + polymer grade** — this is the KTU examiner's benchmark for full marks.
- ⭐ The **5R framework** (Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover, Redesign) is mandatory for the sustainability function.
- ⭐ Print adhesion on PE/PP requires **corona treatment** to raise surface energy above $38 \text{ dyne/cm}$.
- ⭐ MAP and vacuum packaging are *preservation* techniques — not protection techniques.
- ⭐ Multilayer co-extrusion (e.g., PET/EVOH/PE) is the **industry-standard solution** when both $\text{O}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ barriers are required.
- ⭐ Recycling codes ♳–♷ must appear on all consumer-facing plastic packages in India.

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Plastic packaging** refers to the use of polymeric materials — either *thermoplastics* (e.g., PE, PP, PET, PVC) or *thermosets* (e.g., epoxy, phenolic) — fabricated into rigid, semi-rigid, or flexible containers, films, sheets, laminates, and foams, intended for the **containment, protection, preservation, identification, and convenient handling** of a product from the point of manufacture through to the end consumer.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcome (CO1)** of PEPOT748, students must be able to *"identify the role and enumerate the advantages of plastics as a packaging medium over conventional materials."*

The term **"advantage"** in this context is multi-dimensional and is evaluated across four engineering pillars:
1. **Functional Performance** (mechanical, barrier, optical)
2. **Economic Efficiency** (cost-per-unit, mass-saving economics)
3. **Aesthetic & Design Versatility** (formability, printability)
4. **Logistical & Environmental Footprint** (transport, recyclability)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Swiss Army Knife" of Materials

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine you are packing a **delicate glass souvenir** for a long international flight. You are given three options:
>
> - **Wooden box** — heavy, bulky, hard to open, no see-through feature.
> - **Metal tin** — strong, but heavy, prone to denting, cannot be resealed easily.
> - **Plastic blister pack** — light, transparent (you can see the souvenir), can be moulded exactly to the shape of the object, costs almost nothing, and can be opened with a finger push.
>
> That single plastic blister solves *visibility*, *weight*, *customization*, *cost*, and *user convenience* all at once. **This is precisely why plastic packaging dominates modern industry.** It is the *Swiss Army knife* of the packaging world — one material family that adapts to hundreds of different jobs.

---

## 1.3 Why Plastics Became the Dominant Packaging Medium

The shift from traditional packaging materials (glass, metal, paper, wood) to plastics began in the mid-20th century, driven by post-WWII polymer chemistry breakthroughs. Today, **plastics account for approximately 36%–40% of the global packaging market by volume**, making it the single largest packaging material category worldwide.

The fundamental reason is captured by a single engineering reality:

> **Plastics offer the highest *property-to-weight ratio* (specific performance) of any packaging material class.**

This statement is quantified by the concept of **Specific Strength**:

$$\sigma_{sp} = \frac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{\rho}$$

where $\sigma_{ultimate}$ is the ultimate tensile strength and $\rho$ is the density. Plastics consistently outperform metals and glass on this metric for non-structural packaging applications.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Terminology Snapshot (KTU Must-Know)**
> - **Tensile Strength**: Resistance to breaking under tension (MPa)
> - **Elongation at Break**: % stretch before failure (%)
> - **WVTR**: Water Vapour Transmission Rate (g/m²/day)
> - **OTR**: Oxygen Transmission Rate (cc/m²/day)
> - **Modulus (Young's)**: Stiffness indicator (MPa)
> - **Heat Seal Strength**: Force required to peel a heat seal (N/15mm)

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Categorical Framework of Plastic Packaging Advantages

The advantages of plastic packaging can be organized into **six principal engineering categories**. Each category is explained with its underlying scientific basis.

### Category A — Mechanical & Physical Advantages

1. **Lightweight Nature (Low Density)**
   - Most commodity plastics have densities between **0.85 g/cm³ and 1.45 g/cm³**, far lower than glass (~2.5 g/cm³) and steel (~7.85 g/cm³).
   - *Why it matters:* Lower mass → reduced transportation fuel cost → lower carbon footprint in logistics.

2. **High Strength-to-Weight Ratio**
   - Engineering plastics like **PET (~55 MPa tensile)** and **HDPE (~31 MPa tensile)** deliver sufficient mechanical performance at a fraction of metal weight.

3. **Flexibility & Elongation**
   - LDPE can elongate up to **400%–600%** before breaking. This allows drop-impact absorption — critical for food and pharma bottles.

4. **Impact Resistance & Toughness**
   - Plastics do not shatter like glass. A PET bottle survives a 1.2 m drop test that would destroy a glass bottle.

5. **Tear and Puncture Resistance (in films)**
   - Biaxially oriented films (BOPP, BOPA, BOPET) exhibit directional strength that paper and aluminium foil cannot match.

### Category B — Barrier Advantages

6. **Tunable Permeability**
   - Through **co-extrusion, lamination, or metallization**, plastic structures can be engineered to have tailored **WVTR** and **OTR** values.

7. **Chemical Inertness**
   - Most packaging plastics (PE, PP, PET) are **non-reactive** with food, beverages, and pharmaceuticals. This prevents contamination and tainting.

### Category C — Design & Manufacturing Advantages

8. **Ease of Fabrication (Thermoplastic Processability)**
   - Plastics can be processed by injection moulding, blow moulding, extrusion, thermoforming, and rotational moulding.
   - **Cycle times** as short as **5–15 seconds** for injection moulded caps.

9. **Complex Geometry Realization**
   - One moulded plastic part can replace an assembly of 5–10 metal parts — reducing assembly cost and failure points.

10. **Versatility in Form**
    - Plastics can be made **rigid** (PET bottles), **flexible** (PE films), **foamed** (EPS coolers), or **stretchable** (cling wrap).

11. **Printability & Decoratability**
    - Surfaces accept **offset, gravure, flexo, digital, and in-mould labelling (IML)** printing.

### Category D — Economic Advantages

12. **Low Material Cost**
    - Raw polymer cost typically ranges from **₹80–₹150 per kg** for commodity grades, compared to **₹15–₹30 per piece** for glass containers.

13. **Low Production Cost (High Volume Scalability)**
    - Continuous processes like blown-film extrusion produce **kilometres of film per hour**.

14. **Reduced Logistics Cost**
    - Empty plastic containers are **nestable** or **collapsible** — saving return-trip freight cost. A pallet of empty PET bottles takes 1/8th the volume of empty glass bottles.

### Category E — Consumer & Aesthetic Advantages

15. **Transparency / Clarity**
    - Allows consumer to view the product — a marketing advantage over opaque metal cans.

16. **Resealability & Convenience**
    - Zip locks, screw caps, snap lids, and dispensing closures are easily moulded into plastic.

17. **Tamper Evidence**
    - Inductions seals, breakable rings, and blister packs provide visible tamper evidence.

### Category F — Sustainability (Conditional) Advantages

18. **Lower Energy Footprint in Production**
    - Producing 1 kg of plastic packaging typically consumes **60–80 MJ**, versus **~150 MJ** for glass and **~200 MJ** for aluminium.

19. **Recyclability**
    - Plastics like PET, HDPE, and PP have well-established recycling streams (resin codes **#1, #2, #5**).

20. **Reduced Food Waste (Indirect Environmental Benefit)**
    - Effective plastic packaging **extends shelf life**, which reduces food spoilage — currently the larger share of packaging's environmental burden per LCA studies.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All equations below are routinely tested in KTU university exams for PEPOT748.**

| Formula | Meaning | Typical Units | Used For |
|---------|---------|---------------|----------|
| $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_{u}}{\rho}$ | Specific strength | $\dfrac{\text{MPa}}{\text{g/cm}^3}$ | Comparing strength vs weight |
| $\% \varepsilon = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | Elongation at break | % | Film flexibility |
| $\text{WVTR} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t}$ | Water vapour transmission rate | $\dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day}}$ | Moisture barrier |
| $\text{OTR} = \dfrac{V \cdot \Delta p}{A \cdot t \cdot \Delta p}$ | Oxygen transmission rate | $\dfrac{\text{cc}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day}}$ | Oxygen barrier |
| $P = D \cdot S$ | Permeability = Diffusivity × Solubility | $\dfrac{\text{cc}\cdot\text{cm}}{\text{cm}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{atm}}$ | Permeation theory |
| $\eta = \dfrac{1}{\rho} \cdot \dfrac{V}{t}$ | Throughput efficiency | $\dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{kg}\cdot\text{s}}$ | Production rate |
| $C_{unit} = \dfrac{C_{mat} + C_{proc} + C_{log}}{N_{units}}$ | Per-unit cost | ₹/piece | Economics |
| $\text{Efficiency}_{\text{trans}} = 1 - \dfrac{m_{pack}}{m_{prod} + m_{pack}}$ | Packaging mass fraction | dimensionless | Sustainability ratio |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe Symbol Rule (Internal):** All absolute value and ratio bars above use $\dfrac{}$ (display fraction) to avoid conflicts with markdown table syntax. **Never write $\vert x \vert$ inside a table row** — use $\lvert x \rvert$ if absolute bars are absolutely required.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Industry

The above advantages translate directly into commercial engineering practice:

- **Food Industry**: Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) using **EVOH/PE multilayer films** extends fresh meat shelf life from 3 days to 14 days.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry**: **PVC/Aclar blister packs** protect tablets from moisture (WVTR < 0.01 g/m²/day).
- **Beverage Industry**: **PET carbonated soft drink bottles** replaced glass in the 1980s — saving **~30% on logistics cost** per litre shipped.
- **E-Commerce**: **Bubble wrap, air pillows, and foam-in-place** systems leverage plastic's cushioning ability.
- **Automotive & Industrial**: Plastic pails and drums (HDPE) replaced steel drums for chemical storage due to corrosion resistance.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass-Saving Calculation: PET Bottle vs Glass Bottle

> **Engineering Question:** A beverage company currently packs 1 litre of juice in a glass bottle (mass = 350 g). They are evaluating a switch to a PET bottle. Estimate the **annual mass savings** if the company produces **10 million bottles per year**, and the PET bottle mass is **35 g**.

### Step 1 — Define Variables

Let:
- $m_{glass} = 350 \text{ g}$ (mass of one glass bottle)
- $m_{PET} = 35 \text{ g}$ (mass of one PET bottle)
- $N = 10 \times 10^6$ (number of bottles per year)

### Step 2 — Compute Mass Saved Per Bottle

$$\Delta m_{bottle} = m_{glass} - m_{PET}$$

$$\Delta m_{bottle} = 350 \text{ g} - 35 \text{ g} = 315 \text{ g per bottle}$$

### Step 3 — Multiply by Annual Production Volume

$$\Delta M_{annual} = \Delta m_{bottle} \times N$$

$$\Delta M_{annual} = 315 \text{ g/bottle} \times 10 \times 10^6 \text{ bottles}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Tonnes

$$\Delta M_{annual} = 315 \times 10^7 \text{ g} = 3.15 \times 10^9 \text{ g}$$

Converting to tonnes ($1 \text{ tonne} = 10^6 \text{ g}$):

$$\Delta M_{annual} = \dfrac{3.15 \times 10^9}{10^6} = 3150 \text{ tonnes per year}$$

### Step 5 — Interpret

> **Conclusion:** Switching from glass to PET saves **3,150 tonnes of packaging material per year**. At a typical diesel truck capacity of 10 tonnes per load, this represents **315 fewer truck trips annually** — a direct logistics, fuel, and emissions saving. This is the **quantitative face of the "lightweight" advantage**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cost-Per-Unit Comparison

> **Engineering Question:** Compare the **cost per 1000 units** of a 500 mL HDPE milk pouch vs. a 500 mL glass milk bottle.

**Given Data (representative KTU exam-style values):**
- HDPE pouch: $C_{mat} = \text{₹}0.80$, $C_{proc} = \text{₹}0.20$, $C_{log} = \text{₹}0.10$ per unit
- Glass bottle: $C_{mat} = \text{₹}8.00$, $C_{proc} = \text{₹}1.50$, $C_{log} = \text{₹}0.50$ per unit

### Step 1 — Apply Per-Unit Cost Formula

$$C_{unit} = C_{mat} + C_{proc} + C_{log}$$

For HDPE pouch:

$$C_{HDPE} = 0.80 + 0.20 + 0.10 = \text{₹}1.10 \text{ per unit}$$

For Glass bottle:

$$C_{glass} = 8.00 + 1.50 + 0.50 = \text{₹}10.00 \text{ per unit}$$

### Step 2 — Scale to 1000 Units

$$C_{HDPE,1000} = 1.10 \times 1000 = \text{₹}1100$$

$$C_{glass,1000} = 10.00 \times 1000 = \text{₹}10{,}000$$

### Step 3 — Compute Cost Ratio

$$\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{C_{glass}}{C_{HDPE}} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{1100} \approx 9.09$$

### Step 4 — Interpret

> **Conclusion:** The glass bottle costs **~9× more** than the HDPE pouch per 1000 units. This is the **economic face** of plastic packaging's advantage — and the reason the Indian dairy industry transitioned from glass to HDPE/LDPE flexible pouches in the 1970s–80s.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Permeability & Shelf-Life Relationship (Qualitative)

The permeability equation governing barrier advantage is:

$$P = D \cdot S$$

where $P$ = permeability, $D$ = diffusivity of permeant through polymer, $S$ = solubility coefficient of permeant in polymer.

For a multilayer plastic structure (e.g., PET/EVOH/PE), the **total permeability is governed by the inverse-additivity rule**:

$$\dfrac{L_{total}}{P_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{P_i}$$

where $L_i$ and $P_i$ are the thickness and permeability of the $i$-th layer.

> **Step-by-step logic:**
> - Permeation is a **series resistance** problem (like electrical resistors in series).
> - The layer with the **lowest permeability** (e.g., EVOH with $P_{O_2} \approx 0.01$ cc·cm/m²·day·atm) dominates the total barrier.
> - This is why a **thin EVOH layer** (~5–10 µm) sandwiched between two thicker PE/PP layers delivers oxygen barrier comparable to glass or metal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Students who write the **inverse-additivity formula** correctly in a Part B question often score the full 7 marks. Memorize it as the **"Resistance-in-Series" model for permeation.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Material Comparison

| Property | Glass | Steel | Aluminium | **Plastic (PET)** | **Plastic (HDPE)** |
|----------|-------|-------|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Density (g/cm³) | 2.5 | 7.85 | 2.7 | **1.38** | **0.95** |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 30–100 | 300–500 | 70–200 | **55–75** | **22–31** |
| Specific Strength (MPa·cm³/g) | 12–40 | 38–64 | 26–74 | **40–54** | **23–33** |
| OTR (cc/m²·day) | ~0 | ~0 | ~0 | **6–9** | **150–200** |
| WVTR (g/m²·day) | ~0 | ~0 | ~0 | **0.5–1.0** | **0.5–1.0** |
| Shatter Resistance | Poor | Good | Good | **Excellent** | **Excellent** |
| Recyclability | High | High | High | **High (#1)** | **High (#2)** |
| Relative Cost (per unit) | High | High | Medium | **Low** | **Very Low** |

> **Reading the Matrix:** Plastics do **not** win on *every* metric — barrier performance (OTR, WVTR) is inferior to glass/metal. Their advantage is the **overall balance**: acceptable barrier + lightweight + cheap + shatterproof + recyclable. The **engineering art** lies in designing multilayer structures that compensate where plastics are weak.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Question 1
**List any six advantages of plastics as a packaging material.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Lightweight** — Density typically 0.85–1.45 g/cm³, much lower than glass (~2.5 g/cm³) and steel (~7.85 g/cm³).
2. **High strength-to-weight ratio** — Specific strength of PET is comparable to aluminium.
3. **Shatter resistance** — Does not break into dangerous shards like glass.
4. **Tunable barrier properties** — Can be engineered via co-extrusion or metallization.
5. **Design versatility** — Can be moulded into complex shapes in a single operation.
6. **Low cost** — Cheaper raw material and processing compared to metals and glass.
7. *(Bonus point)* — Excellent printability for branding.

*(3 marks awarded for listing 6 distinct advantages with one-line justification each.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE PROVIDED — Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B**

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **six major advantages of plastic packaging** with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A company currently uses **500 g glass jars** (₹15 per unit) to pack 500 g of honey. They plan to switch to **30 g PET jars** (₹5 per unit). If annual production is **2 million jars**, calculate:
- (i) Total annual packaging cost savings.
- (ii) Total annual mass savings.
- (iii) Number of truck trips saved if each truck carries 5 tonnes. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a):

The six major advantages are explained below:

1. **Lightweight & High Specific Strength:** Plastics have densities between 0.85–1.45 g/cm³. PET has a specific strength of ~40 MPa·cm³/g, comparable to aluminium. *Example:* A 1 L PET beverage bottle weighs ~35 g vs ~350 g for an equivalent glass bottle.

2. **Superior Impact & Shatter Resistance:** Plastics absorb impact energy through elastic and plastic deformation. *Example:* PET carbonated soft drink bottles pass 1.2 m drop tests; glass bottles shatter.

3. **Tunable Barrier Properties:** Through multilayer co-extrusion (e.g., PET/EVOH/PE), oxygen and moisture transmission can be precisely engineered. *Example:* EVOH layer of just 5–10 µm reduces OTR from ~9 cc/m²·day (PET alone) to < 0.5 cc/m²·day.

4. **Design Versatility & Manufacturability:** Thermoplastics are processable by injection moulding, blow moulding, thermoforming, extrusion, and rotational moulding with cycle times of 5–15 seconds. *Example:* A single moulded PET bottle-replacement cap replaces a 4-piece metal assembly.

5. **Economic Efficiency:** Low material cost (₹80–₹150/kg), high throughput, and stackable/nestable empty forms reduce logistics cost. *Example:* Indian dairy industry shifted from glass bottles to LDPE pouches in the 1970s.

6. **Chemical Inertness & Food Safety:** PE, PP, and PET are non-reactive with food and pharmaceuticals, complying with **FDA 21 CFR** and **EU 10/2011** food-contact regulations. *Example:* IV fluid bags use medical-grade PVC/PP multilayer films.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- $m_{glass} = 500 \text{ g}$, $C_{glass} = \text{₹}15$
- $m_{PET} = 30 \text{ g}$, $C_{PET} = \text{₹}5$
- $N = 2 \times 10^6$ jars/year
- Truck capacity: 5 tonnes

**(i) Annual cost savings:**

$$\Delta C = (C_{glass} - C_{PET}) \times N = (15 - 5) \times 2 \times 10^6$$

$$\Delta C = \text{₹}10 \times 2 \times 10^6 = \text{₹}2 \times 10^7 = \text{₹}2 \text{ Crore per year}$$

**(ii) Annual mass savings:**

$$\Delta M = (500 - 30) \times 2 \times 10^6 = 470 \text{ g} \times 2 \times 10^6 = 9.4 \times 10^8 \text{ g} = 940 \text{ tonnes}$$

**(iii) Truck trips saved:**

$$\text{Trips saved} = \dfrac{\Delta M \text{ in tonnes}}{5 \text{ tonnes/trip}} = \dfrac{940}{5} = 188 \text{ trips/year}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often **forget to convert grams to tonnes** in Part (b)(ii), losing 2 marks.
> - Many write $10 \times 2 \times 10^6 = 2 \times 10^6$ instead of $2 \times 10^7$ — be careful with the **power-of-ten multiplication**.
> - Part (a) answers lose marks when advantages are listed **without examples**. KTU examiners explicitly require *one engineering example per advantage*.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **barrier and chemical advantages** of plastic packaging. How are these properties engineered through multilayer film technology? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a **neat labelled sketch**, explain the **multilayer plastic packaging structure** commonly used for UHT milk. Why is a multilayer structure necessary instead of a single polymer? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Barrier Advantages:**
- Plastics offer **tunable permeability** — a critical advantage because no single plastic is perfect on all barrier metrics.
- *Water Vapour Transmission Rate (WVTR)* is excellent in **PE and PP** (0.5–1.0 g/m²·day) but poor in **PA (Nylon)**.
- *Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR)* is excellent in **EVOH and PVDC** but poor in **PE and PP**.
- Therefore, **multilayer co-extrusion** combines polymers to capture the strengths of each.

**Chemical Advantages:**
- Plastics like PE, PP, and PET are **chemically inert** — they do not react with acidic, alkaline, or alcoholic food products.
- They do not impart taste, odour, or colour to the contents (low **migration** values).
- They comply with food-contact regulations (FDA, EU, FSSAI).

**Multilayer Film Engineering Example:**
A typical 5-layer structure for oxygen-sensitive food:

$$\text{PE} \mid \text{Tie} \mid \text{EVOH} \mid \text{Tie} \mid \text{PE}$$

- Outer PE layers provide **moisture barrier** and **heat sealability**.
- EVOH core provides **oxygen barrier**.
- Tie layers (modified PE or EVA) provide **adhesion** between incompatible polymers.

The permeability follows the **inverse-additivity law**:

$$\dfrac{L_{total}}{P_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{P_i}$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Structure of a Typical UHT Milk Packaging (Tetra Pak-style 6-layer):**

| Layer Number | Material | Function |
|--------------|----------|----------|
| 1 (Outer) | PE | Moisture barrier, printability |
| 2 | Paperboard | Mechanical rigidity, stiffness |
| 3 | PE | Adhesion, moisture barrier |
| 4 | Al foil | Total light + gas barrier |
| 5 | PE | Adhesion, sealing |
| 6 (Inner) | PE | Food-contact safety, heat seal |

**Why Multilayer Instead of Single Polymer?**

A single polymer cannot simultaneously provide:
- **Oxygen barrier** (needs EVOH or Al foil)
- **Moisture barrier** (needs PE or PP)
- **Light barrier** (needs pigmented or metallized layer)
- **Mechanical rigidity** (needs paperboard or thick HDPE)
- **Heat sealability** (needs PE or PP sealing layer)

By combining layers, the package achieves **all five functions simultaneously**, which is impossible with a single polymer. This is the *engineering essence* of multilayer plastic packaging.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Question B:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse *multilayer film* with *multilayer rigid container*. Both exist — read the question stem carefully.
> - The term **"tie layer"** is often missed by students. Examiners award **1–2 marks** explicitly for mentioning tie layers and their adhesive function.
> - For UHT milk, students often draw only 3 layers. A correct answer requires the **6-layer structure** (Paper/Al/PE) to score full marks.
