# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram note:** For "Design" in Module 1, the most KTU-board-relevant diagrams are (a) the *Package Design Cascade Flow*, (b) the *Barrier Layer Architecture of a Multi-layer Pouch*, and (c) the *Stacking Load Path of a Corrugated Case*. Physical-shape drawings are deliberately replaced by **functional block diagrams** to maximize schematic clarity within the Mermaid-rendering constraints.

## 4.1 The Package Design Cascade — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Product Brief] --> B[Concept Generation]
    B --> C[Material Pre Selection]
    C --> D[Preliminary CAD Geometry]
    D --> E[Wall Thickness Calculation]
    E --> F[Process Selection]
    F --> G[Prototype Fabrication]
    G --> H{Testing: Drop Stacking Barrier}
    H -- Pass --> I[Final BOM and Artwork]
    H -- Fail --> E
    I --> J[Tooling and Production Release]
    J --> K[End of Life Assessment]
```

## 4.2 Multi-layer Flexible Pouch — Barrier Architecture Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Outer Layer: PET 12 micron] --> L2[Adhesive Tie Layer]
    L2 --> L3[Barrier Layer: EVOH 8 micron]
    L3 --> L4[Adhesive Tie Layer]
    L4 --> L5[Inner Sealant Layer: LDPE 60 micron]
    L5 --> L6[Product Contact Face]
    L1 --> LA[Print Surface]
```

## 4.3 Stacking Load Path in a Corrugated Plastic Case

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP[Top Box Weight] --> CT[Top Wall Panel]
    CT --> CV[Corner Verticals]
    CT --> SW[Side Wall Panels]
    CV --> CB[Bottom Wall Panel]
    SW --> CB
    CB --> P[Foundation Pallet]
    P --> FL[Floor Reaction Force]
```

## 4.4 The Design Decision Tree — Polymer Selection for a Rigid Bottle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Product is hot fill above 75 C?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Required clarity?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Choose PET or HDPE]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4[Choose PP Random Copolymer]
    Q2 -- No --> Q5[Choose PP Homopolymer or HDPE]
    Q3 -- Is gas barrier required --> Q6[Choose PET with multilayer option]
    Q3 -- No --> Q7[Choose HDPE for squeezability]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** The decision-tree structure above is the *exact format* KTU examiners use when they ask *"Justify the polymer selection for a given bottle with a 6-line answer."* Memorize the *question-shape* and the *yes/no branches*.

## 4.5 The Five-Pillar Interaction Map (Design Pillar Coupling)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Structural Design] <--> P2[Barrier Design]
    P2 <--> P3[Material Design]
    P3 <--> P4[Process Design]
    P4 <--> P5[Graphic Design]
    P5 <--> P1
    P1 <-.-> P4
    P2 <-.-> P5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the loop?** Every pillar *couples* to every other pillar. Changing the **material** (P3) changes the **process** (P4), the **barrier** (P2), the **graphics** (P5, e.g., printability), and the **structure** (P1, e.g., stiffness). This is the *systems-engineering* essence of packaging design.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**"Define 'package design' in the context of plastics packaging. List the four classical functions of packaging."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Package design is the *systematic engineering process* of specifying a plastic container's geometry, material, barrier, and graphics such that it *contains, protects, distributes, and informs* a product through its supply-chain life. The four classical functions are remembered by the acronym **CPDI**:
- **Contain** — hold the product without leakage.
- **Protect** — from mechanical, chemical, biological, and atmospheric damage.
- **Distribute** — withstand stacking, vibration, and drop during transport.
- **Inform** — through labels, branding, regulatory text, and recycling codes.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four functions.)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**"State the McKee formula for box compression strength and define each variable."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The McKee formula relates the BCT (Box Compression Test) of a corrugated plastic case to its ECT and mean perimeter:

$$BCT = k \cdot (ECT)^{0.75} \cdot (D)^{0.5}$$

- *BCT* = Box Compression Test value in N (force).
- *ECT* = Edge Crush Test value of the fluted sheet in kN/m.
- *D* = Mean perimeter of the box in mm.
- *k* = empirical material/unit-system constant (typically 0.6–2.5).

*(Award: 1 mark for the equation, 2 marks for the variable definitions.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part-B questions in Module-1 are usually 14-mark, two-part problems with **internal choice** between two full alternatives. We model both.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**"A 1 L PET carbonated beverage bottle is to be designed for a 0.6 MPa internal CO₂ pressure. The mean body radius is 35 mm. The long-term creep-allowable stress of PET is 12 MPa, and a safety factor of 2.5 is required. Calculate: (a) the minimum required wall thickness of the body; (b) the predicted BCT of a PP-corrugated shipping case of dimensions 400 × 300 × 250 mm using McKee's formula with ECT = 6.5 kN/m and k = 2.2, and comment on whether the case can carry an 8-box stack of 4 kg each."**

#### (a) Wall Thickness of the PET Bottle — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Hoop-stress equation for thin cylinder (1 mark):

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot r}{t}$$

**Step 2:** Allowable design stress with safety factor (2 marks):

$$\sigma_{design} = \frac{\sigma_{allow}}{SF} = \frac{12 \text{ MPa}}{2.5} = 4.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for t (2 marks):

$$t = \frac{P \cdot r}{\sigma_{design}} = \frac{(0.6 \times 10^{6}) \cdot (0.035)}{4.8 \times 10^{6}} = 4.38 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 4.38 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4:** Add moldability margin and report (2 marks):

$$t_{final} \approx 5.0 \text{ mm} \text{ (with 15 % margin)}$$

#### (b) Stacking Strength of the Shipping Case — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Mean perimeter (1 mark):

$$D = 2(L + W) = 2(400 + 300) = 1400 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $(6.5)^{0.75}$ and $(1400)^{0.5}$ (2 marks):

$$(6.5)^{0.75} \approx 4.07, \quad (1400)^{0.5} \approx 37.42$$

**Step 3:** Apply McKee (2 marks):

$$BCT = 2.2 \cdot 4.07 \cdot 37.42 \approx 335 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4:** Compare with stacking load (2 marks):
- Stack load = $8 \times 4 \times 9.81 = 313.9$ N.
- Safety margin = $(335 - 313.9) / 335 = 6.3\%$.
- **Conclusion:** Marginally acceptable; *industry best practice* demands ≥ 20 % margin. Hence the design should *increase ECT* to ≈ 7.5 kN/m, or *reduce stack height* to 6 boxes.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**"With the help of a block diagram, explain the five-pillar model of plastics package design. (a) Briefly describe the structural and barrier design pillars. (b) For a 500 mL PET bottle of oxygen-sensitive juice, with effective barrier area 250 cm² and barrier thickness 0.30 mm, O₂ permeability of PET = 9 × 10⁻¹² cm³·cm/cm²·s·Pa, and Δp = 0.21 atm, calculate the steady-state oxygen permeation rate and comment on the suitability of plain PET for a 1-year shelf-life."**

#### (a) Five-Pillar Model and Two Pillars Explained — 7 Marks

**Block diagram (2 marks):** Show the five-pillar interaction map (P1 ↔ P2 ↔ P3 ↔ P4 ↔ P5) as in §4.5 above.

**Structural Design Pillar (2.5 marks):**
- Concerned with mechanical loads: internal pressure, stacking, drop, vibration.
- Uses hoop-stress equation, McKee's formula, drop-impact energy balance.
- Output: required wall thickness, rib layout, panel geometry.

**Barrier Design Pillar (2.5 marks):**
- Concerned with permeation of O₂, CO₂, water vapour, flavour molecules.
- Uses Fick's first law: $Q = P_{erm} A \Delta p / l$.
- Output: required lamination structure, wall thickness, or co-extrusion choice.

#### (b) Permeation Rate and Shelf-life Suitability — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Convert l and Δp to consistent units (1 mark):
- l = 0.30 mm = 0.030 cm.
- Δp = 0.21 atm × 101325 Pa/atm = 2.128 × 10⁴ Pa.

**Step 2:** Apply Fick's first law (2 marks):

$$Q = \frac{P_{erm} \cdot A \cdot \Delta p}{l} = \frac{(9 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 250 \cdot (2.128 \times 10^{4})}{0.030}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate (2 marks):

$$Q = 1.596 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}^{3} STP / s = 138.1 \text{ cm}^{3} STP / day \approx 0.197 \text{ mg / day}$$

**Step 4:** Compute required delay for 1-year (2 marks):
- 1 year = 365 days → total O₂ ingress ≈ 0.197 × 365 ≈ **72 mg** over the year.
- For 500 mL bottle with headspace 5 mg/L → 2.5 mg initial → 25 mg spoilage limit → allowable ingress ≈ 22.5 mg.
- Since 72 mg ≫ 22.5 mg, **plain PET is unsuitable**.
- **Design recommendation:** Use multi-layer PET/EVOH/PET, or apply a barrier coating, or use nitrogen flush.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Module-1 (Design) questions:**
> 1. **Unit mix-ups:** Mixing *mm* and *m* in the hoop-stress equation. Always convert all dimensions to **meters** before substituting into SI-unit formulas.
> 2. **Forgetting the safety factor:** KTU answers that quote *t = P·r/σ* without dividing by SF are penalized 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong McKee constant:** The constant *k* is *not universal*. Always use the *value given in the problem statement*; never assume k = 0.6 by default.
> 4. **Permeation unit-conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert *cm³ STP/day* into *mg/day* using the molar volume at STP (22.4 L/mol).
> 5. **Stacking-load unit error:** Forgetting that *1 kgf* = *9.81 N*; using 1 kg ≈ 1 N is a classic KTU board trap.
> 6. **Ignoring the 5 %–20 % safety margin** when reporting "pass/fail" of a BCT-vs-load check.
> 7. **Omitting the design-block-diagram** in *explain* questions: a 14-mark Part-B "explain" question without a *block diagram* typically loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — read this in the last 10 minutes before the exam.**

- **Definition:** Package design = systematic specification of geometry, material, barrier, and graphics to *contain, protect, distribute, inform* (CPDI).
- **Five Pillars:** Structural, Barrier, Material, Process, Graphic — *all are coupled*.
- **Five Rights of Design:** Right product, material, process, cost, end-of-life.
- **Hoop stress:** $\sigma_h = P r / t$ — governs bottle wall thickness.
- **Design thickness:** $t = (SF \cdot P \cdot r) / \sigma_{allow}$ — never forget the safety factor (typically 2.0–4.0).
- **McKee formula:** $BCT = k \cdot (ECT)^{0.75} \cdot (D)^{0.5}$ — k depends on material/unit system.
- **Fick's permeation:** $Q = P_{erm} \cdot A \cdot \Delta p / l$ — used for *shelf-life* estimation.
- **Drop-impact energy balance:** $m g h = \sigma_{allow}^{2} V / (2E)$ — used for *transport-survival* design.
- **Creep rule of thumb:** $\sigma_{allow, creep} \approx 0.30 \sigma_{yield}$ for long-term design.
- **Standard test condition:** **23 °C, 50 % RH** — always quote this when reporting a property.
- **DFM essentials:** uniform wall thickness, draft angles (1°–2°), radii ≥ 0.5 t, no sharp corners.
- **DFR essentials:** mono-material preferred, clear PET, SPI code (♳–♹) on base, avoid PVC labels on PET.
- **Process–product fit:** PET ↔ carbonated drink; PP ↔ hot fill; HDPE ↔ squeezable; PET/EVOH/PE ↔ O₂-sensitive juice.
- **PET plain-bottle shelf-life for O₂-sensitive juice** ≈ 50 days — *insufficient* for 1 year without barrier enhancement.
- **McKee exponent 0.75:** doubling ECT raises BCT by only **~68 %**, not 100 %.
- **Unit conversions to memorize:** 1 atm = 101325 Pa; 1 kgf = 9.81 N; 1 mol gas at STP = 22.4 L; M(O₂) = 32 g/mol; M(N₂) = 28 g/mol.
- **CPDI mnemonic:** **C**ontain, **P**rotect, **D**istribute, **I**nform.
- **Exam mantra:** *"Sketch a block diagram, write the governing equation, state the assumption, show the substitution, evaluate with units, comment on the result."* — this 6-step structure is the KTU 14-mark answer template.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relation Criteria for Packaging Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Relation Criteria for Packaging Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Plastics Packaging Technology**, the term **"Relation Criteria for Packaging Materials"** refers to the systematic, scientifically-engineered framework of evaluation parameters and decision matrices used to establish a direct functional correlation between the **intrinsic physico-chemical properties** of a candidate packaging material and the **end-use requirements** of the packaged product (the "goods to be protected").

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEPOT748**, the *relation criteria* establish the bridge across three interdependent domains:

| Domain | Definition | KTU Terminology |
|--------|------------|-----------------|
| **Product Domain** | The nature, sensitivity, and shelf-life demands of the contained product | *Primary Package Requirements* |
| **Material Domain** | The measurable physical, chemical, mechanical, barrier, and thermal properties of polymers | *Substrate Property Matrix* |
| **Process Domain** | The converting/fabrication method (extrusion, blow molding, thermoforming, lamination) | *Package Manufacturing Compatibility* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Relation Criteria* is defined as the logical and quantitative relationship that must exist between the **functional demands** placed upon a package and the **measurable performance attributes** of the packaging material. It is essentially a **"Property-to-Performance Matching Index"** that drives all packaging material selection decisions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying a raincoat for a specific purpose:
- **Cricket match in light drizzle** → a thin, breathable polyester poncho suffices.
- **Mountain trekking in heavy snow** → you need a thick, multi-layered, waterproof Gore-Tex jacket with sealed seams.

The **"relation criteria"** in this analogy is the **logic your brain performs**:

```
Weather Condition  →  Required Protection Level  →  Material Selection
(External Stimulus)    (Functional Demand)            (Material Property)
```

Similarly, in packaging:
```
Product Characteristics  →  Required Barrier/Mechanics  →  Polymer Choice
(Mangoes/Pharma/Chips)     (Oxygen/Moisture/Tear)        (PET/HDPE/Aluminum Foil)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Definition for Exams:** *Relation criteria are the rules that connect "what the product needs" with "what the packaging material offers." They define the matching logic between product vulnerability and material capability.*

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standardized units and metrics form the quantitative backbone of packaging material relation criteria:

- **Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR)** → measured in **g/m²/day** at specified temperature (typically **38°C**) and relative humidity (typically **90% RH**).
- **Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR)** → measured in **cc/m²/day** at standard conditions (**23°C, 0% RH** or **23°C, 50% RH**).
- **Tensile Strength** → measured in **MPa** (Megapascals) as per ASTM D882.
- **Heat Seal Strength** → measured in **N/15mm** or **g/inch** as per ASTM F88.
- **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg)** → measured in **°C** or **K** (Kelvin).
- **Melt Flow Index (MFI / MFR)** → measured in **g/10 min** as per ASTM D1238.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These six metrics above are the **HIGH-FREQUENCY** items that appear in KTU board examinations on this topic. Memorize the units verbatim.

### 1.4 The Fundamental Triad of Relation Criteria

The KTU syllabus explicitly emphasizes that the **relation criteria** revolve around **three master relationships**:

1. **Product → Material Relationship (P-M):** What does the product demand?
2. **Material → Package Relationship (M-Pkg):** What can the material deliver?
3. **Package → Environment Relationship (Pkg-E):** How does the package perform in the distribution environment?

The intersection of these three relationships, when satisfied simultaneously, defines a **technically validated packaging solution**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deconstruction of the Relation Criteria Framework

The KTU 2024 syllabus positions the *Relation Criteria* as a **multi-tier decision tree** rather than a single linear equation. The decision tree must be traversed sequentially from **broad performance demands** down to **specific quantitative cutoffs**.

#### Tier-1: Primary Functional Criteria (Macro-Level)
These are the four master functions of packaging, originally codified by the **International Safe Transit Association (ISTA)** and adopted by KTU as the foundational logic:

- **Containment:** Does the material hold the product without leakage? (e.g., molten soap, granular detergent, liquid milk)
- **Protection:** Does the material shield the product from external hazards? (e.g., oxygen, UV light, mechanical shock, microbial ingress)
- **Preservation:** Does the material extend shelf-life? (e.g., moisture barrier in biscuits, oxygen barrier in vacuum-packed coffee)
- **Presentation / Information:** Does the material accept printing, branding, and regulatory labeling?

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently frame questions like: *"Relate the four primary functions of packaging to the criteria used for selecting a polymer."* — Always list all four with a packaging material example.

#### Tier-2: Secondary Technical Criteria (Meso-Level)
Once Tier-1 is satisfied, the engineer evaluates:

- **Barrier Properties:** Permeability to gases ($O_2$, $CO_2$, $N_2$), water vapor, aroma compounds, and light.
- **Mechanical Properties:** Tensile strength, elongation at break, impact resistance, puncture resistance, tear strength.
- **Thermal Properties:** Tg, melting point (Tm), Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT), seal initiation temperature.
- **Chemical Resistance:** Compatibility with product (acidic, alkaline, fatty, alcoholic).
- **Optical Properties:** Transparency, haze, gloss (critical for consumer appeal).

#### Tier-3: Processability & Economic Criteria (Micro-Level)
- **Convertibility:** Suitability for extrusion, injection molding, blow molding, thermoforming, or lamination.
- **Sealability:** Hot-tack strength, seal-through-contamination ability, hermeticity.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** FDA / EU / FSSAI food contact approval, recycled content legality.
- **Cost-Performance Ratio (CPR):** The economic feasibility index.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates **all critical equations** for solving numerical and analytical questions on relation criteria.

| # | Formula / Relation | Mathematical Form | Units | Application in Packaging |
|---|--------------------|-------------------|-------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Permeability (general) | $P = \frac{q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta p \cdot t}$ | $\text{cc·cm/cm}^2\text{·s·cmHg}$ | Determines film thickness required for a given shelf-life |
| 2 | Shelf-Life from OTR | $t_{sl} = \frac{m_{O_2}^{critical}}{OTR \cdot A}$ | days | Oxygen-sensitive products (fats, vitamins) |
| 3 | Shelf-Life from WVTR | $t_{sl} = \frac{m_{H_2O}^{critical}}{WVTR \cdot A}$ | days | Moisture-sensitive products (pharma, dry foods) |
| 4 | Heat Seal Strength (peel) | $HSS = \frac{F_{peel}}{W}$ | N/15 mm or N/mm | Determines seal integrity of pouches |
| 5 | Cost-Performance Ratio | $CPR = \frac{Cost_{material}}{Performance_{index}}$ | ₹/unit or $/unit | Economic material selection |
| 6 | Barrier Improvement Factor | $BIF = \frac{P_{base}}{\;P_{composite}\;}$ | dimensionless | Quantifies multilayer improvement |
| 7 | Lamination Rule (additive) | $\frac{1}{P_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{x_i}{P_i}$ | — | Multi-layer barrier design |
| 8 | Volume of Permeant (Henry's) | $V = k_H \cdot P_{partial} \cdot t$ | cc | Quantifies total permeant entering package |
| 9 | Thermal Resistance (R-value) | $R = \frac{\Delta T}{q}$ | K·m²/W | Insulated packaging |
| 10 | Drop Test Impact Energy | $E = m \cdot g \cdot h$ | J | Mechanical protection criterion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** In all equations above, capital $P$ denotes **permeability**, $q$ denotes **quantity permeated**, $L$ is the **film thickness**, $A$ is the **area**, $\Delta p$ is the **partial pressure gradient**, and $t$ is the **time**. Do NOT confuse $P$ (permeability) with $P$ (pressure) — examiners will deduct marks for ambiguous notation.

### 2.3 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Criterion

The pedagogical logic of the KTU board is to test whether the student understands **why** each criterion exists, not merely memorize it.

- **Why is WVTR a primary relation criterion for biscuits?** Because biscuits lose crispness when moisture ingress exceeds ~5% of their weight. Hence, the *critical moisture content* of biscuits must be related to the *WVTR of the packaging film* through a shelf-life equation.
- **Why is OTR critical for edible oils?** Oils oxidize when $O_2$ permeates; rancidity occurs. The *peroxide value (PV)* threshold of the oil defines the *maximum allowable OTR* of the package.
- **Why is glass transition temperature (Tg) a relation criterion for frozen food packaging?** Because the polymer must remain flexible (above Tg) at $-18°C$ to resist cracking — otherwise brittle fracture occurs during drop or stacking.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This framework is used universally in:
- **Food packaging** (FMCG): Matching snack crispness requirements to BOPP/PE laminate WVTR.
- **Pharmaceutical packaging:** Matching drug stability to Alu-Alu blister OTR/WVTR.
- **Cosmetics:** Matching fragrance retention to PET/EVOH barrier jars.
- **Industrial packaging:** Matching heavy-load stacking to HDPE drum tensile strength.
- **Sustainable design:** Matching recycled-content regulations to bio-based polymer property profiles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative-1: Shelf-Life Equation from Barrier Criterion

The KTU 2024 examiners frequently require students to derive the shelf-life relation from the fundamental permeability equation. Below is the **complete, exhaustive derivation** with no skipped steps.

#### Step 1: Start with the Definition of Permeability

Permeability $P$ of a polymer film is defined as the volume of gas (or mass of vapor) passing through unit area, per unit time, per unit thickness, per unit pressure difference.

$$
P = \frac{q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta p \cdot t}
$$

Where:
- $q$ = quantity (volume or mass) of permeant
- $L$ = thickness of the film
- $A$ = area of the film exposed
- $\Delta p$ = partial pressure gradient across the film
- $t$ = time duration

#### Step 2: Rearrange to Find the Transmitted Quantity

We isolate $q$ because we want to know *how much* permeant enters the package over time:

$$
q = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p \cdot t}{L}
$$

This equation states: *the quantity of permeant increases linearly with time, area, and pressure gradient, and decreases with film thickness.*

#### Step 3: Define the Critical Permeant Quantity

Every product has a *critical permeant limit* — the maximum amount of $O_2$ or $H_2O$ it can tolerate before degrading. Let us denote this as $q_{crit}$.

For oxygen-sensitive products (e.g., roasted coffee, vitamin C tablets):
$$
q_{crit} = m_{O_2}^{max}
$$

For moisture-sensitive products (e.g., biscuits, milk powder):
$$
q_{crit} = m_{H_2O}^{max}
$$

#### Step 4: Equate the Critical Quantity to the Permeation Quantity

Setting $q = q_{crit}$ gives us the *time-to-failure*, which is precisely the shelf-life:

$$
q_{crit} = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p \cdot t_{sl}}{L}
$$

#### Step 5: Solve for Shelf-Life $t_{sl}$

$$
t_{sl} = \frac{q_{crit} \cdot L}{P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p}
$$

#### Step 6: Convert to the Industry-Standard OTR Form

Engineers conventionally express this as **OTR** (Oxygen Transmission Rate), which already incorporates the standard test conditions:

$$
OTR = \frac{P \cdot \Delta p_{std}}{L}
$$

Substituting OTR back into the shelf-life expression:

$$
t_{sl} = \frac{q_{crit}}{OTR \cdot A}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU shelf-life formula**. It is the single most-tested equation in Module 1 of PEPOT748. Memorize it in **three equivalent forms**: $t_{sl} = \frac{q \cdot L}{P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p} = \frac{q_{crit}}{OTR \cdot A} = \frac{q_{crit}}{WVTR \cdot A}$.

### 3.2 Derivative-2: Multi-Layer Lamination Barrier Criterion

#### Step 1: Define Single-Layer Permeance

The **permeance** (often confusingly written the same as $P$ in industry datasheets) is permeability divided by thickness:

$$
Pe = \frac{P}{L}
$$

#### Step 2: Recognize the Series-Resistance Analogy

For mass transfer (gas permeation), multiple layers act like electrical resistors **in series**. The total resistance is the sum of individual resistances:

$$
R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \dots + R_n
$$

#### Step 3: Apply the Series Analogy to Permeance

In permeability terms, the inverse of permeance (which is resistance) is additive:

$$
\frac{1}{Pe_{total}} = \frac{L_1}{P_1} + \frac{L_2}{P_2} + \frac{L_3}{P_3} + \dots + \frac{L_n}{P_n}
$$

If we let $x_i = \frac{L_i}{L_{total}}$ (the thickness fraction of layer $i$), then:

$$
\frac{1}{Pe_{total}} = \frac{L_{total}}{P_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{x_i \cdot L_{total}}{P_i}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{1}{P_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{x_i}{P_i}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Lamination Rule** — a definitive KTU 2024 high-yield result. It tells the engineer exactly how much of each polymer (PET, EVOH, PE, etc.) is needed to achieve a target barrier performance.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Product-to-Material Relation Criteria Mapping

The following matrix is the **"spine" of KTU Module 1 questions**. It directly maps each product vulnerability to the corresponding material property that must be selected (the *relation criterion*).

| Product Category | Specific Vulnerability | Critical Relation Criterion | Required Material Property | Typical Material Selected |
|------------------|----------------------|----------------------------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Potato chips | Moisture ingress (loss of crispness) | **WVTR** | WVTR $< 1 \text{ g/m}^2$/day | BOPP / Metallized BOPP / PE laminate |
| Edible oil | Oxygen ingress (rancidity) | **OTR** | OTR $< 10 \text{ cc/m}^2$/day | PET / EVOH multilayer |
| Frozen foods | Brittle fracture at sub-zero T | **Tg** | $T_g < -20°C$ | LDPE / LLDPE / EVA |
| Carbonated drinks | $CO_2$ loss (loss of fizz) | **$CO_2$ TR** | $CO_2$ TR $< 5 \text{ cc/pkg/day}$ | PET bottle (with $O_2$ scavenger) |
| Pharma tablets | Light degradation | **UV barrier** | UV transmittance $< 5\%$ at 200–400 nm | Amber PVC / Alu-Alu blister |
| Liquid milk | Microbial ingress | **Hermetic seal + OTR** | Seal strength $> 2 \text{ N/15mm}$ | HDPE bottle with aluminum foil lid |
| Detergent powder | Aroma loss / moisture pickup | **Aroma + WVTR** | Low aroma permeability | Co-extruded PE/PA/PE |
| Hot-fill beverages | Thermal deformation | **Heat Deflection Temp** | HDT $> 95°C$ | PP / C-PET |
| Microwave food | Heat resistance (no melting) | **Tm** | $T_m > 130°C$ | CPET (crystallized PET) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Strategy:** When asked *"Relate the packaging criteria to the choice of polymer for a given product,"* use this exact table structure. The examiner awards **2 marks** for correctly identifying the *criterion*, **2 marks** for the *quantitative threshold*, and **2 marks** for the *polymer justification*.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A moisture-sensitive snack product can tolerate a maximum water vapor ingress of **$0.5 \text{ g}$** over its target shelf life of **180 days**. The proposed package has a film area of **$0.05 \text{ m}^2$**. Determine the maximum allowable **WVTR** for the packaging film.

#### Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify the governing relation criterion (shelf-life from WVTR):

$$
t_{sl} = \frac{m_{H_2O}^{critical}}{WVTR \cdot A}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute known values. Note the conversion — time $t_{sl}$ is in days, but the equation works directly in days if WVTR is expressed in **g/m²/day** (which is the KTU standard unit):

$$
180 = \frac{0.5}{WVTR \cdot 0.05}
$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to isolate WVTR:

$$
WVTR \cdot 0.05 = \frac{0.5}{180}
$$

$$
WVTR \cdot 0.05 = 0.002777\ldots \text{ g/m}^2/\text{day}
$$

**Step 4:** Solve:

$$
WVTR = \frac{0.002777}{0.05} = 0.0556 \text{ g/m}^2/\text{day}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply engineering judgment (round up to maintain safety margin):

$$
WVTR_{design} \approx 0.05 \text{ g/m}^2/\text{day}
$$

**Step 6:** Material Selection — A polymer with WVTR $\leq 0.05 \text{ g/m}^2$/day at 38°C/90% RH is required. Candidate materials include:

- **Metallized BOPP** (WVTR $\approx 0.3 \text{ g/m}^2$/day) → insufficient.
- **Aluminum foil laminate** (WVTR $\approx 0.001 \text{ g/m}^2$/day) → excellent, often selected.

**Conclusion:** An **Aluminum foil-based laminate** must be chosen for this snack application. This selection is the direct *relation* between the product's vulnerability and the material's barrier criterion.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: The Relation Criteria Decision Framework

The diagram below maps the **complete hierarchical logic** by which relation criteria are applied to choose a packaging material. Each block represents a decision node or transformation stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Product Identification] --> B[Product Vulnerability Analysis]
    B --> C[Vulnerability Categorization]
    C --> D1[Moisture Sensitivity]
    C --> D2[Oxygen Sensitivity]
    C --> D3[Light Sensitivity]
    C --> D4[Mechanical Stress]
    C --> D5[Thermal Exposure]
    D1 --> E1[Define WVTR Criterion]
    D2 --> E2[Define OTR Criterion]
    D3 --> E3[Define UV Barrier Criterion]
    D4 --> E4[Define Tensile/Impact Criterion]
    D5 --> E5[Define Tg Tm HDT Criterion]
    E1 --> F[Apply Numerical Threshold]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G{Quantitative Match Available?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Select Polymer from Database]
    G -- No --> I[Design Multilayer Lamination]
    I --> J[Apply Lamination Rule]
    H --> K[Validate via KTU Test Standards]
    I --> K
    K --> L[ASTM D882 Tensile]
    K --> M[ASTM F88 Seal Strength]
    K --> N[ASTM E96 WVTR]
    K --> O[ASTM D3985 OTR]
    L --> P[Final Material Recommendation]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Cost Performance Ratio Check]
    Q --> R[Approved Packaging Solution]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Trace any path from `A` to `R`. For example, *Pharma tablet → Oxygen-sensitive → OTR Criterion → Threshold → BOPP/EVOH → ASTM D3985 validation → Final selection.* This is the **canonical KTU reasoning path** for a 14-mark problem.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Multi-Layer Lamination Architecture

This schematic illustrates how the **Lamination Rule** is realized in a real packaging film. Each layer performs a specific *relation-criteria function*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OuterLayer[Outer Layer PET 12 micron]
        OL1[Printability]
        OL2[Mechanical Strength]
        OL3[Gloss and Clarity]
    end
    subgraph MiddleLayer[Barrier Layer EVOH 8 micron]
        ML1[Oxygen Barrier]
        ML2[Aroma Barrier]
    end
    subgraph TieLayer[Tie Layer Adhesive 2 micron]
        TL1[Adhesion Between Layers]
    end
    subgraph InnerLayer[Inner Layer LDPE 60 micron]
        IL1[Heat Sealability]
        IL2[Moisture Barrier]
        IL3[Food Contact Safety]
    end
    OuterLayer --> TieLayer
    TieLayer --> MiddleLayer
    MiddleLayer --> TieLayer
    TieLayer --> InnerLayer
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Lamination Rule $\frac{1}{P_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{x_i}{P_i}$ shows that the EVOH layer (very low $P_{O_2}$) dominates the oxygen barrier, while the LDPE layer (low $P_{H_2O}$) dominates the moisture barrier. This **functional layer allocation** is the essence of relation-criteria-driven packaging design.

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Product-Material Compatibility Matrix

This diagram visualizes the **mapping between product types and the dominant relation criterion** that drives material selection.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FoodProducts[Food Products]
        F1[Snacks and Biscuits]
        F2[Edible Oils]
        F3[Frozen Foods]
        F4[Hot Fill Juices]
        F5[Carbonated Drinks]
        F6[Pharma Tablets]
    end
    subgraph DominantCriteria[Dominant Relation Criteria]
        C1[WVTR Critical]
        C2[OTR Critical]
        C3[Tg Critical]
        C4[HDT Critical]
        C5[CO2 TR Critical]
        C6[UV Barrier Critical]
    end
    subgraph SelectedMaterials[Selected Polymer Materials]
        M1[MetBOPP and PE Laminate]
        M2[PET and EVOH Multilayer]
        M3[LLDPE and EVA]
        M4[PP and CPET]
        M5[PET with O2 Scavenger]
        M6[Amber PVC and Alu Alu]
    end
    F1 --> C1 --> M1
    F2 --> C2 --> M2
    F3 --> C3 --> M3
    F4 --> C4 --> M4
    F5 --> C5 --> M5
    F6 --> C6 --> M6
```

> [!NOTE]
> This 3-column block diagram is highly condensed and is the recommended visual answer when a KTU question asks: *"With a flowchart, show how packaging material selection criteria vary with product type."*

### 4.4 Functional Flow: The Shelf-Life Engineering Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Product Critical Moisture Qc grams] --> B[Package Surface Area A m2]
    B --> C[Target Shelf Life tsl days]
    C --> D[Compute Required WVTR]
    D --> E{WVTR Available in Market?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Select Single Film]
    E -- No --> G[Compute Required OTR]
    G --> H[Design Lamination]
    H --> I[Apply Lamination Rule]
    I --> J[Validate by ASTM Tests]
    F --> J
    J --> K{Meets All Criteria?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Release for Production]
    K -- No --> M[Reiterate Selection]
    M --> D
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define "Relation Criteria" in the context of plastic packaging material selection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The *Relation Criteria* for packaging materials is the established framework of technical parameters that quantitatively link the **functional demands of a packaged product** to the **measurable performance properties of the candidate polymer**. It acts as a *property-to-performance matching index*. The criteria are evaluated in three tiers:

1. **Primary criteria:** Containment, Protection, Preservation, Presentation.
2. **Secondary criteria:** Barrier (WVTR, OTR), Mechanical, Thermal, Chemical resistance.
3. **Processability criteria:** Convertibility, Sealability, Regulatory compliance, Cost-performance ratio.

*(Award: 1 mark for the core definition, 1 mark for the three-tier breakdown, 1 mark for an example.)*

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: List any THREE barrier-related relation criteria used for selecting plastic packaging films.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three barrier-related relation criteria are:

1. **Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR)** — measured in g/m²/day. Critical for moisture-sensitive products like biscuits, milk powder, and pharma tablets. Films with low WVTR (e.g., BOPP, LDPE, Aluminum laminates) are preferred.

2. **Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR)** — measured in cc/m²/day. Critical for oxygen-sensitive products like edible oils, roasted coffee, and vitamin-fortified foods. Materials such as EVOH and PVDC provide low OTR.

3. **Carbon Dioxide Transmission Rate ($CO_2$ TR)** — measured in cc/m²/day. Critical for carbonated beverages to retain fizz. PET with $O_2$ scavengers is the industry-standard selection.

*(Award: 1 mark per correctly defined criterion with its unit and application.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: Discuss in detail the "Relation Criteria" used for selecting a plastic packaging material for a moisture-sensitive snack food product. Include the governing equations, material selection logic, and a numerical illustration.**

**Sub-part (a) — [7 Marks] Explain the Relation Criteria framework for moisture-sensitive snack products, including the key governing equation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the product context.

Moisture-sensitive snack foods (e.g., potato chips, biscuits, namkeen) lose crispness, aroma, and microbiological stability when their moisture content rises above a critical threshold (typically **5–8% w/w**).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Identify the dominant relation criterion.

The dominant relation criterion is the **Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR)**, defined as the mass of water vapor (in grams) passing through unit area (1 m²) of the film in 24 hours, under standard test conditions (38°C, 90% RH differential).

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Write the governing shelf-life equation.

$$
t_{sl} = \frac{m_{H_2O}^{critical}}{WVTR \cdot A}
$$

Where:
- $t_{sl}$ = target shelf-life (days)
- $m_{H_2O}^{critical}$ = maximum allowable moisture ingress (g)
- $WVTR$ = water vapor transmission rate of the film (g/m²/day)
- $A$ = effective film area exposed (m²)

**Step 4 (1 mark):** State the practical implication.

The lower the WVTR of the selected film, the longer the achievable shelf-life for the same product mass and package geometry.

---

**Sub-part (b) — [7 Marks] Illustrate with a numerical example and justify a polymer choice.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Assume the following data (a typical KTU-style numerical):
- Critical moisture ingress: $m_{H_2O}^{crit} = 0.6 \text{ g}$
- Package film area: $A = 0.06 \text{ m}^2$
- Target shelf-life: $t_{sl} = 180 \text{ days}$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the maximum allowable WVTR:

$$
WVTR_{max} = \frac{m_{H_2O}^{crit}}{t_{sl} \cdot A} = \frac{0.6}{180 \times 0.06}
$$

$$
WVTR_{max} = \frac{0.6}{10.8} = 0.0556 \text{ g/m}^2/\text{day}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compare with candidate polymers:

| Polymer | Typical WVTR (g/m²/day at 38°C, 90% RH) | Suitable? |
|---------|-------------------------------------------|-----------|
| LDPE | 15–20 | No |
| HDPE | 5–10 | No |
| BOPP | 5–8 | No |
| Met-BOPP | 0.3–1.0 | Marginal |
| **Aluminum foil laminate** | **<0.01** | **Yes (Excellent)** |

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Conclude with engineering recommendation.

A **BOPP/Met-BOPP/LDPE triple laminate** is selected. The outer BOPP provides printability and mechanical strength; the middle metallized BOPP provides the primary moisture/oxygen barrier; the inner LDPE provides heat sealability and food-contact safety. This selection directly satisfies the relation criterion $WVTR \leq 0.0556 \text{ g/m}^2$/day.

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**Q: "Explain the multi-layer lamination rule used as a relation criterion in plastic packaging. Derive the governing equation and illustrate its use in designing an oxygen-barrier packaging structure."**

**Sub-part (a) — [7 Marks] Derive the lamination rule from the series-resistance analogy.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define permeance.

Permeance $Pe$ of a single-layer film is defined as permeability $P$ divided by thickness $L$:

$$
Pe = \frac{P}{L}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** State the series-resistance analogy.

In a multi-layer laminate, each layer acts as a *resistance* to mass transfer. Since permeant must traverse all layers sequentially, the resistances add in **series**, just as electrical resistors add:

$$
\frac{1}{Pe_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{1}{Pe_i}
$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Substitute $Pe_i = \frac{P_i}{L_i}$ and $x_i = \frac{L_i}{L_{total}}$:

$$
\frac{L_{total}}{P_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{L_i}{P_i} = L_{total}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{x_i}{P_i}
$$

Cancelling $L_{total}$ from both sides:

$$
\frac{1}{P_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{x_i}{P_i}
$$

This is the **Lamination Rule**, valid for any number of layers.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** State the engineering significance.

This equation is the *relation criterion* that allows the engineer to design a composite film whose permeability matches the product's barrier demand.

---

**Sub-part (b) — [7 Marks] Apply the lamination rule to design an oxygen-barrier film.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Specify the design problem.

A pharmaceutical blister package requires an effective OTR of $\leq 1 \text{ cc/m}^2$/day. Available materials:

- **PVC** (outer): $P_{O_2} = 200 \text{ cc·cm/m}^2\text{·day·atm}$, thickness fraction $x_1 = 0.4$
- **PVdC coating** (middle): $P_{O_2} = 5 \text{ cc·cm/m}^2\text{·day·atm}$, thickness fraction $x_2 = 0.05$
- **PE** (inner, sealing): $P_{O_2} = 1500 \text{ cc·cm/m}^2\text{·day·atm}$, thickness fraction $x_3 = 0.55$

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Apply the lamination rule.

$$
\frac{1}{P_{total}} = \frac{0.4}{200} + \frac{0.05}{5} + \frac{0.55}{1500}
$$

Computing each term:

$$
\frac{0.4}{200} = 0.00200 \quad ; \quad \frac{0.05}{5} = 0.01000 \quad ; \quad \frac{0.55}{1500} = 0.000367
$$

$$
\frac{1}{P_{total}} = 0.00200 + 0.01000 + 0.000367 = 0.012367
$$

$$
P_{total} = \frac{1}{0.012367} = 80.86 \text{ cc·cm/m}^2\text{·day·atm}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Convert to OTR for a total laminate thickness of, say, $L_{total} = 300 \text{ μm} = 0.03 \text{ cm}$:

$$
OTR_{total} = \frac{P_{total}}{L_{total}} = \frac{80.86}{0.03} = 2695 \text{ cc/m}^2/\text{day}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Re-engineer. The current design exceeds the target OTR by a factor of ~2700. To meet $\leq 1 \text{ cc/m}^2$/day, the engineer must either:
- Increase the PVdC coating thickness (increase $x_2$), or
- Add an aluminum foil layer (effectively $P_{Al} \approx 0$), making the structure **Alu-Alu blister** (industry standard for high-barrier pharma).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Question:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Students often mix cm and μm in permeability calculations. **Always convert to SI or consistent CGS units before applying the lamination rule.**
> 2. **Confusing $P$ (permeability) with $Pe$ (permeance):** Permeability is a material property (independent of thickness); permeance is a system property (depends on thickness). The Lamination Rule applies to **permeability-weighted thickness fractions**.
> 3. **Ignoring the standard test conditions:** WVTR must be quoted at 38°C/90% RH; OTR at 23°C/0% RH (or 50% RH as per ASTM D3985). Forgetting this loses **1 mark** minimum.
> 4. **Forgetting the safety factor:** Always divide the calculated maximum WVTR by a safety factor of **2 to 5** before final material selection. The examiner expects this engineering judgment.
> 5. **Skipping the conclusion:** A numerical answer without a justified polymer recommendation earns only **50% of the marks**. Always end with: *"Therefore, the material X is selected because…."*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Relation Criteria for Packaging Materials: Top 15 Rapid-Revision Points**

- ⭐ **Relation Criteria** = the quantitative bridge between *product demands* and *material properties*.
- ⭐ The framework has **3 tiers**: Primary (containment/protection/preservation/presentation) → Secondary (barrier/mechanical/thermal/chemical/optical) → Tertiary (processability/cost/regulatory).
- ⭐ The **six core quantitative metrics** are WVTR, OTR, Tensile Strength, Heat Seal Strength, Tg, and MFI.
- ⭐ Standard units: **WVTR in g/m²/day**, **OTR in cc/m²/day**, **Tensile in MPa**, **Seal Strength in N/15mm**, **Tg in °C**, **MFI in g/10min**.
- ⭐ The **master shelf-life equation** is $t_{sl} = \dfrac{q_{crit}}{OTR \cdot A}$ (analogous for WVTR).
- ⭐ The **Lamination Rule** is $\dfrac{1}{P_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{x_i}{P_i}$, derived from the series-resistance analogy.
- ⭐ Product-to-criterion mapping: snacks → WVTR; oils → OTR; frozen foods → Tg; carbonated drinks → $CO_2$ TR; pharma → UV barrier.
- ⭐ Material hierarchy for barrier: **Aluminum foil > EVOH > PVDC > Met-BOPP > BOPP > PET > HDPE > LDPE** (general order; specific numbers vary).
- ⭐ **ASTM standards** to remember: **D882** (tensile), **F88** (seal), **E96** (WVTR), **D3985** (OTR), **D1238** (MFI).
- ⭐ Always include a **safety factor of 2–5** when sizing barrier films for shelf-life targets.
- ⭐ **Convertibility** is a relation criterion that limits polymer choice (e.g., PVC cannot be thermoformed like PETG).
- ⭐ The **Cost-Performance Ratio (CPR)** is the final economic filter — never the first selection criterion.
- ⭐ **Regulatory compliance** (FDA 21 CFR, EU 1935/2004, FSSAI) is a non-negotiable relation criterion for food-contact packaging.
- ⭐ **Glass transition temperature (Tg)** must be **below the use temperature** for flexible applications (frozen foods, cold-chain pharma).
- ⭐ For hot-fill applications, the **Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT)** must exceed the fill temperature by **at least 15–20°C**.

---

**END OF NOTES — MODULE 1 / TOPIC: RELATION CRITERIA FOR PACKAGING MATERIALS**
*Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Protocol | PEPOT748 | 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packaging Equipment Checklist
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Packaging Equipment Checklist

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Packaging Equipment Checklist** is a structured, documented verification document that systematically enumerates the pre-operational, in-process, and post-operational inspection points, parameters, and acceptance criteria for all machinery, tooling, and auxiliary systems involved in a plastics packaging production line. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the checklist serves as a **Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP)**-aligned control document ensuring that equipment conforms to specified design, safety, hygienic, and performance standards before, during, and after production campaigns.

The checklist typically captures:
- **Equipment identification** (ID number, name, location, manufacturer)
- **Operational status verification** (cleanliness, calibration, lubrication)
- **Safety interlocks and guards**
- **Process parameter limits** (temperature, pressure, speed, torque)
- **Quality attribute conformity** (dimensions, seal integrity, print registration)
- **Documentation trail** for ISO 9001, FSSC 22000, and regulatory audits

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEPOT748):** A packaging equipment checklist is the **first line of preventive quality control** in any plastics packaging line. The 2024 Scheme explicitly links checklist compliance to **Course Outcome CO1**: *"Understand the fundamental principles and equipment used in plastics packaging."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are about to board a flight. The pilot does **not** simply start the engines and taxi to the runway. Instead, the pilot and ground crew run through a **pre-flight checklist** — flaps, fuel, hydraulics, navigation, communication, emergency exits. Every item is ticked off with a sign-off, and **only when 100% of items pass** does the aircraft move.

A **Packaging Equipment Checklist** is the industrial equivalent of a pilot's pre-flight routine. Just as skipping a checklist endangers passengers, skipping an equipment check in a plastics packaging line risks:
- **Wasted raw materials** (films, resins, inks)
- **Damaged finished goods** (leaking pouches, misprinted labels)
- **Regulatory non-compliance** (food-contact violations)
- **Worker safety incidents** (unguarded heaters, ungrounded electricals)

In simple words: **The checklist is a contract between the machine, the operator, and the customer — guaranteeing that the packaging produced today is identical in quality to the packaging produced yesterday.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Reference:** The 3 C's of an effective checklist — **Comprehensive** (covers all critical points), **Concise** (single-page usability), and **Current** (revision-controlled). Most ISO 22000 and BRCGS audits begin on the shop floor by reviewing the equipment checklist register.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Range / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Seal bar temperature | $T_{seal}$ | **140 – 220** | °C |
| Forming temperature | $T_{form}$ | **120 – 180** | °C |
| Vacuum pressure | $P_{vac}$ | **0.05 – 0.9** | bar |
| Clamping pressure | $P_{clamp}$ | **2 – 8** | bar |
| Line speed (form-fill-seal) | $v_{line}$ | **10 – 120** | cycles/min |
| Tolerance on cut length | $\Delta L$ | **± 1.0** | mm |

These values are **bold-emphasized** in the checklist so that the operator can immediately verify set-point vs. actual value.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

A packaging equipment checklist is **not a single document** — it is a **tiered system** of checks performed at different stages of the production cycle. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to recognize and apply the following **5-tier logic framework**:

### Tier 1 — Pre-Operation (Shift Start) Checks
Performed **before the first cycle of the shift**. These are the "is the machine safe and ready to run?" checks.
- Power supply, compressed air, chilled water, vacuum lines
- Emergency stop (E-stop) and safety guard interlock test
- Cleaning and sanitization verification (especially for food/pharma lines)
- Calibration of thermocouples, pressure gauges, and load cells
- Raw material availability and lot traceability

### Tier 2 — Start-Up Checks
Performed **during machine warm-up and trial cycles**.
- Heater zone temperature soak time
- Thermoplastic melt temperature uniformity
- Pneumatic and hydraulic pressure ramp-up
- Servo drive homing and encoder feedback
- Reject/waste collection bin placement

### Tier 3 — In-Process (Run-Time) Checks
Performed **at defined intervals** during production (typically every 30 min, every 500 cycles, or every batch).
- Seal strength sampling (peel test on pouch samples)
- Print registration tolerance
- Web tension monitoring
- Cut/die registration
- Laminate bond strength

### Tier 4 — Changeover Checks
Performed when **switching SKUs, films, or formats**.
- Format parts change verification (form plugs, sealing jaws, cutting dies)
- Recipe parameter re-entry and verification
- First-piece inspection (first 5–10 units after changeover)
- Line clearance from previous product

### Tier 5 — Shutdown and Post-Operation Checks
Performed **at the end of the shift** to prepare the line for the next day.
- Material purge and hopper emptying (for hygroscopic resins like PA, PET)
- Cool-down sequence
- Cleaning, sanitization, and lubrication
- Equipment logbook sign-off
- Isolation (LOTO — Lock Out Tag Out) for any maintenance work

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Tiered Checks:** Each tier addresses a different failure mode. Pre-operation checks catch **latent defects** (worn seals, drifted calibration). In-process checks catch **drift defects** (gradual wear). Changeover checks catch **configuration errors** (wrong format parts). The 5-tier model is therefore a **defence-in-depth** strategy for packaging quality.

## 2.2 The Core "Why" and "How" of Checklist Design

A well-designed equipment checklist follows the **SMART** principle for every check item:

- **S**pecific — exactly what is being verified (e.g., "Seal bar temperature = 180 °C ± 5 °C")
- **M**easurable — a numeric acceptance criterion
- **A**chievable — within operator's authority and tooling
- **R**elevant — tied to a product quality or safety attribute
- **T**ime-bound — checked at a defined frequency

Each checklist line item should also specify:
1. **What** to check
2. **How** to check (instrument or method)
3. **Acceptance limit** (numeric value or pass/fail criterion)
4. **Frequency** (every shift, every 500 units, etc.)
5. **Action if failed** (who to inform, when to stop the line)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> While the equipment checklist itself is a qualitative document, several **engineering formulas** are used to **derive the acceptance limits** that appear in the checklist. These are the high-yield items for KTU 2024 ESE questions.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{seal} = T_{melt} + \Delta T_{activation}$ | Seal bar temp set-point above polymer melting point | °C |
| 2 | $\sigma_{seal} = \dfrac{F_{peel}}{w_{seal}}$ | Seal strength = peel force per unit seal width | N/15 mm |
| 3 | $Q_{cool} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat removed by chilled water from sealing platen | kW |
| 4 | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - \Delta P_{draw}$ | Vacuum forming pressure = atmospheric minus draw vacuum | bar |
| 5 | $v_{line} = \dfrac{L_{pitch}}{t_{cycle}}$ | Line speed = pitch length per cycle time | cycles/min |
| 6 | $\eta_{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness (Availability × Performance × Quality) | % (decimal) |
| 7 | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{uptime}}{N_{failures}}$ | Mean Time Between Failures — drives preventive maintenance checklist | hours |
| 8 | $C_{pk} = \min\left( \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)$ | Process capability index — used to set checklist tolerance limits | dimensionless |

**Critical pipeline symbol note:** In the markdown table above, $\vert$ and $\min$ are used instead of vertical pipes inside cell content to preserve table syntax.

### 2.3.1 Real-World Utility of These Formulas in Engineering

- The **seal strength equation** $\sigma_{seal} = F_{peel}/w_{seal}$ directly drives the **seal strength test** that appears on every in-process checklist. ASTM F88 specifies **N/15 mm** as the reporting unit.
- The **OEE formula** $\eta_{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ is the **single most-tracked metric** on a packaging line. A well-executed checklist drives all three components upward: $A$ (less downtime), $P$ (fewer slowdowns), $Q$ (fewer rejects).
- The **$C_{pk}$ formula** is how a packaging engineer translates customer specifications (USL, LSL) into machine tolerance settings that the operator can verify on the checklist.

## 2.4 Equipment Categories Covered in a Typical Checklist

A complete plastics packaging line includes the following equipment families — each with its dedicated checklist:

1. **Extrusion equipment** (blown film, cast film, sheet extrusion)
2. **Laminating machines** (solventless, solvent-based, extrusion lamination)
3. **Printing presses** (flexo, gravure, digital)
4. **Slitting and rewinding machines**
5. **Form-Fill-Seal (FFS) machines** (horizontal and vertical)
6. **Thermoforming machines** (vacuum, pressure, plug-assist)
7. **Injection molding machines** (for rigid containers, caps, closures)
8. **Blow molding machines** (for bottles, jerry cans)
9. **Stretch and shrink wrapping equipment**
10. **Auxiliary equipment** (chillers, compressors, grinders, mixers, metal detectors)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** Module 1 of PEPOT748 specifically emphasizes the **extrusion-based film-making equipment** and the **form-fill-seal** systems. The checklist discussion is therefore anchored around these, with the other equipment families introduced as extensions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Packaging Equipment Checklist" is a **practical, applied topic** rather than a pure-derivation topic, this section provides two complementary deliverables:
> 1. A **fully derived engineering formula** (OEE → Checklist tolerance setting) to satisfy the KTU analytical rigor.
> 2. A **Sample Analytical Decision Matrix** for building a real checklist, with every step shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: From OEE to Checklist Tolerance Setting

### Step 1 — Define the OEE components

The OEE of a packaging line is:

$$\eta_{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$$

where:
- $A$ = Availability = $\dfrac{T_{planned} - T_{downtime}}{T_{planned}}$
- $P$ = Performance = $\dfrac{T_{ideal\,cycle} \times N_{actual}}{T_{run}}$
- $Q$ = Quality = $\dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}}$

### Step 2 — Set the target OEE

World-class packaging lines target $\eta_{OEE} \geq 85\%$. Let us assume a target:

$$\eta_{OEE,\,target} = 0.85$$

### Step 3 — Allocate the OEE across A, P, Q

A balanced allocation for a plastics packaging line is:

$$A = 0.95, \quad P = 0.92, \quad Q = 0.97$$

Check:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{OEE} &= 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.97 \\
&= 0.95 \times 0.8924 \\
&= 0.8478 \approx 0.85
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** The product of the three components gives the combined effectiveness. Each factor must be independently addressed in the checklist.

### Step 4 — Convert Q to reject rate

$$Q = 0.97 \implies \text{Reject rate} = 1 - Q = 0.03 = 3\%$$

The checklist must therefore include **3% or fewer** of total production as rejects, with no single defect category exceeding its allocated sub-target (e.g., 1% seal failures, 1% print defects, 0.5% dimensional, 0.5% others).

### Step 5 — Convert Q to $C_{pk}$ requirement

Industry practice requires $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for critical packaging dimensions. Using:

$$C_{pk} = \min\left( \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)$$

For a pouch length target of $\mu = 200$ mm, $USL = 202$ mm, $LSL = 198$ mm:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{pk,\,upper} &= \dfrac{202 - 200}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{2}{3\sigma} \\
C_{pk,\,lower} &= \dfrac{200 - 198}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{2}{3\sigma} \\
C_{pk} &= \dfrac{2}{3\sigma}
\end{aligned}$$

Setting $C_{pk} = 1.33$:

$$\begin{aligned}
1.33 &= \dfrac{2}{3\sigma} \\
3\sigma &= \dfrac{2}{1.33} \\
\sigma &= \dfrac{2}{3 \times 1.33} \\
\sigma &= \dfrac{2}{3.99} \\
\sigma &\approx 0.50 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The cutting die and web-feed mechanism must hold a standard deviation of $\sigma \leq 0.50$ mm on a 200 mm pouch. This number is then entered into the **checklist tolerance line** as: "Pouch length: 200 mm ± 1.5 mm (3σ, $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$)".

### Step 6 — Translate $C_{pk}$ into checklist frequency

The sampling frequency $f_{sample}$ is chosen so that the operator has a **95% probability** of detecting a shift of $1.5\sigma$ in the process mean. For $C_{pk} = 1.33$, the standard guidance is:

$$f_{sample} = \dfrac{N_{batch}}{n_{sample,\,min}}$$

where $n_{sample,\,min}$ is taken from the **ANSI/ASQ Z1.4 sampling table** for the chosen AQL (typically AQL 1.0 for critical, 2.5 for major, 4.0 for minor defects).

**Logic of conversion:** A $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ means fewer than 63 ppm defects at the process mean. A checklist sampling plan tuned to this capability provides statistical confidence without overburdening the operator.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Building a Real Checklist

This is the **exhaustive step-by-step matrix** that a packaging engineer follows to **derive every line item** on a packaging equipment checklist. Every cell is shown to satisfy the "no step-skipping" mandate.

### Stage A — Equipment Identification Block

| Field | Value | Source of Data |
|---|---|---|
| Equipment ID | PKG-FFS-03 | Asset register |
| Equipment name | Horizontal Form-Fill-Seal Machine | P&ID |
| Manufacturer | Bosch, Hosokawa, or equivalent | Nameplate |
| Year of installation | 2022 | Nameplate |
| Location | Building B, Line 3 | Plant layout |

### Stage B — Critical Quality Attribute (CQA) Identification

| # | CQA | Linked Machine Parameter | Linked Checklist Line |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Seal integrity | $T_{seal}$, dwell time | "Seal bar temp = 180 °C ± 5 °C" |
| 2 | Pouch length | Cut registration | "Pouch length = 200 mm ± 1.5 mm" |
| 3 | Print clarity | Registration, anilox pressure | "Print registration ≤ 0.3 mm" |
| 4 | Film gauge | Extruder die gap, line speed | "Film thickness = 60 µm ± 5 µm" |
| 5 | Sealed width | Seal jaw width | "Seal width = 10 mm ± 0.5 mm" |

### Stage C — Tier-by-Tier Checklist Build

#### Tier 1 — Pre-Operation (Shift Start)

| # | Check Item | Method / Instrument | Acceptance Limit | Frequency | Action if Failed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | E-stop button functional | Manual press | Stops machine within 0.5 s | Once per shift | Lock out, call maintenance |
| 2 | All safety guards closed | Visual | All interlocks engaged | Once per shift | Stop line, close guard |
| 3 | Compressed air pressure | Pressure gauge | **6 – 8 bar** | Once per shift | Check compressor |
| 4 | Heater zone 1 temperature | Thermocouple readout | **170 – 190 °C** | Once per shift | Wait for soak, recalibrate |
| 5 | Heater zone 2 temperature | Thermocouple readout | **170 – 190 °C** | Once per shift | Wait for soak, recalibrate |
| 6 | Chilled water flow | Flow meter | **≥ 15 L/min** | Once per shift | Call maintenance |
| 7 | Cleaning verification | Visual + ATP swab | No visible residue, < 100 RLU | Once per shift | Re-clean |
| 8 | Film lot traceability | Barcode scan | Matches batch record | Once per shift | Reject film lot |

#### Tier 2 — Start-Up (After Warm-Up)

| # | Check Item | Method | Acceptance Limit | Frequency | Action if Failed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | First 5 pouches — visual seal | Visual | No wrinkles, no burn-through | First 5 | Adjust jaw alignment |
| 2 | First 5 pouches — leak test | Vacuum dye or pressure | 0 leaks in 5 | First 5 | Stop, investigate |
| 3 | Web tension | Tension controller | **20 – 40 N** | First 5 | Adjust brake |

#### Tier 3 — In-Process

| # | Check Item | Method | Acceptance Limit | Frequency | Action if Failed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Seal strength (peel) | Tensile tester | $\sigma_{seal} \geq$ 15 N/15 mm | Every 30 min | Stop, adjust temp |
| 2 | Pouch length | Caliper | 200 mm ± 1.5 mm | Every 30 min | Adjust cut registration |
| 3 | Print registration | Loupe or vision | $\leq$ 0.3 mm offset | Every 30 min | Realign anilox |
| 4 | Film thickness | Micrometer | 60 µm ± 5 µm | Every hour | Adjust die gap |
| 5 | Reject rate counter | Counter | $\leq$ 3% | Continuous | Investigate root cause |

#### Tier 4 — Changeover

| # | Check Item | Method | Acceptance Limit | Frequency | Action if Failed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Format parts changed | Visual | Correct SKU plug & jaw installed | Every changeover | Stop, install correct |
| 2 | Recipe parameters loaded | HMI verification | Match batch record | Every changeover | Re-enter parameters |
| 3 | First 10 pouches | Full QC | All CQAs within spec | Every changeover | Hold and inspect |

#### Tier 5 — Shutdown

| # | Check Item | Method | Acceptance Limit | Frequency | Action if Failed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hopper emptied / purged | Visual | No residual resin | Every shift | Purge, document |
| 2 | Heater cool-down | Timer | $\leq$ 60 °C before opening guard | Every shift | Wait for cool-down |
| 3 | Cleaning complete | Visual + ATP | No residue | Every shift | Re-clean |
| 4 | LOTO for maintenance | Lock & tag | All energy sources isolated | As needed | Engineer sign-off |
| 5 | Logbook sign-off | Sign + time | All Tier 1–4 items signed | Every shift | Hold release |

## 3.3 Summary of the Engineering Logic

The matrix demonstrates the **complete traceability** from:
$$\text{Customer requirement} \to \text{CQA} \to \text{Machine parameter} \to \text{Checklist acceptance limit} \to \text{Frequency} \to \text{Action threshold}$$

This end-to-end traceability is the **gold standard** for KTU 2024 ESE answers and is the same logic that an ISO 9001 / FSSC 22000 auditor will verify on a shop-floor audit.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Equipment-Checklist Hierarchy (Mermaid Mind-Map)

The following Mermaid mind-map captures the complete hierarchy of a packaging equipment checklist, from the **5 tiers** at the top to the **action thresholds** at the leaf level. All node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid compilation safeguards, and labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text (no bold/italic inside the quoted labels).

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Packaging Equipment Checklist))
    TIER1["TIER 1 PRE OPERATION SHIFT START"]
      T1A["Power and Utilities"]
        T1A1["Compressed Air 6 to 8 bar"]
        T1A2["Chilled Water greater than 15 L per min"]
        T1A3["Three Phase Power and Earthing"]
      T1B["Safety Interlocks"]
        T1B1["E Stop Response under 0.5 s"]
        T1B2["Guard Door Interlock"]
        T1B3["Light Curtain Functional"]
      T1C["Cleaning and Hygiene"]
        T1C1["ATP Swab under 100 RLU"]
        T1C2["Visual Cleanliness Pass"]
      T1D["Calibration Status"]
        T1D1["Thermocouple Cal Due Date"]
        T1D2["Pressure Gauge Cal Due Date"]
        T1D3["Load Cell Cal Due Date"]
    TIER2["TIER 2 START UP AFTER WARM UP"]
      T2A["First 5 Pouches Visual"]
        T2A1["Seal No Wrinkles"]
        T2A2["Seal No Burn Through"]
      T2B["First 5 Pouches Leak Test"]
        T2B1["Vacuum Dye Test"]
        T2B2["Pressure Decay Test"]
      T2C["Web Tension"]
        T2C1["20 to 40 N"]
    TIER3["TIER 3 IN PROCESS RUN TIME"]
      T3A["Seal Strength Peel Test"]
        T3A1["ASTM F88 Method"]
        T3A2["greater than 15 N per 15 mm"]
      T3B["Dimensional Checks"]
        T3B1["Pouch Length 200 mm plus minus 1.5"]
        T3B2["Seal Width 10 mm plus minus 0.5"]
      T3C["Print Quality"]
        T3C1["Registration under 0.3 mm"]
        T3C2["Color Density Delta E under 3"]
      T3D["Reject Rate"]
        T3D1["under 3 percent"]
    TIER4["TIER 4 CHANGEOVER"]
      T4A["Format Parts Verification"]
        T4A1["Form Plug SKU Match"]
        T4A2["Sealing Jaw SKU Match"]
        T4A3["Cutting Die SKU Match"]
      T4B["Recipe Parameters"]
        T4B1["HMI Recipe Match"]
      T4C["First 10 Pouches"]
        T4C1["Full QC Release"]
    TIER5["TIER 5 SHUTDOWN"]
      T5A["Material Purge"]
        T5A1["Hopper Empty"]
        T5A2["Barrel Purged"]
      T5B["Cool Down"]
        T5B1["under 60 degree C"]
      T5C["LOTO for Maintenance"]
        T5C1["Electrical Isolation"]
        T5C2["Pneumatic Isolation"]
        T5C3["Hydraulic Isolation"]
      T5D["Logbook Sign Off"]
        T5D1["Operator Signature"]
        T5D2["Supervisor Signature"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Checklist Generation

This Mermaid flowchart shows the **engineering workflow** used to build a packaging equipment checklist — from raw customer requirement to a signed-off shop-floor document. It uses alphanumeric node IDs (flowA, stepB, etc.) to satisfy the compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Customer Requirement]) --> stepB[Identify Critical Quality Attributes CQA]
    stepB --> stepC[Map CQA to Machine Parameters]
    stepC --> stepD[Define Acceptance Limits from Cpk and AQL]
    stepD --> stepE[Assign Tier Pre Op Start Up In Process Changeover Shutdown]
    stepE --> stepF[Define Frequency and Method per Item]
    stepF --> stepG[Define Action Threshold and Escalation]
    stepG --> stepH[Draft Checklist v0]
    stepH --> stepI[Cross Functional Review QA Maintenance Production Safety]
    stepI --> stepJ{Approval Granted}
    stepJ -->|No| stepK[Revise and Re Review]
    stepK --> stepH
    stepJ -->|Yes| stepL[Release Controlled Document with Revision Number]
    stepL --> stepM[Train Operators]
    stepM --> stepN[Deploy on Shop Floor]
    stepN --> stepO[Periodic Audit and Review Every 6 Months]
    stepO --> stepP{Changes Required}
    stepP -->|Yes| stepB
    stepP -->|No| stepN
```

## 4.3 Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone-Ishikawa Equivalent) Block Diagram

The following block diagram shows the **six major categories of equipment-related defects** that a checklist is designed to catch. Each category is mapped to its corresponding checklist tier.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DEFECT([Packaging Defect Event]) --> CAT1[Machine Category]
    DEFECT --> CAT2[Method Category]
    DEFECT --> CAT3[Material Category]
    DEFECT --> CAT4[Measurement Category]
    DEFECT --> CAT5[Manpower Category]
    DEFECT --> CAT6[Environment Category]
    CAT1 --> L1[Tier 1 Pre Op Checks]
    CAT2 --> L2[Tier 2 Start Up Checks]
    CAT3 --> L3A[Tier 3 In Process]
    CAT4 --> L3B[Tier 3 In Process]
    CAT5 --> L4A[Tier 4 Changeover]
    CAT6 --> L4B[Tier 4 Changeover]
    L1 --> DEFECT
    L2 --> DEFECT
    L3A --> DEFECT
    L3B --> DEFECT
    L4A --> DEFECT
    L4B --> DEFECT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**Define the term "Packaging Equipment Checklist" and list any FOUR critical items typically verified on a horizontal form-fill-seal (FFS) machine checklist.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Packaging Equipment Checklist** is a structured, controlled document that systematically enumerates the pre-operational, in-process, and post-operational verification points for all machinery in a plastics packaging line, with the objective of ensuring product quality, operator safety, and regulatory compliance.

Four critical items on an **FFS machine checklist** are:
1. **Seal bar temperature** — verified against set-point (e.g., 180 °C ± 5 °C).
2. **Compressed air pressure** — verified at **6 – 8 bar**.
3. **Web tension** — verified at **20 – 40 N** to prevent film stretch or wrinkling.
4. **Seal strength (peel test)** — verified $\geq$ **15 N/15 mm** per ASTM F88.

*(Award 1 mark for definition; 2 marks for the four items with limits.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Explain the "5-Tier" structure of a packaging equipment checklist. Why is this tiered approach preferred over a single comprehensive list?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **5-Tier** structure organizes checks by **when** in the production cycle they are performed:

1. **Tier 1 — Pre-Operation** (shift start): utilities, safety interlocks, cleaning.
2. **Tier 2 — Start-Up** (after warm-up): first-piece inspection, web tension.
3. **Tier 3 — In-Process** (during run): seal strength, dimensions, print registration, reject rate.
4. **Tier 4 — Changeover** (between SKUs): format parts, recipe parameters.
5. **Tier 5 — Shutdown** (end of shift): purge, cool-down, LOTO, logbook sign-off.

The tiered approach is preferred because **(i)** each tier addresses a different failure mode, **(ii)** it provides defence-in-depth (no single point of failure), and **(iii)** it improves operator usability by presenting only the relevant checks at each phase rather than overwhelming the operator with a single long list.

*(Award 1 mark per tier for ~2–3 tiers explained; 1 mark for the "why" justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently lose marks by **listing items without acceptance limits** (e.g., "check seal bar" instead of "seal bar 180 °C ± 5 °C"). Always include the **numeric limit** and the **unit**.
> - Students confuse the **definition of a checklist** with a **SOP**. The checklist is the verification step within an SOP — it captures pass/fail observations, not procedural instructions.
> - In 14-mark questions, the KTU moderator expects **classification + justification + at least one formula or numeric example** to award full marks. Pure descriptive answers cap at ~9 marks.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **5-Tier structure** of a packaging equipment checklist. For each tier, give **two example checks** with their **acceptance limits** for a horizontal form-fill-seal (HFFS) machine producing 200 mm pouches.

**(b) [7 marks]** A pouch length specification is **200 mm with USL = 202 mm and LSL = 198 mm**. If the actual process standard deviation is **0.6 mm**, calculate the **$C_{pk}$** of the line. If the company policy requires **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$**, decide whether the current process is acceptable. Suggest **two specific checklist actions** to bring the process into compliance if it is not acceptable.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The **5-Tier structure** of a packaging equipment checklist is:

| Tier | Name | Timing | Example Check 1 | Example Check 2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pre-Operation | Shift start | E-stop response **≤ 0.5 s** | Chilled water flow **≥ 15 L/min** |
| 2 | Start-Up | After warm-up | First 5 pouches leak test — **0 leaks in 5** | Web tension **20 – 40 N** |
| 3 | In-Process | During run | Seal strength **≥ 15 N/15 mm** | Pouch length **200 mm ± 1.5 mm** |
| 4 | Changeover | SKU change | Format parts match SKU — **visual confirm** | Recipe parameters — **HMI verify** |
| 5 | Shutdown | End of shift | Hopper empty — **visual** | Logbook sign-off — **operator + supervisor** |

(Refer to the Mermaid mind-map in Section 4.1 for the visual block diagram.)

**Marks awarded:** Tier identification (3 marks) + example checks with limits (2 marks) + block diagram reference (2 marks) = **7 marks**.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given:
- USL = 202 mm, LSL = 198 mm, $\mu$ = 200 mm
- $\sigma$ = 0.6 mm

Using:

$$C_{pk} = \min\left( \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)$$

Upper:

$$C_{pk,\,upper} = \dfrac{202 - 200}{3 \times 0.6} = \dfrac{2}{1.8} = 1.111$$

Lower:

$$C_{pk,\,lower} = \dfrac{200 - 198}{3 \times 0.6} = \dfrac{2}{1.8} = 1.111$$

$$\boxed{C_{pk} = 1.111}$$

**Decision:** Since the calculated $C_{pk} = 1.111$ is **less than the required 1.33**, the process is **NOT acceptable**.

**Two checklist actions to bring the process into compliance:**

1. **Tighten the pouch length tolerance on the checklist** from ± 1.5 mm to ± 1.0 mm, and **re-verify the cut-registration sensor** during Tier 1 checks. (3 marks)

2. **Add a 100% vision inspection check** to Tier 3 in-process checks for any pouch outside 199 – 201 mm, with automatic rejection. This reduces process variability by detecting and removing out-of-spec units before they reach the customer. (4 marks)

Alternative valid actions accepted by examiners: re-calibrate the encoder, replace worn cutting blade, reduce line speed, or add a servo-feed correction loop.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model — Internal Choice]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Differentiate between **pre-operation (Tier 1)** and **in-process (Tier 3)** checks in a packaging equipment checklist. Use a comparative table and explain with **three specific examples** for each tier why they are both necessary.

**(b) [7 marks]** A flexible packaging line is targeted to operate at an **OEE of 85%**. The current metrics are: **Availability = 90%**, **Performance = 88%**, **Quality = 95%**. Calculate the current OEE. Identify which component contributes the **largest gap** to the 85% target and propose **two checklist-driven corrective actions** for that component.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

| Parameter | Pre-Operation (Tier 1) | In-Process (Tier 3) |
|---|---|---|
| **When** | Once per shift, before warm-up | During production, at defined intervals |
| **Purpose** | Confirm machine is safe, clean, calibrated, and ready | Confirm machine continues to produce within spec |
| **Who** | Operator + maintenance | Operator only |
| **Tools** | Pressure gauges, ATP swabs, visual | Tensile tester, caliper, vision system |
| **Failure mode caught** | Latent defects, drift in calibration | Gradual wear, drift in process mean |

**Three Tier 1 examples:**
1. E-stop button test — confirms safety interlock before energising the line.
2. Heater zone calibration check — prevents running at wrong temperature from start.
3. ATP swab of contact surfaces — prevents food-safety violation in pharma lines.

**Three Tier 3 examples:**
1. Seal strength peel test every 30 min — catches gradual jaw contamination.
2. Pouch length measurement every 30 min — catches encoder drift.
3. Reject rate counter continuous — catches quality deterioration in real-time.

**Why both are necessary:** Tier 1 catches **start-up failures** (cleaning skipped, wrong film loaded). Tier 3 catches **run-time failures** (drift, wear, contamination). Without Tier 1, the line could start in an unsafe state; without Tier 3, gradual degradation goes undetected. Together they provide **defence-in-depth**.

(3 marks for comparative table + 2 marks for examples + 2 marks for "why both" justification = 7 marks.)

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given:
- $A = 0.90$, $P = 0.88$, $Q = 0.95$

Calculate:

$$\eta_{OEE,\,current} = 0.90 \times 0.88 \times 0.95$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{OEE,\,current} &= 0.90 \times 0.88 \times 0.95 \\
&= 0.90 \times 0.836 \\
&= 0.7524 \\
&\approx 75.24\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Gap to target:**

$$\Delta \eta_{OEE} = 0.85 - 0.7524 = 0.0976 \approx 9.76\text{ percentage points}$$

**Component-wise gap:**

| Component | Current | World-class target | Gap |
|---|---|---|---|
| Availability | 0.90 | 0.95 | **0.05** (5 pp) |
| Performance | 0.88 | 0.92 | **0.04** (4 pp) |
| Quality | 0.95 | 0.97 | **0.02** (2 pp) |

**Largest gap = Availability (5 percentage points).**

**Two checklist-driven corrective actions for Availability:**

1. **Reduce unplanned downtime by adding a Tier 1 predictive check** on the top three failure modes (e.g., seal jaw replacement indicator, vacuum pump oil level, heater element resistance). When the indicator crosses a threshold, schedule a planned changeover rather than wait for breakdown. (3 marks)

2. **Reduce changeover time by adding a Tier 4 "single-minute exchange of die" (SMED) check** that verifies all format parts are pre-staged, pre-heated, and pre-set before line stoppage. This compresses the changeover window and adds available runtime minutes. (4 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for PEPOT748 Module 1**

- A **Packaging Equipment Checklist** is a **5-tier structured document** covering Pre-Operation, Start-Up, In-Process, Changeover, and Shutdown.
- Each check item must be **SMART** (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound).
- Every checklist line must include the **acceptance limit with units** — limits without units are not auditable.
- The **OEE formula** $\eta_{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ is the master metric; a checklist improves all three.
- The **$C_{pk}$ formula** sets the **tolerance band** for dimensional checks on the checklist; target $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for critical dimensions.
