- **Seal strength** is reported in **N/15 mm** per **ASTM F88** — this is the single most-tested in-process check.
- **Tier 1 (Pre-Op)** = safety, cleaning, calibration.
- **Tier 3 (In-Process)** = quality attributes (seal, dimension, print, gauge).
- **Tier 4 (Changeover)** = SKU-specific format parts and recipe.
- **Tier 5 (Shutdown)** = purge, cool-down, LOTO, logbook.
- **LOTO** (Lock Out Tag Out) is mandatory for any maintenance activity — never omitted from the checklist.
- **Tiered checks = defence-in-depth**; the KTU examiner expects you to use this phrase in 14-mark answers.
- **Traceability** chain for any checklist item: **Customer Spec → CQA → Machine Parameter → Acceptance Limit → Frequency → Action**.
- The **5-tier model** maps to the **6M Ishikawa categories** (Machine, Method, Material, Measurement, Manpower, Environment) — know this mapping for ESE.
- For a **200 mm pouch with $\pm 1.5$ mm tolerance**, the implied $C_{pk} = 1.0$ (boundary acceptable); for $C_{pk} = 1.33$, the tolerance must be **± 1.0 mm** at $\sigma = 0.5$ mm.
- **Reel change verification** (T1) and **first-piece inspection** (T2) are the two highest-leverage items in the entire checklist — never skip them in revision.
- **Audit-ready** checklists are **revision-controlled, signed, and dated**; unsigned checklists are treated as not performed.
- The KTU 2024 Module 1 emphasis is on **FFS and extrusion equipment**; thermoforming and injection molding are covered in later modules but follow the **same 5-tier template**.
- Memorize the **standard acceptance values**: $T_{seal}$ 140–220 °C, $P_{air}$ 6–8 bar, web tension 20–40 N, seal strength $\geq$ 15 N/15 mm, OEE target 85%.
- In **ESE answers**, always lead with a **definition**, follow with a **classification/table**, then a **formula or worked example** — this is the KTU 14-mark template.
- The **80/20 rule** for checklist design: 20% of the items catch 80% of the failures — focus revision on those high-leverage items.
- The **pitfall to avoid** in 14-mark questions: descriptive answers without **numeric limits, formulas, or diagrams** cap at ~9 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Histories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Case Histories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Plastics Packaging Technology**, a *Case History* refers to a structured, documented engineering record of a real or near-real packaging application — capturing the **material selection rationale, design constraints, processing route, performance evaluation, and end-use outcome** of a specific plastic package. It is a problem-based learning instrument that integrates polymer science, package design, regulatory compliance, and market economics into a single, traceable narrative.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 1 – Case Histories):**
> Students must be able to analyze and present packaging case studies demonstrating the *selection of polymer(s)*, *conversion process*, *barrier/functional performance*, *end-use validation*, and *sustainability footprint* — connecting theory of plastics to industry practice.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a Case History in plastics packaging the same way a **doctor reviews a patient's medical chart**:
- The *patient* = the product to be packaged (e.g., milk, a pharmaceutical tablet, a snack).
- The *symptoms* = the protection/shelf-life/marketing needs.
- The *prescription* = the choice of polymer, structure, and converting process.
- The *follow-up* = real-world performance, recalls, or consumer feedback.

Just as a doctor uses past cases to diagnose new patients, a packaging engineer uses documented Case Histories to make **evidence-based design decisions** rather than guesswork.

## 1.3 Why Case Histories Matter in KTU Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** award marks for:
> - **Application-level analysis** (Revised Bloom's Level 3 & 4)
> - Linking polymer properties (mechanical, barrier, thermal) to **end-use demands**
> - Identifying **failure modes** and their corrective redesign
> - Demonstrating awareness of **regulatory** and **sustainability** issues

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics Used in Case Histories

- **Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR):** measured in **cc/m²/day**
- **Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR):** measured in **g/m²/day**
- **Shelf-life:** measured in **months**
- **Melt Flow Index (MFI):** measured in **g/10 min**
- **Tensile strength:** measured in **MPa**
- **Overall Migration Limit (OML):** **10 mg/dm²** of food contact surface (EU Regulation EU/10/2011)

---

> [!TIP]
> A *good* Case History answer in KTU exams has **5 ingredients**:
> **Product → Problem → Polymer/Process choice → Validation → Outcome/Lesson.**
> Memorize this five-step template; it earns maximum marks consistently.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Case Histories in Plastics Packaging

Case histories can be classified along four orthogonal axes, each of which is a common KTU question stem:

### 2.1.1 By End-Use Industry

| Industry Segment | Typical Case | Dominant Polymer |
|---|---|---|
| Food & Beverage | Carbonated soft drink bottle | **PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate)** |
| Dairy | Milk pouch / Aseptic brick | **LDPE / EVOH coextrusion** |
| Pharmaceutical | Blister pack for tablets | **PVC / PVDC / Aluminum foil laminate** |
| Personal Care | Shampoo bottle | **HDPE / PP multilayer** |
| Industrial | Chemical drum liner | **LLDPE / Barrier PE** |
| Medical Devices | Sterile barrier wrap | **Tyvek®-equivalent spun PE / PP** |
| Fresh Produce | MAP trays | **PET / PP with anti-fog coating** |

### 2.1.2 By Packaging Function Performed

- **Primary Packaging** — direct product contact (e.g., PET bottle).
- **Secondary Packaging** — collation, branding (e.g., shrink film on multi-pack).
- **Tertiary Packaging** — logistics, palletization (e.g., stretch wrap).
- **Active Packaging** — interacts with product (oxygen scavengers, ethanol emitters).
- **Intelligent Packaging** — communicates condition (TTI labels, freshness sensors).

### 2.1.3 By Innovation Type

- *Material innovation* (e.g., **PLA bioplastic** replacing PS in yoghurt cups).
- *Process innovation* (e.g., **co-injection molding** for multi-layer containers).
- *Design innovation* (e.g., **stand-up pouches** replacing rigid cans).
- *System innovation* (e.g., **Refillable PET** with deposit-return scheme).

### 2.1.4 By Outcome (Success / Failure)

- **Success cases** — e.g., transition from glass to PET for carbonated drinks.
- **Failure cases** — e.g., early 1990s PVC bottle phase-out due to dioxin fears.

## 2.2 The Five-Stage Case-History Analysis Framework (KTU Gold Standard)

A KTU examiner expects every case-history answer to follow these structured steps:

### Stage 1 — Product & Market Identification
- What is being packaged?
- Target consumer, geography, price point.
- Required shelf-life and distribution chain (cold chain, ambient, export).

### Stage 2 — Requirement Engineering
- Barrier requirement (OTR, WVTR).
- Mechanical requirement (drop, top-load, burst strength).
- Optical requirement (clarity, haze).
- Chemical resistance (acidic, fatty, alcoholic).
- Regulatory requirement (FSSAI, FDA, EU 10/2011, BPA-free).

### Stage 3 — Polymer & Structure Selection
- Match requirements to polymer properties.
- Decide monolayer vs. multilayer vs. laminate.
- Select additives (anti-block, slip, UV stabilizer, anti-fog, oxygen scavenger).

### Stage 4 — Process Selection
- Bottles → **Blow molding** (ISBM, EBM).
- Pouches → **Film extrusion + lamination**.
- Trays → **Thermoforming**.
- Caps/closures → **Injection molding**.
- Rigid containers → **Injection stretch blow molding**.

### Stage 5 — Validation, Outcome & Lesson
- Real shelf-life test vs. predicted.
- Consumer acceptance, market share change.
- Failures and redesign iterations.
- Sustainability footprint (carbon, recyclability).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet for Case Histories

| Concept | Key Equation / Rule | Use in Case History |
|---|---|---|
| Shelf-life (oxygen-sensitive) | $t_{sl} \;=\; \dfrac{(C_{crit} - C_{0}) \cdot V}{OTR \cdot A}$ | Predict expiry of oxygen-sensitive product in a plastic container |
| Permeability (Fick's Law) | $J \;=\; -D \cdot \dfrac{\partial c}{\partial x}$ | Relates material thickness to barrier performance |
| Arrhenius for shelf-life | $k(T) \;=\; A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Accelerate shelf-life testing at elevated T |
| Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF) | $\log a_T \;=\; \dfrac{-C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$ | Predict polymer relaxation/aging in packaging |
| Stress in container wall | $\sigma_{hoop} \;=\; \dfrac{P \cdot r}{t}$ | Bottle burst strength calculation |
| Migration (Fick's 2nd law) | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} \;=\; D \cdot \dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$ | Predict additive migration into food |
| Recycling efficiency | $\eta \;=\; \dfrac{m_{recycled}}{m_{input}} \times 100\%$ | Quantify case-history sustainability metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes `|` are used in this table** — all absolute value / conditional notations are expressed using $\vert$ or $\mid$ to preserve markdown rendering integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- In **R&D labs**, Case Histories accelerate new product development by providing comparative data on prior launches.
- In **failure analysis**, they enable *root-cause tracing* (e.g., delamination, seal failure, odor scalping).
- In **regulatory submissions**, they form the documentary backbone for *Migration Test Reports*.
- In **sustainability reporting**, they quantify *Life Cycle Inventory (LCI)* outcomes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Shelf-life of an Oxygen-Sensitive Beverage in a PET Bottle

A canonical KTU 14-mark Case History question asks: *"Estimate the shelf-life of a fruit juice packed in a monolayer PET bottle."* Below is the exhaustive step-by-step solution.

### Given Data (typical KTU problem statement)
- Bottle volume: $V = 500 \; \text{cm}^{3}$
- Internal headspace oxygen: $C_0 = 2 \; \text{vol}\%$
- Critical oxygen level (taste failure): $C_{crit} = 5 \; \text{vol}\%$
- Bottle surface area: $A = 350 \; \text{cm}^{2}$
- OTR of monolayer PET (25 µm): $OTR = 100 \; \text{cc/m}^{2}/\text{day}$ at $23\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $50\%\,\text{RH}$

### Step 1 — Convert all units to a single base
Surface area in m²:

$$A = 350 \; \text{cm}^{2} \times \dfrac{1 \; \text{m}^{2}}{10\,000 \; \text{cm}^{2}} = 0.035 \; \text{m}^{2}$$

Headspace volume: the oxygen resides in the gas phase, typically $V_{gas} \approx 0.03 \cdot V$ for a juice bottle.

$$V_{gas} = 0.03 \times 500 = 15 \; \text{cm}^{3}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the governing equation

$$t_{sl} = \dfrac{(C_{crit} - C_{0}) \cdot V_{gas}}{OTR \cdot A}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$t_{sl} = \dfrac{(5 - 2) \cdot 15}{100 \cdot 0.035} \; \text{day}$$

$$t_{sl} = \dfrac{45}{3.5} \; \text{day}$$

$$t_{sl} = 12.86 \; \text{days}$$

### Step 3 — Interpret the result
A monolayer PET bottle would only keep oxygen-sensitive juice fresh for **~13 days** at ambient conditions — far short of the typical 6-month commercial target. This is the *teaching punchline* of the case history:

> [!WARNING]
> **Engineering lesson:** Monolayer PET is **inadequate** for oxygen-sensitive products. The case history therefore recommends a redesign — typically a **multilayer PET/EVOH/PET structure** with OTR reduced to ~$1 \; \text{cc/m}^{2}/\text{day}$, extending shelf-life to **~1280 days**, comfortably above the 180-day commercial target.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Migration from Plastic into Food (Simulant)

**Problem statement (KTU Part B style):** A PVC gasketing compound in a metal jar lid releases a plasticizer (DEHP) at a diffusion coefficient $D = 1 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$ at $25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. Initial concentration $C_0 = 1000 \; \text{mg/kg}$ in a $L = 0.5 \; \text{mm}$ thick gasket. Estimate the surface concentration after 30 days assuming Fickian diffusion from a slab of finite thickness into an infinite food simulant.

### Governing equation (Fick's 2nd law, slab geometry, short time)

$$\dfrac{M_{t}}{M_{\infty}} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{D \cdot t}{L^{2}}}$$

### Step 1 — Compute the dimensionless time parameter

$$\dfrac{D \cdot t}{L^{2}} = \dfrac{(1 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (30 \times 24 \times 3600)}{(0.5 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}$$

$$= \dfrac{(1 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 2.592 \times 10^{6}}{2.5 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$= \dfrac{2.592 \times 10^{-6}}{2.5 \times 10^{-7}} = 10.368$$

### Step 2 — Recognize the limit
Because $\dfrac{D \cdot t}{L^{2}} \gg 0.05$, the short-time approximation is **not valid**. Use the long-time asymptotic form:

$$\dfrac{M_{t}}{M_{\infty}} = 1 - \dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\pi^{2} D t}{L^{2}}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the exponent

$$-\dfrac{\pi^{2} \cdot 10.368}{1} = -32.55 \cdot \pi^{2} \cdot \text{(re-check)} = -102.3$$

Wait — re-derive carefully:

$$-\dfrac{\pi^{2} D t}{L^{2}} = -\pi^{2} \times 10.368 \approx -9.8696 \times 10.368 \approx -102.3$$

### Step 4 — Compute the mass fraction migrated

$$\dfrac{M_{t}}{M_{\infty}} = 1 - \dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot e^{-102.3}$$

$$e^{-102.3} \approx 0$$

$$\dfrac{M_{t}}{M_{\infty}} \approx 1.0$$

### Step 5 — Case-history conclusion
After 30 days, **essentially 100 %** of the available DEHP would have migrated out of a 0.5 mm PVC gasket — a **gross regulatory failure** against the **SML of 1.5 mg/kg** for DEHP in food contact. The redesign lesson:

> [!NOTE]
> Replace PVC gaskets with **TPE or aluminum-faced laminate liners**, or use **polymeric plasticizers with $D < 10^{-14} \; \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Common KTU 7-Mark Sub-Question)

| Parameter | Case A — Glass Bottle | Case B — PET Bottle | Case C — Stand-up Pouch |
|---|---|---|---|
| Weight (1 L) | 380 g | 38 g | 8 g |
| Breakage risk | High | Low | Negligible |
| Recyclability | Infinite loops | Mechanical, 4–6 loops | Difficult (laminate) |
| OTR (25 µm) | 0 (hermetic) | 100 cc/m²/day | 1–5 cc/m²/day (with EVOH) |
| Carbon footprint | 850 g CO₂/L | 165 g CO₂/L | 50 g CO₂/L |
| Consumer convenience | Poor (heavy) | Excellent | Excellent |
| Shelf-life achieved | 24 months | 6–12 months | 6–9 months |

> [!TIP]
> This **A-vs-B-vs-C comparison table** is the highest-yield template for any KTU 14-mark case-history question. It instantly demonstrates *engineering judgment* and earns full marks.

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Generic Case-History Decision Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1([Start]) --> stepA[Identify Product and Market Need]
    stepA --> stepB[Define Shelf Life and Barrier Requirements]
    stepB --> stepC{Single Layer or Multilayer}
    stepC -- Single --> stepD1[Select Single Polymer PET PE or PP]
    stepC -- Multi --> stepD2[Select Coextrusion or Laminate with EVOH PVDC or Aluminum]
    stepD1 --> stepE[Choose Conversion Process Blow Film or Thermoforming]
    stepD2 --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF[Prototype and Lab Testing OTR WVTR Burst Drop]
    stepF --> stepG{Meets Specification}
    stepG -- No --> stepH[Redesign Polymer or Structure]
    stepH --> stepF
    stepG -- Yes --> stepI[Market Launch and Post Launch Monitoring]
    stepI --> stepJ[Document Lessons as Case History]
    stepJ --> stop1([End])
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a Case History in the context of plastics packaging technology. List the five essential components of a well-documented case history.**

**Model Answer:**
A *Case History* in plastics packaging is a structured engineering record of a real packaging application that documents the **material selection, design rationale, conversion process, performance validation, and outcome** of a plastic package.

The five essential components are:
1. **Product identification** — what is being packaged and its market.
2. **Requirement specification** — barrier, mechanical, optical, regulatory targets.
3. **Material and structure selection** — polymer(s) and layer architecture.
4. **Process selection** — conversion technology used.
5. **Outcome and lesson** — validation results, failures, redesign, and sustainability impact.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Active Packaging and Intelligent Packaging. Give one polymer-based example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Active Packaging | Intelligent Packaging |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Interacts with product to extend shelf-life | Communicates condition of product |
| Mechanism | Releases or absorbs substances | Sensor / indicator based |
| Example | Iron-based oxygen scavenger sachet in a multilayer PE/EVOH pouch | Time–Temperature Indicator (TTI) label on a frozen-food pack using a polymer-based diffusion ink |

Polymer example: **Iron-impregnated PE film** (active) and **Photonic-crystal polymer sensor label** (intelligent).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Choice A — Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**"A pharmaceutical company wishes to replace its existing glass ampoule for an injectable drug with a plastic alternative. Prepare a complete Case History covering material selection, barrier performance, regulatory considerations, and validation testing."**

#### Part (a) — Material Selection & Structure [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

1. **Identify the product constraints:**
   - Drug: sterile, aqueous, oxygen- and moisture-sensitive.
   - Container: must be transparent, break-resistant, low leachables.
   - Regulatory: **USP <88>**, **EP 3.2.2**, **FDA 21 CFR 177**, parenteral-grade.

2. **Candidate polymers:**

   | Polymer | Pros | Cons |
   |---|---|---|
   | **COC (Cyclic Olefin Copolymer)** | Glass-like clarity, very low WVTR, USP Class VI | Brittle, costly |
   | **COP (Cyclic Olefin Polymer)** | Excellent moisture barrier, autoclavable | Limited barrier to oxygen |
   | **PP (Polypropylene)** | Tough, autoclavable, cheap | Higher WVTR than COC |
   | **PE** | Tough, flexible | Poor clarity, soft |

3. **Selected structure:** A **COC monolayer** (or COP monolayer) is chosen because the drug is water-based and sensitive primarily to moisture loss and microbial ingress; clarity is essential for visual inspection.

4. **Process selection:** **Injection molding** of COC into a 1 mL ampoule with controlled-tolerance neck, followed by **laser-based tube sealing** to maintain sterility.

**Key markers satisfied:** Material justification (3 marks), comparative table (2 marks), process mapping (2 marks).

#### Part (b) — Barrier, Regulatory, Validation [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

1. **Barrier validation:**
   - COC WVTR at 25 µm ≈ **0.4 g/m²/day** at $38\,^{\circ}\text{C}$/90 % RH.
   - Predicted shelf-life using:

     $$t_{sl} = \dfrac{(M_{crit} - M_{0}) \cdot m_{drug}}{WVTR \cdot A_{container}}$$

     with $M_{crit} = 0.5\,\%$ moisture uptake, $m_{drug} = 1\,\text{g}$, $A_{container} = 6\,\text{cm}^{2} = 0.0006\,\text{m}^{2}$:

     $$t_{sl} = \dfrac{(5 - 0.5)\,\text{mg}}{0.4 \times 0.0006} \approx 18\,750\,\text{days}$$

     More realistically capped by **chemical stability**, not just moisture — the dominant limit becomes API degradation kinetics, reducing practical shelf-life to **24–36 months**.

2. **Regulatory pathway:**
   - **USP <87> Biological Reactivity (in vitro).**
   - **USP <88> Class VI Implantation Test.**
   - **Extractables & Leachables (E&L) profiling** by LC-MS and GC-MS.
   - **BPA-free** declaration; **DEHP-free** declaration.
   - **Annex 1 (EU GMP)** sterile manufacturing compliance.

3. **Validation testing matrix:**
   - Container Closure Integrity (CCI) by dye ingress and Helium leak.
   - **Dye ingress test** sensitivity: $10^{-6}$ mbar·L/s.
   - **Accelerated stability:** $40\,^{\circ}\text{C}$/75 % RH for 6 months → equivalent to 24 months ambient by **Arrhenius factor** of ~4.
   - **Drop test:** 1.5 m onto steel — no fracture, seal intact.
   - **Particulate count:** $< 5$ particles $\ge 10\,\mu\text{m}$ per container (USP <788>).
   - **Sterility test:** USP <71> — no growth in 14 days.

4. **Case-history outcome:** Glass-to-COC substitution yields a **70 % weight reduction**, **elimination of breakage** risk, and **comparable stability** with a validated 24-month shelf-life.

---

### Choice B — Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**"Compare the use of monolayer HDPE and multilayer PET/EVOH/PE stand-up pouches for packaging tomato ketchup. Present this as a Case History with quantitative justification, processing route, and end-of-life assessment."**

#### Part (a) — Comparative Material Analysis & Quantitative Justification [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

1. **Product demand analysis:**
   - Tomato ketchup pH ≈ 3.5–4.0 → acidic.
   - Required shelf-life: 9 months at ambient.
   - Critical failure: browning (Maillard reaction) and vitamin-C loss — both **oxygen-driven**.
   - Required OTR: < 5 cc/m²/day to maintain red color.

2. **Quantitative comparison:**

   | Parameter | Monolayer HDPE (200 µm) | Multilayer PET (12 µm) / EVOH (5 µm) / PE (100 µm) |
   |---|---|---|
   | OTR (cc/m²/day) | 2500 | 1.5 |
   | WVTR (g/m²/day) | 8 | 3 |
   | Predicted shelf-life | 9 days | 1500+ days |
   | Clarity | Translucent | Transparent, glossy |
   | Stiffness (modulus) | 1 GPa | PET layer contributes 3 GPa |
   | Cost per pouch (INR) | 4 | 9 |

3. **Calculation of monolayer failure (worked):**

   $$t_{sl,HDPE} = \dfrac{(5 - 0.5) \cdot V_{headspace}}{OTR \cdot A}$$

   For a 200 g pouch, $V_{headspace} \approx 6\,\text{cm}^{3}$, $A = 250\,\text{cm}^{2} = 0.025\,\text{m}^{2}$:

   $$t_{sl,HDPE} = \dfrac{4.5 \times 6}{2500 \times 0.025} = \dfrac{27}{62.5} \approx 0.43\,\text{days}$$

   With the more realistic OTR of 2500 cc/m²/day, the monolayer is **catastrophically inadequate**.

4. **Pouch architecture decision:** Choose the **PET/EVOH/PE triple-layer** with **adhesive tie layers** (typically maleic-anhydride-grafted PE) on either side of EVOH to prevent delamination at humidity.

**Key markers satisfied:** Comparative table (3 marks), numerical worked example (2 marks), structure decision (2 marks).

#### Part (b) — Processing Route & End-of-Life Assessment [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

1. **Processing route (pouch-making):**
   - **Co-extrusion** of the multilayer flat film using a **feed-block or multi-manifold die** in a blown-film line.
   - **Surface treatment:** Corona discharge on the PET outer face to $42\,\text{dyne/cm}$ for ink adhesion.
   - **Printing:** Rotogravure, 8-color.
   - **Lamination alternative:** If dry-laminating pre-made films, two-component **PU adhesive** at $3\,\text{g/m}^{2}$ coat weight, cured at $40\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ for 48 h.
   - **Pouch forming:** Form-Fill-Seal (FFS) machine — vertical FFS with **fin seal** (longitudinal) and **bottom gusset** for stand-up capability.
   - **Spout insertion (optional):** For cap-friendly convenience, a **PE spout with TPE seal** is ultrasonically welded.

2. **Validation tests:**
   - **Seal strength** > 25 N/15 mm.
   - **Burst test** > 200 kPa.
   - **Drop test** from 1.2 m on 3 faces — no leak.
   - **Oxygen ingress** real-time test over 9 months.

3. **End-of-Life (EoL) assessment:**

   | Pathway | Viability | Comment |
   |---|---|---|
   | Mechanical recycling | Limited | EVOH layer disrupts PET stream |
   | Chemical recycling (hydrolysis) | Excellent | EVOH removed; PET monomer recovery > 90 % |
   | Incineration with energy recovery | Good | Calorific value ~ 25 MJ/kg |
   | Landfill | Worst | Carbon lock-in |

4. **Sustainability case-history lesson:** Despite the **2.25× cost** of multilayer, the **9-month shelf-life** is essential for market viability, and **chemical recycling** is recommended as the EoL pathway to recover PET monomer and incinerate the EVOH/PE fractions for energy.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in Case History questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to quantify** — examiners award full marks only when students provide *numerical justification* (OTR, WVTR, shelf-life). A purely descriptive answer caps at 60–70 % of marks.
> 2. **Ignoring regulatory compliance** — every food/pharma case must reference the relevant standard (EU 10/2011, FDA 21 CFR, FSSAI, USP).
> 3. **Omitting process selection** — a case history that names the polymer but not the conversion process loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **No failure-mode discussion** — a "perfect success" case without at least one redesign iteration is marked down for lacking engineering realism.
> 5. **Misuse of units** — mixing cm² and m² in the same calculation is the single most common arithmetic error; always normalize units in Step 1.
> 6. **No sustainability angle** — under the **KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus**, every case history must conclude with a brief mention of recyclability, biodegradability, or carbon footprint.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Case Histories in Plastics Packaging**
>
> - ✅ A Case History is a **5-stage narrative**: Product → Requirement → Material/Structure → Process → Outcome.
> - ✅ Always quantify barrier requirements with **OTR** and **WVTR** units.
> - ✅ Shelf-life equation: $t_{sl} = \dfrac{(C_{crit} - C_{0}) \cdot V}{OTR \cdot A}$ — derive, do not memorize blindly.
> - ✅ **PET** dominates beverage packaging; **HDPE** dominates household chemicals; **PP** dominates hot-fill and microwave; **PVC** is being phased out of food contact.
> - ✅ **EVOH** is the best oxygen barrier among extrudable polymers; **PVDC** is the best moisture + oxygen co-barrier; **aluminum foil** remains the absolute barrier.
> - ✅ **Migration limit** under EU 10/2011: **OML = 10 mg/dm²**; specific migration limits depend on substance.
> - ✅ **Fick's laws** govern both permeation (gas ingress) and migration (additive loss) — state the correct law for each.
> - ✅ Three pillars of every Case History: **Material**, **Process**, **Validation** — missing any one loses marks.
> - ✅ Real-world iconic cases to remember for KTU viva:
>   - Glass → **PET** transition for carbonated drinks (1970s).
>   - **Tetra Pak** aseptic carton (1960s onwards).
>   - **Stand-up pouch** replacing metal cans for baby food (1990s).
>   - **PVC → PET** in edible-oil bottles (2000s, India).
>   - **BPA phase-out** from polycarbonate baby bottles (2008–2011).
> - ✅ Always end with a **sustainability / end-of-life statement** to satisfy NEP-2020 outcomes.
> - ✅ Use the **A-vs-B-vs-C comparison table** template in any 14-mark case-history question — it is the highest-yield structural device.
> - ✅ Examiner's mnemonic: **"RPMMV"** — *Requirements, Polymer, Migration, Mechanical, Validation*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Major Packaging Plastics — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Major Packaging Plastics** are defined as the industrially significant thermoplastic and thermoset polymers that constitute the bulk of flexible, semi-rigid, and rigid packaging formats worldwide. In the KTU 2024 syllabus, this includes commodity polymers such as **Polyolefins (PE, PP)**, **Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET)**, **Polystyrene (PS)**, **Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)**, and engineering/Barrier polymers like **Ethylene Vinyl Alcohol (EVOH)**, **Polyvinylidene Chloride (PVDC)**, **Polyamides (PA/Nylon)**, and **Ethylene Vinyl Acetate (EVA)**.

These polymers are selected based on a balance of four engineering criteria:
1. **Mechanical Performance** (tensile strength, impact resistance, flexural modulus).
2. **Barrier Properties** (permeability to $\text{O}_2$, $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ vapor, and aroma).
3. **Processability** (MFI, melt temperature, draw-down behavior).
4. **Regulatory & Recyclability Compliance** (FDA, EU 10/2011, FSSAI, IS standards).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Shipping Container" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a packaging plastic as a "specialized shipping container"** for a product. Just like real shipping containers come in different designs — *refrigerated* (for perishables), *insulated* (for chemicals), *open-top* (for machinery) — *polymers* are chemically "tailored" for specific missions. **Polyethylene (PE)** is the *cheap cardboard box* of plastics — flexible, moisture-resistant, everywhere. **PET** is the *clear glass bottle substitute* — strong, transparent, and stiff. **EVOH** is the *vacuum-sealed inner liner* — a barrier that refuses to let oxygen pass. When you stack these "containers" in layers (multilayer films), you get the **functional packaging systems** used in chips, milk, ketchup, and pharmaceuticals.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot

| Category | Polymer Family | Role in Packaging | Density (g/cc) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polyolefins** | LDPE, LLDPE, HDPE, PP | Seals, moisture barrier, structural layer | **0.91 – 0.965** |
| **Polyesters** | PET, PEN | Rigid containers, high-strength films | **1.30 – 1.40** |
| **Styrenics** | PS, HIPS, EPS | Trays, cups, protective foam | **1.04 – 1.07** |
| **Vinyls** | PVC, PVDC, PVOH/EVOH | Cling film, oxygen barrier | **1.16 – 1.40** |
| **Polyamides** | Nylon 6, Nylon 66 | Tough, oxygen-barrier layer | **1.13 – 1.15** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The syllabus demands not just naming the plastics but correlating their **chemical structure → property → packaging application** chain. Memorize the *monomer chemistry* first; the *properties* follow logically.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Polyolefin Family — Chemistry → Property → Application

### 2.1.1 Low-Density Polyethylene (LDPE)
- **Monomer:** Ethylene ($\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2$)
- **Polymerization:** Free-radical, high-pressure process producing a highly **branched** chain.
- **Consequence of branching:** Loose chain packing → lower density (**0.910 – 0.925 g/cc**), high flexibility, excellent transparency, and superior heat-sealability.

### 2.1.2 Linear Low-Density Polyethylene (LLDPE)
- **Polymerization:** Copolymerization of ethylene with α-olefins (1-butene, 1-hexene, 1-octene) using Ziegler–Natta or metallocene catalysts.
- **Result:** Short, uniform short-chain branches on a linear backbone → higher **puncture resistance** and **tensile strength** than LDPE.

### 2.1.3 High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE)
- **Polymerization:** Low-pressure Ziegler–Natta → **linear chains with minimal branching**.
- **Result:** Tight chain packing → density **0.941 – 0.965 g/cc**, high crystallinity, stiff, high tensile strength, excellent moisture barrier.

### 2.1.4 Polypropylene (PP)
- **Monomer:** Propylene ($\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_3$).
- **Tacticity:** Isotactic PP (i-PP) is the packaging-grade form; the methyl group aligns on one side of the chain → high crystallinity.
- **Key property:** Highest melting point of commodity polyolefins ($T_m \approx 165\,^\circ\text{C}$), giving superior **hot-fill** and **microwave** capability.

## 2.2 The Polyester — PET

- **Monomers:** Terephthalic acid + Ethylene glycol.
- **Reaction:** Condensation polymerization with elimination of water.
- **Crystallinity control:** Quenching gives amorphous (clear) PET; orientation + heat-setting gives crystalline (opaque) PET.
- **Packaging role:** Carbonated soft drink bottles (CSD), water bottles, thermoformed trays, biaxially-oriented films (BOPET).

## 2.3 The Barrier Polymers

> [!NOTE]
> **Barrier polymers are characterized by low gas permeability.** They are the unsung heroes that keep chips crispy and milk safe.

- **EVOH (Ethylene Vinyl Alcohol):** Best oxygen barrier of all packaging plastics, but **moisture-sensitive** (loses barrier when wet). Always laminated between two polyolefin layers.
- **PVDC (Polyvinylidene Chloride):** Excellent barrier to **both** $\text{O}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ vapor. Used in Saran™ wrap, multilayer barrier films.
- **PA (Nylon 6, Nylon 6,6):** Tough, good $\text{O}_2$ barrier, poor moisture barrier. Used in vacuum pouches, retort pouches, cheese packaging.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Property Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Equation | Symbol | Typical Range / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Melt Flow Index | $\text{MFI}$ | $0.1 - 50$ g/10 min | Indicates processability — higher MFI = easier flow |
| Permeability Coefficient | $P$ | $\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{mm} / (\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day} \cdot \text{atm})$ | Universal gas permeability metric |
| Water Vapor Transmission Rate | $\text{WVTR}$ | g/m²/day (at 38°C, 90% RH) | Moisture barrier metric |
| Oxygen Transmission Rate | $\text{OTR}$ | cc/m²/day | Oxygen barrier metric |
| Crystallinity (DSC) | $\chi_c$ | $0 - 80\,\%$ | Affects stiffness and barrier |
| Glass Transition Temperature | $T_g$ | °C | Amorphous polymers: $T < T_g$ = glassy |
| Melting Temperature | $T_m$ | °C | Crystalline polymers: $T > T_m$ = melt |
| Density | $\rho$ | g/cc | Separation/recycling metric |

> **Critical Permeability Equation** (used in KTU numerical problems):

$$
P = \frac{q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta p}
$$

Where:
- $q$ = quantity of permeant passing per unit time (cc/day)
- $L$ = thickness of the film (mm)
- $A$ = area of the film ($\text{m}^2$)
- $\Delta p$ = partial pressure difference across the film (atm)

**Real-world engineering utility:** A packaging engineer designing a chip packet must size the EVOH layer thickness using $P$ to ensure $< 5$ cc of $\text{O}_2$ ingress over a **6-month shelf life** at $23\,^\circ\text{C}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Calculating Required EVOH Layer Thickness for Shelf-Life

### Problem Context
A snack food requires an $\text{O}_2$ ingress of **less than 5 cc** over **180 days** at $23\,^\circ\text{C}$ inside a $200\,\text{cm}^2$ pouch with a partial pressure difference $\Delta p = 0.21$ atm. Given EVOH permeability $P_{\text{EVOH}} = 0.05$ cc·mm/(m²·day·atm), calculate the **minimum EVOH layer thickness**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the permeability equation.**

$$
P = \frac{q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta p}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify all knowns.**

- Permeability, $P = 0.05$ cc·mm/(m²·day·atm)
- Quantity, $q = 5$ cc
- Time, $t = 180$ days
- Area, $A = 200\,\text{cm}^2 = 0.02\,\text{m}^2$
- Pressure difference, $\Delta p = 0.21$ atm

**Step 3 — Convert the *rate* by dividing quantity by time.**

$$
\dot{q} = \frac{q}{t} = \frac{5\,\text{cc}}{180\,\text{day}} = 0.02778\,\text{cc/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange the permeability equation to solve for $L$.**

$$
L = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p}{\dot{q}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute values (with unit tracking).**

$$
L = \frac{0.05 \times 0.02 \times 0.21}{0.02778}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute numerator.**

$$
0.05 \times 0.02 = 0.001
$$
$$
0.001 \times 0.21 = 0.00021
$$

**Step 7 — Divide by denominator.**

$$
L = \frac{0.00021}{0.02778} = 0.00756\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 8 — Round up for safety factor and convert to microns.**

$$
L_{\min} \approx 0.008\,\text{mm} = 8\,\mu\text{m}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result:** An EVOH layer of approximately **8 μm** is the engineering minimum. Industry designs typically use **10–15 μm** to incorporate a safety margin and account for pinhole defects.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Polymer Selection Logic

| Property | LDPE | LLDPE | HDPE | PP | PET | PS | PVC | EVOH | PA 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cc) | 0.92 | 0.92 | 0.95 | 0.91 | 1.38 | 1.05 | 1.40 | 1.17 | 1.14 |
| $T_m$ (°C) | 110 | 124 | 130 | 165 | 260 | 240* | 180* | 180 | 225 |
| $T_g$ (°C) | -120 | -120 | -120 | -20 | 75 | 100 | 80 | 55 | 50 |
| OTR (cc/m²/day) | 8000 | 5000 | 3000 | 3000 | 100 | 5000 | 200 | **0.5** | 60 |
| WVTR (g/m²/day) | 18 | 16 | 8 | 10 | 30 | 110 | 30 | 60 (varies) | 250 |
| Tensile (MPa) | 12 | 25 | 30 | 35 | 75 | 40 | 50 | 70 | 80 |
| Transparency | High | Medium | Low | High | Very High | High | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| Typical Use | Squeeze tubes, liners | Stretch film | Bottles, caps | Caps, hot-fill | Bottles, trays | Cups, foam | Cling film | Oxygen barrier | Vacuum pouches |

*$T_g$ for amorphous behavior; PS/PVC soften before melting.

## 3.3 Derivation: Density of a Copolymer (PET / EVOH Blend Approximation)

> **Note:** A common KTU question is to compute the theoretical density of a copolymer using the volume additivity rule.

$$
\frac{1}{\rho_{\text{blend}}} = \frac{w_1}{\rho_1} + \frac{w_2}{\rho_2}
$$

**Example:** 30% EVOH ($\rho_1 = 1.17$) + 70% LDPE ($\rho_2 = 0.92$).

$$
\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{0.30}{1.17} + \frac{0.70}{0.92}
$$
$$
= 0.2564 + 0.7609 = 1.0173
$$
$$
\rho_{\text{blend}} = 0.983\,\text{g/cc}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flowchart — Polymer Selection for Packaging

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Packaging Application] --> B{Is it a moisture barrier needed?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is oxygen barrier also needed?}
    B -- No --> D{Is transparency important?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Multilayer: PE outer + EVOH middle + PE inner]
    C -- No --> F[LDPE / LLDPE / HDPE mono-layer]
    D -- Yes --> G{Heat resistance needed?}
    D -- No --> H[HDPE / PP opaque]
    G -- Yes --> I[PP or PET]
    G -- No --> J[LDPE / LLDPE]
    E --> K[Final Selection: Co-extruded Film]
    F --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    H --> K
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Multilayer Packaging Film Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Outer Layer: PET or BOPP] --> B[Mid Layer: Tie Resin]
    B --> C[Barrier Layer: EVOH or PVDC]
    C --> D[Mid Layer: Tie Resin]
    D --> E[Inner Sealant Layer: LDPE or LLDPE]
    E --> F[Product Contact Surface]
```

> **Functional mapping of the block architecture above:**
> - **Outer Layer (PET/BOPP):** Provides printability, gloss, and mechanical strength.
> - **Tie Resins (e.g., Maleic-Anhydride grafted PE):** Bond dissimilar layers chemically.
> - **Barrier Layer (EVOH):** Blocks $\text{O}_2$ and aroma ingress.
> - **Sealant Layer (LDPE/LLDPE):** Heat-seals at low temperature and protects the barrier from moisture.

## 4.3 Monomer-to-Polymer Schematic (Polyolefin Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Ethylene Monomer CH2=CH2] --> P1[LDPE Branched Chain]
    M2[Propylene CH2=CH-CH3] --> P2[Isotactic PP]
    M3[Ethylene + 1-Hexene] --> P3[LLDPE Short-Branched]
    M4[Ethylene ZN Catalyst] --> P4[HDPE Linear]
    P1 --> App1[Squeeze Tubes, Liners]
    P2 --> App2[Hot-Fill Bottles, Caps]
    P3 --> App3[Stretch Wrap, Pouches]
    P4 --> App4[Milk Bottles, Drums]
```

## 4.4 Barrier Property Comparison Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Select Barrier Function] --> O1{Oxygen Barrier?}
    O1 -- Yes, Dry --> EVOH[EVOH - Best Choice]
    O1 -- Yes, Humid --> PA[PA Nylon or PVDC]
    O1 -- Moderate --> PET[PET or Oriented PP]
    Start --> M1{Moisture Barrier?}
    M1 -- High --> HDPE[HDPE or PP]
    M1 -- Moderate --> LDPE[LDPE or LLDPE]
    M1 -- Low --> PS[PS or PA - Use Outer Layer]
    EVOH --> Lamination[Co-extrude with PE outer/inner]
    PA --> Lamination
    HDPE --> Lamination
    LDPE --> Lamination
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Differentiate between LDPE and HDPE in terms of polymerization process, structure, and packaging application."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- **LDPE** is produced by **free-radical polymerization at high pressure (1000–3000 atm)**, producing a **highly branched** chain. Applications: **squeeze tubes, cling film, general-purpose liners**.
- **HDPE** is produced by **Ziegler–Natta or Phillips catalysts at low pressure**, yielding a **linear, minimally branched** chain with higher crystallinity and density. Applications: **rigid bottles, caps, crates, detergent containers**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Why is EVOH not used as a monolayer in food packaging? How is this limitation overcome?"** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
EVOH is **hygroscopic** — its oxygen-barrier property degrades sharply at high relative humidity (above ~70% RH) because water molecules plasticize the polymer and disrupt hydrogen bonding. It is therefore never used as a monolayer. The limitation is overcome by **sandwiching EVOH between two polyolefin (PE/PP) layers** that act as moisture barriers, keeping the EVOH dry and preserving its barrier performance.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **monomer chemistry, polymerization process, structure–property relationships, and packaging applications of Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET)**. Highlight its suitability for carbonated soft drink bottles. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a **neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical multilayer food packaging film**, identifying each functional layer and giving at least one example polymer for each. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

**1. Monomers & Polymerization:**
- PET is synthesized from **terephthalic acid (PTA)** or its dimethyl ester (DMT) and **ethylene glycol (EG)** via a **two-step condensation polymerization** (esterification followed by polycondensation) with elimination of water/methanol.

$$
\text{HOOC-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-COOH} + \text{HO-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-OH} \longrightarrow [\text{-OOC-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-COO-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-O-}]_n + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**2. Structure:**
- PET has a **linear, aromatic backbone** with the para-substituted benzene ring conferring rigidity and high $T_g$ (~75°C) and $T_m$ (~260°C).

**3. Properties (Structure → Property linkage):**
- The aromatic ring provides **dimensional stability** and **mechanical strength** (tensile ~75 MPa).
- The ester linkage is **polar**, giving moderate **gas barrier** properties (OTR ~100 cc/m²/day).
- The semi-crystalline morphology allows control between **amorphous (transparent)** and **oriented-crystalline (tough)** forms.

**4. Suitability for CSD Bottles:**
- Carbonated beverages contain **2.5–4.5 volumes of $\text{CO}_2$** at internal pressures of 2–5 atm.
- PET's **biaxial orientation (BOPET)** induces strain-hardening that resists creep, allowing the bottle to contain the pressure without creep rupture.
- PET is **transparent** (consumer appeal), **lightweight** (~30 g for a 1 L bottle), **shatter-resistant** (vs. glass), and **recyclable (Resin Code 1)**.
- Good **oxygen barrier** (when oriented) preserves flavor, and **$\text{CO}_2$ retention** is acceptable for 6–12 months.

#### Model Solution — (b)

Refer to **Section 4.2** for the block architecture. A typical 5-layer co-extruded snack-food film contains:

| Layer | Polymer | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Outer | **BOPP / PET (12–20 μm)** | Printability, gloss, puncture resistance |
| 2. Tie | **Maleic-anhydride grafted PE** | Adhesion between dissimilar layers |
| 3. Barrier | **EVOH (8–15 μm)** | Oxygen and aroma barrier |
| 4. Tie | **Maleic-anhydride grafted PE** | Adhesion between barrier and sealant |
| 5. Sealant | **LDPE / LLDPE (40–70 μm)** | Heat-seal, moisture barrier, food contact |

The functional architecture is therefore: **Printable → Adhesive → Barrier → Adhesive → Sealant**. This is the **industry-standard "ABCAS"** design used by Amcor, Uflex, and Sealed Air.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare and contrast **PVDC and EVOH as oxygen-barrier polymers** in food packaging. Discuss their chemical structures, permeability values, moisture sensitivity, and typical laminate constructions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define **permeability coefficient**. A pouch of $250\,\text{cm}^2$ area is constructed with an EVOH barrier layer of $10\,\mu\text{m}$ thickness. If the permeability of EVOH is $0.05$ cc·mm/(m²·day·atm) and the partial-pressure difference of $\text{O}_2$ across the film is $0.20$ atm, calculate the **volume of $\text{O}_2$ that will permeate in 30 days**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

| Criterion | PVDC | EVOH |
|---|---|---|
| Monomer | Vinylidene chloride $\text{CH}_2=\text{CCl}_2$ | Ethylene + Vinyl alcohol |
| Structure | Symmetrical, dense packing, high chain interaction | Amorphous, hydroxyl groups form H-bonds |
| OTR (cc/m²/day) | 10 – 30 | 0.5 – 5 |
| WVTR | Very Low (excellent) | Moderate to High (humidity sensitive) |
| Moisture Sensitivity | Low | High (loses O₂ barrier when wet) |
| Typical Construction | Coating on BOPP/PET films | Co-extruded between PE/PP |
| Recyclability | Difficult (chlorine content) | Easier (no halogens) |

**PVDC** is preferred in **meat, cheese, and MAP** packaging where both $\text{O}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ must be controlled, while **EVOH** is preferred in **dry snack, condiment, and pharma** packaging where moisture is absent or excluded.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Given:**
- $A = 250\,\text{cm}^2 = 0.025\,\text{m}^2$
- $L = 10\,\mu\text{m} = 0.01\,\text{mm}$
- $P = 0.05$ cc·mm/(m²·day·atm)
- $\Delta p = 0.20$ atm
- $t = 30$ days

**Step 1 — Start with the permeability equation:**

$$
P = \frac{q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta p}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for $q$ (total permeation in 30 days):**

$$
q = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p \cdot t}{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute all values:**

$$
q = \frac{0.05 \times 0.025 \times 0.20 \times 30}{0.01}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator:**

$$
0.05 \times 0.025 = 0.00125
$$
$$
0.00125 \times 0.20 = 0.00025
$$
$$
0.00025 \times 30 = 0.0075
$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $L$:**

$$
q = \frac{0.0075}{0.01} = 0.75\,\text{cc}
$$

**Result:** $0.75$ cc of $\text{O}_2$ will permeate in 30 days, which is well within the typical food-quality threshold of $< 5$ cc for a 6-month shelf life.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Students frequently mix up cm and m for area, or μ-m and mm for thickness. Always convert to the units in which the permeability $P$ is expressed. **1 cm² = 0.0001 m²**.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\Delta p$ term:** Oxygen permeation is driven by **partial-pressure difference**, not just contact. Ambient air gives $\Delta p \approx 0.21$ atm (21% oxygen). Vacuum pouches have $\Delta p = 1$ atm.
> 3. **Confusing $\text{O}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$:** EVOH is a *barrier to $\text{O}_2$* but a *poor barrier to $\text{H}_2\text{O}$* — this is the most-failed KTU conceptual question.
> 4. **Ignoring humidity effects on EVOH:** If you mention EVOH in any answer, you **must** mention its moisture sensitivity to score full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Polyolefins (PE family) are the workhorse** of packaging — driven by low cost, easy processing, and excellent moisture barrier.
- **Branching determines properties:** LDPE (branched) = flexible; HDPE (linear) = rigid.
- **PP has the highest $T_m$** among polyolefins — preferred for **hot-fill and microwaveable** applications.
- **PET is the dominant rigid-container plastic** for CSD/water — biaxial orientation is the key to pressure resistance.
- **Barrier polymers (EVOH, PVDC, PA) are never used alone** — they are always laminated or co-extruded with polyolefins.
- **EVOH = best $\text{O}_2$ barrier** but **moisture-sensitive** → protect with PE.
- **PVDC = best combined $\text{O}_2$ + $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ barrier** → meat/cheese films.
- **Nylon (PA) = tough, moderate $\text{O}_2$ barrier**, poor moisture → retort pouches.
- **Permeability Equation $P = \frac{q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta p}$** is the most-tested formula in KTU numericals — memorize and practice unit conversions.
- **Recyclability codes:** PET = 1, HDPE = 2, PVC = 3, LDPE = 4, PP = 5, PS = 6.
- **Multilayer films use tie resins** (e.g., maleic-anhydride grafted PE) to bond dissimilar layers.
- **Crystallinity ↑** → stiffness ↑, transparency ↓, gas barrier ↑.
- **Always quote the structure–property–application triad** in exam answers for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Plastics Processing for Packaging** is the systematic sequence of unit operations and conversion techniques by which raw polymeric resins (in the form of pellets, granules, powders, or liquids) are transformed into finished, functional, and dimensionally stable packaging structures such as films, sheets, bottles, containers, trays, tubes, caps, and laminates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:**
> *Plastics processing bridges the gap between polymer synthesis (Module 1 — Materials) and end-use packaging performance. It is the **manufacturing core** of the entire packaging value chain.*

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme learning framework, processing selection dictates:
1. Final **mechanical properties** of the package.
2. **Barrier characteristics** (O₂, CO₂, moisture vapor).
3. **Aesthetic quality** (clarity, gloss, printability).
4. **Production economics** (cycle time, scrap rate).
5. **Sustainability profile** (recyclability, scrap reuse).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Polymer Kitchen" 🍳**
>
> Think of plastics processing like a **professional bakery**:
> - The **raw polymer resin** is the *flour* (raw ingredient).
> - The **processing machine** is the *oven* (transformation tool).
> - **Heat, pressure, and time** are the *temperature and baking duration*.
> - The **mold / die** is the *cake tin* (gives shape).
> - The **finished package** is the *cake* (final product).
>
> Just as a baker chooses between a convection oven, microwave, or tandoor based on the cake desired, a packaging engineer chooses between **extrusion, blow molding, injection molding, or thermoforming** based on the package geometry and end-use requirement.

### 1.3 Scope of Module 2

The scope typically encompasses the following major conversion families used in the packaging industry worldwide:

| S.No | Processing Family | Typical Packaging Output |
|:----:|:------------------|:-------------------------|
| 1 | **Extrusion** | Films, sheets, pipes, profiles |
| 2 | **Blow Molding** | Bottles, jerry cans, drums |
| 3 | **Injection Molding** | Caps, closures, rigid tubs |
| 4 | **Thermoforming** | Trays, blister packs, cups |
| 5 | **Rotational Molding** | Large hollow containers, tanks |
| 6 | **Calendering** | Thick PVC films/sheets |
| 7 | **Coating & Lamination** | Multi-layer barrier films |
| 8 | **Foam Processing** | Protective expanded packaging |
| 9 | **3D Printing (Additive)** | Prototype/custom luxury packs |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Processing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bolded Standard Constants & Metrics:**
> - **Glass Transition Temperature (T_g):** Typically **70 °C – 130 °C** for amorphous packaging polymers.
> - **Melting Temperature (T_m):** Typically **100 °C – 270 °C** for semi-crystalline packaging polymers.
> - **Melt Flow Index (MFI):** Measured in **g/10 min** under standard load (2.16 kg at 190 °C for PE).
> - **Processing Shear Rate ($\dot{\gamma}$):** Order of **100 – 10,000 s⁻¹** in extrusion.
> - **Mold Shrinkage:** Typically **0.5 % – 3 %** for commodity packaging plastics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Plastics Processing

Every plastics conversion operation — irrespective of whether it is extrusion, molding, or forming — is governed by **four fundamental physical stimuli** applied either individually or in combination. KTU examiners frequently frame 14-mark questions around these "four pillars."

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Pillars:**
> 1. **Heat (Thermal Energy, Q):** To melt or soften the polymer above T_g or T_m.
> 2. **Pressure (Mechanical Force, P):** To push, shape, or pack the molten polymer.
> 3. **Time (Residence Duration, t):** Determines degradation, orientation, and crystallization.
> 4. **Flow (Shear / Elongation, $\dot{\gamma}$):** Drives the polymer through dies and cavities.

### 2.2 Why "Processing Window" is Critical

Every polymer has a **Processing Window** — a narrow band of temperature, shear rate, and pressure where it can be shaped without degradation.

**Visualization of the Processing Window:**

```
          Temperature (°C)
              ▲
              │
   DEGRADATION ZONE (T > T_decomp)   ← ☠ Polymer chars, yellowing, odor
              │────────────────────
              │  PROCESSING WINDOW   ← ✅ Ideal shaping region
              │  (T_m to T_decomp)
              │────────────────────
              │  SOFTENING ZONE     ← 🟡 Insufficient flow, voids
              │  (T_g to T_m)
              │
              └────────────────────►
                   Viscosity (η)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Trap:** Students often confuse **T_g (glass transition)** with **T_m (melting)**. For semi-crystalline polymers like **HDPE, PP, PET**, you have **BOTH** T_g and T_m. Processing is performed **above T_m**, not above T_g. This is a guaranteed 2-mark question every KTU cycle.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:------|
| 1 | $Q = v \cdot A$ | Volumetric throughput (flow rate) | m³/s |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = \rho \cdot v \cdot A$ | Mass throughput | kg/s |
| 3 | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Newton's law of viscosity | Pa |
| 4 | $\eta = \eta_0 \cdot e^{E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius viscosity-temperature relation | Pa·s |
| 5 | $t_{res} = V_{barrel} / Q$ | Mean residence time in barrel | s |
| 6 | $\text{MFI} = \dfrac{m \, (\text{g})}{t \, (10 \, \text{min})}$ | Melt flow index definition | g/10 min |
| 7 | $P = \Delta p \cdot Q$ | Mechanical power consumed by melt | W (watts) |
| 8 | $\dot{\gamma}_{apparent} = \dfrac{32 \, Q}{\pi \, D^3}$ | Apparent wall shear rate (tube flow) | s⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta p \cdot D}{4 \, L}$ | Wall shear stress in pipe | Pa |
| 10 | $E_a$ | Activation energy of viscous flow | J/mol |
| 11 | $\rho_{mold} - \rho_{solid}$ | Free-volume theory baseline (specific volume gap) | m³/kg |
| 12 | $S_{vol} = \dfrac{V_{mold} - V_{part}}{V_{part}} \times 100\%$ | Volumetric mold shrinkage | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The single most-tested equation in Module 2 (based on KTU 2019–2024 paper trends) is the **Newtonian shear stress equation $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$** combined with the **Arrhenius relation for viscosity**. Memorize these.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The processing technology selected for a packaging product determines:
- **Food packaging:** Whether the package can withstand **retort sterilization (121 °C, 15 psi)** — only certain processed PP/EVOH multi-layer bottles qualify.
- **Pharmaceutical blister packs:** Demand **thermoformed PVC/PVDC** with sub-millimeter thickness tolerance.
- **Industrial chemical drums:** Require **blow-molded HDPE** with **ESCR (Environmental Stress Crack Resistance)** > 500 hrs.
- **Cosmetic luxury packs:** Use **injection-molded SAN/PMMA** with optical clarity > 90 % transmission.

### 2.5 Classification of Processes by Stress State

| Stress State | Process Family | KTU Example Question |
|:-------------|:---------------|:---------------------|
| **Shear-dominant flow** | Extrusion, Injection Molding | "Why is extrusion preferred for films?" |
| **Biaxial orientation** | Blown film, BOPP, BOPET | "Compare MD and TD properties" |
| **Free surface flow** | Casting, Coating | "Why is solventless lamination rising?" |
| **Solid-state forming** | Thermoforming, Stretch blow molding | "What is the role of T_g in thermoforming?" |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Volumetric Throughput in Extrusion

We will derive the governing expression for the **mass throughput of a single-screw extruder**, since this is the **most repeated derivation question** in the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption Block:**
> - Polymer melt is treated as an **incompressible Newtonian fluid** (first-order approximation for KTU exams; non-Newtonian corrections are evaluated at higher semesters).
> - Steady-state, isothermal flow.
> - No slip at the barrel wall.
> - Screw channel approximated as a **shallow rectangular channel** of width W and depth H.

**Step 1 — Define the geometric parameters of the screw channel**

- Screw diameter: $D$
- Channel depth: $H$
- Helix angle: $\theta$
- Screw length: $L$
- Channel width: $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta - e$, where $e$ is flight width (neglected for simplicity).

**Step 2 — Resolve the barrel velocity vector into the channel coordinate system**

The barrel moves relative to the stationary screw with a tangential velocity:

$$
v_{bz} = \pi D N
$$

where $N$ is the screw rotational speed in revolutions per second (rev/s). This vector is resolved into:
- **Down-channel component:** $v_bx = \pi D N \cos\theta$
- **Cross-channel component:** $v_bz = \pi D N \sin\theta$

**Step 3 — Express the flow as a superposition of two classical flows**

The total down-channel volumetric flow $Q$ is the sum of two independent flow contributions:

$$
Q = Q_{drag} - Q_{pressure}
$$

- $Q_{drag}$ : **Drag flow** — the pumping action caused by the relative motion of the barrel.
- $Q_{pressure}$ : **Pressure flow** — the back-flow caused by the die-induced pressure gradient.

**Step 4 — Compute the drag flow component $Q_{drag}$**

For a Newtonian fluid in a shallow channel with the upper wall moving at velocity $v_bx$:

$$
Q_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \, v_{bx} \, W \, H
$$

Substituting $v_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\theta$:

$$
Q_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \, \pi D N \cos\theta \, W \, H
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the pressure flow component $Q_{pressure}$**

For pressure-driven flow between parallel plates (Hagen–Poiseuille analogue), the volumetric flow rate per unit width is:

$$
q = - \frac{H^3}{12 \eta} \, \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}
$$

Multiplying by channel width $W$:

$$
Q_{pressure} = \frac{W \, H^3}{12 \eta} \, \left( - \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \right)
$$

Assuming a linear pressure rise along the channel of total length $L / \sin\theta$ (in down-channel direction):

$$
- \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \frac{\Delta p}{L / \sin\theta} = \frac{\Delta p \sin\theta}{L}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Q_{pressure} = \frac{W \, H^3 \, \Delta p \, \sin\theta}{12 \, \eta \, L}
$$

**Step 6 — Combine both contributions into the final throughput expression**

$$
Q = \frac{1}{2} \pi D N \cos\theta \, W \, H \; - \; \frac{W H^3 \Delta p \sin\theta}{12 \eta L}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert volumetric flow to mass throughput**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \, Q = \rho \left[ \frac{1}{2} \pi D N \cos\theta \, W \, H - \frac{W H^3 \Delta p \sin\theta}{12 \eta L} \right]
$$

This is the **canonical KTU extruder equation.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight from the Equation:**
> - To **increase throughput** → increase $N$ (screw speed) or decrease $\Delta p$ (open die).
> - To **increase mixing** → increase $H$ (deeper channel).
> - To **reduce melt temperature rise** → lower $\eta$ by raising barrel temperature (per Arrhenius).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Process Selection Decision Table

| Packaging Requirement | Recommended Process | KTU Justification (Model Answer) |
|:----------------------|:--------------------|:----------------------------------|
| Thin transparent food wrap | **Blown film extrusion** | Biaxial orientation improves tear strength and clarity |
| Rigid PET beverage bottle | **Injection stretch blow molding** | Biaxial stretch enhances barrier and impact strength |
| Yogurt cup (PS) | **Thermoforming** | Low tooling cost, fast cycle for thin-walled cups |
| 20-L water can (HDPE) | **Extrusion blow molding** | Hollow geometry with no internal stress points |
| Pharmaceutical cap (PP) | **Injection molding** | High dimensional accuracy, threaded geometry |
| Multi-layer ketchup sachet | **Co-extrusion** | Combines PET/PE/EVOH in one pass for barrier + seal |
| Foam protective insert (EPS) | **Foam molding (steam chest)** | Expandable polystyrene gives 40× volume expansion |
| Luxury perfume bottle (PETG) | **Injection molding + blow** | Optical clarity + impact strength |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
