## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain any three major plastics processing techniques used in the packaging industry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per technique):**

1. **Extrusion (1 mark):** A continuous process in which thermoplastic polymer melt is forced through a shaped die to produce films, sheets, tubes, or profiles. The screw extruder is the workhorse machine. Widely used for **LDPE/LLDPE food wrap films**.

2. **Blow Molding (1 mark):** A process where a molten polymer parison (tube) is inflated inside a mold cavity using compressed air. Used for **hollow containers** like PET beverage bottles and HDPE jerry cans.

3. **Injection Molding (1 mark):** Molten polymer is injected at high pressure into a closed, cold mold cavity where it solidifies into a rigid part. Used for **caps, closures, and thin-walled containers** requiring high dimensional precision.

4. *(Alternative valid third: Thermoforming — sheet heated and vacuum-formed into trays; Rotational molding — for large tanks; Calendering — for thick PVC films.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a "Processing Window" in polymer processing? Why is it critical for packaging applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** A *processing window* is the range of operating parameters — primarily **temperature, pressure, and shear rate** — within which a polymer can be melted, shaped, and solidified **without undergoing thermal degradation, oxidative degradation, or poor part quality.**

- **Criticality for packaging (1.5 marks):**
  1. Ensures the polymer retains its **food-contact safety** (no toxic degradation products).
  2. Maintains **optical clarity** (no haze from overheated melt).
  3. Preserves **mechanical strength** (no chain scission from overheating).
  4. Guarantees **barrier properties** (intact amorphous/crystalline morphology).

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **single-screw extrusion process** for plastic film production. List the functional zones of the screw and explain the role of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **expression for volumetric throughput** of a single-screw extruder in terms of screw geometry, screw speed, melt viscosity, and die pressure. Discuss **two practical methods to increase the throughput**.

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — Single-Screw Extruder for Film Production**

**Block Diagram of a Single-Screw Extruder Line:**

```
   [Hopper] → [Feed Throat] → [Feed Zone] → [Compression Zone] → [Metering Zone] → [Breaker Plate + Screen Pack] → [Die (Film/Slot)] → [Air Ring/Cooling] → [Film Take-up]
```

**Functional Zones of the Screw (2 marks for zones, 5 marks for explanation):**

1. **Feed Zone (Solids Conveying Zone):**
   - Channel depth: **constant and deep** (typically 0.1 D – 0.15 D).
   - Function: **Transport the solid polymer pellets** from the hopper into the heated barrel. The pellets are pre-heated here.
   - Mechanism: *Friction drag* between pellets and barrel wall (frictional coefficient is critical).

2. **Compression / Transition Zone (Melting Zone):**
   - Channel depth **decreases progressively** from $H_f$ to $H_m$ (typically 0.1 D → 0.04 D).
   - Function: **Melt the polymer** by combining external barrel heat and viscous dissipation (shear heating).
   - Mechanism: A thin **melt film** forms at the barrel wall; the **melt pool** accumulates at the pushing flight; the **solid bed** gradually shrinks (Tadmor melting model).

3. **Metering Zone (Melt Conveying / Pumping Zone):**
   - Channel depth: **constant and shallow** (typically 0.04 D).
   - Function: **Generate a homogeneous, pressurized melt** to be delivered to the die at a **steady rate**.
   - Mechanism: Acts as a **positive displacement pump** (drag flow + pressure flow).

> **Additional Components (1 mark):**
> - **Breaker Plate and Screen Pack:** Remove contaminants, create back-pressure, and straighten flow lines (eliminate "Melt Pulse" or surging).
> - **Die:** Slit die for film, sheet die for sheets, annular die for tubes.
> - **Cooling & Take-up:** Air ring, chill rolls, and nip rolls for film.

**(b) [7 Marks] — Derivation of Volumetric Throughput**

The **final derived equation** is:

$$
Q = \frac{1}{2} \pi D N \cos\theta \, W \, H \; - \; \frac{W H^3 \Delta p \sin\theta}{12 \eta L}
$$

**Two Practical Methods to Increase Throughput (2 marks):**

1. **Increase Screw Speed (N):** Doubling $N$ doubles the **drag-flow** term (since $Q_{drag} \propto N$). Practical limit: Screw speeds above **150–200 rpm** cause **melt fracture** and excessive shear heating.
2. **Reduce Die Pressure Drop ($\Delta p$):** Use a die with **larger land length reduction** or wider flow channels. This reduces the **back-flow (pressure) term**, increasing net $Q$. Practical limit: Insufficient $\Delta p$ causes poor mixing and surging.
3. *(Valid alternative: Increase barrel temperature to reduce $\eta$ — the melt viscosity drops exponentially per Arrhenius, increasing the pressure-driven flow contribution.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students **forget to subtract the pressure flow term** and write only $Q = \frac{1}{2} v_{bx} W H$. This loses **2 marks**.
> - Do **not confuse $W$ (channel width)** with $D$ (diameter) in the final expression.
> - The helix angle $\theta$ appears in **both terms** — do not drop it.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the major **plastics processing techniques** used in the packaging industry. Compare **extrusion, blow molding, and injection molding** with respect to (i) product geometry, (ii) tooling cost, (iii) cycle time, and (iv) typical packaging applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **Melt Flow Index (MFI)**. A polypropylene sample has an MFI of **12 g/10 min** at 2.16 kg load. If the same resin is tested at 5 kg load under identical temperature, predict the qualitative change in MFI. Justify your answer using the **Arrhenius viscosity relation** and the **Newtonian shear stress** definition.

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — Classification & Comparison**

**Classification of Plastics Processing Techniques (3 marks):**

The major processing techniques are classified based on the **dominant stress state** and **melt handling principle**:

- **Continuous processes:** Extrusion (film, sheet, pipe), Calendering.
- **Discontinuous (cyclic) processes:** Injection molding, Blow molding, Thermoforming, Rotational molding.
- **Foaming processes:** Expanded polystyrene (EPS) molding, structural foam.
- **Additive processes:** 3D printing (FDM, SLA) — emerging in luxury/prototype packaging.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Extrusion | Blow Molding | Injection Molding |
|:----------|:----------|:-------------|:------------------|
| **Product geometry** | Continuous 2D (film/sheet) or constant cross-section | Hollow 3D (bottles, cans) | Solid 3D (caps, tubs) |
| **Tooling cost** | Low (die only) | Medium (mold halves) | High (complex mold with cooling) |
| **Cycle time** | Continuous (no cycle) | 10 – 60 s | 5 – 30 s |
| **Typical application** | LDPE food wrap, PP woven sacks | PET bottles, HDPE jerry cans | PP bottle caps, PS yogurt tubs |

**(b) [7 Marks] — Melt Flow Index & Arrhenius Justification**

**MFI Definition (2 marks):**
The **Melt Flow Index (MFI)**, also called **Melt Mass Flow Rate (MFR)**, is defined as the **mass of polymer (in grams) extruded in 10 minutes** through a standard capillary die (diameter 2.095 mm, length 8 mm) under a specified temperature and load (per ASTM D1238 / ISO 1133).

$$
\text{MFI} = \frac{m \, (\text{grams})}{t \, (10 \, \text{minutes})}
$$

**Quantitative Effect of Increasing Load (3 marks):**

Increasing the load from **2.16 kg to 5 kg** increases the **shear stress** applied to the melt:

- Shear stress at the capillary wall: $\tau_w = \dfrac{F}{A}$, where $F$ is the load and $A$ is the piston area.
- Therefore, **shear stress increases proportionally** (≈ 2.31× since $5/2.16 \approx 2.31$).
- Shear rate also increases: $\dot{\gamma}_{apparent} = \dfrac{32 Q}{\pi D^3}$.
- Since the polymer is **pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**, viscosity $\eta$ decreases as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases.
- Mass flow $Q$ (and hence MFI) **increases non-linearly** with applied load.

**Qualitative Prediction (1 mark):**
**MFI will increase** at 5 kg load (typically to **25 – 35 g/10 min** for the same PP resin), but **not in a simple linear ratio** because of the shear-thinning behavior.

**Arrhenius Justification (1 mark):**
The Arrhenius equation $\eta = \eta_0 \exp(E_a / RT)$ shows that **viscosity decreases exponentially** with temperature. While the load test here is at the same temperature, the higher load increases the **effective shear rate**, which — for a pseudoplastic polymer — effectively **lowers the apparent viscosity** in the same way an Arrhenius-type thermal activation does at the molecular level (more chain disentanglement under higher force).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> - Students often write *"MFI is inversely proportional to viscosity"* — this is **incomplete**. MFI is a **mass flow rate**, not a reciprocal of viscosity. The relationship is governed by the **Poiseuille equation for capillary flow** combined with the non-Newtonian rheology of the polymer.
> - Do not write that MFI "is the viscosity" — it is a **rheological index** (an indicator), not a viscosity measurement in Pa·s.
> - For **PP at 5 kg load**, ensure you note that PP is **pseudoplastic** — the increase in MFI is **greater than 2.31×** because of shear thinning.

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **Module 2 = Plastics Processing for Packaging.** It is the bridge between materials (Module 1) and end-use performance.
- 🔑 The **Four Pillars** of all processing: **Heat, Pressure, Time, Shear Rate.**
- 🔑 **T_g vs T_m:** Processing is done **above T_m** (semi-crystalline) or above **T_g + 100 °C** (amorphous). Never confuse them.
- 🔑 **Single-screw extruder** has three functional zones: **Feed → Compression → Metering.**
- 🔑 The **canonical extruder throughput equation** has two terms: **Drag flow** (pumping) and **Pressure flow** (back-flow).
- 🔑 **MFI** is measured in **g/10 min** at a standard load (2.16 kg for PE/PP). It is a **rheological indicator**, not a direct viscosity.
- 🔑 **Polymer melts are non-Newtonian** (pseudoplastic) — viscosity drops with increasing shear rate (shear-thinning).
- 🔑 **Activation energy $E_a$** in the Arrhenius equation determines how **sensitive** viscosity is to temperature changes — critical for process stability.
- 🔑 The **processing window** must be respected to avoid **thermal degradation, oxidative degradation, and loss of food-contact safety**.
- 🔑 Always **draw a block diagram** in 7-mark and 14-mark questions — KTU examiners award **1–2 marks** specifically for neat, labelled diagrams.
- 🔑 For 14-mark derivations: **state assumptions → define variables → write the differential equation → integrate with boundary conditions → substitute physical constants → final boxed equation.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polyesters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Polyesters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Polyesters** are a broad class of condensation (step-growth) polymers whose constitutional repeating units are joined together through **ester functional groups (–C(O)O–)** formed by the reaction of carboxylic acid (–COOH) groups with hydroxyl (–OH) groups, with the elimination of a small molecule (most commonly **water, H₂O**, or methanol, CH₃OH).

In the context of **Plastics Packaging Technology (PEPET748)**, polyesters represent one of the most commercially significant families of packaging polymers because they uniquely combine:
- High mechanical strength and dimensional stability
- Excellent gas barrier properties (especially to CO₂ and O₂)
- Transparency and gloss
- Thermal stability for hot-fill and retort applications
- Recyclability (Resin Identification Code **1** for PET)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination Ready):** *"Polyesters are step-growth polymers formed by the condensation reaction of diols with dicarboxylic acids (or their derivatives), yielding repeating –[–O–CO–R–CO–O–R′–]ₙ– linkages, with by-product elimination of water or methanol."*

### 1.2 Key Polyesters Relevant to Packaging

| Resin | Abbreviation | Primary Packaging Use |
|---|---|---|
| Poly(ethylene terephthalate) | **PET** | Bottles, films, trays |
| Poly(butylene terephthalate) | **PBT** | Engineering trays (limited) |
| Poly(ethylene naphthalate) | **PEN** | Hot-fill bottles, beer kegs |
| Poly(lactic acid) | **PLA** | Biodegradable films/cups |
| Poly(hydroxybutyrate) | **PHB** | Compostable packaging |
| Poly(ε-caprolactone) | **PCL** | Compostable films |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🧩 The LEGO Train Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)**
> Imagine building a long train using two kinds of LEGO bricks:
> - **"Acid bricks"** (with two studs on top representing –COOH groups)
> - **"Alcohol bricks"** (with two holes on top representing –OH groups)
>
> When you snap an acid brick to an alcohol brick, they fuse and **release a tiny water droplet** (the by-product). Now imagine doing this thousands of times in a row — alternating acid-alcohol-acid-alcohol — and you get a **long chain called a polyester**. If you use the *exact same number* of acid and alcohol bricks, the chain becomes very long. If one type runs out first, the chain stops growing. This is why **stoichiometric balance** is critical in polyester synthesis — exactly as covered in step-growth kinetics.

### 1.4 Critical Standard Metrics (Memorize)

- **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg)** of PET ≈ **69–80 °C** (amorphous), **75–85 °C** (semi-crystalline)
- **Melting Temperature (Tm)** of PET ≈ **245–265 °C**
- **Density** of amorphous PET ≈ **1.335 g/cm³**; crystalline PET ≈ **1.455 g/cm³**
- **Intrinsic Viscosity (IV)** of bottle-grade PET ≈ **0.72–0.85 dL/g**
- **Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR)** of PET film ≈ **3–6 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹** at 25 °C
- **Resin Identification Code**: **1 (PETE)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Polyesters

**A. Based on Chemical Structure:**
- **Saturated (Aliphatic) Polyesters** — e.g., PLA, PHB, PBS — biodegradable, flexible, lower Tm
- **Aromatic Polyesters** — e.g., PET, PEN — rigid, high strength, high Tm
- **Unsaturated Polyesters** — used in thermoset reinforced packaging composites

**B. Based on Synthesis Route:**
- **Polycondensation** — diacid + diol → polyester + H₂O (or diester + diol → polyester + ROH)
- **Ring-Opening Polymerization (ROP)** — lactone/lactide rings open to give PLA, PCL, PHB
- **Acidolysis / Aminolysis** — secondary recycling routes

### 2.2 Synthesis Mechanism (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Route 1: Direct Esterification of Terephthalic Acid (TPA) + Ethylene Glycol (EG)**

Step 1: Protonation of carbonyl oxygen of –COOH
Step 2: Nucleophilic attack by –OH oxygen of EG
Step 3: Tetrahedral intermediate collapses → ester linkage + water release
Step 4: Repeat → polymer chain propagation

**Route 2: Transesterification (Methanolysis / Glycolysis)**

Dimethyl terephthalate (DMT) + Ethylene Glycol (EG) → bis(hydroxyethyl) terephthalate (BHET) + 2 CH₃OH
BHET + BHET → PET + EG (polycondensation under vacuum at **~280 °C**)

**Route 3: Ring-Opening Polymerization of Lactide (for PLA)**

Lactide cyclic dimer → under Sn(Oct)₂ catalyst at **150–180 °C** → linear PLA + ring opening

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations below are HIGH-FREQUENCY in KTU Module 2 (Polyesters) and Module 3 (Polymerization Kinetics) questions.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\bar{X}_{n} = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | Carothers equation (stoichiometric balance, equal diol & diacid) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\bar{X}_{n} = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | Carothers equation with non-stoichiometric ratio $r$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | $r = \dfrac{N_{A}}{N_{B}}$ | Stoichiometric ratio (minority/majority functional groups) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\bar{M}_{n} = M_{0} \cdot \bar{X}_{n}$ | Number-average molecular weight | g/mol |
| 5 | $\dfrac{d[\text{COOH}]}{dt} = -k[\text{COOH}][\text{OH}]$ | Third-order (overall) rate law; second-order in [OH] excluded | L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{a}}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius temperature dependence | L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹ |
| 7 | $\text{IV} = \lim_{c \to 0}\!\left(\dfrac{\eta_{\text{sp}}}{c}\right)$ | Intrinsic viscosity (Huggins definition) | dL/g |
| 8 | $[\eta] = K \cdot M^{a}$ | Mark-Houwink-Sakurada equation | dL/g |
| 9 | $\text{OTR} = \dfrac{V \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta p \cdot t}$ | Oxygen Transmission Rate (permeation) | cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹ |
| 10 | $T_{g} = T_{g,0} + \dfrac{K}{M_{n}}$ | Fox-Flory equation (Tg vs molecular weight) | K or °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE for KTU board exams:** Use $p$ for *extent of reaction* (fraction of functional groups reacted) and $r$ for *stoichiometric imbalance ratio*. Do **not** interchange these in derivations — examiners deduct marks for confusion.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PET beverage bottles:** Coca-Cola, water, edible oil — leverages PET's CO₂ barrier and clarity
- **BoPET films (biaxially-oriented PET):** Mylar® — used in retort pouches, microwave susceptors
- **CPET trays:** Crystallized PET for oven-ready frozen-food trays
- **PLA bioplastics:** Compostable cups, clamshells for fresh produce
- **PEN hot-fill bottles:** Preserves, requiring 85–95 °C filling temperature

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Carothers Equation (Stoichiometric Case)

**Problem Setup:** A diacid HOOC–R–COOH reacts with a diol HO–R′–OH in equimolar amounts. We want to find how the **degree of polymerization $\bar{X}_n$** depends on the **extent of reaction $p$**.

**Step 1 — Define Initial Functional Groups:**
Let the initial number of acid groups = initial number of hydroxyl groups = $N_{0}$.

**Step 2 — Number of Unreacted Functional Groups:**
At any time, the fraction of groups that have **reacted** = $p$.
Therefore, the fraction **unreacted** = $(1 - p)$.
Number of unreacted groups remaining = $N_{0}(1 - p)$.

**Step 3 — Number of Polymer Molecules:**
Each condensation event consumes **2 functional groups** and produces **1 molecule of polymer** (plus 1 small molecule like H₂O).
So the number of polymer molecules formed:

$$
N = \dfrac{N_{0} - N_{0}(1-p)}{2} = \dfrac{N_{0} \cdot p}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Degree of Polymerization:**
The degree of polymerization $\bar{X}_n$ = (initial monomers) / (final polymer molecules):

$$
\bar{X}_{n} = \dfrac{N_{0}}{N} = \dfrac{N_{0}}{\dfrac{N_{0} \cdot p}{2}} = \dfrac{2}{2p}
$$

This simplifies to the **elementary Carothers result**. But this counts the entire *backbone units*. For step-growth with **bifunctional** monomers, the more useful form divides by 2:

$$
\bar{X}_{n} = \dfrac{1}{1-p}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Verification (KTU-Style):**
If $p = 0.99$ (99 % conversion), then:

$$
\bar{X}_{n} = \dfrac{1}{1-0.99} = \dfrac{1}{0.01} = 100
$$

If $p = 0.999$:

$$
\bar{X}_{n} = \dfrac{1}{0.001} = 1000
$$

This proves the **famous Step-Growth Polymerization Principle**: *To achieve high molecular weight, conversion must exceed 99 %.*

### 3.2 Derivation of Carothers Equation with Non-Stoichiometric Imbalance

**Setup:** Let $N_A$ = moles of A groups, $N_B$ = moles of B groups, with $N_A < N_B$.
Define stoichiometric ratio $r = N_A / N_B$, so $r < 1$.
Extent of reaction of A groups = $p$.

**Step 1 — Unreacted Groups:**
Unreacted A = $N_A(1-p)$
Unreacted B = $N_B - N_A p$ (because B groups react in proportion to A groups)

**Step 2 — Total Unreacted Groups:**

$$
N_{\text{total, unreacted}} = N_A(1-p) + (N_B - N_A p)
$$

**Step 3 — Number of Polymer Molecules:**
Total molecules = (unreacted groups)/2:

$$
N = \dfrac{N_A(1-p) + (N_B - N_A p)}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Degree of Polymerization:**

$$
\bar{X}_{n} = \dfrac{(N_A + N_B)/2}{N} = \dfrac{N_A + N_B}{N_A(1-p) + N_B - N_A p}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $r = N_A / N_B$:**

$$
\bar{X}_{n} = \dfrac{1 + r}{(1-p) + \dfrac{1}{r} - p} = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}
$$

This is the **general Carothers equation**, valid for any stoichiometric imbalance.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Polyester Types for Packaging

| Property | PET (Aromatic) | PLA (Aliphatic) | PHB (Aliphatic) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_g$ (°C) | 70–80 | 55–60 | 5–10 |
| $T_m$ (°C) | 245–265 | 150–170 | 170–180 |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 55–75 | 50–70 | 25–40 |
| OTR (cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹) | 3–6 | 25–40 | ~15 |
| Biodegradability | No | Yes (industrial) | Yes (soil/marine) |
| Recyclability | Excellent (RIC 1) | Limited (RIC 7) | Limited |
| Heat Resistance | Excellent | Poor (distorts ≥ 60 °C) | Moderate |

### 3.4 Limiting Viscosity Number (Intrinsic Viscosity) Derivation Outline

The specific viscosity $\eta_{\text{sp}} = (\eta - \eta_0)/\eta_0$.
Reduced viscosity $\eta_{\text{red}} = \eta_{\text{sp}}/c$.
Intrinsic viscosity is the limit as $c \to 0$:

$$
[\eta] = \lim_{c \to 0} \eta_{\text{red}}
$$

For PET in phenol/tetrachloroethane at 25 °C, Mark-Houwink constants are $K \approx 7.44 \times 10^{-4}$ dL/g and $a \approx 0.648$, giving the practical relationship:

$$
[\eta] = 7.44 \times 10^{-4} \cdot M^{0.648}
$$

Solving for $M_n$ from a measured IV of 0.80 dL/g gives $M_n \approx 24{,}000$ g/mol, characteristic of bottle-grade PET.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Monomer-to-Polymer Reaction Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Terephthalic Acid TPA"]:::mono
    nodeB["Ethylene Glycol EG"]:::mono
    nodeC["Bis Hydroxyethyl Terephthalate BHET"]:::inter
    nodeD["Low Molecular Weight PET Prepolymer"]:::inter
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style, Module 2]**

**Q1. [3 Marks]**
*Define polyesters. Write any two examples of polyesters used in packaging applications and state their typical glass transition temperatures.*

**Model Answer:**
Polyesters are condensation polymers containing repeating ester linkages (–COO–) in the main chain, formed by the reaction of diols with dicarboxylic acids (or their reactive derivatives), with the elimination of water or methanol as by-products.

**Examples:**
- **Poly(ethylene terephthalate) — PET:** $T_g \approx 70$–$80$ °C — used in beverage bottles, films
- **Poly(lactic acid) — PLA:** $T_g \approx 55$–$60$ °C — used in biodegradable cups and clamshells

---

**Q2. [3 Marks]**
*State the Carothers equation for step-growth polymerization. What is the degree of polymerization when the extent of reaction is 0.98?*

**Model Answer:**
For equimolar bifunctional monomers:
$$
\bar{X}_{n} = \dfrac{1}{1-p}
$$
At $p = 0.98$:
$$
\bar{X}_{n} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.98} = \dfrac{1}{0.02} = 50
$$

---

### 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Module 2 Polyesters:**
> - Students frequently **confuse $p$ (extent of reaction) with $r$ (stoichiometric ratio)**. Mark deduction: **2 marks**.
> - Many forget to **subtract methanol/water by-products** when computing stoichiometric balance. Mark deduction: **1 mark**.
> - Failing to **state units** of molecular weight, IV, and OTR loses **0.5–1 mark** per instance.
> - In mechanism questions, students often **skip the by-product elimination step** — examiners expect explicit H₂O or CH₃OH written out.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Synthesis & Kinetics**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **two industrial routes** for the synthesis of PET from (i) terephthalic acid (TPA) + ethylene glycol (EG), and (ii) dimethyl terephthalate (DMT) + EG. Write balanced equations and state the catalyst used in each route.

**Model Answer:**

**Route (i): Direct Esterification of TPA + EG**

$$
\text{HOOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COOH} + 2\,\text{HO–CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{–OH} \longrightarrow \text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

- **Catalyst:** Typically none (autocatalyzed by TPA itself), or Sb₂O₃ in the polycondensation stage
- **Temperature:** $240$–$260$ °C
- **By-product:** Water (removed continuously)

The intermediate BHET is then polycondensed at **~280 °C under vacuum (< 1 mmHg)** using **Sb₂O₃ catalyst** to give high-molecular-weight PET.

**Route (ii): Transesterification of DMT + EG**

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{OOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COOCH}_3 + 2\,\text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{BHET} + 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH}
$$

- **Catalyst:** Zinc acetate or manganese acetate at **180–200 °C**
- **By-product:** Methanol (distilled off)
- Followed by polycondensation identical to Route (i)

**Comparison:**

| Feature | TPA Route | DMT Route |
|---|---|---|
| By-product | H₂O | CH₃OH |
| Purity of monomer | Lower | Higher (volatile) |
| Energy demand | Higher | Lower |
| Modern preference | **Yes (more common today)** | Legacy |

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Carothers equation** for the number-average degree of polymerization $\bar{X}_n$ in terms of the extent of reaction $p$ for an **equimolar** bifunctional step-growth system. Hence compute the molecular weight $M_n$ of PET when $p = 0.995$, given the repeat-unit molecular weight $M_0 = 192$ g/mol.

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:** *(See Section 3.1 for full derivation.)*
Let $N_0$ = initial moles of each functional group. After extent of reaction $p$, unreacted groups = $N_0(1-p)$. Number of polymer molecules formed = $N_0 p / 2$. Therefore:

$$
\bar{X}_{n} = \dfrac{N_{0}}{N_0 p/2} = \dfrac{1}{1-p}
$$

**Numerical Calculation:**
At $p = 0.995$:

$$
\bar{X}_{n} = \dfrac{1}{1-0.995} = \dfrac{1}{0.005} = 200
$$

$$
M_n = \bar{X}_n \cdot M_0 = 200 \times 192 = 38{,}400 \;\text{g/mol}
$$

This is in the range of **fiber-grade PET**, demonstrating that even 99.5 % conversion yields modest molecular weights — a hallmark of step-growth polymerization.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Structure-Property & Packaging Performance**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **structure, properties, and packaging applications** of PET, PLA, and PHB. Highlight the role of aromatic vs. aliphatic backbones in determining $T_g$, $T_m$, and biodegradability.

**Model Answer:**

| Property | PET (Aromatic) | PLA (Aliphatic) | PHB (Aliphatic) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Repeat Unit | –[OCH₂CH₂O–CO–C₆H₄–CO]– | –[OCH(CH₃)CO]– | –[OCH(CH₃)CH₂CO]– |
| Backbone | Aromatic + aliphatic | Purely aliphatic | Purely aliphatic |
| $T_g$ | ~75 °C | ~58 °C | ~5 °C |
| $T_m$ | ~255 °C | ~170 °C | ~175 °C |
| Crystallinity | Slow; needs nucleation | Slow | High |
| Biodegradation | No | Yes (industrial compost) | Yes (soil + marine) |
| CO₂ barrier | Excellent | Poor | Moderate |
| Packaging Use | Bottles, BoPET films | Cups, fresh-produce trays | Compostable films |

**Role of Aromaticity:**
- **PET's aromatic terephthalate ring** restricts chain mobility → high $T_g$ and $T_m$, excellent mechanical strength, but **non-biodegradable**.
- **PLA & PHB** have **flexible aliphatic chains** → lower $T_g$, susceptible to hydrolysis at the ester bond → **biodegradable** but with inferior heat resistance.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A PET bottle-grade resin has an intrinsic viscosity $[\eta] = 0.80$ dL/g. Using the Mark–Houwink equation $[\eta] = K M^{a}$ with $K = 7.44 \times 10^{-4}$ dL/g and $a = 0.648$, calculate the viscosity-average molecular weight $M_v$. Comment on whether this resin is suitable for **stretch-blow molding of carbonated beverage bottles**.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Substitute into Mark–Houwink:**

$$
0.80 = 7.44 \times 10^{-4} \cdot M^{0.648}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $M$:**

$$
M^{0.648} = \dfrac{0.80}{7.44 \times 10^{-4}} = 1075.27
$$

$$
M = (1075.27)^{1/0.648}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the exponent $1/0.648 \approx 1.5432$:**

$$
M = 1075.27^{1.5432}
$$

**Step 4 — Take logarithms:**

$$
\ln M = 1.5432 \times \ln(1075.27) = 1.5432 \times 6.9803 = 10.774
$$

$$
M = e^{10.774} \approx 47{,}500 \;\text{g/mol}
$$

**Step 5 — Suitability Assessment:**

| IV (dL/g) | Typical Application |
|---|---|
| 0.50–0.65 | Fibers, strapping |
| 0.65–0.78 | Films, sheets |
| **0.72–0.85** | **Bottles (stretch-blow molding)** ✅ |
| 0.85–1.05 | Engineering plastics |

Since $[\eta] = 0.80$ dL/g falls squarely in the **0.72–0.85 dL/g range**, this resin is **suitable for stretch-blow molding of carbonated beverage bottles**. The molecular weight (~47,500 g/mol) provides sufficient melt strength for preform stretching while maintaining clarity and impact resistance.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — Module 2: Polyesters**

- **Polyesters = step-growth (condensation) polymers with –COO– linkages;** by-product is **H₂O (TPA route)** or **CH₃OH (DMT route)**
- **PET is the workhorse packaging polyester** — Resin Identification Code **1**; $T_g \approx 75$ °C; $T_m \approx 255$ °C
- **Carothers equation (stoichiometric):** $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$
- **Carothers equation (general):** $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$
- **Step-growth golden rule:** to achieve $\bar{X}_n > 100$, conversion must exceed **99 %** ($p \geq 0.99$)
- **Mark–Houwink relation:** $[\eta] = K M^a$ — used to compute $M_v$ from IV measurements
- **Fox–Flory:** $T_g = T_{g,0} + K/M_n$ — $T_g$ rises with $M_n$ and saturates
- **PET synthesis:** BHET intermediate; Sb₂O₃ catalyst in polycondensation; **vacuum** mandatory
- **Aromatic polyesters** (PET, PEN) → **non-biodegradable, high strength, high Tg**
- **Aliphatic polyesters** (PLA, PHB, PBS, PCL) → **biodegradable, low Tg, poor heat resistance**
- **Stretch-blow molding** of PET requires IV in the **0.72–0.85 dL/g** window
- **BoPET** = biaxially oriented PET film (e.g., Mylar®) — used in retort pouches and laminates
- **PEN** is preferred over PET for **hot-fill (> 85 °C)** and **beer kegs** due to superior gas barrier
- **PLA** is compostable only in **industrial composting facilities** (≥ 58 °C) — not in home compost
- **Tg of PET matters for:** hot-fill, microwave, retort sterilization limits in packaging design

---

*End of Module 2 — Polyesters Notes*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PVDC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PVDC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Polyvinylidene Chloride (PVDC)** is a thermoplastic polymer derived from the homopolymerisation of the monomer **vinylidene chloride (VDC)**, chemically designated as **1,1-dichloroethylene** with the repeating structural unit –(CH₂–CCl₂)–. Because the homopolymer of VDC is thermally unstable and difficult to process, commercial PVDC is almost always a **statistical copolymer** in which VDC is co-reacted with co-monomers such as **vinyl chloride (VCM)**, **methyl acrylate**, **acrylonitrile**, or **methacrylonitrile**, typically in the ratio range of **80–95 % VDC : 5–20 % co-monomer**.

The most commercially significant PVDC copolymer is the VDC–VC copolymer, registered under the trade names **Saran™ (Dow / Asahi-Kasei)**, **Diofan™ (Solvay)**, and **Ixan™ (Solvay)**. The polymer belongs to the family of **high-barrier thermoplastics** and is characterised by exceptionally low permeability to oxygen, water vapour, aromas, and oils — a property attributed to its dense molecular packing, high degree of crystallinity (40–60 %), and strong dipole–dipole interactions between adjacent C–Cl dipoles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** PVDC is the **benchmark reference material** against which all other barrier plastics (EVOH, PA, PEN) are evaluated in KTU Module 2. A clear understanding of *why* PVDC is such a strong barrier is essential for comparison questions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Molecular Bristle-Brush"

Imagine a flat sheet of cling-film on a kitchen counter. Tiny molecules of oxygen and water vapour try to squeeze through it, like raindrops falling on an umbrella. Now picture the inside of PVDC not as a smooth surface, but as a **dense forest of chlorine "bristles"**, with chlorine atoms arranged so tightly on every carbon backbone that gas molecules simply cannot find a tunnel wide enough to pass through. This is the heart of PVDC's barrier action: **each C–Cl bond is a polar, bulky "boulder"** that physically and electrostatically blocks the diffusing penetrant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Hook for Exams:** *"PVDC is the only commodity polymer that is simultaneously an excellent oxygen barrier AND an excellent water-vapour barrier."* — Most other barrier polymers excel in only one of these two metrics (e.g., EVOH is great for O₂ but poor for H₂O; PA6 is good for O₂ only when dry).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant Numbers)

| Property | Symbol / Unit | Typical Value for PVDC (Copolymer) |
|---|---|---|
| Density | ρ | **1.60 – 1.75 g/cm³** |
| Glass Transition Temperature | Tg | **−15 °C to +2 °C** |
| Melting Point | Tm | **160 – 175 °C** |
| Percent Crystallinity | χc | **40 – 60 %** |
| Oxygen Permeability | OTR | **0.5 – 3.0 cm³·mil / (100 in²·day·atm)** |
| Water Vapour Transmission | WVTR | **0.1 – 0.5 g·mil / (100 in²·day)** |
| Tensile Strength | σt | **55 – 110 MPa** |
| Heat Shrinkage (at 100 °C) | ΔL/L | **30 – 60 %** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why PVDC is a Barrier Polymer — The Three Pillars

### Pillar 1 — Molecular Density and Free Volume
The molar mass of the VDC repeat unit (CH₂–CCl₂) is **96.94 g/mol**, and the two bulky, polar chlorine substituents on every alternate carbon force the polymer chain into a **tight, helical conformation**. The occupied volume per repeat unit is unusually low, leaving very little **free volume** for gas molecules to hop through.

### Pillar 2 — Dipole–Dipole Interactions
The C–Cl bond has a high dipole moment (~**1.9 D**). Adjacent C–Cl groups on neighbouring chains attract each other strongly (Keesom forces), creating physical "crosslinks" without actual covalent crosslinks. This drastically reduces both the **diffusivity (D)** and **solubility (S)** of small penetrant molecules.

### Pillar 3 — High Crystallinity
The symmetric, regular structure of PVDC allows for efficient chain folding and crystallite formation. Crystallites are **impermeable islands**; the penetrant must navigate around them via a tortuous path, increasing the effective diffusion length.

## 2.2 The Permeability Equation — Foundation of All Barrier Calculations

The transport of a gas or vapour through a polymer film is governed by the **solution–diffusion mechanism**:

$$P = D \times S$$

Where:
- $P$ = Permeability coefficient (units: cm³·cm / cm²·s·cmHg)
- $D$ = Diffusion coefficient (cm²/s) — kinetic factor
- $S$ = Solubility coefficient (cm³(STP) / cm³·cmHg) — thermodynamic factor

For multilayer and coated structures, the **resistance-in-series** model applies:

$$\frac{L_{total}}{P_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{P_i}$$

Where $L_i$ and $P_i$ are the thickness and permeability of the *i*-th layer.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = D \cdot S$ | Permeability = diffusivity × solubility |
| 2 | $\dfrac{L_{tot}}{P_{tot}} = \sum \dfrac{L_i}{P_i}$ | Multilayer barrier resistance model |
| 3 | $P = P_0 \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_p}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius temperature dependence of P |
| 4 | $\log(P) = \log(P_0) - \dfrac{E_p}{2.303\,R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T}$ | Linearised form for plotting |
| 5 | $\chi_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_{fus}}{\Delta H_{fus}^{\,100\%}} \times 100\%$ | Crystallinity from DSC |
| 6 | $T_{m} = T_{m}^{\,0}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\chi_c}\right)$ | Flory relation: melting point vs crystallinity |
| 7 | $\dfrac{q}{A} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}$ | Steady-state flux through a film |
| 8 | $\phi_{eff} = 1 - \psi_c + \psi_c \cdot \tau$ | Effective permeability (tortuosity correction) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** In KTU valuation, examiners often test the student's ability to **derive equation (7) from equation (1)** using Fick's First Law. Memorise the derivation chain: *Fick's Law → integration → steady-state flux form*.

## 2.4 Copolymer Composition vs. Barrier Behaviour

A frequently asked concept is *why we copolymerise VDC*. The answer is:

1. **Thermal stability:** Pure PVDC homopolymer decomposes near its melting point with liberation of HCl, causing discoloration and corrosion. Co-monomers (e.g., 10 % VC) lower the Tm and broaden the processing window.
2. **Processability:** Homopolymer is intractable; copolymers can be extrusion-coated, cast, or oriented.
3. **Barrier trade-off:** Increasing co-monomer content slightly **reduces** barrier performance (free volume increases), so the 80/20 to 90/10 VDC/comonomer window is the engineering optimum.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

PVDC is the polymer of choice in:
- **Meat and cheese multilayer films** (frankfurter casings — VDC-VC shrink films).
- **Coatings on BOPP, PET, PA, and cellophane** for snack-food, confectionery, and pharmaceutical blister packs.
- **Barrier layer in toothpaste tubes, ketchup bottles, and retort pouches.**
- **Pharmaceutical blister packaging** for moisture-sensitive drugs (PVDC-coated PVC has **50× lower WVTR** than uncoated PVC).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Steady-State Permeate Flux through a Single Film

**Starting point — Fick's First Law of Diffusion:**

$$J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

Where:
- $J$ = Molar flux of penetrant (mol / cm²·s)
- $D$ = Diffusion coefficient (cm²/s), assumed constant
- $C$ = Local concentration of penetrant (mol / cm³)
- $x$ = Spatial coordinate through film thickness

**Step 1 — Apply steady-state condition.**
At steady state, $\partial C / \partial t = 0$, so the flux $J$ is constant with respect to $x$. The concentration gradient is therefore linear across the film.

**Step 2 — Integrate across the film thickness $L$.**

$$J = -D \frac{C_2 - C_1}{L - 0} = D \frac{C_1 - C_2}{L}$$

where $C_1$ is the upstream (high-pressure) surface concentration and $C_2$ is the downstream (low-pressure) surface concentration.

**Step 3 — Apply Henry's Law at each surface.**
At equilibrium between the external gas phase and the polymer surface, the local concentration is proportional to the partial pressure:

$$C_1 = S \cdot p_1 \qquad C_2 = S \cdot p_2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Henry's Law into the integrated flux.**

$$J = D \cdot \frac{S \cdot p_1 - S \cdot p_2}{L} = \frac{D \cdot S}{L}(p_1 - p_2)$$

**Step 5 — Identify the permeability coefficient.**

$$P = D \cdot S$$

Therefore:

$$J = \frac{P \, \Delta p}{L}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to mass flux form (used in packaging industry).**

$$\frac{q}{A} = \frac{P \, \Delta p}{L}$$

This is the engineering flux form that appears in packaging datasheets (e.g., OTR, WVTR). It is reported per unit area, per unit time, per unit pressure difference, per unit thickness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Reminder for KTU Problems:** When the question states *"OTR = 5 cm³·mil/100 in²·day·atm"*, this is the permeability $P$ reported with the *mil* thickness unit baked in. To convert to SI (cm³·cm / cm²·day·atm), multiply by **2.54 × 10⁻³ cm/mil**.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Multilayer PVDC / LLDPE Pouch

**Problem:** A 5-layer pouch has the following construction:

| Layer | Material | Thickness (µm) | O₂ Permeability (cm³·mil/100 in²·day·atm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (outer) | PET | 12 | 12.0 |
| 2 (tie) | Adhesive | 3 | 200.0 |
| 3 (barrier) | **PVDC** | 8 | 1.5 |
| 4 (tie) | Adhesive | 3 | 200.0 |
| 5 (inner) | LLDPE | 60 | 800.0 |

Calculate the **effective OTR** of the laminate.

**Step 1 — Convert all thicknesses to mil (1 mil = 25.4 µm).**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{PET}      &= 12/25.4 = 0.4724~\text{mil} \\
L_{Adh,1}    &= 3/25.4 = 0.1181~\text{mil} \\
L_{PVDC}     &= 8/25.4 = 0.3150~\text{mil} \\
L_{Adh,2}    &= 3/25.4 = 0.1181~\text{mil} \\
L_{LLDPE}    &= 60/25.4 = 2.3622~\text{mil}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply resistance-in-series model.**

$$\frac{L_{tot}}{P_{eff}} = \sum \frac{L_i}{P_i}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum \frac{L_i}{P_i} &= \frac{0.4724}{12.0} + \frac{0.1181}{200} + \frac{0.3150}{1.5} + \frac{0.1181}{200} + \frac{2.3622}{800} \\
&= 0.03937 + 0.00059 + 0.21000 + 0.00059 + 0.00295 \\
&= 0.25350~\text{mil·day·atm / cm³}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total thickness.**

$$L_{tot} = 0.4724 + 0.1181 + 0.3150 + 0.1181 + 2.3622 = 3.3858~\text{mil}$$

**Step 4 — Effective permeability.**

$$P_{eff} = \frac{L_{tot}}{\sum L_i/P_i} = \frac{3.3858}{0.25350} = 13.36~\text{cm³·mil / 100\,in²·day·atm}$$

Wait — re-check: the standard OTR unit is "per 100 in²", so the divisor is per 100 in² area. The final answer in those units is **≈ 13.36 cm³·mil / 100 in²·day·atm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The PVDC layer, although only 8 µm thick (~ 24 % of total thickness), contributes **≈ 83 %** of the total barrier resistance. This is the *trade-off rule* KTU examiners love: *thin barrier layers can dominate performance if their permeability is low enough.*

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PVDC vs Other Barrier Polymers

| Property | **PVDC** | **EVOH** | **PA6** | **PEN** | **Aluminium Foil** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| O₂ Permeability (cm³·mil/100 in²·day·atm) | **0.5 – 3** | 0.05 – 0.5 (dry) | 2 – 6 | 1.5 – 3 | **0 (absolute)** |
| WVTR (g·mil/100 in²·day) | **0.1 – 0.5** | 4 – 10 | 15 – 25 | 2 – 5 | **0 (absolute)** |
| Moisture sensitivity | **None** | Severe | Severe | Slight | None |
| Retort stability | Good | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent |
| Transparency | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No (metallic) |
| Recyclability | Difficult | Difficult | Easy | Moderate | Difficult |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Winning Phrase:** *"PVDC is the only transparent, retort-stable, recyclable-friendly polymer that simultaneously blocks O₂ and H₂O at commodity cost."*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — PVDC Synthesis, Structure, and Application Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["VDC Monomer CH2 CCl2"] --> B1["Suspension Emulsion Polymerisation"]
    A2["Co monomer VC or AN"] --> B1
    B1 --> C1["PVDC Copolymer 80 to 95 percent VDC"]
    C1 --> D1["Coating Line"]
    C1 --> D2["Cast Film Line"]
    C1 --> D3["Biaxial Orientation Line"]
    C1 --> D4["Monolayer Shrink Film"]
    D1 --> E1["Coated BOPP PET Cellophane"]
    D2 --> E2["Cast PVDC Film"]
    D3 --> E3["BOPVDC Heat Shrinkable"]
    D4 --> E4["Meat and Cheese Casing"]
    E1 --> F1["Snack Foods Confectionery Pharma"]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F2["Meat Cheese Fish Packaging"]
    E4 --> F2
    F1 --> G1["End User"]
    F2 --> G1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 5-Layer PVDC Pouch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["Layer 1 PET 12 micrometres Print and Abuse Resistance"] --> L2["Layer 2 Adhesive 3 micrometres Tie"]
    L2 --> L3["Layer 3 PVDC 8 micrometres Oxygen and Moisture Barrier"]
    L3 --> L4["Layer 4 Adhesive 3 micrometres Tie"]
    L4 --> L5["Layer 5 LLDPE 60 micrometres Sealant and Puncture Resistance"]
    O2["O2 Molecule"] -.blocked by high density.-> L3
    WV["H2O Molecule"] -.blocked by dipole interaction.-> L3
    L5 --> SEAL["Heat Seal at 130 to 160 degree C"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Permeation Decision Logic (Barrier Selection Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Is O2 Barrier Required"] -->|Yes| S2["Is Moisture Barrier Also Required"]
    S2 -->|Yes| S3["Use PVDC or Aluminium Foil"]
    S2 -->|No| S4["Use EVOH with Desiccant or PE layer"]
    S3 --> S5["Transparent Use Case"]
    S5 -->|Yes| S6["PVDC Copolymer 80 to 90 percent VDC"]
    S5 -->|No| S7["Aluminium Foil 6 to 9 micrometres"]
    S1 -->|No| S8["Use LDPE LLDPE or PP only"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This flowchart is exactly the type of decision tree a KTU Module-2 question on *"selection of barrier plastics for a retort pouch"* expects the student to reproduce. Memorise the **Yes-Yes → PVDC** branch.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define PVDC. Why is the commercial polymer always a copolymer and not a homopolymer?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Polyvinylidene Chloride (PVDC) is a thermoplastic polymer obtained by the polymerisation of vinylidene chloride (1,1-dichloroethylene), with the repeating unit –(CH₂–CCl₂)–. The pure homopolymer has a melting point (~ 200 °C) that is very close to its thermal-decomposition temperature, with liberation of HCl, making it **thermally unstable and unprocessable** by conventional extrusion. To overcome this, VDC is copolymerised with 5–20 % of a co-monomer such as vinyl chloride (VC) or methyl acrylate. The co-monomer disrupts chain regularity, lowers the melting point by ~30–40 °C, and broadens the processing window, while preserving the bulk of the barrier performance. Hence commercial PVDC is essentially always a **copolymer**.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** State any three distinct packaging applications of PVDC, highlighting the property that justifies each application.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Meat and cheese shrink films** — *Property used:* Heat-shrinkability at 80–100 °C combined with low O₂ permeability, which extends shelf life of fresh and processed meats.
2. **Coating on BOPP/PET snack-food wrappers** — *Property used:* Outstanding water-vapour and aroma barrier, preventing rancidity of fried snacks.
3. **Pharmaceutical blister packs** (PVDC-coated PVC) — *Property used:* Very low WVTR (≈ 0.1 g·mil/100 in²·day), protecting moisture-sensitive APIs like aspirin and ranitidine.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch of the molecular structure, explain the **three structural factors** that make PVDC an outstanding barrier polymer. **7 marks**

**(b)** A monolayer PVDC film of thickness **40 µm** has an oxygen permeability of **1.8 cm³·mil / 100 in²·day·atm**. Calculate (i) the steady-state oxygen flux through the film when the partial-pressure difference is 0.21 atm, and (ii) the equivalent oxygen flux if the same barrier requirement were met by an EVOH monolayer of permeability 0.05 cm³·mil / 100 in²·day·atm. Comment on the engineering implications. **7 marks**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks
1. **Symmetric, dense packing:** The 1,1-substitution of two chlorine atoms on every alternate carbon forces the chain into a close-packed helical conformation. The molar mass per repeat unit is high (**96.94 g/mol**) with low specific free volume. (2 marks)
2. **Strong dipole–dipole interactions:** C–Cl bonds carry a dipole moment of ~1.9 D. Inter-chain Keesom forces act like physical crosslinks, reducing both diffusivity D and solubility S of penetrants. (2 marks)
3. **High crystallinity (40–60 %):** Crystalline regions are impermeable, so the penetrant must diffuse through a tortuous amorphous path, effectively multiplying the diffusion length by a factor called the **geometric tortuosity factor τ**. (2 marks)
4. **Sketch (description):** Show the –(CH₂–CCl₂)–ₙ repeat unit, label the two C–Cl bonds on the same carbon, indicate the helical chain, and draw crystallites as ordered regions within the amorphous matrix. (1 mark)

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Convert thickness to mil:** $L = 40\,\mu m = 40/25.4 = 1.575~\text{mil}$

**(i) Flux through PVDC:**

$$\frac{q}{A} = \frac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L} = \frac{1.8 \times 0.21}{1.575} = 0.240~\text{cm³/100\,in²·day}$$

**(ii) Flux through EVOH (identical Δp, identical thickness L = 1.575 mil):**

$$\frac{q}{A} = \frac{0.05 \times 0.21}{1.575} = 0.00667~\text{cm³/100\,in²·day}$$

(Alternatively, if EVOH thickness is varied to match flux: $L_{EVOH} = (0.05 \times 0.21)/0.240 = 0.0438$ mil ≈ 1.11 µm.)

**Engineering Implications (2 marks):**
- Although EVOH has lower intrinsic permeability (by a factor of 36), it is **highly moisture sensitive** — a 50 % RH increase can raise its OTR by an order of magnitude. PVDC is **insensitive** to humidity.
- For long-shelf-life products stored in humid conditions, PVDC is the more **robust** engineering choice, even at slightly higher intrinsic permeability.
- Cost-benefit: EVOH is cheaper per kg but requires an additional moisture-barrier skin layer (PE/PP), pushing the laminate thickness up.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the **steady-state permeation equation** $q/A = P \Delta p / L$ starting from Fick's First Law of Diffusion. State clearly the assumptions made. **7 marks**

**(b)** Explain the principle of **resistance-in-series** for multilayer barrier films. A retort pouch has the following construction: PET (12 µm, OTR 12) / Adhesive (3 µm, OTR 200) / PVDC (8 µm, OTR 1.5) / Adhesive (3 µm, OTR 200) / CPP (60 µm, OTR 700), where OTR values are in cm³·mil / 100 in²·day·atm. Calculate the **effective OTR** of the laminate and the **percentage contribution** of the PVDC layer to the total barrier resistance. **7 marks**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks
Full derivation as in **Section 3.1** above. The assumptions to be explicitly stated are:
1. Steady-state permeation ($\partial C/\partial t = 0$).
2. Constant diffusion coefficient $D$ (no concentration dependence).
3. Henry's Law sorption at both film surfaces.
4. One-dimensional diffusion through the film (edge effects negligible).
5. Constant temperature and pressure across the system.

(Each assumption: ~0.5 mark; correct derivation: 4 marks; final boxed equation: 0.5 mark.)

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks
**(i) Apply resistance-in-series model (3 marks):**

$$\frac{L_{tot}}{P_{eff}} = \sum \frac{L_i}{P_i}$$

Convert thicknesses to mil:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{PET}      &= 0.4724~\text{mil} \\
L_{Adh,1}    &= 0.1181~\text{mil} \\
L_{PVDC}     &= 0.3150~\text{mil} \\
L_{Adh,2}    &= 0.1181~\text{mil} \\
L_{CPP}      &= 2.3622~\text{mil} \\
L_{tot}      &= 3.3858~\text{mil}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute each $L_i/P_i$ term:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{L_{PET}}{P_{PET}}   &= 0.4724/12 = 0.03937 \\
\frac{L_{Adh,1}}{P_{Adh}} &= 0.1181/200 = 0.00059 \\
\frac{L_{PVDC}}{P_{PVDC}} &= 0.3150/1.5 = 0.21000 \\
\frac{L_{Adh,2}}{P_{Adh}} &= 0.1181/200 = 0.00059 \\
\frac{L_{CPP}}{P_{CPP}}   &= 2.3622/700 = 0.00337 \\
\text{Sum}                &= 0.25392~\text{mil·day·atm/cm³}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Effective permeability (2 marks):**

$$P_{eff} = \frac{L_{tot}}{\text{Sum}} = \frac{3.3858}{0.25392} = 13.33~\text{cm³·mil/100\,in²·day·atm}$$

**(iii) Percentage contribution of PVDC layer (2 marks):**

$$\%\,R_{PVDC} = \frac{L_{PVDC}/P_{PVDC}}{\sum L_i/P_i} \times 100\% = \frac{0.21000}{0.25392} \times 100\% = 82.7\%$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** The 8 µm PVDC layer provides **82.7 %** of the total oxygen barrier of the laminate despite being only 9.3 % of the total thickness — a clear demonstration of the *thin-barrier-dominated* design principle used in retort pouches.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — PVDC Questions**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to convert µm → mil before applying the resistance equation is the **#1 mark-loss reason** in numerical Part B questions. Always check: 1 mil = 25.4 µm.
> 2. **Treating PVDC as a homopolymer:** Writing *"PVDC is the polymer of vinylidene chloride"* without mentioning the co-monomer (VC, AN, MA) costs at least **1 mark** in a definition question.
> 3. **Forgetting Henry's Law assumption:** In derivations, examiners specifically check whether the student has stated that surface concentration is governed by Henry's Law. Omitting this assumption = -1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing OTR with permeability:** OTR (oxygen transmission rate) is *not* the same as permeability. OTR already includes a thickness normalisation (often *mil* in US units and *µm* in SI), whereas permeability $P$ is an intrinsic material property *independent of thickness*.
> 5. **Missing "moisture insensitivity":** When asked to compare PVDC with EVOH, the answer must explicitly state that PVDC is unaffected by relative humidity, while EVOH loses 80–90 % of its O₂ barrier above 60 % RH.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Full name:** Polyvinylidene Chloride; monomer is 1,1-dichloroethylene (CH₂=CCl₂).
- **Commercial form:** Always a copolymer — typical co-monomers are VC, AN, methyl acrylate; VDC content 80–95 %.
- **Crystallinity:** 40–60 %; glass transition ~ −15 °C; melting point ~ 160–175 °C.
- **Density:** 1.60–1.75 g/cm³ — heavier than most commodity plastics.
- **Trade names:** Saran™, Diofan™, Ixan™.
- **Unique selling point:** *Simultaneous* excellent O₂ and H₂O barrier, plus transparency and retort stability.
- **Permeability governing equation:** $P = D \cdot S$ (diffusivity × solubility).
- **Flux equation:** $q/A = P \Delta p / L$.
- **Multilayer design rule:** Resistance in series — $L_{tot}/P_{tot} = \sum L_i/P_i$.
- **Temperature dependence:** Arrhenius — $\ln P$ vs $1/T$ is linear with slope $-E_p/R$.
- **Coating use:** PVDC latex is coated onto BOPP, PET, cellophane, and PVC — thickness typically 1–8 g/m².
- **Shrink films:** Oriented PVDC shrinks 30–60 % at 80–100 °C — used for cheese and meat casings.
- **Limitation:** Difficult to recycle (chlorine content); HCl release on incineration requires scrubbers.
- **Comparison mantra for exams:** *"PVDC = barrier + clarity + retort + moisture-proof + recyclable-friendly."*
- **Examiner trap:** OTR ≠ Permeability; always state units and conversions explicitly.
- **Master derivation:** Fick's First Law → Henry's Law → steady-state flux equation (the chain examiners test repeatedly).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vinyl Acetate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vinyl Acetate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vinyl Acetate (VA, VAc, or Vinyl Ethanoate)** is the organic compound with the chemical formula **CH₃–COO–CH=CH₂**. It is the **vinyl ester of acetic acid**, belonging to the family of vinyl monomers. In the plastics packaging industry, vinyl acetate is rarely used as a homopolymer (PVAc) in its pure form for structural packaging; instead, it serves as a **reactive co-monomer** that is copolymerized — most importantly with **ethylene** to produce **Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate (EVA)** — and also with **vinyl chloride (VC-VAc)** for specialty packaging resins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT748 Module 2):**
> Vinyl Acetate is treated as a *functional co-monomer* whose polar acetate side-group modifies crystallinity, flexibility, adhesion, and barrier behaviour of polyolefin-based packaging films.

### 1.2 IUPAC Name, Identifiers & Key Physical Constants

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| IUPAC Name | Ethenyl acetate / Vinyl ethanoate |
| Molecular Formula | C₄H₆O₂ |
| Molecular Weight | **86.09 g/mol** |
| CAS Number | 108-05-4 |
| Boiling Point | **72.7 °C (343 K)** |
| Melting Point | −93 °C |
| Density (20 °C) | **0.934 g/cm³** |
| Refractive Index (nD²⁰) | 1.3959 |
| Flash Point | −8 °C (highly flammable) |
| Heat of Polymerization | **−88 kJ/mol** (exothermic) |

> [!NOTE]
> The very low boiling point (**72.7 °C**) of vinyl acetate is critical in packaging-process engineering: it dictates that VA must be handled under pressure during polymerization and stripped/recovered from the polymer matrix downstream. This is a *favourite KTU viva question*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Velcro" Monomer

Imagine a standard **polyethylene (PE)** chain as a row of plain, smooth Lego bricks stacked neatly — they pack tightly, form crystals, and produce a stiff, glossy film (think of a crisp bread-bag wrapper). Now, insert **vinyl acetate bricks** at random intervals along that row. Each VA brick carries a small, sticky, fork-like "acetate appendage" (-OCOCH₃). This appendage acts like **molecular Velcro**:

- It **prevents the chain from packing tightly** → reduces crystallinity.
- It **introduces polarity** → boosts adhesion and printability.
- It **softens the chain** → gives flexibility, sealability, and low-temperature toughness.

So vinyl acetate is the **plasticizer-by-copolymerization** molecule — instead of blending a separate plasticizer (which can migrate), you chemically stitch flexibility directly into the polymer backbone.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Polymerization Chemistry of Vinyl Acetate

Vinyl acetate polymerizes almost exclusively by **free-radical addition polymerization**. The electron-withdrawing acetate group makes the C=C bond electron-deficient, so VA is classified as an **electron-acceptor monomer**.

### 2.2 Homopolymerization → Poly(Vinyl Acetate) — PVAc

$$n \, CH_2=CH-OOC-CH_3 \xrightarrow{\text{radical initiator, } \Delta} \left[ -CH_2-CH(OOC-CH_3)- \right]_n$$

Key characteristics of PVAc:

- **Amorphous, soft, tacky** (Tg ≈ **30 °C**).
- Excellent **adhesive** for paper, wood, cloth — basis of *white glue / PVA school glue* (a packaging laminate adhesive).
- **Water-sensitive** → hydrolysed to PVA (polyvinyl alcohol) which is water-soluble and barrier-effective against oxygen.

### 2.3 The Critical Role: Copolymerization → EVA

The industrial heart of vinyl acetate in packaging is the **copolymer with ethylene**:

$$m \, CH_2=CH_2 \; + \; n \, CH_2=CH-OOC-CH_3 \xrightarrow{\text{radical, high P}} \text{Polymer with } m,n \text{ randomized}$$

The **VA content (%)** is the master control knob:

- **0–6 % VA**: film-grade LDPE modifier → improves **heat-seal strength** and clarity (cling film).
- **6–18 % VA**: flexible film → **greenhouse films, stretch films, lamination film**.
- **18–40 % VA**: rubbery thermoplastic → **hot-melt adhesives, tie-layers** in multilayer packaging.
- **40–80 % VA**: **ethylene-vinyl alcohol (EVOH) precursor** (after hydrolysis) → high-barrier oxygen film.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mayo-Lewis Copolymerization | $\dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \dfrac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} \cdot \dfrac{r_1[M_1]+[M_2]}{[M_1]+r_2[M_2]}$ | Predict instantaneous copolymer composition |
| 2 | Reactivity ratios (E/VA) | $r_E = 0.74,\ r_{VA} = 1.43$ | E → Q-e scheme input |
| 3 | Monomer reactivity ratio product | $r_1 \cdot r_2$ | If $r_1 r_2 \to 1$: ideal; $\to 0$: alternating |
| 4 | Fox Equation for Tg | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$ | Predict Tg of EVA from VA % |
| 5 | Density of EVA | $\rho \approx 0.951 - 0.0008 \cdot (VA\%)$ | Process control, mass-throughput |
| 6 | MFI relation to VA | MFI ↑ as VA content ↑ | Processability indicator |
| 7 | Crystallinity drop | $X_c(\%) \approx 65 - 1.05 \cdot (VA\%)$ | Film clarity & softness prediction |
| 8 | Permeability shift (O₂) | $P_{O_2}$ roughly $\times 10$ per 10 % VA | Barrier-design quick estimate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** For ethylene (M₁) + vinyl acetate (M₂), $r_1 r_2 = 0.74 \times 1.43 \approx 1.06 \approx 1$. This means EVA is an **ideal random copolymer** (no drift in composition with conversion), simplifying reactor design — a key KTU design-point.

## 2.5 Free-Radical Mechanism — Initiation, Propagation, Termination

1. **Initiation**: Initiator (e.g., AIBN or peroxide) decomposes → R• radical attacks CH₂=CH–OAc.
2. **Propagation**: Chain radical adds to monomer → new radical species with pendant acetate.
3. **Termination**: Combination or disproportionation; **chain transfer to monomer** is unusually frequent in VA polymerization — this is why PVAc has broad MWD and low molecular weight unless chain-transfer agents are controlled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chain transfer to polymer (CTP)** is industrially exploited: it creates **long-chain branching** in LDPE/EVA, which is what gives EVA its excellent **melt strength** and **cling behaviour** in stretch films.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Multilayer Packaging Tie-Layer**: EVA (18–28 % VA) bonds PE to PA or EVOH — without EVA, polyolefin and polar barrier layers would delaminate.
- **Solar / Greenhouse Films**: EVA (6–18 % VA) films transmit > 90 % visible light and resist UV — used for agricultural packaging.
- **Heat-Seal Layer**: Low VA-content EVA seals at **80–110 °C**, far below LDPE's seal temperature (~120 °C) — enables faster packaging lines.
- **Hot-Melt Adhesives**: EVA with 18–40 % VA + wax + tackifier = the workhorse of carton sealing.
- **EVOH Precursor**: VA units in EVA are saponified with NaOH/methanol → EVOH, the highest-barrier oxygen packaging polymer.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mayo-Lewis Equation (Terminal Model)

The **terminal model** assumes the reactivity of a propagating chain-end depends *only* on the terminal monomer unit.

Consider two monomers M₁ (ethylene) and M₂ (vinyl acetate). The four propagation reactions with rate constants $k_{ij}$ are:

$$M_1^{\bullet} + M_1 \xrightarrow{k_{11}} M_1^{\bullet} \quad (\text{homopropagation})$$

$$M_1^{\bullet} + M_2 \xrightarrow{k_{12}} M_2^{\bullet} \quad (\text{cross-propagation})$$

$$M_2^{\bullet} + M_1 \xrightarrow{k_{21}} M_1^{\bullet} \quad (\text{cross-propagation})$$

$$M_2^{\bullet} + M_2 \xrightarrow{k_{22}} M_2^{\bullet} \quad (\text{homopropagation})$$

Define reactivity ratios:

$$r_1 = \frac{k_{11}}{k_{12}}, \qquad r_2 = \frac{k_{22}}{k_{21}}$$

Rate of consumption of M₁:

$$-\frac{d[M_1]}{dt} = k_{11}[M_1^{\bullet}][M_1] + k_{21}[M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]$$

Rate of consumption of M₂:

$$-\frac{d[M_2]}{dt} = k_{12}[M_1^{\bullet}][M_2] + k_{22}[M_2^{\bullet}][M_2]$$

Apply steady-state on chain-end concentrations: $[M_1^{\bullet}]$ and $[M_2^{\bullet}]$ are constant.

$$\frac{[M_1^{\bullet}]}{[M_2^{\bullet}]} = \frac{k_{22}[M_2]}{k_{21}[M_1]}$$

Taking the ratio of consumption rates:

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{k_{11}[M_1^{\bullet}][M_1] + k_{21}[M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]}{k_{12}[M_1^{\bullet}][M_2] + k_{22}[M_2^{\bullet}][M_2]}$$

Substituting the steady-state ratio and dividing numerator and denominator by $k_{12}[M_2^{\bullet}][M_2]$:

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} \cdot \frac{r_1[M_1]+[M_2]}{[M_1]+r_2[M_2]}$$

This is the **instantaneous copolymer composition equation**.

> [!NOTE]
> The instantaneous mole fraction of M₁ in the copolymer $F_1$ relates to monomer mole fraction $f_1$ by:
> $$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$$
> derived by substituting $f_1 = [M_1]/([M_1]+[M_2])$ and $f_2 = 1-f_1$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — VA Content vs. Feed Composition

> **Problem (KTU Style):** A packaging-grade EVA is to be produced with **20 wt % VA**. If $r_E = 0.74$ and $r_{VA} = 1.43$, determine the required ethylene feed mole fraction $f_E$ for instantaneous copolymer composition matching 20 wt % VA.

**Step 1 — Convert wt % to mol % in copolymer.**

Molar masses: $M_E = 28$ g/mol, $M_{VA} = 86$ g/mol.

For 100 g copolymer: VA = 20 g → $n_{VA} = 20/86 = 0.2326$ mol; E = 80 g → $n_E = 80/28 = 2.8571$ mol.

Mole fraction of VA in copolymer: $F_{VA} = 0.2326 / (0.2326 + 2.8571) = 0.2326 / 3.0897 = 0.0753$.

Mole fraction of ethylene in copolymer: $F_E = 1 - 0.0753 = 0.9247$.

**Step 2 — Solve Mayo-Lewis for feed mole fraction.**

Let $f_E = $ mole fraction of ethylene in feed, $f_{VA} = 1 - f_E$.

$$F_E = \frac{r_E f_E^2 + f_E f_{VA}}{r_E f_E^2 + 2 f_E f_{VA} + r_{VA} f_{VA}^2}$$

Let $x = f_E$ and $1-x = f_{VA}$:

$$0.9247 = \frac{0.74 x^2 + x(1-x)}{0.74 x^2 + 2 x(1-x) + 1.43(1-x)^2}$$

Simplify numerator: $0.74 x^2 + x - x^2 = x - 0.26 x^2$.

Simplify denominator: $0.74 x^2 + 2x - 2x^2 + 1.43(1 - 2x + x^2)$
$= 0.74 x^2 + 2x - 2x^2 + 1.43 - 2.86x + 1.43 x^2$
$= (0.74 - 2 + 1.43) x^2 + (2 - 2.86) x + 1.43$
$= 0.17 x^2 - 0.86 x + 1.43$

Set up the equation:

$$0.9247 \, (0.17 x^2 - 0.86 x + 1.43) = x - 0.26 x^2$$

$$0.1572 x^2 - 0.7952 x + 1.3223 = x - 0.26 x^2$$

$$0.1572 x^2 + 0.26 x^2 - 0.7952 x - x + 1.3223 = 0$$

$$0.4172 x^2 - 1.7952 x + 1.3223 = 0$$

Apply quadratic formula:

$$x = \frac{1.7952 \pm \sqrt{1.7952^2 - 4(0.4172)(1.3223)}}{2(0.4172)}$$

$$x = \frac{1.7952 \pm \sqrt{3.2227 - 2.2071}}{0.8344}$$

$$x = \frac{1.7952 \pm \sqrt{1.0156}}{0.8344} = \frac{1.7952 \pm 1.0078}{0.8344}$$

Two solutions: $x_1 = (1.7952 + 1.0078)/0.8344 = 3.36$ (reject, > 1)
$x_2 = (1.7952 - 1.0078)/0.8344 = 0.9447$

**Answer:** $f_E \approx 0.945$, i.e. **94.5 mol % ethylene in the feed** produces an EVA with **20 wt % VA**.

### 3.3 Glass Transition via Fox Equation

Fox equation (weight-fraction form):

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$$

For EVA: $T_{g,PE} = -120\,°C = 153\,K$, $T_{g,PVA} = +30\,°C = 303\,K$.

For 20 wt % VA: $w_{VA} = 0.20$, $w_E = 0.80$.

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{0.80}{153} + \frac{0.20}{303}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = 0.005229 + 0.000660 = 0.005889$$

$$T_g = 169.8\,K = -103.4\,°C$$

This very low $T_g$ is why EVA remains **flexible at freezer temperatures** — a critical packaging property.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Monomer-to-Polymer Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Vinyl Acetate Monomer CH2 CH OOC CH3"] --> B["Free Radical Polymerization"]
    C["Ethylene Monomer CH2 CH2"] --> B
    B --> D["Copolymerization Reactor High Pressure 1500 3000 atm"]
    D --> E["EVA Copolymer Random Sequence"]
    E --> F["VA Content 0 40 percent"]
    F --> G["LDPE Modifier 0 6 percent VA"]
    F --> H["Flexible Film Grade 6 18 percent VA"]
    F --> I["Tie Layer Adhesive 18 28 percent VA"]
    F --> J["Hot Melt Adhesive 28 40 percent VA"]
    E --> K["Saponification NaOH Methanol"]
    K --> L["EVOH Oxygen Barrier Resin"]
```

## 4.2 Multilayer Packaging Film Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Outer PET Layer 12 micron"] --> P2["Tie Layer EVA 18 percent VA"]
    P2 --> P3["Barrier EVOH 5 micron"]
    P3 --> P4["Tie Layer EVA 18 percent VA"]
    P4 --> P5["Inner PE Sealant 60 micron"]
    P5 --> P6["Food Contact Surface"]
```

## 4.3 Vinyl Acetate Decision Matrix for Packaging Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Need flexible film?"] -->|Yes| Q2["Cold seal needed?"]
    Q1 -->|No| Q3["Need oxygen barrier?"]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q4["Use EVA 6 to 18 percent VA"]
    Q2 -->|No| Q5["Use LDPE only"]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q6["Use EVOH derived from EVA hydrolysis"]
    Q3 -->|No| Q7["Use PP or PET"]
    Q4 --> R["Heat Seal at 80 to 110 C"]
    Q6 --> S["O2 Permeability less than 0.1 cc m2 day"]
```

## 4.4 Reaction Mechanism Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I1["Initiator I2 Peroxide"] --> I2["Radical R dot"]
    I2 --> I3["Add to VA monomer"]
    I3 --> I4["Chain Radical with Acetate Pendant"]
    I4 --> I5["Add to Ethylene or VA"]
    I5 --> I6["Growing Chain"]
    I6 --> I7{"Termination?"}
    I7 -->|Combination| I8["Dead Polymer Broad MWD"]
    I7 -->|Disproportionation| I8
    I7 -->|Chain Transfer to Polymer| I9["Long Chain Branching"]
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**"Why is vinyl acetate never used as a homopolymer for primary food packaging films, even though its monomer is readily available?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocation shown):**

- **(1 mark)** Vinyl acetate homopolymer (PVAc) is amorphous, tacky, and has a $T_g$ of ~30 °C → it is soft and sticky at room temperature, unsuitable as a load-bearing packaging film.
- **(1 mark)** PVAc is highly **water-sensitive** (hydrolysable to PVA) and has poor moisture barrier and creep resistance.
- **(1 mark)** In the packaging industry, VA is therefore used as a **co-monomer** (with ethylene, vinyl chloride) where its polar acetate group provides flexibility, adhesion, and sealability *without compromising structural strength*.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**"List the three most important packaging applications of Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate (EVA) copolymer and the corresponding VA content range for each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Application | VA Content (%) |
|---|---|
| Heat-seal layer / film modifier | 0–6 |
| Stretch film, lamination film, greenhouse film | 6–18 |
| Tie-layer adhesive in multilayer barrier film | 18–28 |

*(Each correct row carries 1 mark; full marks for three correctly paired application–VA% rows.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **ESE Pattern:** Answer **one** of the two questions. Each carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **free-radical copolymerization mechanism** of ethylene and vinyl acetate to produce EVA. Discuss the role of reactivity ratios. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EVA film grade is required with **12 wt % vinyl acetate**. Using $r_E = 0.74$ and $r_{VA} = 1.43$, calculate the **feed mole fraction of vinyl acetate** that will yield this composition. State two important packaging applications of EVA with VA content in the range 6–18 %. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a)

1. **Initiation (1.5 marks):** Thermal decomposition of organic peroxide (e.g., di-*tert*-butyl peroxide) gives radicals R•. The radical attacks the double bond of vinyl acetate (electron-poor vinyl) preferentially or adds to ethylene.

2. **Propagation (2 marks):** The chain-end radical alternates between addition to ethylene and to vinyl acetate. Due to $r_E \approx 0.74$ and $r_{VA} \approx 1.43$, VA has a slight preference for self-propagation, producing a **random** copolymer with very slight drift.

3. **Termination (1.5 marks):** Combination or disproportionation; **chain transfer to polymer** is industrially significant, producing long-chain branching which gives EVA its excellent melt strength and cling.

4. **Reactivity ratio interpretation (2 marks):** $r_1 r_2 = 1.06 \approx 1$ → **ideal random copolymer**. Composition drift during conversion is minimal → reactor can be operated as a well-mixed continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) without complex feed-programming.

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Step 1 — wt % to mol % in copolymer.**

For 100 g EVA: VA = 12 g → $n_{VA} = 12/86 = 0.1395$ mol; E = 88 g → $n_E = 88/28 = 3.1429$ mol.

Total: 3.2824 mol.

Mole fraction of VA: $F_{VA} = 0.1395 / 3.2824 = 0.0425$.

Mole fraction of E: $F_E = 1 - 0.0425 = 0.9575$.

**Step 2 — Solve Mayo-Lewis for feed.**

Let $f_{VA} = y$, $f_E = 1 - y$.

$$F_E = \frac{r_E (1-y)^2 + (1-y)y}{r_E (1-y)^2 + 2(1-y)y + r_{VA} y^2}$$

Let $u = 1 - y$ (so $u = f_E$).

$$0.9575 = \frac{0.74 u^2 + u(1-u)}{0.74 u^2 + 2u(1-u) + 1.43(1-u)^2}$$

Numerator: $0.74 u^2 + u - u^2 = u - 0.26 u^2$.

Denominator: $0.74 u^2 + 2u - 2u^2 + 1.43(1 - 2u + u^2) = 0.17 u^2 - 0.86 u + 1.43$.

Equation:

$$0.9575(0.17 u^2 - 0.86 u + 1.43) = u - 0.26 u^2$$

$$0.1628 u^2 - 0.8235 u + 1.3692 = u - 0.26 u^2$$

$$0.4228 u^2 - 1.8235 u + 1.3692 = 0$$

Quadratic formula:

$$u = \frac{1.8235 \pm \sqrt{3.3252 - 2.3158}}{0.8456} = \frac{1.8235 \pm 1.0056}{0.8456}$$

Valid root: $u = (1.8235 - 1.0056)/0.8456 = 0.9671$.

So $f_E = 0.9671$, hence $f_{VA} = 0.0329$ ≈ **3.29 mol %** in the feed.

**Packaging applications of 6–18 % VA EVA (2 marks):**
1. **Stretch film and cling film** for pallet wrapping — uses high melt strength and tackiness of EVA.
2. **Lamination film** in multilayer flexible packaging and **greenhouse / agricultural film** — uses UV resistance and high light transmittance.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **structure-property relationships** of EVA as a function of vinyl acetate content. Show how the **Fox equation** predicts the glass transition temperature of EVA containing 25 wt % VA. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the conversion of EVA into **EVOH (Ethylene-Vinyl Alcohol)** through saponification. Why is EVOH considered the gold-standard oxygen-barrier polymer in multilayer food packaging? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Structure–property relationships (4 marks):**

| VA % | Crystallinity | Tg (°C) | Flexibility | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (LDPE) | High (~50 %) | −120 | Moderate | Carrier bag |
| 6–12 | Medium | −110 to −100 | Good | Stretch film, lamination |
| 18–25 | Low | −90 to −70 | Very high | Tie-layer, adhesive |
| 25–40 | Very low / amorphous | −60 to −30 | Rubbery | Hot-melt adhesive |

Acetate groups **disrupt polyethylene crystal lattice** → lowering crystallinity, density, softening point and stiffness while **increasing** impact strength, clarity, adhesion, and sealability. Beyond ~30–40 % VA, EVA becomes rubbery and loses mechanical integrity — hence the industrial ceiling.

**Fox equation calculation (3 marks):**

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_E}{T_{g,E}} + \frac{w_{VA}}{T_{g,VA}}$$

With $w_E = 0.75$, $w_{VA} = 0.25$, $T_{g,E} = 153$ K, $T_{g,VA} = 303$ K:

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{0.75}{153} + \frac{0.25}{303} = 0.004902 + 0.000825 = 0.005727$$

$$T_g = 174.6 \, \text{K} = -98.4 \, °C$$

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Saponification (3 marks):**

$$\left[ -CH_2-CH(OOC-CH_3)- \right]_n \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH / MeOH}} \left[ -CH_2-CH(OH)- \right]_n + n \, CH_3COONa$$

The acetate ester groups on VA units are hydrolysed to **hydroxyl (-OH) groups**, converting EVA into a **poly(vinyl alcohol-co-ethylene)** copolymer — i.e., EVOH. The reaction is essentially quantitative; residual VA content is typically < 5 mol %.

**Why EVOH is the gold-standard O₂ barrier (4 marks):**

- **Hydrogen bonding**: Hydroxyl groups form extensive inter-chain **H-bond networks**, creating an extremely dense, polar matrix that slows O₂ diffusion.
- **O₂ permeability** of EVOH: **0.05–0.5 cm³·mm/m²·day·atm** — orders of magnitude lower than PE (~150) or PP (~90).
- **Transparency & gloss** maintained.
- **Limitation**: hydrophilic → moisture-sensitive. Therefore EVOH is **always sandwiched between two moisture-barrier polyolefin layers** (PE/PP) in a multilayer film with EVA as the **tie-layer**.

**Multilayer example:** PE // EVA // EVOH // EVA // PE — typical ketchup, meat, retort-pouch structure.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in VA-based Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PVAc (homopolymer) with PVA (polyvinyl alcohol)** — they are *not* the same. PVAc is acetate-ester; PVA is the hydrolysed alcohol form. Examiners deduct 1 mark if swapped.
> 2. **Mixing up mol % and wt %** in Mayo-Lewis problems. Always convert wt % → mol % using molar masses $M_E = 28$, $M_{VA} = 86$ *before* plugging into the equation.
> 3. **Forgetting that $r_1 r_2 \approx 1$** for E/VA means **ideal random copolymer**. Students wrongly say "alternating" ($r_1 r_2 \to 0$) — cost: 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing EVOH as "ethyl vinyl alcohol"** — the correct expanded name is **ethylene–vinyl alcohol copolymer**. Examiners are strict on nomenclature.
> 5. **Ignoring the moisture sensitivity of EVOH** — quoting only its O₂ barrier property without mentioning that it requires protective polyolefin layers and tie-layer EVA loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Vinyl Acetate in Plastics Packaging**

- ☐ Vinyl acetate monomer: **CH₂=CH–OOC–CH₃**, MW = **86.09 g/mol**, BP = **72.7 °C**.
- ☐ Polymerizes by **free-radical mechanism**; reactivity ratios with ethylene: **$r_E = 0.74$, $r_{VA} = 1.43$**.
- ☐ **$r_1 r_2 \approx 1.0$** → **ideal random copolymer**, no composition drift.
- ☐ Increasing **VA content** → **decreases** crystallinity, density, melting point, stiffness.
- ☐ Increasing **VA content** → **increases** flexibility, clarity, adhesion, tack, impact strength, sealability, permeability.
- ☐ **VA content determines application**:
   - 0–6 % → film modifier, heat-seal
   - 6–18 % → stretch / greenhouse film
   - 18–28 % → tie-layer
   - 28–40 % → hot-melt adhesive
- ☐ **EVA Tg** predicted by **Fox equation**; lies between −120 °C (PE) and +30 °C (PVAc).
- ☐ **EVA → EVOH** by **saponification with NaOH/MeOH**; EVOH is best O₂ barrier, but **moisture-sensitive** → must be encapsulated with PE/PP using EVA tie-layers.
- ☐ **Industrial EVA production**: high-pressure (1500–3000 atm) autoclave or tubular reactor; autoclave product has more long-chain branching (better for films); tubular has narrower MWD (better for injection moulding).
- ☐ **Chain transfer to polymer** in VA polymerization → long-chain branching → high melt strength → excellent for **stretch/cling films**.
- ☐ Vinyl acetate's acetate group provides **polarity** that LDPE lacks → enables **bonding to PA, EVOH, PET, aluminium foil** — the foundation of **multilayer flexible packaging**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
