topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PV Alcohol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — POLYVINYL ALCOHOL (PVOH / PVA) IN PACKAGING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVOH or PVA)** is a **water-soluble, semi-crystalline synthetic thermoplastic polymer** with the idealized repeat unit —[CH₂—CH(OH)]ₙ—. It is uniquely prepared by the **indirect (post-polymerization) hydrolysis** (saponification) of **poly(vinyl acetate) (PVAc)**, because vinyl alcohol (the true monomer) is thermodynamically unstable and cannot be polymerized directly to a high molecular weight solid polymer. The extent of conversion of acetate (—OCOCH₃) groups to hydroxyl (—OH) groups is termed the **degree of hydrolysis (DH)**, and together with the **degree of polymerization (DPₙ)** it defines almost every packaging-relevant property of the resin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT748 – Module 2):**
> Under "Specialty / Functional Plastics in Packaging," PVOH is studied as the benchmark **oxygen-barrier and water-soluble packaging polymer**, including its chemistry, grades, properties, processing behavior, and multilayer/lamination applications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Controlled Weathering of Plastic"

Imagine a transparent plastic umbrella made of poly(vinyl acetate) — soft, sticky, water-repellent, and useless in oxygen blocking. Now pass this "umbrella" through a controlled chemical "rain" (a methanol + NaOH/KOH bath). The rain strips off the water-repelling acetate "leaves" and replaces them with —OH "petals" that **love water**. The more rain (hydrolysis) you allow, the more water-loving and oxygen-blocking the material becomes — but it also becomes more brittle and thermally sensitive.

> **PVAc (un-hydrolyzed)** = water-shedding, soft, low barrier.
> **PVOH (fully hydrolyzed)** = water-loving, hard, excellent oxygen barrier.
> **Partially hydrolyzed grades** sit on a tunable spectrum — the "sweet spot" for most packaging films.

### 1.3 Why PVOH is a "Specialty" Packaging Polymer

| Driving Requirement in Modern Packaging | What PVOH Delivers |
|---|---|
| Unit-dose, water-soluble sachets (detergent, agro-chem) | Cold-water solubility of partially hydrolyzed grades |
| High oxygen barrier at low humidity for food | Among the **highest OTR of any organic polymer** when dry |
| Transparent, glossy, printable film | Amorphous when cast; high optical clarity |
| Biodegradability / repulpability in paper | Water-dispersible and biodegradable in moist soil |
| Food contact safety | FDA/EU approved grades (fully hydrolyzed, low residual acetate) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary Builders (must be in your KTU answer script):**
> - **Degree of Hydrolysis (DH, mol %)** = 100 × (mol OH) / (mol OH + mol OCOCH₃)
> - **Saponification** = alkaline hydrolysis of the acetate ester.
> - **Cast film** vs. **Extruded film** — PVOH is almost always cast from aqueous solution; melt extrusion requires plasticizers (glycerol, sorbitol).
> - **OTR** = Oxygen Transmission Rate (cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chemistry — How PVOH is Actually Made

Because the free monomer **vinyl alcohol (CH₂=CH–OH)** tautomerizes to acetaldehyde (CH₃–CHO) almost instantly, it cannot be isolated for polymerization. The industrial route is therefore a **two-step process**:

**Step 1 — Free-radical polymerization of vinyl acetate:**

$$n\ \mathrm{CH_2{=}CH{-}OCOCH_3} \xrightarrow{\text{AIBN / BPO, } 60\text{–}70\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}} \left[ -\mathrm{CH_2{-}CH(OCOCH_3)}- \right]_n$$

This gives **poly(vinyl acetate), PVAc**.

**Step 2 — Saponification (alcoholysis) in methanol:**

$$\left[ -\mathrm{CH_2{-}CH(OCOCH_3)}- \right]_n + n\ \mathrm{CH_3OH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{NaOH}} \left[ -\mathrm{CH_2{-}CH(OH)}- \right]_n + n\ \mathrm{CH_3COOCH_3}$$

The byproduct **methyl acetate** is recovered and reconverted to vinyl acetate — giving an essentially closed-loop industrial process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "n" in step 2 is **controllable**. By stopping the reaction at a chosen conversion, the manufacturer can deliver a continuous spectrum of partially hydrolyzed grades (DH 70 – 99 mol%).

### 2.2 The Two Independent "Knobs" that Define a PVOH Grade

1. **Degree of Polymerization (DPₙ) → Molecular Weight (Mₙ)**
   Determined by chain-transfer (usually with acetaldehyde) during step 1.
   Controls melt viscosity, solution viscosity, mechanical strength, and film drawdown.

2. **Degree of Hydrolysis (DH, mol %)**
   Determined by residence time / temperature / alkali level in step 2.
   Controls water solubility, crystallinity, gas barrier, and adhesion.

> **Rule-of-thumb for KTU answers:**
> *Higher DH → better O₂ barrier, higher Tg, lower solubility at room temperature, higher moisture sensitivity of barrier.*
> *Higher DPₙ → higher solution viscosity, stronger (tougher) film, harder to process.*

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Degree of Hydrolysis | $DH = 100 \times \dfrac{n_{OH}}{n_{OH} + n_{OCOCH_3}}$ | mol % | Defines grade classification |
| 2 | Number-average MW | $M_n = DP_n \times M_0$, where $M_0$ = mass of repeat unit (44 for —CH₂—CH(OH)—) | g mol⁻¹ | Strength / viscosity |
| 3 | Intrinsic viscosity (Mark–Houwink) | $[\eta] = K\,M_v^{\,a}$ with $K \approx 6.7 \times 10^{-4}$, $a \approx 0.64$ (water, 25 °C) | dL g⁻¹ | Solution viscosity |
| 4 | Tg of fully hydrolyzed PVOH | $T_g \approx 75\text{–}85\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ | °C | Heat-seal window |
| 5 | Tg of partially hydrolyzed PVOH | $T_g \approx 45\text{–}60\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ | °C | Lower seal temperature |
| 6 | Melting point (crystalline) | $T_m \approx 220\text{–}245\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (with decomposition) | °C | Why melt extrusion is hard |
| 7 | OTR (cast film, 23 °C, 50 % RH) | $\approx 0.2\text{–}2\ \mathrm{cm^3\,m^{-2}\,day^{-1}\,atm^{-1}}$ (fully hydrolyzed) | std. units | O₂ barrier design |
| 8 | OTR sensitivity to RH | OTR increases $\sim 10\times$ per 10 % RH rise (above 60 % RH) | — | Justifies lamination |
| 9 | Solubility parameter | $\delta \approx 25\text{–}26\ \mathrm{(MPa)^{1/2}}$ | (MPa)¹ᐟ² | Solvent selection |
| 10 | Hansen partials | $\delta_d \approx 16$, $\delta_p \approx 14$, $\delta_h \approx 23$ | (MPa)¹ᐟ² | Coating/coating-solvent design |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU mistake:** quoting OTR of PVOH as a single number. OTR is a **strong function of relative humidity** because water plasticizes the matrix and swells the free volume. Always quote RH and temperature with the OTR value.

### 2.4 Why PVOH Works as an Oxygen Barrier — the "Why" Behind the Data

- The dense intra- and inter-chain **hydrogen bonding** network (O–H···O) packs chains tightly.
- This reduces **free volume**, slowing the diffusion of O₂ (which is the rate-limiting step in the permeation equation).
- When water enters, it **disrupts the H-bond network**, increases free volume, and OTR rises sharply — this is why PVOH is **always used as a buried middle layer in a multilayer structure** (e.g., PE // PVOH // PE or PET // PVOH // PE), protected from moisture by the outer polyolefin / polyester layers.

The classical **solution-diffusion permeation** framework still applies:

$$P = D \cdot S$$

where $P$ = permeability, $D$ = diffusivity (reduced by H-bonds), and $S$ = solubility (low for O₂ in dry PVOH). Engineering the *D* term through tight chain packing is the secret of PVOH.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Identifying a PVOH Grade from Analytical Data

A PVOH sample is analyzed:
- $M_n = 44{,}000\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}$
- The ¹H-NMR integration ratio of the **—OH proton** to the total **methine + methyl protons** gives 0.88.

**Step 1 — Identify the repeat unit masses.**

For a partially hydrolyzed chain, the two repeat units coexist:

- Vinyl alcohol unit: —CH₂—CH(OH)—, $M_{VA} = 44.05\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}$
- Vinyl acetate unit: —CH₂—CH(OCOCH₃)—, $M_{VAc} = 86.09\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}$

**Step 2 — Translate the NMR ratio into DH.**

In a typical ¹H-NMR in DMSO-d₆, the integration chosen by examiners is often the **methine proton** (—CH<) appearing at ≈ 3.8–4.1 ppm (alcohol unit) and ≈ 4.6–4.9 ppm (acetate unit). Equivalently, the simpler exam-friendly ratio is the **methyl of acetate** (≈ 1.9 ppm) versus the **methine + methylene** envelope (≈ 1.3–2.0 ppm).

Given the integrated ratio of the —OH to the methine + methyl protons is 0.88, and the relation between DH and the proton ratio $r$ (where $r$ = ratio of OH-bearing methine H to total methine H) is:

$$DH\ (\%) = 100 \times r$$

Substituting $r = 0.88$:

$$DH = 100 \times 0.88 = 88\ \mathrm{mol\%}$$

So this is a **"partially hydrolyzed, medium-DH"** grade.

**Step 3 — Estimate average repeat-unit mass and check Mₙ.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{M_0} &= DH \cdot M_{VA} + (1-DH) \cdot M_{VAc} \\
\overline{M_0} &= 0.88 \times 44.05 + 0.12 \times 86.09 \\
\overline{M_0} &= 38.76 + 10.33 = 49.09\ \mathrm{g\ mol^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

Number-average degree of polymerization:

$$\begin{aligned}
DP_n &= \frac{M_n}{\overline{M_0}} \\
DP_n &= \frac{44{,}000}{49.09} \approx 896
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Classify the grade.**

This DPₙ (≈ 900) and 88 mol% DH correspond to a **"medium-viscosity, partially hydrolyzed"** grade — typical for paper coating, water-soluble film, and adhesive applications.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Predicting OTR of a Multilayer Containing PVOH

A 3-layer film has the following construction (left to right): **LDPE (60 µm) // PVOH (10 µm) // LDPE (60 µm)**. The fully hydrolyzed PVOH layer has an OTR (at 23 °C, 0 % RH-equivalent) of **0.6 cm³ m⁻² day⁻¹ atm⁻¹**. The LDPE has an OTR of **2500 cm³ m⁻² day⁻¹ atm⁻¹**. Estimate the laminate OTR.

**Step 1 — Convert film OTRs to permeances (P / l).**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{LDPE} &= 2500 \times 60 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathrm{m} = 0.15\ \mathrm{cm^3\,m\,m^{-2}\,day^{-1}\,atm^{-1}} \\
P_{PVOH} &= 0.6 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathrm{m} = 0.006\ \mathrm{cm^3\,m\,m^{-2}\,day^{-1}\,atm^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Add reciprocals (resistance-in-series model).**

For a multilayer film, the total resistance is the sum of individual layer resistances:

$$\frac{1}{OTR_{total}} = \frac{1}{OTR_{LDPE1}} + \frac{1}{OTR_{PVOH}} + \frac{1}{OTR_{LDPE2}}$$

Equivalently in permeance form:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{L_{total}}{P_{total}} &= \sum_i \frac{L_i}{P_i} \\
\frac{130 \times 10^{-6}}{P_{total}} &= \frac{60 \times 10^{-6}}{0.15} + \frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{0.006} + \frac{60 \times 10^{-6}}{0.15}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{60 \times 10^{-6}}{0.15} &= 4.00 \times 10^{-4} \\
\frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{0.006} &= 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \\
\frac{60 \times 10^{-6}}{0.15} &= 4.00 \times 10^{-4} \\
\text{Sum} &= 2.467 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

So:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= \frac{130 \times 10^{-6}}{2.467 \times 10^{-3}} \\
P_{total} &= 0.0527\ \mathrm{cm^3\,m\,m^{-2}\,day^{-1}\,atm^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert back to OTR of the laminate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
OTR_{total} &= \frac{P_{total}}{L_{total}} = \frac{0.0527}{130 \times 10^{-6}} \\
OTR_{total} &\approx 405\ \mathrm{cm^3\,m^{-2}\,day^{-1}\,atm^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation for the answer script:**
The **PVOH layer, though only 10 µm thick, dominates the oxygen resistance** (≈ 81 % of the total resistance). This is the practical reason why PVOH is used as a *thin buried barrier* in food packaging — a few microns of PVOH give the same OTR reduction as hundreds of microns of polyolefin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In an exam, if you forget to convert thickness to metres or omit the resistance-in-series logic, you will lose **3 of the 7 marks** of the sub-question. Always write the series-resistance equation first, *then* substitute.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Property ↔ Grade Selector

| Property | "Super-hydrolyzed" (98 – 99 mol%) | "Fully hydrolyzed" (97 – 98 mol%) | "Intermediate" (87 – 89 mol%) | "Partially hydrolyzed" (78 – 81 mol%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Water solubility (cold) | Very low (needs ≥ 80 °C) | Low (needs ≥ 70 °C) | Moderate (40 – 60 °C) | High (10 – 20 °C) |
| O₂ barrier (dry) | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Moderate |
| Adhesion to PET / cellophane | Best | Excellent | Good | Lower |
| Typical use | High-barrier interlayer | O₂-barrier interlayer | Paper coating | Detergent film, water-soluble sachet |
| Tg (approx) | 80 °C | 78 °C | 60 °C | 48 °C |

> **Use this matrix as a one-line answer to "select a PVOH grade for ___ packaging."**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Industrial Synthesis Route of PVOH

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vinyl Acetate Monomer VAc] --> B[Step 1 Free Radical Polymerization in Methanol]
    B --> C[Poly Vinyl Acetate PVAc Solution]
    C --> D[Step 2 Alkaline Saponification NaOH in Methanol]
    D --> E[Poly Vinyl Alcohol PVOH Slurry]
    E --> F[Wash and Dry]
    F --> G[PVOH Granules Powder]
    G --> H1[Grade Fully Hydrolyzed 98 to 99 mol percent]
    G --> H2[Grade Intermediate 87 to 89 mol percent]
    G --> H3[Grade Partially Hydrolyzed 78 to 81 mol percent]
    D -. byproduct .-> I[Methyl Acetate]
    I --> J[Reconversion to VAc]
    J --> A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Multilayer Packaging Film Containing PVOH as Buried Barrier

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OUTER["OUTER LAYER - Sealing and Moisture Protection"]
        L1[LDPE or LLDPE 40 to 60 micrometres]
    end
    subgraph MIDDLE["MIDDLE LAYER - Oxygen Barrier"]
        L2[PVOH Fully Hydrolyzed 5 to 15 micrometres]
    end
    subgraph INNER["INNER LAYER - Food Contact and Seal"]
        L3[LDPE or EVA 40 to 60 micrometres]
    end
    EXT[External Environment O2 and Moisture] --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> FOOD[Packaged Food Product]
    O2A[Oxygen Molecules Blocked at PVOH Layer] -. blocked .-> L2
    M1[Moisture Blocked at Outer LDPE] -. blocked .-> L1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Application Tree of PVOH in Packaging

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PVOH[PVOH RESIN]
    PVOH --> A1[Water Soluble Sachet Film Detergents Agrochemicals Dyes]
    PVOH --> A2[Oxygen Barrier Interlayer Multilayer Food Packaging]
    PVOH --> A3[Paper and Paperboard Coating Grease and Oxygen Barrier]
    PVOH --> A4[Edible Food Coatings with Plasticizer]
    PVOH --> A5[Adhesive Layer in Lamination with PET or BOPP]
    PVOH --> A6[Release and Seed Tape Films in Agriculture]
    A1 --> P1[Polyvinyl Alcohol Film Bag Dissolves in Wash Water]
    A2 --> P2[PET or PE Outer Layer Laminated to PVOH Core]
    A3 --> P3[Carton Board with PVOH Coating for Juice and Milk]
    A4 --> P4[Fruit and Cheese Coating Prolongs Shelf Life]
    A5 --> P5[Boiling and Retort Pouches for Ready to Eat Meals]
    A6 --> P6[Pre seeded Tape Dissolves in Soil After Germination]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
*Polyvinyl alcohol is never prepared by direct polymerization of vinyl alcohol. Justify the statement with the underlying chemistry.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The free monomer vinyl alcohol (CH₂=CH–OH) is **thermodynamically unstable** and undergoes rapid **keto–enol tautomerization** to acetaldehyde (CH₃–CHO), which is the energetically favored keto form. (1 mark)
- Because the equilibrium lies almost entirely on the acetaldehyde side, vinyl alcohol cannot be isolated in sufficient concentration to undergo chain-growth polymerization to a high molecular weight solid. (1 mark)
- Industrially, **vinyl acetate is polymerized first** to give poly(vinyl acetate) (PVAc), which is a stable, processable polymer. PVAc is then **saponified (alkaline hydrolysis)** in methanol to convert the acetate groups to hydroxyl groups, yielding PVOH. (1 mark)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Why is PVOH used as a buried middle layer rather than as a monolayer in food packaging?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- PVOH has an **excellent oxygen barrier** when dry, comparable to EVOH, but its barrier performance **deteriorates sharply at high relative humidity** because water disrupts the inter-chain hydrogen-bond network and plasticizes the matrix. (1 mark)
- To retain barrier performance, the PVOH layer must be **isolated from ambient moisture** by hydrophobic outer/inner layers such as LDPE, LLDPE, or PET. (1 mark)
- In a **multilayer co-extruded or adhesive-laminated structure** (e.g., LDPE // PVOH // LDPE or PET // PVOH // PE), the PVOH acts as a thin (5–15 µm) buried oxygen-scavenging-like barrier, while the polyolefin layers provide moisture protection, mechanical strength, and heat sealability. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2, Plastics Packaging Technology]**
**(a)** With the help of a flow diagram, describe the **two-step industrial route** for the manufacture of polyvinyl alcohol from vinyl acetate. Discuss the role of **(i) degree of hydrolysis** and **(ii) degree of polymerization** in determining the packaging properties of the resin. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **oxygen-barrier performance** of PVOH with that of **(i) LDPE, (ii) EVOH, and (iii) PVDC**, and explain why PVOH is preferred in applications where **environmental sustainability and water solubility** are priorities. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answers:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

1. **Step 1 (Polymerization of VAc):** Vinyl acetate is polymerized in methanol at 60 – 70 °C using AIBN or BPO as the initiator. The reaction is a **free-radical chain polymerization**, yielding poly(vinyl acetate) (PVAc) in methanol. (1 mark)
2. **Step 2 (Saponification):** The PVAc solution is reacted with a catalytic amount of **NaOH (or KOH) in methanol**, which cleaves the acetate ester and replaces it with a hydroxyl group, producing PVOH and methyl acetate as a byproduct. (1 mark)
3. **Byproduct recovery:** Methyl acetate is recovered by distillation and **re-converted to vinyl acetate** by acid-catalyzed transesterification with vinyl acetate recycling, completing the closed industrial loop. (1 mark)
4. **Flow diagram** (must be drawn in the answer book — the verbal description above is sufficient in lieu of the drawing). (1 mark)
5. **Degree of Hydrolysis (DH):** Higher DH increases the density of –OH groups, leading to stronger intra/inter-chain hydrogen bonding, higher crystallinity, **better O₂ barrier**, and **higher Tg**. It simultaneously **reduces cold-water solubility** and increases brittleness. (1 mark)
6. **Degree of Polymerization (DPₙ):** Higher DPₙ raises Mₙ, which **increases solution and melt viscosity, tensile strength, and tear resistance** of the cast film. However, it makes solution casting more difficult and increases the cost of dissolving the resin. (1 mark)
7. **Packaging-property tuning:** By independently varying DH and DPₙ, the manufacturer delivers grades tailored to specific packaging uses — high-DH/high-DP for barrier interlayers, medium-DH/medium-DP for paper coatings, low-DH/low-DP for water-soluble sachets. (1 mark)

**(b) — 7 marks**

1. **LDPE OTR:** ≈ 2500 – 4000 cm³ m⁻² day⁻¹ atm⁻¹ — very high; LDPE is essentially **not an oxygen barrier** but is used for moisture and heat sealing. (1 mark)
2. **EVOH OTR:** ≈ 0.5 – 5 cm³ m⁻² day⁻¹ atm⁻¹ at 0 % RH; the **best-in-class** organic oxygen barrier, but highly humidity-sensitive and expensive. (1 mark)
3. **PVDC OTR:** ≈ 5 – 20 cm³ m⁻² day⁻¹ atm⁻¹, with good moisture tolerance; contains **chlorine**, which is environmentally contentious and difficult to recycle. (1 mark)
4. **PVOH OTR:** ≈ 0.2 – 2 cm³ m⁻² day⁻¹ atm⁻¹ at 0 % RH — comparable to EVOH — but is **fully water-soluble, chlorine-free, biodegradable, and repulpable**. (1 mark)
5. **Sustainability advantage of PVOH:** It does not generate toxic combustion products, can be **dissolved in water and recycled into paper streams**, and degrades in soil/biowaste environments, making it ideal for **single-dose detergent capsules, agro-chemical sachets, and edible coatings**. (1 mark)
6. **Limitation recap:** PVOH loses barrier performance at high RH and **must be laminated** to be useful in long-shelf-life food packs, so the **application must justify the lamination cost**. (1 mark)
7. **Conclusion:** PVOH occupies a unique position where the **oxygen barrier is required + water solubility / sustainability is mandated** — a niche where neither LDPE, EVOH (cost, humidity), nor PVDC (chlorine) can compete. (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2, Plastics Packaging Technology]**
**(a)** Discuss in detail the **mechanism of oxygen permeability reduction in PVOH films**, referring to (i) the **role of hydrogen bonding**, (ii) **free volume**, and (iii) the **solution-diffusion model**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **aqueous solution casting process** for producing PVOH packaging film. Compare it with the **melt extrusion route**, pointing out two advantages and two disadvantages of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answers:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

1. **Solution-diffusion framework:** Permeation of a small molecule through a dense polymer film is described by $P = D \times S$, where $D$ is the diffusivity and $S$ is the solubility. For O₂ in dry PVOH, both $D$ and $S$ are small — the gas molecule is essentially excluded from the matrix. (1 mark)
2. **Hydrogen bonding — chain packing:** The hydroxyl groups on every alternate carbon of the PVOH chain form a **dense 3-D network of intra- and inter-chain O–H···O hydrogen bonds**. This pulls adjacent chains into a tight, pseudo-crystalline arrangement. (1 mark)
3. **Free volume reduction:** The tight packing **minimizes the unoccupied ("free") volume** in the matrix. O₂, being small and non-polar, has very few transient holes to "hop" into, so its **diffusivity (D) collapses by orders of magnitude** versus amorphous polyolefins. (1 mark)
4. **Solubility (S) term:** O₂ has low condensability and low interaction with the polar PVOH matrix, so the **Henry's-law solubility is also low**. The product $P = D \times S$ is therefore exceptionally small. (1 mark)
5. **Effect of moisture:** When water enters the matrix, it **disrupts H-bonds, swells the polymer, and increases free volume**, raising $D$ sharply. The barrier therefore **degrades with increasing RH** — the practical reason for lamination. (1 mark)
6. **Effect of DH:** Higher DH → more H-bonds → lower free volume → lower $D$ → lower OTR. This is the **first-order design knob** for barrier tuning. (1 mark)
7. **Effect of crystallinity:** Dry-cast PVOH films develop crystallites during drying; these act as **impermeable inclusions**, further lowering the effective diffusion cross-section. (1 mark)

**(b) — 7 marks**

1. **Solution casting — step 1 (dope preparation):** PVOH granules are dissolved in hot water (90 – 95 °C) at 10 – 20 wt% under continuous agitation to give a clear, bubble-free **"dope"** (viscous solution). Plasticizers (glycerol, sorbitol) are added at 5 – 20 phr to improve film flexibility. (1 mark)
2. **Solution casting — step 2 (casting):** The dope is cast onto a **continuously moving stainless-steel belt** (or chilled drum) using a doctor blade or slot die, forming a wet film of controlled thickness. (1 mark)
3. **Solution casting — step 3 (drying):** The wet film passes through a **multi-zone drying tunnel** (60 – 110 °C), where water evaporates gradually. Slow drying promotes crystallinity, which enhances barrier properties. (1 mark)
4. **Solution casting — step 4 (winding):** The dry film (typically 20 – 75 µm) is **wound onto rolls**, slit to width, and packaged. (1 mark)
5. **Melt extrusion — process:** PVOH is compounded with **20 – 40 % plasticizer** (glycerol, sorbitol, water as a "volatile plasticizer") in a co-rotating twin-screw extruder, then extruded through a flat die onto a chill roll. (1 mark)
6. **Comparison — advantages of casting:** (i) No thermal degradation (PVOH decomposes near Tₘ), (ii) Higher optical clarity and crystallinity → better barrier, (iii) Lower equipment cost. (1 mark)
7. **Comparison — disadvantages of casting:** (i) Slow production rate, (ii) High energy cost for water evaporation, (iii) Difficult to make sub-20-µm films. (1 mark)
8. **Melt extrusion advantages:** (i) Higher throughput, (ii) Compatibility with conventional polyolefin processing, (iii) Easier multilayer co-extrusion with PE. **Disadvantages:** (i) Thermal degradation window is narrow (must plasticize heavily), (ii) Properties are inferior to cast film, (iii) Yellowing possible. (combined into 1 mark for the comparative table)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B):**
> (i) Do **not** confuse **degree of hydrolysis (DH)** with **degree of polymerization (DPₙ)** — they are independent and answer *different* sub-questions.
> (ii) When asked "why PVOH is a good oxygen barrier," students often write "because it has –OH groups" without linking it to **hydrogen bonding → chain packing → reduced free volume → lower D → lower P**. This logical chain is the marking key.
> (iii) Always write the **resistance-in-series equation** in multilayer OTR problems; otherwise full marks are not awarded.
> (iv) Marks are deducted if you quote OTR without (RH, temperature) conditions.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **PVOH cannot be made by direct polymerization** of vinyl alcohol because the monomer tautomerizes to acetaldehyde. The industrial route is **VAc → PVAc → PVOH via alkaline saponification**.
- Two independent **grade-defining parameters**: **Degree of Hydrolysis (DH, mol %)** and **Degree of Polymerization (DPₙ / Mₙ)**.
- **DH 98 – 99 %** = best O₂ barrier, used as a **buried multilayer interlayer**; **DH 87 – 89 %** = paper coating and adhesive; **DH 78 – 81 %** = cold-water-soluble sachet film.
- **Oxygen barrier is excellent (OTR ≈ 0.2 – 2 cm³ m⁻² d⁻¹ atm⁻¹ at 0 % RH)** but **collapses with rising RH** — must be laminated to PE or PET.
- **Tₘ ≈ 220 – 245 °C (with decomposition)** — explains why **melt extrusion requires heavy plasticization** and why **solution casting** is the preferred industrial route.
- **Permeation model:** $P = D \times S$; PVOH wins by reducing **D** via tight H-bonded chain packing (low free volume) and by also having low **S** for non-polar O₂.
- **Multilayer OTR calculation:** use the **resistance-in-series** model: $1/\text{OTR}_{tot} = \sum_i 1/\text{OTR}_i$.
- **Major packaging applications:** oxygen-barrier interlayer, water-soluble detergent/agrochemical sachet, paperboard coating, edible coating, adhesive lamination layer, agricultural seed tape.
- **Comparison snapshot for viva:** PVOH ≈ EVOH in barrier (when dry), PVDC in versatility, but **unique in water solubility + biodegradability**.
- **Trade-off to always mention in answers:** barrier ↔ moisture sensitivity, performance ↔ processability, crystallinity ↔ flexibility (need plasticizer).
- **Disposal / sustainability angle:** water-soluble, biodegradable in moist soil, repulpable with paper — gives PVOH a strong "**green packaging**" narrative that KTU examiners reward.

> **End of Module 2 — Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVOH) Study Notes**
> *(PEPOT748 — Plastics Packaging Technology, KTU 2024 Scheme)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PC Ionomers & Fluoro Polymers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PC Ionomers & Fluoro Polymers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Polycarbonate (PC) Ionomers

### Formal Definition
**Polycarbonate (PC) Ionomers** are a specialized class of modified thermoplastic polycarbonates (predominantly based on **bisphenol-A polycarbonate**) in which a small molar fraction of the polymer backbone (typically **< 15 mol%**, commonly **2–10 mol%**) carries pendant ionic functional groups. These ionic groups are usually introduced via post-polymerization sulfonation, carboxylation, or by copolymerization with ionic comonomers. The ionic groups cluster into nanoscale multiplets and clusters that act as physical crosslinks, giving the polymer enhanced melt strength, impact resistance, and tunable barrier properties relevant to **rigid food, pharmaceutical, and electronics packaging**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Ionomers are *not* a different base polymer — they are a **modified version** of an existing commodity/specialty polymer with ionic groups (e.g., carboxylate, sulfonate, quaternary ammonium). For PC ionomers, the host backbone is aromatic bisphenol-A polycarbonate.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a regular polycarbonate chain as a **single strand of beaded necklaces lying loosely in a box** — the strands slide past each other easily, which is why ordinary PC has relatively poor melt strength during extrusion and thermoforming. Now imagine sprinkling **tiny magnets (ionic groups)** along each strand. The magnets attract magnets on neighboring strands and form **reversible "sticky spots"** all along the material. The necklace strands no longer slide freely — they grip each other. When you heat the box, the magnets loosen (ionic clusters break apart), so the polymer still flows for processing. When it cools, the magnets re-grip, giving the cooled plastic better toughness, transparency, and barrier properties — perfect for **rigid, clear, high-barrier food trays and medical packaging**.

### Key Terminology Box

> [!NOTE]
> - **Multiplet:** A small aggregate of **4–8 ion pairs** that forms the *primary* ionic domain.
> - **Cluster:** A larger, glassy **microphase-separated region** (≈ 2–10 nm) containing multiplets plus surrounding restricted polymer chains.
> - **Einstein – Mullins Effect:** Reversible stiffening of ionomers at small strains due to ionic crosslink reformation.
> - **Degree of Ionization:** The mol% of repeat units carrying ionic functionality.

### Ionic Group Types in PC Ionomers

| Ionic Group Type | Functional Group | Synthesis Route | Typical Loading |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sulfonated PC (SPC) | –SO₃H / –SO₃⁻ Na⁺ | SO₃ sulfonation of aromatic rings | 3–10 mol% |
| Carboxylated PC | –COO⁻ Na⁺ | Carboxylation + neutralization | 2–8 mol% |
| Quaternary ammonium PC | –N⁺(CH₃)₃ Cl⁻ | Amination + quaternization | 1–5 mol% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Glass transition temperature (T_g) of neat BPA-PC:** **147 °C**
> - **Density of BPA-PC:** **1.20 g/cm³**
> - **Tensile strength of BPA-PC:** **55–75 MPa**
> - **Ionomer ionic content for packaging-grade PC ionomers:** **3–7 mol%** (above this, melt processing becomes difficult).

---

## 1.2 Fluoropolymers (Fluoro Polymers)

### Formal Definition
**Fluoropolymers** are a family of high-performance thermoplastics (and some thermosets) in which some or all of the hydrogen atoms on the polymer carbon backbone are replaced by **fluorine atoms**. The very strong **C–F bond (≈ 485 kJ/mol)** and the dense **fluorine "shield"** around the C–C backbone give these materials exceptional chemical inertness, thermal stability, low surface energy (non-stick), low dielectric constant, and outstanding barrier to gases, moisture, hydrocarbons, and aromas. They are therefore used as **high-barrier, high-temperature, and chemically resistant packaging materials** (e.g., pharmaceutical blister films, sterile medical packaging, aggressive-chemical containers, release liners, and high-temperature cooking pouches).

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine the polymer chain as a **highway of carbon atoms**. In ordinary polyethylene (PE), the highway shoulders are lined with **hydrogen atoms** — small and friendly, so other molecules (oxygen, water vapor, flavor oils) can squeeze past. Now imagine replacing those hydrogen atoms with **bulky, tightly packed fluorine "umbrellas"** that completely cover the highway. The C–F bonds are so strong and the fluorine atoms so tightly packed that almost **nothing can dissolve in, diffuse through, or react with** the polymer. That is why a fluoropolymer film is the *ultimate packaging barrier* — like a **bulletproof vest wrapped around a sandwich**.

### Major Commercial Fluoropolymer Families Used in Packaging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Big Six" Fluoropolymers for Packaging — Memorize This List!**

| Abbreviation | Full Chemical Name | Repeat Unit | Key Property | Packaging Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PTFE** | Polytetrafluoroethylene | –(CF₂–CF₂)ₙ– | Highest chemical & thermal resistance; non-stick | Release films, gaskets |
| **FEP** | Fluorinated Ethylene Propylene | –(CF₂–CF₂)ₙ–(CF₂–CF(CF₃))ₘ– | Melt-processable; transparent | Heat-sealable barrier layers |
| **PFA** | Perfluoroalkoxy | –(CF₂–CF₂)ₙ–(CF₂–CF(OR))ₘ– | Melt-processable; highest T_m of melt-processable FPs | Sterile pharma packaging |
| **ETFE** | Ethylene Tetrafluoroethylene | –(CH₂–CH₂)ₙ–(CF₂–CF₂)ₘ– | Tough, transparent, radiation-resistant | Pharma blister film |
| **PCTFE** | Polychlorotrifluoroethylene | –(CF₂–CFCl)ₙ– | **Best moisture barrier** of all plastics (WVTR ≈ 0.05 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹) | Military & pharma blister films (Aclar®) |
| **PVDF** | Polyvinylidene Fluoride | –(CH₂–CF₂)ₙ– | Piezoelectric; chemically resistant | Reagent-resistant containers, laminates |
| **ECTFE** | Ethylene Chlorotrifluoroethylene | –(CH₂–CH₂)ₙ–(CF₂–CFCl)ₘ– | Tough, chemical resistant | Agrochemical can liners |

### Critical Property Snapshot of Fluoropolymers

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Memorize for KTU 2024 Board Exams:**
> - **C–F bond energy:** **485 kJ/mol** (vs. C–H = 413 kJ/mol)
> - **C–C bond energy:** **347 kJ/mol**
> - **PTFE melting point:** **327 °C**
> - **PTFE service temperature (continuous):** **–260 °C to +260 °C**
> - **PTFE density:** **2.13–2.19 g/cm³** (heaviest commodity plastic)
> - **PVDF melting point:** **177 °C**
> - **PCTFE moisture-vapor transmission rate:** ≈ **0.05 g·mil/m²·day** — best in class.
> - **Surface energy of PTFE:** **≈ 18–20 mJ/m²** (lowest of all solid polymers).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Theory of PC Ionomers

### 2.1.1 Why Add Ions to Polycarbonate?

The parent **bisphenol-A polycarbonate** is a tough, transparent engineering thermoplastic (T_g ≈ 147 °C). However, it has three packaging-relevant limitations:

1. **Poor melt strength** — the linear chains slide past each other too easily, causing sagging during extrusion thermoforming of deep-draw rigid trays.
2. **Moderate oxygen barrier** — OTR of neat BPA-PC ≈ 200–300 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹ at 23 °C / 50% RH — not adequate for oxygen-sensitive foods (meats, cheeses, ready meals).
3. **Static charge buildup** — a problem in **electronics packaging** where dust attraction or ESD damage is critical.

### 2.1.2 Multiplet–Cluster Theory (Tadano/Eisenberg Model)

The structural model universally taught in polymer packaging courses is the **Eisenberg Multiplet–Cluster Model** (1970, refined through 2010s). It states:

- **Step 1:** Ionic groups (e.g., –SO₃⁻Na⁺) on different chains attract one another through **Coulombic forces** and form ion-pair aggregates called **multiplets**.
- **Step 2:** When ionic content exceeds a critical value (≈ 5–6 mol%), the multiplets organize into larger **clusters** (2–10 nm in size) that are glassy at room temperature and **microphase-separate** from the matrix.
- **Step 3:** The cluster-rich regions act as **reversible physical crosslinks**, while the cluster-poor regions remain rubbery/ductile.

$$\boxed{\text{Number of ion pairs per multiplet} \approx 4 \text{ to } 8}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Below the critical ion content, the polymer behaves like neat PC. Above it, you get a **two-phase nanocomposite** in which the ionic clusters act as nanoscale reinforcing fillers — boosting modulus, melt strength, and barrier — without sacrificing optical clarity (because cluster size < visible wavelength).

### 2.1.3 Effect of Ionic Content on Packaging-Relevant Properties

| Property | Neat BPA-PC | PC Ionomer (3 mol% sulfonate) | PC Ionomer (7 mol% sulfonate) | Trend |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| T_g | 147 °C | 152 °C | 160 °C | ↑ |
| Melt viscosity at 250 °C | 500 Pa·s | 1 200 Pa·s | 4 000 Pa·s | ↑↑ |
| Oxygen permeability (cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹) | 250 | 110 | 35 | ↓↓ |
| Water-vapor permeability | Moderate | Low | Very low | ↓ |
| Tensile modulus (GPa) | 2.4 | 2.7 | 3.2 | ↑ |
| Notched Izod impact (J/m) | 600 | 700 | 800 | ↑ |
| Surface resistivity (Ω/sq) | 10¹⁶ | 10¹² | 10⁹ (static-dissipative) | ↓↓↓ |

### 2.1.4 Counter-Ion Effect (Hofmeister-like Trend)

The choice of neutralizing cation dramatically alters properties:

- **Na⁺** → strong ionic aggregation, high modulus, high T_g.
- **Zn²⁺** → moderate aggregation, better processability.
- **K⁺** → similar to Na⁺ but slightly lower T_g.
- **Li⁺** → strong hydration, **antiplasticization**, low humidity sensitivity.

---

## 2.2 Structural Theory of Fluoropolymers

### 2.2.1 The Fluorine "Shield" Mechanism

Three molecular features give fluoropolymers their unique packaging performance:

1. **High C–F bond energy (485 kJ/mol)** — makes chemical attack thermodynamically unfavorable.
2. **Dense fluorine sheath** — the C–F bond is highly polarized (F is the most electronegative element, EN = 3.98 on Pauling scale); fluorine atoms pack tightly around the C–C backbone, leaving **no free volume** for small molecules to diffuse through.
3. **Helical chain conformation in PTFE** — the (CF₂)ₙ backbone adopts a **twisted, rigid helical ribbon** that further reduces free volume.

### 2.2.2 Classification by Hydrogen Substitution

| Class | Definition | Examples | Processability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Perfluoropolymers** | **All** H replaced by F (or by F + ether O) | PTFE, FEP, PFA | PTFE is **not** melt-processable (must be sintered); FEP & PFA are melt-processable |
| **Partially fluorinated** | **Some** H retained | ETFE, ECTFE, PVDF | All melt-processable |
| **Chlorofluoropolymers** | F + Cl substituents | PCTFE, ECTFE | Melt-processable |

### 2.2.3 Permeability Hierarchy (Why Fluoropolymers Excel as Barriers)

The permeability coefficient **P** of a polymer to a small molecule obeys the **solution–diffusion model**:

$$P = D \cdot S$$

where **D** = diffusion coefficient (kinetic), **S** = solubility coefficient (thermodynamic). In fluoropolymers:

- **D is very low** because of the dense, helical, free-volume-poor structure → molecules move slowly.
- **S is very low** because fluoropolymers are **non-polar and highly crystalline** → small molecules do not dissolve in them.

The combined effect makes fluoropolymers the **lowest-permeability** family of packaging plastics, ranking (lowest to highest): **PCTFE < ETFE < PVDF < PFA < FEP < PTFE** for water-vapor; reverse order for oxygen.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas a KTU 2024 student should be able to apply for Module 2 (PC Ionomers & Fluoropolymers):**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Variables / Units | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solution-Diffusion Permeability | $P = D \cdot S$ | P [Barrer], D [cm²/s], S [cm³(STP)/cm³·atm] | Barrier calculations |
| 2 | Permeation Flux | $J = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}$ | J [cm³/m²·day], P, Δp [atm], L [m] | Film shelf-life |
| 3 | Arrhenius Diffusion | $D = D_0 \, e^{-E_d / RT}$ | E_d = activation energy [J/mol] | Temperature effect on barrier |
| 4 | Fox–Flory T_g vs. M_n | $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \dfrac{K}{M_n}$ | K = Fox constant | Effect of ionic groups on T_g |
| 5 | Crystallinity from Density | $\chi_c = \dfrac{\rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)}$ | ρ_a, ρ_c, ρ_s [g/cm³] | % crystallinity of PVDF, PTFE |
| 6 | Mark–Houwink Viscosity | $[\eta] = K \cdot M_v^{a}$ | [η] [dL/g], M_v | Comparing ionomer molecular weights |
| 7 | Number-average M_n from end-groups | $\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | – | Sulfonation titration |
| 8 | Mole fraction of ionic units | $X_{\text{ion}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{ion}}}{n_{\text{ion}} + n_{\text{repeat}}}$ | – | Ionomer design |
| 9 | Critical ion content for clustering | $X_{\text{crit}} \approx 0.05 - 0.06$ | dimensionless | Onset of cluster formation |
| 10 | Free-volume model (Cohen–Turnbull) | $D = D_0 \, e^{-\gamma V^* / V_f}$ | V* = permeant volume, V_f = free volume | Fluoropolymer ultra-low diffusion |
| 11 | Surface energy (Fowkes / Owens–Wendt) | $\gamma_s = \gamma_s^d + \gamma_s^p$ | mJ/m² | Adhesion in laminate design |
| 12 | Hansen solubility parameter | $\delta_t^2 = \delta_d^2 + \delta_p^2 + \delta_h^2$ | MPa^½ | Solvent / polymer compatibility |
| 13 | Contact angle → wettability | $\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}$ | Young's equation | Lamination of fluoropolymer films |
| 14 | WVTR from permeability | $WVTR = \dfrac{P_{H_2O} \cdot \Delta RH \cdot p_{sat}(T)}{L}$ | g/m²·day | Moisture-barrier shelf-life |
| 15 | Multiplet size (Eisenberg) | $n_{\text{multiplet}} \approx 4\text{–}8$ ion pairs | – | Structural model |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> - Formula 2 (Permeation Flux) is what **Nestlé, Amcor, and Sealed Air** use to **predict the shelf life** of a coffee bag or a MAP food tray.
> - Formula 14 (WVTR) is what **pharma packaging designers** use to validate a 2-year shelf life for moisture-sensitive tablets in Aclar® (PCTFE) blister films.
> - Formula 9 (X_crit) tells the **ionomer formulator** whether their PC ionomer is in the "toughened" or "leathery" regime.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The Module-2 topic is **materials-science descriptive** (no differential-equation derivations as in Module-1 thermal analysis). However, KTU 2024 examiners expect students to **derive / quantify** three things rigorously:
> 1. The **multiplet-cluster threshold** (X_crit) and its consequence on T_g.
> 2. The **Fox–Flory equation** applied to ionomers.
> 3. The **shelf-life equation from permeability** applied to a fluoropolymer food pack.

The following three worked derivations are what KTU examiners expect.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fox–Flory Equation Applied to PC Ionomer T_g

**Background:** The glass transition temperature of a polymer depends on its **number-average molecular weight M_n** because chain ends act as defects that gain mobility more easily than mid-chain segments. Adding **ionic groups** lowers effective chain-end concentration by forming *clusters* that pin the chains, and also restricts segmental mobility through ionic aggregation.

**Starting Equation (Fox–Flory, 1950):**

$$
T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{\bar{M}_n}
$$

where $T_{g,\infty}$ = T_g at infinite molecular weight; $K$ = Fox constant (≈ 1.0 × 10⁵ K·g/mol for polycarbonate family).

### Step 1: Express M_n in terms of chain architecture
For a linear polymer with degree of polymerization $\overline{DP}_n$ and repeat-unit molar mass $M_0$:

$$
\bar{M}_n = \overline{DP}_n \cdot M_0
$$

**Logic:** Each repeat unit contributes mass $M_0$, so total mass per chain = (number of repeat units per chain) × (mass per repeat unit).

### Step 2: Incorporate ionic clustering as an effective molecular-weight elevation
For a sulfonated PC ionomer with ionic fraction $X_{\text{ion}}$, each ionic group bridges on average **n_ag ≈ 6** chain segments. The **effective number-average molecular weight** rises from $\bar{M}_n$ to:

$$
\bar{M}_n^{\text{eff}} = \bar{M}_n \cdot \left(1 + X_{\text{ion}} \cdot n_{\text{ag}}\right)
$$

**Logic:** Each ionic group effectively ties together ~6 chain segments, behaving as a physical "tie-molecule" — this is equivalent to multiplying the apparent chain length.

### Step 3: Substitute back into Fox–Flory

$$
T_g^{\text{ionomer}} = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{\bar{M}_n \cdot \left(1 + X_{\text{ion}} \cdot n_{\text{ag}}\right)}
$$

### Step 4: Define the T_g *shift* relative to neat PC

$$
\Delta T_g = T_g^{\text{ionomer}} - T_g^{\text{neat}} = K \cdot \frac{1}{\bar{M}_n} \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{1 + X_{\text{ion}} \cdot n_{\text{ag}}} - \frac{1}{1} \right]
$$

After algebraic simplification:

$$
\boxed{\Delta T_g = - \frac{K \cdot X_{\text{ion}} \cdot n_{\text{ag}}}{\bar{M}_n \left(1 + X_{\text{ion}} \cdot n_{\text{ag}}\right)}}
$$

**Logic:** The negative sign means T_g of the ionomer is **higher** (ΔT_g > 0 numerically when expressed as T_g^ionomer − T_g^neat — careful with sign convention!).

### Step 5: Numerical verification for 5 mol% SO₃Na⁺ PC ionomer with $\bar{M}_n$ = 25 000 g/mol

Plug in values: $K$ = 1.0 × 10⁵ K·g/mol, $X_{\text{ion}}$ = 0.05, $n_{\text{ag}}$ = 6:

$$
\Delta T_g = \frac{(1.0 \times 10^5) \cdot (0.05) \cdot (6)}{(25{,}000) \cdot (1 + 0.05 \cdot 6)}
$$

Step-by-step arithmetic:

$$
\Delta T_g = \frac{30{,}000}{25{,}000 \cdot (1 + 0.30)}
$$

$$
\Delta T_g = \frac{30{,}000}{25{,}000 \cdot 1.30}
$$

$$
\Delta T_g = \frac{30{,}000}{32{,}500}
$$

$$
\Delta T_g = 0.923 \; \text{K} \approx 0.9 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Result:** T_g rises from 147 °C → ≈ 148 °C — this matches the experimentally observed small but consistent shift at low ionic content. At higher loadings (X_ion > 0.06) where clusters dominate, the Eisenberg-modified equation gives larger shifts of 5–15 °C.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shelf-Life of a Fluoropolymer-Packaged Food Product

**Problem:** A ready-meal is packaged in a **PCTFE film of thickness L = 100 µm**. The oxygen permeability coefficient P_O₂ = 1.5 × 10⁻¹² cm³·cm/cm²·s·cmHg (≈ 0.15 Barrer). The package headspace has initial O₂ volume = 20 cm³ at partial pressure 0.21 atm. The food spoils when headspace O₂ exceeds 5 cm³. Find shelf life.

### Step 1: Convert permeability to convenient units
P = 1.5 × 10⁻¹² cm³·cm/cm²·s·cmHg × (24 × 3600 s/day) × (10⁻² m²/10⁴ cm²) × (100 µm/10⁻⁴ m conversion × appropriate scale factor)...

For KTU, use the **direct engineering formula**:

$$
J = \frac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}
$$

Where $\Delta p$ is partial pressure difference, $L$ is film thickness.

### Step 2: State mass balance on headspace oxygen

$$
\frac{dV_{O_2}}{dt} = J \cdot A = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot (p_{\text{out}} - p_{\text{in}})}{L}
$$

But here $p_{\text{out}} - p_{\text{in}}$ is **constant** = 0.21 atm (infinite outside reservoir).

### Step 3: Use ideal-gas relation
Volume of O₂ in headspace:

$$
V_{O_2} = \frac{n_{O_2} \cdot RT}{p}
$$

So $V_{O_2}$ is **constant** — but here the problem assumes the package has a *fixed gas volume* and the spoilage criterion is **O₂ reaching a threshold partial pressure**.

Let us redefine: spoilage when **partial pressure** of O₂ in headspace reaches 0.05 atm (5%).

$$
p_{O_2}(t) = p_{O_2}(0) + \frac{P \cdot A}{V_{\text{head}} \cdot L} \cdot p_{\text{atm}} \cdot t
$$

### Step 4: Solve for shelf life t

$$
\boxed{t_{\text{shelf}} = \frac{V_{\text{head}} \cdot L}{P \cdot A \cdot p_{\text{atm}}} \cdot \left(p_{O_2}^{\text{spoil}} - p_{O_2}^{0}\right)}
$$

### Step 5: Numerical plug-in (A = 200 cm², V_head = 500 cm³, L = 0.01 cm, p_atm = 1 atm)

$P$ in normalized form: convert 0.15 Barrer → 1.5 × 10⁻¹² cm³·cm/cm²·s·cmHg × 76 cmHg/atm × 1 atm = ...

For KTU, use: **P = 1.5 × 10⁻¹⁰ cm³·cm/(cm²·s·atm)**

$$
t_{\text{shelf}} = \frac{(500) \cdot (0.01)}{(1.5 \times 10^{-10}) \cdot (200) \cdot (1)} \cdot (0.05 - 0.21)
$$

$$
t_{\text{shelf}} = \frac{5.0}{3.0 \times 10^{-8}} \cdot (-0.16)
$$

Taking magnitude and noting that O₂ ingress **increases** headspace O₂, so the equation sign is corrected:

$$
t_{\text{shelf}} = \frac{5.0}{3.0 \times 10^{-8}} \cdot 0.16
$$

$$
t_{\text{shelf}} = \frac{0.8}{3.0 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
t_{\text{shelf}} = 2.67 \times 10^{7} \; \text{s}
$$

Convert to days:

$$
t_{\text{shelf}} = \frac{2.67 \times 10^{7}}{86{,}400} \approx 309 \; \text{days}
$$

**Result:** Shelf life ≈ **310 days (~ 10 months)** — this is why PCTFE blister films are used for long-shelf-life pharma and military rations.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Multiplet-Cluster Threshold for a Sulfonated PC Ionomer

**Question:** At what ionic content does clustering begin in a sulfonated BPA-PC ionomer?

### Step 1: Define cluster threshold
The Eisenberg–Hird–Moore criterion for clustering onset is:

$$
X_{\text{crit}} = \frac{N_{\text{multiplet}}^{\max} \cdot \rho_{\text{polymer}}}{M_0}
$$

For BPA-PC, $M_0$ = 254.3 g/mol (repeat unit), $\rho$ = 1.20 g/cm³. Empirically, $N_{\text{multiplet}}^{\max}$ ≈ 6 ion pairs per multiplet.

### Step 2: Use empirical observation
For polycarbonate-based ionomers, $X_{\text{crit}}$ ≈ **0.05–0.06 mol fraction**. Below this, only **multiplets** form; above it, **clusters** form.

### Step 3: Derive the *minimum ionic content* for a packaging-effective ionomer

Packaging-relevant property jumps (50% reduction in OTR, doubling of melt strength) occur only when **clusters** form. Therefore:

$$
X_{\text{ion}}^{\text{min, packaging}} \geq 0.05
$$

For a 1-kg batch of BPA-PC ionomer with $X_{\text{ion}}$ = 0.05, mass of sulfonation agent (e.g., SO₃·pyridine complex) required:

$$
m_{\text{SO}_3} = X_{\text{ion}} \cdot m_{\text{PC}} \cdot \frac{M_{\text{SO}_3}}{M_0}
$$

$$
m_{\text{SO}_3} = 0.05 \cdot 1000 \cdot \frac{80.06}{254.3}
$$

$$
m_{\text{SO}_3} = 0.05 \cdot 1000 \cdot 0.3149
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{\text{SO}_3} \approx 15.7 \; \text{g per kg of PC}}
$$

**Logic:** This is the minimum sulfonation level for the ionomer to deliver packaging-grade barrier enhancement — a key design parameter for an industrial formulator.

**Reading the diagram:** Each orange node is a **sulfonated ionic group**. Solid lines show the polymer backbone. The orange arrows show **Coulombic attractions** between Na⁺ sulfonate groups on neighboring chains, forming a **multiplet** (a tight ion-pair aggregate). When enough such multiplets gather (X_ion > 0.05), the entire local region becomes a **cluster** that physically crosslinks the chains.
