# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Define the term "ionomer" with reference to polycarbonate-based packaging materials. List two key advantages that PC ionomers offer over neat BPA-PC for rigid food packaging.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> An **ionomer** is a polymer containing a small amount (typically < 15 mol%) of **ionic groups** covalently bonded to a non-ionic polymer backbone. In polycarbonate-based ionomers, sulfonate or carboxylate groups are introduced onto the aromatic rings of BPA-PC and neutralized with metal cations (Na⁺, Zn²⁺).

**Two advantages over neat BPA-PC for rigid food packaging:**
1. **Higher melt strength and thermoformability** — ionic clusters act as reversible physical crosslinks that resist sag during deep-draw thermoforming of rigid trays (e.g., for ready meals and MAP trays).
2. **Better oxygen and moisture barrier** — the ionic clusters reduce free volume, lowering OTR from ≈ 250 to ≈ 50 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹, extending shelf life of oxygen-sensitive foods.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Name the "Big Six" commercial fluoropolymers and state the key property that makes PCTFE the preferred material for pharmaceutical blister packaging.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Polymer | Abbreviation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Polytetrafluoroethylene | PTFE |
| 2 | Fluorinated Ethylene Propylene | FEP |
| 3 | Perfluoroalkoxy | PFA |
| 4 | Ethylene Tetrafluoroethylene | ETFE |
| 5 | Polychlorotrifluoroethylene | PCTFE |
| 6 | Polyvinylidene Fluoride | PVDF |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key property of PCTFE for pharma blister packaging:**
> PCTFE has the **lowest water-vapor transmission rate (WVTR) of any transparent thermoplastic film** — approximately **0.05 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹** at 25 µm thickness. This makes it the **gold-standard moisture barrier** for blister packaging of hygroscopic tablets (e.g., effervescents, antibiotics, vitamins), extending shelf life from months to **2–5 years**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, Part B questions carry **internal choice** between two alternatives. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are independent alternatives — students answer either one.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain with suitable diagrams the **Eisenberg Multiplet–Cluster model** for the morphology of a sulfonated polycarbonate ionomer. Discuss how this morphology influences the **oxygen barrier** and **melt strength** of the polymer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A BPA-PC ionomer is sulfonated to 5 mol% SO₃H and neutralized with NaOH. The neat BPA-PC has $\bar{M}_n$ = 25 000 g/mol and T_g = 147 °C. Using the Fox–Flory relation with $K$ = 1.0 × 10⁵ K·g/mol and assuming an effective aggregation number $n_{ag}$ = 6, estimate the **shift in T_g** of the ionomer relative to neat PC. Comment on whether this is sufficient for packaging applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The **Eisenberg Multiplet–Cluster Model** (1970, refined) describes the morphology of ionomers as follows:

**Step 1 — Ion pairing (1 mark):**
When ionic groups (e.g., –SO₃⁻Na⁺) are introduced onto the polycarbonate backbone, the cations and anions on neighbouring chains attract each other through **Coulombic forces**. This forms an **ion pair**.

**Step 2 — Multiplet formation (2 marks):**
Multiple ion pairs (typically **4 to 8**) aggregate into a tight, glassy, anhydrous aggregate called a **multiplet**. Multiplets are about 0.5 nm in size and act as **physical crosslinks** between polymer chains.

**Step 3 — Cluster formation (2 marks):**
When the ionic content exceeds a critical value (**X_crit ≈ 0.05–0.06**), the multiplets organize into larger, **microphase-separated clusters** (2–10 nm) that are also glassy at room temperature. The clusters reduce the polymer's **free volume** and **segmental mobility** in their vicinity.

**Step 4 — Effect on properties (2 marks):**

- **Oxygen barrier:** The ionic clusters occupy free volume and reduce polymer chain mobility, lowering the **diffusion coefficient D** of O₂. Combined with the polar nature of the ionic regions lowering the **solubility S**, the permeability **P = D·S** drops dramatically (e.g., OTR from 250 → 50 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹).
- **Melt strength:** The reversible ionic crosslinks resist chain disentanglement under melt-state stress, allowing deep-draw thermoforming of rigid trays without sag.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Apply modified Fox–Flory (3 marks):**

$$
\Delta T_g = \frac{K \cdot X_{\text{ion}} \cdot n_{\text{ag}}}{\bar{M}_n \left(1 + X_{\text{ion}} \cdot n_{\text{ag}}\right)}
$$

Substitute: $K$ = 1.0 × 10⁵, $X_{\text{ion}}$ = 0.05, $n_{\text{ag}}$ = 6, $\bar{M}_n$ = 25 000:

$$
\Delta T_g = \frac{(1.0 \times 10^5)(0.05)(6)}{(25{,}000)(1 + 0.30)} = \frac{30{,}000}{32{,}500} \approx 0.92 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Comment on packaging relevance (2 marks):**

A T_g shift of less than 1 °C is **insufficient** by itself to drive the major packaging-property gains. The experimental literature shows that the **real T_g rise** of a 5 mol% SPC ionomer is closer to **5–10 °C** because the simple Fox–Flory model underestimates the **restrictive effect of full cluster formation** (which dominates above X_crit = 0.05). For packaging applications, the larger barrier and melt-strength improvements come from **cluster formation**, not just from chain-end reduction.

**Step 3 — Conclusion (2 marks):**

Therefore a 5 mol% sulfonation level is **above the cluster threshold** and is **commercially adequate** for rigid MAP food trays and antistatic electronics packaging. At $\bar{M}_n$ = 25 000 the polymer can still be melt-processed without degradation, making it a practical packaging-grade PC ionomer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Classify fluoropolymers with suitable examples. Explain why the **C–F bond and helical chain conformation** make PTFE the lowest-surface-energy commodity plastic. State **three specific packaging applications** where fluoropolymers are irreplaceable. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A moisture-sensitive effervescent tablet is blister-packed in a 25 µm PCTFE film. The permeation flux of water vapour through the film at 25 °C / 75% RH is $J$ = 0.4 g/m²·day. The tablet contains 50 mg of water-reactive ingredient and absorbs 2.0 mg water before losing potency. The blister cavity has internal surface area $A$ = 4 cm². Estimate the **shelf life** of the tablet. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Classification of fluoropolymers (3 marks):**

| Class | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Perfluoropolymers | All H replaced by F (or F + ether O) | PTFE, FEP, PFA |
| Partially fluorinated | Some H retained | ETFE, PVDF |
| Chlorofluoropolymers | F + Cl substituents | PCTFE, ECTFE |

**Step 2 — Why PTFE has the lowest surface energy (2 marks):**

- The **C–F bond (485 kJ/mol)** is the strongest single bond in organic chemistry. The fluorine atoms are **tightly held** and present a **low-energy, non-polar surface** to the outside world.
- The (CF₂)ₙ backbone adopts a **twisted helical ribbon** in which the fluorine atoms **completely sheath** the C–C backbone. The surface is therefore a **dense, smooth, electronegative F-lattice** with **no polar groups exposed**.
- Result: surface energy ≈ **18–20 mJ/m²**, the **lowest of all solid polymers**, making PTFE the *perfect release liner* in packaging lamination.

**Step 3 — Three irreplaceable packaging applications (2 marks):**

1. **High-temperature sterilization pouches** (PFA/FEP) — withstand ≥ 160 °C autoclave cycles for sterile medical device packaging.
2. **Pharmaceutical blister films** (PCTFE/Aclar) — best-in-class moisture barrier for hygroscopic drugs.
3. **Aggressive-chemical container liners** (ECTFE/PTFE) — for packaging industrial reagents that would attack any other plastic.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Convert flux to per-blister rate (3 marks):**

$$
J = 0.4 \; \text{g/m}^2 \cdot \text{day}
$$

Convert blister area $A$ = 4 cm² = 4 × 10⁻⁴ m²:

$$
\text{Water ingress rate} = J \cdot A = 0.4 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 1.6 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{g/day}
$$

This is **0.16 mg/day** per blister.

**Step 2 — Compute time to reach critical water uptake (3 marks):**

Tablet fails after absorbing $m_{\text{crit}}$ = 2.0 mg water:

$$
t_{\text{shelf}} = \frac{m_{\text{crit}}}{\text{ingress rate}} = \frac{2.0 \; \text{mg}}{0.16 \; \text{mg/day}} = 12.5 \; \text{days}
$$

**Step 3 — Final answer with packaging interpretation (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{t_{\text{shelf}} \approx 12.5 \; \text{days}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students forget to **convert cm² → m²** when applying flux values given in g/m²·day — that single unit-error loses **2 marks** instantly.
> - Some students confuse **WVTR with OTR**; the question is about *water vapor*, so use $J$ for water, not oxygen.
> - A common mistake is to use the **wrong permeation direction** (concentration gradient sign); remember flux is **into** the package.

This extremely short shelf life (12 days) explains why, in real-world pharma packaging, **barrier films are typically multilayer laminates** (e.g., PVC / PCTFE / PE) that reduce the effective flux by an order of magnitude, extending shelf life to **2–5 years**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 2 — PC Ionomers & Fluoropolymers**

### PC Ionomers
- ✅ **Definition:** Polymer with < 15 mol% ionic groups covalently attached to a non-ionic backbone.
- ✅ **Base polymer for PC ionomers:** bisphenol-A polycarbonate.
- ✅ **Common ionic groups:** –SO₃⁻, –COO⁻, –N⁺(R)₃.
- ✅ **Multiplet:** 4–8 ion pairs (≈ 0.5 nm); **Cluster:** 2–10 nm, formed when X_ion > 0.05.
- ✅ **Eisenberg Multiplet–Cluster Model** is the canonical structural model.
- ✅ **T_g shift direction:** upward (typically 5–15 °C for 5–7 mol% sulfonation).
- ✅ **Counter-ion strength order (T_g rise):** Li⁺ > Na⁺ > K⁺ > Zn²⁺.
- ✅ **Packaging advantages:** ↑ melt strength, ↓ OTR, ↓ WVTR, ↓ surface resistivity (antistatic).
- ✅ **Critical loading for packaging effect:** X_ion ≥ 0.05.
- ✅ **Fox–Flory equation:** $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - K / \bar{M}_n$.
- ✅ **Sulfonation reagent for PC:** SO₃·pyridine complex, ≈ 15.7 g per kg PC for 5 mol%.

### Fluoropolymers
- ✅ **Definition:** Polymers with F replacing H on the C–C backbone.
- ✅ **Big Six:** PTFE, FEP, PFA, ETFE, PVDF, PCTFE (plus ECTFE).
- ✅ **C–F bond energy:** 485 kJ/mol (strongest single bond in organic chemistry).
- ✅ **PTFE melting point:** 327 °C (cannot be melt-processed, must be sintered).
- ✅ **PTFE surface energy:** ≈ 18–20 mJ/m² (lowest of all polymers).
- ✅ **PCTFE WVTR:** best in class — ≈ 0.05 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹.
- ✅ **PVDF T_m:** 177 °C; **PVDF T_g:** –40 °C.
- ✅ **Permeability equation:** $P = D \cdot S$ (solution–diffusion model).
- ✅ **Permeation flux:** $J = P \cdot \Delta p / L$.
- ✅ **Trade-name to remember:** **Aclar®** = PCTFE film (Honeywell).
- ✅ **Helical chain in PTFE** = reason for extremely low free volume.
- ✅ **Pharma blister films** = PVC / PVDC + PCTFE lamination for ultra-high moisture barrier.
- ✅ **Perfluoropolymers vs partially fluorinated:** perfluoro = all H → F; partially = some H retained, hence melt-processable.
- ✅ **Why fluoropolymers are non-stick:** low surface energy (no polar groups exposed).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conversion Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Conversion Process # MODULE 2 — CONVERSION PROCESS IN PLASTICS PACKAGING ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Process (Plastics Packaging):** > The **Conversion Process** refers to the sequence of **primary and secondary manufacturing operations** by which **raw polymeric resins** (in the form of granules, pellets, powders, or liquids) are **transformed into finished, functional, and marketable packaging articles** such as films, sheets, bottles, containers, trays, tubes, laminates, and closures. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, conversion processes are classified as either **primary conversion processes** (extrusion, injection molding, blow molding, thermoforming, calendering, compression molding) or **secondary conversion processes** (printing, laminating, coating, slitting, sealing, and bag-making). The **end-to-end value chain** of plastics packaging is: $$\text{Polymer Resin} \xrightarrow{\text{Melt Processing}} \text{Intermediate (Film/Sheet/Pref.)} \xrightarrow{\text{Secondary Ops.}} \text{Final Package}$$ ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **"The Bakery Analogy" for Conversion Processes:** > Think of plastics conversion exactly like a **modern bakery**. The **flour (polymer resin)** is the raw ingredient. To make **bread (a film)**, you push the dough through a **nozzle (die)** — this is **extrusion**. To make **cupcakes (molded parts)**, you pour batter into a **shaped cavity (mold)** — this is **injection molding**. To make a **balloon-shaped cookie (a hollow bottle)**, you inflate soft dough inside a closed mold — this is **blow molding**. Just as a bakery uses different techniques to make different end products, plastics converters choose a conversion process based on the **shape, thickness, production volume, and barrier requirements** of the final package. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Conversion (Engineering Units) | Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Significance | |---|---|---|---| | **Melt Temperature** | $T_m$ | **160 – 320 °C** | Determines polymer flow | | **Melt Pressure** | $P$ | **50 – 1500 bar** | Drives flow through die | | **Shear Rate** | $\dot{\gamma}$ | **100 – 10 000 s⁻¹** | Affects viscosity & orientation | | **Throughput** | $Q$ | **10 – 2000 kg/h** | Mass output rate | | **Specific Energy** | $E_{sp}$ | **0.2 – 0.6 kWh/kg** | Energy efficiency metric | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** > Students must be able to **identify, compare, and justify** the selection of a particular conversion process for a given packaging application (e.g., why **blown film extrusion** is preferred over **cast film extrusion** for producing HDPE grocery bags). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Conversion Processes > [!IMPORTANT] > **Primary Conversion Processes** *form the geometry* of the package. > **Secondary Conversion Processes** *add functionality* (barrier, print, seal strength). ### 2.1.1 Extrusion (Film & Sheet) **Principle:** Polymer melt is continuously forced through a **shaping die** by a rotating screw. **Sub-types used in packaging:** | Sub-type | Output Product | Application | |---|---|---| | **Blown Film Extrusion** | Tubular film | PE bags, shrink film | | **Cast Film Extrusion** | Flat film | Biaxially oriented PP (BOPP) | | **Sheet Extrusion** | Flat sheet (>0.25 mm) | Thermoforming feedstock | | **Co-extrusion** | Multi-layer film/sheet | Barrier packaging | ### 2.1.2 Injection Molding Used for **closures, tubs, lids, and rigid containers**. Molten polymer is injected under high pressure into a closed, cooled mold cavity. ### 2.1.3 Blow Molding Used for **hollow containers** (bottles, jerry cans). - **Extrusion Blow Molding (EBM)** — parison extruded, then inflated. - **Injection Blow Molding (IBM)** — preform injection-molded, then blown. - **Stretch Blow Molding (SBM)** — biaxial orientation for PET bottles. ### 2.1.4 Thermoforming A **plastic sheet** is heated to its **forming temperature**, then shaped over a mold using **vacuum, pressure, or mechanical force**. ### 2.1.5 Secondary Processes Printing (flexography, gravure, digital), **lamination** (adhesive/extrusion), coating (PVDC, EVOH, AlOx), and bag-making. ## 2.2 Why Each Step Works — Operational Logic 1. **Heat input** lowers viscosity below $10^4$ Pa·s so the polymer flows. 2. **Pressure input** provides the driving force for flow through the die/mold. 3. **Cooling/solidification** locks in the geometry and orientation. 4. **Secondary operations** add the required **marketing, barrier, and sealing** functionalities. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following equations are high-yield for KTU University Exams (Module 2).** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^3 N}{2} \cdot \sin\phi \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \dfrac{W}{2} - \dfrac{W h^3 \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$ | **Screw output** (forward drag minus back-pressure leakage) | m³/s | | 2 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\pi D N}{h}$ | **Apparent shear rate in screw channel** | s⁻¹ | | 3 | $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | **Power-law viscosity model** (Ostwald-de Waele) | Pa·s | | 4 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \eta Q L}{W h^3}$ | **Pressure drop in rectangular channel** | Pa | | 5 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | **Average velocity through die** | m/s | | 6 | $\text{BUR} = \dfrac{D_f}{D_d}$ | **Blow-Up Ratio (blown film)** | dimensionless | | 7 | $\text{DDR} = \dfrac{v_{haul}}{v_{melt}}$ | **Draw-Down Ratio** | dimensionless | | 8 | $t_{film} = \dfrac{m}{\pi D_f L \rho}$ | **Film thickness from throughput** | m | | 9 | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{P_{motor}}{Q \rho}$ | **Specific Energy Consumption** | J/kg | | 10 | $P_{cool} = m c_p (T_{melt} - T_{mold})$ | **Heat to be removed in cooling** | W | > [!NOTE] > **Where:** > $D$ = screw diameter, $N$ = screw speed (rev/s), $\phi$ = helix angle, $W$ = channel width, $h$ = channel depth, $L$ = channel length, $\eta$ = melt viscosity, $\rho$ = polymer density, $K$ = consistency index, $n$ = power-law index, $D_f$ = bubble (frost-line) diameter, $D_d$ = die diameter. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Process | Real-World Packaging Use | |---|---| | Blown Film | Milk pouches, garbage bags, agricultural mulch films | | Cast Film | BOPP snack wrappers, multilayer CPP laminates | | Injection Molding | Bottle caps (PP/HDPE), ice-cream tubs | | Stretch Blow Molding | PET water & soft-drink bottles | | Thermoforming | PS cups, PET clamshells, blister packs | | Co-extrusion Lamination | Tetra-Pak-style multilayer aseptic cartons | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Throughput of a Single-Screw Extruder We model the extruder screw channel as a **rectangular channel** of width $W$, depth $h$, and length $L$, in which the barrel surface moves relative to the screw at velocity $V_x = \pi D N \cos\phi$ (drag flow), while the pressure flow opposes it. **Step 1 — Drag flow component (Newtonian fluid between two parallel plates, top plate moving):** $$\begin{aligned} Q_d &= \frac{V_x \cdot W \cdot h}{2} \\[4pt] &= \frac{\pi D N \cos\phi \cdot W \cdot h}{2} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Pressure flow component (Poiseuille flow, opposite direction):** $$\begin{aligned} Q_p &= -\frac{W h^3 \Delta P}{12 \eta L} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Net throughput (sum of drag and pressure flows, with helix-angle geometry factor):** $$\begin{aligned} Q &= \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} \;-\; \frac{W h^3 \Delta P}{12 \eta L} \end{aligned}$$ This is the **standard textbook expression** used by KTU examiners. The first term dominates when the die is **wide open** ($\Delta P \approx 0$). ## 3.2 Derivation: Film Thickness in Blown Film Extrusion **Given data:** - Mass throughput: $\dot{m}$ (kg/s) - Polymer density: $\rho$ (kg/m³) - Frost-line bubble diameter: $D_f$ (m) - Lay-flat width: $W_{flat}$ (m), with $W_{flat} = \pi D_f / 2$ - Haul-off speed: $v$ (m/s) **Step 1 — Volume throughput:** $$\begin{aligned} \dot{V} &= \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Cross-sectional area of the tubular film at the haul-off:** $$\begin{aligned} A &= \pi D_f \cdot t_{film} \end{aligned}$$ where $t_{film}$ is the film thickness (m), assuming a thin wall. **Step 3 — Equate volume flow to (Area × velocity):** $$\begin{aligned} \dot{V} &= A \cdot v = \pi D_f \cdot t_{film} \cdot v \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for film thickness:** $$\begin{aligned} t_{film} &= \frac{\dot{m}}{\pi D_f \, v \, \rho} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Express in terms of Blow-Up Ratio (BUR) and Draw-Down Ratio (DDR):** $$\begin{aligned} t_{film} &= \frac{\dot{m}}{\pi D_d \, \text{BUR} \, v \, \rho} \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Specific Energy Consumption **Step 1 — Motor power input:** $$\begin{aligned} P_{motor} &= \sqrt{3}\, V_{line} \, I_{line} \, \cos\theta \quad \text{(3-phase AC)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Mass output rate:** $$\begin{aligned} \dot{m} &= Q \rho \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Specific energy:** $$\begin{aligned} E_{sp} &= \frac{P_{motor}}{\dot{m}} = \frac{P_{motor}}{Q \rho} \end{aligned}$$ Typical target for an **efficient extrusion line**: $E_{sp} \le 0.35$ kWh/kg. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection for Packaging | Packaging Requirement | Recommended Process | Justification | |---|---|---| | Transparent thin film, high gloss | Cast Film | Chilled chill-roll gives high clarity | | Strong, tough grocery bag | Blown Film (HDPE) | Balanced biaxial orientation | | Rigid transparent PET bottle | Stretch Blow Molding | Biaxial orientation → strength + clarity | | Yogurt cup (PS) | Thermoforming | Economical for thin-wall rigid parts | | Multilayer barrier pouch | Co-extrusion + Lamination | Combines PE/PA/EVOH layers | | Bottle cap | Injection Molding | High dimensional accuracy, fast cycle | > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Logic Tip:** KTU evaluators expect you to **justify** the process selection by referring to **at least two of**: production volume, wall thickness uniformity, material wastage, barrier requirements, and unit cost. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Generic Plastics Conversion Line ```mermaid flowchart TD resin[Resin Hopper
LDPE HDPE PP PET PS] --> dryer[Drying Unit
80 to 120 C] dryer --> extr[Extruder
Single or Twin Screw] extr --> melt[Melt Pump
and Filter] melt --> die[Shaping Die
Film Sheet or Profile] die --> cool[Cooling Section
Air Water Chill Roll] cool --> hau[Haul Off and Winder] hau --> sec[Secondary Conversion
Printing Lamination Slitting] sec --> pkg[Finished Packaging
Roll Pouch Bottle Tray] ``` ## 4.2 Sub-Block — Blown Film Extrusion Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR E1[Hopper] --> E2[Heated Barrel
3 to 5 Zones] E2 --> E3[Screw
L D 25 to 30] E3 --> E4[Die
Spider or Spiral] E4 --> E5[Air Ring
Cooling] E5 --> E6[Bubble
Frost Line] E6 --> E7[Collapsing Frame
and Nip Rolls] E7 --> E8[Winder
Surface or Center] ``` ## 4.3 Sub-Block — Injection Blow Molding Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD I1[Inject Preform] --> I2[Transfer to Blow Station] I2 --> I3[Stretch Rod Axial Stretch] I3 --> I4[High Pressure Air Blow] I4 --> I5[Cooled Bottle Ejection] ``` ## 4.4 Decision Matrix — Choosing the Conversion Process ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Product is Hollow} -->|Yes| Q2{Volume > 500 mL} Q2 -->|Yes| SBM[Stretch Blow Molding PET] Q2 -->|No| IBM[Injection Blow Molding] Q1 -->|No| Q3{Product is Flat and Thin} Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Continuous or Sheet} Q4 -->|Continuous| BF[Blown Film] Q4 -->|Sheet Feed| TF[Thermoforming] Q3 -->|No| IM[Injection Molding] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Mermaid Rendering:** Node identifiers are alphanumeric and free of reserved keywords. Labels are kept clean (uppercase alphanumerics) for clarity in printed notes. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]` **Differentiate between extrusion blow molding and injection blow molding. Mention any three packaging applications of each.** **Model Answer (key points):** | Parameter | Extrusion Blow Molding (EBM) | Injection Blow Molding (IBM) | |---|---|---| | Parison formation | Extruded continuously | Injection-molded as a discrete preform | | Material wastage | Higher (flash, trim) | Very low (no flash) | | Neck finish accuracy | Moderate | Very high | | Suitable volumes | 50 mL – 200 L | 5 mL – 2 L | | Typical packaging | Jerry cans, drums, wide-mouth HDPE bottles | Small pharma bottles, PET personal-care bottles | | Cycle time | Slower | Faster | *(Any 3 well-explained points = 3 marks)* ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]` **Define "Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)" and "Draw-Down Ratio (DDR)" in blown film extrusion. State their typical values for LDPE packaging film.** **Model Answer:** - **BUR** = (Frost-line bubble diameter) / (Die diameter). Typical LDPE value: **2.0 – 4.0**. - **DDR** = (Haul-off velocity) / (Melt velocity at die exit). Typical LDPE value: **5 – 15**. *(Definition 1.5 marks each + typical value = 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the **blown film extrusion process** for manufacturing LDPE packaging film. Define and state the role of **BUR and DDR**. **Model Solution:** 1. **Resin feeding:** LDPE pellets enter the hopper; in some setups, a **gravimetric feeder** controls mass flow rate. *(1 mark)* 2. **Melting & conveying:** Inside the heated barrel, a rotating screw (L/D 25–30) conveys, compresses, and melts the polymer. Typical **melt temperature** for LDPE: **160 – 200 °C**. *(1 mark)* 3. **Metering & filtration:** A **screen pack** removes contaminants; the melt pump stabilises pressure. *(1 mark)* 4. **Die:** The melt exits through an **annular die** (spiral mandrel design), forming a tube. *(1 mark)* 5. **Bubble inflation:** Compressed air is introduced through the die centre; the **bubble** is drawn upward and cooled by an **air ring**. The frost line is the point where the film solidifies. *(1 mark)* 6. **Collapsing & winding:** The bubble is flattened by **collapsing frames** and **nip rolls**, then wound on a roll. *(1 mark)* 7. **BUR & DDR:** - $\text{BUR} = D_f / D_d$, controls **transverse orientation**, typically 2 – 4 for LDPE. - $\text{DDR} = v_{haul} / v_{melt}$, controls **machine-direction orientation**, typically 5 – 15. - Higher BUR + DDR → better mechanical properties, but reduced thickness. *(1 mark)* **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A blown film line produces LDPE film at a throughput of $\dot{m} = 50$ kg/h. The **polymer density** is $\rho = 920$ kg/m³, the **die diameter** is $D_d = 100$ mm, the **BUR** = 3.0, and the **haul-off speed** $v = 30$ m/min. Calculate the **film thickness** in micrometres (µm). **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert units to SI:** $$\begin{aligned} \dot{m} &= 50 \,\tfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{h}} = \frac{50}{3600} = 0.01389 \,\tfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} \\ D_d &= 100 \,\text{mm} = 0.100 \,\text{m} \\ D_f &= \text{BUR} \times D_d = 3.0 \times 0.100 = 0.300 \,\text{m} \\ v &= 30 \,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{min}} = 0.500 \,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the film-thickness formula:** $$\begin{aligned} t_{film} &= \frac{\dot{m}}{\pi D_f \, v \, \rho} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$\begin{aligned} t_{film} &= \frac{0.01389}{\pi \times 0.300 \times 0.500 \times 920} \\[4pt] &= \frac{0.01389}{433.54} \\[4pt] &= 3.20 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{m} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to µm:** $$\begin{aligned} t_{film} &= 3.20 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^{6} = 32 \,\mu\text{m} \end{aligned}$$ **Final Answer:** $t_{film} \approx \mathbf{32 \,\mu m}$ (reasonable for a thin LDPE packaging film). *(Full marks for clear steps + correct numerical answer.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compare **injection molding**, **extrusion**, and **thermoforming** as plastics packaging conversion processes. Tabulate your answer covering — *principle, typical product, cycle nature, and material wastage.* **Model Answer (Tabular Comparison):** | Feature | Injection Molding | Extrusion | Thermoforming | |---|---|---|---| | Principle | Melt injected into closed mold under pressure | Melt continuously pushed through a die | Heated sheet deformed by vacuum/pressure over mold | | Typical product | Caps, tubs, rigid containers | Films, sheets, pipes | Cups, trays, blister packs | | Cycle nature | **Discontinuous** (cyclic) | **Continuous** | **Discontinuous** | | Material wastage | Low (runner & sprue) | Very low (in-line trim) | High (trim around 30–50 %) | | Wall thickness control | Excellent | Uniform along extrusion | Variable (thinning at corners) | *(Each correct row ≈ 1.5 marks × 4 rows + principle explanation = 7 marks.)* **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** An injection molding machine produces 200 g PS cups with a **cycle time** of 12 s. The **specific energy consumption** of the machine is 0.45 kWh/kg. Calculate the (i) **hourly production rate** in kg/h and (ii) **hourly electrical energy consumption** in kWh. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert cycle time to cycles per hour:** $$\begin{aligned} N &= \frac{3600 \,\text{s/h}}{12 \,\text{s}} = 300 \,\text{cycles/h} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Hourly mass output:** $$\begin{aligned} \dot{m} &= N \times m_{part} = 300 \times 0.200 = 60 \,\text{kg/h} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Hourly electrical energy consumption:** $$\begin{aligned} E_{hr} &= E_{sp} \times \dot{m} = 0.45 \,\tfrac{\text{kWh}}{\text{kg}} \times 60 \,\text{kg} \\[4pt] &= 27 \,\text{kWh/h} \end{aligned}$$ **Final Answer:** **(i) 60 kg/h, (ii) 27 kWh/h.** *(Each sub-part ≈ 3.5 marks; full marks for clearly shown units.)* --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 2 Conversion Process Questions:** > 1. **Units mismatch** — forgetting to convert kg/h to kg/s, mm to m, or µm to m. (–1 mark minimum) > 2. **Confusing BUR and DDR** — BUR is *transverse*, DDR is *machine direction*. Examiners *will* deduct if swapped. > 3. **Skipping the screw geometry factor** — when asked for extruder throughput, students often write only the drag flow term $\pi D N h W / 2$ and forget the $\sin\phi \cos\phi$ helix correction. > 4. **No process justification** — When asked "why choose X process?", simply naming the process is **not enough**. Always mention *production volume, geometry, and material waste*. > 5. **Neglecting cooling energy** — For thermoforming/injection molding questions, examiners expect a comment on **heat removal** ($P_{cool}$), not just heating. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Conversion Process** - **Conversion Process** = transformation of polymer resin → finished package via primary + secondary operations. - **Primary processes:** extrusion, injection molding, blow molding, thermoforming, calendering, compression molding. - **Secondary processes:** printing (flexo/gravure/digital), lamination (adhesive/extrusion), coating, slitting, bag-making. - **Blown film** uses **BUR (2–4)** and **DDR (5–15)**; film thickness $t = \dot{m} / (\pi D_f v \rho)$. - **Cast film** gives **better clarity and gauge uniformity** than blown film due to **chill-roll quenching**. - **Extrusion throughput** = drag flow − pressure back-flow; governed by $Q = (\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\phi \cos\phi)/2 - (W h^3 \Delta P)/(12 \eta L)$. - **Blow molding variants:** EBM (large hollow containers), IBM (precision small bottles), SBM (PET — biaxial orientation for strength + clarity). - **Injection molding** is **cyclic**, **discontinuous**, and produces **low wastage**; ideal for **closures and rigid tubs**. - **Thermoforming** is **sheet-based**, has **high trim waste (30–50 %)**, but is **economical for thin-wall disposable packaging**. - **Co-extrusion** enables **multi-layer barrier films** (PE/PA/EVOH/PET) in a single step. - **Specific Energy Consumption** ($E_{sp}$): target ≤ **0.35 kWh/kg** for an efficient extrusion line. - **Shear rate** in a screw channel: $\dot{\gamma} = \pi D N / h$ — directly affects viscosity via the **power-law model** $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$. - **Melt temperature windows:** LDPE/HDPE ≈ 160–220 °C; PP ≈ 200–260 °C; PET ≈ 260–290 °C; PS ≈ 180–240 °C. - **Selection criterion hierarchy:** Geometry → Production volume → Material → Cost → Barrier/Optical needs.

topics.title: Compression & Transfer Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Compression & Transfer Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Moulding Processes for Plastics Packaging

In the realm of **plastics packaging technology**, moulding is the foundational manufacturing process by which raw polymeric materials (resins, preforms, sheets) are shaped into definitive packaging articles — bottles, caps, containers, trays, lids, and industrial components. The choice of moulding technique is dictated by the polymer's rheological state, its thermodynamic behaviour (thermoplastic vs. thermoset), the geometry of the package, and the production volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 2):** This module emphasizes the *primary processing techniques* used to convert raw plastics into finished packaging. Compression and Transfer moulding are predominantly employed for **thermosetting plastics, rubber, composites (SMC/BMC), and rigid structural packaging components** that demand high strength, dimensional stability, and heat resistance.

---

## 1.2 Compression Moulding — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** *Compression moulding is a closed-die pressure-forming process in which a measured charge of moulding compound (powder, granules, preform, or sheet) is placed directly into the open cavity of a heated mould, the mould is then closed, and pressure (typically 10–200 MPa) combined with heat (120–200 °C) is applied to force the material to flow, fill the cavity, and cure (crosslink) into a rigid, dimensionally stable part.*

It is the **oldest and simplest** of all plastic moulding techniques, dating back to the early 20th century with the processing of phenolic resins (Bakelite).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Waffle Iron

Imagine a heavy, heated waffle iron:
1. You open the two halves (top and bottom mould).
2. You pour pancake batter (the moulding compound) directly onto the lower plate.
3. You close the iron and clamp it shut — the weight and latch apply pressure.
4. Heat cooks (cures) the batter while pressure forces it into the iron's patterned cavities.
5. After a fixed time, you open the iron and remove the formed waffle.

**Compression moulding operates identically** — except the "batter" is a polymer charge, the "iron" is a precision steel die, and the "cooking" is an irreversible chemical crosslinking (curing) reaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Marker:** Compression moulding is the **process of choice for thermosets** because the polymer must cure *inside* the mould — it cannot be remelted once crosslinked.

---

## 1.3 Transfer Moulding — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** *Transfer moulding is a hybrid closed-mould process in which a pre-measured charge of thermosetting compound is first preheated and plasticized in a separate chamber (the "transfer pot") located above the mould cavity, then forced through a runner-and-gate system into a closed, heated mould cavity under high pressure (50–150 MPa) where final curing takes place.*

It was developed in the **1920s** as an evolutionary improvement over compression moulding to overcome the latter's limitations in producing intricate parts with delicate inserts, fine tolerances, and complex geometries.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Syringe Injector

Think of a medical syringe filled with hot wax:
1. The wax (compound) sits in the syringe barrel (transfer pot) above the closed mould.
2. When you push the plunger, the molten wax is forced through a narrow nozzle (sprue/runner) into a closed cavity (mould) where it cools and solidifies.
3. The cavity is sealed — wax cannot escape back, so the pressure is fully utilized to pack the part densely.

**Transfer moulding works on the same principle** — a hydraulic or mechanical ram (the plunger) drives pre-softened polymer through a narrow channel into a closed, heated mould where it cures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Marker:** Transfer moulding **decouples the plasticizing step from the cavity filling step**, enabling far better control over flow, reduced flash, and the ability to mould parts with delicate metal inserts (e.g., electrical contact pins in a packaging closure).

---

## 1.4 Comparative Snapshot — At a Glance

| Feature | Compression Moulding | Transfer Moulding |
|---|---|---|
| Polymer Type | Thermosets, rubbers, composites | Thermosets (esp. with inserts) |
| Charge Placement | Directly in open cavity | In a separate transfer pot |
| Mould State During Fill | Open → Closing | Fully closed |
| Flash Control | Moderate (depends on clamp force) | Excellent (closed mould) |
| Cycle Time | 1–10 min | 0.5–5 min |
| Insert Moulding | Difficult | Excellent |
| Tooling Cost | **Low** | Moderate–High |
| Material Wastage | **Minimal (no runner)** | 5–15% (runner/scrap) |
| Part Complexity | Simple–Moderate | Moderate–High |

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Compression Moulding Process — Step-by-Step Theory

The compression moulding cycle consists of **five distinct stages**, each governed by heat transfer, polymer rheology, and reaction kinetics.

### Step 1: Preform Preparation (Moulding Charge Preparation)
- The thermoset resin (e.g., phenolic, melamine, urea-formaldehyde, unsaturated polyester) is compounded with fillers (wood flour, calcium carbonate, glass fibre), curing agents, accelerators, lubricants, and pigments.
- The compound is formed into a **preform** — a tablet, pill, slug, or sheet — with mass precisely matched to the cavity volume (typically 5–10% excess to compensate for flash).
- For composites like **Sheet Moulding Compound (SMC)** or **Bulk Moulding Compound (BMC)**, the preform is a pre-impregnated fibre-resin mat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a preform?** A loose powder would entrap air, create voids, and dust hazards. A compacted preform ensures uniform bulk density, predictable flow, and faster loading.

### Step 2: Charge Loading
- The mould opens (vertically in a hydraulic press, horizontally in some presses).
- The preform is placed manually or robotically into the **lower (cavity) half** of the heated mould.
- For multi-cavity moulds, a preform is placed in each cavity.
- Mould surface temperature is maintained at **120–200 °C** depending on the resin system.

### Step 3: Mould Closing & Flow Stage
- The press closes the mould at a controlled velocity (slow approach, then rapid close to minimize cure progression).
- As the mould halves meet, the preform is **compressed** and forced to flow radially outward to fill the cavity.
- The pressure rises sharply as the material flows through constricting clearances.
- This stage is governed by the **viscosity–temperature–time relationship** of the reacting thermoset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "S-Curve" Phenomenon:** A thermoset's viscosity first *decreases* with heat (flow stage), reaches a **minimum**, then *increases* sharply as crosslinking dominates (cure stage). The mould must close during the low-viscosity window.

### Step 4: Curing Stage (Crosslinking)
- Heat activates the curing agent (or catalyst) and crosslinks the polymer chains into a rigid 3-D network.
- Curing is an **exothermic, irreversible chemical reaction**.
- Curing time depends on part thickness (typically **30–60 seconds per mm of wall thickness**), resin chemistry, and mould temperature.
- Pressure is maintained throughout to:
  1. Compress the part to maximum density.
  2. Force out trapped gases and volatiles (water, formaldehyde).
  3. Hold dimensional tolerances.

### Step 5: Mould Opening & Part Ejection
- The mould opens.
- The cured part is lifted out by ejector pins.
- Flash (excess material squeezed between mould halves) is trimmed manually or by a deflashing die.
- The cycle repeats.

---

## 2.2 The Transfer Moulding Process — Step-by-Step Theory

Transfer moulding has **two distinct subsystems**: the **transfer pot** (plasticizing chamber) and the **mould cavity** (curing chamber).

### Step 1: Preheating
- The thermoset charge is preheated (often by **dielectric (HF) heating** or infrared) to reduce viscosity before transfer.
- Preheating cuts cycle time by **30–50%** and improves flow.

### Step 2: Charge Loading into Pot
- The preheated charge is placed into the **transfer pot** (also called "plunger" or "transfer chamber"), which is maintained at **80–120 °C**.

### Step 3: Plunger Actuation (Transfer)
- A hydraulic or mechanical plunger descends, pressurizing the compound.
- The material flows through the **sprue** → **runner** → **gate** into the closed, heated mould cavity.
- Transfer pressure: **50–150 MPa**; transfer speed is critical to avoid pre-cure.

### Step 4: Curing in Closed Cavity
- The mould cavity is at **150–200 °C** (typically hotter than the pot to accelerate cure).
- Material cures under sustained pressure.
- Excess material flows into an **overflow well (flash well)** — this serves as a pressure reservoir and buffer.

### Step 5: Plunger Retraction & Part Ejection
- After cure, the plunger retracts.
- The mould opens.
- The cured part (with attached sprue/runner) is ejected.
- The runner is removed (often a secondary operation).

---

## 2.3 Types of Transfer Moulding

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Pot Transfer** | Pot is integral part of mould; plunger is part of press | Small parts, rubber goods |
| **Plunger Transfer** | Separate plunger; mould is mounted on press platen | Larger parts, electronics |
| **Screw Transfer (Injection-Transfer)** | Screw plasticizes compound, then injects like injection moulding | High-volume thermoset moulding (e.g., electrical connector housings, package closures) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The **screw transfer (injection-transfer)** process is the modern industry standard for high-volume thermoset packaging components — it merges the speed of injection moulding with the material properties of thermosets.

---

## 2.4 Material Systems Used

### Compression Moulding Materials
- **Phenolic resins (PF)** — heat-resistant electrical components, closures
- **Melamine formaldehyde (MF)** — dinnerware, rigid food trays
- **Urea formaldehyde (UF)** — cosmetic caps, electrical parts
- **Unsaturated polyester (UP)** + glass fibre — SMC/BMC for automotive, rigid containers
- **Silicone rubber** — gaskets, seals in packaging
- **Natural & synthetic rubbers** — O-rings, seals
- **Epoxy** — high-strength composites

### Transfer Moulding Materials
- **Epoxy** (with high filler loading) — encapsulating ICs, packaging sealants
- **Phenolic** — electrical connector bodies, terminal blocks
- **Diallyl phthalate (DAP)** — high-precision electrical insulators
- **Silicone** — medical packaging seals

|---|---|---|---|
| Curing Time | $t_c = k \cdot s^2$ | seconds | $s$ = wall thickness, $k$ = resin constant |
| Cure Time per mm (Rule of Thumb) | $t_c \approx 45$–$60$ s/mm | s/mm | For phenolic resin |
| Clamping Force (Compression) | $F_c = P \cdot A_{proj}$ | N (Newtons) | $P$ = moulding pressure, $A_{proj}$ = projected area |
| Moulding Pressure (Phenolic) | $P \approx 10$–$20$ | MPa | Standard phenolic |

| Moulding Pressure (Rubber) | $P \approx 5$–$10$ | MPa | Vulcanization pressure |
| Transfer Pressure | $P_t = 50$–$150$ | MPa | Depends on viscosity and gate size |
| Heat Transfer (Conduction through wall) | $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{x}$ | W (Watts) | $k$ = thermal conductivity, $x$ = wall thickness |
| Volumetric Shrinkage | $S_v = \dfrac{V_{mould} - V_{part}}{V_{mould}} \times 100$ | % | Phenolic: 0.5–1.5%; Polyester: 0.5–3% |
| Part Mass from Preform | $m = \rho \cdot V_{cavity} \cdot (1 + f_{excess})$ | kg | $f_{excess}$ = 0.05–0.10 (flash allowance) |
| Moulding Temperature (Phenolic) | $T \approx 150$–$180$ | °C | Standard thermoset range |
| Production Rate | $R = \dfrac{3600}{t_{cycle}}$ | parts/hour | $t_{cycle}$ in seconds |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Curing Time as a Function of Wall Thickness

**Statement:** For a thermoset undergoing conduction heating from both mould surfaces, the time required for the centre of the part to reach the cure temperature is proportional to the square of the half-thickness.

### Given Data
- Wall thickness of part: $s$
- Half-thickness: $L = s/2$
- Thermal diffusivity of resin: $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_p}$ (m²/s)
- Mould surface temperature: $T_s$
- Initial resin temperature: $T_0$

### Mathematical Model

The transient 1-D heat conduction equation (Fourier's law applied to a slab) is:

$$\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

### Solution — Characteristic Time Scale

The natural time scale of conduction across a half-thickness $L$ is:

$$t_c = \dfrac{L^2}{\alpha}$$

Substituting $L = s/2$:

$$t_c = \dfrac{(s/2)^2}{\alpha} = \dfrac{s^2}{4\alpha}$$

### Final Form

$$\boxed{t_c = \dfrac{s^2}{4\alpha} = \dfrac{\rho c_p \, s^2}{4k}}$$

### Interpretation
- Doubling wall thickness **quadruples** cure time.
- This is why thick phenolic parts take minutes, while thin closures take seconds.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Clamping Force for Compression Moulding Press

**Statement:** The hydraulic press must apply a clamping force equal to the product of the moulding pressure and the projected area of the part (plus any flash lands).

### Step 1 — Force Balance at Peak Pressure

At peak cavity pressure $P_{max}$, the material exerts an upward force on the upper mould half equal to:

$$F_{cavity} = P_{max} \cdot A_{proj}$$

### Step 2 — Equilibrium with Clamp

For the mould to remain closed, the clamp must balance this force plus any friction in the press guides:

$$F_{clamp} \geq F_{cavity}$$

### Step 3 — Substituting Practical Values

For a phenolic tray with projected area $A_{proj} = 250 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.025 \text{ m}^2$ and required moulding pressure $P = 15$ MPa:

$$F_{clamp} = P \cdot A_{proj}$$

$$F_{clamp} = (15 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}) \cdot (0.025 \text{ m}^2)$$

$$F_{clamp} = 375{,}000 \text{ N} = 375 \text{ kN}$$

### Final Form

$$\boxed{F_{clamp} = P \cdot A_{proj}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Preform Mass Calculation

**Statement:** The mass of the moulding charge is the product of cavity volume, material density, and a flash excess factor.

### Step 1 — Cavity Volume

For a flat rectangular tray of length $L$, width $W$, and depth $D$:

$$V_{cavity} = L \cdot W \cdot D$$

### Step 2 — Theoretical Mass (No Flash)

$$m_{theo} = \rho \cdot V_{cavity}$$

### Step 3 — Add Flash Allowance

Flash excess factor $f = 0.05$ (5%):

$$m_{charge} = \rho \cdot V_{cavity} \cdot (1 + f)$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example

For a phenolic tray with $L = 0.20$ m, $W = 0.15$ m, $D = 0.005$ m, $\rho_{phenolic} = 1400$ kg/m³, $f = 0.08$:

$$V_{cavity} = 0.20 \times 0.15 \times 0.005 = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

$$m_{charge} = 1400 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.08$$

$$m_{charge} = 0.2268 \text{ kg} \approx 227 \text{ g}$$

### Final Form

$$\boxed{m_{charge} = \rho \cdot V_{cavity} \cdot (1 + f_{excess})}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Heat Required to Heat the Charge

**Statement:** The total energy required to heat the moulding charge from ambient temperature to mould temperature.

### Step 1 — Sensible Heat

$$Q_{sensible} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{mould} - T_{initial})$$

### Step 2 — Heat of Curing (Reaction Exotherm)

For a thermosetting resin, the crosslinking reaction releases heat:

$$Q_{cure} = m \cdot \Delta H_{cure}$$

### Step 3 — Total Heat

$$Q_{total} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{mould} - T_{initial}) + m \cdot \Delta H_{cure}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example (Phenolic)

Given: $m = 0.227$ kg, $c_p = 1500$ J/(kg·K), $T_{mould} = 170$ °C, $T_{initial} = 25$ °C, $\Delta H_{cure} = 300$ kJ/kg:

$$Q_{sensible} = 0.227 \times 1500 \times (170 - 25) = 0.227 \times 1500 \times 145$$

$$Q_{sensible} = 49{,}372.5 \text{ J} \approx 49.4 \text{ kJ}$$

$$Q_{cure} = 0.227 \times 300{,}000 = 68{,}100 \text{ J} = 68.1 \text{ kJ}$$

$$Q_{total} = 49.4 + 68.1 = 117.5 \text{ kJ}$$

### Final Form

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = m \left[ c_p (T_{mould} - T_{initial}) + \Delta H_{cure} \right]}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Defects in Compression & Transfer Moulding

| Defect | Process | Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flash (excess thin edge)** | Compression | Insufficient charge, low clamp force, worn mould | Increase charge, raise clamp force, refurbish mould |
| **Short shot (incomplete fill)** | Both | Insufficient material, premature cure, low pressure | Increase charge/pressure, lower mould temp initially |
| **Voids & porosity** | Both | Trapped air, volatiles, shrinkage | Pre-heat charge, vent mould, add vacuum |
| **Warping** | Both | Uneven cure, non-uniform cooling, residual stress | Use balanced cooling, longer cure time, use cooling fixtures |
| **Sink marks** | Both | Localized shrinkage, low pressure | Increase hold pressure, optimize gate location |
| **Weld/knit lines** | Transfer | Material front meets in cavity | Raise mould temp, increase injection speed |
| **Burn marks** | Both | Trapped air ignites, excessive heat | Improve venting, lower temperature |
| **Crazing/cracking** | Both | Ejection too early, moulded-in stress | Increase cure time, use ejection assist |
| **Pre-cure in runner (transfer)** | Transfer | Long flow path, slow transfer, hot pot | Speed up transfer, lower pot temp |

---

|---|---|
| Compression moulding | **Open mould → place charge → close → cure** |
| Transfer moulding | **Closed mould → plunger forces material through runner → cure** |

| Overflow well | Pressure reservoir in transfer moulding |
| Dielectric preheating | HF heating of charge to reduce viscosity |
