# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define compression moulding. List any three advantages of compression moulding for thermosetting plastics.**

**Model Answer:**

Compression moulding is a closed-die pressure forming process in which a pre-measured charge of thermosetting moulding compound is placed directly in the cavity of a heated mould, the mould is closed, and pressure combined with heat forces the material to flow, fill the cavity, and cure into a rigid part.

**Three Advantages:**
1. **Low tooling cost** — the mould is simple (one cavity, one force plug) compared to injection or transfer moulds.
2. **Minimal material wastage** — there is no runner or sprue system; only thin flash is generated.
3. **Suitable for large, bulky parts** — e.g., SMC automotive panels, phenolic battery casings, melamine trays.
4. *(Bonus)* Low fibre damage in fibre-reinforced composites compared to injection moulding.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between compression moulding and transfer moulding on the basis of (i) charge placement, (ii) mould condition during filling, and (iii) flash control.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Compression Moulding | Transfer Moulding |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Charge placement | Directly in the open mould cavity | In a separate transfer pot above the mould |
| (ii) Mould condition during filling | Mould closes on the charge; filling occurs as mould closes | Mould is fully closed; material is injected through a runner |
| (iii) Flash control | Moderate; flash forms at the parting line | Excellent; closed mould restricts flash to overflow well |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice Option 1) — **[14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Adapted]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the compression moulding process for thermosetting plastics. Discuss the role of heat and pressure during the cycle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A phenolic resin tray is to be compression moulded. The cavity dimensions are 200 mm × 150 mm × 6 mm. The phenolic density is 1400 kg/m³. The flash excess factor is 8%. Calculate (i) the theoretical mass of the charge, and (ii) the clamping force required if the moulding pressure is 15 MPa. State one assumption.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Compression Moulding Process — Step-by-Step Description:**

1. **Preform Preparation:** Phenolic resin is compounded with fillers (wood flour), curing agent (hexamine), accelerator, and pigment. The compound is preformed into a tablet of mass equal to cavity volume × density × (1 + flash excess).

2. **Mould Loading:** The heated mould (at 150–180 °C) is opened. The preform is placed in the lower cavity.

3. **Mould Closing & Flow:** The upper mould half descends, compressing the preform. Heat lowers resin viscosity, and the pressure (10–20 MPa) forces the softened material to flow outward and fill the cavity.

4. **Curing:** Heat activates the curing agent. Crosslinking proceeds exothermically. Pressure is maintained to:
   - Compress the part to maximum density
   - Expel trapped air and water vapour
   - Hold dimensional accuracy

5. **Opening & Ejection:** After cure time (≈ 45–60 s/mm of wall thickness), the mould opens. The part is ejected by pins. Flash is trimmed.

**Role of Heat:** Supplies activation energy for crosslinking; reduces viscosity for flow.

**Role of Pressure:** Forces material flow, eliminates voids, ensures intimate contact with mould surface for heat transfer, and compensates for shrinkage during cure.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Cavity: $L = 200$ mm $= 0.200$ m
- Width: $W = 150$ mm $= 0.150$ m
- Depth: $D = 6$ mm $= 0.006$ m
- Density: $\rho = 1400$ kg/m³
- Flash excess: $f = 0.08$
- Moulding pressure: $P = 15$ MPa

**(i) Mass of Charge:**

$$V_{cavity} = L \times W \times D = 0.200 \times 0.150 \times 0.006 = 1.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

$$m = \rho \cdot V_{cavity} \cdot (1 + f)$$

$$m = 1400 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.08$$

$$m = 0.2722 \text{ kg} \approx 272 \text{ g}$$

**(ii) Clamping Force:**

$$A_{proj} = L \times W = 0.200 \times 0.150 = 0.030 \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_{clamp} = P \cdot A_{proj} = 15 \times 10^6 \times 0.030$$

$$F_{clamp} = 450{,}000 \text{ N} = 450 \text{ kN}$$

**Assumption:** Steady-state cavity pressure equal to the press hydraulic pressure (no pressure losses in the system).

---

### Question B (Choice Option 2) — **[14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper Adapted]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the transfer moulding process with a labelled diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations over compression moulding.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A transfer moulding setup produces electrical connector bodies from epoxy resin. The transfer pot has diameter 60 mm. The plunger applies a force of 90 kN. Calculate (i) the transfer pressure in the pot, and (ii) the volume of resin required to fill a single cavity of 40 cm³ plus a runner of 8 cm³, given that the epoxy density is 1150 kg/m³. Comment on the difference if the same part were made by compression moulding.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Transfer Moulding Process — Description:**

1. **Preform:** A pre-measured charge of thermoset (epoxy) compound is prepared and preheated by dielectric (HF) heating to reduce viscosity and cycle time.

2. **Loading:** The preheated charge is placed in the **transfer pot** located above the mould.

3. **Plunger Action:** A hydraulic plunger descends, pressurizing the softened resin (50–150 MPa) and forcing it through the **sprue → runner → gate** into the closed, heated mould cavity.

4. **Curing:** The resin cures in the closed cavity (150–180 °C) under sustained pressure. Excess flows into an **overflow well**.

5. **Ejection:** Plunger retracts, mould opens, and the part (with attached sprue) is ejected. The runner is removed in a secondary operation.

**Advantages over Compression Moulding:**
- Better dimensional accuracy and surface finish
- Reduced flash (closed mould)
- Suitable for parts with **delicate metal inserts** (e.g., electrical contacts)
- Lower cure time (hot charge enters cavity)
- Uniform pressure distribution
- Can mould complex geometries with thin sections

**Limitations:**
- Higher tooling cost (pot, plunger, runner system)
- Material wastage in sprue/runner (5–15%)
- Higher complexity in mould design
- Risk of **pre-cure** in the runner if transfer is slow

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Pot diameter: $d = 60$ mm $= 0.060$ m
- Plunger force: $F = 90$ kN $= 90{,}000$ N
- Cavity volume: $V_{cav} = 40 \text{ cm}^3 = 40 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$
- Runner volume: $V_{run} = 8 \text{ cm}^3 = 8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$
- Epoxy density: $\rho = 1150$ kg/m³

**(i) Transfer Pressure:**

$$A_{pot} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (0.060)^2}{4} = 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$P_{transfer} = \dfrac{F}{A_{pot}} = \dfrac{90{,}000}{2.827 \times 10^{-3}} = 31.83 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$P_{transfer} \approx 31.83 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Volume and Mass of Resin:**

$$V_{total} = V_{cav} + V_{run} = 40 + 8 = 48 \text{ cm}^3 = 48 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$

$$m = \rho \cdot V_{total} = 1150 \times 48 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0552 \text{ kg} = 55.2 \text{ g}$$

**Comparison with Compression Moulding:**

If the same part were made by compression moulding, the required charge would be only the cavity volume (40 cm³), giving a mass of:

$$m_{comp} = 1150 \times 40 \times 10^{-6} = 0.046 \text{ kg} = 46 \text{ g}$$

with a small flash allowance (~4 g → 50 g total). Thus, transfer moulding consumes about **10% more material** (in the runner) but yields better dimensional control and supports inserts, justifying the trade-off for high-precision electrical packaging components.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Compression/Transfer Moulding Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the flash excess factor** in mass calculations — students often calculate $m = \rho V$ only, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing transfer pressure with clamping force** — transfer pressure is in the *pot*; clamping force acts on the *cavity*.
> 3. **Wrong units** — clamping force must be in **Newtons (N)**, not kN, unless specified. Pressures must be in **Pa** for SI consistency, or **MPa** in practical form.
> 4. **Skipping the assumption statement** in numericals — KTU examiners *require* a stated assumption for full marks.
> 5. **Drawing transfer moulding without showing the overflow well** — a labelled overflow well is mandatory in the diagram for full marks.
> 6. **Confusing SMC and BMC** — SMC is a *sheet*; BMC is a *dough/bulk*. Both are used in compression moulding.
> 7. **Ignoring pre-cure** in transfer moulding — the time-temperature relationship must be addressed in long answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Compression moulding** is the simplest, oldest, and most economical moulding process for thermosets, rubber, and composites. The charge is placed *directly* in the open cavity, and the closing mould provides both pressure and confinement.

- **Transfer moulding** decouples plasticizing from cavity filling via a transfer pot, enabling moulding of intricate parts, parts with inserts, and parts with thin sections. Its runner system creates a small material wastage overhead.

- **Curing is exothermic and irreversible** for thermosets; cure time scales with the **square of wall thickness** ($t_c \propto s^2$).

- **Clamping force** for compression moulding: $F_{clamp} = P \cdot A_{proj}$.

- **Preform mass** must include a flash excess of **5–10%** to account for material squeezed out at the parting line.

- **SMC** (Sheet Moulding Compound) and **BMC** (Bulk Moulding Compound) are the two key composite forms for compression moulding of fibre-reinforced packaging components.

- **Dielectric (HF) preheating** in transfer moulding reduces viscosity and shortens cycle time by 30–50%.

- **Overflow well** in transfer moulding acts as a pressure reservoir — its presence distinguishes good mould design from poor.

- **S-Curve rheology:** The thermoset's viscosity must be minimum at the moment the mould closes (or when transfer occurs); if the resin cures prematurely, the part will be short-shot or burnt.

- **Heat transfer** in compression moulding is **conduction-dominated** from the heated mould walls — thick parts cure slowly because heat must diffuse to the centre.

- **Moulding pressure ranges:** Phenolic (10–20 MPa), Rubber (5–10 MPa), SMC (3–7 MPa), Transfer (50–150 MPa).

- **Volumetric shrinkage** on cooling: Phenolic 0.5–1.5%, Polyester 0.5–3.0% — the mould cavity must be oversized accordingly.

- **Key defects:** Flash, short shot, voids, warping, sink marks, weld lines, burn marks, pre-cure in runner.

- **Industrial examples to memorize:** Phenolic closures (compression), melamine trays (compression), SMC automotive panels (compression), epoxy encapsulated ICs (transfer), DAP connector bodies (transfer).

- **Modern trend:** Screw-transfer (injection-transfer) moulding combines injection speed with thermoset properties, increasingly used for high-volume packaging components.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Injection Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Injection Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Injection Moulding** is a high-volume, closed-loop, cyclic thermoplastic (and thermoset) conversion process in which a precisely metered quantity of molten polymer is forced under high pressure into a closed, water-cooled (or oil-heated) steel mould cavity, where it solidifies by conduction and convective heat loss, and is subsequently ejected as a dimensionally accurate finished part. It is the single most commercially dominant primary plastics processing technique in the world, accounting for approximately **30 % – 35 %** of all plastics processed globally and being the workhorse of rigid packaging manufacturing (caps, closures, tubs, containers, preforms, thin-wall buckets, and pharmaceutical vials).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim for 2-mark/3-mark answers):**
> "Injection moulding is a manufacturing process for producing parts by injecting molten plastic material into a mould, where it is cooled and solidified into the final part geometry. The process is highly automated, repeatable, and capable of producing complex geometries at high production rates (typically 1,000 to 100,000+ parts per shift)."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Piping Hot Cake Pastry" Model

Imagine a heavy-duty industrial kitchen scenario:

1. **The dough (polymer melt):** A kitchen mixer churns butter and flour into a thick, homogeneous paste — this is your **plasticating screw + heater bands** converting solid resin pellets into a homogeneous viscoelastic melt.
2. **The pastry bag (injection unit + nozzle):** The chef forces the dough out through a narrow conical tip — this is your **hydraulic plunger / screw injection** pushing melt through the **nozzle** at high pressure.
3. **The shaped cake pan (closed mould):** The paste is squeezed into a finely engraved metallic pan — this is your **two-plate or three-plate mould** with the cavity and core.
4. **The refrigerator (cooling channels):** The pan is placed inside a cold chamber so heat escapes through the metal walls — this represents **conformal cooling channels** circulating chilled water at **10 °C – 30 °C**.
5. **The cake pop-out (ejection system):** Once solidified, pins push the cake out of the pan — this is the **ejector pin / sleeve / stripper plate** mechanism.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Process Memory — "P-I-C-C-E":** **P**lastication → **I**njection (Fill) → **C**ompaction (Pack-Hold) → **C**ooling → **E**jection. These five stages define every injection moulding cycle.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Constants (Boxed for Board Exam)

| Metric | Typical Value | Importance |
|---|---|---|
| Injection Pressure | **50 – 200 MPa** (≈ 500 – 2000 bar) | Drives melt into cavity |
| Clamping Force | **50 kN – 12,000 kN** | Resists cavity pressure |
| Melt Temperature | **180 °C – 320 °C** (PE → PEEK) | Determines viscosity |
| Mould Surface Temp. | **20 °C – 120 °C** | Governs cooling & crystallinity |
| Cycle Time | **2 s – 120 s** | Defines throughput |
| Plasticating Rate | **0.5 – 200 kg/h** | Limits part mass / cycle |
| Specific Mould Pressure | **30 – 80 MPa** (PE/PP) | Used in clamp tonnage |
| Screw L/D Ratio | **18:1 – 24:1** | Standard metric |
| Compression Ratio | **2.0:1 – 3.0:1** | Affects melt homogeneity |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Functional Stages of an Injection Moulding Cycle

### Stage 1 — Plastication (Melting + Metering)
- Solid polymer pellets are fed from the **hopper** into the heated barrel through the **feed throat**.
- A rotating **reciprocating screw** (or plunger in legacy machines) combines three actions:
  * **Conductive heating** from electric heater bands (3 – 5 zones).
  * **Shear heating** from screw rotation and flight compression.
  * **Mechanical conveying** forward toward the nozzle.
- A non-return valve (NRV) at the screw tip prevents backflow during injection.
- Target: Homogeneous melt at **± 2 °C** uniformity, free of unmelted particles and volatiles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** Non-uniform melt causes "silver streaks," voids, and weld-line weakness — top 3 defects in KTU lab viva questions.

### Stage 2 — Injection (Filling Phase)
- Screw acts as a hydraulic ram (translates axially, stops rotating) — this is the **injection stroke**.
- Melt is forced through the **nozzle, sprue, runners, and gates** into the cavity.
- Velocity is **V/P (Velocity/Pressure)** profile-controlled:
  * **1st stage — Fill (velocity-controlled):** Volumetric fill rate Q = A_piston × v_inj
  * **2nd stage — Pack (pressure-controlled):** V/P switch-over at **~ 95 % – 99 %** cavity fill.

### Stage 3 — Packing / Holding (Compensation Phase)
- After V/P switch-over, pressure is held at **P_pack (≈ 50 – 80 % of P_inj)**.
- Compensates for **material shrinkage** (PS ≈ 0.4 %, PP ≈ 1.5 – 2.0 %, PE ≈ 1.5 – 3.0 %).
- Volume of additional melt packed in: **V_pack = V_shrink × n_cavities**

### Stage 4 — Cooling (Solidification Phase)
- **Cooling time** is the rate-limiting step for **> 70 %** of the total cycle.
- Heat is removed primarily by **conduction** from the polymer through the mould steel to **cooling channels**.
- Cooling water parameters: ΔT_water ≈ **2 °C – 5 °C** (turbulent flow, Re > 10,000).
- Part must be cooled below **Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT)** before ejection.

### Stage 5 — Ejection (Demoulding Phase)
- Mould opens (typically along the **parting line**).
- **Ejector system** activates:
  * **Ejector pins** (most common)
  * **Ejector sleeves** (for bosses/through-holes)
  * **Stripper plates** (for thin-walled parts)
  * **Air poppets / valve gates** (for hot-runner systems)
- Parts drop into a chute or onto a conveyor.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 ESE questions frequently combine two of the following formulas in a single 14-mark problem. Memorize the SI units!

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Projected Area of Cavity | $A_{proj} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_{i}$ (mm²) | $A_i$ = projected area of each cavity | Clamp tonnage calculation |
| 2 | **Clamping Force Required** | $F_{clamp} \geq A_{proj} \times P_{cav}$ (N) | $P_{cav}$ = avg. cavity pressure (MPa) | Machine selection |
| 3 | Specific Cavity Pressure | $P_{cav} = \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{A_{proj}}$ (MPa) | Empirical: **30–80 MPa** | Process design |
| 4 | Shot Volume (volumetric) | $V_{shot} = n_{cav} \times V_{part} + V_{sprue} + V_{runner}$ (cm³) | $n_{cav}$ = no. of cavities | Screw diameter sizing |
| 5 | Shot Weight (gravimetric) | $W_{shot} = V_{shot} \times \rho_{melt} \times k_{filler}$ (g) | $\rho_{melt}$ ≈ 0.9–1.4 g/cm³ | Throughput estimation |
| 6 | Injection Pressure Drop (Poiseuille) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi r^{4}}$ (Pa) | $L$ = land length, $Q$ = flow rate | Gate sizing |
| 7 | Shear Rate in Gate | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi r^{3}}$ (s⁻¹) | Typical: 10³ – 10⁵ s⁻¹ | Viscosity reference |
| 8 | Apparent Viscosity (Power Law) | $\eta = m \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ (Pa·s) | $m$ = consistency, $n$ = power-law index | Fill simulation |
| 9 | Cooling Time (1-D Conduction) | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{s^{2}}{\pi^{2} \alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{m} - T_{w}}{T_{e} - T_{w}}\right)$ (s) | $s$ = wall thickness, $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity | Cycle time estimation |
| 10 | Bulk Modulus (Compressibility) | $\beta = -\dfrac{1}{V}\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial P}$ (Pa⁻¹) | Typical: 1 – 2 GPa for melts | PVT correction |
| 11 | Shrinkage Compensation | $D_{cavity} = D_{part} \times (1 + S)$ (mm) | $S$ = volumetric shrinkage | Mould machining |
| 12 | Machine Tonnage Margin | $F_{clamp,machine} \geq 1.1 \times F_{clamp,req}$ (N) | 10% safety factor | Machine selection |
| 13 | Plasticating Capacity | $\dot{m}_{max} = \dfrac{W_{shot} \times 3600}{t_{cycle}}$ (kg/h) | Throughput cap | Cycle optimization |
| 14 | Reynolds Number (Cooling) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D_{h}}{\mu}$ | $Re > 10{,}000$ for turbulent | Cooling circuit design |
| 15 | Part Mass (Wall Volume) | $m_{part} = \sum A_{wall,i} \cdot t_i \cdot \rho$ (g) | $t_i$ = wall thickness | Cost estimation |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Packaging Industry:** Production of PET preforms for bottles (via stretch-blow moulding downstream), PP yogurt cups, HDPE bottle caps (CSD caps at **3,500 – 5,000 caps/hour**), PS disposable cutlery, and food-container lids.
- **Medical:** Syringe barrels, vials, IV components — requires **ISO Class 8 cleanroom** moulding.
- **Automotive:** Under-hood brackets, fascias, gear housings (often reinforced with 30% glass fibre).
- **Electronics:** PCB holders, connector housings (LCP, PBT, PPS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Linkage:** Module 2 question 7 in the 2024 University Exam (Dec 2023) asked to "Explain the role of injection moulding in rigid packaging with a neat diagram of a 2-plate mould" — exactly the type of question appearing below in SECTION_5.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Clamping Force (F_clamp)

### Statement
The clamping force of an injection moulding machine must exceed the opening force generated by plastic melt pressure acting on the projected area of the cavity.

### Given
- A single-cavity mould producing a flat rectangular tray of dimensions $L \times W$ (length × width).
- $L = 200\ \text{mm},\ W = 120\ \text{mm}$.
- Specific cavity pressure $P_{cav} = 40\ \text{MPa}$ (typical for PP).
- 4-cavity mould: $n = 4$.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Calculate the projected area of one cavity** (use only the shadow area on the parting plane, NOT the total surface area).

$$
A_{single} = L \times W = 200 \times 120 = 24{,}000\ \text{mm}^{2} = 0.024\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2: Multiply by the number of cavities.**

$$
A_{proj} = n \times A_{single} = 4 \times 24{,}000 = 96{,}000\ \text{mm}^{2} = 0.096\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the opening force (cavity push-back force).**

$$
F_{open} = A_{proj} \times P_{cav} = 0.096\ \text{m}^{2} \times 40 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} = 3.84 \times 10^{6}\ \text{N} = 3840\ \text{kN}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the safety factor of 1.1 (KTU standard convention).**

$$
F_{clamp,req} = 1.1 \times F_{open} = 1.1 \times 3840 = 4224\ \text{kN} \approx 4250\ \text{kN}
$$

**Step 5: Select the next standard machine (up to 5000 kN).**

> **Selected Machine:** 5000 kN (≈ 500 Tonne) toggle-type clamping unit.

$$
\boxed{F_{clamp} = 4250\ \text{kN (minimum required)};\ \text{Selected: 5000 kN machine}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cooling Time Estimation

### Statement
Estimate the cooling time for a 3 mm thick PP container wall, ejected at a centreline temperature of $T_e = 100\ °\text{C}$.

### Given
- Wall thickness $s = 3\ \text{mm} = 0.003\ \text{m}$.
- Melt temperature $T_m = 220\ °\text{C}$.
- Mould wall temperature $T_w = 25\ °\text{C}$.
- Ejection temperature $T_e = 100\ °\text{C}$.
- Thermal diffusivity of PP: $\alpha = 9.7 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Write the standard 1-D transient conduction solution (exact solution for a slab of finite thickness).**

$$
\dfrac{T(x,t) - T_{w}}{T_{m} - T_{w}} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{4}{(2n+1)\pi} \sin\!\left(\dfrac{(2n+1)\pi x}{s}\right) \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(2n+1)^{2}\pi^{2}\alpha t}{s^{2}}\right)
$$

**Step 2: For the centreline $x = s/2$ and retaining only the first term ($n = 0$), this simplifies to:**

$$
\dfrac{T_{c} - T_{w}}{T_{m} - T_{w}} = \dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\pi^{2}\alpha t}{s^{2}}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $T_c = T_e = 100\ °\text{C}$ and isolate $t$.**

$$
\dfrac{T_{e} - T_{w}}{T_{m} - T_{w}} = \dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\pi^{2}\alpha t}{s^{2}}\right)
$$

$$
\dfrac{100 - 25}{220 - 25} = \dfrac{75}{195} = 0.3846
$$

$$
\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\pi^{2}\alpha t}{s^{2}}\right) = \dfrac{\pi^{2}}{8} \times 0.3846 = \dfrac{9.8696}{8} \times 0.3846 = 0.4746
$$

**Step 4: Take natural log on both sides.**

$$
-\dfrac{\pi^{2}\alpha t}{s^{2}} = \ln(0.4746) = -0.7458
$$

$$
t_{cool} = \dfrac{0.7458 \times s^{2}}{\pi^{2} \times \alpha} = \dfrac{0.7458 \times (0.003)^{2}}{\pi^{2} \times 9.7 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

**Step 5: Numerically evaluate.**

$$
t_{cool} = \dfrac{0.7458 \times 9 \times 10^{-6}}{9.8696 \times 9.7 \times 10^{-8}} = \dfrac{6.712 \times 10^{-6}}{9.574 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 7.01\ \text{s}
$$

> **Result:** The minimum cooling time before ejection is approximately **7 seconds**. Total cycle time would be **~ 12 s** (with 2 s fill + 2 s pack + 1 s ejection).

$$
\boxed{t_{cool} \approx 7.0\ \text{s}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Volumetric Shrinkage Compensation in Mould Design

### Statement
A packaging container has a finished inner diameter of $D_{part} = 80.0\ \text{mm}$. PP volumetric shrinkage $S = 1.8\%$. Calculate the cavity dimension.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Use the linear shrinkage compensation relation.**

$$
D_{cavity} = D_{part} \times (1 + S)
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
D_{cavity} = 80.0 \times (1 + 0.018) = 80.0 \times 1.018 = 81.44\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the mould machining tolerance (typically ± 0.02 mm).**

$$
\boxed{D_{cavity} = 81.44 \pm 0.02\ \text{mm}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Injection Pressure Drop Across a Gate (Poiseuille Model)

### Statement
Polypropylene melt ($\mu = 350\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$) flows through a circular gate of length $L_g = 2\ \text{mm}$ and diameter $D_g = 1.5\ \text{mm}$ at flow rate $Q = 5\ \text{cm}^{3}/\text{s}$. Compute pressure drop.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Convert to SI units.**

$$
L_g = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m},\quad r_g = 0.75 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m},\quad Q = 5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Hagen–Poiseuille equation for a circular channel.**

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi r^{4}}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerically.**

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \times 350 \times (2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (5 \times 10^{-6})}{\pi \times (0.75 \times 10^{-3})^{4}}
$$

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{2.8 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times 3.164 \times 10^{-13}} = \dfrac{2.8 \times 10^{-5}}{9.940 \times 10^{-13}} \approx 2.82 \times 10^{7}\ \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to MPa.**

$$
\boxed{\Delta P \approx 28.2\ \text{MPa (across gate)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is exactly why **gate diameter and land length** are the first parameters tuned in mould-flow analysis — a 0.1 mm change in $D_g$ shifts $\Delta P$ by **~ 50 %** (since $r^4$ in the denominator).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process Defect → Root Cause → Cure

| Defect | Probable Root Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Sink marks** | Insufficient pack pressure, thick wall | Increase pack pressure by **5 MPa**, reduce wall thickness |
| **Short shot** | Inadequate injection velocity, frozen gate | Raise melt temp by **10 °C**, increase injection speed |
| **Weld lines** | Air entrapment at weld interface | Add **vent** at weld (0.02 mm depth), increase V_pack |
| **Flash (burrs)** | Low clamp tonnage, worn parting line | Increase $F_{clamp}$ by **10 %**, polish parting line |
| **Voids** | Excessive wall thickness (> 6 mm) | Redesign ribs/core-out, increase pack time |
| **Warping** | Non-uniform cooling, fibre orientation | Balance cooling circuit, reduce pack pressure |
| **Silver streaks** | Moisture in resin, degradation | Dry resin at **80 °C / 4 h** (PA), reduce melt temp |
| **Burn marks** | Air entrapment, no vent | Add vent at cavity end, reduce injection speed |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Injection Moulding Machine

> The following Mermaid block-diagram maps all functional subsystems and their physical/control relationships — meeting the Mermaid compilation safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs only, no markdown inside labels).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HOP[Hopper Resin Feed] -->|Pellets| BAR[Heated Barrel]
    BAR -->|Heat + Shear| SCW[Reciprocating Screw]
    SCW -->|Melt Accumulation| NRV[Non Return Valve]
    NRV -->|High Pressure| NOZ[Nozzle]
    NOZ -->|Inject| SPR[Sprue and Runner]
    SPR -->|Distribute| GAT[Gate System]
    GAT -->|Fill| CAV[Mould Cavity]
    
    CAV -.->|Heat Rejection| COL[Cooling Channels]
    CAV -.->|Solidified Part| EJE[Ejector System]
    
    HYD[Hydraulic Power Pack] -->|Force| SCW
    HYD -->|Clamp Force| CLP[Clamping Unit]
    CLP -->|Open Close| CAV
    
    CTL[PLC / Machine Controller] -->|V P Profile| HYD
    CTL -->|Heat Zones| BAR
    CTL -->|Cycle Sequence| EJE
    CTL -->|Monitor| SEN[Pressure and Temperature Sensors]
    SEN -->|Feedback| CTL
```

## 4.2 Process Sequence State Machine (Cycle Flow)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Plastication
    Plastication --> Injection: Screw Rotates Melts Meters
    Injection --> PackHold: V P Switchover at 95 percent
    PackHold --> Cooling: Pressure Released
    Cooling --> Ejection: Ejector Pin Forward
    Ejection --> Plastication: Mould Closes Cycle Reset
    Ejection --> [*]: Shutdown
```

## 4.3 Two-Plate Mould Block Architecture (Kern Reference Sketch)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Clamping Plate Fixed Side] --> B[Retainer Plate Cavity Insert]
    B --> C[Cavity Block Cavity Side]
    D[Support Plate Core Side] --> E[Core Insert Moulded Feature]
    D --> F[Ejector Retainer Plate]
    F --> G[Ejector Pins]
    H[Back Plate Support] --> A
    H --> D
    I[Cooling Channel Network] -.-> B
    I -.-> D
    J[Sprue Bushing Entry] --> B
    K[Locating Ring Alignment] --> A
```

## 4.4 Key Subsystem Cross-Reference Table

| Subsystem | Function | Critical Parameter | Material of Construction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hopper** | Gravity feed pellets | Volumetric capacity (kg) | Stainless steel |
| **Barrel** | Melt + convey | L/D = 18–24, hardened | Bimetallic (HRC 60) |
| **Screw** | Plasticate + inject | Compression ratio 2–3 | Tool steel + nitride |
| **Nozzle** | Seal against sprue | Tip radius 10–20 mm | Tool steel, hard chrome |
| **Clamping Unit** | Resist cavity pressure | $F_{clamp}$ 50–12,000 kN | Cast iron frame |
| **Mould Cavity** | Defines geometry | Surface finish Ra 0.05–0.8 µm | P20 / H13 / S7 steel |
| **Cooling Channels** | Heat extraction | $\phi$ 8–12 mm, Re > 10⁴ | Copper / beryllium-copper |
| **Ejector Pins** | Part removal | SKD61 / H13, $\phi$ 2–10 mm | Hardened tool steel |
| **Hot Runner** | Eliminate runner waste | $T_{melt}$ ± 2 °C | Heated manifolds |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part-A question must be answered in **3 – 5 sentences** with a labelled diagram where applicable. Board examiners deduct marks for exceeding word limit and for missing units.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023] Define injection moulding. List any four advantages.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Injection moulding** is a cyclic plastics manufacturing process in which a precisely metered quantity of molten polymer is injected under high pressure (**50–200 MPa**) into a closed mould cavity, where it solidifies by cooling and is ejected as a finished part.
>
> **Four advantages:**
> 1. **High production rate** — single-cycle times of 2 – 60 s enable 100,000+ parts per shift.
> 2. **Excellent dimensional accuracy** — typical tolerance ± 0.05 mm.
> 3. **Minimal post-processing** — flash-trim and assembly are usually the only secondary operations.
> 4. **Wide material range** — processable: PE, PP, PS, ABS, PC, PA, POM, PBT, PPS, LCP, and reinforced/filled grades.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] What is a **non-return valve (NRV)** in a reciprocating screw? Why is it essential?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **non-return valve** (also called a **check-ring** or **sliding-ring valve**) is a one-way mechanical seal located at the screw tip that prevents backflow of molten polymer into the screw flights during the **injection (ram-forward) stroke**.
>
> **Why essential:**
> 1. Maintains a fixed **shot volume** at the front of the screw — without it, melt would leak back, causing inconsistent shot weight.
> 2. Converts the screw into a true **plunger** during injection, ensuring the full hydraulic pressure is transmitted to the melt front.
> 3. Prevents **over-packing** and **weld-line voids** by enabling clean V/P switch-over.
>
> Failure modes (KTU viva question): "**screw ring hang-up**" causes over-packing and flash.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE 2024 Part-B question on Module 2 is a **14-mark question with internal choice** between two full sub-options. Each sub-question carries **7 marks**. The following provides both Question A and Question B, each with exhaustive model solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

#### (a) [7 marks] Explain the construction and working of a **reciprocating-screw injection moulding machine** with a neat block diagram. State the function of each major component.

**Model Answer:**

A **reciprocating-screw injection moulding machine** consists of two principal units mounted on a common base frame:

**1. INJECTION UNIT (Plasticating + Injecting):**

- **Hopper (1):** Stainless-steel gravity feed reservoir, capacity 25 – 200 kg, with magnet at throat to trap ferrous contamination.
- **Feed Throat (2):** Water-cooled collar (15 – 25 °C) preventing premature melt bridging at the barrel entrance.
- **Heated Barrel (3):** Bimetallic (centrifugally cast iron-boride liner), 3 – 5 electrical heater bands (each 1.5 – 4 kW), L/D = 18 – 24. Heats pellets to **180 – 320 °C** depending on polymer.
- **Reciprocating Screw (4):** Three-zone geometry — feed (deep flight, h = 0.15D), compression (tapered), metering (shallow flight, h = 0.05D). Compression ratio 2 : 1 – 3 : 1. Rotates via hydraulic motor during plastication; translates axially during injection.
- **Non-Return Valve (5):** Check-ring at screw tip, prevents backflow during injection.
- **Nozzle (6):** Seats against the mould sprue bushing; tip radius 10 – 20 mm. Heated to avoid freeze-off.
- **Hydraulic Cylinder (7):** Drives screw rotation (via thrust bearing) and axial translation.

**2. CLAMPING UNIT (Mould Closing + Holding):**

- **Toggle Mechanism (8):** Four-bar linkage (moving platen, stationary platen, two toggle arms) providing mechanical advantage — clamp force amplification of **~ 20:1** to 50:1.
- **Moving Platen (9):** Carries the mould core side.
- **Stationary Platen (10):** Fixed to the barrel; carries cavity side and is bolted to the locating ring.
- **Tie Bars (11):** Four high-tensile steel bars (diameter 80 – 250 mm) that guide platen motion and absorb reaction load.
- **Ejector System (12):** Hydraulic ejector ram operating ejector pins through the moving platen.

**3. CONTROL UNIT (13):**

- PLC-based controller with HMI touch screen; monitors **150 – 500** process points (pressure, position, temperature, time); executes **V/P switch-over** and **clamp tonnage** regulation with ± 0.5 % closed-loop accuracy.

**Working Cycle (V/P Profile):**

- **Fill phase:** Velocity-controlled, screw advances at **10 – 200 mm/s**, ram pressure ramps to **P_peak = 80 – 200 MPa**.
- **Pack phase:** Pressure-controlled, holds at **P_pack = 50 – 80 % of P_peak**, compensates **volumetric shrinkage 1.5 – 3 %**.
- **Hold → Cool → Eject** as described in SECTION 2.1.

**Labelled Block Diagram (in 2-D description):**

> From left to right: Hopper → Barrel (3 zones) → Screw (rotating) → NRV → Nozzle → Sprue → Cavity between Moving Platen and Stationary Platen, supported by Tie Bars. Toggle Linkage below. Ejector Pins project forward from Moving Platen.

**Key working relationships:**

- **Plasticating capacity (kg/h) limits cycle time:** $t_{min} = (W_{shot} \times 3600) / \dot{m}_{max}$
- **Clamp tonnage must exceed cavity push-back force** by 10% safety margin.

---

#### (b) [7 marks] For a 4-cavity mould producing PP bottle caps, the projected area of each cap is **$25\ \text{cm}^2$** and the specific cavity pressure is **$40\ \text{MPa}$**. Calculate the minimum clamping force required and recommend a suitable machine tonnage. If the cycle time is **8 s**, calculate the **plasticating capacity** required in kg/h, given that the shot weight (4 caps + runner) is **$48\ \text{g}$** and PP melt density is **$0.91\ \text{g/cm}^3$**.

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1: Total projected area.**

$$
A_{proj} = 4 \times 25 = 100\ \text{cm}^{2} = 10{,}000\ \text{mm}^{2} = 0.01\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2: Cavity opening force.**

$$
F_{open} = A_{proj} \times P_{cav} = 0.01 \times 40 \times 10^{6} = 4.0 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N} = 400\ \text{kN}
$$

**Step 3: Apply 10 % safety factor.**

$$
F_{clamp,req} = 1.1 \times 400 = 440\ \text{kN}
$$

**Step 4: Select standard machine.** Closest standard tonnage above 440 kN is **500 kN (50 tonne)**.

$$
\boxed{F_{clamp,selected} = 500\ \text{kN (50 Tonne toggle machine)}}
$$

**Step 5: Plasticating capacity (kg/h).**

$$
\dot{m}_{max} = \dfrac{W_{shot}}{t_{cycle}} \times 3600 = \dfrac{48\ \text{g}}{8\ \text{s}} \times 3600 = 21{,}600\ \text{g/h} = 21.6\ \text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 6: With 25 % margin (for screw recovery time):**

$$
\dot{m}_{design} = 1.25 \times 21.6 = 27\ \text{kg/h}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{required} \geq 27\ \text{kg/h (select a 35 – 50 kg/h machine)}}
$$

**Step 7 (Verification of shot volume):**

$$
V_{shot} = \dfrac{48\ \text{g}}{0.91\ \text{g/cm}^{3}} = 52.75\ \text{cm}^{3} \rightarrow \text{Screw diameter } \phi 30\ \text{ mm is adequate (1 shot} = 5.2\ \text{cm stroke)}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]

#### (a) [7 marks] Discuss the various **process parameters** that control the quality of an injection-moulded part. Explain how **V/P switch-over** and **mould temperature** affect part quality.

**Model Answer:**

The injection moulding process is governed by approximately **20 – 30** interlinked process parameters. For KTU purposes, classify them under **four groups:**

**1. Temperature Parameters (affect melt viscosity, crystallinity, surface):**

- **Barrel / Melt temperature ($T_m$):** 180 – 320 °C. Higher $T_m$ → lower viscosity → easier fill but more degradation and longer cooling.
- **Mould temperature ($T_w$):** 20 – 120 °C. Affects:
  * **Surface finish:** Higher $T_w$ → glossier surface (gloss units 60+).
  * **Crystallinity (for semi-crystalline polymers like PP, PA, POM):** Higher $T_w$ → more crystalline → stronger but more shrinkage.
  * **Residual stress:** Lower $T_w$ → frozen-in stress → warpage and brittleness.
- **Nozzle temperature:** 5 – 10 °C above $T_m$ to prevent freeze-off.
- **Drying temperature (hygroscopic polymers):** 80 °C × 4 h for PA, 120 °C × 4 h for PC, 180 °C × 2 h for PET.

**2. Pressure Parameters (affect fill, pack, density, dimensions):**

- **Injection pressure ($P_{inj}$):** 50 – 200 MPa — drives melt flow; affects fill rate and weld-line strength.
- **Pack / hold pressure ($P_{pack}$):** 50 – 80 % of $P_{inj}$ — compensates shrinkage.
- **Back pressure (during plastication):** 5 – 20 MPa — removes air bubbles, improves melt homogeneity.
- **Clamping tonnage ($F_{clamp}$):** must exceed cavity push-back force × 1.1.

**3. Velocity / Time Parameters:**

- **Injection velocity ($v_{inj}$):** 10 – 200 mm/s — affects fill time and shear rate.
- **Fill time ($t_{fill}$):** 0.5 – 5 s — too slow → frozen skin, too fast → jetting and burns.
- **Pack time ($t_{pack}$):** 1 – 10 s.
- **Cooling time ($t_{cool}$):** 2 – 60 s — rate-limiting for cycle.
- **V/P switch-over point:** 95 – 99 % of cavity fill volume.

**4. Positional / Geometric Parameters:**

- **Shot volume:** 1 – 10× part volume.
- **Cushion (melt reserve at screw tip):** 3 – 10 mm.
- **Decompression (suck-back):** 2 – 5 mm to prevent drool.

**The V/P Switch-Over — Critical Control Point:**

> The **V/P switch-over** is the moment during injection when control transitions from **velocity (ram speed control)** to **pressure (hydraulic pressure control)**. Setting it at **95 – 99 %** fill is critical:
> - **Too early (< 90 %):** Pressure-controlled mode attempts to push into a half-filled cavity → **over-packing, flash, sink marks**.
> - **Too late (> 99.5 %):** Velocity mode persists until the cavity is sealed → **short shots, air entrapment, burn marks**.
> - **Optimum:** Use **cavity pressure curve** to switch at the moment of peak pressure inflection (typically 95 – 99 %).
>
> Modern machines use **"cavity pressure triggered V/P switch-over"** with closed-loop control to ± 0.5 % repeatability.

**Mould Temperature Effect — Quality Linkage:**

| Quality Attribute | Effect of Increasing $T_w$ |
|---|---|
| Surface gloss | ↑ (rises) |
| Tensile strength (semi-crystalline) | ↑ (rises) |
| Shrinkage | ↑ (rises) |
| Cycle time | ↑ (rises) |
| Weld-line strength | ↑ (rises) |
| Ejectability | ↑ (rises) |
| Sink marks | ↓ (falls) |

---

#### (b) [7 marks] A polystyrene (PS) drinking cup weighs **5.5 g** and has a wall thickness of **1.2 mm**. The cycle time is **4 s**. Calculate the **(i) cooling time** using the empirical formula $t_{cool} = 0.5 \cdot s^2$ (with $s$ in mm, $t$ in s) and **(ii) the hourly production rate**.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Cooling time:**

$$
t_{cool} = 0.5 \times s^{2} = 0.5 \times (1.2)^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.44 = 0.72\ \text{s}
$$

> (For thin-wall PS packaging, this is the dominant solidification time.)

**(ii) Hourly production rate:**

$$
n_{parts} = \dfrac{3600\ \text{s/h}}{t_{cycle}} = \dfrac{3600}{4} = 900\ \text{parts/h}
$$

> **Note:** With a 16-cavity mould (typical for thin-wall packaging):
>
> $$
> n_{parts} = 16 \times 900 = 14{,}400\ \text{cups/h}
> $$

**Bonus — material throughput:**

$$
\dot{m}_{PS} = 14{,}400 \times 5.5 = 79{,}200\ \text{g/h} = 79.2\ \text{kg/h}
$$

> This justifies selecting a machine with plasticating capacity $\geq 100\ \text{kg/h}$ (PS grade).

**Summary box:**

$$
\boxed{t_{cool} = 0.72\ \text{s},\quad n_{parts} = 14{,}400\ \text{cups/h (16-cavity)},\quad \dot{m} = 79.2\ \text{kg/h}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 10 % safety factor** on clamping force → loses 1 mark in Step 3.
> 2. **Mixing up projected area vs. total surface area** — only projected area is used for clamp calculation. Total area is used for **shrinkage** and **heat balance**.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversion** of cm² to m² in $F_{clamp}$ calculation — most common 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the empirical relationship** $t_{cool} = 0.5 s^2$ for thin-wall PS — must be quoted even in derivation questions.
> 5. **Writing "V/P switch-over" without explaining both branches** (velocity → pressure) — examiners expect **both** the trigger point and the consequence.
> 6. **Citing polymer names without stating grades or melt flow indices** (e.g., "PP" instead of "PP H110MA, MFI 11 g/10 min") — loses 0.5 mark per instance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (read 3 times before exam):**

- **P-I-C-C-E mnemonic:** Plastication → Injection → Compaction → Cooling → Ejection.
- **Reciprocating screw L/D ratio:** 18 – 24. **Compression ratio:** 2 – 3.
- **Five machine components:** Hopper, Barrel, Screw, Clamping Unit, Mould.
- **Three pressure phases:** Injection (50 – 200 MPa) → Pack (50 – 80 % inj.) → Hold/Cooling.
- **V/P switch-over:** at 95 – 99 % fill — **most critical** process control point.
- **Clamping force equation:** $F_{clamp} \geq 1.1 \times A_{proj} \times P_{cav}$. Safety factor = **1.1** always.
- **Specific cavity pressure (PE/PP):** 30 – 80 MPa. For ABS/PC: 40 – 100 MPa.
- **Cushion (melt reserve):** 3 – 10 mm at screw tip — must never be zero (indicates over-packing).
- **Cooling channels:** Diameter 8 – 12 mm, **Reynolds number > 10,000** for turbulent flow, ΔT water 2 – 5 °C.
- **Cooling time formula (1-D conduction):** $t_{cool} = (s^{2}/\pi^{2}\alpha) \ln(8/\pi^{2} \cdot (T_m - T_w)/(T_e - T_w))$.
- **Empirical shortcut for thin-wall PS:** $t_{cool} = 0.5 s^{2}$ (s in mm, t in s).
- **Shrinkage compensation:** $D_{cavity} = D_{part} \times (1 + S)$ where $S$ = volumetric shrinkage %.
- **Gate pressure drop:** Poiseuille $\Delta P = 8\mu LQ/\pi r^{4}$ — the $r^4$ dependency makes gate radius the single most sensitive parameter.
- **Eight common defects and their cure:** sink, short shot, weld line, flash, void, warpage, silver streak, burn mark.
- **Packaging-specific applications:** PET preforms, PP cups, HDPE caps, PS cutlery, medical vials.
- **Production economics:** Selection of $n_{cavities}$ balances cycle time, clamp tonnage, and shot weight.
- **Hot-runner systems** eliminate runner waste and reduce cycle by 5 – 15 %.
- **Two-plate vs. three-plate moulds:** 2-plate for single-face parts; 3-plate for multi-cavity with automatic de-gating.
- **Mandatory pre-processing:** Drying hygroscopic resins (PA: 80 °C × 4 h; PC: 120 °C × 4 h; PET: 180 °C × 2 h).
- **Ejector system components:** Ejector pins, sleeves, stripper plates, air poppets.
- **Recycling (regulatory, KTU 2024):** Reject parts are ground and re-fed at 10 – 30 % virgin blend — must not exceed 2 thermal cycles to avoid molecular-weight degradation.
- **Industry 4.0 linkage:** Modern machines use **cavity-pressure-based closed-loop control** with ± 0.5 % repeatability (vs. ± 3 % for legacy V/P profile).
- **Quick sanity check:** for a thin-wall packaging part, **cooling time is the bottleneck**; for a thick-wall structural part, **packing time dominates**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blow Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Blow Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blow Moulding (KTU 2024 Definition)** — *Blow moulding is a thermoplastic fabrication process in which a hollow molten or heat-softened polymeric parison (a tube-like preform) is confined inside a cooled mould cavity and inflated by compressed air against the inner walls of the mould, where it solidifies by conductive cooling to take the geometry of the cavity. The process is the polymer analogue of glass-blowing and is the dominant mass-production route for hollow plastic containers used in packaging.*

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, blow moulding is classified as a **secondary plastics processing (conversion) operation** that converts a *primary formed shape* (parison or preform) into a *finished hollow article* through the combined action of **internal pneumatic pressure**, **external mould constraint**, and **transient conductive heat transfer**.

The governing equation of the process is the **Clausius–Clapeyron-based solidification criterion**:

$$
T_{\text{part}}(x,\,t) \;\le\; T_{\text{eject}} \;\;\text{at the inner mould surface before demoulding}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it is used in packaging:** Blow moulding offers the lowest *material-to-volume ratio* for hollow containers, the highest *production rate (cycles per hour)*, and the lightest *part weight per unit volume* among all hollow-part processes.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of blow moulding as **blowing up a balloon inside a cookie-cutter mould**.

1. You take a soft, warm plastic tube (the **parison**) — imagine a long, squishy drinking straw made of hot wax.
2. You trap this soft tube inside a two-piece metal mould shaped like a bottle.
3. You then blow compressed air through the centre of the tube, just as you would inflate a balloon.
4. The soft plastic expands, presses against the cold metal mould walls, and "freezes" in the shape of the bottle.
5. When you open the mould, you have a perfectly shaped, hollow plastic bottle.

The cold metal mould is what gives the bottle its solid shape — the plastic *cannot* remain soft once it touches the chilled surface. This is **conductive quenching** in plain English.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** The 1-litre water bottle, the HDPE milk can, the PET soft-drink bottle, and the shampoo container on your shelf — almost every hollow plastic container you have ever held was made by blow moulding.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology (KTU Board-Relevant)

| Term | Symbol | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Melt Temperature | $T_m$ | **180 °C – 230 °C** for HDPE; **260 °C – 280 °C** for PET | Determines parison sag and surface finish |
| Mould Surface Temperature | $T_{\text{mould}}$ | **20 °C – 40 °C** (HDPE); **5 °C – 15 °C** (PET) | Governs cycle time and crystallinity |
| Blow Pressure | $P_b$ | **0.4 MPa – 1.0 MPa** | Determines surface replication |
| Air Pressure (Pre-blow) | $P_{\text{pre}}$ | **0.1 MPa – 0.3 MPa** | Pre-stretches the parison |
| Blow-Up Ratio | BUR | **1.5 : 1 to 4 : 1** (typical); up to **7 : 1** for fuel tanks | Ratio of bottle diameter to parison diameter |
| Parison Diameter | $D_p$ | **10 mm – 200 mm** | Initial molten tube diameter |
| Cycle Time | $t_c$ | **6 s – 30 s** | Total time per moulded part |
| Wall Thickness | $t_w$ | **0.2 mm – 6.0 mm** | Final container wall thickness |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always quote *both* BUR and stretch ratio when discussing PET stretch-blow moulding — the examiner awards a separate mark each.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Blow Moulding (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognises **three principal variants** of blow moulding:

1. **Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)** — parison is *extruded* continuously, then clamped and blown.
2. **Injection Blow Moulding (IBM)** — parison (called a *preform*) is *injection-moulded* on a steel core pin, transferred to a blow station, and inflated.
3. **Stretch Blow Moulding (SBM)** — special variant of IBM where the preform is *axially stretched* by a stretch rod *before* radial inflation (used exclusively for crystalline PET bottles for carbonated beverages).

A fourth sub-variant — **Dip Blow Moulding / Co-extrusion Blow Moulding** — is also testable for multilayer packaging applications (e.g., fuel tanks, toothpaste tubes).

---

## 1.5 Why Blow Moulding Dominates Plastics Packaging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-shot hollow formation** — blow moulding forms a sealed hollow body in *one* machine cycle without seams, joining operations, or secondary trimming (for flashless / hot-knife designs).

**Industrial advantages:**

- **Material efficiency:** Wall thickness can be controlled to ±5 % with modern *parison programming*.
- **Design freedom:** Complex curvatures, integral handles, and living hinges can be moulded in.
- **Throughput:** Continuous EBM lines reach **> 10,000 bottles/hour**; rotary wheel systems reach **> 50,000 bottles/hour**.
- **Recyclability:** Mono-material HDPE and PET containers are 100 % mechanically recyclable.
- **Tooling cost:** Aluminium moulds for short runs are ~**60 % cheaper** than steel moulds of equivalent geometry.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Architecture — The Generic Blow Moulding Cycle

Every blow-moulding variant obeys the same **five-stage canonical cycle**:

1. **Parison / Preform Formation** — molten polymer is shaped into a hollow tube (extrusion) or onto a core pin (injection).
2. **Mould Closure & Parison Capture** — the two mould halves clamp around the parison, pinching one end (the *bottom*) and sealing the other (the *neck*).
3. **Inflation (Blow)** — compressed air at $P_b$ expands the parison against the cold mould cavity.
4. **Cooling & Solidification** — conductive heat transfer through the mould wall freezes the part.
5. **Mould Opening, Ejection & Deflashing** — the mould opens, the part is ejected, and excess flash is trimmed.

---

## 2.2 Variant-Wise Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)

- **Parison source:** Continuous melt from an *extruder–die assembly* (annular die).
- **Parison control:** Either **fixed gap** (cheap, gives variable wall thickness) or **programmed gap** (dynamic parison programming via a hydraulic servo; gives uniform wall thickness).
- **Process steps:**
  1. Extruder melts and meters the polymer at constant *specific throughput* $\dot{m}$.
  2. Melt passes through an *accumulator head* (for high-MFI materials, this prevents parison sag).
  3. Parison is extruded downward between two open mould halves.
  4. Mould closes, pinching the parison at the parting line.
  5. A *blow pin* or *needle* injects compressed air.
  6. The part cools, is ejected, and the flash is trimmed.
- **Defects typical:** *parison sag* (gravity-driven elongation), *bottleneck ovality*, *weld-line weakness*, *flash*.

### 2.2.2 Injection Blow Moulding (IBM)

- **Three-station rotary machine:** *Injection → Conditioning → Blow*.
- **Preform injection:** Polymer is injection-moulded onto a *core rod* through a *cavity mould*, producing a test-tube-shaped preform with a finished bottle neck (threading is fully formed at injection).
- **Conditioning:** Preform is temperature-conditioned (above $T_g$ but below $T_m$).
- **Blow:** Preform is transferred to the blow mould and inflated.
- **Advantage over EBM:** No flash, no pinch-off scrap, superior neck finish, higher dimensional accuracy.

### 2.2.3 Stretch Blow Moulding (SBM)

- **Two-step SBM (most common for PET):** Preforms are *injection-moulded, stored*, then re-heated and stretch-blown (e.g., for carbonated soft-drink bottles).
- **Single-step SBM:** Injection, conditioning, stretch-blow in one machine (used for PET beer bottles and hot-fill jars).
- **Key parameters:**
  - **Axial stretch ratio:** $S_{\text{axial}} = L_{\text{preform}} / L_{\text{bottle}}$ typically **2.0 – 2.5**.
  - **Hoop stretch ratio:** $S_{\text{hoop}} = D_{\text{bottle}} / D_{\text{preform}}$ typically **3.5 – 4.5**.
  - **Total areal stretch ratio:** $S_{\text{total}} = S_{\text{axial}} \times S_{\text{hoop}}$ → for PET carbonated bottles, $S_{\text{total}}$ must lie in **10 – 16** for optimal *strain-induced crystallisation* and barrier performance.
- **Physics:** Biaxial stretching aligns polymer chains, raising *tensile modulus*, *toughness*, *gas barrier*, and *clarity*.

---

## 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' — Heat Transfer, Mechanics, Rheology

| Stage | Governing Physics | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Parison extrusion | Polymer melt flow through annular die | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\pi \, D^4 \, \Delta P}{128 \, \mu \, L}$ (Hagen–Poiseuille analogue for annulus) |
| Parison sag | Viscoelastic creep under self-weight | $\epsilon_{\text{sag}}(t) = \dfrac{\rho g L_p^2}{2 E_a} \cdot t$ |
| Inflation | Membrane stretching under internal pressure | $P_b = \dfrac{2 \, \sigma_y \, t_w}{R_c}$ (thin-walled sphere hoop stress) |
| Cooling | Transient 1-D conduction through wall | Fourier's law + semi-infinite slab solution |
| Solidification | Crystallisation (for HDPE, PP) or vitrification (for PET) | Avrami equation: $X_c = 1 - e^{-k t^n}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** A packaging engineer uses the inflation equation to predict the *minimum blow pressure* required to push the polymer into the corners of a complex mould without leaving *unfilled* regions, while the cooling equation sets the *cycle time* — and hence the *economic throughput* — of the line.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Mandate:** The following equations are the **highest-frequency tested formulas** for blow moulding in the 2024 Scheme.

$$
\boxed{\; \text{BUR} \;=\; \dfrac{D_{\text{bottle}}}{D_{\text{parison}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{A_{\text{mould}}}{A_{\text{parison}}}} \;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; \text{Areal Stretch Ratio} \;=\; S_{\text{axial}} \times S_{\text{hoop}} \;=\; \dfrac{L_b \, D_b}{L_p \, D_p} \;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; t_w \;=\; \dfrac{D_p \, t_p}{D_b} \;=\; \dfrac{t_p}{\text{BUR}} \quad (\text{by mass conservation}) \;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; P_{\text{blow,min}} \;=\; \dfrac{2 \, \sigma_y \, t_w}{R_c} \;\;+\;\; P_{\text{atm}} \;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; t_{\text{cool}} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho \, c_p \, t_w}{\pi \, k_m} \;\left[\, \text{Bi}_{\text{crit}} \;-\; 2 \,\text{Bi}_{\text{crit}} \, \theta_{\text{eject}} \,\right]^{2} \;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; \text{Productivity} \;=\; \dfrac{3600 \, \eta \, n_c}{t_c} \quad \text{(parts/hour)} \;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; \epsilon_{\text{sag}} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho \, g \, L_p^{\,2}}{2 \, E_a} \cdot t_{\text{hang}} \;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; X_c \;=\; 1 - \exp(-k \, t^n) \quad \text{(Avrami crystallisation)} \;}
$$

### Symbol Glossary

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $D_b$ | Bottle diameter | mm |
| $D_p$ | Parison / preform diameter | mm |
| $t_p$ | Parison wall thickness | mm |
| $t_w$ | Final wall thickness | mm |
| $R_c$ | Mould cavity radius | mm |
| $\sigma_y$ | Polymer yield stress at inflation $T$ | MPa |
| $\rho$ | Polymer density | kg/m³ |
| $c_p$ | Specific heat capacity | J/(kg·K) |
| $k_m$ | Mould thermal conductivity | W/(m·K) |
| $E_a$ | Apparent elastic modulus (melt) | Pa |
| $n_c$ | Number of cavities | – |
| $\eta$ | Yield efficiency (typically **0.85 – 0.95**) | – |
| $X_c$ | Fractional crystallinity | – |
| $L_p$, $L_b$ | Length of parison / bottle | mm |

### Numerical Constants to Memorise

| Constant | Value | Context |
|---|---|---|
| Glass transition of PET | **76 °C – 80 °C** | Stretch-blow window |
| Melting of HDPE | **125 °C – 135 °C** | Process temperature ceiling |
| Melting of PET | **245 °C – 265 °C** | Injection temperature |
| Biaxial orientation peak | $S_{\text{total}}$ = **10 – 16** | PET CSD bottles |
| Typical EBM blow pressure | **0.4 – 0.8 MPa** | HDPE containers |

---

## 2.5 Materials Used in Blow Moulding (KTU-Relevant)

| Polymer | Typical Use | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| **HDPE** | Milk bottles, detergent containers, fuel tanks | Easy processability, chemical resistance |
| **LDPE / LLDPE** | Squeeze bottles | Flexibility, ESCR |
| **PP** | Hot-fill bottles, closures | Higher melting point, clarity |
| **PET** | CSD bottles, water bottles | Clarity, biaxial orientation, barrier |
| **PVC** | Edible oil bottles (legacy) | Clarity, oil resistance |
| **EVOH / PA / MXD6** | Barrier layer in co-extrusion | Oxygen barrier |
| **Recycled PET (rPET)** | Sustainable packaging | Circular economy mandate |
| **Bio-based PLA** | Emerging applications | Sustainability |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 emphasis:** The syllabus explicitly asks about *co-extrusion blow moulding* for multilayer packaging (e.g., 5-layer fuel tanks: HDPE / tie / EVOH / tie / HDPE).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Blow-Up Ratio (BUR) by Mass Conservation

> **Statement:** *For an isochoric (constant-volume, incompressible) inflation of a parison into a thin-walled bottle, show that the wall thickness is inversely proportional to the Blow-Up Ratio.*

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the parison geometry.**
The parison is a thin-walled cylindrical tube of initial diameter $D_p$, wall thickness $t_p$, and length $L_p$.

The parison's *circumferential cross-sectional area* is:

$$
A_p \;=\; \pi \, D_p \, t_p
$$

**Step 2 — Define the final bottle geometry.**
After inflation, the bottle is a thin-walled cylindrical body of diameter $D_b$, wall thickness $t_w$, and length $L_b$. Its cross-sectional area is:

$$
A_b \;=\; \pi \, D_b \, t_w
$$

**Step 3 — Apply conservation of polymer mass.**
For an incompressible melt (valid for thermoplastics at processing temperature where $\rho$ changes < 5 %):

$$
\rho_p \, A_p \, L_p \;=\; \rho_b \, A_b \, L_b
$$

Assuming $\rho_p \approx \rho_b$ (isothermal quench is *small*):

$$
A_p \, L_p \;=\; A_b \, L_b
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel length terms (geometric simplification).**
For a *purely radial* inflation (no axial stretch — valid for EBM):

$$
L_p \;\approx\; L_b \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A_p \;\approx\; A_b
$$

$$
\pi \, D_p \, t_p \;=\; \pi \, D_b \, t_w
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the final wall thickness.**

$$
t_w \;=\; \dfrac{D_p}{D_b} \cdot t_p \;=\; \dfrac{t_p}{\text{BUR}}
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{\; t_w \;=\; \dfrac{t_p}{\text{BUR}} \;=\; \dfrac{t_p \, D_p}{D_b} \;}
$$

**Real-world utility:** A packaging engineer designing a 60 mm diameter bottle with 0.5 mm wall from a parison of 0.9 mm thickness computes:

$$
\text{BUR} = \dfrac{t_p}{t_w} = \dfrac{0.9}{0.5} = 1.8 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; D_p = \text{BUR} \times D_b / \text{BUR}_{\text{actual}} \;\text{(re-derive)}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Blow Pressure by Membrane Mechanics

> **Statement:** *Derive the minimum internal pressure $P_b$ required to inflate a polymer parison into a thin-walled spherical mould cavity of radius $R_c$, assuming the polymer behaves as an elastic membrane at yield.*

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Treat the parison as a thin spherical membrane.**
For a thin-walled sphere of radius $R_c$ and wall thickness $t_w$, the *hoop (meridional) stress* $\sigma_h$ under internal pressure $P_b$ is given by the **membrane equation of equilibrium**:

$$
\sigma_h \;=\; \dfrac{P_b \, R_c}{2 \, t_w}
$$

This is the standard *thin-pressure-vessel* formula from solid mechanics (Beer–Johnston, *Mechanics of Materials*, Ch. 9).

**Step 2 — Set the hoop stress equal to the yield stress of the polymer at the inflation temperature.**
The polymer will just begin to deform plastically (and fill the mould corners) when:

$$
\sigma_h \;\ge\; \sigma_y(T_{\text{infl}})
$$

**Step 3 — Equate and solve.**

$$
\dfrac{P_b \, R_c}{2 \, t_w} \;=\; \sigma_y(T_{\text{infl}})
$$

$$
P_b \;=\; \dfrac{2 \, \sigma_y \, t_w}{R_c}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for atmospheric counter-pressure.**
The mould cavity is at atmospheric pressure, so the *gauge* pressure reading on the supply line is:

$$
\boxed{\; P_{b,\text{min,gauge}} \;=\; \dfrac{2 \, \sigma_y(T_{\text{infl}}) \, t_w}{R_c} \;+\; P_{\text{atm}} \;}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
Take $t_w = 1.0$ mm, $R_c = 30$ mm, $\sigma_y(150\,°\text{C}) = 4.0$ MPa for HDPE.

$$
P_{b,\text{min}} \;=\; \dfrac{2 \times 4.0 \times 1.0}{30} \;=\; 0.267 \text{ MPa}
$$

Adding atmospheric pressure: $P_{b,\text{total}} = 0.267 + 0.101 = 0.368$ MPa ≈ **3.7 bar**.

This matches the empirical *blow pressure range of 0.4 – 0.8 MPa* observed in HDPE EBM lines.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cooling Time by Transient Conduction

> **Statement:** *Estimate the cooling time required to solidify a 1.5 mm HDPE parison in contact with a chilled aluminium mould, using the lumped-capacitance approximation and the semi-infinite-slab solution.*

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the transient conduction equation.**

For a slab of thickness $t_w$ initially at $T_i$ and surface at $T_s$, the dimensionless temperature is:

$$
\theta \;=\; \dfrac{T(x,t) - T_s}{T_i - T_s}
$$

**Step 2 — Use the semi-infinite-slab solution at the centreline.**

$$
\theta_{\text{centre}} \;=\; \dfrac{4}{\pi} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^n}{2n+1} \exp\!\left[-\,\dfrac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 \, \alpha \, t}{4 \, t_w^{\,2}}\right]
$$

For $t$ sufficiently long that only the **n = 0 term dominates**:

$$
\theta_{\text{centre}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{4}{\pi} \exp\!\left[-\,\dfrac{\pi^2 \, \alpha \, t}{4 \, t_w^{\,2}}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for time.**

$$
t_{\text{cool}} \;=\; -\,\dfrac{4 \, t_w^{\,2}}{\pi^2 \, \alpha} \;\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\pi \, \theta_{\text{eject}}}{4}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Insert numerical values.**
- $\alpha = k / (\rho \, c_p)$ for HDPE: $k = 0.5$ W/(m·K), $\rho = 950$ kg/m³, $c_p = 2300$ J/(kg·K).
- $\alpha = 0.5 / (950 \times 2300) = 2.29 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s.
- $t_w = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m.
- $\theta_{\text{eject}} = (T_{\text{eject}} - T_s)/(T_i - T_s) = (110 - 30)/(220 - 30) = 0.42$.

$$
t_{\text{cool}} \;=\; -\,\dfrac{4 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{\pi^2 \times 2.29 \times 10^{-7}} \;\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\pi \times 0.42}{4}\right)
$$

$$
t_{\text{cool}} \;=\; -\,\dfrac{9 \times 10^{-6}}{2.26 \times 10^{-6}} \;\ln(0.330)
$$

$$
t_{\text{cool}} \;=\; -\,3.98 \times (-1.108) \;\approx\; 4.4 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Conclusion:**
$$
\boxed{\; t_{\text{cool}} \;\approx\; 4.4 \text{ s for a 1.5 mm HDPE parison} \;}
$$

This sets the *minimum cycle time* for a single-cavity EBM station.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Parison Sag (Viscoelastic Elongation)

> **Statement:** *Estimate the axial elongation of an extruded HDPE parison hanging under its own weight during the time between extrusion and mould clamping.*

**Step 1 — Model the parison as a cylindrical viscoelastic rod.**
The axial stress at the bottom of a hanging parison of length $L_p$ and density $\rho$ is:

$$
\sigma_{\text{axial}} \;=\; \rho \, g \, L_p
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to creep strain using a simple Maxwell element.**
For a Maxwell fluid of viscosity $\eta$ and modulus $E$:

$$
\dot{\epsilon} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \epsilon(t) \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma \, t}{\eta} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho \, g \, L_p \, t}{\eta}
$$

**Step 3 — A more accurate estimate uses the apparent modulus $E_a$:**

$$
\epsilon_{\text{sag}}(t) \;=\; \dfrac{\rho \, g \, L_p^{\,2}}{2 \, E_a} \cdot t
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical sanity check.**
HDPE parison: $\rho = 950$ kg/m³, $L_p = 0.5$ m, $E_a = 5 \times 10^4$ Pa (melt at 200 °C), $t = 2$ s.

$$
\epsilon_{\text{sag}} \;=\; \dfrac{950 \times 9.81 \times (0.5)^2}{2 \times 5 \times 10^4} \times 2 \;=\; 0.047 \;\approx\; 4.7\,\%
$$

This explains why **accumulator heads** are mandatory for high-MFI materials — parison sag > 5 % causes inconsistent wall thickness.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Strain-Induced Crystallisation in PET Stretch-Blow

> **Statement:** *Show that the in-plane biaxial stretch ratio governs the final crystallinity of a PET bottle.*

The **total areal stretch ratio** is:

$$
S_{\text{total}} \;=\; S_{\text{axial}} \times S_{\text{hoop}}
$$

For PET, the empirical correlation for final crystallinity $X_c$ is:

$$
\boxed{\; X_c \;=\; X_{c,\text{max}} \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-k \, (S_{\text{total}} - 1)^n\right)\right] \;}
$$

with $X_{c,\text{max}} = 0.30$, $k = 0.18$, $n = 1.4$ for typical CSD bottles.

**Numerical example:**
For $S_{\text{total}} = 12$:

$$
X_c \;=\; 0.30 \times \left[1 - \exp(-0.18 \times 11^{1.4})\right] \;=\; 0.30 \times \left[1 - \exp(-4.04)\right]
$$

$$
X_c \;=\; 0.30 \times (1 - 0.0175) \;\approx\; 0.295 \;\approx\; 29.5\,\%
$$

This matches the empirical 25 – 30 % crystallinity measured by DSC on commercial PET CSD bottles.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Variant Comparison

| Parameter | EBM | IBM | SBM (PET) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parison source | Extruder | Injection mould | Injection mould + reheating |
| Neck finish quality | Poor (must be reamed) | Excellent (as-moulded) | Excellent |
| Flash / scrap | High (15 – 30 %) | Zero | Zero |
| Tooling cost | Low | High (3-station) | Very high |
| Cycle time | 6 – 20 s | 4 – 10 s | 2 – 6 s |
| Material | HDPE, LDPE, PP | HDPE, PP, PET | PET, PEN |
| Production volume | Medium-high | Low-medium | Ultra-high |
| Clarity | Translucent | Clear | Crystal-clear |
| Orientation | None | Low | High (biaxial) |
| Barrier property | Moderate | Moderate | Excellent |
| Examples | Milk cans, drums | Pharmaceutical bottles | Soft-drink bottles |

---

## 3.7 Defects and Their Root Causes (KTU High-Yield)

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| **Bottleneck ovality** | Insufficient material in neck region | Use profiled parison programming |
| **Weld / parting line weakness** | Poor fusion at pinch-off | Increase pinch-land width, raise melt $T$ |
| **Bubbles / voids** | Air entrapment during inflation | Increase blow rate, degas melt |
| **Sink marks** | Local thick section, slow cooling | Optimise mould cooling channels |
| **Flash** | Excessive parison, poor pinch-off | Reduce parison length, sharpen pinch edge |
| **Orange peel** | Mould too cold | Raise mould $T$ to 40 – 60 °C |
| **Haze / poor clarity** | Low mould $T$ for amorphous PET | Optimise stretch ratio |
