# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PEPOT748]
**Q: Differentiate between extrusion blow moulding and injection blow moulding. List three advantages of IBM over EBM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | EBM | IBM |
|---|---|---|
| Parison source | Continuously extruded tube | Injection-moulded preform on a core pin |
| Neck finish | Formed by blow pin, requires reaming | Fully moulded at injection, no reaming |
| Flash / scrap | 15 – 30 % | Zero |
| Cycle time | Slower (6 – 20 s) | Faster (4 – 10 s) |

**Three advantages of IBM over EBM:**

1. **Zero flash / zero scrap** — the preform is fully enclosed in the mould, eliminating pinch-off scrap and saving material cost.
2. **Superior neck finish** — the bottle thread is moulded to ±0.05 mm tolerance, eliminating the need for post-mould reaming and enabling direct cap fitment.
3. **Higher dimensional accuracy and clarity** — injection-moulded preforms have controlled crystallinity and no weld-line contamination, giving better optical and mechanical properties.

*(1 mark for differentiation table, 2 marks for the three IBM advantages — examiner's distribution.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]
**Q: What is the Blow-Up Ratio (BUR) in blow moulding? State the typical BUR range for HDPE bottles and explain what happens if BUR exceeds 5.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** BUR is the ratio of the maximum diameter of the blow-moulded bottle to the outer diameter of the extruded parison:

$$
\text{BUR} = \dfrac{D_{\text{bottle}}}{D_{\text{parison}}}
$$

- **Typical range for HDPE (1 mark):** BUR = **1.5 : 1 to 4 : 1**.
- **Effect of BUR > 5 (1 mark):** When BUR exceeds 5, the parison wall becomes excessively thin (since $t_w = t_p / \text{BUR}$), leading to *non-uniform wall distribution*, *splitting during inflation*, *poor surface finish*, and *reduced mechanical strength*. The polymer chains also become over-stretched, risking *brittle failure* under drop-impact.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **omit the derivation of BUR** from mass conservation and merely *state* the formula — this costs the 2 marks reserved for *derivation / proof* sub-parts.
> - In stretch-blow questions, students **confuse axial stretch ratio with hoop stretch ratio** — both must be defined and computed separately.
> - Numerical answers must carry **units** in every step; missing units can cost 1 mark per sub-question even when the final number is correct.
> - Avoid *hand-waving* the cooling time derivation — write out the Fourier conduction assumption explicitly to claim full marks.
> - In IBM/EBM comparison questions, students who list only 2 differences lose the third mark — always list *at least* three points in tabular form.

---

### Question B (Choice) — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper, Module 3, Q3(a)]**

**(a)** Explain the **Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)** process in detail, with a neat schematic. Discuss the role of the **accumulator head** and **parison programming** in controlling wall thickness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 750-ml HDPE bottle is to be blow-moulded from a parison of outer diameter **20 mm** and wall thickness **1.2 mm**. If the bottle outer diameter is **75 mm**, calculate:
- (i) The Blow-Up Ratio.
- (ii) The final bottle wall thickness, assuming isochoric inflation.
- (iii) The minimum blow pressure if the polymer yield stress at inflation temperature is **5 MPa** and the cavity radius is **37.5 mm**.
- (iv) Comment on whether the calculated wall thickness is suitable for a carbonated beverage bottle. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Process flow (3 marks):**

The Extrusion Blow Moulding cycle proceeds through the following stages:

1. **Plasticisation:** HDPE resin is fed into a single-screw extruder, melted at **180 – 230 °C**, and metered at a specific throughput $\dot{m}$.
2. **Parison formation:** The melt is forced through an *annular die* to form a hollow tube (parison) that descends vertically under gravity.
3. **Mould closure:** Two mould halves close around the parison, pinching one end (bottom) and forming the other end (neck) via a *blow pin* and *calibrated neck ring*.
4. **Inflation:** Compressed air at **0.4 – 0.8 MPa** is injected through the blow pin, expanding the parison against the chilled mould walls (mould at **20 – 40 °C**).
5. **Cooling & ejection:** After **4 – 12 seconds** of conductive cooling, the mould opens and the part is ejected; the flash is trimmed.

**Step 2 — Accumulator head (2 marks):**

For high-MFI polymers or large parisons, the melt accumulates in a *ram-accumulator* between the extruder and the die. This **decouples extruder throughput from parison drop time**, eliminating:
- *Parison sag* (gravity-driven elongation) — sag can be 5 – 10 % without an accumulator.
- *Melt temperature variation* between successive parisons.
- *Throughput limitations* for large fuel-tank parisons.

**Step 3 — Parison programming (2 marks):**

Modern EBM machines use a *hydraulic servo* to axially move the *die mandrel* during parison extrusion, dynamically changing the annular gap. This produces a *variable-thickness parison* whose local thickness is *inversely proportional* to the local stretch required by the mould. The result is a bottle with **uniform wall thickness ±5 %**, despite complex geometry (handles, shoulders, corners).

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
$D_p = 20$ mm, $t_p = 1.2$ mm, $D_b = 75$ mm, $\sigma_y = 5$ MPa, $R_c = 37.5$ mm.

**(i) Blow-Up Ratio (2 marks):**

$$
\text{BUR} = \dfrac{D_b}{D_p} = \dfrac{75}{20} = \mathbf{3.75}
$$

**(ii) Final wall thickness (2 marks):**

$$
t_w = \dfrac{t_p}{\text{BUR}} = \dfrac{1.2}{3.75} = \mathbf{0.32 \; mm}
$$

**(iii) Minimum blow pressure (2 marks):**

$$
P_{b,\text{min}} = \dfrac{2 \, \sigma_y \, t_w}{R_c} + P_{\text{atm}} = \dfrac{2 \times 5 \times 0.32}{37.5} + 0.101
$$

$$
P_{b,\text{min}} = \dfrac{3.2}{37.5} + 0.101 = 0.0853 + 0.101 = \mathbf{0.186 \; MPa \; \approx \; 1.86 \; bar}
$$

**(iv) Suitability for carbonated beverage (1 mark):**

A wall thickness of **0.32 mm** is **insufficient** for a 750-ml CSD bottle, because:
- CSD bottles require a minimum wall thickness of **0.45 – 0.60 mm** to withstand internal pressure of 0.5 – 0.8 MPa from carbonation.
- A higher parison thickness (e.g., 2.0 mm) or lower BUR is recommended to achieve a robust container.
- **Recommendation:** Increase $t_p$ to **1.8 mm** to obtain $t_w = 1.8 / 3.75 = 0.48$ mm, which is within the safe design range.

---

### Question B (Choice) — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Paper, Module 3, Q4(b)]**

**(a)** Discuss the **Stretch Blow Moulding (SBM)** process used for PET carbonated soft-drink bottles. Explain the role of *biaxial orientation* in improving the mechanical and barrier properties of PET. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PET preform has a diameter of **25 mm** and a length of **120 mm**. It is stretch-blown into a bottle of diameter **75 mm** and length **270 mm** with a wall thickness of **0.30 mm**.
- (i) Calculate the *axial stretch ratio* and *hoop stretch ratio*.
- (ii) Calculate the *total areal stretch ratio* and the *final crystallinity* using the empirical correlation $X_c = 0.30 [1 - \exp(-0.18 (S_{\text{total}} - 1)^{1.4})]$.
- (iii) Comment on whether the resulting bottle is suitable for a carbonated beverage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Process description (3 marks):**

The PET Stretch Blow Moulding process is a *two-step* (or *single-step*) variant of injection blow moulding designed specifically for biaxially oriented PET (BOPET) bottles:

1. **Preform injection:** PET is injection-moulded at **270 – 285 °C** into a test-tube-shaped preform with a finished neck. The preform is amorphous (crystallinity < 5 %).
2. **Reheating:** Preforms are stored, then re-heated to **100 – 120 °C** (above $T_g = 76$ °C, below crystallisation onset) by infrared lamps.
3. **Stretching:** A *stretch rod* advances into the preform, axially stretching it by a ratio of typically **2.0 – 2.5**.
4. **Blowing:** Compressed air at **3 – 4 MPa** radially inflates the preform while the stretch rod holds the axial position, achieving hoop stretch ratios of **3.5 – 4.5**.
5. **Cooling:** The oriented bottle cools in the mould, with strain-induced crystallisation raising the final crystallinity to **25 – 30 %**.

**Step 2 — Biaxial orientation and property enhancement (4 marks):**

During biaxial stretching, the *amorphous polymer chains* are forced to align in the plane of the bottle wall. This produces:

- **Improved tensile strength:** modulus rises from ~**2.5 GPa** (isotropic PET) to ~**4.0 – 6.0 GPa** (biaxially oriented PET).
- **Improved impact resistance:** ductile behaviour at drop heights up to **2 metres** for 500-ml CSD bottles.
- **Improved gas barrier:** O₂ permeability drops by **3 – 5×** and CO₂ retention improves by **4 – 6×**, which is *critical* for carbonated beverages with a 6 – 12 month shelf life.
- **Improved clarity:** biaxial orientation suppresses crystallite size above the wavelength of visible light, giving **> 90 % transmittance** and the characteristic glass-like sparkle.
- **Improved creep resistance:** oriented PET retains shape under long-term stacking loads.

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
$D_p = 25$ mm, $L_p = 120$ mm, $D_b = 75$ mm, $L_b = 270$ mm, $t_w = 0.30$ mm.

**(i) Stretch ratios (3 marks):**

$$
S_{\text{axial}} = \dfrac{L_b}{L_p} = \dfrac{270}{120} = \mathbf{2.25}
$$

$$
S_{\text{hoop}} = \dfrac{D_b}{D_p} = \dfrac{75}{25} = \mathbf{3.00}
$$

**(ii) Total areal stretch ratio and crystallinity (3 marks):**

$$
S_{\text{total}} = S_{\text{axial}} \times S_{\text{hoop}} = 2.25 \times 3.00 = \mathbf{6.75}
$$

Using the empirical correlation:

$$
X_c = 0.30 \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-0.18 \times (6.75 - 1)^{1.4}\right)\right]
$$

$$
X_c = 0.30 \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-0.18 \times 5.75^{1.4}\right)\right] = 0.30 \left[1 - \exp(-0.18 \times 11.39)\right]
$$

$$
X_c = 0.30 \left[1 - \exp(-2.05)\right] = 0.30 \times (1 - 0.1287) = 0.30 \times 0.871 = \mathbf{0.261 \; \text{or} \; 26.1\,\%}
$$

**(iii) Suitability assessment (1 mark):**

The total stretch ratio of **6.75** is *below* the recommended industry range of **10 – 16** for carbonated beverage bottles. Consequences:

- Crystallinity of 26 % is acceptable but on the lower end (commercial CSD bottles are typically 28 – 32 %).
- **Recommendation:** Increase either $S_{\text{axial}}$ (longer bottle) or $S_{\text{hoop}}$ (wider bottle) to achieve $S_{\text{total}} \geq 10$, ensuring adequate biaxial orientation, gas barrier, and burst strength for carbonation pressures of 0.5 – 0.8 MPa at 25 °C.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and carry it to the exam hall for a final 5-minute revision pass.**

- ✅ Blow moulding is the **dominant process** for hollow plastic packaging containers (HDPE, PET, PP).
- ✅ The **five canonical stages**: parison formation → mould closure → inflation → cooling → ejection.
- ✅ **Three principal variants**: EBM, IBM, SBM.
- ✅ **BUR = $D_b / D_p$**; wall thickness $t_w = t_p / \text{BUR}$.
- ✅ **Minimum blow pressure** = $\dfrac{2 \, \sigma_y \, t_w}{R_c} + P_{\text{atm}}$.
- ✅ **Cooling time** scales with $t_w^{\,2}$ — doubling wall thickness *quadruples* cycle time.
- ✅ **EBM** has 15 – 30 % flash; **IBM** and **SBM** have *zero* flash.
- ✅ **PET SBM** uses biaxial stretching; optimal $S_{\text{total}}$ = **10 – 16**.
- ✅ **Accumulator head** eliminates parison sag for large or high-MFI containers.
- ✅ **Parison programming** uses servo-driven die mandrels to achieve uniform wall thickness (±5 %).
- ✅ **Co-extrusion blow moulding** stacks 5 – 7 layers (HDPE / tie / EVOH / tie / HDPE) for barrier packaging.
- ✅ **Defects to memorise**: bottleneck ovality, weld-line weakness, sink marks, parison sag, flash, orange peel, haze.
- ✅ **HDPE** process temp: 180 – 230 °C; **PET** injection: 270 – 285 °C; **PET** stretch window: 100 – 120 °C.
- ✅ Typical **blow pressure** range: 0.4 – 0.8 MPa; **cycle time** range: 6 – 30 s.
- ✅ **Strain-induced crystallisation** (Avrami-type) gives PET CSD bottles 25 – 30 % crystallinity and 4 – 6× improvement in CO₂ retention.
- ✅ **Aluminium moulds** are 60 % cheaper than steel and are used for short runs; **steel moulds** are used for high-volume production (> 1 million cycles).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extrusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Extrusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Extrusion** is a continuous, high-volume thermo-mechanical polymer processing operation in which a thermoplastic material (in the form of pellets, granules, or powder) is **plasticated, melted, homogenized, and continuously forced under high pressure through a shaped die** to produce a continuous profile of uniform cross-section. The solidified (or still molten) extrudate is then cooled, sized, hauled off, and cut (or wound) into the desired packaging form such as films, sheets, pipes, profiles, blown bottles, or coatings.

In the context of **Plastics Packaging Technology (PEPOT748)**, extrusion is the **primary conversion process** used to manufacture the vast majority of flexible and rigid packaging structures — including **LDPE/LLDPE/HDPE films, multilayer co-extruded barrier films, PP/PS sheets, drinking straws, tubes, and parisons for blow moulding**.

The single most important piece of equipment is the **Screw Extruder**, which performs the dual functions of:
1. **Plastics processing** (melting, mixing, conveying) — analogous to a heated positive-displacement pump.
2. **Pressure generation** — to push the homogeneous melt through the downstream die assembly.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Giant Heated Meat Mincer with a Shaped Nozzle**
>
> Imagine pushing a block of cold butter through a garlic press. Hard work, messy, and the butter comes out chunky. Now replace the butter with **softened clay**, push it through a **hollow metal tube wrapped in a heater**, and use a **rotating Archimedes screw** (like a corkscrew) inside the tube to drag the clay forward while heating it. Out of the other end, attach a **shaped disc (the die)** — a slit, a ring, a star — and the softened clay exits as a continuous ribbon, tube, or star-shaped profile that you can cut into packaging components.
>
> The extruder is exactly this: a **heated barrel + a rotating screw + a shaping die**. Plastics pellets go in at the **hopper**, the screw drags, melts, mixes, and pressurises them, and the die shapes them into the continuous packaging form (film, sheet, tube, coating).

## 1.3 Classification of Extruders (At a Glance)

| Type | Drive / Mechanism | Typical Use in Packaging |
|------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| **Single-Screw Extruder (SSE)** | One flighted screw in a heated barrel | Films, sheets, pipes, blow moulding parisons, profiles |
| **Counter-Rotating Twin-Screw Extruder (CR-TSE)** | Two intermeshing screws, rotating in opposite directions | Compounding, rigid PVC, scrap recycling |
| **Co-Rotating Twin-Screw Extruder (CO-TSE)** | Two intermeshing screws, rotating in the same direction | Compounding, masterbatch, co-extrusion feed |
| **Ram Extruder** | Plunger (no screw) | PTFE (Teflon), ultra-high-viscosity polymers, UHMWPE |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the KTU 2024 syllabus, the **Single-Screw Extruder** is the **most heavily tested** because it is the workhorse of the packaging industry. The remaining notes are therefore built around this machine.

## 1.4 Why Extrusion Dominates Packaging Manufacturing

- **Continuous** → high productivity, low unit cost.
- **Versatile** → one machine can produce films, sheets, tubes, coatings, profiles, and parisons simply by **changing the die**.
- **Compatible with multi-layer co-extrusion** → essential for barrier packaging (e.g., EVOH / PA / PE).
- **Easily coupled** with downstream units (calender, chill-roll, blown-film tower, corrugator, thermoformer).
- **Wastes** (edge trim, regrind) can be reprocessed at the feed throat — high material economy.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Single-Screw Extruder

A single-screw extruder, although visually a long horizontal cylinder, is a **precisely engineered multi-zone reactor**. Reading the machine from upstream (feed) to downstream (die), the polymer encounters the following subsystems:

1. **Feed Hopper** — gravity-fed reservoir of solid pellets. Often equipped with a **crammer feeder** for high-throughput lines.
2. **Barrel** — a thick-walled, bimetallic (e.g., nitrided steel inner liner) cylinder, externally wrapped in **band heaters** (typically 3 to 5 zones, each independently PID-controlled). Internal bore diameter is **D** (the **barrel inner diameter**).
3. **Screw** — a long, hardened steel rotor with a helical flight. Outer root diameter **D**, inner flight-tip diameter **D_o**, helix angle **θ**, channel depth **H**, channel width **W**, flight width **e**.
4. **Screw Tip / Breaker Plate & Screen Pack** — at the discharge end. A perforated plate supporting a **wire-mesh screen pack** (typically 20/40/60/80 mesh) acts as a **melt filter**, removes unmelted particles, and converts **rotational (drag) flow into axial (pressure) flow** by eliminating the swirling vortex.
5. **Adapter & Die Assembly** — shapes the molten extrudate. The die land length **L_d** provides the final pressure drop.
6. **Downstream Equipment** — chill rolls, air rings (blown film), calibrators, haul-off, cutter/winder.
7. **Drive & Thrust Bearing Assembly** — typically a thyristor/DC/AC variable-speed motor coupled through a gear reducer to the screw; rated for the **screw thrust load** generated by the internal pressure.

## 2.2 The Three Functional Zones of the Screw

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The screw is **not uniform**. Its **channel depth (H) and pitch (and therefore compression ratio)** change along its length to perform three sequential jobs. For a typical L/D = 24:1 to 30:1 screw used in film/sheet extrusion:

| Zone | Function | Length (approx.) | Channel Depth (H) | Mechanism |
|------|----------|------------------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Feed Zone (Solids Conveying)** | Compacts and conveys cold pellets | 25 – 30 % of L | Deepest, constant (~0.15 D for thermoplastics) | Friction between pellets, barrel wall, and screw root |
| **Compression / Transition Zone (Melting)** | Melts the compacted bed, mixes melt with residual solids | 30 – 40 % of L | Tapers down (e.g., 0.15 D → 0.05 D) | Combination of barrel heating + **shear heating** (viscous dissipation) |
| **Metering Zone (Melt Conveying / Pumping)** | Homogenises melt and pumps it at a steady rate | 25 – 35 % of L | Shallowest, constant (~0.05 D for thermoplastics) | **Drag flow** of a viscous fluid in a rectangular channel |

**Compression Ratio (CR)** is defined as:

$$
\text{CR} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Volume of one flight in the feed zone}}{\text{Volume of one flight in the metering zone}} \;=\; \dfrac{H_f \, W_f}{H_m \, W_m}
$$

Typical values: **2.5 : 1 to 3.0 : 1** for polyolefins; **1.5 : 1 to 2.0 : 1** for PVC and other heat-sensitive polymers.

## 2.3 The Three Components of Extruder Output

The volumetric output rate **Q** (m³/s) of a melt-pumping (metering) extruder is the **algebraic sum of three independent flow contributions**. This is the **single most important equation in extrusion theory** for KTU:

$$
Q \;=\; Q_d \;-\; Q_p \;-\; Q_f
$$

where:

- **Q_d** = **Drag Flow** — the forward pumping caused by the relative motion of the screw flight over the stationary barrel wall (the "Archimedes pump" component).
- **Q_p** = **Pressure Flow** — the *backward* leak driven by the pressure gradient that the extruder itself builds up at the die. It always **subtracts** from the output.
- **Q_f** = **Leakage (Flight) Flow** — the small back-flow through the *clearance* between the screw flight tip and the barrel wall. Usually **small (≤ 5 % of Q_d)** and often neglected in KTU problems.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** *"D minus P minus F — what drags forward, what pressures back, and what slips through the flights."*

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the following expressions — they appear in almost every KTU ESE question on extrusion.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Drag flow rate | $Q_d \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^{2}\,D^{2}\,N\,d\,\sin\theta\cos\theta$ | m³/s | $D$ = barrel ID, $d$ = channel depth (= $H_m$ in metering zone), $N$ = screw rpm, $\theta$ = helix angle. |
| 2 | Drag flow — simplified (using $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta - e$) | $Q_d \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^{2}\,D^{2}\,N\,d\,\sin\theta\cos\theta$ | m³/s | Identical to (1); often written with channel width $W$ explicitly: $Q_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N d\sin\theta\cos\theta$. |
| 3 | Pressure flow (Newtonian melt) | $Q_p \;=\; \dfrac{\pi\,D\,d^{3}\,\sin^{2}\theta}{12\,\eta\,L_m}\,\Delta P$ | m³/s | $L_m$ = axial length of metering zone; $\eta$ = melt viscosity. |
| 4 | Net output | $Q \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N d\sin\theta\cos\theta \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}{12 \eta L_m}\Delta P$ | m³/s | Ignoring flight leakage $Q_f$. |
| 5 | Throughput (mass) | $\dot{m} \;=\; \rho_{melt}\,Q$ | kg/s | $\rho_{melt}$ ≈ 0.74 – 0.97 g/cm³ for polyolefins. |
| 6 | Specific energy | $E_{sp} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Input power}}{\dot{m}}$ | J/kg | Typical range: $3 \times 10^{6}$ – $6 \times 10^{6}$ J/kg for LDPE film. |
| 7 | Compression ratio | $\text{CR} \;=\; H_f / H_m$ | – | For uniform $W$. |
| 8 | Extruder characterisation line | $\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{12\,\eta\,L_m}{\pi\,D\,d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}\,\Bigl(\tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N d\sin\theta\cos\theta - Q\Bigr)$ | Pa | Useful for die / pump matching. |
| 9 | Shear rate in metering channel (approx.) | $\dot{\gamma} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D N}{d - \delta}$ (where $\delta$ is flight clearance) | s⁻¹ | $10^{2}$ – $10^{3}$ s⁻¹ typical. |
| 10 | Die-swell (extrudate swell) ratio | $B \;=\; D_{extrudate} / D_{die}$ | – | 1.10 – 1.50 for polyolefins; due to elastic recoil. |
| 11 | Mean residence time | $\bar{t} \;=\; V_{free} / Q$ | s | $V_{free}$ = free volume in the metering channel. |
| 12 | Power required to drive the screw (approx.) | $P \;=\; \tau_{avg} \,\pi D L N$ | W | Used for motor sizing. |

> [!NOTE]
> In all the above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ (absolute value) notation has been written as `vert` or `mid` to keep the markdown table parser safe — but when writing the equations inline in the KTU answer sheet, use standard $\vert$ symbols.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Equations

- **Sizing a new extruder**: Knowing the required throughput (kg/h) of an LDPE packaging film line, the manufacturer solves Equation (4) for a feasible $D$, $N$ pair, then selects a screw with the matching compression ratio.
- **Troubleshooting a fluctuating output**: A drifting melt temperature changes $\eta$, which in turn changes $Q_p$ and hence $Q$. The line operator detects the surge and tightens the heater band tuning.
- **Die-swell compensation**: In tubing/straw extrusion, the die is intentionally **undercut** by the inverse of the swell ratio $B$ to deliver a finished product of correct diameter.
- **Energy audit**: The specific energy $E_{sp}$ quantifies how much electrical energy is consumed per kg of packaging produced — a key KPI in sustainable packaging.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Net Output Equation $Q = Q_d - Q_p$

We model the melt inside the metering-zone channel as a **Newtonian fluid** in steady, laminar, fully-developed flow. We then "unwind" the helical channel and analyse flow between two parallel plates:

- **Lower plate** (the screw root) moves with tangential velocity $\pi D N$ at helix angle $\theta$ relative to the channel axis.
- **Upper plate** (the barrel wall) is **stationary**.

This geometric *unwinding* converts the helical coordinate system into a flat Cartesian channel of width $W$ and depth $d$ (channel depth = $H_m$).

### Step 1 — Decompose the barrel velocity

The barrel's tangential surface velocity $V_b = \pi D N$ has two components relative to the channel axis (the down-channel direction, $z$):

- **Down-channel component** (drives the fluid forward): $V_{bz} \;=\; \pi D N \cos\theta$
- **Cross-channel component** (causes circulatory secondary flow, ignored in 1-D theory): $V_{bx} \;=\; \pi D N \sin\theta$

### Step 2 — Drag flow from Couette-Poiseuille superposition

The volumetric flow per unit width in a rectangular channel of depth $d$ with relative plate motion $V_{bz}$ and a pressure gradient $dP/dz$ is a standard result of lubrication theory:

$$
q(z) \;=\; \underbrace{\dfrac{V_{bz}\,d}{2}}_{\text{Couette (drag) component}} \;-\; \underbrace{\dfrac{d^{3}}{12\,\eta}\,\dfrac{dP}{dz}}_{\text{Poiseuille (pressure) component}}
$$

> **Couette part** — pure shear of a Newtonian fluid between a moving and a stationary plate; the velocity profile is linear, so the average velocity is half the plate velocity, multiplied by the channel depth.

> **Poiseuille part** — a pressure gradient $dP/dz < 0$ drives a parabolic (back-flow) profile; the volumetric flow is the well-known result $-(d^3/12\eta)(dP/dz)$.

### Step 3 — Integrate over the channel width

The total volumetric flow is $Q = W \cdot q(z)$, where $W$ is the channel width measured perpendicular to the flight:

$$
Q \;=\; \dfrac{V_{bz}\,W\,d}{2} \;-\; \dfrac{W\,d^{3}}{12\,\eta}\,\dfrac{dP}{dz}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $V_{bz} = \pi D N \cos\theta$ and $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta - e$

The width of the helical channel (perpendicular to the flights) is $W \;\approx\; \pi D \sin\theta - e$. For simplicity in KTU derivations, the flight width $e$ is **neglected**, giving $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta$. Substituting:

$$
Q \;=\; \dfrac{(\pi D N \cos\theta)(\pi D \sin\theta)\,d}{2} \;-\; \dfrac{(\pi D \sin\theta)\,d^{3}}{12\,\eta}\,\dfrac{dP}{dz}
$$

Simplify the trigonometric product $\sin\theta \cos\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\theta$:

$$
Q \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N\,d\,\sin\theta\cos\theta \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D d^{3}\sin\theta}{12\,\eta}\,\dfrac{dP}{dz}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert the axial pressure gradient to a finite pressure difference

The pressure at the start of the metering zone is approximately atmospheric, and at its end it rises to the head pressure $\Delta P$. Assuming a **linear pressure rise** over the axial length of the metering zone $L_m$:

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dz} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{L_m}
$$

Substitute this into the expression for $Q$:

$$
Q \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N\,d\,\sin\theta\cos\theta \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}{12\,\eta\,L_m}\,\Delta P
$$

### Step 6 — Identify the two terms as $Q_d$ and $Q_p$

This is exactly the expression listed in the formula sheet:

$$
\boxed{\,Q \;=\; \underbrace{\dfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N\,d\,\sin\theta\cos\theta}_{Q_d\,\text{(drag flow, forward)}} \;-\; \underbrace{\dfrac{\pi D d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}{12\,\eta\,L_m}\,\Delta P}_{Q_p\,\text{(pressure flow, backward)}}\,}
$$

If the flight clearance leakage $Q_f$ is included:

$$
Q \;=\; Q_d \;-\; Q_p \;-\; Q_f
$$

### Step 3.2 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A single-screw extruder used for LDPE packaging film has the following data. Calculate the volumetric output $Q$ and the mass throughput $\dot{m}$.
>
> - Barrel inner diameter: $D = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
> - Metering channel depth: $d = 3.0 \text{ mm} = 0.003 \text{ m}$
> - Helix angle: $\theta = 17.65^{\circ}$ (so $\sin\theta = 0.303$, $\cos\theta = 0.953$)
> - Screw speed: $N = 60 \text{ rpm} = 1.0 \text{ rev/s}$
> - Axial length of metering zone: $L_m = 0.75 \text{ m}$
> - Pressure developed at die: $\Delta P = 7 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$
> - Melt viscosity of LDPE at processing temperature: $\eta = 350 \text{ Pa·s}$
> - Density of molten LDPE: $\rho_{melt} = 780 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$

**Step 1 — Compute $Q_d$:**

$$
Q_d \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^{2}\,(0.075)^{2}\,(1.0)\,(0.003)\,(0.303)(0.953)
$$

Compute the coefficient $\tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2} \approx 4.9348$:

$$
Q_d \;=\; 4.9348 \times (0.005625) \times (0.003) \times 0.2887
$$

$$
Q_d \;=\; 4.9348 \times 0.005625 \times 0.000866
$$

$$
Q_d \;\approx\; 2.41 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $Q_p$:**

$$
Q_p \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (0.075)(0.003)^{3}\,(0.303)^{2}}{12\,(350)\,(0.75)}\;\times\;(7 \times 10^{6})
$$

Numerator:

$$
\pi \times 0.075 \times 2.7\times10^{-8} \times 0.0918 \times 7\times10^{6}
$$

$$
\;\approx\; 3.1416 \times 0.075 \times 2.7\times10^{-8} \times 0.0918 \times 7\times10^{6}
$$

$$
\;\approx\; 4.36 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

Denominator:

$$
12 \times 350 \times 0.75 \;=\; 3150
$$

So:

$$
Q_p \;=\; \dfrac{4.36 \times 10^{-6}}{3150} \;\approx\; 1.38 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Net output (ignoring $Q_f$):**

$$
Q \;=\; Q_d - Q_p \;=\; 2.41 \times 10^{-5} - 1.38 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
Q \;\approx\; 2.27 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass throughput:**

$$
\dot{m} \;=\; \rho_{melt}\,Q \;=\; 780 \times 2.27 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\dot{m} \;\approx\; 1.77 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/s} \;\approx\; 63.7 \text{ kg/h}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** A 63.7 kg/h net throughput is realistic for a 75 mm extruder running at 60 rpm on LDPE film — KTU board examiners love this scale of answer because it confirms that the student understood the magnitudes involved.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Optimal Helix Angle $\theta = 20°$ to $30°$

The drag flow expression $Q_d \propto \sin\theta\cos\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\theta$ is **maximised** when $\sin 2\theta = 1$, i.e., $2\theta = 90°$, $\theta = 45°$. However, at $\theta = 45°$ the pressure-flow term also **grows rapidly** (proportional to $\sin^2\theta$), the screw becomes a *poor pump*, and the residence time distribution becomes unfavourable. In practice, the **optimum compromise** between high drag flow, reasonable pressure-generating ability, and good solids conveying is:

$$
\theta_{opt} \;\approx\; 20^{\circ} \text{ to } 30^{\circ}
$$

For LDPE/PP, screws are commonly cut at $\theta = 17.65°$ (which is the angle for a square-pitched flight where the pitch equals $D$, and $\tan\theta = 1/\pi$). The KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to be able to state the **optimum range** and justify it qualitatively.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Single-Screw Extrusion Line

The following Mermaid block renders the upstream → downstream flow of material and information, which is the typical "block diagram" KTU expects in 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hopper with Solid Pellets] --> B[Feed Throat and Gravity Feeder]
    B --> C[Feed Zone of Screw - Solids Conveying]
    C --> D[Compression Zone - Melting by Shear and Conduction]
    D --> E[Metering Zone - Melt Pumping and Mixing]
    E --> F[Breaker Plate and Screen Pack - Melt Filter]
    F --> G[Adapter and Shaped Die]
    G --> H[Haul Off and Cooling]
    H --> I[Take Up Winder or Cutter]
    H2[Thrust Bearing] --> E
    J[PID Heater Bands Zone 1] --> C
    K[PID Heater Bands Zone 2] --> D
    L[PID Heater Bands Zone 3] --> E
    M[Drive Motor and Gearbox] --> C
    M2[Pressure Transducer] --- G
    M2 --> N[Controller]
    N --> J
    N --> K
    N --> L
    N --> M
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **upper horizontal chain (A → I)** is the **material path** — polymer pellets in, finished packaging out.
- The **lower chain (J, K, L, M)** is the **energy and control path** — heaters and motor that drive the process.
- The **M2 → N** path is the **feedback loop** — the die-pressure transducer signals the controller, which modulates screw speed and heater powers to keep output stable.

## 4.2 Screw Geometry — Functional Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    scRoot[Screw Root - Solid Steel Shaft]
    scFlight1[Feed Zone Flights - Deep Channel - Constant Pitch]
    scFlight2[Compression Zone Flights - Tapered Channel - Decreasing Depth]
    scFlight3[Metering Zone Flights - Shallow Channel - Constant Pitch]
    scTip[Screw Tip and Drive Dog]
    scFlight1 --> scFlight2
    scFlight2 --> scFlight3
    scFlight3 --> scTip
```

**Annotations (key dimensions):**

- **Helix angle $\theta$** — measured between the flight edge and the plane perpendicular to the screw axis.
- **Channel depth $d$** — perpendicular distance from the screw root to the flight tip.
- **Channel width $W$** — measured perpendicular to the flight land.
- **Pitch $p$** — axial distance between two consecutive flights of the same helix hand; for a square-pitched screw $p = D$.
- **L/D ratio** — the *total* screw length divided by the barrel inner diameter. **L/D = 24:1** is standard for film; **L/D = 30:1** is used for compounding and difficult-to-melt resins.

## 4.3 Co-extrusion Feed Block (Block Diagram for Multilayer Packaging Films)

For high-barrier food packaging films, multiple extruders feed a common **co-extrusion die** through a **feed block** that stacks the melt layers in the desired order (e.g., PE / tie / EVOH / tie / PE).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    extA[Extruder A - Outer PE Layer] --> fb[Feed Block with Layer Sequencer]
    extB[Extruder B - Tie Resin Layer] --> fb
    extC[Extruder C - EVOH Barrier Layer] --> fb
    extD[Extruder D - Inner PE Sealant Layer] --> fb
    fb --> cd[Co-extrusion Die - Slit or Annular]
    cd --> bft[Blown Film Tower or Cast Chill Roll]
    bft --> wn[Winder and Slitter]
```

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 examiners frequently include a **labelled block diagram** (typically for 4 marks within a 14-mark question). Mastery of the above block diagrams will earn full marks.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]** 
Define the term **"compression ratio"** of an extruder screw. What is the typical range used for polyolefins?

**Model Answer:**

The compression ratio (CR) of an extruder screw is defined as the ratio of the **channel volume per flight in the feed zone** to the **channel volume per flight in the metering zone**:

$$
\text{CR} \;=\; \dfrac{H_f \times W_f}{H_m \times W_m}
$$

For a screw with uniform channel width ($W_f = W_m$), this simplifies to $\text{CR} = H_f/H_m$. The compression ratio quantifies the **volumetric compaction** the solid polymer bed undergoes as it travels from the feed throat to the metering zone, ensuring complete melting and air removal.

**Typical range for polyolefins (LDPE, LLDPE, HDPE, PP):** **CR = 2.5 : 1 to 3.0 : 1**.

For heat-sensitive polymers such as rigid PVC, the range is lower (**1.5 : 1 to 2.0 : 1**) to avoid shear-induced degradation. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary]** 
Distinguish between **drag flow** and **pressure flow** in the metering zone of a single-screw extruder. What is the algebraic sign of each term in the net output equation?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Drag Flow ($Q_d$) | Pressure Flow ($Q_p$) |
|---------|--------------------|------------------------|
| **Cause** | Relative motion between the rotating screw flight and the stationary barrel wall | Pressure gradient $\Delta P$ built up at the die |
| **Direction** | **Down-channel (forward, positive)** | **Up-channel (backward, negative)** |
| **Dependence** | $Q_d \propto N, D^{2}, d, \sin\theta\cos\theta$ | $Q_p \propto \Delta P, d^{3}, \sin^{2}\theta / \eta$ |
| **Sign in $Q = Q_d - Q_p - Q_f$** | **Plus (+)** | **Minus (−)** |

The **drag flow** is the *desired* pumping action, while the **pressure flow** represents the *back-leak* caused by the very pressure the extruder generates to push melt through the die. The net output is the algebraic difference. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled block diagram, describe the construction and working of a **single-screw extruder** used in plastics packaging. Explain the functions of the three functional zones of the screw. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from first principles, derive the expression for the **net volumetric output** of a single-screw extruder operating in the metering zone. State clearly the assumptions made. A 60 mm extruder running at 80 rpm with a metering depth of 2.5 mm and helix angle $17.65°$ produces an output of $6.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s. The metering length is 0.6 m, melt viscosity 400 Pa·s. Calculate the **die pressure $\Delta P$** developed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

The single-screw extruder consists of:

1. **Hopper** — gravity feeds the polymer pellets into the barrel.
2. **Barrel** — externally heated by **3 to 5 PID-controlled heater bands**; bore = $D$ (the reference diameter).
3. **Screw** — a helical flight on a steel shaft. The **three zones** and their functions are:
   - **Feed Zone (Solids Conveying)** — the deep, constant-pitch flights drag pellets forward by **frictional drag** with the barrel wall. Compaction of the loose bed occurs here.
   - **Compression / Transition Zone (Melting)** — the channel depth **tapers down**, generating intense **shear** and **frictional (viscous) heating** that melts the polymer. A thin melt film forms against the barrel wall.
   - **Metering Zone (Melt Pumping)** — the shallow, constant-depth channel acts as a **positive-displacement pump**. Homogeneous melt is delivered at a steady rate and pressure to the die.
4. **Breaker Plate and Screen Pack** — supported by the breaker plate, it filters contaminants and converts swirling rotational flow into straight axial flow.
5. **Die** — shapes the extrudate (slit → film/sheet; annular → tube/blown film; profile → straw).
6. **Drive, Gearbox, Thrust Bearing** — supplies torque and absorbs the axial reaction force.

A **labelled block diagram** (as drawn in Section 4.1) accompanies the description in the answer booklet. **(7 marks)**

**(b) — Derivation and Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Derivation (5 marks of the 7):**

1. Unwind the helical channel of width $W$ and depth $d$ into a flat rectangular geometry between a moving plate (screw root, velocity $V_b = \pi D N$) at angle $\theta$ and a stationary plate (barrel wall).
2. Decompose the plate velocity into **down-channel** $V_{bz} = \pi D N \cos\theta$ and **cross-channel** $V_{bx} = \pi D N \sin\theta$ components.
3. The 1-D flow in the $z$-direction (Couette + Poiseuille superposition) gives per unit width:

$$
q(z) \;=\; \dfrac{V_{bz}\,d}{2} \;-\; \dfrac{d^{3}}{12\,\eta}\,\dfrac{dP}{dz}
$$

4. Multiply by channel width $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta$:

$$
Q \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N d \sin\theta\cos\theta \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}{12\eta}\dfrac{dP}{dz}
$$

5. With linear pressure rise $dP/dz = \Delta P/L_m$ over the metering length $L_m$:

$$
\boxed{\,Q \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N d \sin\theta\cos\theta \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}{12\eta L_m}\Delta P\,}
$$

**Assumptions (1 mark):** Newtonian melt; steady, laminar, fully-developed flow; incompressible; negligible flight leakage; $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta$ (flight width $e$ ignored); negligible cross-channel flow contribution; $e$ (flight width) and screw clearance $\delta$ are small.

**Numerical Calculation (1 mark):**

Given: $D = 0.060$ m, $N = 80/60 = 1.333$ rev/s, $d = 0.0025$ m, $\theta = 17.65°$ ⇒ $\sin\theta = 0.303$, $\cos\theta = 0.953$, $L_m = 0.6$ m, $\eta = 400$ Pa·s, $Q = 6.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s.

First compute $Q_d$:

$$
Q_d \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}(0.060)^{2}(1.333)(0.0025)(0.303)(0.953)
$$

$$
Q_d \;\approx\; 4.9348 \times 3.6\times10^{-3} \times 1.333 \times 0.0025 \times 0.2887
$$

$$
Q_d \;\approx\; 1.71 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

From $Q = Q_d - Q_p$, solve for $Q_p$:

$$
Q_p \;=\; Q_d - Q \;=\; 1.71 \times 10^{-5} - 6.5 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 1.06 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

Now solve for $\Delta P$ from the $Q_p$ expression:

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{12\,\eta\,L_m\,Q_p}{\pi D d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}
$$

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{12 \times 400 \times 0.6 \times 1.06\times10^{-5}}{\pi \times 0.060 \times (0.0025)^{3} \times (0.303)^{2}}
$$

Numerator $\approx 3.05 \times 10^{-2}$.
Denominator $\approx 0.1885 \times 1.5625\times10^{-8} \times 0.0918 \approx 2.70 \times 10^{-10}$.

$$
\Delta P \;\approx\; \dfrac{3.05\times 10^{-2}}{2.70 \times 10^{-10}} \;\approx\; 1.13 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa}
$$

**Result:** $\Delta P \approx 1.13 \times 10^{8}$ Pa $\approx 113$ MPa. (Note: a typical LDPE die pressure is 7 – 35 MPa; the higher value here reflects the very small $Q$ relative to the screw's drag capacity, i.e., the die is much too restrictive for this screw speed.)

**(Total = 7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **extrudate swell (die swell)** in polymer extrusion. Discuss the factors that influence it and its significance in the design of **packaging tubing and blown films**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two different extruders are used to process **LDPE film**. Extruder X has $D = 90$ mm, $d = 4$ mm, $\theta = 17.65°$, $N = 50$ rpm. Extruder Y has $D = 60$ mm, $d = 3$ mm, $\theta = 20°$, $N = 90$ rpm. Ignoring pressure flow, determine which extruder has the higher **theoretical drag-flow capacity**, and by what factor. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Die Swell (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Die swell (also called **extrudate swell** or the **Barus effect**) is the phenomenon in which the cross-sectional dimensions of an extrudate emerging from a die are **larger** than the corresponding die dimensions. Quantitatively, the swell ratio is

$$
B \;=\; \dfrac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}}
$$

For polyolefins, $B$ typically lies in the range **1.10 – 1.50**.

**Mechanism (2 marks):** When polymer melt flows through a converging die entry, it experiences both **shear** and **elongational** deformation. Polymer chains become **oriented and stretched** in the flow direction. Upon exit, the elastic (recoverable) component of this strain is released, causing the chains to recoil toward their coiled equilibrium state. The macroscopic result is a **radial expansion** of the extrudate.

**Factors (2 marks):** 
- **Melt elasticity and relaxation time** — higher elasticity ⇒ higher $B$.
- **Shear rate in the die** — higher shear ⇒ higher $B$.
- **Residence time / temperature** — higher temperature reduces $B$ (more relaxation in the die).
- **Die land length** — longer land reduces $B$ (more relaxation during passage).
- **Polymer molecular structure** — long-chain-branched LDPE exhibits higher $B$ than linear LLDPE.
- **Polymer molecular weight / MFI** — lower MFI (higher MW) ⇒ higher $B$.

**Significance in packaging (1 mark):**
- In **blown film extrusion**, the die diameter is intentionally **smaller** than the final bubble diameter by the inverse of the swell ratio.
- In **packaging tubing and drinking straws**, the die is **undercut** by $1/B$ so that the cooled product attains the correct nominal diameter.
- In **wire and cable insulation**, die swell governs the minimum achievable insulation thickness.

**(b) — Comparative Drag-Flow Calculation (7 marks):**

For each extruder, drag flow (ignoring pressure flow) is:

$$
Q_d \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N d \sin\theta\cos\theta
$$

The factor $f = \sin\theta\cos\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\theta$ is:
- For $\theta = 17.65°$: $f_X = 0.303 \times 0.953 = 0.2887$
- For $\theta = 20°$: $f_Y = 0.342 \times 0.940 = 0.3214$

**Extruder X** ($D = 0.090$ m, $d = 0.004$ m, $N = 50/60 = 0.8333$ rev/s, $f_X = 0.2887$):

$$
Q_{d,X} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}(0.090)^{2}(0.8333)(0.004)(0.2887)
$$

$$
Q_{d,X} \;=\; 4.9348 \times 8.1\times10^{-3} \times 0.8333 \times 0.004 \times 0.2887
$$

$$
Q_{d,X} \;\approx\; 3.86 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Extruder Y** ($D = 0.060$ m, $d = 0.003$ m, $N = 90/60 = 1.5$ rev/s, $f_Y = 0.3214$):

$$
Q_{d,Y} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}(0.060)^{2}(1.5)(0.003)(0.3214)
$$

$$
Q_{d,Y} \;=\; 4.9348 \times 3.6\times10^{-3} \times 1.5 \times 0.003 \times 0.3214
$$

$$
Q_{d,Y} \;\approx\; 2.57 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Comparison (1 mark):**

$$
\dfrac{Q_{d,X}}{Q_{d,Y}} \;=\; \dfrac{3.86 \times 10^{-5}}{2.57 \times 10^{-5}} \;\approx\; 1.50
$$

**Extruder X has the higher drag-flow capacity, by a factor of approximately 1.5×.** Although Extruder Y runs faster and has a slightly higher helix-angle factor, the $D^2$ term in Extruder X dominates.

**(Total = 7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Extrusion Questions — KTU 2024 ESE**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the minus sign between $Q_d$ and $Q_p$** — pressure flow is *back-flow*; writing $Q = Q_d + Q_p$ costs **2 marks** outright.
> 2. **Mixing up units** — $D$, $d$, $L_m$ must all be in **metres**; $N$ in **revolutions per second** (not rpm); $\eta$ in **Pa·s**. KTU examiners specifically check unit consistency.
> 3. **Neglecting to state the assumptions** in the derivation (Newtonian, steady, fully-developed, $e$ neglected, etc.) — at least **1 mark** is reserved for the assumption list in any 7-mark derivation.
> 4. **Drawing the screw diagram without labels** — $D$, $d$, $W$, $H_f$, $H_m$, $L_f$, $L_m$, $L_c$ must each be marked. An unlabelled diagram scores **0 / 2 marks** allocated to the figure.
> 5. **Confusing feed/metering depth with screw diameter** — students often write $H_m = D$ instead of $H_m \approx 0.05D$.
> 6. **Sign error in $Q = Q_d - Q_p$ when the die pressure is zero** — if $\Delta P = 0$, students sometimes write $Q = 0$ instead of $Q = Q_d$. Remember: **zero die pressure gives maximum (open-discharge) output $Q = Q_d$**, not zero output.
> 7. **Using $L$ (total screw length) instead of $L_m$ (metering length only)** in the $Q_p$ expression — the pressure gradient is built up only over the metering zone.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Extrusion (PEPOT748, Module 3)**
>
> - **Extrusion** is a *continuous* melt-shaping process; the **single-screw extruder** is the workhorse of plastics packaging.
> - The three functional zones of the screw are **Feed (solids conveying) → Compression (melting) → Metering (pumping)**.
> - **Compression ratio CR** for polyolefins is **2.5 – 3.0**; for PVC it is **1.5 – 2.0**.
> - The **L/D ratio** of a typical film/sheet screw is **24:1 to 30:1**.
> - The **net volumetric output** of the metering zone is $Q = Q_d - Q_p - Q_f$, with $Q_f$ often negligible.
> - **Drag flow** $Q_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\theta\cos\theta$ is **forward (positive)** and proportional to screw speed.
> - **Pressure flow** $Q_p = \dfrac{\pi D d^3 \sin^2\theta}{12 \eta L_m}\Delta P$ is **backward (negative)** and proportional to die pressure.
> - **Optimum helix angle** for an extruder screw is **20° – 30°** (often 17.65° for square pitch).
> - **Barrel heaters** are typically **3 – 5 PID-controlled zones**, each with its own thermocouple.
> - The **breaker plate + screen pack** filters the melt and converts swirling flow to straight axial flow.
> - **Die swell (Barus effect)** is caused by elastic recovery of oriented polymer chains; swell ratio $B = D_{extrudate}/D_{die}$ is typically **1.10 – 1.50** for polyolefins.
> - **Mass throughput** $\dot{m} = \rho_{melt} Q$.
> - **Specific energy** $E_{sp} = P/\dot{m}$ is typically **3 – 6 MJ/kg** for LDPE film extrusion.
> - **Co-extrusion** of 3 to 7 layers (e.g., PE/tie/EVOH/tie/PE) is the industry standard for high-barrier food packaging films.
> - **Twin-screw extruders** (co-rotating for compounding; counter-rotating for PVC) are used in compounding, masterbatch, and rigid packaging.
> - For the KTU 2024 ESE, be ready to (i) **derive** the output equation, (ii) **calculate** a numerical value, (iii) **draw and label** a block diagram, and (iv) **list the three functional zones** with their functions.
> - Always include the **assumption list** whenever you write a derivation — it is a guaranteed 1-mark freebie.
> - Always carry **units through the calculation**; final answers should be in **m³/s (for $Q$)**, **kg/h or kg/s (for $\dot{m}$)**, and **Pa or MPa (for $\Delta P$)**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotary Thermoforming
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rotary Thermoforming

# Rotary Thermoforming

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rotary Thermoforming** is a continuous, high-volume thermoforming variant in which a thermoplastic sheet is mechanically indexed or continuously transported through a **rotating carousel (drum/turret)** that carries the sheet sequentially past multiple processing stations — *clamping & feeding → pre-heating → forming → cooling → trimming & stacking*. It is the **highest throughput** sheet thermoforming architecture and is the industry standard for producing **disposable cups, containers, blister packs, lids, and tray-based food packaging** in mass quantities.

The rotary system is the only thermoforming architecture capable of combining **mold indexing**, **multi-zone heating**, and **in-line trimming** into a single synchronized mechanical loop, achieving cycle times of **0.5 to 4 seconds per part** depending on part area and material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Marker (KTU 2024 Syllabus Alignment):** Rotary thermoforming sits under *Module 3 – Thermoforming Processes* and bridges *basic vacuum forming* (Module 2/3) and *industrial packaging line design*. Expect direct questions on station layout, indexing mechanics, and draw-ratio calculations.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **Ferris wheel** at a fairground. Each passenger seat is a "mold" carrying a plastic sheet. As the wheel rotates, every seat passes through the same *sequence* of stations in a fixed order:
- A **sun-lamp booth** (heater station) warms the sheet until it sags like soft dough.
- A **vacuum cleaner** (forming station) sucks the warm sheet into a metal mold.
- A **refrigerator** (cooling station) hardens the sheet.
- A **pair of scissors** (trim station) cuts the formed part out.

Each rotation produces one finished part. Now replace the Ferris wheel with a **horizontal carousel/drum carrying 10 to 50 molds**, rotating at 5–30 RPM, and you have a rotary thermoformer producing **hundreds of parts per minute** — the backbone of the global disposable packaging industry.

### Standard Engineering Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Operating temperature window:** 120 °C – 200 °C (polymer-dependent; PS ~130 °C, PP ~160 °C, PET ~170 °C).
- **Forming pressure:** 0.05 – 0.9 MPa (vacuum to low-pressure compressed air).
- **Indexing cycle time:** 0.5 – 4 s per station.
- **Maximum sheet width:** up to **2.5 m** in industrial units.
- **Sheet thickness range:** **0.2 – 3.0 mm**.
- **Draw ratio limit (DR):** typically **≤ 1.5 : 1** for uniaxial and **≤ 2.0 : 1** for biaxial flow in rotary systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Stepwise Logic

A rotary thermoformer operates on the **closed-loop indexing principle**:

1. **Sheet Feed & Clamping:** A roll-fed or cut-sheet thermoplastic web is fed into the indexer. Pneumatically actuated **clamp frames** grip the sheet edges (pinch-off zone) — this clamp zone is *dead material* that becomes trim waste.
2. **Indexing Transfer:** The clamp frame rotates with the carousel to the next station. The **index time** is mechanically locked and synchronized to heater output.
3. **Pre-Heating Station (Radiant IR):** Top and bottom **ceramic, quartz, or calrod infrared heaters** bring the sheet to its *forming temperature* (Tg + 30 °C for amorphous, Tm – 10 °C for semi-crystalline). Sheet is heated **from both sides** to avoid sag and gradient through-thickness.
4. **Forming Station (Vacuum / Pressure / Plug-Assist):** The pre-heated sheet is draped over a temperature-controlled (typically 50 – 80 °C) male or female mold. Vacuum (~ –85 kPa gauge), compressed air, or a mechanical plug-assist forces the sheet into the cavity.
5. **Cooling Station:** Internal mold cooling channels + optional air/water mist jets drop the polymer below its *heat-distortion temperature* so it retains geometry.
6. **In-Mold or In-Line Trimming:** Steel-rule dies, router cutters, or hot-wire trims the formed sheet — at the **trim station** on the rotary carousel itself (single-stage) or on a downstream in-line station (two-stage).
7. **Unloading & Stacking:** Finished parts drop onto a conveyor or into a stacker; skeleton web is wound for recycling.

## 2.2 Why a Rotary Architecture? The Core Engineering 'Why'

- **Throughput:** Continuous indexing eliminates the *load/heat/form/cool/unload* idle time of single-station machines.
- **Energy Efficiency:** The heater zone is *always full* of sheet — no idle heater on cold sheet.
- **Station Specialization:** Each station is *purpose-built* (only heaters at the heater station; only vacuum at the form station). This decouples process variables and allows tight control.
- **Trim-In-Place:** Because the sheet is still clamped, trimming on the rotary loop reduces part-handling damage.

## 2.3 Types of Rotary Thermoforming

| Architecture | Carousel Motion | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous Rotary** | Smooth, non-stop rotation | Thin-gauge packaging (cups, lids) |
| **Indexing Rotary** | Step-and-hold rotation | Heavy-gauge industrial parts |
| **In-Line Rotary** | Carousel with downstream trim line | Long sheet, high throughput |
| **Drum Thermoformer** | Sheet wraps a heated rotating drum | Cups, yogurt containers, lids |

> [!NOTE]
> **Drum thermoforming** is the *most commercially deployed* rotary variant. The sheet is fed onto a large rotating steel drum, vacuum-formed at the bottom of the drum's rotation, and trimmed in-line. Used for **billions of cups and lids per year** worldwide.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> All formulas below are KTU-exam-relevant. Use `\vert` notation in handwritten work to avoid LaTeX ambiguity.

| # | Formula | Variables | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $DR = \dfrac{A_{part}}{A_{sheet\,footprint}}$ | DR = Draw Ratio (dimensionless); A = area | Material distribution check |
| 2 | $DR = \dfrac{d_{cavity}}{w_{sheet}}$ | d = cavity depth; w = sheet width (uniaxial) | Uniaxial cup forming |
| 3 | $t_{final} = \dfrac{t_{initial}}{DR}$ | t = thickness (mm) | Wall-thickness prediction |
| 4 | $\dot{Q} = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{L}$ | k = thermal conductivity; L = sheet thickness; ΔT = T_forming – T_initial | Steady-state heat flux |
| 5 | $t_{heat} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot L \cdot \Delta T}{q^{''}}$ | ρ = density; c_p = specific heat; q'' = heater flux (W/m²) | Heating time estimate |
| 6 | $Bi = \dfrac{h \cdot L}{k}$ | Bi = Biot number; h = convective coeff. | Lumped vs. distributed heating |
| 7 | $t_{cool} \approx \dfrac{\rho c_p L}{\pi h_{eff}}$ | h_eff = effective mold heat-transfer coeff. | Cooling time (1D slab) |
| 8 | $N_{parts/hr} = \dfrac{60}{t_{cycle}} \times n_{stations\,effective}$ | t_cycle = seconds per index | Production rate |
| 9 | $P_{cycle} = N \times m_{part} \times c_p \times \Delta T$ | m = part mass; N = parts/hr | Heater power sizing |
| 10 | $s_{trim} = w_{sheet} - 2 \cdot w_{clamp}$ | s_trim = usable sheet width | Yield calculation |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food packaging** (yogurt cups, drinking cups, meat trays, blister clamshells) — **> 70% of all rotary thermoforming output**.
- **Pharmaceutical blister packs** (PVC/PVDC, PETG) — high optical clarity and dimensional precision.
- **Electronics trays** (conductive PS, PET anti-static) — ESD-safe disposable trays.
- **Automotive interior liners** and refrigerator door liners (heavy-gauge ABS rotary systems).

> The *draw ratio* formula is the single most-tested quantitative concept in KTU ESE on thermoforming. **Memorize the trio: $DR$, $t_{final}$, and $N_{parts/hr}$.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wall Thickness After Forming (from Draw Ratio)

The defining geometric relationship of any thermoforming operation is that *volume of polymer is conserved*. Before forming, the polymer is a flat sheet of area $A_s$ and thickness $t_i$. After forming, the polymer spreads over the larger mold surface $A_p$ at a new, reduced thickness $t_f$. Mass conservation:

$$ \rho \cdot V_{sheet} = \rho \cdot V_{part} $$

Since density $\rho$ is unchanged (incompressible melt in forming window), volume is conserved:

$$ A_s \cdot t_i = A_p \cdot t_f $$

Solve for final thickness:

$$ t_f = \frac{A_s \cdot t_i}{A_p} = \frac{t_i}{(A_p / A_s)} $$

By definition, the **Draw Ratio** is $DR = A_p / A_s$. Therefore:

$$ \boxed{\,t_{final} = \dfrac{t_{initial}}{DR}\,} $$

**Engineering interpretation:** A draw ratio of 2.0 halves the original wall thickness. A draw ratio of 1.5 reduces it to ~67 % of the original.

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity range:** Assumes *uniform thinning* — true for shallow cavities. For deep cavities with corners, plug-assist redistributes material and the simple $1/DR$ rule under-predicts corner thickness by 10–25 %.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Production Rate of a Rotary Indexing Machine

Let:
- $T_{index}$ = time for one index step (s)
- $n$ = number of molds mounted on the carousel
- $f_{util}$ = utilization factor (0.6 – 0.95) accounting for heater warm-up and trim dwell

Each index step rotates one mold forward and advances the sheet by one *pitch*. The effective cycle time per part is $T_{cycle} = T_{index} / n_{par}$ where $n_{par}$ is the number of parts produced per index (typically 2–8 for cup/lid webs).

Parts per hour:

$$ N_{parts/hr} = \frac{3600 \times n \times n_{par} \times f_{util}}{T_{index}} $$

For a single-part-per-mold system ($n_{par} = 1$):

$$ N_{parts/hr} = \frac{3600 \cdot n \cdot f_{util}}{T_{index}} $$

**Numerical sanity check:**
For a 12-station cup-forming rotary with $T_{index} = 2$ s and $f_{util} = 0.85$:

$$ N_{parts/hr} = \frac{3600 \times 12 \times 0.85}{2} = \frac{36720}{2} = 18\,360 \text{ cups/hr} $$

That is **~ 306 cups per minute** — consistent with industrial cup lines.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heating Time (1D Transient Conduction, Lumped Capacitance)

If the sheet is thin and the heater IR flux is high, the through-thickness Biot number is small and the sheet can be modeled as a *lumped* thermal mass:

$$ Bi = \frac{h \cdot L}{k} $$

For $Bi < 0.1$, the temperature is essentially uniform through the thickness, and:

$$ \frac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{h A}{\rho V c_p} t\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{h\,t}{\rho c_p L}\right) $$

Solving for the time to reach forming temperature $T_f$:

$$ t_{heat} = \frac{\rho c_p L}{h} \ln\!\left(\frac{T_i - T_\infty}{T_f - T_\infty}\right) $$

Equivalent in terms of the **applied heater flux** $q''$:

$$ t_{heat} = \frac{\rho c_p L (T_f - T_i)}{q''} $$

**Numerical example (PS cup):**
- $\rho = 1050 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 1300 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$
- $L = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
- $T_i = 25 \text{ °C}$, $T_f = 145 \text{ °C}$, $T_\infty = 700 \text{ °C}$ (heater filament)
- Heater flux $q'' = 30\,000 \text{ W/m}^2$

$$ t_{heat} = \frac{1050 \times 1300 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \times (145 - 25)}{30\,000} $$

Stepwise:

$$ \rho c_p L = 1050 \times 1300 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 682.5 \text{ J/(m}^2\text{·K)} $$

$$ \Delta T = 145 - 25 = 120 \text{ K} $$

$$ t_{heat} = \frac{682.5 \times 120}{30\,000} = \frac{81\,900}{30\,000} = 2.73 \text{ s} $$

**Interpretation:** A 0.5 mm PS sheet needs about **2.7 s** of IR heating at 30 kW/m² to reach forming temperature. This sets the **minimum index time** for the heater station.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cooling Time Estimate

Cooling is dominated by *conduction into the mold* (1D transient, semi-infinite slab solution). Using the lumped capacitance approximation for the formed part against a mold of much higher thermal mass:

$$ t_{cool} \approx \frac{\rho c_p L}{\pi h_{eff}} $$

For aluminum mold with $h_{eff} = 500 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ and same PS:

$$ t_{cool} = \frac{1050 \times 1300 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi \times 500} = \frac{682.5}{1570.8} \approx 0.43 \text{ s} $$

Cooling is *much faster* than heating — confirming why rotary formers have a shorter cool station than heat station.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix (Choosing Rotary Type)

| Criterion | Continuous Rotary Drum | Indexing Rotary | In-Line Rotary |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Best for** | Cups, lids (thin) | Trays, clamshells | Long-sheet heavy gauge |
| **Cycle time** | < 1 s | 2 – 6 s | 3 – 10 s |
| **Trim method** | In-line steel-rule die | In-mold steel-rule | Downstream router |
| **Material waste** | 15–25 % | 20–35 % | 25–40 % |
| **Tooling cost** | Low (matched die) | Medium | High |
| **Volume** | > 50 M parts/yr | 5–20 M parts/yr | 1–5 M parts/yr |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Rotary Thermoforming Process Flow (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rollA[Sheet Roll Unwind] --> clampA[Clamp Frame Pickup]
    clampA --> indexA[Indexer Carousel Rotation]
    indexA --> heatA[Station 1 - Pre Heater IR Top]
    heatA --> heatB[Station 2 - Pre Heater IR Bottom]
    heatB --> formA[Station 3 - Form Vacuum Mold]
    formA --> coolA[Station 4 - Cool Air Water Mist]
    coolA --> trimA[Station 5 - Trim Steel Rule Die]
    trimA --> unloadA[Station 6 - Unload Stacker]
    unloadA --> prodA[Finished Container Output]
    trimA --> recycleA[Skeleton Web Regrind]
    recycleA --> rollA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above diagram is a **process flow**, not a spatial layout. In an actual rotary machine, *Station 1* through *Station 6* are arranged around a **circular carousel**, with each mold passing through the same sequence as the carousel indexes.

## 4.2 Detailed Station-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph RotaryCarousel["ROTARY CAROUSEL ASSEMBLY"]
        m1[Mold Position 1 - Heater Zone]
        m2[Mold Position 2 - Heater Zone]
        m3[Mold Position 3 - Form Zone]
        m4[Mold Position 4 - Cool Zone]
        m5[Mold Position 5 - Cool Zone]
        m6[Mold Position 6 - Trim Zone]
    end
    subgraph ControlSystem["PLC AND SERVO CONTROL"]
        plc[Master PLC Controller]
        servo[Servo Drive Indexer]
        thermo[Thermocouple Feedback Loop]
    end
    subgraph Auxiliaries["ANCILLARY SYSTEMS"]
        vac[Vacuum Pump 0.09 MPa]
        htr[IR Ceramic Heater Bank]
        chiller[Mold Chiller 10 deg C]
        comp[Air Compressor 0.7 MPa]
    end
    plc --> servo
    servo --> m1
    servo --> m2
    servo --> m3
    servo --> m4
    servo --> m5
    servo --> m6
    htr --> m1
    htr --> m2
    vac --> m3
    chiller --> m4
    chiller --> m5
    comp --> m6
    thermo --> plc
```

## 4.3 Drum Thermoformer Schematic (Side-View Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feed[Sheet Feed Roller] --> drumMain[Heated Rotating Drum]
    drumMain --> vacDrum[Vacuum Forming Shoe Below Drum]
    vacDrum --> coolDrum[Cooling Air Jet Array]
    coolDrum --> trimDrum[In Line Steel Rule Die]
    trimDrum --> stackDrum[Stacker and Conveyor]
    drumMain --> scrapDrum[Skeleton Wind Up]
    scrapDrum --> grinder[Regrind and Re-extrusion]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Drum thermoformer** is functionally a *linear rotary* — the "carousel" is the drum surface itself, and the forming/cooling/trimming stations are spatially distributed around its circumference. It is the workhorse of the global disposable cup industry.

## 4.4 Process Parameter Relationship Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sheet Thickness L] --> B[Heater Power q double prime]
    B --> C[Index Time t index]
    C --> D[Production Rate N parts per hr]
    A --> E[Draw Ratio DR]
    E --> F[Final Wall Thickness t final]
    F --> G[Part Rigidity]
    D --> H[Economic Yield]
    G --> I[Package Quality]
    H --> I
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define rotary thermoforming. List any three advantages of rotary thermoforming over single-station vacuum forming.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Rotary thermoforming is a continuous thermoforming process in which a thermoplastic sheet is mechanically indexed through multiple specialized processing stations (heating, forming, cooling, trimming) mounted on a rotating carousel, enabling high-volume, sequential production of formed parts.

**Three Advantages (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Higher throughput** — multiple parts in simultaneous process; cycle time drops to < 4 s.
2. **Better process control** — each station is purpose-built, allowing independent optimization of heater, vacuum, and cooling parameters.
3. **Lower unit cost** — continuous indexing amortizes energy and labor across many parts; trim-in-place reduces handling.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`
**What is meant by the term "Draw Ratio" in thermoforming? State the formula relating initial sheet thickness to final formed part thickness.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Draw Ratio (DR) is the dimensionless ratio of the *mold surface area* (or part footprint area) to the *original flat sheet area* from which the part is formed. It quantifies how much the polymer is stretched during forming.

**Formula (1.5 marks):**

$$ DR = \frac{A_{part}}{A_{sheet}} \quad \text{and} \quad t_{final} = \frac{t_{initial}}{DR} $$

**Engineering note:** A DR greater than ~1.5 in uniaxial or ~2.0 in biaxial flow generally risks excessive thinning and part failure.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

### `Question A (14 Marks)` — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]`

**(a)** With the aid of a labeled schematic, explain the working principle of a **multi-station indexing rotary thermoformer**. Describe the function of any **five stations** on the carousel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PS sheet of **0.6 mm thickness** is to be formed into a cup of **cavity depth 60 mm** and **cavity diameter 80 mm**. Calculate the **draw ratio** and the **final wall thickness** at the cup sidewall. Comment on whether the draw ratio is acceptable for uniaxial rotary forming. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

**Schematic (2 marks):** Draw a circular carousel with 6–8 stations; mark sheet feed, clamp frame, heater zones, forming zone, cooling zone, trim zone, and stacker.

**Working Principle (2 marks):** The sheet is fed continuously into the indexer, clamped, and the clamp frame rotates stepwise with the carousel. At each station, a single dedicated process (heating, forming, cooling, trimming) is performed. The cycle repeats as the carousel indexes; parts are produced in a continuous loop.

**Five stations (3 marks — 0.6 each):**
1. **Sheet feed & clamp station:** Sheet is gripped between upper and lower clamp frames, isolating a forming zone from the rest of the web.
2. **Top IR heater station:** Raises sheet temperature to forming point (Tg + 30 °C).
3. **Bottom IR heater station:** Reduces temperature gradient through thickness, preventing sag and surface blemishes.
4. **Forming (vacuum/pressure) station:** Vacuum (~ –85 kPa) or compressed air (up to 0.9 MPa) forces the sheet into the mold cavity; plug-assist is used for deep draws.
5. **Cooling station:** Mold cooling channels and air/water jets solidify the part below its heat-distortion temperature.
6. **Trim station:** Steel-rule die or hot wire cuts the formed part from the skeleton; trim can be in-mold or in-line.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $t_i = 0.6 \text{ mm}$
- Cavity depth $d = 60 \text{ mm}$
- Cavity diameter $\phi = 80 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Uniaxial Draw Ratio (1.5 marks):**

For an uniaxial cup, the relevant dimension ratio is depth to width of the heated zone:

$$ DR_{uniaxial} = \frac{d_{cavity}}{w_{sheet}} $$

Assuming the heated zone width equals the cavity diameter (80 mm):

$$ DR = \frac{60}{80} = 0.75 $$

> Note: In a uniaxial cup draw, the *effective* draw ratio is also defined as cavity depth ÷ (cavity diameter/2) for the sidewall — but for KTU we use the standard $d / w$ convention. The DR of 0.75 is mild, indicating a *shallow* draw.

**Step 2 — Final Wall Thickness (2.5 marks):**

$$ t_{final} = \frac{t_{initial}}{DR} = \frac{0.6 \text{ mm}}{0.75} = 0.80 \text{ mm} $$

> ⚠ **Important correction:** In a uniaxial cup, the *sidewall* thickness is not reduced — material is drawn from the flat flange into the wall. The *flange* thickness drops as $1/DR$. The sidewall thickness equals the original sheet thickness ≈ 0.6 mm, while the *base* and *corner* regions are thinnest.

If the question demands a biaxial draw ratio (full part area / sheet area):

- Part surface area ≈ lateral wall + base = $\pi \phi d + \pi (\phi/2)^2$
- $= \pi \times 80 \times 60 + \pi \times 40^2 = 15\,080 + 5027 = 20\,107 \text{ mm}^2$
- Sheet footprint area = $\pi (\phi/2)^2 = 5027 \text{ mm}^2$

$$ DR_{biaxial} = \frac{20\,107}{5027} = 4.0 $$

$$ t_{min} = \frac{0.6}{4.0} = 0.15 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 3 — Acceptability Comment (3 marks):**

A biaxial DR of **4.0** is **excessive** for unassisted uniaxial rotary forming. Standard practice limits DR to ~1.5 (uniaxial) and ~2.0 (biaxial) without plug-assist. To form this cup, a **plug-assist** is required to redistribute polymer from the center toward the sidewalls, lowering effective corner DR to ~1.8. The final wall thickness of 0.15 mm at the thinnest point is at the **lower practical limit** for PS cups (risk of cracking, poor rigidity).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall #1:** Students often confuse *uniaxial* and *biaxial* draw ratios. The DR formula depends on whether the *flow is unidirectional* (uniaxial, like a deep-draw cup) or *spreads radially* (biaxial, like a hemisphere). KTU expects you to *state the assumption* and *show the formula derivation* before plugging numbers. Skipping the assumption statement costs 1–2 marks even if the arithmetic is correct.

---

### `Question B (14 Marks)` — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **continuous rotary drum thermoformer** used for disposable cup production. Compare its features with an **indexing rotary thermoformer**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 12-station rotary thermoformer operates with an **index time of 2.5 seconds per station** and mounts **2 cups per mold**. The utilization factor is **0.88**. Calculate the **hourly production rate** in cups/hour. If the mold layout is changed to **3 cups per mold** with the same index time, what is the **percentage increase** in production rate? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Construction of Drum Thermoformer (3.5 marks):**
- **Heated rotating drum** (large steel cylinder, 1.5–3 m diameter) — outer surface carries the thermoplastic sheet.
- **Infrared heater bank** mounted *inside* the drum, radiating heat through the drum surface to the sheet wrapped on the outside.
- **Vacuum forming shoe** — stationary mold positioned at the bottom of the drum's rotation; as the sheet passes the shoe, vacuum (~ –85 kPa) pulls it into the mold cavity.
- **Cooling air jets** — fixed array below the drum that blasts cold air onto the formed cup.
- **In-line steel-rule die trimmer** — synchronised with drum rotation; cuts each cup from the web.
- **Stacker/conveyor** — collects finished cups.
- **Skeleton wind-up** — rewinds the leftover web for regrinding.

**Working (2 marks):** Sheet is fed from a roll onto the rotating drum. As the drum rotates, IR heat from within softens the sheet. At the bottom of the rotation, the sheet contacts the stationary mold; vacuum is applied for a fraction of a second, forming the cup. Continued rotation carries the formed cup past cooling jets and a synchronized trim die. Cups drop into a stacker; the skeleton is wound and recycled.

**Comparison with Indexing Rotary (1.5 marks):**

| Feature | Continuous Drum | Indexing Rotary |
|---|---|---|
| Motion | Continuous rotation | Step-and-hold |
| Sheet handling | Wrapped on drum | Clamped on frames |
| Cycle time | < 1 s | 2–6 s |
| Best for | Cups, lids, thin-gauge | Trays, heavy-gauge |
| Heater position | Inside drum | Above/below sheet |
| Trim | In-line synchronized | On-carousel station |

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $n = 12$ stations
- $T_{index} = 2.5 \text{ s}$
- $n_{par,1} = 2$ cups per mold (initial)
- $f_{util} = 0.88$

**Step 1 — Hourly Production Rate (Initial) (3.5 marks):**

$$ N_1 = \frac{3600 \times n \times n_{par,1} \times f_{util}}{T_{index}} $$

$$ N_1 = \frac{3600 \times 12 \times 2 \times 0.88}{2.5} $$

Stepwise:

$$ 3600 \times 12 = 43\,200 $$

$$ 43\,200 \times 2 = 86\,400 $$

$$ 86\,400 \times 0.88 = 76\,032 $$

$$ N_1 = \frac{76\,032}{2.5} = 30\,412.8 \approx 30\,413 \text{ cups/hr} $$

**Step 2 — Production Rate with 3 Cups/Mold (2 marks):**

$$ N_2 = \frac{3600 \times 12 \times 3 \times 0.88}{2.5} = \frac{114\,048}{2.5} = 45\,619.2 \approx 45\,619 \text{ cups/hr} $$

**Step 3 — Percentage Increase (1.5 marks):**

$$ \% \text{ increase} = \frac{N_2 - N_1}{N_1} \times 100 = \frac{45\,619 - 30\,413}{30\,413} \times 100 $$

$$ = \frac{15\,206}{30\,413} \times 100 = 50.0\% $$

> **Engineering interpretation:** Increasing cups per mold from 2 to 3 (a 50 % increase in parts per index) gives a direct 50 % production boost because the index time is unchanged. This is a common scale-up strategy in cup lines before investing in a faster carousel.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall #2:** A frequent mistake is forgetting the *utilization factor* $f_{util}$. Including it correctly is worth **1 full mark** in 14-mark problems. Also, KTU expects you to **show the unit cancellation explicitly** in the production rate calculation — graders look for $\frac{\text{seconds}}{\text{hour}}$ conversion as $\times 3600$ on the first line. Omitting this gives a numerically wrong answer (off by factor of 3600) and full credit is forfeited.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Rotary thermoforming** = continuous indexing/rotating carousel with sequential heating → forming → cooling → trimming stations; the **highest throughput** sheet thermoforming architecture.
- ✅ **Two main commercial variants:** (i) indexing rotary (multi-station carousel) and (ii) continuous drum thermoformer.
- ✅ **Draw Ratio (DR)** is *the* core quantitative concept: $DR = A_{part}/A_{sheet}$ and $t_{final} = t_{initial}/DR$.
- ✅ **Uniaxial DR limit** ≈ 1.5; **biaxial DR limit** ≈ 2.0 (without plug-assist).
- ✅ **Forming temperature window:** $T_g + 30 \text{ °C}$ (amorphous) or $T_m - 10 \text{ °C}$ (semi-crystalline).
- ✅ **Heating time** (lumped): $t_{heat} = \rho c_p L (T_f - T_i) / q''$ — set by IR heater flux.
- ✅ **Cooling time** ≈ 0.4 – 0.5 × heating time for thin sheets on aluminum molds.
- ✅ **Production rate** formula: $N = (3600 \times n \times n_{par} \times f_{util}) / T_{index}$.
- ✅ **Plug-assist** is used for deep draws (DR > 1.5) to redistribute material and prevent corner thinning.
- ✅ **Drum thermoformer** is the industry standard for **disposable cups, lids, and yogurt containers** — production rates of 30 000 – 50 000 cups/hr are common.
- ✅ **Station count** typically 6–12 for indexing rotary; the heater station is the longest (largest angular sector).
- ✅ **Trim waste** = 15–35 % of sheet, recycled as *regrind* and re-extruded.
- ✅ **Materials used:** PS (cups), PP (microwaveable trays), PET (blister packs), PVC (clamshells), PETG (pharma), ABS (heavy-gauge).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 3
