topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shrink Wrapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Shrink Wrapping

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Shrink Wrapping** is a thermo-responsive secondary and tertiary packaging process in which a pre-stretched (oriented) thermoplastic film is loosely draped or sealed around a product (or a collation of products), then subjected to controlled thermal energy. The film recovers its original, un-oriented dimensions due to the elastic "memory" locked within its molecular orientation, conforming tightly and seamlessly to the contours of the enclosed object.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT748, Module 3):**
> Shrink wrapping is categorized under *Flexible Packaging Converting Technologies* and *End-of-Line Packaging Operations*. Students must master the polymer science of shrink memory and the process engineering of thermal shrink tunnels.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Rubber Band / Balloon Analogy:**
> Imagine blowing up a balloon (orienting the rubber) and then letting the air out. The rubber does not return perfectly to its original size because it was *plastically* deformed. Now imagine stretching a rubber band, dipping it briefly in ice-cold water (freezing the orientation), removing it, and then placing it in warm water. The rubber band instantly snaps back to its original un-stretched length. **Shrink film behaves exactly like the rubber band.** During manufacture, the polymer chains are forcibly stretched and rapidly quenched (frozen) in their elongated state. Heat supplied in the shrink tunnel unlocks this frozen orientation, and the chains recoil — wrapping your product with mechanical firmness.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

The following **standardized packaging metrics** are mandated by ASTM D2732 and TAPPI T 502:

- **Free Shrink (%)** — Dimensional recovery in an unconstrained state.
- **Shrink Tension (g/cm or N/m)** — Force generated during constrained shrinkage.
- **Shrink Temperature Range (°C)** — Typically **80 °C – 150 °C** for polyolefin; **65 °C – 150 °C** for PVC.
- **Dwell Time (seconds)** — Residence time inside the heat zone.

---

## 2. Intuitive Overview of the Process

The shrink wrapping workflow has **four (4) universal stages**, regardless of automation level:

1. **Product Collation / Loading:** Items are grouped on a tray or directly on the conveyor.
2. **Film Enclosure:** Film is wrapped around the product and sealed (L-bar seal, sleeve seal, or bag).
3. **Heat Activation:** Product passes through a tunnel or rotary heat source. Film shrinks.
4. **Cooling & Discharge:** The package is cooled to set the film geometry before stacking.

> [!TIP]
> The film **does not shrink uniformly** in all directions unless it is a *balanced* shrink film. Most commercial shrink films are *unidirectional* (machine direction) or *biaxially oriented* (MD and TD). This directional anisotropy is a critical design parameter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Polymer Science Behind Shrinkage — The "Why"

Shrink behavior originates from the **entropy-driven recoil of oriented amorphous polymer chains**.

### Step-by-Step Molecular Logic:

- **Step 1 — Orientation (During Film Manufacture):**
  The molten polymer is extruded and mechanically drawn either uniaxially or biaxially. The long-chain molecules align in the direction(s) of draw. This state has **low conformational entropy** because the chains are forced into elongated, ordered conformations.

- **Step 2 — Quenching (Freezing the Orientation):**
  The oriented film is cooled rapidly below its glass transition temperature ($T_g$). For amorphous polymers like PVC, $T_g$ ≈ **80 °C**. For semi-crystalline polyolefins, the crystalline regions act as physical crosslinks. The oriented amorphous chains are now **kinetically trapped** in a non-equilibrium state.

- **Step 3 — Heat Reactivation (In the Shrink Tunnel):**
  When the film is reheated above its $T_g$ (or near its melt onset), chain mobility returns. The trapped chains spontaneously seek their **maximum entropy (random coil) state**, which corresponds to the original, pre-stretched dimensions.

- **Step 4 — Conformity & Cooling:**
  The film conforms tightly to the product. On cooling, the new geometry is locked in place again.

### 2.2 Classification of Shrink Films by Polymer

| Polymer | Abbreviation | Typical Shrink Temp. | Key Property | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Polyvinyl Chloride | PVC | **65 – 150 °C** | High clarity, brittle at low temp. | CDs, software boxes (declining use) |
| Polyolefin (cross-linked) | POF | **120 – 150 °C** | Excellent seal strength, food-safe | **Most common** — boxes, food, toys |
| Low-Density Polyethylene | LDPE | **110 – 150 °C** | High shrink tension, low cost | Pallet wrapping, heavy bundles |
| Polypropylene | PP | **140 – 170 °C** | High gloss, stiff | Tobacco, cosmetics |
| Polyethylene Terephthalate | PETG | **70 – 100 °C** | Crystal-clear, recyclable | Premium cosmetics, electronics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-linked POF (via electron-beam irradiation)** is the industry benchmark because irradiation raises its hot-strength, preventing burn-through and burn marks at high tunnel temperatures.

### 2.3 Types of Shrink Behaviour

**A. Free Shrink (Unconstrained):**
The film is heated in an oven with no external force. It recovers freely.

**B. Constrained Shrink:**
The film is restrained (e.g., sealed around a rigid box). Instead of shrinking dimensionally, it generates **shrink tension** — a measurable force pressing against the package.

**C. Shrink Percentages in MD and TD:**
A biaxially oriented film will exhibit *Machine Direction (MD)* shrink and *Transverse Direction (TD)* shrink values that are independently engineered during the tenter-frame or blown-film process.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulae below are examination-grade. Units must be carried in every step.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Free Shrink Percentage** | $S_\% = \dfrac{L_0 - L_f}{L_0} \times 100$ | % | $L_0$ = original length, $L_f$ = final length |
| 2 | **Shrink Tension (Force per width)** | $\sigma_{sh} = \dfrac{F}{w}$ | N/m or g/cm | ASTM D2838 compliant |
| 3 | **Heat Energy Required to Shrink** | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | J (or kJ) | $m$ = film mass, $c_p$ = specific heat, $\Delta T$ = tunnel T − ambient T |
| 4 | **Convective Heat Transfer to Film** | $Q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{tunnel} - T_{film})$ | W | $h$ = convective coefficient |
| 5 | **Dwell Time in Tunnel** | $t_{dwell} = \dfrac{L_{tunnel}}{v_{conveyor}}$ | s | $L$ = tunnel length, $v$ = belt speed |
| 6 | **Shrink Force on Product** | $F_{product} = \sigma_{sh} \cdot w_{contact}$ | N | Critical for fragile goods |
| 7 | **Dimensional Yield After Shrink** | $L_f = L_0 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{S_\%}{100}\right)$ | mm, cm, m | Used for label/register design |
| 8 | **Glass Transition Relation (WLF)** | $\log a_T = \dfrac{-C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$ | dimensionless | Time-temperature superposition; $C_1 \approx 17.44$, $C_2 \approx 51.6$ K |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Free-shrink percentages are used for *sleeve* applications (where the film must close around the product), while shrink tension values are used for *banding* and *tamper-evident* applications (where mechanical grip on a bottle cap is required).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Shrink Percentage for a Tight Sleeve Fit

**Problem:** A rectangular box of dimensions $80 \text{ mm} \times 60 \text{ mm} \times 25 \text{ mm}$ is to be sleeve-wrapped using a flat film sleeve of circumference slightly larger than the box perimeter. The film currently available has an MD free shrink of **55 %** and a TD free shrink of **15 %**. Determine whether this film is suitable if the final sleeve must overlap by **10 mm** along the seam.

**Given:**
- Box perimeter around its largest cross-section: $P = 2(80 + 60) = 280$ mm
- Available film sleeve perimeter before shrink: $P_0 = P + \text{overlap allowance}$
- Required overlap after shrink: 10 mm

**Step 1 — Compute the sleeve perimeter before shrinking**

We need the seam to overlap by 10 mm *after* shrinking. Therefore, the perimeter before shrinking must account for shrink loss in the TD direction (the direction that closes the sleeve around the box).

$$
P_0 = 280 + 10 + (\text{TD shrink loss})
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the TD shrink loss**

$$
\text{TD shrink loss} = P_0 \times \dfrac{S_{TD}}{100}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the geometry equation**

$$
P_0 = 290 + P_0 \cdot \dfrac{15}{100}
$$

$$
P_0 - 0.15 P_0 = 290
$$

$$
0.85 \, P_0 = 290
$$

$$
P_0 = \dfrac{290}{0.85} = 341.18 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify final perimeter after shrink**

$$
P_f = P_0 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{15}{100}\right) = 341.18 \times 0.85 = 290.0 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Final overlap} = P_f - 280 = 10 \text{ mm} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** A sleeve of approximately **341 mm** starting perimeter is required; the film is **suitable**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heat Energy Required in a Shrink Tunnel

**Problem:** A continuous shrink tunnel processes **120 polyethylene-wrapped boxes per minute**. Each box carries **8 g** of LDPE film at an initial temperature of **25 °C**. The tunnel operates at **140 °C**. Compute the steady-state heat energy that must be delivered to the film per minute.

Assume $c_p$ for LDPE = **2300 J/(kg·K)**.

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate**

$$
\dot{m} = 120 \times 8 \text{ g/min} = 960 \text{ g/min} = 0.016 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Temperature rise**

$$
\Delta T = 140 - 25 = 115 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat energy per second**

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
\dot{Q} = 0.016 \times 2300 \times 115
$$

$$
\dot{Q} = 4232 \text{ W} \approx 4.23 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical correction**

Because convective heat transfer is not 100 % efficient, and the film does not necessarily reach tunnel air temperature (it reaches ~shrink-onset temperature), a typical derating factor of **1.3 – 1.6** is applied in industrial design:

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{tunnel heater}} = 4.23 \times 1.4 \approx 5.92 \text{ kW}
$$

**Conclusion:** The shrink tunnel heater must supply approximately **5.9 kW** of thermal energy for this throughput.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Shrink vs. Stretch Wrapping

| Property | Shrink Wrapping | Stretch Wrapping |
|---|---|---|
| Activation mechanism | **Heat** (thermal recoil) | **Mechanical tension** (elastic stretch) |
| Film type | Oriented, heat-shrinkable | Cast or blown, non-shrink |
| Energy input | High (thermal) | Low (kinetic) |
| Holding force | Generated during cooling | Maintained by elastic tension |
| Common application | Retail packs, tamper evidence | Pallet unitization |
| Equipment cost | Higher (tunnel/sealer) | Lower (turntable/robot) |
| Package appearance | Tight, glossy, "second skin" | Loose, sometimes cloudy |
| Recyclability | Often multi-layer | Easier (mono-LLDPE) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram of Shrink Wrapping Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Infeed Conveyor] --> B[Product Collation]
    B --> C[Film Unwind Station]
    C --> D[L Bar Sealer]
    D --> E[Shrink Tunnel Heat Zone]
    E --> F[Cooling Zone]
    F --> G[Discharge Conveyor]
    G --> H[Labelling or Coding Station]
    H --> I[Carton Packing]

    stepA[Heat Source: Electric IR / Gas Convection] -.-> E
    stepB[Exhaust Blower] -.-> E
    stepC[Circulation Fan] -.-> E
```

## 4.2 Polymer Orientation & Shrink Memory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Virgin Polymer Pellets] --> P2[Extrusion Die]
    P2 --> P3[Machine Direction MD Draw]
    P3 --> P4[Transverse Direction TD Tenter Frame]
    P4 --> P5[Rapid Quench Below Tg]
    P5 --> P6[Oriented Shrink Film Roll]
    P6 --> P7[Customer: Heat Above Tg]
    P7 --> P8[Entropy Driven Chain Recoil]
    P8 --> P9[Tight Conformity to Product]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Shrink Tunnel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INLET_INFEED
        A1[Conveyor Entry]
        A2[Film Around Product]
    end

    subgraph HEATING_ZONE
        B1[Heater Bank 1: Top IR Lamps]
        B2[Heater Bank 2: Bottom IR Lamps]
        B3[Side Air Nozzles]
        B4[Recirculation Blower]
    end

    subgraph COOLING_ZONE
        C1[Fan Array]
        C2[Ambient Air Intake]
    end

    subgraph OUTLET_DISCHARGE
        D1[Cooled Package]
        D2[Inspection / Reject]
    end

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
```

## 4.4 Decision Matrix — Film Selection for Common Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the product a food item?}
    Q1 -- YES --> F1[Use POF cross linked]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Is clarity critical?}
    Q2 -- YES --> F2[Use PETG or POF]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Is high holding force needed?}
    Q3 -- YES --> F3[Use LDPE heavy gauge]
    Q3 -- NO --> F4[Use PVC or PP]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define "free shrink" and "shrink tension". Why are both properties independently specified for shrink films?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Free shrink** is defined as the percentage dimensional recovery of a shrink film when it is heated *without any external constraint*. It is a measure of the film's ability to recoil dimensionally when unrestricted.

**Shrink tension** is the force (per unit width) generated by the film when it is heated *while restrained from shrinking*. It represents the mechanical grip the film exerts on the product.

**Why both?** A film can exhibit high free shrink but low shrink tension (soft, high-MD orientation), or high tension but low free shrink (stiffer films). For *sleeve wrapping*, free shrink dominates. For *tamper-evident banding*, shrink tension is critical. Hence both must be independently reported. **(1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**List any three (3) commonly used shrink film polymers and one characteristic application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Polyolefin (POF)** – Used for retail boxes and food packaging due to FDA approval and high clarity. **(1 mark)**
2. **PVC** – Used for software boxes, CDs, and low-cost retail packs due to easy sealing at low temperature. **(1 mark)**
3. **LDPE** – Used for pallet and heavy-bundle wrapping due to high shrink tension and impact resistance. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students commonly lose **3–4 marks** by:
> - Confusing **shrink %** with **shrink tension**.
> - Omitting **units** in every numerical step (KTU mandates SI or CGS units in every line).
> - Failing to mention the **role of glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** when asked *why* a film shrinks.
> - Writing only the *name* of polymers without stating **shrink temperature** or **property trade-offs**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEPOT748]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the molecular mechanism of shrink film. Discuss the role of the glass transition temperature ($T_g$) in activating shrink memory. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A heat-shrinkable polyolefin film has an MD free shrink of 60% and a TD free shrink of 20%. The film is supplied as a flat tube of circumference **40 cm**, which is to be used as a sleeve over a cylindrical bottle of circumference **30 cm**. Calculate:
   (i) The final circumference after full shrink in both directions.
   (ii) The longitudinal length remaining if the original tube length was 25 cm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Molecular Mechanism (7 marks):**

During film manufacturing, polymer chains in the molten state are forcibly oriented in the Machine Direction (MD) and Transverse Direction (TD) using either a tenter-frame or double-bubble process. **(2 marks)**

The oriented chains are in a state of low conformational entropy. Rapid quenching below the glass transition temperature ($T_g$) kinetically traps this orientation, freezing the chains in their elongated, low-entropy state. The film is now dimensionally stable at room temperature. **(2 marks)**

When the film is reheated in a shrink tunnel to a temperature *at or above* $T_g$ (typically 120–150 °C for POF), molecular mobility returns. The chains spontaneously recoil to their high-entropy random-coil conformation, which corresponds to the pre-orientation (smaller) dimensions. **(2 marks)**

The **role of $T_g$** is therefore to act as a "switch": below $T_g$, orientation is frozen; above $T_g$, recoil is activated. This is why shrink films are *thermo-responsive* and not *mechanically responsive*. **(1 mark)**

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Final circumference after full MD and TD shrink:** **(3 marks)**

> Note: The circumference change is governed by the **TD shrink** (transverse direction closes the loop around the bottle).

$$
C_f = C_0 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{S_{TD}}{100}\right)
$$

$$
C_f = 40 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{20}{100}\right) = 40 \times 0.80 = 32 \text{ cm}
$$

**(ii) Final tube length after MD shrink:** **(4 marks)**

The longitudinal length change is governed by **MD shrink**.

$$
L_f = L_0 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{S_{MD}}{100}\right)
$$

$$
L_f = 25 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{60}{100}\right) = 25 \times 0.40 = 10 \text{ cm}
$$

**Conclusion:** After shrinking, the sleeve becomes **32 cm in circumference** and **10 cm long** — perfectly wrapping the bottle. **(Final remark carries the 14th mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEPOT748]**

**(a)** Compare shrink wrapping with stretch wrapping in terms of activation mechanism, holding force, energy requirement, and typical applications. State two situations where shrink wrapping is the only viable choice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shrink tunnel is 2.5 m long. The conveyor speed is set so that each package spends 12 seconds inside the heating zone. Calculate:
   (i) The conveyor linear speed.
   (ii) The total heat required per minute for 90 packages/min, each carrying 10 g of LDPE film, if the film enters at 28 °C and leaves at 130 °C. Take $c_p = 2300 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Comparative Analysis (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Shrink Wrapping | Stretch Wrapping |
|---|---|---|
| Activation | Heat (above $T_g$) | Mechanical tension (no heat) |
| Holding force | Generated upon cooling | Maintained by elastic stretch |
| Energy | High (thermal) | Low (kinetic) |
| Application | Retail sleeves, tamper-evident bands | Pallet unitization |

**(5 marks for table)** + **(2 marks for stating two situations):**

1. **Tamper-evident bottle caps:** Stretch film cannot generate the radial compressive force required to seal under a cap.
2. **Irregular-shaped products (e.g., toys, book bundles):** Stretch film cannot conform to non-uniform geometries, whereas shrink film creates a uniform second skin.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Conveyor linear speed:** **(3 marks)**

$$
v = \dfrac{L_{tunnel}}{t_{dwell}} = \dfrac{2.5 \text{ m}}{12 \text{ s}} = 0.2083 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(ii) Heat required per minute:** **(4 marks)**

Mass flow rate:

$$
\dot{m} = 90 \text{ pkg/min} \times 10 \text{ g/pkg} = 900 \text{ g/min} = 0.015 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Temperature rise:

$$
\Delta T = 130 - 28 = 102 \text{ K}
$$

Heat transfer rate:

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 0.015 \times 2300 \times 102
$$

$$
\dot{Q} = 3519 \text{ W} \approx 3.52 \text{ kW}
$$

**Conclusion:** The tunnel must deliver roughly **3.5 kW** of heat to maintain steady-state shrink.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam:**

- **Definition:** Shrink wrapping = heat-activated recoil of oriented polymer film.
- **Molecular Trigger:** Heating above **$T_g$** unlocks chain orientation (entropy-driven).
- **Two Key Metrics:** Free shrink (%) **≠** Shrink tension (N/m). Know the difference.
- **Standard Polymers:** **PVC, POF (cross-linked), LDPE, PP, PETG** — each with a unique shrink temperature range.
- **Process Stages:** Collation → Film Enclosure → Heat Activation → Cooling.
- **Critical Process Variables:** Tunnel temperature, conveyor speed, dwell time, film gauge, orientation balance (MD/TD ratio).
- **Common Defects:**
  - *Dog ears* — uneven shrink at corners (low TD shrink).
  - *Bull's eyes* — circular distortion due to trapped air.
  - *Burn-through* — excessive tunnel temperature or dwell time.
  - *Insufficient shrink* — tunnel too cool or speed too high.
- **Numerical Must-Knows:**
  - Free shrink formula: $S_\% = (L_0 - L_f)/L_0 \times 100$
  - Dwell time: $t = L_{tunnel}/v$
  - Heat: $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$
- **Comparison Mantra:** *Heat shrinks, stretch pulls — never confuse the activation mechanism.*
- **Industry Benchmark:** Cross-linked POF is the most versatile food-grade shrink film.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "HOT-DUCT":**
> **H**eat activation, **O**riented polymer, **T**unnel sealing, **D**well time control, **U**niform film gauge, **C**ooling before stacking, **T**ension-free sleeve vs. tension-band distinction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pallet & Stretch Wrapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pallet & Stretch Wrapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Pallet Wrapping?

**Pallet Wrapping** (also known as **unit load wrapping** or **palletizing**) is a packaging technique in which a thin, flexible **plastic film** is wound helically around a palletized load of products to unitize them into a single, secure, transport-stable package. The primary objective is to **bind the individual packages together** so that they behave as one consolidated unit during handling, storage, and distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Pallet wrapping is a secondary packaging process that uses pre-stretched or un-stretched thermoplastic films (predominantly LLDPE-based) to apply a controlled containment force on a unit load, ensuring product integrity, stability, and protection against dust, moisture, and pilferage during the logistics chain.*

## 1.2 What is Stretch Wrapping?

**Stretch Wrapping** is a specific *sub-type* of pallet wrapping in which the plastic film is **mechanically elongated (stretched)** — typically between **150% to 300%** of its original length — *before* it is applied around the load. The stretched film then attempts to recover its original length, generating a powerful **elastic "holding-back" force** that compresses and stabilizes the load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams):**
> - **Stretch Wrapping** = Film is **stretched during application** → relies on **elastic recovery** of the film.
> - **Shrink Wrapping** = Film is **loosely applied first**, then **heat-shrunk** in an oven → relies on **thermal memory** of oriented film.
> These are **two entirely different technologies**, though students often confuse them.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Rubber Band Principle

Imagine a **rubber band** stretched around a bundle of pencils:

1. You stretch the rubber band (film gets elongated).
2. You slip it over the pencils (load).
3. The rubber band constantly *pulls inward*, gripping the pencils tightly.
4. Even if you shake the bundle, the pencils stay together because the rubber band never stops pulling.

**Stretch wrap works on exactly this principle.** When the LLDPE film is stretched around a pallet, the polymer chains are pulled into alignment, and the film *continuously tries to recoil*, clamping the load. The technical name for this inward pull is the **Load Containment Force (LCF)**.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in the Industry

- **Pre-stretch Ratio:** Typically **150% to 300%** (modern machines achieve up to **400%**).
- **Standard Film Thickness:** **12 µm to 35 µm** (most common: **17, 20, 23 µm**).
- **Standard Film Widths:** **500 mm (20")** is industry standard; **750 mm (30")** and **1000 mm (40")** for heavy/international pallets.
- **Load Containment Force (LCF):** Typically maintained between **1.0 kg/ply to 4.0 kg/ply** depending on load type.

> [!TIP]
> A *ply* refers to one complete helical revolution (one full turn around the pallet: top → bottom → top).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why is Stretch Wrapping So Widely Used?

The dominance of stretch wrapping in modern logistics stems from a unique combination of properties that no other packaging system can match at comparable cost:

| **Property** | **Mechanism in Stretch Film** | **Engineering Benefit** |
|---|---|---|
| **Elastic Recovery** | Amorphous LLDPE chains recoil after stretching | Generates continuous LCF, no heat/adhesive needed |
| **Two-Sided Cling** | Additives (PIB - Polyisobutylene) migrate to surface | Film adheres to itself, not to product |
| **Puncture Resistance** | LLDPE has high tear strength and dart impact | Protects against sharp corners (metal parts) |
| **Optical Clarity** | Cast films are highly transparent | Enables barcode/RFID scanning through the wrap |
| **Yield (m²/kg)** | Very thin films possible due to pre-stretch | Lower material cost per pallet |
| **Recyclability** | Single-polymer (mostly LLDPE) structure | SPI Code #4 — widely accepted in PE recycling streams |

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Stretch Film

Modern stretch film is a **co-extruded multi-layer structure**, typically **3, 5, 7, or even 9 layers**, each contributing a specific function:

1. **Outer Layer (Cling side)**: Contains **Polyisobutylene (PIB)** for self-cling. Also provides **slip** (anti-block) and **UV resistance**.
2. **Core Layer(s)**: Bulk LLDPE / mLLDPE providing **mechanical strength, dart impact, and load retention**.
3. **Inner Layer (Cling side)**: Contains higher PIB concentration to ensure self-adhesion during winding.

> [!NOTE]
> **mLLDPE** = *metallocene Linear Low-Density Polyethylene* — a high-performance variant offering superior puncture resistance and down-gauging capability (allowing thinner films with same strength).

## 2.3 Types of Stretch Wrapping Systems

### A) Manual Stretch Wrapping
- Operator walks around the pallet with a roll of film and a **disposable handle**.
- Used for low-volume operations (< 10 pallets/day).
- **Disadvantage:** Inconsistent tension, operator fatigue, no measurable LCF.

### B) Semi-Automatic Stretch Wrapping Machines
- **Turntable type** — pallet rotates on a turntable, film carriage is stationary.
- **Operator places pallet, presses start, machine wraps, operator removes pallet.**
- Throughput: **20-40 pallets/hour**.

### C) Automatic Stretch Wrapping Machines
- Fully integrated with conveyor systems.
- **Throughput: up to 120+ pallets/hour**.
- Include features like **automatic film cutting, clamping, and roping**.

### D) Orbital / Ring Stretch Wrappers
- Film is wrapped around a *stationary* pallet by an **orbiting ring**.
- Used for **long, awkward loads** (timber, doors, pipes) that cannot fit on a turntable.

## 2.4 Pre-Stretch — The Most Critical Concept

**Pre-stretch** is the process of elongating the film *before* it touches the load. It is achieved by passing the film between **two rollers rotating at different speeds**:

$$\text{Pre-stretch Ratio (\%)} = \left( \frac{V_{fast} - V_{slow}}{V_{slow}} \right) \times 100$$

Where:
- $V_{fast}$ = Speed of the faster (downstream) roller
- $V_{slow}$ = Speed of the slower (upstream) roller

> [!EXAMPLE]
> If the second roller runs at 250% the speed of the first, the film is stretched by 150% (i.e., from 100 m to 250 m of effective wrap length from 100 m of original film).

**Benefits of Pre-Stretching:**
1. **Film savings** — 1 m of film becomes 2.5 m of wrap → **60% less material used**.
2. **Consistent tension** — mechanical pre-stretch is more uniform than operator hand-stretching.
3. **Higher holding force** — stretched film is "energized" and applies stronger LCF.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Equation** | **Description** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-stretch Ratio** | $PSR = \left( \frac{L_s - L_0}{L_0} \right) \times 100$ | Where $L_0$ = original film length, $L_s$ = stretched length | **%** |
| **Film Yield (Yield per kg)** | $Y = \frac{W \times L}{m}$ | Where $W$ = width, $L$ = length, $m$ = mass | **m²/kg** |
| **Total Film Length per Roll** | $L_{roll} = \frac{m_{roll}}{t \times \rho \times W}$ | Where $t$ = thickness, $\rho$ = density (0.92 g/cm³ for LLDPE) | **m** |
| **Number of Wraps** | $N_w = \frac{H_{pallet} \times N_{top} \times 2 + H_{pallet} \times N_{body}}{H_{turn}}$ | Where $H_{turn}$ = vertical advance per turn | **wraps** |
| **Load Containment Force (LCF)** | $LCF = n \times T \times \mu$ | Simplified form: $n$ = number of plies, $T$ = tension, $\mu$ = friction | **kg or N** |
| **Effective Film Width Required** | $W_{eff} = W_{pallet} + 2 \times O_h$ | Where $O_h$ = overhang allowance (typically 50 mm) | **mm** |
| **Cost per Pallet** | $C_p = \frac{M_{film}}{Y \times N_p}$ | Where $M_{film}$ = cost of film roll, $N_p$ = pallets per roll | **₹/pallet** |
| **Stretch Force on Load** | $F = E \times A \times \varepsilon$ | Where $E$ = Young's modulus, $A$ = cross-section, $\varepsilon$ = strain | **N** |
| **Holds per Roll** | $H_r = \frac{L_{roll}}{L_{wrap}}$ | Where $L_{wrap}$ = length used per pallet | **pallets/roll** |
| **Throughput (m/min)** | $T_h = \frac{60 \times V_{carriage}}{H_{turn}}$ | Wrapping speed calculation | **pallets/hr** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Do not confuse **Pre-stretch %** with **Elongation at break %**. Pre-stretch is the *working elongation* (150-300%); Elongation at break is the *failure elongation* (typically 400-600% for LLDPE).

## 2.6 Real-World Industrial Utility

1. **FMCG Industry** — Coca-Cola, Unilever, P&G: every pallet of bottled/boxed goods is stretch-wrapped.
2. **Cold Chain Logistics** — Stretch film protects against condensation and minor water ingress.
3. **Pharmaceuticals** — Tamper-evident wrapping without heat (no risk of damaging heat-sensitive APIs).
4. **E-commerce Fulfillment** — Amazon, Flipkart warehouses wrap millions of pallets monthly.
5. **Construction Materials** — Bricks, tiles, cement bags are unitized with stretch + hood film.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Film Length Required for One Pallet

**Given:**
- Pallet dimensions: $1200 \text{ mm} \times 1000 \text{ mm} \times H$ (height)
- Film width: $W_f = 500 \text{ mm}$
- Pre-stretch ratio: $PS = 200\%$ (i.e., 1 m of film becomes 3 m of wrap)
- Number of bottom wraps: $N_b = 4$
- Number of top wraps: $N_t = 4$
- Body wraps (helical turns): such that they cover full height.

**Step 1:** Calculate the perimeter of the pallet base.

$$P_{base} = 2 \times (L + W) = 2 \times (1200 + 1000) = 4400 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate film length per bottom/top wrap (these are full-perimeter wraps).

$$L_{wrap} = P_{base} = 4400 \text{ mm} = 4.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate total film length for top + bottom wraps (assuming 4 bottom + 4 top = 8 plies).

$$L_{top+bot} = 8 \times L_{wrap} = 8 \times 4.4 = 35.2 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the effective film length per helical turn (after pre-stretch).

A helical turn covers vertical height = film width contribution along the height. For a 500 mm film on a 1200 mm wide pallet, the film makes approximately $\frac{H}{500}$ revolutions per unit height.

**Step 5:** Compensate for pre-stretch — the **raw film length** required is reduced.

$$L_{raw} = \frac{L_{total\_wrap}}{1 + PS/100} = \frac{L_{total\_wrap}}{1 + 200/100} = \frac{L_{total\_wrap}}{3}$$

This means **1 m of raw film yields 3 m of applied wrap**.

**Step 6:** Total raw film needed.

$$L_{raw\_total} = \frac{35.2 + L_{helical}}{3}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Number of Pallets per Roll (Holds per Roll)

**Given:**
- Roll weight: $M_{roll} = 2500 \text{ g} = 2.5 \text{ kg}$
- Film thickness: $t = 23 \text{ \mu m} = 23 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- Film width: $W_f = 0.5 \text{ m}$
- LLDPE density: $\rho = 920 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1:** Calculate total film length on the roll.

$$L_{roll} = \frac{M_{roll}}{t \times \rho \times W_f}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$L_{roll} = \frac{2.5}{23 \times 10^{-6} \times 920 \times 0.5}$$

**Step 3:** Compute denominator.

$$23 \times 10^{-6} \times 920 \times 0.5 = 23 \times 10^{-6} \times 460 = 10580 \times 10^{-6} = 0.01058 \text{ kg/m}$$

**Step 4:** Final length.

$$L_{roll} = \frac{2.5}{0.01058} = 236.30 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** If 15 m of raw film (pre-stretch) is consumed per pallet, number of pallets covered.

$$N_{pallets} = \frac{L_{roll}}{L_{per\_pallet}} = \frac{236.30}{15} \approx 15.75 \approx 15 \text{ pallets/roll}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Load Containment Force (LCF) — Simplified Model

The LCF depends on the film's **tensile properties** and the **number of plies** wound around the load.

**Given:**
- Film thickness: $t = 23 \text{ \mu m}$
- Film width: $W_f = 500 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \text{ m}$
- Tensile stress at working stretch: $\sigma = 12 \text{ MPa} = 12 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$
- Number of plies: $n = 8$ (4 bottom + 4 top)

**Step 1:** Cross-sectional area of one ply.

$$A = t \times W_f = 23 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.5 = 11.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Tension (force) in one ply.

$$T = \sigma \times A = 12 \times 10^6 \times 11.5 \times 10^{-6} = 138 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3:** Total Load Containment Force.

$$F_{LCF} = n \times T = 8 \times 138 = 1104 \text{ N} \approx 112.6 \text{ kgf}$$

**Step 4:** Verify against load weight recommendation — typically LCF should be **1.5% to 3% of load weight** for stable unitization.

$$\text{Load capacity} = \frac{F_{LCF}}{0.02} = \frac{112.6}{0.02} = 5630 \text{ kg}$$

This means the 8-ply wrap can adequately unitize loads up to ~**5.6 tonnes**, typical of heavy industrial pallets.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Wrap Pattern

| **Load Type** | **Top Wraps** | **Bottom Wraps** | **Body Wraps** | **Pre-stretch** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Light boxes (< 500 kg)** | 2 | 2 | Helical @ 50 mm pitch | 150% | Standard A-B wrap |
| **Medium (500-1000 kg)** | 3 | 3 | Helical @ 40 mm pitch | 200% | Reinforced |
| **Heavy (> 1000 kg)** | 4 | 4 | Helical + cross-plies | 250-300% | Maximum stability |
| **Tippy / Slippery (Bottles, Drums)** | 5+ | 5+ | Tight helical | 250% | Use net/vented film |
| **Irregular shapes** | 4 | 4 | Orbital + turntable | 200% | May require corner boards |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top wraps are MORE critical than bottom wraps** for top-heavy or tippy loads, because they hold the top plies of the load together. KTU examiners love to test this practical knowledge.

## 3.5 Cost Optimization Worked Example

**Given:**
- Film roll cost: $M_{roll} = ₹2000$
- Roll weight: $2.5 \text{ kg}$
- Pre-stretch = 200%
- Pallets covered per roll = 15.75

**Step 1:** Cost per kg of film.

$$C_{kg} = \frac{2000}{2.5} = 800 \text{ ₹/kg}$$

**Step 2:** Cost per pallet (raw).

$$C_p = \frac{2000}{15.75} = 126.98 \text{ ₹/pallet}$$

**Step 3:** If pre-stretch is increased to 300% (more film savings per pallet), pallets per roll increases to ~21, cost drops to:

$$C_p = \frac{2000}{21} = 95.24 \text{ ₹/pallet}$$

This is a **25% cost reduction** by simply adjusting the pre-stretch setting — a common KTU question on cost optimization.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define stretch wrapping. List any two advantages of stretch wrapping over shrink wrapping.*

**Model Answer:**

**Stretch Wrapping** is a packaging process in which a thermoplastic film (usually LLDPE) is **mechanically elongated and wrapped helically** around a unit load. The stretched film generates a continuous elastic recovery force, which unitizes the load without the application of heat.

**Two Advantages over Shrink Wrapping:**
1. **No heat required** — saves energy cost and is safe for heat-sensitive products (chocolate, pharmaceuticals, explosives).
2. **Higher material efficiency** — pre-stretch (150-300%) gives 2-4× more wrap per kg of film, reducing packaging cost.
3. *Any other valid point* (e.g., faster cycle time, no fire hazard, no fumes in the warehouse).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the function of PIB (Polyisobutylene) in stretch film?*

**Model Answer:**

**PIB (Polyisobutylene)** is a tackifier additive blended into the outer/inner surface layers of stretch film. Its function is to provide **two-sided cling** — enabling the film to adhere to itself when wound in overlapping plies, without sticking to the actual product. This self-cling eliminates the need for adhesives or heat sealing to anchor the film tail. Typical PIB concentration: **2-5% by weight** in the skin layers.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**
**(a)** Explain the various types of stretch wrapping machines with neat diagrams. Discuss their throughput and suitable applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pallet of dimensions 1200 mm × 1000 mm × 1500 mm (L × W × H) is to be stretch wrapped using 23 µm LLDPE film (density 920 kg/m³) of width 500 mm. The pre-stretch ratio is 250%. If 8 plies (4 top + 4 bottom) of full-perimeter wraps are applied, calculate: **(i)** Raw film length required, **(ii)** Number of pallets that can be wrapped from a 2.5 kg roll. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**1. Manual Stretch Wrapping:**
- Operator hand-wraps the pallet using a film roll + handle.
- Throughput: **5-10 pallets/hour**.
- Application: Small warehouses, low-volume dispatch.

**2. Turntable Type (Semi-Automatic):**
- Pallet sits on a motor-driven rotating turntable; film carriage is stationary.
- Throughput: **20-40 pallets/hour**.
- Application: Medium-volume FMCG, distribution centers.

**3. Automatic Turntable:**
- Fully conveyor-integrated; auto film cut, clamp, and seal.
- Throughput: **60-120 pallets/hour**.
- Application: High-volume manufacturing, e-commerce hubs.

**4. Orbital Wrapper:**
- Film orbits a *stationary* pallet.
- Throughput: **30-50 pallets/hour**.
- Application: Long/awkward loads — timber, pipes, doors, building boards.

**5. Ring Wrapper:**
- A high-speed ring rotates around a stationary pallet.
- Throughput: **up to 200 pallets/hour**.
- Application: Mass-production (beverages, paper, cement).

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given Data:**
- $L = 1200 \text{ mm}$, $W = 1000 \text{ mm}$, $H = 1500 \text{ mm}$
- $t = 23 \text{ \mu m} = 23 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- $\rho = 920 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $W_f = 0.5 \text{ m}$
- Pre-stretch: $PS = 250\%$
- Number of full-perimeter plies: $n = 8$

**Step 1:** Calculate pallet base perimeter.

$$P = 2 \times (L + W) = 2 \times (1.2 + 1.0) = 4.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate total length of *applied* (stretched) wrap per pallet.

$$L_{applied} = n \times P = 8 \times 4.4 = 35.2 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate *raw* (un-stretched) film length using the pre-stretch formula.

$$L_{raw} = \frac{L_{applied}}{1 + PS/100} = \frac{35.2}{1 + 2.5} = \frac{35.2}{3.5} = 10.06 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the total length of film on one roll.

$$L_{roll} = \frac{M_{roll}}{t \times \rho \times W_f} = \frac{2.5}{23 \times 10^{-6} \times 920 \times 0.5}$$

$$L_{roll} = \frac{2.5}{0.01058} = 236.30 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate number of pallets per roll.

$$N_{pallets} = \frac{L_{roll}}{L_{raw}} = \frac{236.30}{10.06} \approx 23.49 \approx 23 \text{ pallets/roll}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) **Raw film length required per pallet = 10.06 m**
- (ii) **Number of pallets per roll ≈ 23**

---

### Question B (Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**(a)** Discuss the properties of LLDPE that make it the most preferred polymer for stretch film. Compare LLDPE with LDPE and mLLDPE for stretch film applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is Load Containment Force (LCF)? An 8-ply stretch wrap is applied on a pallet using 23 µm × 500 mm film. If the film is working at 12 MPa tensile stress, calculate: **(i)** LCF per ply, **(ii)** Total LCF, **(iii)** Maximum recommended load weight for stable unitization (assume 2% LCF-to-load ratio). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Key Properties of LLDPE for Stretch Film:**

| **Property** | **Value/Characteristic** | **Benefit in Stretch Film** |
|---|---|---|
| **Density** | 0.918-0.940 g/cm³ | Lightweight, high yield (m²/kg) |
| **Tensile Strength (MD)** | 25-45 MPa | Withstands pre-stretch forces |
| **Elongation at Break** | 400-700% | Allows 200-300% working stretch |
| **Dart Drop Impact** | 150-400 g | Resists puncture from sharp corners |
| **Cling Compatibility** | Excellent with PIB | Uniform cling without tackiness |
| **Tear Strength (Elmendorf)** | 80-150 g/µm | Resists tear propagation |

**Comparison Table:**

| **Property** | **LDPE** | **LLDPE** | **mLLDPE** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polymerization** | Free radical, high-pressure | Ziegler-Natta or metallocene, low-pressure | Single-site metallocene catalyst |
| **Chain Structure** | Long, short, branched randomly | Linear with short, uniform branches | Highly uniform, narrow MWD |
| **Tensile Strength** | Low (15-25 MPa) | Medium-High (25-45 MPa) | Very High (40-60 MPa) |
| **Puncture Resistance** | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| **Cling** | High natural cling | Moderate (needs PIB) | Low (needs PIB) |
| **Down-gauging Potential** | Poor | Good | Excellent (25% reduction possible) |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Medium | Highest (1.5-2× LLDPE) |
| **Application in Stretch Film** | Hand-wrap, low-end | **Industry standard** | Premium, high-performance films |

**Conclusion:** LLDPE offers the best balance of cost, strength, and processability. mLLDPE is used as a **blend (10-30%)** with conventional LLDPE in high-performance films.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Definition:** Load Containment Force (LCF) is the total inward radial force exerted by the stretched film plies on the unit load, holding all individual packages together as a single stable unit.

**Given:**
- $t = 23 \text{ \mu m} = 23 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- $W_f = 500 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \text{ m}$
- $\sigma = 12 \text{ MPa} = 12 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$
- $n = 8$ plies

**(i) LCF per Ply:**

Cross-sectional area of one ply:

$$A = t \times W_f = 23 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.5 = 11.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

LCF per ply:

$$F_{ply} = \sigma \times A = 12 \times 10^6 \times 11.5 \times 10^{-6} = 138 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Total LCF:**

$$F_{total} = n \times F_{ply} = 8 \times 138 = 1104 \text{ N}$$

Converting to kgf ($1 \text{ kgf} = 9.81 \text{ N}$):

$$F_{total} = \frac{1104}{9.81} = 112.54 \text{ kgf}$$

**(iii) Maximum Recommended Load Weight:**

For stable unitization, LCF should be at least **2% of load weight**.

$$W_{load} = \frac{F_{total}}{0.02} = \frac{112.54}{0.02} = 5627 \text{ kg} \approx 5.6 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) LCF per ply = **138 N** (or 14.07 kgf)
- (ii) Total LCF = **1104 N** (or 112.54 kgf)
- (iii) Max load weight = **~5.6 tonnes**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert µm → m, MPa → N/m², or N → kgf. These cost **1-2 marks** per question.
> 2. **Pre-stretch direction:** Students often divide instead of using the correct formula. Recall: $L_{raw} = \frac{L_{applied}}{1 + PS/100}$ (NOT $L_{raw} = L_{applied} \times PS$).
> 3. **Confusing stretch wrap with shrink wrap:** Examiners will deduct marks if a question specifically asks about stretch wrap and the answer describes shrink wrap.
> 4. **Skipping "given data" step:** Always write down the given data and label your final answers — this shows logical structuring and earns method marks even if the final answer is wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ Stretch wrapping uses **mechanical elongation**; shrink wrapping uses **thermal shrinkage** — these are different technologies.
- ✅ The most common stretch film polymer is **LLDPE** (often with **mLLDPE** blend).
- ✅ **PIB (Polyisobutylene)** is the cling additive — typically 2-5% in the skin layers.
- ✅ Standard film thickness: **17-23 µm**; width: **500 mm (20")** is industry standard.
- ✅ **Pre-stretch ratio** in modern machines: **150-300%** (high-end up to 400%).
- ✅ **Load Containment Force (LCF)** should be **1.5-3% of load weight** for stable unitization.
- ✅ **Top wraps are more critical** than bottom wraps for tippy/top-heavy loads.
- ✅ **LCF formula:** $F_{LCF} = n \times \sigma \times (t \times W_f)$ — know this derivation cold.
- ✅ **Pre-stretch saves material:** 1 m of raw film = 3 m of applied wrap at 200% pre-stretch → **66% savings**.
- ✅ Film **yield (m²/kg)** is the key cost metric — higher yield = lower cost per pallet.
- ✅ **Orbital wrappers** are used for long, awkward loads (timber, pipes, doors).
- ✅ **Ring wrappers** are the highest-speed automatic systems (up to 200 pallets/hr).
- ✅ Modern stretch film is **multi-layer co-extruded** (3, 5, 7, or 9 layers).
- ✅ Cling is **two-sided** (film sticks to itself, NOT to the product).
- ✅ **Recycling code:** LLDPE = SPI #4.
- ✅ Film calculation: $L_{roll} = \frac{M_{roll}}{t \times \rho \times W_f}$ — memorize this formula.
- ✅ Cost per pallet = $\frac{\text{Roll cost}}{\text{Pallets per roll}}$ — increasing pre-stretch reduces cost per pallet.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sealing Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sealing Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sealing** in plastics packaging technology refers to the process of permanently joining two or more thermoplastic (or multi-layer laminate) surfaces together using a combination of **heat**, **pressure**, and **dwell time**, such that the bonded interface achieves sufficient **peel strength**, **burst strength**, and **hermetic integrity** to protect the product throughout its intended shelf-life and supply chain journey.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEPOT748, sealing methods are broadly classified into:

1. **Thermal Sealing Methods** – Heat Seal, Impulse Seal, Hot Bar Seal
2. **Friction-Based Sealing Methods** – Ultrasonic Seal, RF (Radio-Frequency) Seal, Friction Seal
3. **Induction-Based Sealing** – Electromagnetic Induction Seal
4. **Adhesive-Based Sealing** – Cold Seal, Pressure-Sensitive Seal
5. **Mechanical/Residual Heat Sealing** – Cold Knife, Hot Wire Seal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** A *seal* is NOT merely a "joint" — it is a **molecular-level interdiffusion interface** where polymer chains from two surfaces entangle across the boundary to form a homogenous weld. Understanding this is critical for board exam answers worth 7-14 marks.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you have **two pieces of butter** on a plate. If you press them together, they stick but come apart easily. Now, imagine you **lightly warm** them and then press — they merge into one continuous piece of butter. The warmth makes the butter molecules "excited" enough to intermingle across the boundary.

This is **exactly** what happens in plastic sealing:
- The **heat** excites the polymer chains (gives them kinetic energy)
- The **pressure** pushes the chains across the boundary
- The **dwell time** allows the chains to fully interdiffuse
- On cooling, the chains are "frozen" in this entangled state → **permanent seal**

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it as molecular-level Velcro:** Millions of tiny polymer chains from each side hook into each other. The stronger the interdiffusion, the harder it is to peel apart.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sealing Temperature | $T_s$ | °C (Celsius) | **80 – 220 °C** |
| Sealing Pressure | $P_s$ | N/mm² (MPa) | **0.1 – 0.6 MPa** |
| Dwell Time | $t_d$ | seconds (s) | **0.3 – 5 s** |
| Seal Strength | $\sigma_s$ | N/15mm | **≥ 15 N/15mm** (food grade) |
| Hot Tack Strength | $\sigma_{HT}$ | N/15mm | **≥ 2 N/15mm** (form-fill-seal) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When a question asks "list the sealing parameters," always write the **three primary parameters (T, P, t)** AND the **two secondary quality metrics (Seal Strength, Hot Tack)**. Examiners award extra marks for completeness.

---

## 1.4 Why Sealing is Critical in Plastics Packaging

Sealing is the **last line of defence** for the packaged product. A weak or defective seal leads to:
- **Leakage** → product spoilage
- **Oxygen ingress** → oxidation of food
- **Moisture loss/gain** → texture degradation
- **Contamination** → microbial growth
- **Tampering vulnerability** → safety risks

Therefore, every KTU exam question on sealing tests both the **methodology** AND the **quality assurance** angle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Sealing

Every sealing method — regardless of heat source — operates on three fundamental inputs:

### Pillar 1: **Temperature ($T_s$)**
- Must exceed the **Vicat Softening Point** of the polymer
- Must approach but NOT exceed the **melting point ($T_m$)** for semi-crystalline polymers (e.g., PE, PP)
- Must approach but NOT exceed the **glass transition ($T_g$)** for amorphous polymers (e.g., PS, PET)
- **Too low** → incomplete fusion → weak seal
- **Too high** → polymer degradation, burn-through, pinholes

### Pillar 2: **Pressure ($P_s$)**
- Forces the molten/semi-molten polymer surfaces into intimate contact
- Squeezes out trapped air bubbles
- Determines seal thickness (over-pressure → "blow-out" defects)
- Typical range: **0.1 – 0.6 MPa**

### Pillar 3: **Dwell Time ($t_d$)**
- Allows heat conduction to penetrate the seal layer
- Allows polymer chain interdiffusion across the boundary
- Must be long enough for **complete fusion** but short enough to avoid **heat damage**

---

## 2.2 Polymer Chain Interdiffusion — The Science Behind the Seal

When two polymer surfaces are brought together above their softening temperature, the polymer chain ends (and entanglements) begin a process called **reptation** (proposed by **de Gennes**, 1971).

The interdiffusion depth $\xi(t)$ follows:

$$\xi(t) = \sqrt{D_p \cdot t_d}$$

Where:
- $D_p$ = polymer self-diffusion coefficient (m²/s)
- $t_d$ = dwell time (s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Relevance:** The depth of interdiffusion must be **greater than the radius of gyration ($R_g$)** of the polymer chain for a coherent seal to form. This is why some polymers seal better than others — they have more mobile chains.

---

## 2.3 The Heat Seal Curve — A KTU Favourite Concept

The **Heat Seal Curve** is a graph of **Seal Strength vs. Sealing Temperature**, holding pressure and dwell time constant. It has **four distinct regions**:

| Region | Temperature Range | Behavior | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I. No Seal** | $T < T_{seal-onset}$ | Strength ≈ 0 | Surface not yet activated |
| **II. Rapid Rise** | $T_{seal-onset}$ to $T_{melt-onset}$ | Strength climbs sharply | Surface flows and wets |
| **III. Plateau** | $T_{melt-onset}$ to $T_{degrade-onset}$ | Strength ≈ maximum, stable | Optimal sealing window |
| **IV. Degradation** | $T > T_{degrade-onset}$ | Strength drops again | Polymer chains break down |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick Question:** "Why does seal strength *drop* at very high temperatures?" Answer: Because the polymer undergoes **thermal degradation, chain scission, and oxidative breakdown**, weakening the seal interface.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\xi(t) = \sqrt{D_p \cdot t_d}$ | Interdiffusion depth | Predicts seal quality |
| 2 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat energy required to raise seal layer | Heat sealer sizing |
| 3 | $t_{heat} = \frac{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot L^2}{2k}$ | Time for heat to penetrate thickness $L$ (Fourier conduction) | Setting dwell time |
| 4 | $\sigma_{seal} = \dfrac{F_{peel}}{w_{seal}}$ | Seal strength definition | Quality testing |
| 5 | $T_{hot-tack} = f(T, P, \epsilon)$ | Hot tack strength as function of strain rate | FFS (Form-Fill-Seal) packaging |
| 6 | $E_{act} = R \cdot T_s \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)^{-1}$ | Arrhenius activation energy for seal failure | Predicting seal aging |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Pipes:** In the table above, all mathematical operators use \vert-style absolute value notation only where physically meaningful. The formulas are rendered with standard LaTeX syntax to avoid breaking markdown table structure.

---

## 2.5 Classification of Sealing Methods — Engineering Comparison

| Sealing Method | Heat Source | Suitable Polymers | Production Speed | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat Seal (Hot Bar)** | Conductive jaw | PE, PP, laminated films | Medium | Pouches, sachets |
| **Impulse Seal** | Resistive Ni-Cr wire | PE, PP, laminates | High | Food pouches |
| **Ultrasonic Seal** | 20–40 kHz vibration | PE, PP, PET | Very High | Tube sealing, blister packs |
| **Induction Seal** | Electromagnetic (HF) | Foil + polymer layer | Very High | Bottle caps (tamper evident) |
| **RF Seal** | 27.12 MHz field | PVC, EVA, PU | Medium | Medical pouches |
| **Cold Seal** | Pressure + adhesive | Coated films (latex adhesive) | Very High | Candy wrappers |
| **Hot Wire Seal** | Hot wire cuts + seals | Thermoplastics | High | T-shirt bags |
| **Friction Seal** | Frictional heat | Films, woven PP | Medium | Specialty bags |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **real packaging industry**, the choice of sealing method is governed by:

1. **Polymer type** — PE needs ~120°C, PET needs ~180°C, PVC needs ~140°C
2. **Production speed** — FFS lines need >200 packs/min → ultrasonic or impulse
3. **Product sensitivity** — chocolate needs cold seal (no heat exposure)
4. **Hygiene** — pharmaceutical needs validated hermetic seals (RF/ultrasonic)
5. **Cost** — cold seal is fastest but requires adhesive-coated films

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Induction sealing creates a **tamper-evident hermetic seal** on bottle caps — the foil liner is bonded to the bottle mouth by induction heating of an aluminum layer, while the cap is removable. This is the gold standard for pharma and food bottles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Seal Strength from First Principles

The **seal strength** $\sigma_{seal}$ is defined as the **peel force per unit width** required to separate the sealed interface.

### Step 1: Start with the Definition
The peel force $F_{peel}$ acts along the width $w$ of the seal strip. By definition:

$$\sigma_{seal} = \frac{F_{peel}}{w_{seal}}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_{seal}$ = seal strength (N/m or N/15mm)
- $F_{peel}$ = peel force at failure (N)
- $w_{seal}$ = seal width (m)

### Step 2: Express the Failure Condition
At failure, the peel force equals the **interfacial bond force**, which depends on the depth of polymer interdiffusion $\xi$:

$$F_{peel} = \eta \cdot \xi(t) \cdot G_{interface}$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = efficiency factor (0 to 1)
- $G_{interface}$ = interfacial shear modulus (N/m²)

### Step 3: Substitute Interdiffusion Depth
From Section 2.2, we have:

$$\xi(t) = \sqrt{D_p \cdot t_d}$$

So:

$$F_{peel} = \eta \cdot \sqrt{D_p \cdot t_d} \cdot G_{interface}$$

### Step 4: Derive the Final Seal Strength Equation

$$\sigma_{seal} = \frac{\eta \cdot G_{interface} \cdot \sqrt{D_p \cdot t_d}}{w_{seal}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Increasing **dwell time** ($\sqrt{t_d}$) increases seal strength — but only as a **square-root law**. Doubling dwell time increases seal strength by only **41.4%** (i.e., $\sqrt{2}$). This is why industry prefers to increase **temperature** instead.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Time Required to Heat the Seal Layer

For a film of thickness $L$ being sealed from one side, the time $t_{heat}$ for the heat front to penetrate is derived from **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction** for a semi-infinite slab:

### Step 1: Apply Fourier's Law
For 1-D conduction:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

Where $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \cdot c_p}$ = thermal diffusivity (m²/s).

### Step 2: Apply Semi-Infinite Slab Solution
The solution to this PDE for a surface temperature step is the **error function** solution:

$$T(x,t) = T_s + (T_0 - T_s) \cdot \text{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha \cdot t}}\right)$$

### Step 3: Set the Boundary Condition
At the seal interface, the temperature must reach $T_{interface}$ for sealing to occur. At depth $x = L$:

$$\frac{T_{interface} - T_s}{T_0 - T_s} = \text{erf}\left(\frac{L}{2\sqrt{\alpha \cdot t_{heat}}}\right)$$

### Step 4: Solve for Dwell Time
Using the inverse error function:

$$t_{heat} = \frac{L^2}{4\alpha \cdot \left[\text{erf}^{-1}\left(\dfrac{T_{interface} - T_s}{T_0 - T_s}\right)\right]^2}$$

### Step 5: Simplification for Engineering Use
For a typical scenario where the interface must reach ~63% of the seal temperature:

$$t_{heat} \approx \frac{L^2}{2\alpha}$$

$$\boxed{t_{heat} \approx \frac{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot L^2}{2k}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Result:** Doubling the film thickness **quadruples** the required dwell time. This is why thick laminate films require either higher temperature OR longer dwell times. This single result can earn **7 marks** if explained correctly.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Process Parameters vs. Defect Types

| Defect Type | Likely Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Weak / Peeling Seal** | Temperature too low OR dwell too short | Increase $T_s$ by 5°C increments; increase $t_d$ by 0.2s |
| **Burn-Through / Pinholes** | Temperature too high | Reduce $T_s$ immediately |
| **Wrinkled Seal** | Pressure too high OR film misaligned | Reduce $P_s$; check web tension |
| **Cloudy / Milky Seal** | Incomplete fusion OR contamination | Clean sealing jaws; raise $T_s$ |
| **Sticking to Jaws** | Anti-stick coating worn OR $T_s$ too high | Replace Teflon® coating; lower $T_s$ |
| **Inconsistent Seal Across Width** | Non-uniform jaw pressure | Calibrate pressure distribution |
| **Delamination in Laminate Seal** | Wrong seal layer selection | Use PE or Surlyn® seal layer |

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis: Hot Tack Strength vs. Seal Strength

A frequently tested KTU concept:

| Property | Seal Strength | Hot Tack Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **When Measured** | After full cooling | Immediately after sealing (hot state) |
| **Test Temperature** | 23°C (ambient) | Just below $T_m$ (~80-100°C) |
| **Critical For** | Storage and transport | Form-Fill-Seal (FFS) lines |
| **Failure Mode** | Peel failure | Hot tearing failure |
| **Typical Minimum** | ≥ 15 N/15mm | ≥ 2 N/15mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Hot Tack Matters:** In a Form-Fill-Seal machine, the product (e.g., liquid soup) is dropped into the bag IMMEDIATELY after sealing, while the seal is still hot. If hot tack is too low, the seal **opens up** under the weight of the product. This is a real industry failure mode — write this example in your exam!

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A PE film of thickness $L = 100 \, \mu m$ is being heat sealed. The thermal conductivity $k = 0.33 \, W/(m \cdot K)$, density $\rho = 920 \, kg/m^3$, specific heat $c_p = 2300 \, J/(kg \cdot K)$. Find the minimum dwell time.

### Step 1: Identify Parameters

$$L = 100 \times 10^{-6} \, m = 1 \times 10^{-4} \, m$$

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho \cdot c_p} = \frac{0.33}{920 \times 2300}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Thermal Diffusivity

$$\alpha = \frac{0.33}{2.116 \times 10^6}$$

$$\alpha = 1.56 \times 10^{-7} \, m^2/s$$

### Step 3: Apply the Formula

$$t_{heat} = \frac{L^2}{2\alpha}$$

$$t_{heat} = \frac{(1 \times 10^{-4})^2}{2 \times 1.56 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$t_{heat} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-8}}{3.12 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\boxed{t_{heat} \approx 0.032 \, s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For a typical PE film, only about **30 milliseconds** of heat exposure is theoretically needed. In practice, dwell times are **10–100× longer** to account for actual contact resistance, polymer rheology, and process variability.
