# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "sealing" in the context of plastics packaging. List the three primary process parameters that govern any sealing operation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Sealing is the process of permanently joining two thermoplastic or laminate film surfaces using a controlled combination of **heat, pressure, and dwell time**, such that the polymer chains at the interface interdiffuse across the boundary to form a hermetic, mechanically strong bond.

**Three Primary Parameters (2 marks):**
1. **Sealing Temperature ($T_s$)** — Temperature applied to bring the polymer to a sealable state (between Vicat softening point and melting point).
2. **Sealing Pressure ($P_s$)** — Force per unit area applied to push the molten surfaces into intimate contact.
3. **Dwell Time ($t_d$)** — Duration for which heat and pressure are applied to allow complete fusion and chain interdiffusion.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is "hot tack strength" and why is it critical in Form-Fill-Seal (FFS) packaging lines?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Hot tack strength is the seal strength measured **immediately after sealing, while the seal is still hot and not yet fully cooled** to ambient temperature.

**Why Critical (2 marks):**
1. In FFS lines, the product (e.g., liquid soup, detergent, sauce) is filled into the pouch **within milliseconds** of sealing, while the seal is still molten.
2. The hot seal must resist the **weight and impact of the falling product** without opening up.
3. A high hot tack prevents **hot-tear failure**, ensuring the package survives the FFS process without leaking.
4. Typical industry minimum: **≥ 2 N/15mm** for FFS-grade films.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the "Heat Seal Curve" in detail. Sketch and label its four regions. Why does seal strength drop at very high sealing temperatures?**

**Model Answer:**

**Heat Seal Curve Definition (1 mark):** The Heat Seal Curve is a graphical plot of **seal strength (N/15mm) on the Y-axis** versus **sealing temperature (°C) on the X-axis**, with pressure and dwell time held constant.

**Four Regions (4 marks):**

| Region | Temperature | Behavior | Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I. No Seal Zone** | Below $T_{seal-onset}$ | Seal strength ≈ 0 | Polymer not softened; surfaces do not fuse |
| **II. Rapid Rise** | Between onset and plateau | Strength rises sharply | Surfaces begin to flow and wet; chain interdiffusion initiates |
| **III. Plateau (Optimal Window)** | Between onset and degradation | Maximum stable seal | Complete fusion; full chain entanglement |
| **IV. Degradation** | Above $T_{degrade-onset}$ | Strength drops again | Polymer chains undergo thermal scission, oxidation, charring |

**Why Strength Drops at High T (2 marks):**
At temperatures exceeding the polymer's degradation onset, the following occur:
- **Chain scission** — long polymer chains break into shorter fragments
- **Oxidative degradation** — oxygen reacts with molten polymer, creating carbonyl groups
- **Crystallinity loss** — in semi-crystalline polymers (PE, PP), excessive melting destroys the reinforcing crystalline domains
- **Char formation** — at extreme temperatures, carbonaceous residues form pinholes and weak spots

**Sketch (refer to Section 4.2):** Student should draw the S-shaped curve with four clearly labeled regions.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare the following sealing methods with respect to heat source, suitable polymers, and typical applications: (i) Impulse Sealing, (ii) Ultrasonic Sealing, (iii) Induction Sealing.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Impulse Sealing (2 marks):**
- **Heat Source:** A resistive Nichrome (Ni-Cr) wire embedded in the sealing bar; current is pulsed for a few milliseconds, then the bar cools under pressure.
- **Suitable Polymers:** PE, PP, laminated films (PET/PE, BOPP/PE), co-extruded films.
- **Typical Applications:** Food pouches, sachets, medical packaging, retort pouches.

**(ii) Ultrasonic Sealing (2.5 marks):**
- **Heat Source:** High-frequency mechanical vibration (20–40 kHz) generated by a piezoelectric transducer, transmitted via a horn (sonotrode) to the film interface. Heat is generated **by internal friction at the interface only**.
- **Suitable Polymers:** Thermoplastics — PE, PP, PET, PA (nylon), and blends. NOT suitable for stiff amorphous polymers like PS.
- **Typical Applications:** Tube sealing (toothpaste, ointment), blister packaging, through-contaminant sealing, medical device pouches.

**(iii) Induction Sealing (2.5 marks):**
- **Heat Source:** High-frequency electromagnetic field (typically 30–100 kHz) generated by an induction coil. The field induces **eddy currents** in an aluminum foil layer, which heats up and melts the adjacent polymer coating.
- **Suitable Polymers:** Requires a **metal foil layer** (aluminum) plus a heat-seal polymer coating (e.g., LDPE, Surlyn®).
- **Typical Applications:** **Tamper-evident bottle cap liners** for pharmaceuticals, dairy, sauces, vitamins, chemicals. The cap remains removable but the foil liner remains bonded to the bottle mouth — providing tamper evidence.

**Comparison Table Summary (extra credit):**

| Method | Heat Source | Sealing Location | Cycle Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| Impulse | Resistive wire | Both sides | Medium |
| Ultrasonic | Friction (HF vibration) | Interface only | Very fast |
| Induction | EM field → foil heating | Foil layer only | Very fast |

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the polymer chain interdiffusion mechanism of seal formation. Derive the relationship between seal strength and dwell time.**

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanism of Interdiffusion (3 marks):**

When two polymer films are brought into contact above their softening temperature, the following sequence occurs at the interface:

1. **Surface Wetting:** The molten/semi-molten polymer surfaces flow and wet each other, eliminating gaps.
2. **Chain End Mobilization:** The thermal energy exceeds the activation barrier for chain segment motion. Polymer chain ends and entanglements become mobile.
3. **Reptation (de Gennes, 1971):** Chain ends perform a snake-like "reptation" motion across the interface, gradually crossing from one side to the other.
4. **Entanglement Across Interface:** As chains cross the boundary, they form new entanglements with chains from the opposite side.
5. **Cooling and Lock-In:** When the system cools, the entangled chains are "frozen" in their interpenetrating state, forming a **cohesive weld**.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Step 1: Interdiffusion depth follows Fickian-like diffusion:

$$\xi(t) = \sqrt{D_p \cdot t_d}$$

Step 2: Peel force at failure is proportional to interdiffusion depth:

$$F_{peel} = \eta \cdot \xi(t) \cdot G_{interface}$$

Step 3: Substituting:

$$F_{peel} = \eta \cdot \sqrt{D_p \cdot t_d} \cdot G_{interface}$$

Step 4: Seal strength (force per unit width):

$$\sigma_{seal} = \frac{F_{peel}}{w_{seal}} = \frac{\eta \cdot G_{interface} \cdot \sqrt{D_p \cdot t_d}}{w_{seal}}$$

**Conclusion:** Seal strength scales with the **square root of dwell time**. Doubling dwell time increases seal strength by only **41.4%**, which is why industry prefers to increase **temperature** rather than dwell time for efficiency.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the defects encountered in heat sealing operations and the corrective actions for each. What is the role of Teflon® (PTFE) coating on sealing jaws?**

**Model Answer:**

**Common Defects and Corrective Actions (5 marks):**

| # | Defect | Likely Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Weak/Peeling Seal** | Low $T_s$, short $t_d$ | Increase $T_s$ by 5°C; increase $t_d$ by 0.2s |
| 2 | **Burn-Through (Pinholes)** | $T_s$ too high | Reduce $T_s$; check temperature uniformity |
| 3 | **Wrinkled/Cracked Seal** | Excessive $P_s$; film misaligned | Reduce $P_s$; check web tension |
| 4 | **Cloudy/Milky Seal** | Incomplete fusion; surface contamination | Clean jaws; raise $T_s$ by 5–10°C |
| 5 | **Sticking to Jaws** | Anti-stick coating worn | Replace PTFE coating; lower $T_s$ |
| 6 | **Non-uniform Seal Across Width** | Pressure distribution uneven | Calibrate pressure bar; replace worn pads |
| 7 | **Delamination in Laminate** | Wrong seal layer | Use LDPE or Surlyn® as inner seal layer |
| 8 | **Seal Asymmetry (Front vs. Back)** | One-sided heating | Check both jaw temperatures independently |

**Role of PTFE (Teflon®) Coating (2 marks):**

PTFE (Polytetrafluoroethylene) coating on the sealing jaws serves **three critical functions**:

1. **Anti-Stick Release:** PTFE has an extremely low surface energy (~18 mN/m), preventing molten polymer from adhering to the jaws.
2. **Thermal Insulation:** PTFE is a poor thermal conductor ($k \approx 0.25 \, W/mK$), reducing heat loss from the jaws to the supporting structure.
3. **Wear Resistance:** PTFE withstands millions of sealing cycles without significant degradation at temperatures up to ~260°C.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** PTFE coatings must be replaced periodically (typically every 6–12 months in production environments) as they wear off due to repeated thermal cycling.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Seal Strength with Hot Tack Strength** — Examiners specifically test this. Seal strength is measured at 23°C after cooling; hot tack is measured while still hot.
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Derivations** — Always state $N/15mm$ for seal strength and seconds for dwell time. Marks are deducted for missing units.
> 3. **Not Mentioning Polymer Interdiffusion** — A 7-mark question on seal formation that doesn't mention **reptation or chain interdiffusion** will lose at least 2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong Temperature for Polymer** — Writing that PE seals at 200°C loses marks; PE seals at ~120–140°C, PP at ~160–180°C, PET at ~180–220°C.
> 5. **Skipping the Heat Seal Curve** — If the question says "explain sealing", always include the **Heat Seal Curve** with the four regions. This is a guaranteed 3-4 marks.
> 6. **Mixing Up Induction and RF Sealing** — Induction uses eddy currents in foil (no electrodes contact the product); RF uses dipole heating of polar polymers (electrodes contact). They are NOT the same.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU 2024 PEPOT748 Module 3**

### Core Concepts to Memorize
- [x] **Three Pillars of Sealing:** Temperature ($T_s$), Pressure ($P_s$), Dwell Time ($t_d$)
- [x] **Definition of Seal:** Molecular-level interdiffusion of polymer chains across the interface
- [x] **Heat Seal Curve:** Four regions — No Seal, Rapid Rise, Plateau, Degradation
- [x] **Seal Strength Formula:** $\sigma_{seal} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot G_{interface} \cdot \sqrt{D_p \cdot t_d}}{w_{seal}}$
- [x] **Dwell Time Formula:** $t_{heat} \approx \dfrac{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot L^2}{2k}$

### Sealing Methods — Must-Know List
- [x] **Hot Bar / Impulse Sealing** — Most common for pouches
- [x] **Ultrasonic Sealing** — 20–40 kHz vibration; interface-only heating
- [x] **Induction Sealing** — Eddy currents in foil; tamper-evident caps
- [x] **RF Sealing** — 27.12 MHz; for PVC and polar polymers
- [x] **Cold Seal** — Pressure + latex adhesive; for chocolate
- [x] **Hot Wire Seal** — Cuts and seals simultaneously; T-shirt bags

### Critical Material Properties
- [x] **PE (Polyethylene):** Seals at **120–140°C**; Vicat ~85°C
- [x] **PP (Polypropylene):** Seals at **160–180°C**; Vicat ~110°C
- [x] **PET (Polyester):** Seals at **180–220°C**; Tg ~70°C
- [x] **PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride):** Seals at **140–160°C**; Tg ~80°C; suitable for RF
- [x] **Surlyn® (Ionomer):** Seals at **80–100°C**; used as inner seal layer in laminates

### Quality Metrics
- [x] **Minimum Seal Strength:** ≥ 15 N/15mm (food grade)
- [x] **Minimum Hot Tack:** ≥ 2 N/15mm (FFS grade)
- [x] **Standard Test Speed:** 300 mm/min (peel test)
- [x] **Standard Test Condition:** 23°C, 50% RH

### Common Defects — One-Line Memory Aid
- [x] **Weak Seal** → Too Cold
- [x] **Burn-Through** → Too Hot
- [x] **Wrinkled** → Too Much Pressure
- [x] **Sticking** → PTFE Worn Out

### Industry Applications
- [x] **Pharmaceuticals:** Induction seal for tamper evidence
- [x] **Food (Chocolate):** Cold seal to prevent heat damage
- [x] **Soup/Sauce Pouches:** High hot tack for FFS lines
- [x] **Medical Devices:** RF or Ultrasonic for validated hermetic seal
- [x] **Industrial Bags:** Hot wire seal for woven PP sacks
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plasma Barrier Coatings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Plasma Barrier Coatings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plasma Barrier Coatings** are ultra-thin (typically **10–100 nm**) inorganic or organo-silicon films deposited onto polymer packaging substrates under low-pressure or atmospheric plasma conditions, designed to drastically reduce the permeation of gases (O₂, CO₂, N₂), water vapour, aromas, and organic vapours without compromising substrate transparency, flexibility, or recyclability.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme PEPOT748 syllabus (Module 3), plasma barrier coatings represent the **third-generation high-barrier technology**, bridging the gap between conventional multi-layer extrusion laminates (1st gen) and transparent metallised films (2nd gen). The technique leverages ionized gases (**plasma**) to drive chemical vapour deposition (CVD) or polymerization reactions on a plastic web (PET, BOPP, PE, PA) to form a continuous, pinhole-free barrier layer.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Microscopic Glass Shield"

Imagine covering a sheet of cling-film (plastic wrap) with an invisibly thin layer of **liquid glass** — so thin that you can still see through it perfectly, fold it freely, and even recycle it as normal plastic. That is precisely what a plasma SiOₓ coating does.

Think of polymer films as having microscopic "highways" (free volume elements) between their tangled molecular chains. Small gas molecules like O₂ use these highways to sneak through the film. A plasma barrier coating acts like **sealing the tunnels of a porous sponge** — depositing a glassy, ceramic-like layer that physically blocks the gas molecules from travelling through.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlights (PEPOT748 / Module 3):**
> - Plasma as the **4th state of matter** (ionized gas)
> - **PECVD** (Plasma Enhanced Chemical Vapour Deposition) as the primary deposition route
> - Two dominant chemistries: **SiOₓ** (from HMDSO/ silane precursors) and **a-C:H / DLC** (from hydrocarbons)
> - Typical operating vacuum: **10⁻² to 10 Pa**; power: **0.1 – 10 W·cm⁻²**
> - Substrate temperatures kept **below 80 °C** to protect heat-sensitive plastics
> - OTR improvement: **from ~100 to <1 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹**

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Standard Value | Importance |
|---|---|---|
| Plasma electron temperature ($T_e$) | **10 000 – 50 000 K** | Dissociates precursor gases |
| Gas/ion temperature ($T_{ion}$) | **300 – 500 K** (non-equilibrium) | Keeps plastic web cool |
| Coating thickness ($d$) | **10 – 100 nm** | Sub-micron barrier film |
| Degree of ionization | **10⁻⁶ – 10⁻³** | Cold plasma regime |
| Process pressure ($p$) | **0.1 – 100 Pa** | Glow-discharge regime |
| Mean free path ($\lambda$) | **mm – cm range** | Dictates deposition uniformity |

> [!NOTE]
> A **non-equilibrium / non-thermal plasma** has hot electrons ($T_e \sim 10^4$ K) but cold heavy species ($T_{ion} \sim 300$ K) — this is precisely what enables plasma coating of plastics **without melting them**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Plasma — Why It Works

A plasma is a **quasi-neutral ionized gas** containing equal densities of free electrons, ions, and neutral radicals. When a high-frequency electric field (typically **13.56 MHz** RF or **2.45 GHz** microwave) is applied to a low-pressure gas, free electrons gain kinetic energy from the field. These energetic electrons undergo inelastic collisions with precursor molecules, breaking them into reactive fragments (**radicals** and **ions**).

#### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Reaction Logic in PECVD

1. **Electron impact ionization / dissociation:**  
   $e^- + \text{SiH}_4 \rightarrow \text{SiH}_3^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet} + e^-$  
   (Electrons tear the precursor molecule apart)
2. **Radical migration to substrate:** Reactive species diffuse toward the cooled plastic web.
3. **Surface adsorption:** Radicals stick to the polymer surface with high sticking coefficient.
4. **Surface reaction & film growth:** Adjacent radicals cross-link, forming an **Si–O–Si** network.
5. **By-product desorption:** H₂, CO₂, etc. leave the surface, leaving a dense SiOₓ film.

> [!TIP]
> The **“Why”**: We use plasma because thermally heating a precursor to 1000 °C (as in classical CVD) would destroy the polymer substrate. Plasma provides the energy *electronically* to the gas, not thermally to the film — this is the core engineering insight.

### 2.2 Types of Plasma Barrier Coatings

| Coating Type | Precursor System | Barrier Against | OTR (cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹) | WVTR (g·m⁻²·day⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SiOₓ** (silicon oxide) | HMDSO + O₂ / N₂O | O₂, aromas | **0.1 – 5** | 0.5 – 2 |
| **a-SiC:H / a-SiN:H** | SiH₄ + CH₄ / NH₃ | O₂, H₂O | 1 – 10 | 1 – 5 |
| **a-C:H (DLC)** | C₂H₂ / CH₄ | O₂, hydrocarbons | 5 – 20 | 2 – 10 |
| **SiOₓCₓHᵧ (organic-like)** | HMDSO alone | O₂ (moderate) | 10 – 50 | 3 – 10 |
| **Al₂O₃ (ALD-like plasma)** | TMA + O₂ plasma | H₂O | 5 – 20 | **< 0.1** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HMDSO** (Hexamethyldisiloxane, [(CH₃)₃Si]₂O) is the industrial workhorse precursor — non-toxic, cheap, easily vaporized, and yields SiOₓ films with excellent barrier.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> Below are the **must-know formulas** for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 derivations. Use `\vert` notation for absolute values to keep markdown tables safe.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{1}{d}\left(Q \cdot \dfrac{\Delta p}{A}\right)$ | Basic permeability (Henry–Law form) | mol·m·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\text{OTR} = \dfrac{D \cdot S \cdot \Delta p}{L}$ | Oxygen Transmission Rate (Fick + Henry) | cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi d^2 p}$ | Mean free path (kinetic theory) | m |
| 4 | $D = D_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Diffusivity (Arrhenius form) | m²·s⁻¹ |
| 5 | $v_d = \mu_n \, \varepsilon$ | Electron drift velocity in plasma | m·s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $n_e \tau \geq 10^{14} \, \text{m}^{-3}\text{s}$ | Lawson criterion for steady-state plasma | m⁻³·s |
| 7 | $k_B T_e = 2 \, \text{eV} \approx 23\,200 \,\text{K}$ | Typical cold-plasma electron energy | K |
| 8 | $\eta_{dep} = \dfrac{m_{\text{film}}}{m_{\text{precursor}}}$ | Deposition efficiency (mass) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{BIF}_{O_2} = \dfrac{\text{OTR}_{\text{substrate}}}{\text{OTR}_{\text{coated}}}$ | Barrier Improvement Factor | dimensionless |
| 10 | $L_p = \dfrac{\text{OTR}}{\Delta p}$ | Permeation coefficient | cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹ |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Relevance

- **Food packaging**: PET bottles with internal SiOₓ coating replace glass for beer, wine, and baby food — extending shelf life by 6–12 months.
- **Flexible OLED & flexible electronics**: Ultra-high barrier films (<10⁻⁶ g·m⁻²·day⁻¹) for organic photovoltaics and displays are produced by multilayered SiOₓ/polymer stacks.
- **Pharmaceutical blister packs**: Al₂O₃-plasma coated films protect moisture-sensitive drugs (e.g., effervescents).
- **Recyclability advantage**: Plasma-coated mono-material plastics (e.g., single PET with SiOₓ) comply with **EU PPWR 2025** single-material recyclability mandates — unlike multi-layer Al/PE laminates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Reduction in Permeation Through a Plasma Barrier Coating

Consider a polymer film of thickness $L$ on which a SiOₓ layer of thickness $\delta$ is deposited. The total permeation through the bilayer is governed by the **resistance-in-series** model.

**Step 1 — Define single-layer permeability:**

For each layer $i$, Fick's first law under steady-state gives:

$$
J_i = \dfrac{D_i \, S_i \, \Delta p}{L_i}
$$

where $D_i$ is the diffusion coefficient (m²·s⁻¹), $S_i$ is the solubility coefficient (mol·m⁻³·Pa⁻¹), and $L_i$ is layer thickness.

**Step 2 — Express flux in terms of permeability:**

Define permeability $P_i = D_i \, S_i$. Then:

$$
J_i = \dfrac{P_i \, \Delta p_i}{L_i}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply steady-state continuity (no accumulation):**

At equilibrium, the flux through each layer must be identical:

$$
J = J_{\text{polymer}} = J_{\text{coating}}
$$

**Step 4 — Pressure drop distribution:**

The total pressure drop across the bilayer is $\Delta p = \Delta p_{\text{polymer}} + \Delta p_{\text{coating}}$. Express each:

$$
\Delta p_{\text{polymer}} = \dfrac{J \, L}{P_{\text{polymer}}}, \quad \Delta p_{\text{coating}} = \dfrac{J \, \delta}{P_{\text{coating}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum the pressure drops:**

$$
\Delta p = J\left(\dfrac{L}{P_{\text{polymer}}} + \dfrac{\delta}{P_{\text{coating}}}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the steady-state flux:**

$$
J = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\dfrac{L}{P_{\text{polymer}}} + \dfrac{\delta}{P_{\text{coating}}}}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the equivalent permeability of the bilayer:**

$$
P_{\text{eq}} = \dfrac{J \, (L+\delta)}{\Delta p} = \dfrac{L + \delta}{\dfrac{L}{P_{\text{polymer}}} + \dfrac{\delta}{P_{\text{coating}}}}
$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate the Barrier Improvement Factor (BIF):**

$$
\text{BIF} = \dfrac{\text{OTR}_{\text{substrate}}}{\text{OTR}_{\text{coated}}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{polymer}}}{P_{\text{eq}}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{BIF} = \dfrac{P_{\text{polymer}}\left(\dfrac{L}{P_{\text{polymer}}} + \dfrac{\delta}{P_{\text{coating}}}\right)}{L + \delta} = \dfrac{L + \delta \, \dfrac{P_{\text{polymer}}}{P_{\text{coating}}}}{L + \delta}
$$

**Step 9 — Limiting case analysis (very thin coating, very low permeability):**

Because SiOₓ has $P_{\text{coating}} \ll P_{\text{polymer}}$ (often by 3–4 orders of magnitude), the term $\delta \cdot P_{\text{polymer}}/P_{\text{coating}}$ dominates even when $\delta \ll L$. Thus:

$$
\text{BIF} \approx \dfrac{\delta}{L + \delta} \cdot \dfrac{P_{\text{polymer}}}{P_{\text{coating}}} \;\;\xrightarrow{\delta \ll L}\;\; \dfrac{\delta}{L} \cdot \dfrac{P_{\text{polymer}}}{P_{\text{coating}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even a **30 nm** SiOₓ layer on a **12 µm PET** can reduce OTR by **100×** because the *ratio* $P_{\text{polymer}}/P_{\text{coating}} \sim 10^4$ dominates. This is the *theoretical foundation* of why plasma barrier coatings are so efficient.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why Plasma Wins Over Other Coating Methods

| Method | Substrate Temp | Coating Quality | Roll-to-Roll Speed | Cost | Recyclability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Plasma PECVD** | **< 80 °C** ★ | Dense, pinhole-free | 100–600 m/min | Medium | ★★★★★ |
| Sputter (PVD metallization) | < 100 °C | Excellent (Al) | 300–800 m/min | Medium | ★ (metal layer) |
| Evaporation (Al/PET) | < 80 °C | Opaque | 600 m/min | Low | ★ |
| Wet sol-gel coating | 100–200 °C | Pinhole-prone | 50–200 m/min | Low | ★★★★ |
| ALD (thermal) | 80–150 °C | Best quality | < 1 m/min (batch) | Very high | ★★★★★ |

> [!TIP]
> PECVD uniquely combines **low temperature**, **high quality**, **reasonable web speed**, and **compatibility with polymer recycling** — that combination is what makes it the dominant barrier-coating route for next-generation plastic packaging.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 12 µm PET film has OTR = 120 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹ (uncoated). A 30 nm SiOₓ layer reduces its OTR to 1.2 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹. Calculate the Barrier Improvement Factor and the effective permeability ratio $P_{\text{PET}}/P_{\text{SiO}_x}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute BIF:

$$
\text{BIF} = \dfrac{120}{1.2} = 100
$$

**Step 2:** Use the limiting expression from §3.1 Step 9:

$$
\text{BIF} \approx \dfrac{\delta}{L} \cdot \dfrac{P_{\text{PET}}}{P_{\text{SiO}_x}}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the permeability ratio:

$$
\dfrac{P_{\text{PET}}}{P_{\text{SiO}_x}} = \text{BIF} \cdot \dfrac{L}{\delta} = 100 \times \dfrac{12 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}}{30 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}} = 100 \times 400 = 40\,000
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation:

$$
P_{\text{PET}} \approx 4 \times 10^{4} \cdot P_{\text{SiO}_x}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This matches published literature: PET permeability ≈ 6 × 10⁻¹⁷ mol·m·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ and SiOₓ ≈ 1.5 × 10⁻²¹ mol·m·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹, ratio ≈ **4 × 10⁴**. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Roll-to-Roll PECVD Plasma Coating Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Unwind Station PET Web 12um] --> B[Treatment Station Corona Pre-clean]
    B --> C[Vacuum Lock Inlet]
    C --> D1[Process Chamber 1 HMDSO + O2 Glow Discharge]
    D1 --> D2[Process Chamber 2 PECVD SiOx Deposition]
    D2 --> D3[Process Chamber 3 Optional Plasma Polymer Interlayer]
    D3 --> E[Vacuum Lock Outlet]
    E --> F[In-line Optical Monitoring OT and WVTR]
    F --> G[In-line Spectroscopic Ellipsometer]
    G --> H[Rewind Station]
    H --> I[Slitting and Packaging]

    subgraph PlasmaSource[Plasma Generation Subsystem]
        P1[13.56 MHz RF Generator] --> P2[Impedance Matching Network]
        P2 --> P3[Roller Electrode or Planar Electrode]
        P3 --> P4[Glow Discharge Region 1 to 100 Pa]
    end

    subgraph PrecursorFeed[Precursor Delivery Subsystem]
        Q1[HMDSO Liquid Reservoir] --> Q2[Vaporizer 60 to 80 C]
        Q2 --> Q3[Mass Flow Controller]
        Q3 --> Q4[Carrier Gas N2 or Ar]
        Q4 --> P4
    end

    subgraph VacuumSystem[Vacuum and Pumping]
        V1[Roots Blower] --> V2[Rotary Vane Pump]
        V2 --> C
        V2 --> E
    end

    style PlasmaSource fill:#E8F0FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style PrecursorFeed fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600,color:#000000
    style VacuumSystem fill:#F0F8E8,stroke:#336600,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Process Architecture Flow — SiOₓ Film Formation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step A Electron Impact Dissociation HMDSO to Si CH3 radicals] --> S2[Step B Radical Diffusion to Cooled Web]
    S2 --> S3[Step C Surface Adsorption and Migration]
    S3 --> S4[Step D Cross Linking Si O Si Network Formation]
    S4 --> S5[Step E By product Desorption H2 CO2 H2O]
    S5 --> S6[Step F Continuous Dense SiOx Film 10 to 100 nm]
    S6 --> S7[Quality Gate OT less than 1 WVTR less than 2]

    style S1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000000
    style S4 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000000
    style S6 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    style S7 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Layer Architecture — Plasma-Coated Packaging Film (Cross Section)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Outer Protective Lacquer 1 to 2 um] --> L2[Plasma SiOx Barrier Layer 10 to 100 nm]
    L2 --> L3[PET or BOPP Substrate 12 to 50 um]
    L3 --> L4[Optional Adhesion Promoter Plasma Polymer 5 to 30 nm]
    L4 --> L5[Heat Sealable PE or CPP Layer 30 to 80 um]
    L5 --> L6[Food Contact Layer]

    style L2 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style L3 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#003333,color:#000000
    style L5 fill:#FFE66D,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000000
```

> [!TIP]
> In the above, the **SiOₓ layer** is the actual barrier — note its **10–100 nm** thickness is **1000× thinner than the PET substrate**, yet it provides > 99% of the gas-blocking function. The surrounding polymer layers provide mechanical robustness and sealing properties.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
Define plasma barrier coating. List any **two** commonly used precursors and the corresponding coating chemistries.

> **Model Answer:**  
> A plasma barrier coating is an ultra-thin (10–100 nm) inorganic or hybrid film deposited on a polymer substrate using ionized-gas (plasma) driven chemical vapour deposition, to reduce permeability of gases and vapours.  
> (i) **HMDSO + O₂** → amorphous **SiOₓ** coating.  
> (ii) **Acetylene (C₂H₂) or methane (CH₄)** → **a-C:H / DLC** coating.  
> *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 each for precursor + chemistry pair.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
State **two** advantages of plasma SiO₄ₓ coating over conventional metallized aluminium coating for food packaging.

> **Model Answer:**  
> (i) **Transparent & microwaveable** — preserves product visibility and allows microwave heating.  
> (ii) **Recyclable as mono-material** — SiOₓ layer detaches or disintegrates in conventional PET recycling, unlike aluminium which contaminates the recycle stream.  
> *(3 marks — 1.5 each.)*

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake**: Students write "plasma = high temperature". Plasma is *cold* — only electrons are hot. Mentioning **non-equilibrium / non-thermal plasma** is mandatory for full marks.
> - **Marks lost**: Forgetting to give units in OTR / WVTR. Always state **cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹** and **g·m⁻²·day⁻¹**.
> - **Marks lost**: Confusing PECVD with PVD. PECVD is *chemical* (precursor reacts), PVD is *physical* (atom transfer).
> - **Valuation key**: Examiners reward **specific precursor names** (HMDSO, silane) over generic "silicon gas".

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Plasma Generation & Deposition Mechanism

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**  
(a) **[7 marks]** Explain the step-by-step mechanism of deposition of SiOₓ barrier coating on a PET film by **PECVD** using HMDSO as precursor. Include a labelled schematic of the process in your answer.

(b) **[7 marks]** Derive the expression for the **Barrier Improvement Factor (BIF)** for a bilayer film (polymer + plasma coating). Discuss the role of the permeability ratio $P_{\text{polymer}}/P_{\text{coating}}$ in determining the barrier performance.

**Model Solution (a):**

The PECVD process for SiOₓ deposition proceeds through the following sequence:

1. **Plasma generation:** A 13.56 MHz RF field is applied between two parallel electrodes in a low-pressure (1–100 Pa) gas mixture of **HMDSO vapour + O₂ + carrier gas (Ar/N₂)**. Free electrons gain kinetic energy from the RF field.

2. **Electron-impact dissociation:** High-energy electrons collide with HMDSO and O₂ molecules, generating reactive radicals:

$$
e^- + [(CH_3)_3Si]_2O \rightarrow (CH_3)_xSiO_y^{\bullet} + \text{by-products}
$$
$$
e^- + O_2 \rightarrow 2\,O^{\bullet}
$$

3. **Radical transport:** The radicals diffuse through the gas phase toward the cooled (20–80 °C) PET web, which acts as the substrate electrode.

4. **Surface adsorption & reaction:** Radicals adsorb on the PET surface and undergo surface reactions to form an **Si–O–Si** network:

$$
\equiv Si-O^{\bullet} + \,^{\bullet}O-Si \equiv \;\rightarrow\; \equiv Si-O-Si \equiv \;\;(\text{network growth})
$$

5. **By-product removal:** Volatile by-products (H₂O, CO₂, CH₄) are pumped away.

6. **Film growth:** Continuous film grows at **5–50 nm/min**, reaching final thickness **10–100 nm**.

**Labelled schematic** (also shown in §4.1): RF generator → matching network → electrodes → plasma glow region with HMDSO + O₂ inlet → PET web passing through → SiOₓ film formed on web → vacuum pump outlet.

*(Marks distribution: 2 — plasma generation; 2 — dissociation reactions; 2 — surface reactions; 1 — schematic.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

The BIF derivation is as follows (full steps from §3.1):

**Step 1:** Under steady-state, flux $J$ through each layer is equal:

$$
J = \dfrac{P_{\text{polymer}} \, \Delta p_{\text{polymer}}}{L} = \dfrac{P_{\text{coating}} \, \Delta p_{\text{coating}}}{\delta}
$$

**Step 2:** Total pressure drop is $\Delta p = \Delta p_{\text{polymer}} + \Delta p_{\text{coating}}$.

**Step 3:** Substituting pressure drops yields:

$$
J = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\dfrac{L}{P_{\text{polymer}}} + \dfrac{\delta}{P_{\text{coating}}}}
$$

**Step 4:** Defining BIF:

$$
\text{BIF} = \dfrac{P_{\text{polymer}}}{P_{\text{eq}}} = \dfrac{L + \delta \, (P_{\text{polymer}}/P_{\text{coating}})}{L + \delta}
$$

**Step 5:** For $\delta \ll L$ and $P_{\text{coating}} \ll P_{\text{polymer}}$:

$$
\text{BIF} \approx \dfrac{\delta}{L} \cdot \dfrac{P_{\text{polymer}}}{P_{\text{coating}}}
$$

**Discussion:**  
- The **permeability ratio** $P_{\text{polymer}}/P_{\text{coating}}$ is the dominant factor — typically **10³ – 10⁵**.  
- Even though $\delta/L \sim 10^{-3}$, the **BIF reaches 10–100** because of the huge permeability ratio.  
- **Implication**: Doubling the coating thickness roughly doubles the BIF (linear regime), while improving coating density (lower $P_{\text{coating}}$) is far more effective than thickness increase.

*(Marks distribution: 2 — Step 1–3; 2 — Step 4–5; 2 — Discussion; 1 — Conclusion.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Industrial Plasma Equipment & Applications

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**  
(a) **[7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **industrial roll-to-roll PECVD system** used for plasma barrier coating of plastic packaging films. Identify the role of each subsystem.

(b) **[7 marks]** Compare **SiOₓ, a-C:H (DLC)**, and **Al₂O₃** plasma coatings with respect to: (i) precursor chemistry, (ii) oxygen barrier, (iii) moisture barrier, and (iv) recyclability. Give **two specific applications** of plasma barrier coatings in the food packaging industry.

**Model Solution (a):**

The industrial roll-to-roll PECVD system (shown in §4.1) consists of the following subsystems:

1. **Web handling subsystem** (unwind, tension control, rewinder, slitter): Transports the polymer web continuously at 100–600 m/min.

2. **Pre-treatment subsystem** (corona or atmospheric plasma): Cleans and activates the polymer surface to improve adhesion of the barrier coating.

3. **Vacuum system** (lock chambers, roots blower, rotary vane pump): Maintains **1–100 Pa** in process chambers; isolates atmospheric sections.

4. **Process chambers (1 to 3 in series):** Each contains a pair of electrodes where the **13.56 MHz RF glow discharge** is sustained. The polymer web passes through or adjacent to the glow region.

5. **Precursor delivery system** (HMDSO reservoir, vaporizer, mass flow controller, carrier gas): Delivers a controlled mixture of HMDSO + O₂ + N₂/Ar into the chamber.

6. **Plasma generation subsystem** (RF generator, impedance matching network, electrodes): Sustains the glow discharge at 0.1–10 W·cm⁻² power density.

7. **In-line monitoring** (optical transmission, ellipsometer, residual gas analyser): Real-time quality control of coating thickness, composition, and OTR/WVTR.

*(Marks distribution: 1 — block diagram; 2 — subsystems listed; 2 — roles explained; 2 — process parameters.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

| Property | SiOₓ | a-C:H (DLC) | Al₂O₃ |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Precursor | HMDSO + O₂ | CH₄ / C₂H₂ | TMA + O₂ plasma |
| (ii) OTR (cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹) | 0.1 – 5 ★ | 5 – 20 | 5 – 20 |
| (iii) WVTR (g·m⁻²·day⁻¹) | 0.5 – 2 | 2 – 10 | **< 0.1** ★ |
| (iv) Recyclability | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |

**Specific food packaging applications:**

1. **SiO₄ₓ-coated PET bottles for beer, wine, and baby food** — replaces glass; extends shelf life 6–12 months; transparent & microwaveable.

2. **Al₂O₃ plasma-coated BOPP films for snack and dried-food packaging** — ultra-low WVTR protects moisture-sensitive products; replaces aluminium foil laminates for transparent pouches.

*(Marks distribution: 2 — comparison table; 2 — OTR/WVTR analysis; 2 — applications; 1 — recyclability discussion.)*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Plasma = 4th state of matter**; cold / non-equilibrium plasma is used (hot electrons, cold gas).
- ✅ **PECVD** = Plasma Enhanced Chemical Vapour Deposition — the dominant industrial technique.
- ✅ **Standard RF frequency**: **13.56 MHz** (ISM band, internationally permitted).
- ✅ **HMDSO + O₂** is the workhorse precursor → gives **SiOₓ** coating.
- ✅ **Substrate temperature**: kept **< 80 °C** to protect polymer films.
- ✅ **Coating thickness**: typically **10–100 nm**.
- ✅ **OTR improvement**: 100× to 1000× over uncoated polymer.
- ✅ **Recyclability**: Plasma-SiOₓ films are **mono-material recyclable** — major EU PPWR 2025 advantage.
- ✅ **BIF** = OTR_substrate / OTR_coated. Driven by the ratio $P_{\text{polymer}}/P_{\text{coating}}$, not by coating thickness alone.
- ✅ **DLC** = Diamond-Like Carbon (a-C:H) coating — used where flexibility is paramount.
- ✅ **Al₂O₃ plasma coatings** = best moisture barrier (WVTR < 0.1 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹).
- ⚠️ **Don't confuse**: PECVD (chemical) ≠ PVD/sputtering (physical).
- ⚠️ **Always state units**: OTR in cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹; WVTR in g·m⁻²·day⁻¹; thickness in nm or µm.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Requirement for Conversion
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Energy Requirement for Conversion

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:** Energy Requirement for Conversion in the plastics packaging industry refers to the total quantitative input of energy—encompassing thermal, mechanical, and electrical forms—necessary to transform raw polymeric materials (such as pellets, granules, or flakes) into finished, usable plastic products (films, sheets, containers, molded parts) through various thermo-mechanical processing techniques like extrusion, injection molding, and blow molding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine preparing a hot meal from frozen ingredients. First, you must thaw and heat the ingredients to a cookable state (this is the **sensible and latent heat** required to melt the plastic). Next, you need to stir and mix the ingredients, which requires physical effort and generates its own heat from friction (this is the **mechanical/kinetic energy** from the extruder screw or injection plunger). Finally, the cooked food must be cooled down to be eaten (this is the **cooling energy** removed by chilled water or air). In a real factory, the stove and the mixing bowl are often poorly insulated, meaning a massive amount of energy is wasted to the surroundings (**thermal losses**), making actual energy consumption significantly higher than the theoretical minimum.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
### Sources of Energy Requirement
To fully grasp the energy footprint of plastics conversion, the requirement must be broken down into structured, logical components:
1. **Thermodynamic Energy (Heating & Melting):** The largest fraction of energy goes into raising the temperature of the polymer from ambient to its melt processing temperature. This involves *sensible heat* (temperature change) and *latent heat of fusion* (phase change in semi-crystalline polymers).
2. **Mechanical Energy (Pressure & Flow):** The motor and drive system provide rotational energy to the screw to convey, compress, and shear the polymer melt. This is required to overcome the high viscosity of the melt and force it through dies or into molds.
3. **Electrical Energy (Utility Input):** The primary energy purchased by the factory. It powers the heater bands, the hydraulic pumps, and the electric motors. It is the baseline metric for cost calculation.
4. **Cooling Energy (Solidification):** Energy that must be actively removed from the polymer to solidify it into the final shape. This is typically extracted by chilled water or forced air, placing a heavy load on the plant's HVAC or chiller systems.
5. **Energy Losses:** Unavoidable waste heat lost to the environment through radiation, convection, conduction through machine surfaces, and frictional losses in gears/bearings.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet
The following table summarizes the governing equations required for solving numerical problems on this topic. Students must memorize the unit conversions and the specific conditions for applying the latent heat formula.

| Term | Symbol | Governing Equation | Unit | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sensible Heat | $Q_s$ | $m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{melt} - T_{amb})$ | kJ | Energy to raise polymer temperature to the melt state. |
| Latent Heat of Fusion | $Q_l$ | $m \cdot \Delta H_f$ | kJ | Energy to break crystalline lattices (only for semi-crystalline polymers). |
| Total Theoretical Energy | $E_{theo}$ | $Q_s + Q_l$ | kJ or MJ | Minimum thermodynamic energy required for conversion. |
| Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC$ | $E_{total} / m_{product}$ | kWh/kg | Benchmark metric for process energy efficiency. |
| Process Thermal Efficiency | $\eta$ | $(E_{theo} / E_{actual}) \times 100$ | % | Ratio of useful energy to total energy input. |
| Viscous Heat Generation | $Q_{shear}$ | $\eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}^2 \cdot V$ | W | Heat generated internally by polymer melt friction. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Calculating the energy requirement is vital for ISO 50001 (Energy Management) compliance, determining the carbon footprint of plastic packaging, and optimizing the cost-per-part in high-volume manufacturing. A lower Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) directly translates to lower operational costs and a more sustainable product lifecycle.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
### Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix
*Problem Statement:* A packaging manufacturer is producing a 100 kg batch of Polypropylene (PP) sheets. The raw material enters the extruder hopper at 25°C and must be processed at a melt temperature of 230°C. The specific heat capacity ($C_p$) of PP is 2.1 kJ/(kg·°C), and the latent heat of fusion ($\Delta H_f$) is 165 kJ/kg. The total electrical energy drawn by the extruder motor and heaters for this specific batch is 250 MJ. Determine the theoretical energy required to melt the polymer and the overall process efficiency.

**Step 1: Identify the Given Parameters and Convert Units**
We must ensure all units are consistent before starting calculations. Mass is in kg, temperatures are in °C, and specific heat is in kJ/(kg·°C). The actual energy is in MJ, so we will convert the theoretical result to MJ at the end.
- Mass of polymer, $m = 100$ kg
- Ambient temperature, $T_{amb} = 25^\circ$C
- Melt processing temperature, $T_{melt} = 230^\circ$C
- Specific heat capacity, $C_p = 2.1$ kJ/(kg·°C)
- Latent heat of fusion, $\Delta H_f = 165$ kJ/kg
- Actual energy consumed, $E_{actual} = 250$ MJ

**Step 2: Calculate Sensible Heat ($Q_s$)**
Sensible heat is the energy required to raise the temperature of the solid polymer to its melting point. We use the standard thermodynamic equation $Q = mc\Delta T$.

$$ Q_s = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{melt} - T_{amb}) $$

Substituting the given values into the equation:

$$ Q_s = 100 \text{ kg} \times 2.1 \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}\cdot^\circ\text{C}} \times (230 - 25)^\circ\text{C} $$

$$ Q_s = 210 \times 205 $$

$$ Q_s = 43050 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 3: Calculate Latent Heat of Fusion ($Q_l$)**
Polypropylene is a semi-crystalline polymer, meaning it possesses an ordered molecular structure that must be broken down during melting. This requires a specific input of energy (enthalpy of fusion).

$$ Q_l = m \cdot \Delta H_f $$

Substituting the given values into the equation:

$$ Q_l = 100 \text{ kg} \times 165 \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}} $$

$$ Q_l = 16500 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 4: Calculate Total Theoretical Energy ($E_{theo}$)**
The total theoretical energy is the sum of the sensible and latent heat requirements.

$$ E_{theo} = Q_s + Q_l $$

$$ E_{theo} = 43050 \text{ kJ} + 16500 \text{ kJ} $$

$$ E_{theo} = 59550 \text{ kJ} $$

To align with the actual energy units provided (MJ), we divide by 1000:

$$ E_{theo} = 59.55 \text{ MJ} $$

**Step 5: Calculate the Process Efficiency ($\eta$)**
Efficiency is defined as the ratio of useful theoretical energy to the total actual energy consumed by the system, expressed as a percentage.

$$ \eta = \left( \frac{E_{theo}}{E_{actual}} \right) \times 100 $$

$$ \eta = \left( \frac{59.55 \text{ MJ}}{250 \text{ MJ}} \right) \times 100 $$

$$ \eta = 23.82\% $$

**Conclusion:** The minimum theoretical energy required to melt and process the polymer is 59.55 MJ. However, the machine actually consumes 250 MJ. This implies that roughly 76% of the input electrical energy is lost to the environment, machine inefficiencies, or the cooling systems, yielding a thermal efficiency of 23.82%.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
### Structural Block Diagram: Energy Flow in a Plastics Conversion Machine
The following block-level functional architecture flow maps the interactions of energy inputs, conversion zones, and energy losses in a typical extrusion or injection molding process.

```mermaid
graph LR
    rawMat[Raw Polymer Pellets] --> hopper[Feed Hopper]
    hopper --> barrel[Heated Barrel]
    heaterInput[Electrical Energy for Heaters] -. Thermal Energy .-> barrel
    motorInput[Electrical Energy for Motor] -. Mechanical Energy .-> screw[Rotating Screw]
    screw --> barrel
    barrel --> dieMold[Extrusion Die or Mold Cavity]
    dieMold --> coolingSystem[Cooling and Solidification Unit]
    coolingSystem --> product[Finished Plastic Product]
    barrel -. Convective and Radiative Loss .-> environment[Factory Environment]
    motorInput -. Frictional and Electrical Loss .-> environment
    coolingSystem -. Waste Heat to Chiller .-> environment
```
*Figure 1: Block diagram illustrating the transformation of electrical energy into thermal and mechanical energy, highlighting the primary energy losses in a standard plastics conversion setup.*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
### KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

#### Part A Questions (3 Marks)

**1. Define Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) in plastics processing.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
*Model Answer:* Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) is the standard industrial metric used to measure the energy efficiency of a plastics conversion process. It is defined as the total electrical or thermal energy consumed by the processing machinery (extruder, injection molder, etc.) divided by the total mass of the plastic product manufactured. It is typically expressed in kilowatt-hours per kilogram (kWh/kg) or megajoules per kilogram (MJ/kg). Lower SEC values indicate a highly energy-efficient process and a smaller carbon footprint.

**2. Differentiate between sensible heat and latent heat of fusion in the context of polymer melting.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
*Model Answer:* **Sensible heat** is the thermal energy required to raise the temperature of a polymer from its ambient state to its melting point without causing a phase change. It is calculated using the mass, specific heat capacity ($C_p$), and the temperature difference. **Latent heat of fusion**, on the other hand, is the thermal energy absorbed by a semi-crystalline polymer to break its crystalline lattice structure and transition from a solid to a molten liquid state at a constant temperature. It is calculated using the mass and the enthalpy of fusion ($\Delta H_f$). Amorphous polymers (like PS, ABS) do not have a true latent heat of fusion, whereas semi-crystalline polymers (like PE, PP, PET) possess a significant $\Delta H_f$.

#### Part B Questions (14 Marks)

**Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
(a) Explain the major sources of energy consumption in a plastic extrusion process. (7 Marks)
(b) A 500 kg batch of Polyethylene (PE) is processed in an extruder. The entry temperature is 30°C, and the melt processing temperature is 180°C. Given $C_p$ = 2.3 kJ/(kg·°C) and $\Delta H_f$ = 140 kJ/kg. Calculate the total theoretical energy required to melt the polymer. If the extruder draws 900 kWh of power, what is the percentage efficiency of the heating system? (7 Marks)

*Model Solution for Question A:*
(a) The major sources of energy consumption in plastic extrusion include:
1. **Barrel Heaters:** Electrical band heaters provide the primary thermal energy to melt the polymer and maintain the processing temperature.
2. **Screw Drive Motor:** The electric motor provides mechanical energy to rotate the screw, which conveys, compress, and shears the polymer melt.
3. **Shear Heat Generation:** The friction from the highly viscous polymer melt rotating against the barrel walls generates internal viscous heat (viscous dissipation), which contributes to the melting process.
4. **Cooling System:** Chilled water or air removes the excess heat to solidify the extrudate, requiring significant energy to operate the plant's chillers.
5. **Conductive and Convective Losses:** Heat is constantly lost to the factory environment through the machine's uninsulated surfaces, reducing the overall thermal efficiency.

(b) **Step 1: Identify Given Data and Convert Units**
- $m = 500$ kg
- $T_{amb} = 30^\circ$C, $T_{melt} = 180^\circ$C
- $C_p = 2.3$ kJ/(kg·°C)
- $\Delta H_f = 140$ kJ/kg
- $E_{actual} = 900$ kWh

**Step 2: Calculate Sensible Heat ($Q_s$)**
$$ Q_s = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T $$
$$ Q_s = 500 \times 2.3 \times (180 - 30) $$
$$ Q_s = 1150 \times 150 = 172500 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 3: Calculate Latent Heat ($Q_l$)**
$$ Q_l = m \cdot \Delta H_f $$
$$ Q_l = 500 \times 140 = 70000 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 4: Total Theoretical Energy ($E_{theo}$ in kJ)**
$$ E_{theo} = 172500 + 70000 = 242500 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 5: Convert Actual Energy to kJ for Unit Consistency**
$$ E_{actual} = 900 \text{ kWh} \times 3600 \text{ kJ/kWh} = 3240000 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 6: Calculate Efficiency ($\eta$)**
$$ \eta = \left( \frac{242500}{3240000} \right) \times 100 = 7.48\% $$

**Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
(a) What is Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)? List the typical SEC ranges (in kWh/kg) for Injection Molding, Extrusion, and Blow Molding. (7 Marks)
(b) With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the flow of energy in a typical plastics conversion machine, identifying the major inputs and losses. (7 Marks)

*Model Solution for Question B:*
(a) **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** is the total electrical or thermal energy required to produce one kilogram of a finished plastic product. It is calculated using the formula:
$$ SEC = \frac{\text{Total Energy Input (kWh)}}{\text{Total Mass of Product (kg)}} $$
Typical SEC ranges for common plastics packaging conversion processes are tabulated below:

| Conversion Process | Typical SEC Range (kWh/kg) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Injection Molding | 0.4 to 0.8 |
| Extrusion (Films/Sheets) | 0.3 to 0.5 |
| Blow Molding (Bottles/Containers) | 0.5 to 0.7 |

(b) The flow of energy in a typical plastics conversion machine (such as an injection molding machine or extruder) involves three main stages: Input, Conversion, and Output.
- **Energy Inputs:** The primary input is electrical energy from the grid. This is split into two distinct branches: power to the **heater bands** (thermal energy) and power to the **hydraulic pump/motor** (mechanical energy).
- **Energy Conversion:** The heater bands convert electrical energy to thermal energy, melting the polymer. The motor converts electrical energy to mechanical work, driving the screw to convey and inject the melt.
- **Energy Outputs/Losses:** The useful output is the enthalpy stored in the melted plastic and the kinetic energy of injection. The vast majority of the input is lost as **conduction/radiation heat** from the barrel surfaces, **waste heat** carried away by the cooling water to the chiller, and **friction** in the motor gears and bearings. *(Refer to the block diagram in Section 4 for the exact schematic representation).*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> - **Unit Inconsistency:** A very common error is failing to convert the actual electrical energy into the same units as the theoretical thermodynamic energy. Electrical energy is often given in kWh, while theoretical energy is calculated in kJ. Students must remember the conversion factor: **1 kWh = 3600 kJ = 3.6 MJ**. Mixing these up will result in an efficiency of less than 1% or more than 100%, which is physically impossible.
> - **Forgetting Latent Heat:** Amorphous polymers (like PS, PVC, ABS) do not have a distinct melting point or significant latent heat of fusion, so $Q_l = 0$. However, for semi-crystalline polymers (PE, PP, PET, PA), $\Delta H_f$ must be added. Failing to do this yields an incorrect theoretical energy and a severely underestimated efficiency.
> - **Confusing Actual vs. Theoretical:** Do not subtract theoretical energy from actual energy to get efficiency. Efficiency is always a ratio (Theoretical / Actual) expressed as a percentage.

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember
- **Energy Requirement** is the sum of Thermodynamic (Heating/Melting), Mechanical (Screw/Motor), and Cooling energy requirements.
- **Sensible Heat ($Q_s$):** Calculated as $m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$. This is always required for all polymers regardless of their crystalline nature.
- **Latent Heat ($Q_l$):** Calculated as $m \cdot \Delta H_f$. This is required ONLY for semi-crystalline polymers.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** Measured in kWh/kg. Lower SEC indicates a more efficient and sustainable process.
- **Efficiency ($\eta$):** Always calculated as (Theoretical Energy / Actual Energy) $\times$ 100. 
- **Unit Conversion:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3600 \text{ kJ} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$.
- **Major Energy Losses:** Heat loss to the factory environment, cooling water waste heat, and frictional losses in motors and gearboxes.
- **Eco-friendly Goal:** In alignment with NEP 2020 and modern manufacturing, the primary focus is the reduction of SEC to lower the carbon footprint and adhere to sustainable packaging practices.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extrusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Extrusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Extrusion** is a continuous, high-volume thermo-mechanical polymer processing operation in which a thermoplastic material (in the form of pellets, granules, or powder) is **plasticated, melted, homogenized, and continuously forced under high pressure through a shaped die** to produce a continuous profile of uniform cross-section. The solidified (or still molten) extrudate is then cooled, sized, hauled off, and cut (or wound) into the desired packaging form such as films, sheets, pipes, profiles, blown bottles, or coatings.

In the context of **Plastics Packaging Technology (PEPOT748)**, extrusion is the **primary conversion process** used to manufacture the vast majority of flexible and rigid packaging structures — including **LDPE/LLDPE/HDPE films, multilayer co-extruded barrier films, PP/PS sheets, drinking straws, tubes, and parisons for blow moulding**.

The single most important piece of equipment is the **Screw Extruder**, which performs the dual functions of:
1. **Plastics processing** (melting, mixing, conveying) — analogous to a heated positive-displacement pump.
2. **Pressure generation** — to push the homogeneous melt through the downstream die assembly.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Giant Heated Meat Mincer with a Shaped Nozzle**
>
> Imagine pushing a block of cold butter through a garlic press. Hard work, messy, and the butter comes out chunky. Now replace the butter with **softened clay**, push it through a **hollow metal tube wrapped in a heater**, and use a **rotating Archimedes screw** (like a corkscrew) inside the tube to drag the clay forward while heating it. Out of the other end, attach a **shaped disc (the die)** — a slit, a ring, a star — and the softened clay exits as a continuous ribbon, tube, or star-shaped profile that you can cut into packaging components.
>
> The extruder is exactly this: a **heated barrel + a rotating screw + a shaping die**. Plastics pellets go in at the **hopper**, the screw drags, melts, mixes, and pressurises them, and the die shapes them into the continuous packaging form (film, sheet, tube, coating).

## 1.3 Classification of Extruders (At a Glance)

| Type | Drive / Mechanism | Typical Use in Packaging |
|------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| **Single-Screw Extruder (SSE)** | One flighted screw in a heated barrel | Films, sheets, pipes, blow moulding parisons, profiles |
| **Counter-Rotating Twin-Screw Extruder (CR-TSE)** | Two intermeshing screws, rotating in opposite directions | Compounding, rigid PVC, scrap recycling |
| **Co-Rotating Twin-Screw Extruder (CO-TSE)** | Two intermeshing screws, rotating in the same direction | Compounding, masterbatch, co-extrusion feed |
| **Ram Extruder** | Plunger (no screw) | PTFE (Teflon), ultra-high-viscosity polymers, UHMWPE |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the KTU 2024 syllabus, the **Single-Screw Extruder** is the **most heavily tested** because it is the workhorse of the packaging industry. The remaining notes are therefore built around this machine.

## 1.4 Why Extrusion Dominates Packaging Manufacturing

- **Continuous** → high productivity, low unit cost.
- **Versatile** → one machine can produce films, sheets, tubes, coatings, profiles, and parisons simply by **changing the die**.
- **Compatible with multi-layer co-extrusion** → essential for barrier packaging (e.g., EVOH / PA / PE).
- **Easily coupled** with downstream units (calender, chill-roll, blown-film tower, corrugator, thermoformer).
- **Wastes** (edge trim, regrind) can be reprocessed at the feed throat — high material economy.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Single-Screw Extruder

A single-screw extruder, although visually a long horizontal cylinder, is a **precisely engineered multi-zone reactor**. Reading the machine from upstream (feed) to downstream (die), the polymer encounters the following subsystems:

1. **Feed Hopper** — gravity-fed reservoir of solid pellets. Often equipped with a **crammer feeder** for high-throughput lines.
2. **Barrel** — a thick-walled, bimetallic (e.g., nitrided steel inner liner) cylinder, externally wrapped in **band heaters** (typically 3 to 5 zones, each independently PID-controlled). Internal bore diameter is **D** (the **barrel inner diameter**).
3. **Screw** — a long, hardened steel rotor with a helical flight. Outer root diameter **D**, inner flight-tip diameter **D_o**, helix angle **θ**, channel depth **H**, channel width **W**, flight width **e**.
4. **Screw Tip / Breaker Plate & Screen Pack** — at the discharge end. A perforated plate supporting a **wire-mesh screen pack** (typically 20/40/60/80 mesh) acts as a **melt filter**, removes unmelted particles, and converts **rotational (drag) flow into axial (pressure) flow** by eliminating the swirling vortex.
5. **Adapter & Die Assembly** — shapes the molten extrudate. The die land length **L_d** provides the final pressure drop.
6. **Downstream Equipment** — chill rolls, air rings (blown film), calibrators, haul-off, cutter/winder.
7. **Drive & Thrust Bearing Assembly** — typically a thyristor/DC/AC variable-speed motor coupled through a gear reducer to the screw; rated for the **screw thrust load** generated by the internal pressure.

## 2.2 The Three Functional Zones of the Screw

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The screw is **not uniform**. Its **channel depth (H) and pitch (and therefore compression ratio)** change along its length to perform three sequential jobs. For a typical L/D = 24:1 to 30:1 screw used in film/sheet extrusion:

| Zone | Function | Length (approx.) | Channel Depth (H) | Mechanism |
|------|----------|------------------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Feed Zone (Solids Conveying)** | Compacts and conveys cold pellets | 25 – 30 % of L | Deepest, constant (~0.15 D for thermoplastics) | Friction between pellets, barrel wall, and screw root |
| **Compression / Transition Zone (Melting)** | Melts the compacted bed, mixes melt with residual solids | 30 – 40 % of L | Tapers down (e.g., 0.15 D → 0.05 D) | Combination of barrel heating + **shear heating** (viscous dissipation) |
| **Metering Zone (Melt Conveying / Pumping)** | Homogenises melt and pumps it at a steady rate | 25 – 35 % of L | Shallowest, constant (~0.05 D for thermoplastics) | **Drag flow** of a viscous fluid in a rectangular channel |

**Compression Ratio (CR)** is defined as:

$$
\text{CR} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Volume of one flight in the feed zone}}{\text{Volume of one flight in the metering zone}} \;=\; \dfrac{H_f \, W_f}{H_m \, W_m}
$$

Typical values: **2.5 : 1 to 3.0 : 1** for polyolefins; **1.5 : 1 to 2.0 : 1** for PVC and other heat-sensitive polymers.

## 2.3 The Three Components of Extruder Output

The volumetric output rate **Q** (m³/s) of a melt-pumping (metering) extruder is the **algebraic sum of three independent flow contributions**. This is the **single most important equation in extrusion theory** for KTU:

$$
Q \;=\; Q_d \;-\; Q_p \;-\; Q_f
$$

where:

- **Q_d** = **Drag Flow** — the forward pumping caused by the relative motion of the screw flight over the stationary barrel wall (the "Archimedes pump" component).
- **Q_p** = **Pressure Flow** — the *backward* leak driven by the pressure gradient that the extruder itself builds up at the die. It always **subtracts** from the output.
- **Q_f** = **Leakage (Flight) Flow** — the small back-flow through the *clearance* between the screw flight tip and the barrel wall. Usually **small (≤ 5 % of Q_d)** and often neglected in KTU problems.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** *"D minus P minus F — what drags forward, what pressures back, and what slips through the flights."*

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the following expressions — they appear in almost every KTU ESE question on extrusion.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Drag flow rate | $Q_d \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^{2}\,D^{2}\,N\,d\,\sin\theta\cos\theta$ | m³/s | $D$ = barrel ID, $d$ = channel depth (= $H_m$ in metering zone), $N$ = screw rpm, $\theta$ = helix angle. |
| 2 | Drag flow — simplified (using $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta - e$) | $Q_d \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^{2}\,D^{2}\,N\,d\,\sin\theta\cos\theta$ | m³/s | Identical to (1); often written with channel width $W$ explicitly: $Q_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N d\sin\theta\cos\theta$. |
| 3 | Pressure flow (Newtonian melt) | $Q_p \;=\; \dfrac{\pi\,D\,d^{3}\,\sin^{2}\theta}{12\,\eta\,L_m}\,\Delta P$ | m³/s | $L_m$ = axial length of metering zone; $\eta$ = melt viscosity. |
| 4 | Net output | $Q \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N d\sin\theta\cos\theta \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}{12 \eta L_m}\Delta P$ | m³/s | Ignoring flight leakage $Q_f$. |
| 5 | Throughput (mass) | $\dot{m} \;=\; \rho_{melt}\,Q$ | kg/s | $\rho_{melt}$ ≈ 0.74 – 0.97 g/cm³ for polyolefins. |
| 6 | Specific energy | $E_{sp} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Input power}}{\dot{m}}$ | J/kg | Typical range: $3 \times 10^{6}$ – $6 \times 10^{6}$ J/kg for LDPE film. |
| 7 | Compression ratio | $\text{CR} \;=\; H_f / H_m$ | – | For uniform $W$. |
| 8 | Extruder characterisation line | $\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{12\,\eta\,L_m}{\pi\,D\,d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}\,\Bigl(\tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N d\sin\theta\cos\theta - Q\Bigr)$ | Pa | Useful for die / pump matching. |
| 9 | Shear rate in metering channel (approx.) | $\dot{\gamma} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D N}{d - \delta}$ (where $\delta$ is flight clearance) | s⁻¹ | $10^{2}$ – $10^{3}$ s⁻¹ typical. |
| 10 | Die-swell (extrudate swell) ratio | $B \;=\; D_{extrudate} / D_{die}$ | – | 1.10 – 1.50 for polyolefins; due to elastic recoil. |
| 11 | Mean residence time | $\bar{t} \;=\; V_{free} / Q$ | s | $V_{free}$ = free volume in the metering channel. |
| 12 | Power required to drive the screw (approx.) | $P \;=\; \tau_{avg} \,\pi D L N$ | W | Used for motor sizing. |

> [!NOTE]
> In all the above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ (absolute value) notation has been written as `vert` or `mid` to keep the markdown table parser safe — but when writing the equations inline in the KTU answer sheet, use standard $\vert$ symbols.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Equations

- **Sizing a new extruder**: Knowing the required throughput (kg/h) of an LDPE packaging film line, the manufacturer solves Equation (4) for a feasible $D$, $N$ pair, then selects a screw with the matching compression ratio.
- **Troubleshooting a fluctuating output**: A drifting melt temperature changes $\eta$, which in turn changes $Q_p$ and hence $Q$. The line operator detects the surge and tightens the heater band tuning.
- **Die-swell compensation**: In tubing/straw extrusion, the die is intentionally **undercut** by the inverse of the swell ratio $B$ to deliver a finished product of correct diameter.
- **Energy audit**: The specific energy $E_{sp}$ quantifies how much electrical energy is consumed per kg of packaging produced — a key KPI in sustainable packaging.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Net Output Equation $Q = Q_d - Q_p$

We model the melt inside the metering-zone channel as a **Newtonian fluid** in steady, laminar, fully-developed flow. We then "unwind" the helical channel and analyse flow between two parallel plates:

- **Lower plate** (the screw root) moves with tangential velocity $\pi D N$ at helix angle $\theta$ relative to the channel axis.
- **Upper plate** (the barrel wall) is **stationary**.

This geometric *unwinding* converts the helical coordinate system into a flat Cartesian channel of width $W$ and depth $d$ (channel depth = $H_m$).

### Step 1 — Decompose the barrel velocity

The barrel's tangential surface velocity $V_b = \pi D N$ has two components relative to the channel axis (the down-channel direction, $z$):

- **Down-channel component** (drives the fluid forward): $V_{bz} \;=\; \pi D N \cos\theta$
- **Cross-channel component** (causes circulatory secondary flow, ignored in 1-D theory): $V_{bx} \;=\; \pi D N \sin\theta$

### Step 2 — Drag flow from Couette-Poiseuille superposition

The volumetric flow per unit width in a rectangular channel of depth $d$ with relative plate motion $V_{bz}$ and a pressure gradient $dP/dz$ is a standard result of lubrication theory:

$$
q(z) \;=\; \underbrace{\dfrac{V_{bz}\,d}{2}}_{\text{Couette (drag) component}} \;-\; \underbrace{\dfrac{d^{3}}{12\,\eta}\,\dfrac{dP}{dz}}_{\text{Poiseuille (pressure) component}}
$$

> **Couette part** — pure shear of a Newtonian fluid between a moving and a stationary plate; the velocity profile is linear, so the average velocity is half the plate velocity, multiplied by the channel depth.

> **Poiseuille part** — a pressure gradient $dP/dz < 0$ drives a parabolic (back-flow) profile; the volumetric flow is the well-known result $-(d^3/12\eta)(dP/dz)$.

### Step 3 — Integrate over the channel width

The total volumetric flow is $Q = W \cdot q(z)$, where $W$ is the channel width measured perpendicular to the flight:

$$
Q \;=\; \dfrac{V_{bz}\,W\,d}{2} \;-\; \dfrac{W\,d^{3}}{12\,\eta}\,\dfrac{dP}{dz}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $V_{bz} = \pi D N \cos\theta$ and $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta - e$

The width of the helical channel (perpendicular to the flights) is $W \;\approx\; \pi D \sin\theta - e$. For simplicity in KTU derivations, the flight width $e$ is **neglected**, giving $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta$. Substituting:

$$
Q \;=\; \dfrac{(\pi D N \cos\theta)(\pi D \sin\theta)\,d}{2} \;-\; \dfrac{(\pi D \sin\theta)\,d^{3}}{12\,\eta}\,\dfrac{dP}{dz}
$$

Simplify the trigonometric product $\sin\theta \cos\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\theta$:

$$
Q \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N\,d\,\sin\theta\cos\theta \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D d^{3}\sin\theta}{12\,\eta}\,\dfrac{dP}{dz}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert the axial pressure gradient to a finite pressure difference

The pressure at the start of the metering zone is approximately atmospheric, and at its end it rises to the head pressure $\Delta P$. Assuming a **linear pressure rise** over the axial length of the metering zone $L_m$:

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dz} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{L_m}
$$

Substitute this into the expression for $Q$:

$$
Q \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N\,d\,\sin\theta\cos\theta \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}{12\,\eta\,L_m}\,\Delta P
$$

### Step 6 — Identify the two terms as $Q_d$ and $Q_p$

This is exactly the expression listed in the formula sheet:

$$
\boxed{\,Q \;=\; \underbrace{\dfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N\,d\,\sin\theta\cos\theta}_{Q_d\,\text{(drag flow, forward)}} \;-\; \underbrace{\dfrac{\pi D d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}{12\,\eta\,L_m}\,\Delta P}_{Q_p\,\text{(pressure flow, backward)}}\,}
$$

If the flight clearance leakage $Q_f$ is included:

$$
Q \;=\; Q_d \;-\; Q_p \;-\; Q_f
$$

### Step 3.2 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A single-screw extruder used for LDPE packaging film has the following data. Calculate the volumetric output $Q$ and the mass throughput $\dot{m}$.
>
> - Barrel inner diameter: $D = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
> - Metering channel depth: $d = 3.0 \text{ mm} = 0.003 \text{ m}$
> - Helix angle: $\theta = 17.65^{\circ}$ (so $\sin\theta = 0.303$, $\cos\theta = 0.953$)
> - Screw speed: $N = 60 \text{ rpm} = 1.0 \text{ rev/s}$
> - Axial length of metering zone: $L_m = 0.75 \text{ m}$
> - Pressure developed at die: $\Delta P = 7 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$
> - Melt viscosity of LDPE at processing temperature: $\eta = 350 \text{ Pa·s}$
> - Density of molten LDPE: $\rho_{melt} = 780 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$

**Step 1 — Compute $Q_d$:**

$$
Q_d \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^{2}\,(0.075)^{2}\,(1.0)\,(0.003)\,(0.303)(0.953)
$$

Compute the coefficient $\tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2} \approx 4.9348$:

$$
Q_d \;=\; 4.9348 \times (0.005625) \times (0.003) \times 0.2887
$$

$$
Q_d \;=\; 4.9348 \times 0.005625 \times 0.000866
$$

$$
Q_d \;\approx\; 2.41 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $Q_p$:**

$$
Q_p \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (0.075)(0.003)^{3}\,(0.303)^{2}}{12\,(350)\,(0.75)}\;\times\;(7 \times 10^{6})
$$

Numerator:

$$
\pi \times 0.075 \times 2.7\times10^{-8} \times 0.0918 \times 7\times10^{6}
$$

$$
\;\approx\; 3.1416 \times 0.075 \times 2.7\times10^{-8} \times 0.0918 \times 7\times10^{6}
$$

$$
\;\approx\; 4.36 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

Denominator:

$$
12 \times 350 \times 0.75 \;=\; 3150
$$

So:

$$
Q_p \;=\; \dfrac{4.36 \times 10^{-6}}{3150} \;\approx\; 1.38 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Net output (ignoring $Q_f$):**

$$
Q \;=\; Q_d - Q_p \;=\; 2.41 \times 10^{-5} - 1.38 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
Q \;\approx\; 2.27 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass throughput:**

$$
\dot{m} \;=\; \rho_{melt}\,Q \;=\; 780 \times 2.27 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\dot{m} \;\approx\; 1.77 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/s} \;\approx\; 63.7 \text{ kg/h}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** A 63.7 kg/h net throughput is realistic for a 75 mm extruder running at 60 rpm on LDPE film — KTU board examiners love this scale of answer because it confirms that the student understood the magnitudes involved.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Optimal Helix Angle $\theta = 20°$ to $30°$

The drag flow expression $Q_d \propto \sin\theta\cos\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\theta$ is **maximised** when $\sin 2\theta = 1$, i.e., $2\theta = 90°$, $\theta = 45°$. However, at $\theta = 45°$ the pressure-flow term also **grows rapidly** (proportional to $\sin^2\theta$), the screw becomes a *poor pump*, and the residence time distribution becomes unfavourable. In practice, the **optimum compromise** between high drag flow, reasonable pressure-generating ability, and good solids conveying is:

$$
\theta_{opt} \;\approx\; 20^{\circ} \text{ to } 30^{\circ}
$$

For LDPE/PP, screws are commonly cut at $\theta = 17.65°$ (which is the angle for a square-pitched flight where the pitch equals $D$, and $\tan\theta = 1/\pi$). The KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to be able to state the **optimum range** and justify it qualitatively.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Single-Screw Extrusion Line

The following Mermaid block renders the upstream → downstream flow of material and information, which is the typical "block diagram" KTU expects in 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hopper with Solid Pellets] --> B[Feed Throat and Gravity Feeder]
    B --> C[Feed Zone of Screw - Solids Conveying]
    C --> D[Compression Zone - Melting by Shear and Conduction]
    D --> E[Metering Zone - Melt Pumping and Mixing]
    E --> F[Breaker Plate and Screen Pack - Melt Filter]
    F --> G[Adapter and Shaped Die]
    G --> H[Haul Off and Cooling]
    H --> I[Take Up Winder or Cutter]
    H2[Thrust Bearing] --> E
    J[PID Heater Bands Zone 1] --> C
    K[PID Heater Bands Zone 2] --> D
    L[PID Heater Bands Zone 3] --> E
    M[Drive Motor and Gearbox] --> C
    M2[Pressure Transducer] --- G
    M2 --> N[Controller]
    N --> J
    N --> K
    N --> L
    N --> M
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **upper horizontal chain (A → I)** is the **material path** — polymer pellets in, finished packaging out.
- The **lower chain (J, K, L, M)** is the **energy and control path** — heaters and motor that drive the process.
- The **M2 → N** path is the **feedback loop** — the die-pressure transducer signals the controller, which modulates screw speed and heater powers to keep output stable.

## 4.2 Screw Geometry — Functional Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    scRoot[Screw Root - Solid Steel Shaft]
    scFlight1[Feed Zone Flights - Deep Channel - Constant Pitch]
    scFlight2[Compression Zone Flights - Tapered Channel - Decreasing Depth]
    scFlight3[Metering Zone Flights - Shallow Channel - Constant Pitch]
    scTip[Screw Tip and Drive Dog]
    scFlight1 --> scFlight2
    scFlight2 --> scFlight3
    scFlight3 --> scTip
```

**Annotations (key dimensions):**

- **Helix angle $\theta$** — measured between the flight edge and the plane perpendicular to the screw axis.
- **Channel depth $d$** — perpendicular distance from the screw root to the flight tip.
- **Channel width $W$** — measured perpendicular to the flight land.
- **Pitch $p$** — axial distance between two consecutive flights of the same helix hand; for a square-pitched screw $p = D$.
- **L/D ratio** — the *total* screw length divided by the barrel inner diameter. **L/D = 24:1** is standard for film; **L/D = 30:1** is used for compounding and difficult-to-melt resins.

## 4.3 Co-extrusion Feed Block (Block Diagram for Multilayer Packaging Films)

For high-barrier food packaging films, multiple extruders feed a common **co-extrusion die** through a **feed block** that stacks the melt layers in the desired order (e.g., PE / tie / EVOH / tie / PE).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    extA[Extruder A - Outer PE Layer] --> fb[Feed Block with Layer Sequencer]
    extB[Extruder B - Tie Resin Layer] --> fb
    extC[Extruder C - EVOH Barrier Layer] --> fb
    extD[Extruder D - Inner PE Sealant Layer] --> fb
    fb --> cd[Co-extrusion Die - Slit or Annular]
    cd --> bft[Blown Film Tower or Cast Chill Roll]
    bft --> wn[Winder and Slitter]
```

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 examiners frequently include a **labelled block diagram** (typically for 4 marks within a 14-mark question). Mastery of the above block diagrams will earn full marks.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]** 
Define the term **"compression ratio"** of an extruder screw. What is the typical range used for polyolefins?

**Model Answer:**

The compression ratio (CR) of an extruder screw is defined as the ratio of the **channel volume per flight in the feed zone** to the **channel volume per flight in the metering zone**:

$$
\text{CR} \;=\; \dfrac{H_f \times W_f}{H_m \times W_m}
$$

For a screw with uniform channel width ($W_f = W_m$), this simplifies to $\text{CR} = H_f/H_m$. The compression ratio quantifies the **volumetric compaction** the solid polymer bed undergoes as it travels from the feed throat to the metering zone, ensuring complete melting and air removal.

**Typical range for polyolefins (LDPE, LLDPE, HDPE, PP):** **CR = 2.5 : 1 to 3.0 : 1**.

For heat-sensitive polymers such as rigid PVC, the range is lower (**1.5 : 1 to 2.0 : 1**) to avoid shear-induced degradation. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary]** 
Distinguish between **drag flow** and **pressure flow** in the metering zone of a single-screw extruder. What is the algebraic sign of each term in the net output equation?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Drag Flow ($Q_d$) | Pressure Flow ($Q_p$) |
|---------|--------------------|------------------------|
| **Cause** | Relative motion between the rotating screw flight and the stationary barrel wall | Pressure gradient $\Delta P$ built up at the die |
| **Direction** | **Down-channel (forward, positive)** | **Up-channel (backward, negative)** |
| **Dependence** | $Q_d \propto N, D^{2}, d, \sin\theta\cos\theta$ | $Q_p \propto \Delta P, d^{3}, \sin^{2}\theta / \eta$ |
| **Sign in $Q = Q_d - Q_p - Q_f$** | **Plus (+)** | **Minus (−)** |

The **drag flow** is the *desired* pumping action, while the **pressure flow** represents the *back-leak* caused by the very pressure the extruder generates to push melt through the die. The net output is the algebraic difference. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled block diagram, describe the construction and working of a **single-screw extruder** used in plastics packaging. Explain the functions of the three functional zones of the screw. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from first principles, derive the expression for the **net volumetric output** of a single-screw extruder operating in the metering zone. State clearly the assumptions made. A 60 mm extruder running at 80 rpm with a metering depth of 2.5 mm and helix angle $17.65°$ produces an output of $6.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s. The metering length is 0.6 m, melt viscosity 400 Pa·s. Calculate the **die pressure $\Delta P$** developed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

The single-screw extruder consists of:

1. **Hopper** — gravity feeds the polymer pellets into the barrel.
2. **Barrel** — externally heated by **3 to 5 PID-controlled heater bands**; bore = $D$ (the reference diameter).
