3. **Screw** — a helical flight on a steel shaft. The **three zones** and their functions are:
   - **Feed Zone (Solids Conveying)** — the deep, constant-pitch flights drag pellets forward by **frictional drag** with the barrel wall. Compaction of the loose bed occurs here.
   - **Compression / Transition Zone (Melting)** — the channel depth **tapers down**, generating intense **shear** and **frictional (viscous) heating** that melts the polymer. A thin melt film forms against the barrel wall.
   - **Metering Zone (Melt Pumping)** — the shallow, constant-depth channel acts as a **positive-displacement pump**. Homogeneous melt is delivered at a steady rate and pressure to the die.
4. **Breaker Plate and Screen Pack** — supported by the breaker plate, it filters contaminants and converts swirling rotational flow into straight axial flow.
5. **Die** — shapes the extrudate (slit → film/sheet; annular → tube/blown film; profile → straw).
6. **Drive, Gearbox, Thrust Bearing** — supplies torque and absorbs the axial reaction force.

A **labelled block diagram** (as drawn in Section 4.1) accompanies the description in the answer booklet. **(7 marks)**

**(b) — Derivation and Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Derivation (5 marks of the 7):**

1. Unwind the helical channel of width $W$ and depth $d$ into a flat rectangular geometry between a moving plate (screw root, velocity $V_b = \pi D N$) at angle $\theta$ and a stationary plate (barrel wall).
2. Decompose the plate velocity into **down-channel** $V_{bz} = \pi D N \cos\theta$ and **cross-channel** $V_{bx} = \pi D N \sin\theta$ components.
3. The 1-D flow in the $z$-direction (Couette + Poiseuille superposition) gives per unit width:

$$
q(z) \;=\; \dfrac{V_{bz}\,d}{2} \;-\; \dfrac{d^{3}}{12\,\eta}\,\dfrac{dP}{dz}
$$

4. Multiply by channel width $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta$:

$$
Q \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N d \sin\theta\cos\theta \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}{12\eta}\dfrac{dP}{dz}
$$

5. With linear pressure rise $dP/dz = \Delta P/L_m$ over the metering length $L_m$:

$$
\boxed{\,Q \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N d \sin\theta\cos\theta \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}{12\eta L_m}\Delta P\,}
$$

**Assumptions (1 mark):** Newtonian melt; steady, laminar, fully-developed flow; incompressible; negligible flight leakage; $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta$ (flight width $e$ ignored); negligible cross-channel flow contribution; $e$ (flight width) and screw clearance $\delta$ are small.

**Numerical Calculation (1 mark):**

Given: $D = 0.060$ m, $N = 80/60 = 1.333$ rev/s, $d = 0.0025$ m, $\theta = 17.65°$ ⇒ $\sin\theta = 0.303$, $\cos\theta = 0.953$, $L_m = 0.6$ m, $\eta = 400$ Pa·s, $Q = 6.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s.

First compute $Q_d$:

$$
Q_d \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}(0.060)^{2}(1.333)(0.0025)(0.303)(0.953)
$$

$$
Q_d \;\approx\; 4.9348 \times 3.6\times10^{-3} \times 1.333 \times 0.0025 \times 0.2887
$$

$$
Q_d \;\approx\; 1.71 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

From $Q = Q_d - Q_p$, solve for $Q_p$:

$$
Q_p \;=\; Q_d - Q \;=\; 1.71 \times 10^{-5} - 6.5 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 1.06 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

Now solve for $\Delta P$ from the $Q_p$ expression:

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{12\,\eta\,L_m\,Q_p}{\pi D d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}
$$

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{12 \times 400 \times 0.6 \times 1.06\times10^{-5}}{\pi \times 0.060 \times (0.0025)^{3} \times (0.303)^{2}}
$$

Numerator $\approx 3.05 \times 10^{-2}$.
Denominator $\approx 0.1885 \times 1.5625\times10^{-8} \times 0.0918 \approx 2.70 \times 10^{-10}$.

$$
\Delta P \;\approx\; \dfrac{3.05\times 10^{-2}}{2.70 \times 10^{-10}} \;\approx\; 1.13 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa}
$$

**Result:** $\Delta P \approx 1.13 \times 10^{8}$ Pa $\approx 113$ MPa. (Note: a typical LDPE die pressure is 7 – 35 MPa; the higher value here reflects the very small $Q$ relative to the screw's drag capacity, i.e., the die is much too restrictive for this screw speed.)

**(Total = 7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **extrudate swell (die swell)** in polymer extrusion. Discuss the factors that influence it and its significance in the design of **packaging tubing and blown films**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two different extruders are used to process **LDPE film**. Extruder X has $D = 90$ mm, $d = 4$ mm, $\theta = 17.65°$, $N = 50$ rpm. Extruder Y has $D = 60$ mm, $d = 3$ mm, $\theta = 20°$, $N = 90$ rpm. Ignoring pressure flow, determine which extruder has the higher **theoretical drag-flow capacity**, and by what factor. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Die Swell (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Die swell (also called **extrudate swell** or the **Barus effect**) is the phenomenon in which the cross-sectional dimensions of an extrudate emerging from a die are **larger** than the corresponding die dimensions. Quantitatively, the swell ratio is

$$
B \;=\; \dfrac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}}
$$

For polyolefins, $B$ typically lies in the range **1.10 – 1.50**.

**Mechanism (2 marks):** When polymer melt flows through a converging die entry, it experiences both **shear** and **elongational** deformation. Polymer chains become **oriented and stretched** in the flow direction. Upon exit, the elastic (recoverable) component of this strain is released, causing the chains to recoil toward their coiled equilibrium state. The macroscopic result is a **radial expansion** of the extrudate.

**Factors (2 marks):** 
- **Melt elasticity and relaxation time** — higher elasticity ⇒ higher $B$.
- **Shear rate in the die** — higher shear ⇒ higher $B$.
- **Residence time / temperature** — higher temperature reduces $B$ (more relaxation in the die).
- **Die land length** — longer land reduces $B$ (more relaxation during passage).
- **Polymer molecular structure** — long-chain-branched LDPE exhibits higher $B$ than linear LLDPE.
- **Polymer molecular weight / MFI** — lower MFI (higher MW) ⇒ higher $B$.

**Significance in packaging (1 mark):**
- In **blown film extrusion**, the die diameter is intentionally **smaller** than the final bubble diameter by the inverse of the swell ratio.
- In **packaging tubing and drinking straws**, the die is **undercut** by $1/B$ so that the cooled product attains the correct nominal diameter.
- In **wire and cable insulation**, die swell governs the minimum achievable insulation thickness.

**(b) — Comparative Drag-Flow Calculation (7 marks):**

For each extruder, drag flow (ignoring pressure flow) is:

$$
Q_d \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N d \sin\theta\cos\theta
$$

The factor $f = \sin\theta\cos\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\theta$ is:
- For $\theta = 17.65°$: $f_X = 0.303 \times 0.953 = 0.2887$
- For $\theta = 20°$: $f_Y = 0.342 \times 0.940 = 0.3214$

**Extruder X** ($D = 0.090$ m, $d = 0.004$ m, $N = 50/60 = 0.8333$ rev/s, $f_X = 0.2887$):

$$
Q_{d,X} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}(0.090)^{2}(0.8333)(0.004)(0.2887)
$$

$$
Q_{d,X} \;=\; 4.9348 \times 8.1\times10^{-3} \times 0.8333 \times 0.004 \times 0.2887
$$

$$
Q_{d,X} \;\approx\; 3.86 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Extruder Y** ($D = 0.060$ m, $d = 0.003$ m, $N = 90/60 = 1.5$ rev/s, $f_Y = 0.3214$):

$$
Q_{d,Y} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}(0.060)^{2}(1.5)(0.003)(0.3214)
$$

$$
Q_{d,Y} \;=\; 4.9348 \times 3.6\times10^{-3} \times 1.5 \times 0.003 \times 0.3214
$$

$$
Q_{d,Y} \;\approx\; 2.57 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Comparison (1 mark):**

$$
\dfrac{Q_{d,X}}{Q_{d,Y}} \;=\; \dfrac{3.86 \times 10^{-5}}{2.57 \times 10^{-5}} \;\approx\; 1.50
$$

**Extruder X has the higher drag-flow capacity, by a factor of approximately 1.5×.** Although Extruder Y runs faster and has a slightly higher helix-angle factor, the $D^2$ term in Extruder X dominates.

**(Total = 7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Extrusion Questions — KTU 2024 ESE**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the minus sign between $Q_d$ and $Q_p$** — pressure flow is *back-flow*; writing $Q = Q_d + Q_p$ costs **2 marks** outright.
> 2. **Mixing up units** — $D$, $d$, $L_m$ must all be in **metres**; $N$ in **revolutions per second** (not rpm); $\eta$ in **Pa·s**. KTU examiners specifically check unit consistency.
> 3. **Neglecting to state the assumptions** in the derivation (Newtonian, steady, fully-developed, $e$ neglected, etc.) — at least **1 mark** is reserved for the assumption list in any 7-mark derivation.
> 4. **Drawing the screw diagram without labels** — $D$, $d$, $W$, $H_f$, $H_m$, $L_f$, $L_m$, $L_c$ must each be marked. An unlabelled diagram scores **0 / 2 marks** allocated to the figure.
> 5. **Confusing feed/metering depth with screw diameter** — students often write $H_m = D$ instead of $H_m \approx 0.05D$.
> 6. **Sign error in $Q = Q_d - Q_p$ when the die pressure is zero** — if $\Delta P = 0$, students sometimes write $Q = 0$ instead of $Q = Q_d$. Remember: **zero die pressure gives maximum (open-discharge) output $Q = Q_d$**, not zero output.
> 7. **Using $L$ (total screw length) instead of $L_m$ (metering length only)** in the $Q_p$ expression — the pressure gradient is built up only over the metering zone.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Extrusion (PEPOT748, Module 3)**
>
> - **Extrusion** is a *continuous* melt-shaping process; the **single-screw extruder** is the workhorse of plastics packaging.
> - The three functional zones of the screw are **Feed (solids conveying) → Compression (melting) → Metering (pumping)**.
> - **Compression ratio CR** for polyolefins is **2.5 – 3.0**; for PVC it is **1.5 – 2.0**.
> - The **L/D ratio** of a typical film/sheet screw is **24:1 to 30:1**.
> - The **net volumetric output** of the metering zone is $Q = Q_d - Q_p - Q_f$, with $Q_f$ often negligible.
> - **Drag flow** $Q_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\theta\cos\theta$ is **forward (positive)** and proportional to screw speed.
> - **Pressure flow** $Q_p = \dfrac{\pi D d^3 \sin^2\theta}{12 \eta L_m}\Delta P$ is **backward (negative)** and proportional to die pressure.
> - **Optimum helix angle** for an extruder screw is **20° – 30°** (often 17.65° for square pitch).
> - **Barrel heaters** are typically **3 – 5 PID-controlled zones**, each with its own thermocouple.
> - The **breaker plate + screen pack** filters the melt and converts swirling flow to straight axial flow.
> - **Die swell (Barus effect)** is caused by elastic recovery of oriented polymer chains; swell ratio $B = D_{extrudate}/D_{die}$ is typically **1.10 – 1.50** for polyolefins.
> - **Mass throughput** $\dot{m} = \rho_{melt} Q$.
> - **Specific energy** $E_{sp} = P/\dot{m}$ is typically **3 – 6 MJ/kg** for LDPE film extrusion.
> - **Co-extrusion** of 3 to 7 layers (e.g., PE/tie/EVOH/tie/PE) is the industry standard for high-barrier food packaging films.
> - **Twin-screw extruders** (co-rotating for compounding; counter-rotating for PVC) are used in compounding, masterbatch, and rigid packaging.
> - For the KTU 2024 ESE, be ready to (i) **derive** the output equation, (ii) **calculate** a numerical value, (iii) **draw and label** a block diagram, and (iv) **list the three functional zones** with their functions.
> - Always include the **assumption list** whenever you write a derivation — it is a guaranteed 1-mark freebie.
> - Always carry **units through the calculation**; final answers should be in **m³/s (for $Q$)**, **kg/h or kg/s (for $\dot{m}$)**, and **Pa or MPa (for $\Delta P$)**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Film and Flexible Packaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Film and Flexible Packaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Film packaging** refers to packaging structures whose thickness is substantially smaller than their length and width, typically manufactured as continuous webs with thicknesses below **250 µm (10 mils)**. **Flexible packaging** is the broader category encompassing any non-rigid package whose shape can be readily changed when filled and sealed, including films, foils, papers, and laminations thereof.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, flexible packaging is defined as a **multi-material, multi-layer engineered structure** designed to protect, preserve, present, and transport goods while optimizing material usage, logistics efficiency, and consumer convenience.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The term "film" denotes the substrate itself, whereas "flexible packaging" denotes the engineered *final package* built by combining films, coatings, inks, adhesives, and sealants.

### Standard Industry Metrics (KTU Reference Data)

| Metric | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Gauge | **1 mil = 25.4 µm** | µm / mil |
| Film Thickness Range | **10 – 250 µm** | µm |
| Yield (Area/kg) | Calculated from density | m²/kg |
| WVTR | 0.1 – 100 | g/m²·day |
| OTR | 1 – 1000 | cm³/m²·day·atm |
| COF (Static/Dynamic) | 0.1 – 0.8 | Dimensionless |

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Engineered Sandwich" of Packaging**
>
> Imagine a sandwich you buy from a deli. A single slice of bread protects the filling, but it gets soggy and falls apart. Now imagine wrapping that filling in **multiple specialized layers**: an inner moisture-proof plastic sheet, a middle aluminum foil that blocks light and oxygen, an outer tough printable polymer, and a heat-sealable inner coating to keep it closed. **That is exactly how flexible packaging works.**
>
> Each layer (called a *ply*) in a flexible laminate has one specific job: one blocks oxygen, one gives mechanical strength, one prints beautifully, one seals under heat. The combination produces a packaging system far superior to any single material. The "flexibility" simply means the package can be folded, squeezed, or rolled — unlike a rigid bottle or can.

## 1.3 Classification of Flexible Packaging

### A. By Form (Converted Form)
- **Wraps** – cling film, pallet stretch film
- **Bags** – T-shirt bags, courier bags
- **Pouches** – stand-up pouches, retort pouches, sachets
- **Sleeves** – shrink sleeves, stretch sleeves
- **Lidding films** – sealed onto rigid trays/cups

### B. By Construction
- **Monolayer films** – single polymer (e.g., LDPE produce bag)
- **Multilayer coextruded films** – 3, 5, 7, or 9 layers in one operation
- **Laminates** – two or more films joined with adhesives or extrusion
- **Metallized films** – vacuum-deposited aluminum on polymer substrate
- **Foil-based laminates** – true aluminum foil + polymer layers

### C. By Application Function
- **Primary packaging** – direct product contact
- **Secondary packaging** – grouping units (e.g., shrink wrap)
- **Tertiary packaging** – logistics (stretch hood, pallet wrap)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Engineering Functions of a Flexible Package

A flexible package must simultaneously satisfy five engineering functions. Each function maps to specific layer properties:

1. **Containment** – Hermetic sealing (heat seal strength **> 1.5 N/15 mm** for most applications, **> 8 N/15 mm** for heavy-duty).
2. **Protection** – Barrier against **moisture (WVTR)**, **oxygen (OTR)**, **light (UV/visible)**, **aroma/gas**, and **microbes**.
3. **Preservation** – Extended shelf life via vacuum, MAP (Modified Atmosphere Packaging), retort sterilization.
4. **Presentation** – Printability, gloss, transparency (haze < **5%** for clear films).
5. **Performance** – Machinability on FFS (Form-Fill-Seal) lines: COF, stiffness (1% secant modulus), slip, hot-tack, seal-through-contamination.

## 2.2 Core Manufacturing Processes

### 2.2.1 Blown Film Extrusion
- Molten polymer is extruded through an **annular die** vertically upward.
- Air is injected through the die center to inflate the polymer bubble.
- Bubble is cooled, flattened through nip rolls, and wound.
- **Key parameters:** Blow-Up Ratio (BUR), Draw-Down Ratio (DDR), frost line height, neck length.

### 2.2.2 Cast Film Extrosion (Chill Roll)
- Molten polymer is extruded through a **slot die** onto chilled polished rollers.
- Rapid quenching gives **high clarity** and **high gloss**.
- Preferred for **CPP, PET, PA (Nylon)**.

### 2.2.3 Extrusion Coating & Lamination
- Polymer melt is coated onto a substrate (paper, foil, fabric) at high speed.
- Used to bond layers and provide **heat-seal capability** to non-sealable substrates.

### 2.2.4 Adhesive Lamination
- Two webs joined with solvent-based, water-based, or **solventless** polyurethane adhesives.
- Cured in aging rooms at **40–50 °C for 24–48 h**.

### 2.2.5 Metallization
- Aluminum vapor deposited under high vacuum (**10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵ mbar**) onto PET/OPP/BOPP.
- Achieves **OTR < 1 cm³/m²·day·atm** and brilliant metallic appearance.

## 2.3 Key Material Substrates — Property Profile

| Polymer | Abbreviation | Tensile Strength (MPa) | WVTR (g/m²·day) | OTR (cm³/m²·day·atm) | Heat Seal | Key Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low-Density Polyethylene | LDPE | 10 – 17 | 12 – 20 | 7,000 – 8,000 | Excellent | Sealant, bread bags |
| Linear LDPE | LLDPE | 20 – 35 | 10 – 16 | 7,000 | Good | Stretch film, liners |
| High-Density PE | HDPE | 25 – 40 | 5 – 8 | 2,500 | Fair | Grocery bags |
| Polypropylene (Cast) | CPP | 30 – 50 | 6 – 10 | 3,000 – 4,000 | Excellent | Retort pouches |
| Oriented PP | BOPP/OPP | 130 – 200 | 4 – 6 | 1,500 – 2,500 | Heat-seal layer added | Snack wrappers |
| Polyester | PET/BOPET | 180 – 280 | 18 – 25 | 100 – 130 | None alone | High-barrier laminates |
| Polyamide (Nylon) | PA (BOPA) | 200 – 300 | 200 – 300 | 50 – 80 | None alone | Vacuum, retort |
| Polyvinylidene Chloride | PVDC | 60 – 100 | 1 – 5 | 5 – 25 | Good | Meat, cheese wrap |
| Ethylene Vinyl Alcohol | EVOH | 70 – 100 | 30 – 100 | 0.5 – 5 (dry) | None alone | Oxygen barrier |
| Aluminum Foil | Al | 60 – 100 | < 0.1 | < 0.1 | N/A | Ultimate barrier |
| Polystyrene | PS | 35 – 55 | 70 – 100 | 4,000 | Poor | Thermoformed trays |

## 2.4 The KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **most frequently tested formulas** for KTU ESE and internal assessments on flexible packaging.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Yield** (Area per unit mass) | $$ Y = \dfrac{1}{\rho \cdot t} \;\left[\dfrac{m^{2}}{kg}\right] $$ | $\rho$ = density (g/cm³), $t$ = thickness (µm) |
| 2 | **WVTR calculation from permeability** | $$ WVTR = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta p}{t} $$ | P = permeability coefficient, $\Delta p$ = vapor pressure difference |
| 3 | **Blow-Up Ratio** | $$ BUR = \dfrac{D_{bubble}}{D_{die}} $$ | Determines MD/TD orientation balance |
| 4 | **Draw-Down Ratio** | $$ DDR = \dfrac{t_{die}}{t_{film}} $$ | Reduction in film thickness |
| 5 | **Lay-Flat Width** | $$ LFW = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_{bubble}}{2} $$ | Width of the flattened bubble |
| 6 | **Permeability Coefficient** | $$ P = q \cdot \dfrac{t}{A \cdot \Delta p} $$ | $q$ = steady-state permeation rate |
| 7 | **Permeance (for laminate)** | $$ \dfrac{1}{P_{L}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{X_{i}}{P_{i}} $$ | Series-resistance model; $X_i$ = thickness fraction |
| 8 | **Tensile Strength (engineering)** | $$ \sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_{0}} $$ | Force per original cross-section |
| 9 | **Elongation at break** | $$ \varepsilon = \dfrac{L - L_{0}}{L_{0}} \times 100\% $$ | Ductility indicator |
| 10 | **Shrinkage (oriented film)** | $$ S = \dfrac{L_{0} - L_{s}}{L_{0}} \times 100\% $$ | $L_s$ = shrunken length |
| 11 | **Heat Seal Strength requirement** | $$ HSS \geq 1.5 \text{ N/15 mm (light)}; \geq 8 \text{ N/15 mm (heavy)} $$ | Per ASTM F88 |
| 12 | **Cobb Value (paper laminates)** | $$ Cobb_{60} = m_{wet} - m_{dry} \;\left[\dfrac{g}{m^{2}}\right] $$ | Water absorption of paper |

## 2.5 Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering Practice

- **Yield equation** is critical for **cost-per-pack** calculations in FMCG converters — every micron saved translates to thousands of rupees annually.
- **Series-resistance model** is used daily by **barrier-film designers** to predict OTR of new laminate structures before lab trials, saving R&D time.
- **BUR and DDR** govern the **mechanical and optical balance** of blown films — wrong ratios cause split webs, poor tear strength, or hazy films.
- **Heat Seal Strength** determines **leak-proof performance** during drop, vibration, and altitude testing in logistics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Series-Resistance Model for Multilayer Laminate Permeation

### Problem Context
A flexible laminate consists of **n** layers in series. Each layer acts as a resistance to gas permeation. We must derive the **overall permeance** $P_L$ of the laminate.

### Step 1 — Define Steady-State Permeation Through a Single Layer

At steady state, the flux $J$ of permeant through any layer $i$ is constant (mass conservation):

$$ J = \dfrac{P_{i} \cdot \Delta p_{i}}{X_{i}} $$

Where:
- $J$ = steady-state flux (cm³ STP / m² · day)
- $P_i$ = permeability of layer $i$ (cm³ · cm / m² · day · atm)
- $X_i$ = thickness of layer $i$ (cm)
- $\Delta p_i$ = partial pressure drop across layer $i$ (atm)

### Step 2 — Apply Continuity of Flux

Because gas permeating through the laminate is the **same flux** through every layer:

$$ J_{1} = J_{2} = J_{3} = \cdots = J_{n} = J $$

$$ \Rightarrow \dfrac{P_{1} \cdot \Delta p_{1}}{X_{1}} = \dfrac{P_{2} \cdot \Delta p_{2}}{X_{2}} = \cdots = \dfrac{P_{L} \cdot \Delta p_{T}}{X_{T}} $$

Where $X_T$ is total thickness and $\Delta p_T$ is total partial pressure difference.

### Step 3 — Sum the Pressure Drops

Applying Kirchhoff's voltage analogy (additive pressure drops in series):

$$ \Delta p_{T} = \Delta p_{1} + \Delta p_{2} + \cdots + \Delta p_{n} $$

$$ \Delta p_{i} = \dfrac{J \cdot X_{i}}{P_{i}} $$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify

$$ \Delta p_{T} = J \cdot \left[ \dfrac{X_{1}}{P_{1}} + \dfrac{X_{2}}{P_{2}} + \cdots + \dfrac{X_{n}}{P_{n}} \right] $$

But by definition of laminate permeance:

$$ J = \dfrac{P_{L} \cdot \Delta p_{T}}{X_{T}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta p_{T} = \dfrac{J \cdot X_{T}}{P_{L}} $$

### Step 5 — Equate Both Expressions for $\Delta p_T$

$$ \dfrac{J \cdot X_{T}}{P_{L}} = J \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{X_{i}}{P_{i}} $$

Cancel $J$ and divide by $X_T$:

$$ \dfrac{1}{P_{L}} = \dfrac{1}{X_{T}} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{X_{i}}{P_{i}} $$

If all layers have the same thickness fraction $X_i / X_T$, this simplifies to:

$$ \boxed{\;\dfrac{1}{P_{L}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{x_{i}}{P_{i}} \quad \text{where } x_i = \dfrac{X_i}{X_T}\;} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is mathematically analogous to **electrical resistors in series** and **thermal conduction through composite walls**. The **overall barrier performance is dominated by the layer with the lowest permeability** — doubling the thickness of a poor barrier layer barely helps, but a thin EVOH layer can transform performance.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

### Question
A laminate has three layers: **PET (12 µm, OTR = 130 cm³·µm/m²·day·atm), Adhesive (3 µm, OTR = 4000), and LDPE (60 µm, OTR = 8000)**. Calculate the **overall laminate OTR**.

### Step 1 — Convert to Common Units

Total thickness:
$$ X_T = 12 + 3 + 60 = 75 \,\mu m $$

Thickness fractions:
$$ x_{PET} = \dfrac{12}{75} = 0.160 $$
$$ x_{Adh} = \dfrac{3}{75} = 0.040 $$
$$ x_{LDPE} = \dfrac{60}{75} = 0.800 $$

### Step 2 — Apply Series-Resistance Formula

$$ \dfrac{1}{P_{L}} = \dfrac{0.160}{130} + \dfrac{0.040}{4000} + \dfrac{0.800}{8000} $$

$$ \dfrac{1}{P_{L}} = 0.001231 + 0.0000100 + 0.000100 $$

$$ \dfrac{1}{P_{L}} = 0.001341 \;\dfrac{m^{2} \cdot day \cdot atm}{cm^{3} \cdot \mu m} $$

### Step 3 — Invert to Get $P_L$

$$ P_{L} = \dfrac{1}{0.001341} = 745.7 \;\dfrac{cm^{3} \cdot \mu m}{m^{2} \cdot day \cdot atm} $$

### Step 4 — Convert Permeance to OTR at Standard Conditions

For OTR at 1 atm driving pressure:

$$ OTR_{laminate} = \dfrac{P_{L}}{X_{T}} = \dfrac{745.7}{75} $$

$$ \boxed{\;OTR_{laminate} \approx 9.94 \;\dfrac{cm^{3}}{m^{2} \cdot day \cdot atm}\;} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Although the LDPE layer alone has OTR = 8000, the laminate achieves **OTR ≈ 10**, an **800× improvement**. This is exactly why multilayer barrier packaging is the industry standard for food, pharma, and sensitive electronics.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selecting Materials for Common Applications

| Application | Critical Requirement | Typical Laminate Structure (outside → inside) | Shelf Life Achieved |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Biscuits / Snacks** | Moisture barrier, print | BOPP (18) / Print / Adhesive / LDPE (30) | 6 – 9 months |
| **Milk Pouch** | Seal integrity, food contact | LDPE (60) monolayer | 1 – 3 days |
| **Vacuum Coffee** | Oxygen + aroma barrier | PET (12) / Al foil (7) / LDPE (50) | 12 – 18 months |
| **Retort Curry Pouch** | Heat resistance, O₂ barrier | PET (12) / Al (7) / BOPA (15) / CPP (60) | 1 – 2 years |
| **Fresh Meat MAP** | High O₂ transmission | BOPET (12) / EVOH (5) / LDPE (60) | 5 – 10 days |
| **Pharma Blister** | Moisture + light barrier | PVC (250) / PVDC (60 g/m²) | 2 – 5 years |
| **Stand-up Pouch (juice)** | Clarity, stiffness, seal | PET (12) / Print / Adhesive / LLDPE (80) | 6 – 12 months |
| **Hot-fill Spout Pouch** | Heat resistance | PET / Foil / PA / PP | 12 months |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 General Architecture of a Multilayer Flexible Laminate

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["OUTER LAYER: BOPET or BOPP - Print, Gloss, Scuff Resistance"]
    B["ADHESIVE LAYER: PU Adhesive or Extrusion Tie"]
    C["BARRIER LAYER: EVOH, PVDC, Al Foil, Metallized Film"]
    D["ADHESIVE OR TIE LAYER: Maleic Anhydride Grafted Polymer"]
    E["BULK LAYER: LLDPE or LDPE - Strength, Puncture Resistance"]
    F["SEALANT LAYER: LDPE, LLDPE, CPP, mLLDPE, Ionomer"]
    
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F
    F --> G["PRODUCT CONTACT SEAL"]
    
    style A fill:#2E86AB,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#A23B72,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#F18F01,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#A23B72,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#6A994E,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#386641,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#BC4749,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Reading from outside (print surface) to inside (product contact) follows industry convention. Each color represents a distinct functional category.

---

## 4.2 Flexible Packaging Manufacturing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["Polymer Resin Pellets"]
    R2["Adhesives, Inks, Solvents"]
    R3["Aluminum Foil / Paper"]
    
    R1 --> E1["Extrusion: Blown or Cast Film"]
    E1 --> W1["Winding: Master Rolls"]
    W1 --> S1["Surface Treatment: Corona or Flame"]
    S1 --> P1["Printing: Gravure or Flexo"]
    P1 --> L1["Lamination: Adhesive or Extrusion"]
    R2 --> L1
    R3 --> L1
    L1 --> C1["Curing Aging 24-48 h at 45 C"]
    C1 --> SL1["Slitting to Converter Width"]
    SL1 --> PKG["Form-Fill-Seal or Pouch Making"]
    PKG --> END["Final Pouch, Bag, Sachet, Wrap"]
    
    style E1 fill:#3D5A80,color:#ffffff
    style P1 fill:#E07A5F,color:#ffffff
    style L1 fill:#81B29A,color:#ffffff
    style PKG fill:#F2CC8F,color:#000000
    style END fill:#BC4749,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Selecting the Right Flexible Package

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Start: What product needs packaging?"]
    Q2{"Is the product moisture sensitive?"}
    Q3{"Does it need oxygen barrier above OTR 100?"}
    Q4{"Is retort sterilization above 121 C needed?"}
    Q5{"Is the product liquid or solid?"}
    
    L1["Use LDPE or LLDPE monolayer"]
    L2["Add WVTR barrier: BOPP, HDPE, metallized PET"]
    L3["Add EVOH or PVDC or metallized layer"]
    L4["Add aluminum foil 7-9 micron"]
    L5["Use PET Al PA CPP retort laminate"]
    L6["Use spouted pouch or pillow pouch"]
    L7["Use flow wrap or lidding film"]
    
    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 -->|No| L1
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3
    Q3 -->|No| L2
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4
    Q4 -->|No| L3
    Q4 -->|Yes| L4
    L4 --> Q5
    Q5 -->|Liquid| L6
    Q5 -->|Solid| L5
    L3 --> Q5
    
    style L1 fill:#A8DADC,color:#000000
    style L2 fill:#A8DADC,color:#000000
    style L3 fill:#A8DADC,color:#000000
    style L4 fill:#A8DADC,color:#000000
    style L5 fill:#A8DADC,color:#000000
    style L6 fill:#A8DADC,color:#000000
    style L7 fill:#A8DADC,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.4 Coextrusion vs. Lamination — Conceptual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COEX["COEXTRUSION PROCESS - Single Pass"]
        direction LR
        E1A["Extruder 1: Outer Polymer"]
        E2A["Extruder 2: Barrier Polymer"]
        E3A["Extruder 3: Sealant Polymer"]
        DIE["Feedblock or Multimanifold Die"]
        FB["Combined Melt Stream"]
        OUT1["Multilayer Film Output"]
        
        E1A --> DIE
        E2A --> DIE
        E3A --> DIE
        DIE --> FB --> OUT1
    end
    
    subgraph LAM["LAMINATION PROCESS - Sequential Pass"]
        direction LR
        F1["Film 1 Wound"]
        F2["Film 2 Wound"]
        AD["Adhesive Application"]
        NL["Nip Lamination"]
        OUT2["Bonded Laminate Output"]
        
        F1 --> AD --> NL
        F2 --> NL
        NL --> OUT2
    end
    
    style COEX fill:#E0FBFC,color:#000000
    style LAM fill:#FFE5D9,color:#000000
    style DIE fill:#9D8189,color:#ffffff
    style NL fill:#9D8189,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coextrusion** produces all layers simultaneously from molten polymers — excellent for tie-layer bonding and clean interfaces. **Lamination** joins pre-made films — preferred when one layer must be **aluminum foil, paper, or a pre-printed web**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define flexible packaging. List any four major forms of flexible packaging with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

Flexible packaging is defined (as per KTU syllabus PEPOT748) as a non-rigid package whose shape can be readily altered when filled, sealed, and handled, typically employing polymeric films, papers, foils, or their laminations with a total thickness below **250 µm**.

**Four major forms:**

| # | Form | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pouch | Stand-up zipper pouch for coffee |
| 2 | Sachet | Single-serve shampoo sachet (Combiflex) |
| 3 | Bag | T-shirt carry bag in supermarkets |
| 4 | Wrap | Cling film for fresh produce / pallet stretch film |

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + Four examples in correct form category (2).

---

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Explain the term "Barrier Property" in flexible packaging. Differentiate between WVTR and OTR.**

**Model Answer:**

**Barrier property** refers to the ability of a packaging material to **resist the transmission of gases, vapors, and aromas** through its structure. A high-barrier film restricts permeation; a low-barrier film permits it.

| Property | Symbol | Full Form | Unit | Key Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| WVTR | Water Vapor Transmission Rate | Moisture vapor | g/m²·day | Polymer polarity, crystallinity |
| OTR | Oxygen Transmission Rate | Oxygen gas | cm³/m²·day·atm | Polymer free volume, polarity |

> WVTR is measured under **90 % RH, 38 °C** (ASTM E96/E398); OTR is measured under **0 % RH, 23 °C** (ASTM D3985). EVOH and PVDC are excellent O₂ barriers; LDPE and PP are excellent **moisture** barriers but poor O₂ barriers.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + Tabular differentiation with units (2).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **blown film extrusion process** for manufacturing plastic films. With a neat diagram, explain the role of **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)** and **Draw-Down Ratio (DDR)** on the final film properties.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A laminate has three layers: **PET (12 µm) / EVOH (5 µm) / LLDPE (80 µm)**. The permeability coefficients are PET = **130**, EVOH = **2**, and LLDPE = **8000 cm³·µm/m²·day·atm**. Calculate the **overall laminate OTR**. Comment on the dominant barrier layer.

---

### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**1. Process Description (4 marks):**
- Polymer pellets are fed into an **extruder hopper**, melted by a rotating screw (with heating zones set at **180–230 °C** depending on polymer).
- The melt is forced through an **annular die** (circular ring-shaped die) oriented vertically upward.
- Compressed air is introduced through the die's center, **inflating the molten polymer tube** into a bubble.
- The bubble is **cooled by an air ring** surrounding it; a "**frost line**" forms where the polymer solidifies.
- The cooled bubble is **flattened** through collapsing frames and nip rolls.
- The flat tube (lay-flat) is **wound** onto rolls as **single-wound sheeting** or slit on edges to make **center-fold sheeting**.

**2. Diagram (2 marks):** [Students must draw: Hopper → Screw → Die → Bubble → Air Ring → Frost Line → Collapsing Frame → Nip Rolls → Windup]

**3. BUR and DDR Effects (1 mark):**

| Ratio | Definition | Effect on Film |
|---|---|---|
| **BUR = D_bubble / D_die** | Inflation ratio | BUR > 1 gives **balanced MD/TD orientation**, improves tear and impact |
| **DDR = t_die / t_film** | Thickness reduction | Higher DDR gives more **MD orientation**, higher tensile MD, lower TD tear |

**Common Range:** BUR = **2–4**; DDR = **10–30** for LLDPE.

---

### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Total Thickness:**
$$ X_T = 12 + 5 + 80 = 97 \,\mu m $$

**Step 2 — Thickness Fractions:**
$$ x_{PET} = \dfrac{12}{97} = 0.1237 $$
$$ x_{EVOH} = \dfrac{5}{97} = 0.0515 $$
$$ x_{LLDPE} = \dfrac{80}{97} = 0.8247 $$

**Step 3 — Series Resistance Model:**
$$ \dfrac{1}{P_L} = \dfrac{0.1237}{130} + \dfrac{0.0515}{2} + \dfrac{0.8247}{8000} $$

$$ \dfrac{1}{P_L} = 0.000952 + 0.025770 + 0.000103 $$

$$ \dfrac{1}{P_L} = 0.026825 \;\dfrac{m^{2} \cdot day \cdot atm}{cm^{3} \cdot \mu m} $$

**Step 4 — Invert:**
$$ P_L = \dfrac{1}{0.026825} = 37.28 \;\dfrac{cm^{3} \cdot \mu m}{m^{2} \cdot day \cdot atm} $$

**Step 5 — Laminate OTR:**
$$ OTR_{laminate} = \dfrac{P_L}{X_T} = \dfrac{37.28}{97} $$

$$ \boxed{\;OTR_{laminate} \approx 0.384 \;\dfrac{cm^{3}}{m^{2} \cdot day \cdot atm}\;} $$

**Step 6 — Comment on Dominant Layer:**
The term $\dfrac{0.0515}{2} = 0.0258$ accounts for **96 %** of the total resistance. Therefore, **EVOH (only 5 µm thick) is the dominant barrier layer**. Even though EVOH is just **5 %** of total thickness, it controls the OTR. This explains why **microscopic EVOH layers dominate modern high-barrier food packaging**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mixing Error:** Many students use $\rho$ in g/cm³ but thickness in µm — always convert thickness to cm when using permeability in cm³·cm units. KTU strictly penalizes inconsistent units.
> 2. **Inverting the Formula:** A frequent mistake is computing $P_L$ as a sum rather than reciprocal sum. **Series resistances add as reciprocals, not directly.**
> 3. **Omitting the Comment:** KTU awards the final 1 mark only when the student identifies **which layer dominates** and why. Numerical answer alone = 6 marks; numerical + comment = 7 marks.
> 4. **Process Diagram:** Drawing only boxes without labeling the **frost line, air ring, collapsing frame** loses 1 mark under "neat diagram" requirement.

---

### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Blown Film Extrusion** and **Cast Film Extrusion** in terms of process, optical properties, mechanical balance, and typical polymers used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **role of EVOH and PVDC** as barrier polymers in flexible packaging. Why are they always used in **multilayer structures** and never as monolayers? Support your answer with a relevant property table.

---

### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

| Parameter | Blown Film | Cast Film |
|---|---|---|
| Die Type | Annular (circular) | Slot (flat) |
| Cooling | Air (gradual) | Chill roll (rapid) |
| Orientation | Biaxial (MD + TD) | Mostly MD |
| Clarity / Gloss | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| Mechanical Balance | **Balanced MD/TD** | MD-dominant, weak TD |
| Production Rate | Slower | **Higher (up to 500 m/min)** |
| Polymers | LDPE, LLDPE, HDPE blends | CPP, PET, PA, CPE |
| Thickness Range | 10–200 µm | 5–250 µm |

**Marks Split:** Process comparison (3) + Properties (2) + Polymers (1) + Conclusion (1).

---

### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**EVOH (Ethylene-Vinyl Alcohol):**
- Excellent **oxygen barrier** when dry (OTR as low as **0.5 cm³/m²·day·atm**).
- Molecular structure has **–OH groups** that hydrogen-bond strongly with O₂.
- **Major drawback:** Highly hydrophilic — barrier degrades catastrophically at RH > 70 %.

**PVDC (Polyvinylidene Chloride):**
- Excellent barrier to **both O₂ and moisture vapor** simultaneously (unique property).
- Resistant to oils, grease, chemicals.
- Chlorine-containing; generates HCl on incineration — environmental concern.

**Why Always Multilayer?**
1. **Both polymers are mechanically weak and expensive** — must be sandwiched between structural layers.
2. **EVOH must be protected from moisture** by hydrophobic outer layers (PE, PP).
3. **PVDC requires primer or coating equipment** — typically coated onto BOPP, PVC, or PET substrates.
4. **Regulatory & cost reasons** — a typical EVOH layer is only **3–10 µm**; total cost stays low.

**Property Table (supporting the answer):**

| Property | EVOH | PVDC | LDPE (compare) |
|---|---|---|---|
| OTR (cm³/m²·day·atm) | 0.5 – 5 | 5 – 25 | 7,000 |
| WVTR (g/m²·day) | 30 – 100 | 1 – 5 | 12 |
| Tensile (MPa) | 70 | 60 | 17 |
| Heat Sealability | None | Good | Excellent |

**Marks Split:** EVOH explanation (2) + PVDC explanation (2) + Multilayer justification (2) + Property table (1).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> 1. **"Always multilayer" is a trick question.** Students often write that EVOH is used because of "cost reasons" alone. The **humidity sensitivity of EVOH** is the *primary* technical reason; mark loss if omitted.
> 2. **PVDC barrier uniqueness** — PVDC is one of the few polymers with **both** low WVTR and low OTR. Students often confuse this with PVC (which is *not* a good barrier).
> 3. **Comparing LDPE in the table** is mandatory for context — KTU expects reference polymers for comparison.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Film and Flexible Packaging (Module 3)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Film = substrate (≤ 250 µm); Flexible packaging = engineered package using films/foils/papers/laminates.
- ✅ **Five Functions:** Containment, Protection, Preservation, Presentation, Performance.
- ✅ **Processes:** Blown (bubble), Cast (chill roll), Coextrusion (multi-melt), Lamination (sequential), Metallization (vacuum Al vapor).
- ✅ **Key Formulae:** Yield, BUR, DDR, Lay-Flat Width, Series-resistance permeance.
- ✅ **Barrier Hierarchy:** Al Foil > EVOH > PVDC > BOPET > BOPA > BOPP > LDPE > PVC.
- ✅ **EVOH weakness:** humidity — must be encapsulated between hydrophobic layers.
- ✅ **PVDC uniqueness:** Best combined O₂ + moisture barrier; environmental concern with chlorine.
- ✅ **Laminate construction order (outside → inside):** Print / Strength / Barrier / Bulk / Sealant.
- ✅ **Heat Seal Strength thresholds:** Light ≥ **1.5 N/15 mm**; Heavy ≥ **8 N/15 mm** (ASTM F88).
- ✅ **Conversion factors:** 1 mil = **25.4 µm**; 1 m² = **10.76 ft²**; 1 atm = **101.325 kPa**.
- ✅ **Testing Standards to remember:** WVTR — ASTM E96 / F1249; OTR — ASTM D3985; Seal — ASTM F88; Tensile — ASTM D882; COF — ASTM D1894; Haze — ASTM D1003.
- ✅ **Sustainability buzzwords (frequent in KTU viva):** Mono-material PE, recycle-ready PP, paper-based barrier, bio-PLA, chemical recycling.
- ✅ **Common viva question:** *"Why is aluminum foil only 7 µm and not thicker in laminates?"* — Pinholes form above **15 µm** due to foil brittleness; **7 µm** is the optimal pinhole-free grade.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extrusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Extrusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Extrusion** is a continuous, high-volume thermo-mechanical polymer processing operation in which a thermoplastic material (in the form of pellets, granules, or powder) is **plasticated, melted, homogenized, and continuously forced under high pressure through a shaped die** to produce a continuous profile of uniform cross-section. The solidified (or still molten) extrudate is then cooled, sized, hauled off, and cut (or wound) into the desired packaging form such as films, sheets, pipes, profiles, blown bottles, or coatings.

In the context of **Plastics Packaging Technology (PEPOT748)**, extrusion is the **primary conversion process** used to manufacture the vast majority of flexible and rigid packaging structures — including **LDPE/LLDPE/HDPE films, multilayer co-extruded barrier films, PP/PS sheets, drinking straws, tubes, and parisons for blow moulding**.

The single most important piece of equipment is the **Screw Extruder**, which performs the dual functions of:
1. **Plastics processing** (melting, mixing, conveying) — analogous to a heated positive-displacement pump.
2. **Pressure generation** — to push the homogeneous melt through the downstream die assembly.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Giant Heated Meat Mincer with a Shaped Nozzle**
>
> Imagine pushing a block of cold butter through a garlic press. Hard work, messy, and the butter comes out chunky. Now replace the butter with **softened clay**, push it through a **hollow metal tube wrapped in a heater**, and use a **rotating Archimedes screw** (like a corkscrew) inside the tube to drag the clay forward while heating it. Out of the other end, attach a **shaped disc (the die)** — a slit, a ring, a star — and the softened clay exits as a continuous ribbon, tube, or star-shaped profile that you can cut into packaging components.
>
> The extruder is exactly this: a **heated barrel + a rotating screw + a shaping die**. Plastics pellets go in at the **hopper**, the screw drags, melts, mixes, and pressurises them, and the die shapes them into the continuous packaging form (film, sheet, tube, coating).

## 1.3 Classification of Extruders (At a Glance)

| Type | Drive / Mechanism | Typical Use in Packaging |
|------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| **Single-Screw Extruder (SSE)** | One flighted screw in a heated barrel | Films, sheets, pipes, blow moulding parisons, profiles |
| **Counter-Rotating Twin-Screw Extruder (CR-TSE)** | Two intermeshing screws, rotating in opposite directions | Compounding, rigid PVC, scrap recycling |
| **Co-Rotating Twin-Screw Extruder (CO-TSE)** | Two intermeshing screws, rotating in the same direction | Compounding, masterbatch, co-extrusion feed |
| **Ram Extruder** | Plunger (no screw) | PTFE (Teflon), ultra-high-viscosity polymers, UHMWPE |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the KTU 2024 syllabus, the **Single-Screw Extruder** is the **most heavily tested** because it is the workhorse of the packaging industry. The remaining notes are therefore built around this machine.

## 1.4 Why Extrusion Dominates Packaging Manufacturing

- **Continuous** → high productivity, low unit cost.
- **Versatile** → one machine can produce films, sheets, tubes, coatings, profiles, and parisons simply by **changing the die**.
- **Compatible with multi-layer co-extrusion** → essential for barrier packaging (e.g., EVOH / PA / PE).
- **Easily coupled** with downstream units (calender, chill-roll, blown-film tower, corrugator, thermoformer).
- **Wastes** (edge trim, regrind) can be reprocessed at the feed throat — high material economy.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Single-Screw Extruder

A single-screw extruder, although visually a long horizontal cylinder, is a **precisely engineered multi-zone reactor**. Reading the machine from upstream (feed) to downstream (die), the polymer encounters the following subsystems:

1. **Feed Hopper** — gravity-fed reservoir of solid pellets. Often equipped with a **crammer feeder** for high-throughput lines.
2. **Barrel** — a thick-walled, bimetallic (e.g., nitrided steel inner liner) cylinder, externally wrapped in **band heaters** (typically 3 to 5 zones, each independently PID-controlled). Internal bore diameter is **D** (the **barrel inner diameter**).
3. **Screw** — a long, hardened steel rotor with a helical flight. Outer root diameter **D**, inner flight-tip diameter **D_o**, helix angle **θ**, channel depth **H**, channel width **W**, flight width **e**.
4. **Screw Tip / Breaker Plate & Screen Pack** — at the discharge end. A perforated plate supporting a **wire-mesh screen pack** (typically 20/40/60/80 mesh) acts as a **melt filter**, removes unmelted particles, and converts **rotational (drag) flow into axial (pressure) flow** by eliminating the swirling vortex.
5. **Adapter & Die Assembly** — shapes the molten extrudate. The die land length **L_d** provides the final pressure drop.
6. **Downstream Equipment** — chill rolls, air rings (blown film), calibrators, haul-off, cutter/winder.
7. **Drive & Thrust Bearing Assembly** — typically a thyristor/DC/AC variable-speed motor coupled through a gear reducer to the screw; rated for the **screw thrust load** generated by the internal pressure.

## 2.2 The Three Functional Zones of the Screw

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The screw is **not uniform**. Its **channel depth (H) and pitch (and therefore compression ratio)** change along its length to perform three sequential jobs. For a typical L/D = 24:1 to 30:1 screw used in film/sheet extrusion:

| Zone | Function | Length (approx.) | Channel Depth (H) | Mechanism |
|------|----------|------------------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Feed Zone (Solids Conveying)** | Compacts and conveys cold pellets | 25 – 30 % of L | Deepest, constant (~0.15 D for thermoplastics) | Friction between pellets, barrel wall, and screw root |
| **Compression / Transition Zone (Melting)** | Melts the compacted bed, mixes melt with residual solids | 30 – 40 % of L | Tapers down (e.g., 0.15 D → 0.05 D) | Combination of barrel heating + **shear heating** (viscous dissipation) |
| **Metering Zone (Melt Conveying / Pumping)** | Homogenises melt and pumps it at a steady rate | 25 – 35 % of L | Shallowest, constant (~0.05 D for thermoplastics) | **Drag flow** of a viscous fluid in a rectangular channel |

**Compression Ratio (CR)** is defined as:

$$
\text{CR} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Volume of one flight in the feed zone}}{\text{Volume of one flight in the metering zone}} \;=\; \dfrac{H_f \, W_f}{H_m \, W_m}
$$

Typical values: **2.5 : 1 to 3.0 : 1** for polyolefins; **1.5 : 1 to 2.0 : 1** for PVC and other heat-sensitive polymers.

## 2.3 The Three Components of Extruder Output

The volumetric output rate **Q** (m³/s) of a melt-pumping (metering) extruder is the **algebraic sum of three independent flow contributions**. This is the **single most important equation in extrusion theory** for KTU:

$$
Q \;=\; Q_d \;-\; Q_p \;-\; Q_f
$$

where:

- **Q_d** = **Drag Flow** — the forward pumping caused by the relative motion of the screw flight over the stationary barrel wall (the "Archimedes pump" component).
- **Q_p** = **Pressure Flow** — the *backward* leak driven by the pressure gradient that the extruder itself builds up at the die. It always **subtracts** from the output.
- **Q_f** = **Leakage (Flight) Flow** — the small back-flow through the *clearance* between the screw flight tip and the barrel wall. Usually **small (≤ 5 % of Q_d)** and often neglected in KTU problems.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** *"D minus P minus F — what drags forward, what pressures back, and what slips through the flights."*

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the following expressions — they appear in almost every KTU ESE question on extrusion.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Drag flow rate | $Q_d \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^{2}\,D^{2}\,N\,d\,\sin\theta\cos\theta$ | m³/s | $D$ = barrel ID, $d$ = channel depth (= $H_m$ in metering zone), $N$ = screw rpm, $\theta$ = helix angle. |
| 2 | Drag flow — simplified (using $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta - e$) | $Q_d \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^{2}\,D^{2}\,N\,d\,\sin\theta\cos\theta$ | m³/s | Identical to (1); often written with channel width $W$ explicitly: $Q_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N d\sin\theta\cos\theta$. |
| 3 | Pressure flow (Newtonian melt) | $Q_p \;=\; \dfrac{\pi\,D\,d^{3}\,\sin^{2}\theta}{12\,\eta\,L_m}\,\Delta P$ | m³/s | $L_m$ = axial length of metering zone; $\eta$ = melt viscosity. |
| 4 | Net output | $Q \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N d\sin\theta\cos\theta \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}{12 \eta L_m}\Delta P$ | m³/s | Ignoring flight leakage $Q_f$. |
| 5 | Throughput (mass) | $\dot{m} \;=\; \rho_{melt}\,Q$ | kg/s | $\rho_{melt}$ ≈ 0.74 – 0.97 g/cm³ for polyolefins. |
| 6 | Specific energy | $E_{sp} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Input power}}{\dot{m}}$ | J/kg | Typical range: $3 \times 10^{6}$ – $6 \times 10^{6}$ J/kg for LDPE film. |
| 7 | Compression ratio | $\text{CR} \;=\; H_f / H_m$ | – | For uniform $W$. |
| 8 | Extruder characterisation line | $\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{12\,\eta\,L_m}{\pi\,D\,d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}\,\Bigl(\tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N d\sin\theta\cos\theta - Q\Bigr)$ | Pa | Useful for die / pump matching. |
| 9 | Shear rate in metering channel (approx.) | $\dot{\gamma} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D N}{d - \delta}$ (where $\delta$ is flight clearance) | s⁻¹ | $10^{2}$ – $10^{3}$ s⁻¹ typical. |
| 10 | Die-swell (extrudate swell) ratio | $B \;=\; D_{extrudate} / D_{die}$ | – | 1.10 – 1.50 for polyolefins; due to elastic recoil. |
| 11 | Mean residence time | $\bar{t} \;=\; V_{free} / Q$ | s | $V_{free}$ = free volume in the metering channel. |
| 12 | Power required to drive the screw (approx.) | $P \;=\; \tau_{avg} \,\pi D L N$ | W | Used for motor sizing. |

> [!NOTE]
> In all the above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ (absolute value) notation has been written as `vert` or `mid` to keep the markdown table parser safe — but when writing the equations inline in the KTU answer sheet, use standard $\vert$ symbols.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Equations

- **Sizing a new extruder**: Knowing the required throughput (kg/h) of an LDPE packaging film line, the manufacturer solves Equation (4) for a feasible $D$, $N$ pair, then selects a screw with the matching compression ratio.
- **Troubleshooting a fluctuating output**: A drifting melt temperature changes $\eta$, which in turn changes $Q_p$ and hence $Q$. The line operator detects the surge and tightens the heater band tuning.
- **Die-swell compensation**: In tubing/straw extrusion, the die is intentionally **undercut** by the inverse of the swell ratio $B$ to deliver a finished product of correct diameter.
- **Energy audit**: The specific energy $E_{sp}$ quantifies how much electrical energy is consumed per kg of packaging produced — a key KPI in sustainable packaging.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Net Output Equation $Q = Q_d - Q_p$

We model the melt inside the metering-zone channel as a **Newtonian fluid** in steady, laminar, fully-developed flow. We then "unwind" the helical channel and analyse flow between two parallel plates:

- **Lower plate** (the screw root) moves with tangential velocity $\pi D N$ at helix angle $\theta$ relative to the channel axis.
- **Upper plate** (the barrel wall) is **stationary**.

This geometric *unwinding* converts the helical coordinate system into a flat Cartesian channel of width $W$ and depth $d$ (channel depth = $H_m$).

### Step 1 — Decompose the barrel velocity

The barrel's tangential surface velocity $V_b = \pi D N$ has two components relative to the channel axis (the down-channel direction, $z$):

- **Down-channel component** (drives the fluid forward): $V_{bz} \;=\; \pi D N \cos\theta$
- **Cross-channel component** (causes circulatory secondary flow, ignored in 1-D theory): $V_{bx} \;=\; \pi D N \sin\theta$

### Step 2 — Drag flow from Couette-Poiseuille superposition

The volumetric flow per unit width in a rectangular channel of depth $d$ with relative plate motion $V_{bz}$ and a pressure gradient $dP/dz$ is a standard result of lubrication theory:

$$
q(z) \;=\; \underbrace{\dfrac{V_{bz}\,d}{2}}_{\text{Couette (drag) component}} \;-\; \underbrace{\dfrac{d^{3}}{12\,\eta}\,\dfrac{dP}{dz}}_{\text{Poiseuille (pressure) component}}
$$

> **Couette part** — pure shear of a Newtonian fluid between a moving and a stationary plate; the velocity profile is linear, so the average velocity is half the plate velocity, multiplied by the channel depth.

> **Poiseuille part** — a pressure gradient $dP/dz < 0$ drives a parabolic (back-flow) profile; the volumetric flow is the well-known result $-(d^3/12\eta)(dP/dz)$.

### Step 3 — Integrate over the channel width

The total volumetric flow is $Q = W \cdot q(z)$, where $W$ is the channel width measured perpendicular to the flight:

$$
Q \;=\; \dfrac{V_{bz}\,W\,d}{2} \;-\; \dfrac{W\,d^{3}}{12\,\eta}\,\dfrac{dP}{dz}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $V_{bz} = \pi D N \cos\theta$ and $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta - e$

The width of the helical channel (perpendicular to the flights) is $W \;\approx\; \pi D \sin\theta - e$. For simplicity in KTU derivations, the flight width $e$ is **neglected**, giving $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta$. Substituting:

$$
Q \;=\; \dfrac{(\pi D N \cos\theta)(\pi D \sin\theta)\,d}{2} \;-\; \dfrac{(\pi D \sin\theta)\,d^{3}}{12\,\eta}\,\dfrac{dP}{dz}
$$

Simplify the trigonometric product $\sin\theta \cos\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\theta$:

$$
Q \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N\,d\,\sin\theta\cos\theta \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D d^{3}\sin\theta}{12\,\eta}\,\dfrac{dP}{dz}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert the axial pressure gradient to a finite pressure difference

The pressure at the start of the metering zone is approximately atmospheric, and at its end it rises to the head pressure $\Delta P$. Assuming a **linear pressure rise** over the axial length of the metering zone $L_m$:

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dz} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{L_m}
$$

Substitute this into the expression for $Q$:

$$
Q \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N\,d\,\sin\theta\cos\theta \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}{12\,\eta\,L_m}\,\Delta P
$$

### Step 6 — Identify the two terms as $Q_d$ and $Q_p$

This is exactly the expression listed in the formula sheet:

$$
\boxed{\,Q \;=\; \underbrace{\dfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N\,d\,\sin\theta\cos\theta}_{Q_d\,\text{(drag flow, forward)}} \;-\; \underbrace{\dfrac{\pi D d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}{12\,\eta\,L_m}\,\Delta P}_{Q_p\,\text{(pressure flow, backward)}}\,}
$$

If the flight clearance leakage $Q_f$ is included:

$$
Q \;=\; Q_d \;-\; Q_p \;-\; Q_f
$$

### Step 3.2 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A single-screw extruder used for LDPE packaging film has the following data. Calculate the volumetric output $Q$ and the mass throughput $\dot{m}$.
>
> - Barrel inner diameter: $D = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
> - Metering channel depth: $d = 3.0 \text{ mm} = 0.003 \text{ m}$
> - Helix angle: $\theta = 17.65^{\circ}$ (so $\sin\theta = 0.303$, $\cos\theta = 0.953$)
> - Screw speed: $N = 60 \text{ rpm} = 1.0 \text{ rev/s}$
> - Axial length of metering zone: $L_m = 0.75 \text{ m}$
> - Pressure developed at die: $\Delta P = 7 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$
> - Melt viscosity of LDPE at processing temperature: $\eta = 350 \text{ Pa·s}$
> - Density of molten LDPE: $\rho_{melt} = 780 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$

**Step 1 — Compute $Q_d$:**

$$
Q_d \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^{2}\,(0.075)^{2}\,(1.0)\,(0.003)\,(0.303)(0.953)
$$

Compute the coefficient $\tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2} \approx 4.9348$:

$$
Q_d \;=\; 4.9348 \times (0.005625) \times (0.003) \times 0.2887
$$

$$
Q_d \;=\; 4.9348 \times 0.005625 \times 0.000866
$$

$$
Q_d \;\approx\; 2.41 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $Q_p$:**

$$
Q_p \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (0.075)(0.003)^{3}\,(0.303)^{2}}{12\,(350)\,(0.75)}\;\times\;(7 \times 10^{6})
$$

Numerator:

$$
\pi \times 0.075 \times 2.7\times10^{-8} \times 0.0918 \times 7\times10^{6}
$$

$$
\;\approx\; 3.1416 \times 0.075 \times 2.7\times10^{-8} \times 0.0918 \times 7\times10^{6}
$$

$$
\;\approx\; 4.36 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

Denominator:

$$
12 \times 350 \times 0.75 \;=\; 3150
$$

So:

$$
Q_p \;=\; \dfrac{4.36 \times 10^{-6}}{3150} \;\approx\; 1.38 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Net output (ignoring $Q_f$):**

$$
Q \;=\; Q_d - Q_p \;=\; 2.41 \times 10^{-5} - 1.38 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
Q \;\approx\; 2.27 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass throughput:**

$$
\dot{m} \;=\; \rho_{melt}\,Q \;=\; 780 \times 2.27 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\dot{m} \;\approx\; 1.77 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/s} \;\approx\; 63.7 \text{ kg/h}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** A 63.7 kg/h net throughput is realistic for a 75 mm extruder running at 60 rpm on LDPE film — KTU board examiners love this scale of answer because it confirms that the student understood the magnitudes involved.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Optimal Helix Angle $\theta = 20°$ to $30°$

The drag flow expression $Q_d \propto \sin\theta\cos\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\theta$ is **maximised** when $\sin 2\theta = 1$, i.e., $2\theta = 90°$, $\theta = 45°$. However, at $\theta = 45°$ the pressure-flow term also **grows rapidly** (proportional to $\sin^2\theta$), the screw becomes a *poor pump*, and the residence time distribution becomes unfavourable. In practice, the **optimum compromise** between high drag flow, reasonable pressure-generating ability, and good solids conveying is:

$$
\theta_{opt} \;\approx\; 20^{\circ} \text{ to } 30^{\circ}
$$

For LDPE/PP, screws are commonly cut at $\theta = 17.65°$ (which is the angle for a square-pitched flight where the pitch equals $D$, and $\tan\theta = 1/\pi$). The KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to be able to state the **optimum range** and justify it qualitatively.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Single-Screw Extrusion Line

The following Mermaid block renders the upstream → downstream flow of material and information, which is the typical "block diagram" KTU expects in 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hopper with Solid Pellets] --> B[Feed Throat and Gravity Feeder]
    B --> C[Feed Zone of Screw - Solids Conveying]
    C --> D[Compression Zone - Melting by Shear and Conduction]
    D --> E[Metering Zone - Melt Pumping and Mixing]
    E --> F[Breaker Plate and Screen Pack - Melt Filter]
    F --> G[Adapter and Shaped Die]
    G --> H[Haul Off and Cooling]
    H --> I[Take Up Winder or Cutter]
    H2[Thrust Bearing] --> E
    J[PID Heater Bands Zone 1] --> C
    K[PID Heater Bands Zone 2] --> D
    L[PID Heater Bands Zone 3] --> E
    M[Drive Motor and Gearbox] --> C
    M2[Pressure Transducer] --- G
    M2 --> N[Controller]
    N --> J
    N --> K
    N --> L
    N --> M
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **upper horizontal chain (A → I)** is the **material path** — polymer pellets in, finished packaging out.
- The **lower chain (J, K, L, M)** is the **energy and control path** — heaters and motor that drive the process.
- The **M2 → N** path is the **feedback loop** — the die-pressure transducer signals the controller, which modulates screw speed and heater powers to keep output stable.

## 4.2 Screw Geometry — Functional Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    scRoot[Screw Root - Solid Steel Shaft]
    scFlight1[Feed Zone Flights - Deep Channel - Constant Pitch]
    scFlight2[Compression Zone Flights - Tapered Channel - Decreasing Depth]
    scFlight3[Metering Zone Flights - Shallow Channel - Constant Pitch]
    scTip[Screw Tip and Drive Dog]
    scFlight1 --> scFlight2
    scFlight2 --> scFlight3
    scFlight3 --> scTip
```

**Annotations (key dimensions):**

- **Helix angle $\theta$** — measured between the flight edge and the plane perpendicular to the screw axis.
- **Channel depth $d$** — perpendicular distance from the screw root to the flight tip.
- **Channel width $W$** — measured perpendicular to the flight land.
- **Pitch $p$** — axial distance between two consecutive flights of the same helix hand; for a square-pitched screw $p = D$.
- **L/D ratio** — the *total* screw length divided by the barrel inner diameter. **L/D = 24:1** is standard for film; **L/D = 30:1** is used for compounding and difficult-to-melt resins.

## 4.3 Co-extrusion Feed Block (Block Diagram for Multilayer Packaging Films)

For high-barrier food packaging films, multiple extruders feed a common **co-extrusion die** through a **feed block** that stacks the melt layers in the desired order (e.g., PE / tie / EVOH / tie / PE).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    extA[Extruder A - Outer PE Layer] --> fb[Feed Block with Layer Sequencer]
    extB[Extruder B - Tie Resin Layer] --> fb
    extC[Extruder C - EVOH Barrier Layer] --> fb
    extD[Extruder D - Inner PE Sealant Layer] --> fb
    fb --> cd[Co-extrusion Die - Slit or Annular]
    cd --> bft[Blown Film Tower or Cast Chill Roll]
    bft --> wn[Winder and Slitter]
```

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 examiners frequently include a **labelled block diagram** (typically for 4 marks within a 14-mark question). Mastery of the above block diagrams will earn full marks.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]** 
Define the term **"compression ratio"** of an extruder screw. What is the typical range used for polyolefins?

**Model Answer:**

The compression ratio (CR) of an extruder screw is defined as the ratio of the **channel volume per flight in the feed zone** to the **channel volume per flight in the metering zone**:

$$
\text{CR} \;=\; \dfrac{H_f \times W_f}{H_m \times W_m}
$$

For a screw with uniform channel width ($W_f = W_m$), this simplifies to $\text{CR} = H_f/H_m$. The compression ratio quantifies the **volumetric compaction** the solid polymer bed undergoes as it travels from the feed throat to the metering zone, ensuring complete melting and air removal.

**Typical range for polyolefins (LDPE, LLDPE, HDPE, PP):** **CR = 2.5 : 1 to 3.0 : 1**.

For heat-sensitive polymers such as rigid PVC, the range is lower (**1.5 : 1 to 2.0 : 1**) to avoid shear-induced degradation. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary]** 
Distinguish between **drag flow** and **pressure flow** in the metering zone of a single-screw extruder. What is the algebraic sign of each term in the net output equation?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Drag Flow ($Q_d$) | Pressure Flow ($Q_p$) |
|---------|--------------------|------------------------|
| **Cause** | Relative motion between the rotating screw flight and the stationary barrel wall | Pressure gradient $\Delta P$ built up at the die |
| **Direction** | **Down-channel (forward, positive)** | **Up-channel (backward, negative)** |
| **Dependence** | $Q_d \propto N, D^{2}, d, \sin\theta\cos\theta$ | $Q_p \propto \Delta P, d^{3}, \sin^{2}\theta / \eta$ |
| **Sign in $Q = Q_d - Q_p - Q_f$** | **Plus (+)** | **Minus (−)** |

The **drag flow** is the *desired* pumping action, while the **pressure flow** represents the *back-leak* caused by the very pressure the extruder generates to push melt through the die. The net output is the algebraic difference. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled block diagram, describe the construction and working of a **single-screw extruder** used in plastics packaging. Explain the functions of the three functional zones of the screw. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from first principles, derive the expression for the **net volumetric output** of a single-screw extruder operating in the metering zone. State clearly the assumptions made. A 60 mm extruder running at 80 rpm with a metering depth of 2.5 mm and helix angle $17.65°$ produces an output of $6.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s. The metering length is 0.6 m, melt viscosity 400 Pa·s. Calculate the **die pressure $\Delta P$** developed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

The single-screw extruder consists of:

1. **Hopper** — gravity feeds the polymer pellets into the barrel.
2. **Barrel** — externally heated by **3 to 5 PID-controlled heater bands**; bore = $D$ (the reference diameter).
3. **Screw** — a helical flight on a steel shaft. The **three zones** and their functions are:
   - **Feed Zone (Solids Conveying)** — the deep, constant-pitch flights drag pellets forward by **frictional drag** with the barrel wall. Compaction of the loose bed occurs here.
   - **Compression / Transition Zone (Melting)** — the channel depth **tapers down**, generating intense **shear** and **frictional (viscous) heating** that melts the polymer. A thin melt film forms against the barrel wall.
   - **Metering Zone (Melt Pumping)** — the shallow, constant-depth channel acts as a **positive-displacement pump**. Homogeneous melt is delivered at a steady rate and pressure to the die.
4. **Breaker Plate and Screen Pack** — supported by the breaker plate, it filters contaminants and converts swirling rotational flow into straight axial flow.
5. **Die** — shapes the extrudate (slit → film/sheet; annular → tube/blown film; profile → straw).
6. **Drive, Gearbox, Thrust Bearing** — supplies torque and absorbs the axial reaction force.

A **labelled block diagram** (as drawn in Section 4.1) accompanies the description in the answer booklet. **(7 marks)**

**(b) — Derivation and Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Derivation (5 marks of the 7):**

1. Unwind the helical channel of width $W$ and depth $d$ into a flat rectangular geometry between a moving plate (screw root, velocity $V_b = \pi D N$) at angle $\theta$ and a stationary plate (barrel wall).
2. Decompose the plate velocity into **down-channel** $V_{bz} = \pi D N \cos\theta$ and **cross-channel** $V_{bx} = \pi D N \sin\theta$ components.
3. The 1-D flow in the $z$-direction (Couette + Poiseuille superposition) gives per unit width:

$$
q(z) \;=\; \dfrac{V_{bz}\,d}{2} \;-\; \dfrac{d^{3}}{12\,\eta}\,\dfrac{dP}{dz}
$$

4. Multiply by channel width $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta$:

$$
Q \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N d \sin\theta\cos\theta \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}{12\eta}\dfrac{dP}{dz}
$$

5. With linear pressure rise $dP/dz = \Delta P/L_m$ over the metering length $L_m$:

$$
\boxed{\,Q \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N d \sin\theta\cos\theta \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}{12\eta L_m}\Delta P\,}
$$

**Assumptions (1 mark):** Newtonian melt; steady, laminar, fully-developed flow; incompressible; negligible flight leakage; $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta$ (flight width $e$ ignored); negligible cross-channel flow contribution; $e$ (flight width) and screw clearance $\delta$ are small.

**Numerical Calculation (1 mark):**

Given: $D = 0.060$ m, $N = 80/60 = 1.333$ rev/s, $d = 0.0025$ m, $\theta = 17.65°$ ⇒ $\sin\theta = 0.303$, $\cos\theta = 0.953$, $L_m = 0.6$ m, $\eta = 400$ Pa·s, $Q = 6.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s.

First compute $Q_d$:

$$
Q_d \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}(0.060)^{2}(1.333)(0.0025)(0.303)(0.953)
$$

$$
Q_d \;\approx\; 4.9348 \times 3.6\times10^{-3} \times 1.333 \times 0.0025 \times 0.2887
$$

$$
Q_d \;\approx\; 1.71 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

From $Q = Q_d - Q_p$, solve for $Q_p$:

$$
Q_p \;=\; Q_d - Q \;=\; 1.71 \times 10^{-5} - 6.5 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 1.06 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

Now solve for $\Delta P$ from the $Q_p$ expression:

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{12\,\eta\,L_m\,Q_p}{\pi D d^{3}\sin^{2}\theta}
$$

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{12 \times 400 \times 0.6 \times 1.06\times10^{-5}}{\pi \times 0.060 \times (0.0025)^{3} \times (0.303)^{2}}
$$

Numerator $\approx 3.05 \times 10^{-2}$.
Denominator $\approx 0.1885 \times 1.5625\times10^{-8} \times 0.0918 \approx 2.70 \times 10^{-10}$.

$$
\Delta P \;\approx\; \dfrac{3.05\times 10^{-2}}{2.70 \times 10^{-10}} \;\approx\; 1.13 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa}
$$

**Result:** $\Delta P \approx 1.13 \times 10^{8}$ Pa $\approx 113$ MPa. (Note: a typical LDPE die pressure is 7 – 35 MPa; the higher value here reflects the very small $Q$ relative to the screw's drag capacity, i.e., the die is much too restrictive for this screw speed.)

**(Total = 7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **extrudate swell (die swell)** in polymer extrusion. Discuss the factors that influence it and its significance in the design of **packaging tubing and blown films**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two different extruders are used to process **LDPE film**. Extruder X has $D = 90$ mm, $d = 4$ mm, $\theta = 17.65°$, $N = 50$ rpm. Extruder Y has $D = 60$ mm, $d = 3$ mm, $\theta = 20°$, $N = 90$ rpm. Ignoring pressure flow, determine which extruder has the higher **theoretical drag-flow capacity**, and by what factor. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Die Swell (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Die swell (also called **extrudate swell** or the **Barus effect**) is the phenomenon in which the cross-sectional dimensions of an extrudate emerging from a die are **larger** than the corresponding die dimensions. Quantitatively, the swell ratio is

$$
B \;=\; \dfrac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}}
$$

For polyolefins, $B$ typically lies in the range **1.10 – 1.50**.

**Mechanism (2 marks):** When polymer melt flows through a converging die entry, it experiences both **shear** and **elongational** deformation. Polymer chains become **oriented and stretched** in the flow direction. Upon exit, the elastic (recoverable) component of this strain is released, causing the chains to recoil toward their coiled equilibrium state. The macroscopic result is a **radial expansion** of the extrudate.

**Factors (2 marks):** 
- **Melt elasticity and relaxation time** — higher elasticity ⇒ higher $B$.
- **Shear rate in the die** — higher shear ⇒ higher $B$.
- **Residence time / temperature** — higher temperature reduces $B$ (more relaxation in the die).
- **Die land length** — longer land reduces $B$ (more relaxation during passage).
- **Polymer molecular structure** — long-chain-branched LDPE exhibits higher $B$ than linear LLDPE.
- **Polymer molecular weight / MFI** — lower MFI (higher MW) ⇒ higher $B$.

**Significance in packaging (1 mark):**
- In **blown film extrusion**, the die diameter is intentionally **smaller** than the final bubble diameter by the inverse of the swell ratio.
- In **packaging tubing and drinking straws**, the die is **undercut** by $1/B$ so that the cooled product attains the correct nominal diameter.
- In **wire and cable insulation**, die swell governs the minimum achievable insulation thickness.

**(b) — Comparative Drag-Flow Calculation (7 marks):**

For each extruder, drag flow (ignoring pressure flow) is:

$$
Q_d \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}D^{2}N d \sin\theta\cos\theta
$$

The factor $f = \sin\theta\cos\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\theta$ is:
- For $\theta = 17.65°$: $f_X = 0.303 \times 0.953 = 0.2887$
- For $\theta = 20°$: $f_Y = 0.342 \times 0.940 = 0.3214$

**Extruder X** ($D = 0.090$ m, $d = 0.004$ m, $N = 50/60 = 0.8333$ rev/s, $f_X = 0.2887$):

$$
Q_{d,X} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}(0.090)^{2}(0.8333)(0.004)(0.2887)
$$

$$
Q_{d,X} \;=\; 4.9348 \times 8.1\times10^{-3} \times 0.8333 \times 0.004 \times 0.2887
$$

$$
Q_{d,X} \;\approx\; 3.86 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Extruder Y** ($D = 0.060$ m, $d = 0.003$ m, $N = 90/60 = 1.5$ rev/s, $f_Y = 0.3214$):

$$
Q_{d,Y} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2}(0.060)^{2}(1.5)(0.003)(0.3214)
$$

$$
Q_{d,Y} \;=\; 4.9348 \times 3.6\times10^{-3} \times 1.5 \times 0.003 \times 0.3214
$$

$$
Q_{d,Y} \;\approx\; 2.57 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Comparison (1 mark):**

$$
\dfrac{Q_{d,X}}{Q_{d,Y}} \;=\; \dfrac{3.86 \times 10^{-5}}{2.57 \times 10^{-5}} \;\approx\; 1.50
$$

**Extruder X has the higher drag-flow capacity, by a factor of approximately 1.5×.** Although Extruder Y runs faster and has a slightly higher helix-angle factor, the $D^2$ term in Extruder X dominates.

**(Total = 7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Extrusion Questions — KTU 2024 ESE**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the minus sign between $Q_d$ and $Q_p$** — pressure flow is *back-flow*; writing $Q = Q_d + Q_p$ costs **2 marks** outright.
> 2. **Mixing up units** — $D$, $d$, $L_m$ must all be in **metres**; $N$ in **revolutions per second** (not rpm); $\eta$ in **Pa·s**. KTU examiners specifically check unit consistency.
> 3. **Neglecting to state the assumptions** in the derivation (Newtonian, steady, fully-developed, $e$ neglected, etc.) — at least **1 mark** is reserved for the assumption list in any 7-mark derivation.
> 4. **Drawing the screw diagram without labels** — $D$, $d$, $W$, $H_f$, $H_m$, $L_f$, $L_m$, $L_c$ must each be marked. An unlabelled diagram scores **0 / 2 marks** allocated to the figure.
> 5. **Confusing feed/metering depth with screw diameter** — students often write $H_m = D$ instead of $H_m \approx 0.05D$.
> 6. **Sign error in $Q = Q_d - Q_p$ when the die pressure is zero** — if $\Delta P = 0$, students sometimes write $Q = 0$ instead of $Q = Q_d$. Remember: **zero die pressure gives maximum (open-discharge) output $Q = Q_d$**, not zero output.
> 7. **Using $L$ (total screw length) instead of $L_m$ (metering length only)** in the $Q_p$ expression — the pressure gradient is built up only over the metering zone.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Extrusion (PEPOT748, Module 3)**
>
> - **Extrusion** is a *continuous* melt-shaping process; the **single-screw extruder** is the workhorse of plastics packaging.
> - The three functional zones of the screw are **Feed (solids conveying) → Compression (melting) → Metering (pumping)**.
> - **Compression ratio CR** for polyolefins is **2.5 – 3.0**; for PVC it is **1.5 – 2.0**.
> - The **L/D ratio** of a typical film/sheet screw is **24:1 to 30:1**.
> - The **net volumetric output** of the metering zone is $Q = Q_d - Q_p - Q_f$, with $Q_f$ often negligible.
> - **Drag flow** $Q_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\theta\cos\theta$ is **forward (positive)** and proportional to screw speed.
> - **Pressure flow** $Q_p = \dfrac{\pi D d^3 \sin^2\theta}{12 \eta L_m}\Delta P$ is **backward (negative)** and proportional to die pressure.
> - **Optimum helix angle** for an extruder screw is **20° – 30°** (often 17.65° for square pitch).
> - **Barrel heaters** are typically **3 – 5 PID-controlled zones**, each with its own thermocouple.
> - The **breaker plate + screen pack** filters the melt and converts swirling flow to straight axial flow.
> - **Die swell (Barus effect)** is caused by elastic recovery of oriented polymer chains; swell ratio $B = D_{extrudate}/D_{die}$ is typically **1.10 – 1.50** for polyolefins.
> - **Mass throughput** $\dot{m} = \rho_{melt} Q$.
> - **Specific energy** $E_{sp} = P/\dot{m}$ is typically **3 – 6 MJ/kg** for LDPE film extrusion.
> - **Co-extrusion** of 3 to 7 layers (e.g., PE/tie/EVOH/tie/PE) is the industry standard for high-barrier food packaging films.
> - **Twin-screw extruders** (co-rotating for compounding; counter-rotating for PVC) are used in compounding, masterbatch, and rigid packaging.
> - For the KTU 2024 ESE, be ready to (i) **derive** the output equation, (ii) **calculate** a numerical value, (iii) **draw and label** a block diagram, and (iv) **list the three functional zones** with their functions.
> - Always include the **assumption list** whenever you write a derivation — it is a guaranteed 1-mark freebie.
> - Always carry **units through the calculation**; final answers should be in **m³/s (for $Q$)**, **kg/h or kg/s (for $\dot{m}$)**, and **Pa or MPa (for $\Delta P$)**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cast Film & Sheet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cast Film & Sheet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cast Film & Sheet** refers to a family of flat, uniaxially oriented thermoplastic products manufactured by **extruding a polymer melt through a flat (slit) die — typically a T-die or coat-hanger die — and solidifying the molten web on one or more highly polished, internally cooled rotating chill rolls.** The process is technically designated as **Chill Roll Extrusion**, **Flat Die Extrusion**, or **Slot Die Casting** in standard polymer processing literature.

- **Cast Film** is generally defined as a flat extruded web with a finished thickness typically **less than 0.25 mm (10 mil / 250 µm)**.
- **Cast Sheet** is the thicker counterpart, with finished thickness generally **between 0.25 mm and 12.5 mm (10 mil – 500 mil)**.
- The boundary between "film" and "sheet" is industry-specific; ASTM and ISO often use **0.254 mm (10 mil)** as the conventional dividing line.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** In the PEPOT748 syllabus, *Cast Film & Sheet* is treated as a **secondary shaping process** following primary melt extrusion. The cooling/solidification stage is the *defining* step that distinguishes cast extrusion from blown film extrusion (which uses a tubular bubble and air cooling).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hot Crepe & Cold Pan" Model

Imagine pouring a thin stream of **hot pancake batter (the polymer melt)** onto a **cold, polished, rotating steel drum (the chill roll)**. The instant the batter touches the cold drum, it **flashes into a solid, glossy sheet**, which is then peeled off and rolled up. This is exactly what happens in cast extrusion:

| Component in Analogy | Real Cast Extrusion Component |
|---|---|
| Pancake batter | Thermoplastic melt (LDPE, LLDPE, PP, PET, PS, PVC, PA) |
| Ladle / pourer | Flat T-die / coat-hanger die |
| Cold polished drum | Chrome-plated chill roll (internally water-cooled) |
| Spatula | Air knife / vacuum box / nip roll |
| Plate for serving | Take-up roll / winder |
