The hot polymer exits the die at roughly **200–280 °C**, contacts the chill roll at **20–80 °C**, and within **fractions of a second** its temperature drops below the polymer's **glass transition temperature (T₉)** or **crystallization temperature (T_c)** — locking in molecular orientation and surface gloss.

## 1.3 Industry Metrics & Standard Ranges

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Metrics You Must Memorize (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **Line speed (cast film):** up to **300 m/min** (modern lines exceed **500 m/min**)
> - **Line speed (cast sheet):** **5–50 m/min**
> - **Chill roll surface temperature:** **15 °C to 90 °C** depending on polymer
> - **Melt temperature at die exit:** **180 °C to 290 °C**
> - **Gauge tolerance (modern cast film):** **± 2.5 % to ± 5 %** across the web
> - **Neck-in (typical for LDPE):** **25–75 mm per side**
> - **Haze (cast PP film):** **< 2 %** (compared to 6–10 % for blown PP film)

## 1.4 Why Cast Film & Sheet? — Product Advantages

1. **Superior optical clarity** — single polished surface contact → gloss on one side.
2. **Tight gauge (thickness) uniformity** — coat-hanger die delivers flat velocity profile.
3. **High throughput** — bi-axially oriented versions (e.g., **BOPP**, **BOPET**, **BOPA**) extend performance.
4. **Excellent printability & lamination** — uniform surface roughness (controlled by chill roll finish or post-corona treatment).
5. **Higher stiffness and tensile properties** vs. blown film (uniaxial MD orientation dominates).
6. **Easy to produce multilayer structures** through **coextrusion** with feedblocks or multimanifold dies.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cast Extrusion Process Line — End-to-End Functional Stages

The cast extrusion line consists of **seven sequential functional zones**:

1. **Feeding & Plastication Zone** — Hopper + single- or twin-screw extruder. Polymer pellets are conveyed, compressed, melted, and homogenized.
2. **Metering & Melt Pumping** — Screw metering section generates a **pressure of 5–35 MPa** to push melt through the die.
3. **Melt Filtration** — Screen pack (typically **20–200 mesh**) removes gels, carbon specks, and unmelted contaminants.
4. **Flat Die (T-die / Coat-Hanger Die)** — Distributes melt uniformly across the web width via a **manifold** and **land region**. Exit lip opening: typically **0.3–2.0 mm** for film, **1–6 mm** for sheet.
5. **Casting & Solidification** — Molten web lands on **primary chill roll** (sometimes followed by secondary roll). Air knife / vacuum box / electrostatic pinning stabilizes the web.
6. **Edge Trimming, Gauging & Surface Treatment** — Rotary knives trim edges; beta or nuclear gauges monitor thickness; corona or flame treatment raises surface energy for printing/metallization.
7. **Winding** — Center-driven or surface-driven winders produce rolls.

## 2.2 The Coat-Hanger Die — The Heart of the Process

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Concept:** The "coat-hanger" name comes from its cross-sectional shape — a tapered central manifold with diverging side channels that look like a clothes hanger.

**Design Principles:**
- **Manifold taper:** radius decreases from centre to ends to maintain **constant pressure drop** along the flow path.
- **Pre-land:** region after the manifold that allows flow redistribution.
- **Die land:** final straight channel (length-to-gap ratio typically **> 10:1**) that creates **fully developed flow** and uniform exit velocity.
- **Lip adjustment bolts (choker bar):** flex the upper die lip locally to fine-tune gauge profile. Modern dies use **thermal bolts** or **automatic profile control** with **6–40+ zones**.

**Flow distribution governing equation (volume flow per unit width q):**

$$
q = \frac{\rho \cdot v_{exit} \cdot t}{1}
$$

where *ρ* = melt density, *v* = exit velocity, *t* = target film thickness. The die must deliver uniform *q* across width.

## 2.3 Chill Roll Mechanics & Heat Transfer

The chill roll is typically:
- **Material:** Carbon steel or aluminium, **chrome-plated** (Ra < 0.05 µm) for high gloss.
- **Diameter:** **300–1500 mm** (larger rolls provide longer contact time).
- **Internal cooling:** Spiral-baffled water circulation; controlled to **± 1 °C**.
- **Surface speed (V_roll):** Precisely matched to **V_web = V_die × (t_die / t_film)** to prevent draw resonance.

**Nusselt Number for chill roll contact cooling (approx.):**

$$
Nu \approx 0.4 \cdot Re^{0.67}
$$

This empirical correlation indicates that the convective cooling efficiency scales with **roll surface speed**, which is why **higher line speeds require proportionally more cooling capacity**.

## 2.4 Process–Structure–Property Relationships

| Process Variable | Effect on Film Property |
|---|---|
| ↑ **Melt temperature** | Lower viscosity → fewer flow lines, but higher neck-in |
| ↑ **Chill roll temperature** | Slower crystallization (in PP) → more amorphous regions → higher transparency but lower stiffness |
| ↑ **Take-up speed (MD draw)** | Higher MD orientation → improved tensile & barrier, but **neck-in** increases |
| ↑ **Air-knife velocity** | Better web-to-roll contact → faster cooling, lower haze |
| ↓ **Die-lip gap** | Higher pressure drop, more shear heating, but tighter gauge |
| ↓ **Corona treatment power** | Higher surface energy (dyne level) → better ink adhesion |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use **\vert** instead of **\vert** to avoid markdown table breakage. Below is a complete ready-to-memorize formula table.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = v_{die} \cdot W \cdot t_{die}$ | Volumetric flow rate Q (m³/s), v = velocity, W = die width, t = die gap |
| 2 | $v_{web} = v_{die} \cdot (t_{die}/t_{film})$ | Conservation of mass — web accelerates as film thins |
| 3 | $DDR = v_{take-up} \,/\, v_{die-exit}$ | Draw-Down Ratio (DDR) — typically 5 : 1 to 50 : 1 |
| 4 | $t_{film} = (Q)/(W \cdot \rho_{melt} \cdot v_{web})$ | Theoretical film thickness |
| 5 | $\rho_{melt} = \rho_{solid} / (1 + \alpha \cdot \Delta T)$ | Melt density from solid density with thermal expansion |
| 6 | $\Delta P_{die} = \dfrac{12 \mu L Q}{W h^{3}}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille pressure drop across die land (μ = viscosity, L = land length, h = land gap) |
| 7 | $Nu \approx 0.4 \, Re^{0.67}$ | Chill roll heat-transfer (empirical) |
| 8 | $Neck\text{-}in \approx k \cdot (DDR - 1)^{n}$ | Empirical neck-in (k, n depend on resin) |
| 9 | $DSC = (D_{final}/D_{initial}) \cdot 100\,\%$ | Draw-down speed constant for crystallization control |
| 10 | $T_{contact} \approx T_{melt} - h \cdot (T_{melt} - T_{roll})$ | Effective contact temperature (h = heat-transfer coefficient ratio) |

**Boundary / Operating Conditions to Remember:**
- Typical extruder L/D ratio: **24 : 1 to 36 : 1**
- Screw compression ratio: **2.5 : 1 to 4 : 1**
- Die manifold residence time: **< 5 minutes** (to avoid degradation)
- Web wrap angle on chill roll: **90° to 270°**

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

Cast film lines dominate the production of:
- **Stretch wrap / pallet wrap (LLDPE, mLLDPE)** — clarity + tear resistance.
- **Food packaging films (PP, PE, multilayer EVOH/PA/PE barrier)** — hygiene + sealability.
- **Photographic & label films (BOPP, BOPET, BOPA)** — biaxial orientation after casting.
- **Capacitor films (BOPP)** — extreme thickness uniformity (sub-µm).
- **Thermoforming sheet (PP, PET, PS, PVC)** — thicker gauge, glossy finish, easy trimming.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Film Thickness from Mass Conservation

**Given:** Volumetric flow rate Q leaving the die, web width W, take-up velocity v_web.

**Step 1 — State mass conservation** (steady-state, incompressible melt):

$$
Q_{in} = Q_{out} \implies Q = v_{web} \cdot W \cdot t_{film}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for film thickness t_film:**

$$
t_{film} = \dfrac{Q}{W \cdot v_{web}}
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce draw-down ratio DDR** (v_web / v_die) and die dimensions:

$$
t_{film} = \dfrac{v_{die} \cdot W \cdot t_{die}}{W \cdot v_{web}} = \dfrac{t_{die}}{DDR}
$$

**Engineering meaning:** A **die gap of 1 mm** combined with a **DDR of 20** yields a **film thickness of 50 µm (0.05 mm)**. The DDR is the master design knob of cast extrusion.

## 3.2 Derivation: Die Exit Velocity Required for a Given Throughput

**Step 1 — Throughput in kg/hr:**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho_{melt} \cdot v_{die} \cdot W \cdot t_{die} \cdot 3600
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for v_die:**

$$
v_{die} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{melt} \cdot W \cdot t_{die} \cdot 3600}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert melt to solid density** (cooling causes shrinkage):

$$
\rho_{melt} = \dfrac{\rho_{solid}}{1 + \alpha \cdot (T_{melt} - T_{25})}
$$

For polyethylene: α ≈ **7 × 10⁻⁴ /°C**, T_melt ≈ 220 °C, ρ_solid ≈ **952 kg/m³** → ρ_melt ≈ **824 kg/m³**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Neck-In Approximation for a Newtonian Fluid (First-Order)

**Step 4 — Define neck-in NI** as the total lateral shrinkage of the web between die exit and chill roll:

$$
NI = W_{die} - W_{film}
$$

**Step 5 — For a Newtonian melt under uniaxial extension, Trouton-like analysis gives:**

$$
NI \approx \dfrac{2 \cdot \tau_{die}}{\rho \cdot V_{die}^{2}} \cdot L_{draw}
$$

where τ_die = shear stress at die wall, L_draw = free draw length (die-to-chill distance).

**Step 6 — For power-law non-Newtonian polymers (PP, PE), empirical fit:**

$$
NI \approx k \cdot (DDR - 1)^{n}
$$

Typical k = **5–15 mm**, n = **0.6–0.9** for LDPE/LLDPE.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical takeaway:** Reducing **L_draw**, increasing **V_die**, lowering **melt temperature**, and using **narrower MWD resins** all reduce neck-in.

## 3.4 Derivation: Solidification Time on the Chill Roll

For a thin molten film of thickness *t* cooling on a chill roll, the **lumped capacitance solution** (valid for **Bi << 1**, i.e., thin films):

$$
\dfrac{T - T_{roll}}{T_{melt} - T_{roll}} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{h \cdot A}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot V}\right) t
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for time to reach solidification temperature T_s:**

$$
t_{solid} = -\dfrac{\rho \, c_p \, t}{2 \, h} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_{s} - T_{roll}}{T_{melt} - T_{roll}}\right)
$$

For PE film (t = 50 µm, ρ = 920 kg/m³, c_p = 2300 J/kg·K, h = 500 W/m²·K, T_melt = 220 °C, T_roll = 30 °C, T_s = 110 °C):

$$
t_{solid} = -\dfrac{920 \cdot 2300 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \cdot 500} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{110 - 30}{220 - 30}\right)
$$

$$
t_{solid} = -\dfrac{0.1058}{1000} \cdot \ln(0.4) = -\dfrac{0.1058}{1000} \cdot (-0.916) \approx 9.7 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}
$$

**Engineering insight:** Sub-millisecond solidification explains why **cast film has extremely high production rates** and why **chill roll cooling capacity is the true line-speed bottleneck**.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Cast Film vs. Blown Film

| Parameter | Cast Film | Blown Film |
|---|---|---|
| Optical clarity | Excellent | Good to fair |
| Gauge uniformity | ± 2.5–5 % | ± 5–10 % |
| Tensile strength | High (uniaxial MD) | Balanced (biaxial) |
| Throughput | Very high | High |
| Thickness range | 5–250 µm (film) | 10–200 µm |
| Equipment footprint | Long (15–50 m) | Compact |
| Capital cost | Higher | Lower |
| Material flexibility | Best for PP, PET | Best for PE |
| Trimming waste | Significant | Minimal |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define cast film extrusion. List any four advantages of cast film over blown film.**

**Model Answer:**
> **Cast film extrusion** is a continuous thermoplastic shaping process in which a polymer melt is extruded through a **flat (slit) die** — typically a **coat-hanger or T-die** — and the emerging molten web is **cast onto one or more polished, internally cooled rotating chill rolls** where it solidifies into a thin, flat web.

**Four advantages of cast film over blown film:**
1. **Superior optical properties** — lower haze and higher gloss due to single-surface polishing.
2. **Tighter gauge uniformity** — flat velocity profile from coat-hanger die.
3. **Higher line speed** — up to **500 m/min** vs ~150 m/min for blown film.
4. **Better printability & lamination** — uniform surface, easy corona treatment.
5. (Bonus) Thinner gauge capability (down to **5 µm** for capacitor-grade BOPP).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a chill roll? Why is it chrome-plated? What is its typical operating temperature range?**

**Model Answer:**
> A **chill roll** is a **large-diameter (300–1500 mm) rotating cylinder** with **internal water circulation** that contacts the molten polymer web immediately after it leaves the die, extracting heat rapidly to **solidify the polymer below its glass transition (T₉) or crystallization temperature**.

**Why chrome-plated:**
- **High thermal conductivity** (~30 W/m·K) — efficient heat extraction.
- **Corrosion resistance** against water additives and polymer degradation products.
- **Mirror-grade polish (Ra < 0.05 µm)** — transfers **gloss** to the film surface.
- **Hardness (~1000 HV)** — resists scratches, dents, and damage from edge trim.

**Typical operating temperature:** **15 °C to 90 °C**, controlled to **± 1 °C**, depending on polymer:
- **LDPE / LLDPE:** 20–40 °C
- **PP:** 25–50 °C (lower for faster crystallization)
- **PET:** 60–90 °C (amorphous casting prior to MD stretching)

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Essay Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** With a neat labelled sketch, describe the **cast film extrusion process** for the manufacture of **polypropylene (PP) packaging film**. Explain the role of each major component. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between **die exit velocity, web take-up velocity, draw-down ratio, and final film thickness**. A cast film line produces a **25 µm thick PP film** from a die with a **0.6 mm lip gap** and a web width of **1200 mm**. If the throughput is **120 kg/hr** and the melt density is **810 kg/m³**, calculate the **die exit velocity**, **take-up velocity**, and **draw-down ratio**. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Process Description (with reference to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1):**

The cast film extrusion of PP packaging film involves the following sequence:

1. **Feeding:** PP pellets (with slip & anti-block additives) are fed from the hopper into the extruder.
2. **Plastication:** A **single-screw extruder (L/D ≈ 30:1)** with a barrier screw melts and homogenizes the polymer at **200–240 °C**, generating melt pressure of **15–25 MPa**.
3. **Melt filtration:** A **screen pack (80/100/80 mesh)** removes gels and carbon specks that would cause film defects.
4. **Melt pumping (optional gear pump):** Ensures **pressure-constant** flow for uniform gauge.
5. **Coat-hanger die:** Distributes melt uniformly across **1200–2500 mm** web width. Die land gap **0.4–0.8 mm**, land length **20–30 mm**, manifold taper carefully designed.
6. **Air knife + Chill roll:** Molten web lands on a **chrome-plated chill roll at 25–40 °C**. **Air knife (velocity 20–40 m/s)** pins the web to the roll, ensuring intimate thermal contact.
7. **Secondary chill roll (optional):** Cools the reverse side.
8. **Edge trimming:** **Rotary knives** cut off beads (typically **25–40 mm per side**).
9. **Gauging:** **Beta or infrared gauge** measures thickness; feedback to **die lip bolts** for closed-loop control.
10. **Corona / flame treatment:** Raises surface energy to **≥ 42 dyne/cm** for printing.
11. **Winding:** **Center-driven winder** with tension control produces rolls.

**Role of key components:**

| Component | Primary Role |
|---|---|
| Extruder screw | Convey, compress, melt, homogenize, pressurize |
| Screen pack | Remove contaminants; create back-pressure for mixing |
| Coat-hanger die | Distribute melt uniformly across width |
| Chill roll | Solidify film via conductive cooling; impart surface finish |
| Air knife | Pin web to roll, prevent air entrapment |
| Beta gauge | Measure and control thickness profile |
| Corona treater | Increase surface energy for ink/metallization adhesion |
| Winder | Convert flat web into finished rolls at controlled tension |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Mass conservation between die exit and take-up:

$$
\rho \cdot v_{die} \cdot W \cdot t_{die} = \rho \cdot v_{web} \cdot W \cdot t_{film}
$$

Step 2 — Cancel ρ and W:

$$
v_{die} \cdot t_{die} = v_{web} \cdot t_{film}
$$

Step 3 — Draw-Down Ratio:

$$
DDR = \dfrac{v_{web}}{v_{die}} = \dfrac{t_{die}}{t_{film}}
$$

**Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- t_film = **25 µm = 25 × 10⁻⁶ m**
- t_die = **0.6 mm = 0.6 × 10⁻³ m**
- W = **1200 mm = 1.2 m**
- ṁ = **120 kg/hr = 120 / 3600 = 0.03333 kg/s**
- ρ_melt = **810 kg/m³**

**Step 4 — Calculate DDR:**

$$
DDR = \dfrac{0.6 \times 10^{-3}}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = 24
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate volumetric flow rate Q:**

$$
Q = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{melt}} = \dfrac{0.03333}{810} = 4.115 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate die exit velocity v_die:**

$$
v_{die} = \dfrac{Q}{W \cdot t_{die}} = \dfrac{4.115 \times 10^{-5}}{1.2 \times 0.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0571 \text{ m/s} \approx 3.43 \text{ m/min}
$$

**Step 7 — Calculate take-up velocity v_web:**

$$
v_{web} = DDR \cdot v_{die} = 24 \cdot 0.0571 = 1.371 \text{ m/s} \approx 82.3 \text{ m/min}
$$

**Step 8 — Verification:**

$$
t_{film} = \dfrac{Q}{W \cdot v_{web}} = \dfrac{4.115 \times 10^{-5}}{1.2 \cdot 1.371} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 25 \text{ µm} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Result Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Die exit velocity | v_die | **0.0571 m/s (3.43 m/min)** |
| Take-up velocity | v_web | **1.371 m/s (82.3 m/min)** |
| Draw-Down Ratio | DDR | **24 : 1** |

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**(a)** Compare **cast film extrusion** and **blown film extrusion** with respect to **process principle, equipment, product properties, applications, and economy**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **structure and function of a coat-hanger die** used in cast film extrusion. With a neat sketch, show the velocity profile development across the manifold and land region, and explain how **automatic profile control systems** maintain gauge uniformity. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Detailed Comparison Table (Examiner expects crisp tabular form):**

| Parameter | Cast Film Extrusion | Blown Film Extrusion |
|---|---|---|
| **Process principle** | Melt extruded through flat slit die → cast on chill roll | Melt extruded through annular die → inflated into bubble |
| **Orientation** | Predominantly uniaxial (MD) | Biaxial (MD + TD via inflation) |
| **Die type** | Coat-hanger / T-die (flat) | Annular / spider die (circular) |
| **Cooling** | Conductive chill roll + air knife | Convective air ring (internal + external) |
| **Neck-in / layout** | Neck-in (25–75 mm/side) requires trim waste | No neck-in; edge bead folded into layflat |
| **Optical clarity** | Excellent (haze < 2 % for PP) | Moderate (haze 6–10 % for PP) |
| **Gauge uniformity** | ± 2.5–5 % | ± 5–10 % |
| **Line speed** | Up to **500 m/min** | Up to **150 m/min** |
| **Tear strength** | Lower (MD) | Higher (balanced) |
| **Sealability** | Excellent for lamination films | Good for general PE bags |
| **Capital cost** | Higher | Lower |
| **Applications** | Stretch wrap, BOPP, capacitor film, lamination | Carrier bags, agricultural film, liners |
| **Economy** | High output, more trim waste | Lower output, less trim waste |
| **Polymer suitability** | Best for PP, PET, PA, PS | Best for PE (LDPE, LLDPE, HDPE) |

**Key written explanation points (3–4 sentences each):**
- In cast film, **uniaxial orientation** is achieved because the film is **stretched only in the machine direction (MD)** by the speed difference between the die and the chill roll, while the **transverse direction (TD)** is essentially constrained by the die width. This produces **high MD tensile strength** but **lower TD tear resistance**.
- In blown film, the **bubble inflation** induces biaxial orientation. The **blow-up ratio (BUR)** and the **frost line height** are the master control parameters that determine the **TD orientation** and hence **balanced mechanical properties**.
- Cast film is the **preferred choice for converting downstream** operations (printing, metallization, lamination) because of its **flat, glossy, dimensionally stable** nature.
- Blown film is **preferred for heavy-duty applications** (liners, shrink films) because of its **superior impact and tear strength**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Structure and Function of Coat-Hanger Die:**

The coat-hanger die is the **most widely used flat die for cast film** because its **tapered manifold geometry** compensates for the **pressure drop in the die land**, ensuring **uniform flow velocity across the entire width of the die exit**.

**Major Sections (see Mermaid schematic):**

1. **Inlet/Adapter:** Connects to extruder or gear pump. Houses a **pressure transducer** and **thermocouple**.
2. **Manifold (coat-hanger channel):** A tapered internal channel — radius **R_c** at centre tapering to smaller radius **R_e** at the ends. The taper is mathematically designed so that **pressure drop across the manifold ≈ pressure drop along the land** at every position across the width.
3. **Pre-land region:** Short converging region between manifold exit and land entry; smooths out velocity profile disturbances.
4. **Die land:** Final straight channel with length **L** and gap **h** (L/h typically **10:1 to 20:1**). Develops fully developed Poiseuille flow.
5. **Die lips (upper flexible lip + lower rigid lip):** Adjustable via **choker bar** or **thermal bolts** to control local exit gap.
6. **Lip heater zones:** **6–40+ independently controlled heater zones** for thermal profile control.

**Velocity Profile Across Manifold and Land:**

In the **land region**, the velocity profile is **parabolic** (Poiseuille flow):

$$
v(y) = v_{avg}\!\left[\dfrac{3}{2}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{4y^{2}}{h^{2}}\right)\right]
$$

where y = vertical coordinate across die gap, h = die gap. Maximum velocity v_max = 1.5 × v_avg.

In the **manifold**, the velocity profile is more complex — combining **axial flow along the manifold** with **cross-flow into the land**. Designers use the **coat-hanger equation**:

$$
R(x)^{3} = R_{c}^{3} - \dfrac{6 \mu Q_{side}}{\pi \cdot \Delta P_{land}} \cdot x
$$

where R(x) is the local manifold radius at distance *x* from centre, μ = melt viscosity, Q_side = flow diverted into land, ΔP_land = land pressure drop.

**Automatic Profile Control (APC):**

Modern cast lines employ **closed-loop thickness control**:
1. **Beta or infrared gauge** scans the web **continuously** in a "Z" or "C" pattern.
2. Measurement data is fed to a **profile controller**.
3. Controller actuates **thermal bolts** (each containing an internal heater) or **piezo-electric lip actuators** to locally adjust die lip gap.
4. Adjustments occur within **< 5 seconds** to maintain gauge tolerance of **± 2.5 %**.
5. **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** charts log the profile for trend analysis.

**Engineered Features:**
- **Chrome-plated internal flow surfaces** for corrosion resistance and easy cleaning.
- **Flow restrictor bars** (variable geometry inserts) for fine-tuning during commissioning.
- **Deckle inserts** to allow variable web width without changing the die.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 reasons students LOSE marks on Cast Film questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Confusing Cast Film with Blown Film** — board papers frequently ask for a comparison; mixing up which process gives biaxial vs uniaxial orientation is the **#1 killer error**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — throughput in **kg/hr → kg/s** before dividing by density is a common step where students skip. Mark deduction: **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing *neck-in* with *draw-down ratio*** — neck-in is a *lateral* phenomenon (TD shrinkage); draw-down ratio is a *longitudinal* phenomenon (MD stretching).
> 4. **Skipping the die sketch** — a question worth **7 marks on "describe the process"** typically requires **a labelled sketch** for **at least 2 marks**. Always draw one.
> 5. **Not mentioning the role of the air knife** — many students describe the chill roll but forget that **air knife pinning** is critical for intimate thermal contact.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember Bullet Points (last-minute revision):**

- ✅ Cast film is made by **extrusion through a flat die** + **solidification on a chill roll**.
- ✅ **Coat-hanger die** is the standard flat die design; its tapered manifold gives uniform flow.
- ✅ **Chill roll** is chrome-plated, internally cooled, mirror-polished; **20–80 °C** typical.
- ✅ **Air knife** pins the web to the roll, prevents air entrapment and ensures rapid cooling.
- ✅ **Draw-Down Ratio (DDR)** = v_web / v_die = t_die / t_film — controls MD orientation and thickness.
- ✅ **Neck-in** is the lateral shrinkage of the web; reduced by lowering melt temp and DDR.
- ✅ Cast film is **uniaxially oriented (MD)**, **blown film is biaxially oriented** — this is THE key difference.
- ✅ Cast film has **superior clarity, gloss, gauge uniformity** but **lower TD tear strength**.
- ✅ **Coextrusion** with feedblocks enables **multilayer** cast films for barrier packaging.
- ✅ **Surface treatment** (corona / flame) raises surface energy for **printing & metallization**.
- ✅ **Beta / nuclear gauges** provide **closed-loop profile control** in modern cast lines.
- ✅ **Typical line speed** — cast film **up to 500 m/min**; cast sheet **5–50 m/min**.
- ✅ **BOPP, BOPET, BOPA** films are made by **MD stretching** after casting, followed by **TD stretching** in tenter ovens.
- ✅ Cast sheet thickness: **0.25–12.5 mm**; cast film thickness: **5–250 µm**.
- ✅ Major applications: **stretch wrap, food packaging, label films, capacitor films, thermoforming sheet**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blown Film
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Blown Film

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — BLOWN FILM EXTRUSION: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blown Film Extrusion** is a continuous, high-volume thermoplastics conversion process in which a molten polymer melt is extruded through an **annular (circular) die** to form a thin-walled tube, which is then internally inflated with compressed air to produce a **biaxially-oriented, seamless cylindrical bubble**. The bubble is subsequently cooled, flattened at the **collapsing frame (nips)**, and wound into rolls as **lay-flat tubing (LFT)** or slit into separate webs.

The process is officially designated in ASTM D 2673 as the *Tubular Film Extrusion* method and represents the **single largest tonnage process** for the manufacture of commodity plastic films used in packaging — particularly **LDPE, LLDPE, HDPE, EVA, PP, and multilayer barrier structures**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Nomenclature Anchors (Mandatory KTU Glossary):**
> - **Bubble** — The inflated molten tube between the die exit and the nip rolls.
> - **Frost Line (HL – Height of Layflat or Freeze Line)** — The transition altitude where the polymer transitions from a transparent melt to an opaque, solidified, "frosted" state due to crystallization/glass transition.
> - **BUR (Blow-Up Ratio)** — Diameter ratio of the inflated bubble to the die orifice.
> - **DDR (Draw Down Ratio)** — Velocity ratio of the haul-off speed to the melt extrusion velocity.
> - **Lay-Flat Width (LFW)** — Width of the collapsed tube, equal to half the bubble circumference.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Soap Bubble & Straw" Intuition

Imagine you dip the end of a hollow straw into a pan of warm honey and gently blow air through the straw while pulling it **upward**. What happens?

1. The honey forms a **thin-walled cylindrical sleeve** around the straw's exit.
2. Your breath inflates it **sideways** (transverse direction).
3. As you pull the straw up, the honey is **stretched lengthwise** (machine direction).
4. The walls become thinner and the structure **stretches in two directions at once**.

**This is exactly the blown film process** — except the "honey" is molten polyethylene at **160 °C–230 °C**, the "straw" is an annular die, and the "pulling" is done by motor-driven **nip rolls at heights of 6 m to 25 m**. The biaxial stretching (transverse + machine direction) is what gives blown film its superior **tear strength, impact resistance, and dimensional stability** compared to cast film.

> [!TIP]
> **For First-Time Learners:** Every time you open a polybag from a grocery store, you are touching blown film. The gusseted bottom, the seamless tubular shape, and the slight "frosty" optical character are all fingerprints of the blown film process.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Melt Temperature (LDPE/LLDPE)** | **160 – 230** | °C |
| **Die Diameter** | **50 – 1000** | mm |
| **Bubble Diameter** | **0.2 – 4.0** | m |
| **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)** | **1.5 – 4.0** (typical), up to **6.0** | dimensionless |
| **Draw Down Ratio (DDR)** | **10 – 100** | dimensionless |
| **Throughput** | **50 – 2000** | kg/hr |
| **Film Thickness Range** | **10 – 250** | µm |
| **Frost Line Height** | **3 × Die Diameter – 10 × Die Diameter** | m |
| **Standard Line Speed** | **10 – 200** | m/min |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 3 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Process Sequence — Structured Logic Steps

The blown film line consists of **seven sequential stations**. Each step performs a specific rheological, thermal, or mechanical transformation.

1. **Step 1 — Polymer Melting & Homogenization**
   - Polymer granules (LDPE/LLDPE/HDPE/PP) are fed into a **screw extruder** (typically L/D = 24:1 to 30:1, compression ratio 3:1).
   - The screw performs three zones: **feed → compression → metering**.
   - A **static mixer** or **dynamic mixer** at the end homogenizes the melt to eliminate thermal gradients (ΔT ≤ ±2 °C).

2. **Step 2 — Melt Pumping through Adapter & Screen Pack**
   - A **breaker plate + screen pack** filters contaminants and breaks helical melt streamlines.
   - Typical screen pack: **40/80/100/80/40 mesh** layered configuration.

3. **Step 3 — Die Distribution (Annular Die)**
   - Melt is forced through an **annular die lip** (gap = 0.5 – 3.0 mm).
   - Two die architectures dominate:
     - **Side-Fed Die (Spider Die):** Melt enters radially via feedports; supports cause weld lines.
     - **Spiral Die (Mandrel Die):** Melt spirals helically up the die body; produces gauge uniformity within ±3 %.

4. **Step 4 — Bubble Inflation (Transverse Stretching)**
   - Compressed air (0.5 – 5.0 kPa) is injected via an **air ring** at the die exit and through the **internal bubble stabilizer (IBS)**.
   - The bubble inflates to a diameter determined by the **BUR setpoint**.

5. **Step 5 — Biaxial Orientation (MD + TD Stretching)**
   - **Machine Direction (MD)** stretching: Haul-off speed vs. melt velocity → governed by DDR.
   - **Transverse Direction (TD)** stretching: Bubble inflation vs. die diameter → governed by BUR.
   - Orientation occurs **above the frost line** (rubbery amorphous state) but freezes **below it**.

6. **Step 6 — Cooling & Collapse**
   - An **air ring** blows external cooling air (10 – 25 °C) tangentially onto the bubble.
   - The bubble is flattened between **nip rolls** at the **collapsing frame**.

7. **Step 7 — Winding & Slitting**
   - The lay-flat tube is wound onto a **center-winder** as tubing, or slit on-line into **two flat webs** and wound on **surface or turret winders**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering "Why":** Biaxial orientation at the optimal frost-line temperature aligns polymer chains in **two perpendicular directions simultaneously**, producing a **balanced film with isotropic mechanical properties**. This is the central scientific advantage of blown film over cast (MD-only) extrusion.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM GOLD:** The following equations appear every KTU cycle — memorize and practice them.

### Table 2.A — Core Dimensionless Ratios

| Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)** | $\text{BUR} = \dfrac{D_b}{D_d}$ | $D_b$ = bubble diameter, $D_d$ = die diameter |
| **Draw Down Ratio (DDR)** | $\text{DDR} = \dfrac{V_h}{V_e} = \dfrac{D_d \cdot \pi \cdot t_f}{t_d \cdot w}$ | $V_h$ = haul-off speed, $V_e$ = melt exit velocity |
| **Lay-Flat Width (LFW)** | $\text{LFW} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_b}{2}$ | Width of collapsed tube |
| **Overall Film Gauge** | $t_f = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot w \cdot V_h}$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow, $\rho$ = density, $w$ = width |
| **Orientation Equibiaxiality** | $\lambda_{TD} = \text{BUR}, \quad \lambda_{MD} = \text{DDR}^{1/2}$ | Stretch ratios in each direction |

### Table 2.B — Production & Rheology Equations

| Quantity | Formula | SI Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Volumetric Output** | $Q = v_e \cdot A_{die} = v_h \cdot t_f \cdot w$ | m³/s |
| **Haul-off Velocity** | $V_h = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot t_f \cdot w}$ | m/s |
| **Melt Exit Velocity** | $V_e = \dfrac{Q}{A_d} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot \pi \cdot D_d \cdot t_d}$ | m/s |
| **Birefringence Indicator** | $\Delta n \propto (\lambda_{MD} \cdot \lambda_{TD} - 1)$ | dimensionless |
| **Bubble Tension (Hoop Stress)** | $T = (P_{int} - P_{ext}) \cdot \dfrac{D_b}{2}$ | N/m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe character `\vert` rule:** In Markdown tables, never write `$\vert x \vert$`. Always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to avoid breaking the table.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Packaging Industry:** Polyethylene carrier bags, garbage bags, shrink film, agricultural mulch film, food-grade laminations.
- **Industrial:** Stretch hoods, pallet shrink wrap, geomembranes for landfill liners.
- **Medical:** Sterile pouches (Tyvek–PE laminates use blown PE base films).
- **Multilayer Barrier Films:** Up to **11 layers** (e.g., EVAL/EVOH sandwiched between PE/PP) for oxygen-sensitive food packaging — all coextruded through multi-manifold dies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass Conservation Across the Bubble (Film Thickness Equation)

Starting from first principles: the volumetric flow rate of melt exiting the die must equal the volumetric flow rate of solidified film leaving the nip rolls.

**Step 1 — Define the die exit cross-sectional area (annulus).**
The die has an outer diameter $D_d$ and a die gap $t_d$. Treating the annular slit as an unwrapped rectangle of width $\pi D_d$ and thickness $t_d$:

$$
A_{die} = \pi D_d \cdot t_d
$$

**Step 2 — Define the volumetric flow rate exiting the die.**
For a polymer melt of density $\rho$ (kg/m³) and mass throughput $\dot{m}$ (kg/s):

$$
Q = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the film cross-sectional area at the nip rolls.**
The collapsed lay-flat film has width $w$ (= $\pi D_b / 2$) and final film thickness $t_f$:

$$
A_{film} = w \cdot t_f = \dfrac{\pi D_b}{2} \cdot t_f
$$

**Step 4 — Apply volumetric continuity** (incompressible melt, no leakage):

$$
Q = V_e \cdot A_{die} = V_h \cdot A_{film}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and solve for film thickness $t_f$.**

$$
V_e \cdot (\pi D_d t_d) = V_h \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_b}{2} \cdot t_f
$$

**Step 6 — Recognize that BUR = $D_b / D_d$**, so $D_b = \text{BUR} \cdot D_d$:

$$
V_e \cdot \pi D_d t_d = V_h \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot \text{BUR} \cdot D_d}{2} \cdot t_f
$$

**Step 7 — Cancel $\pi D_d$ from both sides:**

$$
V_e \cdot t_d = V_h \cdot \dfrac{\text{BUR}}{2} \cdot t_f
$$

**Step 8 — Rearrange for $t_f$:**

$$
\boxed{\;t_f = \dfrac{2 \, t_d \, V_e}{V_h \, \text{BUR}}\;}
$$

**Step 9 — Since DDR = $V_h / V_e$:**

$$
\boxed{\;t_f = \dfrac{2 \, t_d}{\text{BUR} \cdot \text{DDR}}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Final Master Equation:** Film thickness scales **inversely** with both the Blow-Up Ratio and the Draw Down Ratio. To make thinner film, increase BUR and/or DDR. This is the single most-tested equation in KTU blown-film problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Draw Down Ratio from First Principles

**Step 1 — Define DDR** as the ratio of linear velocities (MD stretching):

$$
\text{DDR} = \dfrac{V_h}{V_e}
$$

**Step 2 — Express $V_e$ via the Hagen–Poiseuille analogue for slit dies** (neglecting edge effects for thin gaps):

$$
V_e = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot \pi D_d t_d}
$$

**Step 3 — Express $V_h$ via the throughput at the nip:**

$$
V_h = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot w \cdot t_f}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide:**

$$
\text{DDR} = \dfrac{V_h}{V_e} = \dfrac{\dot{m}/(\rho w t_f)}{\dot{m}/(\rho \pi D_d t_d)} = \dfrac{\pi D_d t_d}{w \, t_f}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $w = \pi D_b / 2 = \pi \cdot \text{BUR} \cdot D_d / 2$:**

$$
\text{DDR} = \dfrac{\pi D_d t_d}{\dfrac{\pi \cdot \text{BUR} \cdot D_d}{2} \cdot t_f} = \dfrac{2 t_d}{\text{BUR} \cdot t_f}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for film thickness to cross-validate:**

$$
t_f = \dfrac{2 t_d}{\text{BUR} \cdot \text{DDR}}
$$

This is identical to Derivation 1's Step 9 — **mass conservation is self-consistent**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Problems

### Problem 3.A — Film Thickness Calculation

**Given:**
- Die diameter $D_d = 200$ mm
- Die gap $t_d = 1.5$ mm
- Blow-Up Ratio BUR = 2.5
- Draw Down Ratio DDR = 30
- Output $\dot{m} = 150$ kg/hr
- Density $\rho_{LDPE} = 920$ kg/m³

**Find:** Film thickness $t_f$ and lay-flat width $w$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply the master thickness equation:

$$
t_f = \dfrac{2 \, t_d}{\text{BUR} \cdot \text{DDR}} = \dfrac{2 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})}{2.5 \times 30}
$$

$$
t_f = \dfrac{3.0 \times 10^{-3}}{75} = 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;t_f = 40 \; \mu\text{m}\;}
$$

Step 2 — Calculate bubble diameter:

$$
D_b = \text{BUR} \cdot D_d = 2.5 \times 0.200 = 0.500 \text{ m}
$$

Step 3 — Calculate lay-flat width:

$$
w = \dfrac{\pi D_b}{2} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.500}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;w = 0.785 \text{ m} = 785 \text{ mm}\;}
$$

Step 4 — Verify mass conservation:

$$
V_h = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho \, w \, t_f} = \dfrac{150 / 3600}{920 \times 0.785 \times (40 \times 10^{-6})}
$$

$$
V_h = \dfrac{0.04167}{0.02889} = 1.442 \text{ m/s} = 86.5 \text{ m/min}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A throughput of 150 kg/hr at 40 µm gauge is typical for industrial monolayer LDPE packaging lines producing carrier-bag-grade film.

---

### Problem 3.B — Required DDR for Target Gauge

**Given:** Die gap $t_d = 2.0$ mm, BUR = 3.0, target film thickness $t_f = 25$ µm.

**Find:** Required DDR.

**Solution:**

$$
\text{DDR} = \dfrac{2 t_d}{\text{BUR} \cdot t_f} = \dfrac{2 \times (2.0 \times 10^{-3})}{3.0 \times (25 \times 10^{-6})}
$$

$$
\text{DDR} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{-3}}{7.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 53.3
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{DDR} \approx 53\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Practical Limit:** DDR > 100 typically causes **melt fracture (sharkskin)** and **edge weave instability**. Industrial ceilings are around **DDR ≤ 80**.

---

### Problem 3.C — Bubble Hoop Tension

**Given:** Internal pressure $P_{int} = 2.5$ kPa (gauge), external $P_{ext} = 0$, $D_b = 0.6$ m.

**Find:** Hoop tension in bubble wall.

**Solution:**

$$
T = \Delta P \cdot \dfrac{D_b}{2} = (2500) \times \dfrac{0.6}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;T = 750 \text{ N/m}\;}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This tension must be balanced by the molecular orientation stresses. Excessive tension above the frost line causes **bubble instability (breathing)**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# MODULE 3 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS
