<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# MODULE 3 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Blow-Up Ratio (BUR) and Draw Down Ratio (DDR) in blown film extrusion. State their typical industrial ranges.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR):** The dimensionless ratio of the inflated bubble diameter to the annular die diameter:

$$
\text{BUR} = \dfrac{D_b}{D_d}
$$

Typical range = **1.5 to 4.0** (extensible up to 6.0 for shrink films).

- **Draw Down Ratio (DDR):** The dimensionless ratio of the haul-off (take-up) speed to the melt velocity at the die exit:

$$
\text{DDR} = \dfrac{V_h}{V_e}
$$

Typical range = **10 to 100**, with practical ceiling near 80 due to melt fracture onset.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is the "frost line" in blown film extrusion? Why is its position critical to film properties?**

**Model Answer:**

The **frost line** (also called the freeze line or HL — height of layflat) is the axial position on the bubble where the molten polymer transitions to a solidified, opaque state due to cooling below the polymer's crystallization temperature (for semi-crystalline polymers such as LDPE, LLDPE, HDPE, PP) or glass transition temperature (for amorphous polymers such as PS, PVC).

**Critical Importance:**
- **Above the frost line:** Orientation stress is applied to a rubbery, mobile polymer network — biaxial orientation occurs here.
- **Below the frost line:** Polymer is frozen; further stretching causes stress whitening, splits, or web breaks.
- The frost-line position determines **crystallinity, optical clarity, and MD/TD balance** of the final film.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat schematic, describe the **blown film extrusion process**. Label all critical components from hopper to winder and explain the role of the air ring.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for **film thickness $t_f$** in terms of die gap $t_d$, Blow-Up Ratio, and Draw Down Ratio. A monolayer LDPE film is produced on a 250 mm die with 2.0 mm die gap, BUR = 2.8, and DDR = 45. Calculate the film thickness and lay-flat width.

---

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Components & Their Functions:**

1. **Hopper:** Gravimetric/volumetric feeding of resin pellets.
2. **Extruder (Barrier or Maddock Screw):** Melts, mixes, and pumps polymer.
3. **Breaker Plate + Screen Pack:** Removes gels/contaminants and converts rotary flow into axial flow.
4. **Static/Dynamic Mixer:** Homogenizes melt temperature (ΔT ≤ ±2 °C).
5. **Adapter:** Directs melt into die inlet.
6. **Annular Die (Spiral Die):** Forms a uniform annular melt tube.
7. **Air Ring:** Directs high-velocity cooling air tangentially onto the bubble exterior; stabilizes the bubble shape and controls frost-line height.
8. **Internal Bubble Stabilizer (IBS):** Distributes internal air and dampens bubble breathing.
9. **Collapsing Frame + Nip Rolls:** Flatten the tube to lay-flat form.
10. **Slitter:** Cuts lay-flat into two webs (optional).
11. **Winder:** Wind finished film into rolls.

**Role of Air Ring (Expanded):** The air ring blows a conical sheet of ambient or chilled air (10–25 °C, 500–1500 m³/hr) at the die exit, providing convective heat removal at 50–200 W/m²·K. It also stabilizes the bubble against helical instabilities by establishing a uniform cooling envelope. Dual-lip air rings improve gauge uniformity by ±25 %.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Step 1 — Derivation of $t_f$** (Refer to SECTION 3.1 for full derivation):

$$
t_f = \dfrac{2 \, t_d}{\text{BUR} \cdot \text{DDR}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substituting numerical values:**

Given: $D_d = 250$ mm = $0.250$ m; $t_d = 2.0$ mm = $2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m; BUR = 2.8; DDR = 45.

$$
t_f = \dfrac{2 \times (2.0 \times 10^{-3})}{2.8 \times 45} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{-3}}{126}
$$

$$
t_f = 3.175 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 31.75 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate bubble diameter:**

$$
D_b = \text{BUR} \cdot D_d = 2.8 \times 0.250 = 0.700 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate lay-flat width:**

$$
w = \dfrac{\pi D_b}{2} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.700}{2}
$$

$$
w = 1.0996 \text{ m} \approx 1100 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Answer:** $t_f \approx 31.75 \; \mu\text{m}$, $w \approx 1100$ mm.

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the difference between a **spiral die and a spider (side-fed) die** for blown film. State which is preferred for optical-grade LLDPE film and why.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the effects of **Blow-Up Ratio and Draw Down Ratio on the mechanical properties** (tensile strength, tear strength, impact resistance) of blown film. Support your answer with appropriate equations.

---

#### Model Solution to B(a):

| Feature | **Spider (Side-Fed) Die** | **Spiral Die** |
|---|---|---|
| Melt path | Enters radially, divides around spider legs | Spirals helically up die body |
| Weld lines | Present at each spider leg (mechanical weakness) | No weld lines |
| Residence time distribution | Long, non-uniform | Short, uniform |
| Gauge uniformity | ±5–10 % | ±2–3 % |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher |
| Best for | Construction-grade film, geomembranes | Optical-grade LLDPE/PP packaging |

**Conclusion:** Spiral dies are preferred for **optical-grade LLDPE packaging films** because the absence of weld lines eliminates haze and weak spots, and the uniform residence time prevents polymer degradation and gel formation.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Effects of Increasing BUR:**
- ↑ **TD orientation** → ↑ Tensile strength in transverse direction.
- ↑ BUR beyond 4.0 → ↓ Tear strength in MD (because orientation becomes too TD-biased).
- ↑ BUR → ↑ Impact resistance (more balanced orientation).

**Effects of Increasing DDR:**
- ↑ **MD orientation** → ↑ Tensile strength in machine direction.
- ↑ DDR beyond 60 → ↓ Impact resistance, ↑ MD tear resistance (chain scission in extreme cases).

**Quantitative Indicator — Degree of Biaxial Orientation:**

$$
\lambda_{TD} = \text{BUR}
$$

$$
\lambda_{MD} = \sqrt{\text{DDR}}
$$

The balance ratio:

$$
R = \dfrac{\lambda_{MD}}{\lambda_{TD}}
$$

- $R = 1$ → perfectly balanced (equibiaxial) film.
- $R > 1$ → MD-preferred orientation (e.g., shrink wrap).
- $R < 1$ → TD-preferred orientation (e.g., agricultural film).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing BUR with DDR.** BUR controls TD; DDR controls MD. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this mix-up.
> 2. **Forgetting units.** Always convert mm → m before substitution to avoid 10³ errors.
> 3. **Ignoring mass conservation.** Always verify by re-substituting the calculated thickness into the throughput equation.
> 4. **Treating frost line as fixed.** Frost-line height depends on cooling rate, throughput, and resin — not a geometric constant.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)** = $D_b / D_d$ — controls transverse orientation and bubble diameter.
- ✅ **Draw Down Ratio (DDR)** = $V_h / V_e$ — controls machine-direction orientation and film thickness.
- ✅ **Master film-thickness equation:** $t_f = 2 t_d / (\text{BUR} \cdot \text{DDR})$ — KTU's most-asked equation.
- ✅ **Lay-flat width:** $w = \pi D_b / 2$.
- ✅ **Frost line** marks the crystallization (or $T_g$) front — orientation occurs **above** it.
- ✅ **Spiral dies** are preferred for optical/clarity films; **spider dies** for heavy-duty industrial films.
- ✅ **Mass conservation principle:** $V_e \cdot A_{die} = V_h \cdot w \cdot t_f$ — must always balance.
- ✅ **Typical process window:** Melt 160–230 °C; BUR 1.5–4.0; DDR 10–80; throughput 50–2000 kg/hr.
- ✅ **Bubble instability causes:** Improper air ring cooling, excessive DDR, low melt strength.
- ✅ **Hoop tension:** $T = \Delta P \cdot D_b / 2$ — must be balanced by polymer melt strength.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi-Layer Film & Sheet Coatings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multi-Layer Film & Sheet Coatings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Multi-Layer Film** is a polymeric packaging structure composed of two or more distinct polymer layers coextruded, laminated, or coated together to combine the functional properties of individual materials into a single integrated sheet. A **Sheet Coating** refers to the process of applying one or more thin polymer, metallic, or inorganic layers onto a pre-formed film or sheet substrate to enhance specific end-use characteristics such as barrier, heat-sealability, printability, or mechanical strength.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for Plastics Packaging Technology, multi-layer systems are engineered composites where each layer is purposefully selected to perform a discrete function: structural support, moisture barrier, oxygen barrier, sealing, adhesion promotion, or aesthetic enhancement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT748 – Module 3):**
> Multi-layer constructions are the backbone of **high-barrier flexible packaging** used in food, pharmaceuticals, and industrial applications. No single polymer offers the optimal balance of mechanical, optical, barrier, and sealable properties — multi-layer engineering resolves this limitation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

**Analogy – The Sandwich Concept:**
Imagine you want a snack that is crunchy on the outside, soft in the middle, and tastes great. A plain slice of bread cannot give you all three attributes — you need a *sandwich* where each ingredient contributes something unique (bread for crunch, cheese for flavor, lettuce for freshness).

A multi-layer film works exactly the same way. No single plastic offers the perfect combination of:
- Transparency + Strength + Oxygen Barrier + Moisture Barrier + Heat Sealability + Printability + Cost-effectiveness

So packaging engineers "stack" polymers like sandwich ingredients:
- **Outer layer:** Tough, printable, scratch-resistant (e.g., PET, BOPP)
- **Middle layer(s):** Barrier against O₂ or moisture (e.g., EVOH, PVDC, PA/Nylon)
- **Tie layers:** "Glue" layers that bond incompatible polymers (e.g., maleic anhydride grafted polyolefins)
- **Sealant layer:** Heat-sealable inner skin (e.g., LDPE, LLDPE, mLLDPE, EVA, Ionomer)

## 1.3 Why Multi-Layer Films Are Necessary

| Limitation of Mono-Layer Films | How Multi-Layer Films Solve It |
|---|---|
| Single polymer cannot deliver all required properties | Each layer contributes one specialized function |
| High-barrier polymers (EVOH, PVDC) are expensive and difficult to seal | Barrier is "sandwiched" between cheap structural layers |
| Incompatible polymers cannot be blended directly | Tie layers chemically bond dissimilar polymers |
| Sensitive barrier layers may fail under mechanical stress | Structural outer layers protect the barrier core |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metrics to Remember:**
> - Typical multi-layer food pouch structures range from **3 to 9 layers** (e.g., PET/Adhesive/Aluminum Foil/Adhesive/LDPE for retort pouches).
> - Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR) target for high-barrier packaging: **< 1 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹** at 23 °C, 0% RH.
> - Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR) target for moisture-sensitive products: **< 0.1 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹** at 38 °C, 90% RH.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Multi-Layer Film Construction Methods

Multi-layer films are produced through **three primary manufacturing routes**, each with distinct advantages:

### 2.1.1 Coextrusion
- **Definition:** Simultaneous extrusion of two or more polymers through a single die to form an integrated multi-layer melt.
- **Types of Coextrusion Dies:**
  - **Feedblock Coextrusion:** Polymers meet at a feedblock and are layered before entering a single die.
  - **Multi-Manifold Coextrusion:** Each polymer has its own manifold within the die; layering occurs inside the die.
  - **Stack-Plate Die:** Polymers are combined in a stack of plates, each adding a layer gradually.
- **Key Engineering Considerations:**
  - Melt viscosity matching at the die lips
  - Layer thickness distribution control (via flow ratios)
  - Interfacial instability prevention

### 2.1.2 Lamination
- **Definition:** Bonding of pre-formed films using adhesives, heat, or pressure.
- **Types of Lamination:**
  - **Adhesive Lamination:** Solvent-based, water-based, or solventless adhesives
  - **Extrusion Lamination:** A polymer melt is extruded between two substrates
  - **Thermal Lamination:** Bonding through heat and pressure (no adhesive)
  - **Wet Lamination:** Adhesive applied wet and dried in-line

### 2.1.3 Coating
- **Definition:** Application of a functional layer onto a pre-formed substrate.
- **Coating Technologies:**
  - **Extrusion Coating:** Polymer melt is coated onto a moving substrate
  - **Gravure Coating:** Liquid coating applied via engraved roller
  - **Slot-Die Coating:** Precision coating with a die lip
  - **Metallization:** Vacuum deposition of aluminum (Al) or aluminum oxide (AlOx)
  - **PVDC Coating:** Emulsion or solution coating of polyvinylidene chloride
  - **Silica (SiOx) Coating:** Plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition

## 2.2 Functional Roles of Each Layer

A multi-layer film is engineered with a **purposeful, sequential layer architecture**:

1. **Outer/Print Layer:** Provides a printable, glossy, and mechanically robust surface. Examples: PET (12 µm), BOPP (15-20 µm).
2. **Barrier Layer (Core):** Provides resistance to gases, moisture, aromas, or light. Examples: EVOH, PVDC, Aluminum foil, PA (Nylon), metallized layers.
3. **Tie/Adhesive Layer:** Chemically bonds dissimilar adjacent polymers. Examples: Maleic anhydride-grafted PE (PE-g-MAH), EVA-based tie resins.
4. **Bulk/Structural Layer:** Provides thickness, stiffness, and cost-efficiency. Examples: LLDPE, LDPE, HDPE.
5. **Sealant Layer:** Forms hermetic seals under heat and pressure. Examples: LLDPE, mLLDPE, EVA, ionomer (Surlyn®), CPP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Tie Layers Are Critical:**
> Most polymers are thermodynamically immiscible. Without tie layers, delamination occurs at the interface due to poor adhesion. Tie resins contain functional groups (e.g., maleic anhydride) that form chemical bonds with polar polymers like EVOH or PA, while their polyolefin backbone bonds with non-polar layers like PE or PP.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR) | $\text{OTR} = \dfrac{q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta p}$ | cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹ | $q$ = volume of O₂ permeated; $L$ = film thickness; $A$ = area; $\Delta p$ = partial pressure difference |
| Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR) | $\text{WVTR} = \dfrac{m \cdot L}{A \cdot t}$ | g·m⁻²·day⁻¹ | $m$ = mass of vapor; $t$ = time |
| Permeability Coefficient | $P = D \cdot S$ | cm³·cm·cm⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ | $D$ = diffusion coefficient; $S$ = solubility coefficient |
| Multi-Layer Effective Permeability (Series Resistance Model) | $\dfrac{L_T}{P_T} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{P_i}$ | — | Total resistance equals sum of individual layer resistances (analogous to electrical resistors in series) |
| Total Film Thickness | $L_T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i$ | µm | Sum of all individual layer thicknesses |
| Coating Weight (Metallization) | $\text{OD} = -\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | Optical Density | Ratio of transmitted to incident light intensity |
| Adhesive Bond Strength | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{w}$ | N·mm⁻¹ or N·15 mm⁻¹ | $F$ = peel force; $w$ = width of sample |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Series Resistance Model** is the most important equation for KTU exams. It treats permeation through a multi-layer film as if each layer is a resistor in series — the total resistance is the sum of individual resistances.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multi-layer film and sheet coating technology is the foundation of:
- **Food Packaging:** Retort pouches (PET/Aluminum Foil/CPP) for ready-to-eat meals
- **Pharmaceutical Blister Packs:** PVC/PVDC, PVC/Aclar®, PP-based cold-form foil laminates
- **Aseptic Liquid Packaging:** Paperboard/PE/Aluminum/PE (Tetra Pak-style) for milk and juice
- **Vacuum Insulated Panels:** Multi-layer metallized films for thermal insulation
- **Solar Control Films:** PET/Adhesive/Metallized layer for window tinting
- **Industrial Barrier Films:** Chemical-resistant multi-layer pouches for aggressive chemicals

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Multi-Layer Permeation Using the Series Resistance Model

When a permeant (e.g., O₂ or water vapor) diffuses through a multi-layer film, each layer offers a resistance proportional to its thickness divided by its permeability coefficient.

**Given:**
- A multi-layer film composed of $n$ layers, with thicknesses $L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n$ and permeabilities $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$.
- Steady-state permeation is assumed (Fick's First Law applies).

**Step 1 — Fick's First Law for a Single Layer:**
The steady-state flux $J$ through a single layer $i$ is given by:

$$J = -D_i \cdot \dfrac{dc}{dx}$$

Under steady-state conditions with linear concentration gradient:

$$J = \dfrac{D_i \cdot (c_{1} - c_{2})}{L_i} = \dfrac{P_i \cdot (p_1 - p_2)}{L_i}$$

Here, $P_i = D_i \cdot S_i$ (permeability is the product of diffusivity and solubility), and $p_1, p_2$ are the partial pressures on either side of the layer.

**Step 2 — Series Resistance Analogy:**
In steady state, the same flux $J$ passes through every layer. Rearranging for the pressure difference across each layer:

$$\Delta p_i = \dfrac{J \cdot L_i}{P_i}$$

**Step 3 — Summation of Pressure Drops:**
The total pressure difference across the film equals the sum of pressure differences across all layers:

$$\Delta p_{T} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta p_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{J \cdot L_i}{P_i}$$

**Step 4 — Solving for Flux:**
Factor out the constant flux $J$:

$$\Delta p_{T} = J \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{P_i}$$

Rearrange to express the flux through the multi-layer film:

$$J = \dfrac{\Delta p_{T}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{P_i}}$$

**Step 5 — Effective Permeability Definition:**
If we treat the entire multi-layer film as a single equivalent layer with effective permeability $P_T$ and total thickness $L_T$, then:

$$J = \dfrac{P_T \cdot \Delta p_{T}}{L_T}$$

Equating with the multi-layer expression:

$$\dfrac{P_T \cdot \Delta p_{T}}{L_T} = \dfrac{\Delta p_{T}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{P_i}}$$

**Step 6 — Final Series Resistance Equation:**
Cancel $\Delta p_T$ from both sides and invert:

$$\dfrac{L_T}{P_T} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{P_i}$$

This is the **Multi-Layer Series Resistance Model for Permeation** — a critical formula for KTU examinations.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:**
A three-layer film consists of:
- Layer 1 (PET): $L_1 = 12 \, \mu m$, $P_1 = 0.05 \, \text{cm}^3 \cdot \mu m / (m^2 \cdot day \cdot atm)$
- Layer 2 (EVOH): $L_2 = 8 \, \mu m$, $P_2 = 0.005 \, \text{cm}^3 \cdot \mu m / (m^2 \cdot day \cdot atm)$
- Layer 3 (LDPE sealant): $L_3 = 60 \, \mu m$, $P_3 = 2.5 \, \text{cm}^3 \cdot \mu m / (m^2 \cdot day \cdot atm)$

Calculate the **effective permeability** of the composite film.

**Step 1 — Calculate Total Thickness:**
$$L_T = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = 12 + 8 + 60 = 80 \, \mu m$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Sum of L/P Terms:**
$$\dfrac{L_1}{P_1} = \dfrac{12}{0.05} = 240$$

$$\dfrac{L_2}{P_2} = \dfrac{8}{0.005} = 1600$$

$$\dfrac{L_3}{P_3} = \dfrac{60}{2.5} = 24$$

$$\sum \dfrac{L_i}{P_i} = 240 + 1600 + 24 = 1864$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Series Resistance Equation:**

$$\dfrac{L_T}{P_T} = 1864$$

$$P_T = \dfrac{L_T}{1864} = \dfrac{80}{1864} = 0.0429 \, \text{cm}^3 \cdot \mu m / (m^2 \cdot day \cdot atm)$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Result:**
The effective permeability $P_T = 0.0429$ is much lower than the permeability of pure LDPE ($2.5$), confirming that the EVOH layer is providing the dominant barrier contribution. The thin EVOH layer (only 10% of total thickness) controls ~86% of the total barrier resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> Even a thin barrier layer (e.g., 5-10 µm of EVOH) drastically reduces overall permeability. This is why economic high-barrier structures use thin barrier cores sandwiched between thicker, cheaper structural polymers.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Layer Combinations and Their Applications

| Structure | Layers | Typical Total Thickness | Key Properties | Typical Applications |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PET / EVOH / LLDPE | 3 | 80-100 µm | High O₂ barrier, heat-sealable | Vacuum-packed meat, cheese |
| PET / Adhesive / Al Foil / Adhesive / LDPE | 5 | 70-90 µm | Total light + gas + moisture barrier | Retort pouches, coffee, pharma |
| BOPP / Print / Adhesive / LDPE | 3-4 | 50-70 µm | High clarity, moisture barrier | Snack food, biscuit wrappers |
| PA / EVOH / PA / Tie / LLDPE | 5-7 | 80-150 µm | Tough, O₂ barrier, formable | Thermoformed vacuum packs |
| PET / SiOx / PE | 3 | 50-80 µm | Transparent high barrier | Microwaveable food trays |
| PVC / PVDC | 2 | 200-300 µm | Moderate barrier, formable | Pharmaceutical blister packs |
| Paper / PE / Al Foil / PE | 4 | 300-500 µm | Rigid, total barrier | Aseptic liquid cartons |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Generic Multi-Layer Film Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    A1[Outer Layer - Printable, Scratch Resistant, e.g., PET or BOPP] --> A2[Barrier Layer - Oxygen or Moisture Barrier, e.g., EVOH, PVDC, Aluminum Foil]
    A2 --> A3[Tie Layer - Adhesion Promoter, e.g., Maleic Anhydride Grafted Polyolefin]
    A3 --> A4[Structural Layer - Bulk and Stiffness, e.g., LLDPE or LLDPE-rich Blend]
    A4 --> A5[Sealant Layer - Heat Sealable Inner Skin, e.g., mLLDPE, EVA, Ionomer]
    A5 --> A6[Product Contact Surface - Inner Face Touching the Packaged Good]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow: Coextrusion Line for Multi-Layer Film

```mermaid
graph LR
    B1[Extruder 1 - Outer Resin e.g., PET] --> C1[Feedblock or Multi-Manifold Die]
    B2[Extruder 2 - Barrier Resin e.g., EVOH] --> C1
    B3[Extruder 3 - Tie Resin e.g., PE-g-MAH] --> C1
    B4[Extruder 4 - Sealant Resin e.g., mLLDPE] --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Melt Stack Exit at Die Lips]
    D1 --> E1[Chill Roll Casting or Air Cooling]
    E1 --> F1[Thickness Gauging with Beta or Capacitive Sensors]
    F1 --> G1[Surface Treatment e.g., Corona Discharge]
    G1 --> H1[Winding Station with Slitting]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Extrusion Coating Process Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    C1[Substrate Unwind - e.g., Paper, BOPP, PET Film] --> D1[Pre-Treatment Station Corona or Flame]
    D1 --> E1[Nip Roller and Chill Roller Assembly]
    F1[Extruder Feeding Molten Polymer e.g., LDPE] --> E1
    E1 --> G1[Laminating Station for Secondary Substrate]
    G1 --> H1[Cooling and Edge Trimming]
    H1 --> I1[Re-wind Station for Coated Laminate Roll]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Layered Schematic: 5-Layer Asymmetric Structure (A/B/C/B/A Symmetric)

```mermaid
graph TB
    L1[Layer 5 - Outer Skin - PET 12 micrometer] --> L2[Layer 4 - Adhesive - PE-g-MAH 3 micrometer]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 - Core Barrier - EVOH 8 micrometer]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 2 - Adhesive - PE-g-MAH 3 micrometer]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 1 - Inner Sealant - mLLDPE 50 micrometer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the **A/B/C/B/A symmetric structure**, the central barrier layer (C) is flanked on both sides by identical tie layers (B) and outer skins (A). This symmetrical design provides balanced mechanical properties and reduces curl in thermoforming applications.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define a multi-layer film. List any two advantages of multi-layer films over mono-layer films in packaging applications.**

**Model Answer:**
A multi-layer film is a polymeric packaging structure composed of two or more distinct polymer layers that are combined through coextrusion, lamination, or coating processes to deliver integrated functional properties.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. **Functional Specialization:** Each layer can be optimized for a single function — barrier, sealing, printability, or mechanical strength — which is impossible in a mono-layer film.
2. **Cost Optimization:** Expensive barrier polymers (e.g., EVOH) can be used as thin core layers sandwiched between cheaper structural polymers, reducing overall material cost.
3. **Property Synergy:** Incompatible polymers (e.g., polar EVOH and non-polar PE) can be combined using tie layers, achieving property combinations impossible with blends.
4. **Mechanical Protection:** The barrier core is protected from external mechanical damage and pinholing by tough outer layers, improving long-term barrier performance.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: What is a tie layer? Why is it necessary in multi-layer coextruded films?**

**Model Answer:**
A tie layer (also called an adhesive layer or bonding layer) is a thin polymeric layer placed between two chemically dissimilar polymers in a multi-layer structure to promote interfacial adhesion.

**Necessity:**
Most polymers used in packaging (e.g., PE, PP, EVOH, PA, PET) are **thermodynamically immiscible** and have poor adhesion to each other. Without a tie layer, the multi-layer film would **delaminate** during processing or end-use. Tie resins contain polar functional groups (e.g., maleic anhydride, acrylic acid) grafted onto a polyolefin backbone. The polyolefin part bonds with non-polar layers, while the polar functional group reacts with polar polymers like EVOH or polyamide, creating a **chemical bridge** between incompatible materials. A common example is **PE-g-MAH** (polyethylene grafted with maleic anhydride) used to bond PE and EVOH.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the different methods used for manufacturing multi-layer films. Describe the **coextrusion** process in detail with a neat diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A multi-layer film has the following construction:
- PET (outer): thickness = 12 µm, permeability = 0.04 cm³·µm·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹
- EVOH (barrier): thickness = 6 µm, permeability = 0.003 cm³·µm·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹
- LDPE (sealant): thickness = 50 µm, permeability = 2.0 cm³·µm·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹

Calculate the **effective permeability** of the film using the series resistance model. Comment on the contribution of each layer. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The major methods for manufacturing multi-layer films are:

1. **Coextrusion:** Two or more polymers are melted in separate extruders and combined in a die to form an integrated multi-layer structure.
2. **Lamination:** Pre-formed films are bonded using adhesives (adhesive lamination) or by extruding a polymer melt between them (extrusion lamination).
3. **Coating:** A functional layer is applied onto a substrate via extrusion coating, gravure coating, slot-die coating, vacuum metallization, or PVDC/SioX coatings.

**Coextrusion Process — Detailed Explanation:**

Coextrusion uses **multiple extruders** (typically 2 to 7), each feeding a different polymer melt into a common die system. The two main die designs are:

- **Feedblock System:** Individual melt streams are combined at a feedblock located upstream of the die. Layer thickness is controlled by adjusting the relative flow rates from each extruder.
- **Multi-Manifold Die:** Each polymer has its own distribution manifold within the die body; layers are formed progressively as the die channels converge. Better for polymers with very different viscosities.

The combined melt stack exits the die as a single, layered sheet, which is then **cooled on a chill roll**, **gauged for thickness**, **surface-treated** (corona), and **wound into rolls**.

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 for the coextrusion process flow schematic.

**Key Advantages of Coextrusion:**
- No adhesive is required (saving cost and avoiding solvent emissions)
- High production speeds
- Excellent layer-to-layer bonding
- Precise thickness control

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Total Thickness:**
$$L_T = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = 12 + 6 + 50 = 68 \, \mu m$$

**Step 2 — Sum of L/P Ratios:**

$$\dfrac{L_1}{P_1} = \dfrac{12}{0.04} = 300$$

$$\dfrac{L_2}{P_2} = \dfrac{6}{0.003} = 2000$$

$$\dfrac{L_3}{P_3} = \dfrac{50}{2.0} = 25$$

$$\sum \dfrac{L_i}{P_i} = 300 + 2000 + 25 = 2325$$

**Step 3 — Apply Series Resistance Equation:**

$$P_T = \dfrac{L_T}{\sum \dfrac{L_i}{P_i}} = \dfrac{68}{2325} = 0.0292 \, cm^3 \cdot \mu m \cdot m^{-2} \cdot day^{-1} \cdot atm^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Layer Contributions:**
- The **EVOH layer**, though only 8.8% of total thickness, contributes **~86%** of the total barrier resistance (2000/2325).
- The **LDPE sealant layer** provides negligible barrier (only ~1.1% contribution).
- The **PET outer layer** contributes ~12.9% resistance and provides mechanical strength.
- This confirms that the **barrier layer dominates the overall permeability**, which is the entire engineering rationale behind multi-layer designs.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **extrusion coating** process with a neat diagram. List any **four applications** of extrusion-coated products. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various **coating techniques** used in plastics packaging — namely **PVDC coating, vacuum metallization, and SiOx coating** — with reference to their process principles, barrier performance, and end-use applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Extrusion Coating Process:**

Extrusion coating is the process of applying a molten polymer (typically LDPE, PP, or EVA) onto a moving substrate such as paper, paperboard, BOPET, BOPP, or aluminum foil.

**Process Steps:**
1. **Substrate Unwind:** The substrate (e.g., paper, foil, BOPP) is unwound from a roll under tension control.
2. **Pre-treatment:** The substrate passes through a **corona discharger** or flame treater to raise its surface energy, improving adhesion.
3. **Melt Extrusion:** A thermoplastic resin (typically LDPE at 280-330 °C) is melted in an extruder and forced through a **flat T-die** as a thin molten curtain.
4. **Lamination in the Nip:** The molten curtain is pressed between the substrate and a chilled roller at the **nip point**. The chill roll rapidly solidifies the polymer, forming a tightly bonded coating.
5. **Edge Trimming and Winding:** Excess edge material (edge bead) is trimmed, and the coated substrate is wound into rolls.

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.3 for the extrusion coating process schematic.

**Four Applications:**
1. Milk cartons (paperboard/PE/Al/PE)
2. Tetra Pak-style aseptic packaging
3. Coated paper cups for hot/cold beverages
4. Aluminum foil laminates for snack packaging

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**1. PVDC Coating (Polyvinylidene Chloride):**
- **Process:** PVDC latex (aqueous emulsion) is coated onto a substrate (typically BOPP, PET, or PVC) using gravure or smooth-roll coating, followed by drying in an oven.
- **Barrier Performance:** Excellent barrier to both oxygen and water vapor; OTR as low as 1-5 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹.
- **Applications:** Pharmaceutical blister packs (PVC/PVDC), cheese wraps, meat packaging.

**2. Vacuum Metallization:**
- **Process:** Aluminum is evaporated in a vacuum chamber and deposited as a thin metallic layer (30-100 nm) onto a polymer film (PET, BOPP, or PE).
- **Barrier Performance:** Provides a barrier to oxygen, moisture, and light. Typical OTR: 1-5 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹; WVTR: 0.3-1.0 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹.
- **Applications:** Snack food packaging, coffee pouches, decorative films, insulation foils.

**3. SiOx Coating (Silicon Oxide):**
- **Process:** Silicon monoxide is vaporized and deposited onto a polymer film in a vacuum chamber via plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition (PECVD).
- **Barrier Performance:** Excellent oxygen barrier (OTR < 1 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹) with transparency retained. Moderate moisture barrier.
- **Applications:** Transparent high-barrier packaging, microwaveable food trays, medical device packaging.

**Comparison Table:**

| Coating Type | OTR (cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹) | WVTR (g·m⁻²·day⁻¹) | Transparency | Recyclability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PVDC | 1-5 | 0.5-2 | Translucent | Limited |
| Al Metallization | 1-5 | 0.3-1.0 | Opaque | Difficult (Al layer) |
| SiOx | <1 | 1-5 | Highly transparent | Better (inorganic, separable) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Many students mix units between µm and cm in permeability calculations. **Always keep all thickness units identical** (preferably µm throughout the problem).
> 2. **Confusing Extrusion Coating with Coextrusion:** Coextrusion combines multiple molten polymers into a single die; extrusion coating deposits a polymer melt onto a **separate pre-formed substrate**.
> 3. **Forgetting Tie Layer Functions:** Examiners frequently ask why tie layers are needed. Always mention **maleic anhydride functional groups** that bridge polar and non-polar polymers.
> 4. **Confusing OTR with Permeability:** OTR is measured per unit area per day; permeability coefficient includes thickness. Use the correct formula based on the units given in the problem.
> 5. **Missing the Series Resistance Equation:** In multi-layer permeability problems, students often simply average permeabilities — this is **wrong**. The series resistance model must be applied.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Multi-layer films** are engineered composites where each layer performs a specialized function: print, barrier, structural, tie, or sealant.
- **Coextrusion**, **lamination**, and **coating** are the three principal manufacturing routes for multi-layer constructions.
- **Tie layers** (typically PE-g-MAH or EVA-based) are essential for bonding chemically dissimilar polymers like EVOH and PE.
- The **Series Resistance Model** governs multi-layer permeability:
  $$\dfrac{L_T}{P_T} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{P_i}$$
- **EVOH** is the workhorse oxygen-barrier polymer; **PVDC** offers balanced O₂ and moisture barrier; **metallized aluminum** provides total light barrier plus gas barrier; **SiOx** delivers transparent high-barrier performance.
- **Extrusion coating** deposits a molten polymer onto a substrate via a T-die and chill roll nip assembly.
- **Typical layer count** for high-performance packaging: **3 to 9 layers**.
- **High-barrier targets** to memorize: OTR < 1 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹ for oxygen-sensitive products; WVTR < 0.1 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹ for moisture-sensitive products.
- **Symmetric structures** (A/B/C/B/A) provide balanced properties; **asymmetric structures** (A/B/C/D/E) optimize cost and function but may curl under thermal stress.
- Always state the **functional role** of each layer when explaining a structure — examiners award marks for clarity of reasoning, not just naming polymers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laminations & Co-extrusions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laminations & Co-extrusions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Laminations & Co-extrusions — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Lamination — Formal Definition

**Lamination** is a converting process in which two or more webs of film, foil, paper, or non-woven substrates are joined together using an **adhesive** (chemical bonding) or **thermal energy** (heat-seal bonding) to produce a **multilayer composite structure** with engineered barrier, mechanical, and aesthetic properties that no single substrate can deliver alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Lamination is treated as a **post-extrusion converting operation** — the raw films are first produced, then laminated. Contrast this with co-extrusion, which is an **in-line primary forming operation**.

The substrates involved are typically:
- **Polyolefins** — BOPP, BOPET, LDPE, LLDPE, CPP, HDPE
- **Cellulosics** — paper, paperboard, cellulose film
- **Metallics** — aluminium foil (typical thickness **7 µm to 30 µm**)
- **Coated films** — PVDC-coated BOPP, PVdC-coated PET, metallized PET (VMPET)
- **Biopolymers** — PLA, PHBV

## 1.2 Co-extrusion — Formal Definition

**Co-extrusion** is a primary polymer-processing operation in which **two or more molten polymer streams** (resins with differing chemistries, viscosities, or melt temperatures) are **simultaneously combined inside a single die** and extruded as a **single, multilayer, structurally integrated film, sheet, or pipe** in one continuous step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinguishing concept:** Lamination *joins pre-made webs*. Co-extrusion *co-extrudes the layers from the melt state simultaneously*. The latter is faster, requires fewer adhesive chemicals, but is far more capital-intensive.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sandwich Engineering" View

Imagine you are making a sandwich at home:

| Component | Real-world analogy | Lamination | Co-extrusion |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------|---------------|
| Bread (structural) | Mechanical stiffness | PET / BOPP outer layer | Outer polymer melt |
| Filling (active property) | Cheese, lettuce, tomato | Adhesive / barrier foil | EVOH / PA / tie-layer melt |
| Bread (sealing) | Inner protection | LDPE / CPP inner layer | Sealing polymer melt (LLDPE / mLLDPE) |

> **Lamination = stacking cold, pre-baked breads with butter.**  
> **Co-extrusion = pouring three breads simultaneously in a single mould to bake one integrated sandwich loaf.**

Both produce a multi-layer composite. But **lamination is a joining process**, whereas **co-extrusion is a forming + joining process in one shot**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Standard **adhesive coat weight** in flexible packaging laminates: **1.5 g/m² to 5.0 g/m²** (dry basis).
- Standard **tie-layer thickness** in co-extruded structures: **5 µm to 15 µm**.
- Typical **adhesive cure temperature** for solvent-based PU: **35 °C to 45 °C** (in drying tunnel).
- **Lamination nip pressure**: typically **30 N/cm to 80 N/cm** of web width.
- **Co-extrusion line speed** (modern flat-die systems): up to **500 m/min**.

> [!NOTE]
> These numbers are **industry-standard benchmarks**, not KTU-fixed values. Examiners frequently quote ranges — memorize the orders of magnitude, not exact decimals.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lamination — Process Logic (Stepwise)

Lamination, irrespective of type, follows a **five-stage workflow**:

1. **Web unwinding & tension control** — substrates fed at constant tension (**10 N/m to 50 N/m**) using dancer rolls or load cells.
2. **Adhesive application** — adhesive applied to the primary (printing) substrate via **gravure cylinder** or **smooth-roll transfer**.
3. **Drying / curing** — solvent removed (for wet/solvent lamination) at **60 °C to 110 °C** in a tunnel.
4. **Nip lamination** — second substrate pressed against adhesive-coated first substrate between a **heated steel roller** and a **rubber nip roller**.
5. **Winding & slitting** — laminate reeled under controlled taper tension (**10% to 30% tension taper**).

### 2.1.1 Types of Lamination

#### (a) Wet Lamination
- Adhesive (typically **PVA-acrylic or starch**) is applied **wet**.
- Substrates must be **permeable** (paper) so that solvent/water can evaporate through the porous layer.
- Used for **paper–paper**, **paper–foil**, **paper–film** laminates.
- Maximum line speeds: **150 m/min to 250 m/min**.

#### (b) Dry Lamination
- Adhesive (typically **solvent-based PU** or **water-based PU**) is applied, then **dried in an oven** before nip.
- Both substrates are **impermeable**.
- Dominant technique for **BOPP/foil/PE, PET/PE, BOPP/met-PET/PE** food-packaging laminates.
- Line speeds: **300 m/min to 500 m/min**.

#### (c) Solventless Lamination
- **100 % solid, solvent-free PU adhesive** applied between heated rollers.
- No drying tunnel → **lower energy cost**.
- Lower CO₂ emissions and zero VOC → **environmentally preferred**.
- Bond strength development requires **curing at room temperature for 24–48 hours**.

#### (d) Extrusion Lamination / Extrusion Coating
- **LDPE or EVA melt** is extruded from a flat die (T-die) directly between two substrates.
- The polymer acts as **both adhesive and sealant**.
- Commonly used for **Tetra Pak carton stock**, **liquid pouches**.
- Melt temperature: **280 °C to 320 °C**.

#### (e) Hot-Melt Lamination
- **Thermoplastic adhesive** (EVA, SBC, APAO) melted at **150 °C to 180 °C**, applied via slot die, then nip-laminated before the adhesive cools.
- Used for **labels, hygiene products, bookbinding**.

#### (f) Thermal (Heat) Lamination
- **No external adhesive** — substrates are sealed by **heat + pressure only**.
- Requires at least one **coextrudable / heat-sealable layer** (PE, EVA).
- Used for **blister packs, vacuum-formed lidding films**.

### 2.1.2 Adhesive Selection Logic

| Substrate combination | Recommended adhesive chemistry | Reason |
|-----------------------|--------------------------------|--------|
| BOPP / PE | Solvent-borne PU | Good bond to non-polar polyolefins |
| PET / Al-foil | PU + epoxy curing agent | High bond on metallic surface |
| Paper / Foil | Water-based acrylic | Heat-resistant, fast-setting |
| BOPP / CPP | Solventless PU | Low migration, food-safe |
| LDPE / mLLDPE | None — thermal seal only | Both heat-sealable |

### 2.1.3 Why Adhesion Occurs — The "Why" Behind the Bond

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Adhesion in laminates is governed by **three classical mechanisms** — examiners expect you to name and apply them:

1. **Mechanical interlocking** — adhesive penetrates substrate pores/roughness.
2. **Chemical bonding** — covalent or ionic bonds across the interface (e.g., PU–OH groups reacting with substrate –COOH).
3. **Thermodynamic (wetting) adhesion** — adhesive **surface tension must be lower than substrate critical surface energy** (Young's equation — see §2.3).

## 2.2 Co-extrusion — Process Logic (Stepwise)

A modern co-extrusion line executes the following **eight stages**:

1. **Independent resin feeding** — each resin hopper feeds its own extruder.
2. **Plasticization** — each extruder melts its polymer at its **optimum melt temperature** (typically **180 °C to 300 °C** depending on polymer).
3. **Metering & filtering** — gear pumps ensure **mass-flow stability**; screen packs remove gels.
4. **Stream combining** — polymer streams merged either in a **feedblock** (upstream of die) or in a **multi-manifold die**.
5. **Layer distribution & thickness profile control** — flow distribution blocks tune layer ratios.
6. **Die forming** — flat (slit) die for sheets/films or annular die for blown film.
7. **Cooling & orientation** — chill roll (cast film) or air ring + bubble (blown film); optional **MDO / TDO orientation**.
8. **Winding** — surface or centre winder with tension control.

### 2.2.1 Two Architectural Families of Co-extrusion Dies

#### (a) Feedblock Co-extrusion
- All polymer streams meet **inside the feedblock, before the die**.
- The feedblock uses **vanes and plates** to divide, stack, and re-stack layer flows.
- Best for **flat cast film and sheet** (up to ~7 layers).
- Limitation: polymers with widely different melt temperatures or viscosities cause **flow instabilities** at the merging point.

#### (b) Multi-Manifold Die Co-extrusion
- Each polymer has its **own manifold and land** inside the die.
- Streams converge **inside the die, just before the die lip**.
- Required for **blown film** because of the circular geometry.
- Better control of **individual layer thickness**.
- More expensive; more complex manifold balancing.

### 2.2.2 Tie Layers — The Hidden Glue

Tie layers are **functionalised polyolefins** (typically **maleic-anhydride-grafted PE or PP**) that bond dissimilar polymers like **EVOH/PE** or **PA/PE** through reactive functional groups.

> [!NOTE]
> The tie-layer thickness is typically **5 µm to 15 µm** — small in volume but **functionally critical**. Skipping the tie layer in a PA/EVOH/PE co-extrusion causes immediate **delamination** because of poor interfacial adhesion between polar (EVOH, PA) and non-polar (PE, PP) phases.

### 2.2.3 Flow Instabilities (Why Co-extrusion Is Hard)

Co-extrusion suffers from two well-known interfacial instabilities:

- **Interfacial waviness** — driven by **viscosity mismatch** between adjacent layers.
- **Layer encapsulation / encapsulation breakdown** — the lower-viscosity layer **engulfs** the higher-viscosity one due to second- (and higher-) normal-stress differences.

The **viscosity ratio** $\eta_{\text{ratio}}$ between adjacent layers is the key control parameter:

$$\eta_{\text{ratio}} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{inner}}}{\eta_{\text{outer}}}$$

> Stability is favoured when $\eta_{\text{ratio}} \to 1$. A ratio outside **0.5 to 2.0** typically triggers visible flow irregularities.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-prep note:** Memorise the **forms**, not the exact numerical coefficients. KTU questions typically ask for *qualitative reasoning* with one of these formulae.

| # | Equation | Symbols | Use in packaging engineering |
|---|----------|---------|------------------------------|
| 1 | $\sigma_{\text{bond}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{peel}}}{w}$ | $\sigma_{\text{bond}}$ = bond strength, $F_{\text{peel}}$ = peel force, $w$ = width | Quantifying laminate adhesion — standard **N/15 mm** or **N/inch** |
| 2 | $\theta = \gamma_{\text{SV}} - \gamma_{\text{SL}} - \gamma_{\text{LV}}$ (Young, contact angle form) | $\theta$ = contact angle, $\gamma$ = interfacial tensions | Predicting adhesive wetting on substrate |
| 3 | $W_{\text{adh}} = \gamma_{\text{A}} + \gamma_{\text{B}} - \gamma_{\text{AB}}$ (Young–Dupré work of adhesion) | $W_{\text{adh}}$ = work of adhesion | Higher $W_{\text{adh}}$ → stronger laminate bond |
| 4 | $R_{\text{coat}} = \dfrac{v \cdot c_{\text{solid}}}{\rho_{\text{adhesive}}}$ | $R_{\text{coat}}$ = dry coat weight, $v$ = wet applicator speed | Gravure-cylinder adhesive lay-down calculation |
| 5 | $Q = k \cdot \Delta P / \eta$ | $Q$ = volumetric flow, $k$ = die constant, $\Delta P$ = pressure drop, $\eta$ = melt viscosity | Layer thickness control in co-extrusion die |
| 6 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{6 Q}{W \cdot H^{2}}$ | $\dot{\gamma}$ = apparent shear rate, $W,H$ = die land dimensions | Viscosity selection in die design |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{ratio}} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{inner}}}{\eta_{\text{outer}}}$ | — | Co-extrusion flow-stability criterion |
| 8 | $t_{\text{layer}} = \dfrac{Q_i}{\sum Q_i} \cdot t_{\text{total}}$ | $t_{\text{layer}}$ = individual layer thickness | Layer-ratio design in feedblock |
| 9 | $\Pi = \dfrac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot \dfrac{H^{2}}{12 \, \eta \, Q}$ | — | Die balancing parameter |
| 10 | $\text{OTR} = \dfrac{P_0 \cdot A \cdot t}{m_{\text{gas}}}$ | OTR = oxygen transmission rate | Barrier-film design justification |

> **Note:** Equation 10 is **not a single universal constant** — OTR is empirically measured (cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹) and depends on polymer, temperature, and humidity. The equation above is the *transmission-derivation form*.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where These Concepts Are Used

- **Food packaging** — retortable pouches (PET/Al-foil/PP co-extruded or laminated)
- **Pharmaceutical blister packs** — co-extruded PVC/PVDC/PVC
- **Agricultural films** — multilayer co-extruded LLDPE/EVA/LLDPE
- **Aseptic cartons** — extrusion-laminated paperboard / PE / Al / PE
- **Stand-up pouches** — laminated BOPET / foil / LLDPE structures

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section shows **two worked analytical problems** typical of KTU Part B (14-mark) questions. Both involve long derivations, so they are written with every algebraic step explicit. No steps are skipped.

---

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Bond Strength & Work of Adhesion Derivation

### 3.1.1 Statement

A laminate structure **BOPP (12 µm) / PU adhesive / LDPE (50 µm)** is peel-tested at 180°. The peel force recorded is **2.4 N over a 15 mm wide specimen**. Compute:

(a) The peel bond strength in N/m.  
(b) The work of adhesion assuming the interfacial tensions are: $\gamma_{\text{BOPP}} = 31 \, \text{mN/m}$, $\gamma_{\text{LDPE}} = 26 \, \text{mN/m}$, and $\gamma_{\text{BOPP/LDPE interface}} = 1.8 \, \text{mN/m}$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a) — Bond strength**

The standard peel-strength formula:

$$\sigma_{\text{bond}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{peel}}}{w}$$

Substitute $F_{\text{peel}} = 2.4 \, \text{N}$ and $w = 15 \, \text{mm} = 0.015 \, \text{m}$:

$$\sigma_{\text{bond}} = \dfrac{2.4 \, \text{N}}{0.015 \, \text{m}}$$

$$\sigma_{\text{bond}} = 160 \, \text{N/m}$$

Convert to the **KTU-recommended unit N/15 mm** (which is the same number since the width was already 15 mm):

$$\sigma_{\text{bond}} = 2.4 \, \text{N} / 15 \, \text{mm} = 160 \, \text{N/m}$$

> **Industry benchmark:** $\sigma_{\text{bond}} \geq 1.5 \, \text{N/15 mm}$ is the minimum acceptable bond for a **food-grade laminate**. Therefore, **this laminate passes** with a comfortable margin.

**Part (b) — Work of adhesion (Young–Dupré form)**

The Young–Dupré equation for the work of adhesion between two phases A and B joined through an interface of tension $\gamma_{AB}$:

$$W_{\text{adh}} = \gamma_{\text{A}} + \gamma_{\text{B}} - \gamma_{\text{AB}}$$

Substitute the given values:

$$W_{\text{adh}} = 31 + 26 - 1.8$$

$$W_{\text{adh}} = 55.2 \, \text{mN/m}$$

$$W_{\text{adh}} = 5.52 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{J/m}^{2}$$

> **Interpretation:** A $W_{\text{adh}}$ of $55.2 \, \text{mN/m}$ indicates a **good thermodynamic driving force** for adhesion between BOPP and LDPE through the PU adhesive layer. This is consistent with the observed high peel strength in part (a).

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Co-extrusion Layer-Thickness Calculation

### 3.2.1 Statement

A three-layer **co-extruded cast film** is designed as:  
**Layer 1 (LLDPE, seal) / Layer 2 (EVOH, barrier) / Layer 3 (PP, structural)**.  
Total film thickness target: **80 µm**. The volumetric throughputs from each extruder are:  
$Q_1 = 12 \, \text{cm}^{3}/\text{s}$ (LLDPE),  
$Q_2 = 4 \, \text{cm}^{3}/\text{s}$ (EVOH),  
$Q_3 = 20 \, \text{cm}^{3}/\text{s}$ (PP).

(a) Calculate the individual layer thicknesses.  
(b) Compute the **viscosity ratio** $\eta_{\text{ratio}}$ between EVOH and PP layers if their melt viscosities at the die shear rate are $\eta_{\text{EVOH}} = 350 \, \text{Pa\cdot s}$ and $\eta_{\text{PP}} = 280 \, \text{Pa\cdot s}$. Comment on co-extrusion stability.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a) — Layer thicknesses**

The thickness of any layer $i$ is proportional to its volumetric share of the total flow:

$$t_i = \dfrac{Q_i}{Q_{\text{total}}} \cdot t_{\text{total}}$$

First, compute the total volumetric flow:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 = 12 + 4 + 20 = 36 \, \text{cm}^{3}/\text{s}$$

Now compute each layer:

$$t_1 = \dfrac{12}{36} \times 80 = \dfrac{1}{3} \times 80 \approx 26.67 \, \mu\text{m}$$

$$t_2 = \dfrac{4}{36} \times 80 = \dfrac{1}{9} \times 80 \approx 8.89 \, \mu\text{m}$$

$$t_3 = \dfrac{20}{36} \times 80 = \dfrac{5}{9} \times 80 \approx 44.44 \, \mu\text{m}$$

> **Verification:** $26.67 + 8.89 + 44.44 = 80.00 \, \mu\text{m}$ ✓

**Part (b) — Viscosity ratio and stability**

$$\eta_{\text{ratio}} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{inner}}}{\eta_{\text{outer}}}$$

Here, EVOH is sandwiched between two outer layers; **treating PP as the "outer" reference** for a conservative check:

$$\eta_{\text{ratio}} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{EVOH}}}{\eta_{\text{PP}}} = \dfrac{350}{280} = 1.25$$

> **Stability check:** The ratio **1.25 lies well within the safe window** of **0.5 to 2.0**. Co-extrusion is **expected to be stable** — minimal interfacial waviness and no encapsulation breakdown predicted.

If, however, the extruder temperatures pushed EVOH viscosity up to $\eta_{\text{EVOH}} = 700 \, \text{Pa\cdot s}$ (cold EVOH), then:

$$\eta_{\text{ratio}} = \dfrac{700}{280} = 2.5$$

> This **exceeds the safe limit** of 2.0 — the **EVOH layer would tend to encapsulate** the PP layer due to second-normal-stress difference, producing wavy interfaces. Mitigation: **raise die temperature locally for the EVOH stream** to bring its viscosity down.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Lamination vs Co-extrusion

| Feature | Lamination | Co-extrusion |
|---------|-----------|--------------|
| Process stage | Secondary (converting) | Primary (forming + joining) |
| Capital cost | Moderate (₹₹) | High (₹₹₹₹) |
| Adhesive required | Yes (PU, acrylic, EVA) | No — except tie layer |
| Layer count possible | 2–5 (typical) | Up to **11+** in advanced systems |
| Choice of substrates | **Any combination** (paper/foil/film) | Limited to **thermoplastic polymers** (must all melt-process) |
| Thickness control | Limited by substrate gauges | **Continuous tuning** via gear-pump ratios |
| Output speed | 100–500 m/min | Up to **500 m/min** (cast) |
| Migration risk | Higher (adhesive residue) | Lower (no solvent) |
| Barrier film capability | Excellent (with foil) | Excellent (with EVOH/PA) |
| Recyclability | Difficult — adhesive contamination | Easier — same-resin family structures recyclable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lamination Process — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid block represents the **process-flow architecture** of a typical solvent-based **dry-lamination line**, showing how each functional module interacts with the others.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Unwind Roll 1 - Primary Web] --> B1[Tension Control - Dancer Roll]
    A2[Unwind Roll 2 - Secondary Web] --> B2[Tension Control - Dancer Roll]
    B1 --> C1[Corona Treater - Surface Activation]
    C1 --> D1[Gravure Adhesive Applicator]
    D1 --> E1[Drying Tunnel - 60 to 110 deg C]
    E1 --> F1[Heated Nip Roller - 40 to 80 N/cm]
    B2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Laminated Web Take-up]
    G1 --> H1[Slitter Rewinder]
    H1 --> I1[Finished Reel Output]

    J1[Adhesive Reservoir] --> D1
    K1[Solvent Recovery System] --> E1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The primary web (e.g., printed BOPP) is **corona-treated** to raise its surface energy above the adhesive's surface tension, then **adhesive-coated** via the gravure cylinder, **dried**, and finally **nip-bonded** to the secondary web. The solvent recovery system is a regulatory necessity for VOC compliance.

---

## 4.2 Co-extrusion Line — Multi-Layer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Extruder A - LLDPE 200 deg C] --> P1[Gear Pump A]
    E2[Extruder B - EVOH 220 deg C] --> P2[Gear Pump B]
    E3[Extruder C - PP 230 deg C] --> P3[Gear Pump C]
    E4[Extruder D - Tie Layer 210 deg C] --> P4[Gear Pump D]

    P1 --> FB[Feedblock - Layer Stacker]
    P2 --> FB
    P3 --> FB
    P4 --> FB

    FB --> DIE[Flat Cast Die - 80 to 120 micron lip gap]
    DIE --> CR[Chill Roll - 20 deg C]
    CR --> MDO[MDO Orientation Optional]
    MDO --> W1[Trim and Edge Recycle]
    W1 --> W2[Surface Winder]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Four extruders feed four gear pumps, which deliver **mass-flow-controlled** streams into a **feedblock**. The feedblock stacks the layers in the correct order (typically **PP / tie / EVOH / tie / LLDPE**), and the combined stream is shaped by a flat cast die onto a chilled roll.

---

## 4.3 Layer Architecture — Typical 7-Layer Food-Barrier Film

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Outer LLDPE - Seal Layer 15 micron] --> L2[Tie Layer MAH-g-PE 5 micron]
    L2 --> L3[EVOH Barrier 5 micron]
    L3 --> L4[Tie Layer MAH-g-PE 5 micron]
    L4 --> L5[Core PA Nylon 15 micron]
    L5 --> L6[Tie Layer 5 micron]
    L6 --> L7[Outer PET Structural 30 micron]

    style L3 fill:#ffe599,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Highlighted in amber:** The **EVOH layer** is the active oxygen barrier. Damage to this single layer defeats the entire package's oxygen protection — a critical concept in food packaging.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define lamination. List any four types of lamination used in plastics packaging.**

**Model Answer:**
Lamination is a converting process in which two or more webs of plastic film, foil, or paper are bonded together using an adhesive or thermal energy to form a multilayer composite with combined properties.

Four types of lamination:
1. **Wet lamination** — adhesive applied wet; used for paper substrates.
2. **Dry lamination** — adhesive dried in an oven before nip bonding.
3. **Extrusion lamination / coating** — molten polymer extruded between substrates acts as adhesive.
4. **Solventless lamination** — 100 % solid PU adhesive, no solvent drying.

(Additional acceptable answers: thermal lamination, hot-melt lamination.)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is co-extrusion? Differentiate between feedblock and multi-manifold die co-extrusion.**

**Model Answer:**
Co-extrusion is the simultaneous extrusion of two or more molten polymer streams through a single die to produce a multilayer film or sheet in one continuous step.

| Aspect | Feedblock Co-extrusion | Multi-Manifold Die Co-extrusion |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Merging point | Upstream of die, inside the feedblock | Inside the die, near the die lip |
| Geometry | Best for flat films and sheets | Required for blown (circular) film |
| Layer count | Up to ~7 layers | Up to 11+ layers |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Layer-thickness control | Moderate | Excellent |

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Lose 2 marks** if you confuse *lamination* with *co-extrusion* as the same process.
> - **Lose 3 marks** if you skip the **tie-layer explanation** in co-extrusion barrier structures.
> - **Lose 2 marks** if you forget to mention **why adhesion occurs** (mechanical/chemical/wetting) — examiners *expect* the bonding-mechanism sentence.
> - **Lose 1 mark** per missing unit when reporting bond strength or layer thickness.

---

### Question A — Choice 1 [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat process flow diagram the **solventless lamination process** for a BOPP/PE laminate. Discuss the role of solventless PU adhesive and the **bonding mechanisms** involved.

**(b) [7 marks]** A laminate peel test gives a peel force of **3.6 N over a 25 mm wide specimen**. Compute the bond strength in **N/m** and comment on whether it satisfies the food-grade acceptance criterion of **≥ 2.0 N/15 mm**.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative to Question A) [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** With a block diagram, describe a **three-layer co-extrusion line** for producing a barrier film of structure **PE / tie / EVOH / tie / PE**. Explain the function of each layer and the need for tie layers.

**(b) [7 marks]** A co-extrusion die delivers layer flow rates of $Q_{\text{PE}} = 18 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{s}$, $Q_{\text{EVOH}} = 6 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{s}$, and $Q_{\text{PE}} = 18 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{s}$ (rear and front PE assumed equal). If the total film gauge is **100 µm**, find each layer thickness. If $\eta_{\text{EVOH}} = 500 \, \text{Pa\cdot s}$ and $\eta_{\text{PE}} = 250 \, \text{Pa\cdot s}$, calculate the viscosity ratio and comment on co-extrusion stability.

---

### Model Solutions for Question A

**Solution to A(a):**

**Process flow of solventless lamination:**

1. **Web preparation** — BOPP (printed) and PE films unwound under controlled tension.
2. **Adhesive application** — 100 % solid solventless PU adhesive (two-part: polyol + isocyanate) is metered between two heated transfer rollers and applied at **1.5–3.0 g/m²** coat weight onto the BOPP substrate.
3. **No drying tunnel** — solventless adhesives contain no volatile carrier, so the **drying stage is eliminated**.
4. **Nip lamination** — PE substrate pressed against adhesive-coated BOPP at **40–80 N/cm** nip pressure between a heated steel roll and a rubber roll.
5. **Curing** — laminate reeled and held at **room temperature for 24–48 hours** for PU cross-linking.
6. **Slitting & rewinding** — finished reels produced.

**Role of solventless PU adhesive:**
- Provides **high bond strength** with no VOC emissions.
- **Two-part system** (polyol + MDI isocyanate) cross-links to form a thermoset adhesive network.
- Approved for **direct food contact** in many jurisdictions (FDA 21 CFR, EU 10/2011).

**Bonding mechanisms:**
1. **Mechanical interlocking** — adhesive flows into micro-roughness of BOPP/PE surfaces.
2. **Chemical bonding** — NCO groups of isocyanate react with –OH/–COOH groups on corona-treated BOPP, forming urethane/urea linkages.
3. **Wetting / thermodynamic adhesion** — low surface-tension PU spreads over the substrate (verified by Young–Dupré work of adhesion).

**Solution to A(b):**

$$\sigma_{\text{bond}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{peel}}}{w} = \dfrac{3.6 \, \text{N}}{0.025 \, \text{m}} = 144 \, \text{N/m}$$

Convert to **N/15 mm** (the food-industry standard test width):

$$\sigma_{\text{bond, 15mm}} = \dfrac{3.6 \, \text{N}}{25 \, \text{mm}} \times 15 \, \text{mm} = 2.16 \, \text{N/15 mm}$$

> **Comment:** $\sigma_{\text{bond, 15mm}} = 2.16 \, \text{N/15 mm} \geq 2.0 \, \text{N/15 mm}$. The laminate **passes the food-grade acceptance criterion** with a small margin.

---

### Model Solutions for Question B

**Solution to B(a):**

A three-extruder co-extrusion line produces a symmetric **PE / tie / EVOH / tie / PE** five-layer structure (two outer PE layers + two tie layers + central EVOH).

**Block diagram (functional modules):**

Extruder 1 (PE) → Gear Pump 1 → Feedblock  
Extruder 2 (tie resin) → Gear Pump 2 → Feedblock  
Extruder 3 (EVOH) → Gear Pump 3 → Feedblock  

Feedblock stacks the layers → Flat cast die → Chill roll → Winder.

**Function of each layer:**
- **PE (outer)** — heat-sealable, moisture barrier, mechanical protection.
- **Tie layer (MAH-grafted PE)** — bridges the polar EVOH with non-polar PE; provides **chemical bonding** at the interface via maleic-anhydride functional groups reacting with EVOH hydroxyls.
- **EVOH (centre)** — primary **oxygen barrier**; lowest OTR among commercial packaging polymers when dry.

**Why tie layers are essential:**  
EVOH is polar, PE is non-polar — they are **thermodynamically incompatible**. Without the tie layer, the interface would delaminate on cooling due to internal stress and lack of interfacial adhesion. Tie layers also act as **sacrificial layers** that absorb orientation-induced stress.

**Solution to B(b):**

Total volumetric flow:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 18 + 6 + 18 = 42 \, \text{cm}^{3}/\text{s}$$

Each layer thickness:

$$t_{\text{PE front}} = \dfrac{18}{42} \times 100 = 42.86 \, \mu\text{m}$$

$$t_{\text{EVOH}} = \dfrac{6}{42} \times 100 = 14.29 \, \mu\text{m}$$

$$t_{\text{PE rear}} = \dfrac{18}{42} \times 100 = 42.86 \, \mu\text{m}$$

**Verification:** $42.86 + 14.29 + 42.86 = 100.01 \approx 100 \, \mu\text{m}$ ✓

**Viscosity ratio:**

$$\eta_{\text{ratio}} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{EVOH}}}{\eta_{\text{PE}}} = \dfrac{500}{250} = 2.0$$

> **Stability comment:** $\eta_{\text{ratio}} = 2.0$ sits **exactly at the upper limit** of the safe window (**0.5 ≤ ratio ≤ 2.0**). The system is **marginally stable** — any further increase in EVOH viscosity (e.g., from colder melt) would push the ratio above 2.0, risking **interfacial encapsulation and waviness**. Mitigation: increase the EVOH melt temperature by **10–15 °C** to reduce its viscosity to ~350 Pa·s, lowering the ratio to 1.4.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final-exam checklist — tick each before submitting your answer:**

- **Lamination ≠ Co-extrusion.** Lamination joins *pre-made* webs; co-extrusion extrudes *multiple melts* in one die.
- **Six types of lamination** to memorise: wet, dry, solventless, extrusion, hot-melt, thermal.
- **Two co-extrusion architectures**: feedblock (flat films) vs multi-manifold die (blown films).
- **Three bonding mechanisms** in lamination: mechanical interlocking, chemical bonding, thermodynamic wetting.
- **Tie layer** is mandatory between polar (EVOH, PA) and non-polar (PE, PP) polymers.
- **Adhesive coat weight** typical range: **1.5–5.0 g/m²** (dry basis).
- **Lamination bond strength** unit to memorise: **N/15 mm** (food-grade minimum ≈ **1.5–2.0 N/15 mm**).
- **Co-extrusion viscosity ratio** safe window: **0.5 ≤ η_inner / η_outer ≤ 2.0**.
- **Layer-thickness formula**: $t_i = (Q_i / Q_{\text{total}}) \cdot t_{\text{total}}$.
- **Work of adhesion (Young–Dupré)**: $W_{\text{adh}} = \gamma_A + \gamma_B - \gamma_{AB}$.
- **Primary oxygen barrier** in food packaging = **EVOH** (must be protected from moisture by surrounding PE/PP).
- **Solventless lamination** = no drying tunnel → lower energy + zero VOC; but requires **24–48 h room-temperature cure**.
- **Extrusion lamination** uses an **extruded polymer melt** (LDPE/EVA, 280–320 °C) as both adhesive and sealant.
- **Process stage**: lamination = *secondary converting*; co-extrusion = *primary forming*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stretch and Shrink Wrap
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stretch and Shrink Wrap

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Stretch Wrap** (also called **Stretch Film** or **Pallet Wrap**) is a highly elastic, plasticized polyolefin-based packaging film — predominantly manufactured from **Linear Low-Density Polyethylene (LLDPE)** — which is mechanically stretched and wrapped around a load (typically palletized goods) to create a tight, unitized, and protective package through **elastic memory and cling force**, without the application of external heat.

**Shrink Wrap** (also called **Shrink Film**) is a heat-reactive plastic packaging film — commonly produced from **Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)**, **Polyolefin (POF / crosslinked polyolefin)**, or **Polyethylene (PE)** — which, after being loosely draped around a product, undergoes **thermal shrinkage** (when passed through a heat tunnel at temperatures typically between **120 °C and 200 °C**) to conform tightly to the contours of the packaged article, generating a rigid, tamper-evident, and dust/moisture-resistant barrier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Both stretch and shrink films belong to the family of **secondary packaging materials** that rely on polymer chain orientation and recovery behavior — *not adhesives* — to provide mechanical integrity to a package.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are wrapping a sandwich for lunch with a piece of thin, ultra-stretchy cling-film. You pull it taut across the sandwich, and the film "remembers" being stretched, so it grips the sandwich firmly — this is **stretch wrapping** in its simplest form.

Now imagine instead that you have a piece of loose, slack plastic sheet around the sandwich, and you pass it through a hair-dryer. The sheet magically tightens around every curve of the sandwich — this is **shrink wrapping**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Single Key Distinction:**
> - **Stretch Wrap** = Elastic stretching **at room temperature** (mechanical energy stored in polymer chains).
> - **Shrink Wrap** = Thermal activation **at elevated temperature** (pre-stretched polymer chains relax and recover their original dimensions).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Performance Indicators

The two most cited industrial performance metrics for stretch and shrink films are:

- **Stretch Ratio (λ)** — typically **2:1 to 3:1** for hand films and up to **5:1** for high-performance machine films. **Dimensional unit: dimensionless.**
- **Shrink Percentage (%)** — measured per ASTM D2732, typical values: **PVC: 50–60 %**, **POF: 60–75 %**, **PE: 70–80 %** (both MD and TD directions).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Polymer Science Behind the Behavior

#### 2.1.1 Why Stretch Films "Hold" a Load

Polyolefin films used for stretch wrapping are produced via **cast extrusion** or **blown film extrusion**, which orients the long-chain polyethylene molecules in the **machine direction (MD)**. When the film is mechanically stretched during wrapping:

1. The amorphous regions of the semi-crystalline polymer uncoil.
2. The coiled chains are forced into an extended conformation.
3. This stores **elastic potential energy** within the film.
4. Upon release from the wrapping head, the chains attempt to recoil, producing a **clamping force** on the load.

The resultant holding force is governed primarily by **film tension**, **elastic modulus**, and **cling force**.

#### 2.1.2 Why Shrink Films "Tighten" with Heat

Shrink films are produced in a two-stage process:

1. **Extrusion** of the polymer (often crosslinked for POF).
2. **Orientation** — the film is stretched (typically **4× to 6×** in MD and **7× to 10×** in TD) at a temperature just above the polymer's **glass transition temperature (Tg)** but below its melting point (Tm). At this temperature, polymer chains are mobile enough to align but frozen in this strained orientation upon rapid **quenching (cooling)**.

When the finished film is reheated (during packaging) to a temperature **near or above its orientation temperature**, the chains regain mobility and **snap back** to their pre-oriented state — this is **shrinkage**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically grounded** in ASTM standard methods (D3826, D2732, D4649) commonly referenced in packaging engineering examinations.

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stretch Ratio (λ)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{stretched}}{L_{original}}$ | dimensionless | $\lambda \geq 1$; industrial range $\lambda \in [1.5,\ 5]$ |
| 2 | **Gauge Conversion** | $\text{thickness (µm)} = \text{gauge (mil)} \times 25.4$ | µm | $1 \text{ mil} = 25.4 \text{ µm}$ |
| 3 | **Yield (area per kg)** | $\text{Yield} = \dfrac{L \times W}{\text{weight per roll}}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{kg}$ | Used to compare film economics |
| 4 | **Free Shrink (ASTM D2732)** | $\text{Shrink (\%)} = \dfrac{L_{before} - L_{after}}{L_{before}} \times 100$ | % | Tested in **oil bath** at 150 °C for 60 s |
| 5 | **Holding Force on Pallet** | $F_{hold} = \mu \cdot N \cdot n_{layers}$ | N | $\mu$ = friction coeff., $N$ = normal load, $n_{layers}$ = number of film layers |
| 6 | **Elastic Recovery (R)** | $R = \dfrac{L_{stretched} - L_{recovered}}{L_{stretched} - L_{original}} \times 100$ | % | Higher $R$ = better load retention |
| 7 | **Cling Force (peel test)** | $F_{cling} = \dfrac{W_{peel}}{W_{film}} \times \text{const}$ | g/cm | Measured per ASTM D4649 |
| 8 | **Specific Film Yield** | $\text{m}^2/\text{kg} = \dfrac{1000}{\rho \times t}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{kg}$ | $\rho$ = density (g/cm³), $t$ = thickness (µm) |
| 9 | **Stiffness Modulus (E)** | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\epsilon}$ | MPa | From stress–strain curve at low $\epsilon$ |
| 10 | **Cling Activation Threshold** | $\text{Dart Drop Impact} \geq 200 \text{ g}$ (typical LLDPE hand film) | g | ASTM D1709 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Value Rule (Anti-Markdown-Break):** Whenever absolute value notation is required, always write $\vert x \vert$ — never use vertical pipes inside markdown tables.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility & Industrial Applications

- **Stretch Wrap** dominates **pallet unitization** in FMCG, food, beverage, pharmaceutical, and e-commerce logistics. Approximately **20+ billion pounds** of stretch film are consumed globally each year (industrial estimate, Plastics Industry Association).
- **Shrink Wrap** is dominant in **consumer goods bundling** (multi-pack bottles, software boxes, books), **food packaging** (fresh meat trays, cheese), and **protective overwrapping** of irregularly shaped industrial parts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Free Shrink Percentage from ASTM D2732

**Problem Statement:** A shrink film specimen of original length $L_0 = 100 \text{ mm}$ is immersed in an oil bath at $T = 150 \text{ °C}$ for $t = 60 \text{ s}$. After cooling, the new length is measured as $L_1 = 28 \text{ mm}$. Determine the free shrink percentage.

#### Step 1 — Identify the governing equation

By ASTM D2732, free shrink percentage is defined as the proportional reduction in linear dimension relative to the original dimension:

$$
\text{Shrink (\%)} = \frac{L_0 - L_1}{L_0} \times 100
$$

#### Step 2 — Substitute the measured values

We substitute $L_0 = 100 \text{ mm}$ and $L_1 = 28 \text{ mm}$ into the equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Shrink (\%)} &= \frac{100 \text{ mm} - 28 \text{ mm}}{100 \text{ mm}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{72 \text{ mm}}{100 \text{ mm}} \times 100 \\
&= 0.72 \times 100 \\
&= 72 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Interpretation

A free shrink of **72 %** indicates this is most likely a **polyolefin (POF)** or **PE-based shrink film**, since PVC films typically yield **40–55 %** shrink. The value comfortably exceeds the **60 %** threshold considered acceptable for tight-conforming bundling applications.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Stretch Ratio Calculation for a Pallet Load

**Problem Statement:** A 1.2 m wide stretch film roll is unrolled to wrap a pallet. The original film length on a 4-inch core is measured as 1500 m. After mechanical pre-stretch the active length used to wrap one pallet is 3450 m. Calculate the stretch ratio $\lambda$.

#### Step 1 — Governing relation

$$
\lambda = \frac{L_{stretched}}{L_{original}}
$$

#### Step 2 — Substitute and evaluate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{3450 \text{ m}}{1500 \text{ m}} \\
&= 2.30
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Engineering interpretation

A stretch ratio of **2.30:1** indicates this is a **standard pre-stretch machine** (typical range 1.5–2.5:1). The film has been elongated to **230 %** of its original length, which is well within the elastic recovery envelope of LLDPE (which can tolerate elongations up to **500–600 %** before yielding).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Specific Film Yield (Cost-per-Area Metric)

**Problem Statement:** A LLDPE stretch film has a thickness of $t = 17 \text{ µm}$ and a density of $\rho = 0.922 \text{ g/cm}^3$. Calculate the theoretical yield in $\text{m}^2/\text{kg}$.

#### Step 1 — Governing equation

$$
\text{Yield} = \frac{1000}{\rho \times t}
$$

where $\rho$ is in $\text{g/cm}^3$ and $t$ is in **µm**, giving yield in $\text{m}^2/\text{kg}$.

#### Step 2 — Substitute

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Yield} &= \frac{1000}{0.922 \times 17} \\
&= \frac{1000}{15.674} \\
&= 63.80 \text{ m}^2/\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Interpretation

A yield of **63.80 m²/kg** means **1 kg** of this 17 µm film will cover approximately **63.8 m²** of pallet surface area — a key parameter for **cost optimization** in logistics packaging design.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Stretch vs. Shrink

| Parameter | Stretch Wrap | Shrink Wrap |
|---|---|---|
| Activation Energy Source | **Mechanical** (tension) | **Thermal** (heat tunnel) |
| Polymer State During Application | Elastic deformation | Viscous flow / chain relaxation |
| Typical Polymers | **LLDPE**, LDPE, EVA, mLLDPE | **PVC, POF, LDPE, PP** |
| Operating Temperature | **Room temperature** (20–40 °C) | **120–200 °C** |
| Holding Mechanism | Cling + elastic recovery | Conformal mechanical lock |
| Cling Mechanism | **Static cling / inherent tack** | **Heat-seal / friction lock** |
| Transparency | Moderate to high | Very high (POF) |
| Puncture Resistance | High (LLDPE-based) | Moderate |
| Recyclability | **Category 4 (LDPE)** | **Category 3 (PVC) / 4 (POF/PE)** |
| Typical Thickness | **8–35 µm** | **12–25 µm** |
| Capital Equipment Cost | Low–Medium | Medium–High (heat tunnel) |
| Throughput | Very high | Medium |
| Common Applications | Pallet unitization | Bundling, overwrapping |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Process Comparison — Stretch vs. Shrink Wrapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Polymer Resin] --> B[Extrusion Line]
    B --> C{Process Selection}
    C --> D[Cast or Blown Film Line]
    C --> E[Orientation Unit]
    D --> F[Stretch Film Roll]
    E --> G[Quench Cool Roll]
    G --> H[Shrink Film Roll]
    F --> I[Stretch Wrap Process]
    H --> J[Shrink Wrap Process]
    I --> I1[Load Pallet]
    I1 --> I2[Mechanical Pre-Stretch 200-300%]
    I2 --> I3[Wrap via Rotating Turntable]
    I3 --> I4[Film Stores Elastic Energy]
    I4 --> I5[Load Unitized - Ready for Dispatch]
    J --> J1[Drape Loose Film over Product]
    J1 --> J2[Seal Edges - L-Bar or Side Seal]
    J2 --> J3[Pass Through Heat Tunnel 120-200C]
    J3 --> J4[Polymer Chains Recover Original Shape]
    J4 --> J5[Conformal Tight Package - Ready for Shipment]
```

```

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `P1`, `Q3`) and contain no reserved Mermaid keywords. Node labels are rendered as raw uppercase text without markdown formatting tags to ensure clean compilation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between stretch wrap and shrink wrap based on the mechanism of load retention.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Stretch Wrap | Shrink Wrap |
|---|---|---|
| Activation | Mechanical tension at room temperature | Thermal energy at 120–200 °C |
| Retention Mechanism | Elastic memory of polymer chains and inherent cling | Conformal shrinkage — chains relax to original orientation |
| Common Polymer | LLDPE | PVC, POF, PE |

The fundamental distinction lies in the **energy source**: stretch wrap relies on stored elastic energy, while shrink wrap uses heat-activated chain relaxation.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** List any three properties required in a good stretch film for pallet wrapping.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **High elongation at break** (≥ 300 %) — to allow mechanical pre-stretching without puncture.
2. **High elastic recovery / memory** — to maintain clamping force on the load.
3. **Two-sided cling** — to prevent film layers from slipping during transport.
