4. **High puncture and tear resistance** — to withstand sharp edges of palletized goods.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice as per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **cast film extrusion process** used for manufacturing stretch film. Discuss the role of **LLDPE** and **mLLDPE** in achieving desired cling and puncture resistance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Cast film extrusion is the most common process for stretch film manufacture. The molten polymer is extruded through a flat **T-die** onto a chilled, polished chill roll, where it solidifies rapidly. The film is then trimmed at the edges and wound into rolls.

- **LLDPE** provides excellent **elongation, tear resistance, and tensile strength** due to its short-chain branching.
- **mLLDPE (metallocene LLDPE)** offers a narrower molecular weight distribution, giving superior **puncture resistance, optical clarity, and down-gauging ability** — allowing thinner films (15–17 µm) to match the performance of thicker conventional LLDPE films (23 µm).
- A typical formulation includes **3–5 %** of a tackifier additive (e.g., polyisobutylene) to provide **two-sided cling**.

**(b)** A LLDPE stretch film has thickness 17 µm and density 0.918 g/cm³. Calculate the **specific yield** in m²/kg. If a pallet requires 7 layers of 1.0 m wide film applied at a stretch ratio of 2.5:1, and the total pallet height circumference to be wrapped is 4.5 m, find the **mass of film** required per pallet. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Specific yield:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Yield} &= \frac{1000}{\rho \times t} \\
&= \frac{1000}{0.918 \times 17} \\
&= \frac{1000}{15.606} \\
&= 64.08 \text{ m}^2/\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total film area required (un-stretched):**

Total wrapped length (stretched) = 7 layers × 4.5 m = 31.5 m. At stretch ratio 2.5:1, un-stretched length required = 31.5 / 2.5 = 12.6 m. Film width = 1.0 m.

$$
A_{unstretched} = 12.6 \times 1.0 = 12.6 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of film required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass} &= \frac{A}{\text{Yield}} \\
&= \frac{12.6}{64.08} \\
&= 0.197 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

So, **approximately 197 g** of film is required per pallet.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative choice*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **orientation process** used in the manufacture of shrink films. Discuss the difference between **uniaxial** and **biaxial** orientation and their effect on shrinkage behavior. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Shrink films are produced by extruding the polymer and then **stretching it above its glass transition temperature (Tg)** but below its melting point, followed by rapid **quenching**. This freezes the polymer chains in a stretched, high-energy state.

- **Uniaxial orientation:** film is stretched in only one direction (typically MD). Shrinkage occurs predominantly in the stretched direction. Used for **shrink sleeves and labels**.
- **Biaxial orientation:** film is stretched simultaneously (or sequentially) in MD and TD (using tenter frame or double-bubble process). Shrinkage occurs in **both directions**, yielding balanced films suitable for **bundling and overwrapping**.

The amount of shrinkage depends on the **orientation ratio** and **degree of quenching** — rapid quench yields higher shrink memory.

**(b)** A POF shrink film specimen of original dimensions 100 mm × 100 mm is heated in an oil bath at 150 °C for 60 s. After cooling, the dimensions are 26 mm × 32 mm. Calculate the **MD and TD shrink percentages** and identify the **film type**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — MD shrink:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Shrink}_{MD} &= \frac{100 - 26}{100} \times 100 \\
&= 74 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — TD shrink:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Shrink}_{TD} &= \frac{100 - 32}{100} \times 100 \\
&= 68 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identification:**

Both MD and TD shrink values fall in the **60–75 %** range, which is characteristic of **crosslinked Polyolefin (POF / polyolefin) shrink film**. PVC films would typically show 40–55 %, while standard PE films would show higher TD than MD shrink (often 70–80 %).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing stretch and shrink:** Many students interchange the two terms. Always clarify the **energy source** — mechanical vs. thermal.
> 2. **Unit errors in stretch ratio:** Stretch ratio is **dimensionless**, but unit conversions (e.g., µm ↔ mil) often cause mark loss in numerical problems.
> 3. **Forgetting the cling mechanism:** In stretch wrap, do not attribute retention to adhesive — it is **static cling + elastic memory**.
> 4. **Not specifying MD/TD:** For shrink films, always mention the **direction (MD vs. TD)** of measurement.
> 5. **Yield formula sign error:** The denominator is $\rho \times t$, not $\rho / t$ — a very common slip.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stretch wrap** uses **mechanical pre-stretch** at room temperature; **shrink wrap** uses **heat activation**.
- The dominant polymer for stretch films is **LLDPE / mLLDPE**; for shrink films it is **PVC, POF, or PE**.
- **Stretch ratio** $\lambda$ is dimensionless, typically **2:1 to 3:1** for hand films and up to **5:1** for machine films.
- **Free shrink %** is measured per **ASTM D2732** by immersing film in a **150 °C oil bath for 60 seconds**.
- **Holding force** in a stretch-wrapped pallet arises from **elastic recovery** and **inter-layer cling**, not from adhesives.
- **Shrink memory** is generated by **stretching polymer above Tg, then quenching** to freeze chain orientation.
- **POF films** offer the best balance of clarity, strength, and shrink %, but are more expensive than PVC.
- **PVC shrink films** raise environmental concerns (chlorine content, dioxin precursors) — many countries are phasing them out in favor of **POF**.
- Key equipment for shrink wrapping: **L-bar sealer**, **heat tunnel**, **cooling fan**, **conveyor system**.
- Key equipment for stretch wrapping: **turntable**, **film carriage**, **pre-stretch rollers**, **clamp-cut unit**, **HMI control panel**.
- **Specific yield** (m²/kg) is the principal metric for cost comparison among films of different thicknesses.
- For KTU exam: always present **numerical answers with units** and state **assumptions** (e.g., "assuming uniform stretch, no film waste").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pouching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pouching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **pouch** in the context of Plastics Packaging Technology is defined as a **flexible, sealed container** manufactured from one or more plies of plastic films, paper, aluminium foil, or their laminates, formed by **folding, sealing, or shaping** a flat web of material into a three-dimensional enclosure capable of holding, protecting, and dispensing a wide range of solid, liquid, viscous, or particulate products. 

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a pouch is the **primary flexible packaging format** that replaces rigid containers such as glass bottles, metal cans, and blow-moulded plastics, offering advantages in **source reduction, logistics efficiency, and consumer convenience**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A pouch is not merely a "bag." It is a **pre-formed or in-line formed** flexible package that integrates **form-fill-seal (FFS)** operations or is manufactured offline and subsequently filled on a separate machine.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **Tetra Pak milk carton** versus a **milk pouch**. The carton is rigid — it holds its shape because of stiff paperboard and an aluminium barrier. The pouch, however, is **pliable** — it conforms to the product, is lightweight, and uses far less material for the same volume.

- **Analogy 1:** Think of a pouch like a **sealed envelope** that has been engineered to survive dropping, stacking, and shipping — but instead of holding a letter, it holds shampoo, coffee, or frozen peas.
- **Analogy 2:** A pouch is to a rigid bottle what a **paper folder** is to a **filing cabinet** — both store information (or product), but the flexible version is lighter, cheaper, and uses less material.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Pouching

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics are **board-exam favourites**:
> - **Pouch Yield (m²/kg):** Area of pouch material produced per kilogram of film.
> - **Seal Strength (N/15 mm):** Force required to peel a 15 mm wide seal.
> - **Hermetic Seal:** A leak-proof seal that prevents gas, vapour, and microbial ingress.
> - **Conversion Cost (₹/pouch):** Total cost per unit pouch produced.

### 1.4 Importance of Pouching in the Packaging Industry

Pouches dominate the modern **fast-moving consumer goods (FMCG)** sector because of:
1. **Lightweighting** — typically 70–90% lighter than rigid equivalents.
2. **Lower carbon footprint** per unit product packaged.
3. **Superior printability** using **rotogravure** and **flexography**.
4. **Consumer-friendly features** — zippers, spouts, tear notches, and microwave compatibility.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Pouches

Pouches are classified based on **construction, geometry, and end-use application**:

#### A. Based on Geometry / Shape

| Pouch Type | Construction Logic | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Pillow Pouch (Fin Seal)** | Made on a Horizontal Form Fill Seal (HFFS) machine; sealed on the back (fin) and two ends | Snacks, biscuits, confectionery |
| **Three-Side Seal Pouch** | Two face-to-face films sealed on three sides, leaving one open for filling | Pharmaceuticals, sachets, single-serve |
| **Four-Side Seal Pouch** | Two films sealed on all four sides | Tea, coffee, powders, retort foods |
| **Stand-Up Pouch (SUP)** | Bottom gusset or Doy-Pak style allows it to stand on shelves | Liquids, pet food, detergents |
| **Spouted Pouch** | Fitted with a fitment (spout + cap) for dispensing | Juices, baby food, motor oil |
| **Retort Pouch** | Multi-layer laminate capable of surviving 121 °C autoclaving | Ready-to-eat meals, pet food |
| **Zipper Pouch** | Resealable mechanical closure (PE zipper profile) integrated into the seal | Cheese, dried fruits, coffee |
| **Stick Pack** | Narrow, tubular, single-serve sachet | Powdered drinks, sugar, salt |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** The **Doy-Pak (Doyen-style) stand-up pouch** uses a **bottom gusset with a "W" or doyen fold**, while the **K-Seal stand-up pouch** uses a **K-shaped bottom gusset** for improved stability on the shelf.

#### B. Based on Manufacturing Process

- **Form-Fill-Seal (FFS):** Pouch formed in-line from a roll of film → filled → sealed.
  - Horizontal FFS (HFFS)
  - Vertical FFS (VFFS)
- **Pre-Made Pouch (PMP):** Pouches fabricated offline on dedicated pouch-making machines, then loaded into a filler.

### 2.2 The Four Pillars of Pouch Engineering

1. **Material Selection** — selecting the right laminate structure (e.g., PET/AL/PE for retort).
2. **Sealing Technology** — heat sealing, ultrasonic sealing, impulse sealing.
3. **Barrier Properties** — OTR (Oxygen Transmission Rate), MVTR (Moisture Vapour Transmission Rate).
4. **Mechanical Integrity** — seal strength, drop resistance, burst strength, flex crack resistance.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are essential for KTU Board Examinations under PEPOT748.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pouch Length | $L_p = L_{prod} + 2 \times S_{end}$ | mm |
| 2 | Pouch Width | $W_p = W_{prod} + 2 \times S_{side}$ | mm |
| 3 | Film Consumption per Pouch | $C = (L_p \times W_p) / 10^6$ | m²/pouch |
| 4 | Number of Pouches from Reel | $N = (L_{reel} \times 1000) / L_p$ | pouches |
| 5 | Reel Length | $L_{reel} = (\pi \times (D_o^2 - D_i^2)) / (4 \times t)$ | m |
| 6 | Production Rate | $R = (V \times 60) / L_p$ | pouches/hour |
| 7 | Seal Strength Index | $SSI = F_{peel} / W_{seal}$ | N/15 mm |
| 8 | Laminate Yield | $Y = A_{film} / m_{film}$ | m²/kg |
| 9 | Oxygen Transmission Rate (target) | $OTR_{target} \leq Q_{O_2} / (A \times t)$ | cc/m²·day |
| 10 | Burst Strength (Mullen) | $P_{burst} = 2 \times \sigma \times t / r$ | kPa |

> Where:
> - $L_{prod}, W_{prod}$ = product dimensions
> - $S_{end}, S_{side}$ = end and side sealing margins
> - $D_o, D_i$ = outer and inner diameter of reel
> - $t$ = film thickness
> - $V$ = linear web speed (m/min)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- The **pouch yield equation** is used by converters to **price pouch jobs** accurately.
- **Seal Strength Index (SSI)** is used in **QC labs** to validate heat-seal temperatures.
- **OTR and MVTR calculations** drive the selection of **EVOH, PVDC, or aluminium foil** barriers in retort pouches.
- **Production rate equations** are used by FMCG companies to **plan capacity** during product launches.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Reel Length for a Given Number of Pouches

**Problem Statement:** Determine the length of a film reel required to produce **N pouches** of length $L_p$ with a sealing allowance $\alpha$ per pouch (accounting for trim waste).

**Given:**
- Number of pouches required: $N = 5000$
- Pouch length: $L_p = 180$ mm
- Sealing allowance per pouch: $\alpha = 5$ mm
- Inter-pouch trim: $\beta = 3$ mm

**Step 1: Effective linear length per pouch**

Each pouch consumes its own length $L_p$, plus the trim $\beta$ between pouches, plus the sealing allowance $\alpha$.

$$
l_{eff} = L_p + \alpha + \beta
$$

**Step 2: Substitute numerical values**

$$
l_{eff} = 180 + 5 + 3 = 188 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: Total film length required**

$$
L_{total} = N \times l_{eff}
$$

$$
L_{total} = 5000 \times 188 = 940{,}000 \text{ mm} = 940 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to reel length with 10% safety stock**

$$
L_{reel} = L_{total} \times 1.10 = 940 \times 1.10 = 1034 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A converter needs to order a reel of at least **1034 m** to safely produce 5000 pouches without running short.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Production Rate of an HFFS Machine

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the production rate of an HFFS machine running at **30 m/min** with pouch length 200 mm.

**Step 1: Web speed conversion**

$$
V = 30 \text{ m/min} = 30{,}000 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 2: Pouches per minute**

$$
P_{min} = V / L_p
$$

$$
P_{min} = 30{,}000 / 200 = 150 \text{ pouches/min}
$$

**Step 3: Pouches per hour (with 85% machine efficiency $\eta$)**

$$
R = P_{min} \times 60 \times \eta
$$

$$
R = 150 \times 60 \times 0.85 = 7650 \text{ pouches/hour}
$$

**Step 4: Daily output (single 8-hour shift)**

$$
R_{day} = R \times 8 = 7650 \times 8 = 61{,}200 \text{ pouches/day}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 85% efficiency factor accounts for **changeovers, start-up waste, and operator interventions** — a value assumed by KTU examiners in plant design problems.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Seal Strength Quality Index

**Problem Statement:** A 25 mm wide seal requires a peel force of **42.5 N** to fail. Calculate the Seal Strength Index (SSI).

**Step 1: Normalize the seal width to KTU standard of 15 mm**

$$
W_{std} = 15 \text{ mm}, \quad W_{actual} = 25 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Apply linear normalization**

$$
F_{std} = F_{peel} \times (W_{std} / W_{actual})
$$

$$
F_{std} = 42.5 \times (15 / 25) = 42.5 \times 0.6 = 25.5 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Seal Strength Index**

$$
SSI = F_{std} / W_{std}
$$

$$
SSI = 25.5 / 15 = 1.7 \text{ N/mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Acceptance Criterion (KTU Convention):** A heat seal is considered commercially acceptable if **$SSI \geq 1.5$ N/15 mm** for non-sterile food contact, and **$SSI \geq 2.5$ N/15 mm** for retort pouches.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pouch Selection Decision Matrix

The following decision matrix is a high-yield tool for KTU Part B answers involving **pouch selection for a given product**.

| Product Type | Recommended Pouch | Laminate Structure | Sealing Method | Key Barrier |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Potato Chips | Pillow / Stand-Up | BOPP / LDPE / Met-PET / LDPE | Heat seal | MVTR |
| Liquid Milk | Pillow (VFFS) | LLDPE / LLDPE | Hot-tack seal | OTR |
| Ready-to-Eat Meals | Retort (Stand-Up) | PET / AL / NY / CPP | Heat + Retort | OTR + MVTR |
| Detergent Powder | Stand-Up (Zipper) | PET / PE / Paper / PE | Heat seal | Mechanical |
| Coffee Beans | Stand-Up + Valve | PET / AL / PE | Heat seal | OTR + Aroma |
| Pharmaceutical Tablets | Four-Side Seal | PET / AL / PVC | Heat seal | OTR + MVTR + Light |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Horizontal Form-Fill-Seal (HFFS) Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    reelA[Film Reel A - Top Web] --> unw1[Unwind Station]
    reelB[Film Reel B - Bottom Web] --> unw2[Unwind Station]
    unw1 --> form1[Forming Shoulder]
    unw2 --> form2[Forming Tube]
    form1 --> sealA[Longitudinal Fin Seal]
    form2 --> sealB[Cross Seal Station]
    sealA --> cross[Cross Seal Jaws]
    sealB --> cross
    cross --> cut[Cutting Blade]
    cut --> fill[Product Filling]
    fill --> disp[Discharge Conveyor]
    disp --> qc[QC Inspection]
    qc --> pack[Carton Packing]

    style reelA fill:#ffd166
    style reelB fill:#ffd166
    style disp fill:#06d6a0
    style qc fill:#ef476f
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Anatomy of a Stand-Up Pouch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    layer1[Outer Layer - PET 12 micron] --> lam1[Adhesive Laminant]
    lam1 --> layer2[Middle Barrier - AL Foil 9 micron]
    layer2 --> lam2[Adhesive Laminant]
    lam2 --> layer3[Tie Layer - NY 15 micron]
    layer3 --> layer4[Sealant Layer - CPP 70 micron]

    pouch1[Top - Zipper Closure] --> top[Top Seal]
    top --> body[Pouch Body]
    body --> bottom[K-Seal Bottom Gusset]
    bottom --> stand[Stand-Up Base]

    style layer1 fill:#118ab2
    style layer2 fill:#ef476f
    style layer3 fill:#06d6a0
    style layer4 fill:#ffd166
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Classification Tree of Pouches

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Flexible Pouch Universe] --> geo[By Geometry]
    root --> proc[By Process]
    root --> app[By Application]

    geo --> g1[Pillow - Fin Seal]
    geo --> g2[Three Side Seal]
    geo --> g3[Four Side Seal]
    geo --> g4[Stand Up - Doyen]
    geo --> g5[Stand Up - K Seal]
    geo --> g6[Spouted Pouch]
    geo --> g7[Stick Pack]

    proc --> p1[HFFS - Horizontal FFS]
    proc --> p2[VFFS - Vertical FFS]
    proc --> p3[PMP - Pre Made Pouch]

    app --> a1[Food]
    app --> a2[Pharma]
    app --> a3[Industrial]
    app --> a4[Personal Care]

    style root fill:#073b4c
    style geo fill:#118ab2
    style proc fill:#ef476f
    style app fill:#06d6a0
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence: Sealing Process Stages

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OP as Operator
    participant M as HFFS Machine
    participant F as Film Web
    participant S as Seal Jaws
    participant Q as QC Camera

    OP->>M: Start Machine & Set Parameters
    M->>F: Pull Film at Speed V
    F->>M: Position Under Forming Shoulder
    M->>S: Engage Seal Jaws at Temp T
    S->>F: Apply Heat + Pressure + Dwell
    F-->>M: Form Hermetic Seal
    M->>Q: Vision Inspection of Seal
    Q-->>M: Pass/Fail Signal
    M->>OP: Reject if Fail
    OP->>M: Accept if Pass
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a "stand-up pouch" and list any two advantages it offers over a rigid plastic bottle.**

**Model Answer:**
A stand-up pouch (SUP) is a flexible package with a **bottom gusset** that allows it to stand upright on retail shelves without external support. It is typically manufactured from a multi-layer laminate such as PET/AL/PE or PET/NY/CPP.

**Two advantages over rigid plastic bottles:**
1. **Material reduction:** An SUP uses approximately **70–80% less plastic** by mass than an equivalent rigid bottle, reducing raw material cost and shipping weight.
2. **Better shelf presence:** The flat front and back panels offer a larger printable surface for branding compared to the curved surface of a bottle.

*(3 marks — 1 mark definition + 1 mark per advantage)*

---

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is a "fin seal" and how does it differ from a "lap seal" in pillow pouch construction?**

**Model Answer:**
A **fin seal** is formed when the inside (sealant) layer of the film is folded over itself and sealed, creating a protruding "fin" along the back of the pillow pouch.

A **lap seal** is formed when the outside of one film layer is sealed against the inside of the other film layer, producing a flat, flush seam without any protruding fin.

**Key difference:** In a fin seal, the **sealant-to-sealant interface** is bonded; in a lap seal, the **sealant-to-substrate interface** is bonded. Fin seals are stronger but visible externally; lap seals offer better aesthetics.

*(3 marks — 1.5 marks each for fin and lap seal)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — **14 Marks**

**(a)** Classify the different types of flexible pouches based on **geometry**, and explain with neat sketches the construction of a **Doy-Pak stand-up pouch**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A VFFS machine runs at **40 m/min** and produces pillow pouches of length **220 mm** with a 5 mm trim allowance. If the machine operates for **16 hours/day** at **80% efficiency**, calculate the **number of pouches produced per day** and the **total film consumed (in metres)**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Pouches by Geometry:**

1. **Pillow Pouch (Fin Seal)** — formed on HFFS/VFFS by wrapping a single web around a forming collar.
2. **Three-Side Seal Pouch** — two separate webs sealed on three sides.
3. **Four-Side Seal Pouch** — two separate webs sealed on all four sides.
4. **Stand-Up Pouch (Doy-Pak)** — bottom doyen-style gusset allows the pouch to stand.
5. **Spouted Pouch** — incorporates a spout fitment for liquid dispensing.
6. **Stick Pack** — narrow tubular sachet for single-serve powders.

**Construction of a Doy-Pak Stand-Up Pouch:**

The Doy-Pak is made from a **single web** that is folded such that a **rounded bottom gusset** is formed. The web passes through a forming set where two side gussets are folded inward, and a **bottom seal** is applied in a curved or oval shape. When the pouch is filled and the top is sealed, the bottom gusset unfolds to form a stable base. The pouch has **two side seams** (vertical) and **one top and one bottom seam** (horizontal).

*(7 marks: 3 for classification + 4 for Doy-Pak construction explanation)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Effective pouch length**

$$
l_{eff} = L_p + \alpha = 220 + 5 = 225 \text{ mm} = 0.225 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Pouches per minute (theoretical)**

$$
P_{min} = V / l_{eff}
$$

$$
P_{min} = 40 / 0.225 = 177.78 \text{ pouches/min}
$$

**Step 3: Apply 80% efficiency**

$$
P_{eff} = 177.78 \times 0.80 = 142.22 \text{ pouches/min}
$$

**Step 4: Pouches per hour**

$$
R_{hr} = 142.22 \times 60 = 8533.33 \text{ pouches/hour}
$$

**Step 5: Pouches per day (16 hours)**

$$
R_{day} = 8533.33 \times 16 = 136{,}533 \text{ pouches/day}
$$

**Step 6: Total film consumed per day**

$$
L_{film} = V \times 60 \times 16 \times 0.80 = 40 \times 60 \times 16 \times 0.80 = 30{,}720 \text{ m}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- **Pouches/day ≈ 1,36,533**
- **Film consumed = 30,720 m**

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for pouches/day, 2 for film length, 1 for units & conclusion)*

---

#### Question B: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — **14 Marks** (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a)** Explain the **Form-Fill-Seal (FFS)** concept in flexible packaging. Differentiate between **HFFS and VFFS** processes with their typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A retort pouch is constructed from a four-layer laminate: **PET (12 µm) / AL (9 µm) / NY (15 µm) / CPP (70 µm)**. Calculate the **total laminate thickness**, the **seal strength** if the seal requires 38 N for a 15 mm width, and comment on the suitability of this structure for **sterilization at 121 °C**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Form-Fill-Seal (FFS) Concept:**

Form-Fill-Seal is an **integrated packaging process** in which a pouch is **formed in-line** from a roll of flexible film, **filled** with the product, and **sealed** in a single continuous operation — eliminating the need for separate pouch-making and filling steps.

**HFFS (Horizontal Form-Fill-Seal):**
- Film moves **horizontally**.
- Product is dropped into the formed tube from above.
- **Applications:** Solid items — biscuits, bread, chocolates, medical devices.

**VFFS (Vertical Form-Fill-Seal):**
- Film moves **vertically** over a forming collar.
- Product drops vertically through the centre of the formed tube.
- **Applications:** Free-flowing solids, granules, powders, and liquids — snacks, sugar, rice, milk.

*(7 marks: 3 for FFS concept + 2 each for HFFS/VFFS differentiation)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Total laminate thickness**

$$
t_{total} = t_{PET} + t_{AL} + t_{NY} + t_{CPP}
$$

$$
t_{total} = 12 + 9 + 15 + 70 = 106 \text{ µm}
$$

**Step 2: Seal Strength Index**

$$
SSI = F_{peel} / W_{seal}
$$

$$
SSI = 38 / 15 = 2.53 \text{ N/mm}
$$

**Step 3: Suitability for 121 °C Retort Sterilization**

For retort sterilization, the laminate must:
- Contain an **aluminium foil barrier** to block oxygen and light → ✅ Present (9 µm AL).
- Use a **heat-resistant sealant** like **CPP** (cast polypropylene) → ✅ Present.
- Use a **tough outer layer** like **PET** for printability and puncture resistance → ✅ Present.
- Use **NY (nylon)** for flex-crack resistance → ✅ Present.

Since $SSI \geq 2.5$ N/mm is the KTU convention for retort pouches, this structure **passes the seal strength criterion**.

**Conclusion:** The laminate PET(12)/AL(9)/NY(15)/CPP(70) with a total thickness of **106 µm** is **structurally suitable** for retort sterilization at 121 °C, provided the seal process is optimized for CPP (typical sealing temperature: 180–200 °C).

*(7 marks: 2 for thickness, 2 for seal strength, 3 for retort suitability)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Pouching Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** in production rate problems — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing fin seal with lap seal** — a frequent 1-mark loss in 3-mark questions.
> 3. **Omitting machine efficiency** in daily output calculations — reduces score from 7/7 to 5/7.
> 4. **Using the wrong sealant layer** for retort — CPP must be specified, not LDPE.
> 5. **Ignoring OTR and MVTR units** — they are **cc/m²·day** and **g/m²·day** respectively, not percentage values.
> 6. **Stating "aluminium provides strength"** instead of "aluminium provides **oxygen and light barrier**" — concept error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **pouch** is a **flexible, sealed container** — the fastest-growing packaging format globally.
- **Pillow pouches** are formed on HFFS/VFFS; **Stand-Up Pouches** use a bottom gusset for shelf stability.
- **Doy-Pak** uses a rounded (doyen) bottom; **K-Seal** uses a K-shaped bottom — K-Seals are more stable but use more material.
- **Seal Strength Index (SSI)** is normalized to **N/15 mm**; threshold = **1.5** for general food, **2.5** for retort.
- **Retort pouches** must survive **121 °C** autoclaving — typical laminate = **PET/AL/NY/CPP**.
- **Form-Fill-Seal (FFS)** integrates forming, filling, and sealing into one continuous operation.
- **HFFS** suits **solid products**; **VFFS** suits **free-flowing solids and liquids**.
- **OTR (Oxygen Transmission Rate)** is critical for oxygen-sensitive products; **MVTR** is critical for moisture-sensitive products.
- **Aluminium foil (AL)** offers the **best total barrier** but is non-microwavable and non-transparent.
- **Conversion cost** of a pouch depends on **material yield (m²/kg)**, **machine speed**, and **labour efficiency**.
- **Zipper pouches** use **PE profile zippers** heat-sealed between the front and back webs.
- **Spouted pouches** use **fitments** welded onto a pre-made pouch via **ultrasonic or hot-plate welding**.
- **Pre-Made Pouches (PMP)** offer **superior aesthetics** and **faster changeovers** compared to in-line FFS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Board Exam Tip:** When asked to *"select a suitable pouch for a product,"* always justify your answer using **three parameters**: (1) **Barrier requirement**, (2) **Mechanical requirement**, and (3) **Consumer convenience feature**. This is the KTU gold-standard answer template.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sealing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sealing

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sealing** in plastics packaging technology refers to the process of joining two or more thermoplastic surfaces (films, sheets, or laminates) by the application of **heat**, **pressure**, and **dwell time** to form a permanent, semi-permanent, or peelable bond, without the use of additional adhesives, solvents, or mechanical fasteners. The mechanism is fundamentally **autohesive fusion**, where polymer chains across the interface interdiffuse beyond their entanglement molecular weight, creating a continuous, monolithic weld.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 — Sealing):** Sealing is classified by the **mode of energy transfer** (conduction, convection, radiant, induction, ultrasonic, dielectric) and by the **functional output** (hermetic, peelable, resealable, tamper-evident).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of sealing two plastic films like **pressing two bars of warm chocolate together**: if you simply press cold chocolate, the pieces crack apart; but if you warm the surfaces just enough that the outer layer becomes soft, then press them together for a few seconds, the softened molecules from each side mingle and, on cooling, become a single, continuous mass. The "warmth" in plastic sealing is the **heat seal temperature**, the "pressing" is **seal pressure**, and the "few seconds" is the **dwell time**. Just as overheating chocolate makes a mess and under-warming leaves crumbly pieces, sealing demands a precise **process window**.

## 1.3 Key Sealing Metrics (Standard Industrial Units)

- **Heat Seal Temperature (°C):** Typically **120 °C – 220 °C** for polyolefins.
- **Seal Pressure (N/15 mm or psi):** Standard test pressure is **0.275 N/15 mm** (≈ 40 psi).
- **Dwell Time (s):** Industrial range **0.25 s – 3.0 s**; lab standard **1.0 s**.
- **Heat Seal Strength (HSS) (N/15 mm or g/in):** ASTM F88 standard.
- **Hot Tack Strength (N/15 mm):** Measured at the seal bar temperature (still molten).
- **Seal Initiation Temperature (SIT) (°C):** Lowest temperature at which a usable seal of ≥ 1 N/15 mm forms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Seal Initiation Temperature (SIT)** is a critical KTU-board favorite: it is the lowest temperature where the polymer's amorphous regions reach sufficient mobility for chain interdiffusion.

## 1.4 Classification of Sealing Techniques (At a Glance)

| Category | Energy Mode | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat Sealing (Conduction)** | Direct contact | Pouches, bags, sachets |
| **Impulse Sealing** | Conduction + cool cycle | Polyethylene, shrink films |
| **Hot Wire / Hot Knife** | Convective cutting-seal | Tubs, blister trim |
| **Hot Air / Hot Gas Sealing** | Convection | Tubing, aseptic cartons |
| **Ultrasonic Sealing** | Mechanical vibration (20–40 kHz) | Blisters, rigid containers |
| **Induction Sealing** | Electromagnetic induction | Bottle caps, foil liners |
| **Dielectric (RF) Sealing** | Dielectric loss (PVC, PU) | Medical, vinyl pouches |
| **Laser Sealing** | Radiant IR | Automotive, medical |
| **Friction Sealing** | Mechanical heat | Continuous webs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Stage Mechanism of Heat Sealing

Heat sealing is not instantaneous fusion. It is a **kinetically controlled, four-stage molecular process**:

1. **Stage I — Heating & Melting (Latent Heat Absorption):**
   The film surfaces are heated to above the **melting point (Tm)** for semi-crystalline polymers (e.g., LDPE ~115 °C, PP ~165 °C) or above the **glass transition (Tg)** for amorphous polymers (e.g., PET ~75 °C, PS ~100 °C). Heat flows from the heated jaw (T_jaw) into the film by conduction. The energy required is governed by the **specific heat capacity (Cp)** and **latent heat of fusion (ΔHf)**.

2. **Stage II — Wetting & Interfacial Contact:**
   The molten or softened polymer surfaces deform under the applied **seal pressure (P_seal)**, conforming to micro-roughness. This produces intimate **wetting**, eliminating air gaps. Without wetting, no chain interdiffusion can occur.

3. **Stage III — Interdiffusion (Healing / Autohesion):**
   Polymer chains from each side cross the interface by **reptation** (de Gennes' tube model). The interdiffusion depth grows as:
   $$ L(t) \;\approx\; 2 \cdot \sqrt{D \cdot t} $$
   where D is the self-diffusion coefficient (strongly temperature-dependent) and *t* is contact time. The seal strength rises monotonically until full interpenetration is achieved.

4. **Stage IV — Crystallization / Solidification (Cooling under Pressure):**
   Cooling under maintained pressure allows the polymer to recrystallize (or vitrify) across the interface, locking the interdiffused chains in place. Premature pressure release causes voids, warping, and weak seals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why pressure is held during cooling:** The seal must solidify while chains are still entangled across the interface. Releasing pressure too early triggers **shrink-back stress**, producing **wrinkles, pinholes, and stress-whitening** in the seal region.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $L(t) \approx 2\sqrt{D \cdot t}$ | Reptation interdiffusion depth | m | Molecular healing kinetics |
| $D = D_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R\,T}\right)$ | Arrhenius self-diffusion | m²/s | Temperature dependence of D |
| $Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot \Delta H_f$ | Heat required to reach seal temp | J | Sealing energy budget |
| $t_{\min} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot C_p \cdot L_f^2}{2\,k}$ | Minimum heating time (1-D slab) | s | Jaw design, dwell time selection |
| $R_{\text{seal}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{peel}}}{w}$ | Seal strength (force per width) | N/15 mm | ASTM F88 measurement |
| $\eta_0 = A \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_v}{R\,T}\right)$ | Zero-shear viscosity (WLF/Arrhenius) | Pa·s | Polymer flow during wetting |
| $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_{\text{contact}}}$ | Seal pressure (Clamp force / Area) | Pa or N/mm² | Dwell pressure setting |
| $\text{SIT} \;(\text{°C}) \approx T_g + 30 \text{ to } 50$ | Approximation for amorphous polymers | °C | Quick SIT estimation |
| $\tan \delta_{\text{peel}} = \dfrac{E''}{E'}$ | Viscoelastic loss tangent (peelable design) | dimensionless | Peel vs. lock seal selection |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, never use the `|` character inside cells (e.g., for absolute value). Use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the table syntax.

## 2.3 Seal Strength Modes (KTU-Frequently-Tested)

- **Peel Strength (Mode I — Opening):** Force per unit width required to separate the seal by peeling at a controlled angle (typically 180° or T-peel). Standard: **ASTM F88**.
- **Shear Strength (Mode II — Sliding):** Force parallel to the seal plane. Relevant for vertical form-fill-seal (VFFS) bag stacks.
- **Cleavage / Tensile Strength (Mode III — Tearing):** Force perpendicular to the seal plane; mimics pinhole-type failures.
- **Hot Tack Strength:** The seal's load-bearing capacity **immediately after sealing**, while still molten. Critical for VFFS where product is dropped onto a hot seal.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food Packaging:** Hermetic seals for retort pouches, MAP (Modified Atmosphere Packaging).
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Tamper-evident induction seals on HDPE bottle necks.
- **Medical Devices:** Peelable Tyvek®/film seals for sterilization pouches.
- **Industrial Films:** Continuous ultrasonic sealing of automotive interior laminates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Dwell Time for a 1-D Heat-Seal Slab

Consider a thermoplastic film of thickness $L_f$ and thermal conductivity $k$, initially at temperature $T_0$, contacted on both sides by heated jaws at $T_j$. The film surface instantly reaches $T_j$, and heat conducts inward. For thin films, the **lumped capacitance** assumption fails, so we use the **transient 1-D conduction solution** (semi-infinite slab approach for short times).

**Step 1 — Governing equation** (Fourier's law, 1-D, constant k):

$$ \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} \;=\; \alpha \,\dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} $$

where $\alpha = k / (\rho \, C_p)$ is the **thermal diffusivity** (m²/s).

**Step 2 — Boundary conditions:**

$$ T(0,\,t) \;=\; T_j \quad ; \quad T(x,\,0) \;=\; T_0 $$

**Step 3 — Solution** (semi-infinite slab with constant surface temperature, Carslaw & Jaeger):

$$ \dfrac{T(x,t) - T_0}{T_j - T_0} \;=\; \operatorname{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha\,t}}\right) $$

**Step 4 — Set the seal criterion.** A seal is formed when the **mid-plane temperature** $T(L_f/2,\,t)$ reaches a critical temperature $T_c$ (≈ Seal Initiation Temperature, SIT).

**Step 5 — Substitute $x = L_f/2$ and solve for $t$:**

$$ \dfrac{T_c - T_0}{T_j - T_0} \;=\; \operatorname{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{L_f}{4\sqrt{\alpha\,t_{\min}}}\right) $$

**Step 6 — Invert the complementary error function.** Let

$$ \eta \;=\; \dfrac{L_f}{4\sqrt{\alpha\,t_{\min}}} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad t_{\min} \;=\; \dfrac{L_f^{\,2}}{16\,\alpha\,\eta^{2}} $$

**Step 7 — Engineering approximation (Biot < 0.1, lumped capacitance).** When the film is thin and the jaw temperature rise is moderate, the time to reach an average temperature $T_{\text{avg}}$ is:

$$ t_{\min} \;\approx\; \dfrac{\rho \, C_p \, L_f \, (T_{\text{avg}} - T_0)}{2 \, h \, (T_j - T_{\text{avg}})} $$

where $h$ is the **interfacial heat transfer coefficient** (W/m²·K) at the jaw–film contact.

**Engineering Insight (KTU expected):** A **doubling of film thickness** requires a **quadrupling of dwell time**, all else being equal. This is why thick laminated structures (e.g., 100 µm foil/PE) demand 1.5–2.0 s dwell, while 25 µm LDPE seals in 0.3 s.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Seal Strength from Autohesion Theory

For two identical amorphous polymers brought into contact above Tg, the **adhesive fracture energy** G(t) follows the **de Gennes reptation recovery law**:

**Step 1 — Define the entanglement molecular weight** $M_e$ and the tube disengagement time** $\tau_d$ (the time for a chain to fully exit its original tube).

**Step 2 — For times $t \ll \tau_d$:**

$$ G(t) \;=\; G_\infty \,\left(\dfrac{t}{\tau_d}\right)^{1/2} $$

**Step 3 — For $t \gg \tau_d$ (full interdiffusion):**

$$ G(t) \;=\; G_\infty \;\approx\; G_{c,\text{cohesive}} $$

i.e., the seal becomes indistinguishable from the parent material — a **cohesive, not adhesive, failure**.

**Step 4 — Temperature dependence** of $\tau_d$ via the WLF equation (above Tg):

$$ \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\tau_d(T)}{\tau_d(T_g)}\right) \;=\; -\dfrac{C_1\,(T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)} $$

with **universal constants** $C_1 \approx 17.44$ and $C_2 \approx 51.6$ K for many amorphous thermoplastics.

**Step 5 — Practical implication.** Increasing the jaw temperature by **10 °C** above SIT can **cut the required dwell time by 50–70 %** because $\tau_d$ collapses exponentially. This is the foundation of the **time–temperature superposition** principle used in seal-strength optimization.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Sealing Parameters on Seal Quality

| Parameter | Increased → Effect on HSS | Increased → Effect on Hot Tack | Risk of Over-Increase |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | ↑ (then plateau) | ↑↑ (peaks, then ↓) | Burn-through, distortion |
| **Pressure** | ↑ (then plateau) | ↑ | Thinning, squeeze-out |
| **Dwell Time** | ↑ (then plateau) | ↑ (then ↓) | Cycle-time penalty, degradation |
| **Film Thickness** | ↑ (more material to fuse) | ↑ | Requires ↑ dwell & ↑ temp |
| **Crystallinity** | ↓ (less chain mobility) | ↓ (sharpens drop) | Brittle, pinhole seal |
| **Surface Treatment (corona)** | ↑ (polar, wettable) | minor | Over-treatment → block, ink smearing |

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem:** A 50 µm thick LDPE film ($C_p = 2300$ J/kg·K, $\rho = 920$ kg/m³, $k = 0.33$ W/m·K) at 25 °C is sealed against jaws at 150 °C. The seal initiation temperature is 110 °C. Estimate the minimum dwell time using the lumped-capacitance approximation, assuming $h = 1500$ W/m²·K.

**Step 1:** Set $T_{\text{avg}} = 110$ °C (SIT).

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$ t_{\min} \;\approx\; \dfrac{\rho \, C_p \, L_f \, (T_{\text{avg}} - T_0)}{2 \, h \, (T_j - T_{\text{avg}})} $$

**Step 3:** Numerator $= 920 \times 2300 \times 50\times10^{-6} \times (110 - 25)$.

Compute:
$$ 920 \times 2300 \;=\; 2.116 \times 10^{6} $$
$$ 2.116\times10^{6} \times 50\times10^{-6} \;=\; 105.8 $$
$$ 105.8 \times 85 \;=\; 8993 $$

**Step 4:** Denominator $= 2 \times 1500 \times (150 - 110) = 2 \times 1500 \times 40 = 1.2 \times 10^{5}$.

**Step 5:**

$$ t_{\min} \;\approx\; \dfrac{8993}{1.2\times10^{5}} \;\approx\; 0.075 \text{ s} $$

**Step 6:** Engineering rounding + safety factor of 2× → **design dwell time ≈ 0.15 – 0.25 s.** Industrial sealers typically run 0.5–1.0 s for LDPE for safety, since the lumped model underestimates the time needed to fully crystallize across the interface.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Heat-Seal Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Film Web Feed] --> B[Pre-Heating Zone optional]
    B --> C[Sealing Jaws Top + Bottom]
    C --> C1[Jaw Temperature T_j set 120-220 C]
    C --> C2[Dwell Pressure P_s applied]
    C --> C3[Dwell Time t_d 0.25-3 s]
    C3 --> D[Stage I Heating + Melting]
    D --> E[Stage II Wetting + Contact]
    E --> F[Stage III Chain Interdiffusion]
    F --> G[Stage IV Cooling under Pressure]
    G --> H{Cooled below Tg or Tm}
    H -- yes --> I[Solidified Hermetic Seal]
    H -- no --> J[Hot Tack Phase]
    J --> K[Seal Strength Test ASTM F88]
    I --> K
    K --> L{Strength meets spec}
    L -- yes --> M[Accept Batch]
    L -- no --> N[Reject - Adjust T P or t]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sealing Technology Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Packaging Requirement] --> S1{Is the polymer thermoplastic}
    S1 -- yes --> S2{Is the seal through a coated foil}
    S1 -- no --> S3[Use Adhesive Lamination - Not a Sealing Process]
    S2 -- yes --> S4[Induction Sealing]
    S2 -- no --> S5{Need peelable seal}
    S5 -- yes --> S6[Use Contamination-Grade Heat Seal or Ultrasonic]
    S5 -- no --> S7{High volume continuous}
    S7 -- yes --> S8[Rotary Band Sealer or Impulse Sealer]
    S7 -- no --> S9[Lab or Short Run]
    S9 --> S10[Bar or Platen Heat Sealer]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Seal Failure Mode Analysis (Fishbone-Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Weak or Failed Heat Seal] --> M1[Material Variables]
    P --> M2[Process Variables]
    P --> M3[Equipment Variables]
    P --> M4[Environmental Variables]
    M1 --> M1a[Wrong grade of PE or PP]
    M1 --> M1b[High slip or antiblock additives]
    M1 --> M1c[High moisture in film]
    M2 --> M2a[Jaw temperature too low]
    M2 --> M2b[Dwell pressure too low]
    M2 --> M2c[Dwell time too short]
    M3 --> M3a[Teflon cover worn]
    M3 --> M3b[Non-uniform jaw heating]
    M3 --> M3c[Pressure cylinder leak]
    M4 --> M4a[Product contamination in seal]
    M4 --> M4b[Ambient temperature swing]
    M4 --> M4c[Excessive line speed]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Impulse Sealing Cycle (Time Domain)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Film in Position] --> T1[Jaw Closes]
    T1 --> T2[Heat Pulse ON 0.3 to 1.5 s]
    T2 --> T3[Cool Pulse ON 1.0 to 3.0 s]
    T3 --> T4[Jaw Opens]
    T4 --> T5[Film Advances]
    T5 --> T0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Impulse sealing** alternates a **Nichrome heating element** with a **water-cooled dwell** while the jaws remain closed, producing lower residual stress than constant-heat bar sealers and is preferred for **LDPE/LLDPE shrink films**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Seal Initiation Temperature (SIT) and explain its importance in heat-seal process design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** SIT is the lowest jaw temperature at which a thermoplastic film forms a seal with a strength of ≥ 1 N/15 mm under a standard pressure of 0.275 N/15 mm and 1 s dwell.
- **Importance (1.5 marks):** SIT defines the **lower bound of the sealing window**. Operating below SIT yields leaky, low-strength seals. Knowing SIT allows the converter to **set the upper jaw temperature 15–30 °C above SIT** to achieve robust seals with short dwell times, and also helps in **troubleshooting** — a sudden rise in SIT often indicates surface contamination or additive migration.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Distinguish between a peelable seal and a permanent (lock) seal in plastic packaging.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Peelable Seal | Lock (Permanent) Seal |
|---|---|---|
| **Failure mode** | Interfacial / adhesive | Cohesive — seal stronger than film |
| **Opening force** | Low and controlled (typically 5–25 N/15 mm) | High — requires tearing the film |
| **Mechanism** | Selective interdiffusion + controlled delamination | Full reptation, fully interdiffused interface |
| **Application** | Yogurt lids, medical pouches | Vacuum pouches, retort pouches |
| **Design levers** | Contaminant layer, blend of incompatible polymers | High SIT, high temperature, long dwell |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]

**(a)** Describe the **four-stage mechanism of heat sealing** with a clear energy and material flow diagram. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Derive the **minimum dwell time** for a thermoplastic film sealed between two heated jaws, starting from the 1-D transient heat conduction equation, and discuss the engineering implications of your result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Four-Stage Mechanism (7 marks)**

| Stage | Description | Energy / Material Action | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| I — Heating & Melting | Film surface reaches above Tm or Tg | $Q = m C_p \Delta T + m \Delta H_f$ absorbed from jaws | 1.5 |
| II — Wetting & Contact | Surfaces deform under $P_{\text{seal}}$, intimate contact | Viscous flow; surface energy minimized | 1.5 |
| III — Interdiffusion | Polymer chains reptate across interface | $L(t) = 2\sqrt{Dt}$ governs healing depth | 2.0 |
| IV — Crystallization / Solidification | Cooling under maintained pressure | Lock-in of interdiffused chains; covalent-like entanglement network | 2.0 |

**Key points to mention:** (1) Interdiffusion is the rate-limiting step; (2) Pressure must be maintained during cooling; (3) The seal becomes **cohesive** only when $L(t) \gg M_e$-related length; (4) Seal strength follows de Gennes' $\sqrt{t}$ recovery law.

**(b) Minimum Dwell Time Derivation (7 marks)**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** State the governing equation:
  $$ \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha\,\dfrac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}} $$
- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Boundary & initial conditions: $T(0,t) = T_j$, $T(x,0) = T_0$.
- **Step 3 (2 marks):** Solution:
  $$ \dfrac{T(x,t)-T_0}{T_j-T_0} = \operatorname{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right) $$
- **Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply the seal-criterion: mid-plane reaches $T_c$ (SIT) at $x = L_f/2$.
- **Step 5 (1 mark):** Solve for $t_{\min}$:
  $$ t_{\min} = \dfrac{L_f^{2}}{16\,\alpha\,\eta^{2}}, \quad \eta = \operatorname{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{T_c-T_0}{T_j-T_0}\right) $$
- **Step 6 (1 mark):** **Engineering implications** — (i) $t_{\min} \propto L_f^{2}$, so thicker films need disproportionately longer dwell; (ii) Higher $T_j$ exponentially reduces $t_{\min}$ via $\alpha$ and $\eta$; (iii) Real sealers use a 1.5–3× safety factor.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students forget to **convert units consistently** ($L_f$ in metres, $\alpha$ in m²/s). Another common error is to use the lumped-capacitance formula for **thick films** where Bi > 0.1, leading to grossly underestimated $t_{\min}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]

**(a)** Compare **heat sealing, ultrasonic sealing, and induction sealing** with respect to principle of energy transfer, applicable polymers, and typical end-use applications. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** What is **hot tack strength**? Why is it critical in **vertical form-fill-seal (VFFS)** operations, and how is it measured experimentally? Discuss three process variables that influence hot tack. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparative Table (7 marks)**

| Feature | Heat Sealing | Ultrasonic Sealing | Induction Sealing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy mode** | Conduction (contact) | Mechanical vibration 20–40 kHz | Electromagnetic induction (e.g., 30 MHz) |
| **Heat generation** | External jaws | Internal friction at interface | Eddy currents in metal/foil layer |
| **Suitable polymers** | Thermoplastics, esp. PE, PP, PET | PP, PE, PET, PA; not PVC | Any with conductive foil (Al) liner |
| **Seal appearance** | Smooth or textured | Dot-pattern or line; no thermal damage | Foil bonded to bottle lip |
| **Cycle time** | 0.3 – 3 s | 0.1 – 0.5 s | 0.5 – 1.5 s |
| **Applications** | Pouches, sachets, lids | Blisters, gusset tubes, filter housings | Pharmaceutical bottles, jar caps |
| **Advantages** | Simple, low cost | Clean, energy-efficient | Hermetic, tamper-evident |
| **Limitations** | Heat-sensitive product damage | Limited to certain joint geometries | Requires aluminum foil layer |

**(b) Hot Tack Strength (7 marks)**

- **Definition (2 marks):** Hot tack strength is the **force per unit width** required to separate or peel a heat seal **while it is still in the molten state**, measured **within a few hundred milliseconds** of the jaws opening, typically at the same temperature as the seal bar.
- **Why critical in VFFS (2 marks):** In VFFS lines, the freshly made bottom seal of a new pouch must **support the weight of the falling product** (e.g., 500 g of rice or 1 L of liquid) while still hot. If hot tack is insufficient, the seal **delaminates or bursts** under product load — a catastrophic production loss.
- **Experimental method (1 mark):** Per **ASTM F1921**, the sealed specimen is gripped in tension, the jaws are rapidly retracted, and peak force is recorded as a function of seal bar temperature and **delay time** (typically 100 ms – 1 s).
- **Three process variables influencing hot tack (2 marks):**
  1. **Seal bar temperature** — Higher T up to an optimum raises hot tack; beyond the optimum the polymer degrades and hot tack collapses.
  2. **Seal bar dwell time** — Longer dwell → deeper interdiffusion → higher hot tack (until thermal degradation).
  3. **Cooling rate** — Slow cooling (maintained pressure) preserves interdiffused chains; rapid cooling (jaw opens too soon) freezes-in voids, lowering hot tack.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse **heat seal strength (HSS)** (measured cold) with **hot tack strength (HTS)** (measured hot). Examiners look for the clear distinction: *HSS* = cohesive, cold; *HTS* = load-bearing, hot, and time-dependent. A strong cold seal does **not** guarantee strong hot tack.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Sealing = autohesive fusion**, not adhesive bonding. No third material is introduced.
- **Three primary process variables:** Temperature, Pressure, Dwell Time — the **"sealing triangle."**
- **Four stages:** Heating → Wetting → Interdiffusion → Solidification.
- **SIT (Seal Initiation Temperature)** = the lower bound of the seal window; raise jaw T to **SIT + 15–30 °C** for production.
- **Heat Seal Strength (HSS)** is measured **cold** (ASTM F88); **Hot Tack Strength (HTS)** is measured **hot** (ASTM F1921).
- **Reptation depth** scales as $L(t) \approx 2\sqrt{D t}$, with $D$ being strongly Arrhenius in temperature.
- **Pressure must be held during cooling**, or the seal will shrink-back and weaken.
- **WLF equation** describes how $\tau_d$ (chain disengagement time) drops rapidly above $T_g$.
- **Minimum dwell time** scales as $L_f^{2}$ — a thicker film needs a *quadratically* longer dwell.
- **Seal types:** Permanent (cohesive) vs. Peelable (interfacial/adhesive) — chosen by material formulation and process design.
- **Impulse sealing** alternates heat & cool pulses; preferred for LDPE/LLDPE shrink films.
- **Ultrasonic sealing** uses 20–40 kHz mechanical vibration → internal frictional heat, ideal for blister and gusset seals.
- **Induction sealing** bonds a foil liner to a bottle lip using eddy currents; the plastic itself does not melt directly.
- **Hot tack** is the rate-limiting property in **VFFS** lines — without adequate HTS, product-filling ruptures the fresh seal.
- **Contamination in the seal area** (grease, powder, dust) drastically reduces HSS — production lines use **heated seal-initiation zones** to displace contamination.
- **Crystallinity matters:** highly crystalline polymers (PP, HDPE) have sharp seal windows; amorphous polymers (PET, PVC) have wider windows but require higher absolute temperatures.
- **Failure modes to identify:** adhesive failure (clean separation) vs. cohesive failure (tears within film) — the desired mode is **cohesive** for permanent seals.
- **Standard test conditions:** $T_{\text{ambient}} = 23 \pm 2$ °C, RH = 50 ± 5 %; jaw pressure = 0.275 N/15 mm; dwell = 1.0 s (per ASTM F88 unless otherwise noted).
- **Engineering rule of thumb:** Doubling the jaw temperature above SIT can reduce required dwell time by **up to 70 %**.
- **Equipment classes:** Platen (lab), Bar (batch), Rotary band (continuous high-speed), Impulse (polyolefin films), Through-heat (laminates).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always (i) state the governing equation, (ii) define all symbols with **SI units**, (iii) perform a **numerical estimate** where possible, and (iv) close with a **practical engineering recommendation** — examiners reward this four-part structure generously.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation of Seals in Flexible Packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evaluation of Seals in Flexible Packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 📘 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Seal Evaluation in Flexible Packages** is the systematic, quantitative, and qualitative assessment of the integrity, strength, and functional performance of closures (seams) formed between two or more layers of flexible packaging films. A **seal** is defined as the localized region where two polymeric film surfaces have been joined through the application of **thermal energy, pressure, and dwell time**, with or without an adhesive layer, to produce a hermetic or semi-hermetic barrier that preserves product quality, prevents contamination, and ensures mechanical integrity throughout the supply chain.

In the context of the **KTU PEPOT748 syllabus (Module 3)**, evaluation of seals is treated as a critical quality assurance (QA) and quality control (QC) operation encompassing:

- **Quantitative Seal Strength Measurement** — Determination of peel, shear, and tensile load-bearing capacity of the sealed interface.
- **Hermeticity / Leak Integrity Testing** — Verification that the seal prevents ingress of gases, moisture, and microorganisms.
- **Process-Parameter Correlation** — Linking seal properties to converting line variables (bar temperature, **3 bar jaw pressure**, **dwell time**, and cooling).
- **Functional Fitness-for-Use Assessment** — Evaluating seals under simulated end-use stresses (drop, vibration, thermal cycling, aging).

### Standards Governing Seal Evaluation

| Standard | Scope |
|----------|-------|
| **ASTM F88** | Seal strength of flexible barrier materials (peel test) |
| **ASTM F1921** | Hot tack strength of thermoplastic sealants |
| **ASTM F1140** | Burst strength of flexible packaging |
| **ASTM F2054** | Burst testing of flexible package seals |
| **ASTM F2029** | Heat sealability of flexible webs (lab method) |
| **ASTM D882** | Tensile properties of thin plastic sheeting |

## 🌐 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Dosa Tawa Press" Metaphor**
> Imagine you are making a *masala dosa* at home. You pour batter on a hot tawa (the **heated jaw**), then use a flat spatula (the **pressure element**) to press the top surface, holding it for a few seconds (**dwell time**). If the tawa is too cold or the press time too short, the layers peel apart — just like a **weak seal**. If you press too hard or too long, the surface burns — analogous to **seal burn-through or thinning**.
> Now imagine checking the cooked dosa: you lift one edge and slowly peel it apart. The force you need to pull it apart is the **peel seal strength**. If you blow air into a sealed pouch instead of peeling it, the pressure at which it bursts is the **burst strength**. Both tests tell you how "well-cooked" your seal is.

### Why Evaluate Seals?

A flexible package is only as strong as its weakest seal. Common failures during distribution include:

- **Peel failure** at the seal interface (peelable laminates fail at design force; non-peelable must remain intact).
- **Leak paths** through micro-channels (pinholes, contamination, wrinkles).
- **Seal creep** under sustained static load (e.g., heavy product).
- **Hot tack failure** on form-fill-seal lines immediately after sealing but before cooling.

The evaluation discipline ensures **regulatory compliance**, **shelf-life integrity**, and **consumer safety** — particularly critical for food, pharma, and medical device packaging.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧠 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Seal Formation Triad

A thermoplastic seal is created by simultaneously applying three controlled variables. The resulting weld zone forms through a sequence of physical and rheological events:

### Step-by-Step Seal Formation Logic

1. **Heating Stage (Heat Transfer Phase)**
   - Heat flows from the sealing bar (or impulse wire) into the sealant layer.
   - The polymer reaches its **Vicat Softening Temperature (VST)** or **melting onset (Tm)**.
   - For LDPE: ~95–110 °C; for LLDPE: ~115–125 °C; for ionomer (Surlyn): ~70–95 °C.

2. **Fusion / Inter-diffusion Stage**
   - Polymer chains from opposing film surfaces undergo **inter-diffusion across the interface** (reptation theory, de Gennes).
   - A continuous, monolithic bond forms — analogous to cold welding at the molecular scale.

3. **Pressure Application Stage**
   - **Bar pressure** compresses molten films, expelling trapped air and ensuring intimate contact.
   - Typical values: **1.5 to 4.0 bar** (≈ 150–400 kPa).

4. **Cooling / Crystallization Stage**
   - Pressure is maintained during cooling so chains freeze in the inter-diffused state.
   - Cooling rate governs **crystallinity** in semi-crystalline sealants and thus final seal strength.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Hot Tack" exists:** Immediately after the jaws open, the seal is still molten and mechanically weak. On high-speed form-fill-seal lines, the product drops onto the freshly made seal — this is where **hot tack strength** becomes the limiting parameter, NOT the ambient peel strength.

## 📐 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are mandatory for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) numericals in Module 3.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Applicable Test |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | **Seal (Peel) Strength** | $S_p = \dfrac{F_{max}}{W}$ | **N/15 mm** or N/mm | ASTM F88 |
| 2 | **Average Seal Strength** | $\overline{S_p} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{F_i}{W}$ | N/15 mm | ASTM F88 |
| 3 | **Tensile Strength of Seal** | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_{cs}}$ | MPa | ASTM D882 |
| 4 | **Hoop Stress (Burst)** | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{t}$ | MPa | ASTM F1140 |
| 5 | **Burst Pressure (Thin-walled)** | $P_b = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot t}{D}$ | kPa | ASTM F1140 |
| 6 | **Elongation at Break** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100$ | % | ASTM D882 |
| 7 | **Heat Seal Strength Index (HSSI)** | $HSSI = \dfrac{F_{max}}{t_{sealant}}$ | N/mm² | Internal QA |
| 8 | **Hot Tack Strength** | $F_{HT} = \dfrac{f(t_{open},\ T_{seal})}{W}$ | N/15 mm | ASTM F1921 |
| 9 | **Creep Compliance (Seal)** | $J(t) = \dfrac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0}$ | 1/Pa | Long-term load |
| 10 | **Activation Energy (Seal Failure)** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | 1/s | Kinetics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nomenclature Notes:**
> - $F_{max}$ = maximum recorded peel force (N)
> - $W$ = specimen width (typically **15 mm or 25.4 mm**)
> - $\sigma_y$ = yield stress of film
> - $t$ = film thickness
> - $D$ = pouch diameter (for burst)
> - $T_{seal}$ = seal jaw temperature
> - $t_{open}$ = time after jaw opening (hot tack test)

## 🏭 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Critical Seal Property | Why It Matters |
|-----------------|------------------------|----------------|
| **Food Packaging** | Peel + Burst | Tamper evidence + product protection |
| **Pharmaceutical Blister** | Hermeticity + Peel | Child resistance + sterile barrier |
| **Retort Pouches** | Heat resistance + Creep | Survives 121 °C sterilization |
| **Stand-up Pouches** | Seal-through-contamination resistance | Powder/liquid product safety |
| **Medical Sterile Pouches** | Peel + Sterile integrity | Tyvek/film breathable seals |

> [!TIP]
> In **retort applications**, seal evaluation goes beyond ambient peel — samples are tested after autoclaving at **121 °C for 30 minutes** under steam pressure, simulating worst-case sterilization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🔬 3.1 Derivation of Seal Strength (ASTM F88 Method)

The ASTM F88 test measures the force required to peel apart a sealed specimen at a controlled angle (typically 90° or 180°).

### Step 1: Define the fundamental quantity

By definition, seal strength is force per unit width of the sealed specimen:

$$
S_p \;\triangleq\; \lim_{\Delta W \to 0}\frac{\Delta F}{\Delta W} \;=\; \frac{F_{max}}{W}
$$

### Step 2: Express dimensions for dimensional analysis

$$
[S_p] = \frac{[F]}{[W]} = \frac{\text{N}}{\text{mm}}
$$

### Step 3: Practical specification

Since the ASTM F88 specimen is conventionally cut to **W = 15 mm**, the seal strength is reported in **N / 15 mm**:

$$
S_p^{\text{(practical)}} = \frac{F_{max}\ [\text{N}]}{15\ [\text{mm}]}
$$

### Step 4: Average over n replicates

For statistical reliability, the mean of $n$ samples is used:

$$
\overline{S_p} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{F_i}{W_i}
$$

### Step 5: Standard deviation (QA reporting)

$$
\sigma_{S_p} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(S_{p,i} - \overline{S_p}\right)^2}
$$

## 🔬 3.2 Derivation of Burst Pressure (ASTM F1140 — Constrained Burst)

This derivation assumes the pouch behaves as a thin-walled pressure vessel.

### Step 1: Free Body of a Half-Pouch

Consider the burst pouch as a thin-walled cylinder of radius $r$, wall thickness $t$, and internal pressure $P_b$. Cut the cylinder longitudinally and balance forces on the projected area.

### Step 2: Force Balance on Projected Area

Pressure force on projected area = Resistive hoop force × 2 (two walls):

$$
P_b \cdot (2r \cdot L) = 2 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot t \cdot L
$$

Where $L$ is the longitudinal length considered.

### Step 3: Simplification

The $L$ cancels out:

$$
P_b \cdot 2r = 2 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot t
$$

### Step 4: Solve for $P_b$

$$
P_b = \frac{\sigma_y \cdot t}{r}
$$

Equivalently, using diameter $D = 2r$:

$$
P_b = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot t}{D}
$$

### Step 5: Modification for Seal Region

If the seal is the weakest link, $\sigma_y$ is replaced by the **seal shear strength** $\tau_s$:

$$
P_{b,seal} = \frac{2 \cdot \tau_s \cdot t_{seal}}{D}
$$

## 🔬 3.3 Hot Tack Strength — Time-Dependent Decay

Immediately after jaw release, the seal strength decays with time as the polymer crystallizes.

### Empirical Model

$$
F_{HT}(t) = F_\infty + (F_0 - F_\infty) \cdot e^{-t/\tau_c}
$$

Where:

- $F_\infty$ = fully cooled seal strength
- $F_0$ = peak hot tack strength (often **lower** than $F_\infty$ — depends on polymer)
- $\tau_c$ = crystallization time constant
- $t$ = time after jaw opening

### Physical Meaning

- **At $t = 0$:** $F_{HT} = F_0$ (just released, still hot)
- **As $t \to \infty$:** $F_{HT} \to F_\infty$ (fully crystallized)
- For LDPE: $F_0 < F_\infty$ → hot tack is the weakest phase
- For ionomers: $F_0$ can approach $F_\infty$ rapidly — preferred for high-speed FFS

## 🔬 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:**
> A flexible pouch made of LDPE film (yield stress $\sigma_y = 12$ MPa, thickness $t = 80$ μm) is subjected to a constrained burst test. The seal zone width is $w_s = 3$ mm with seal shear strength $\tau_s = 4.5$ MPa. The pouch has effective diameter $D = 100$ mm. Find:
> (i) Burst pressure of the unsealed film.
> (ii) Burst pressure limited by the seal.
> (iii) Identify the failure mode and percentage reduction.

### Solution

**(i) Film burst pressure**

$$
P_b^{film} = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot t}{D} = \frac{2 \times 12 \times 10^6 \times 80 \times 10^{-6}}{100 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
P_b^{film} = \frac{2 \times 12 \times 80}{100 \times 10^3}\ [\text{kPa}] = \frac{1920}{100}\ \text{kPa} = 19.2\ \text{kPa}
$$

**(ii) Seal-limited burst pressure**

$$
P_b^{seal} = \frac{2 \cdot \tau_s \cdot w_s}{D} = \frac{2 \times 4.5 \times 10^6 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}{100 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
P_b^{seal} = \frac{2 \times 4.5 \times 3}{100 \times 10^3}\ [\text{kPa}] \times 10^6 \times 10^{-3} = \frac{27}{100}\ \text{kPa} \times 10^3
$$

Re-checking with proper units: $4.5 \times 10^6 \times 3 \times 10^{-3} = 13{,}500\ \text{N/m}$. Divided by $0.1$ m = $135{,}000\ \text{Pa}$ = **135 kPa**.

**(iii) Comparison**

The film can withstand **19.2 kPa** while the seal can withstand **135 kPa** → **Film fails first** (the seal is over-engineered for this geometry). The failure mode is **film burst**, not seal rupture. To verify seal integrity, the test is repeated with a smaller diameter or thicker film.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🗺️ 4.1 Seal Evaluation Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Sample Received] --> B[Visual Inspection]
    B --> C{Defects Visible?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Reject and Document]
    C -- No --> E[Cut Specimen 15 mm x 100 mm]
    E --> F[Condition at 23 deg C 50 percent RH for 24 hr]
    F --> G{Test Type?}
    G --> H1[Peel Test ASTM F88]
    G --> H2[Burst Test ASTM F1140]
    G --> H3[Hot Tack Test ASTM F1921]
    G --> H4[Creep Test Custom]
    H1 --> I1[Tensile Machine 90 deg Peel 200 mm/min]
    H2 --> I2[Pressure Ramp until Burst]
    H3 --> I3[Jaws Open, Pull at Set Delay]
    H4 --> I4[Static Load over Time]
    I1 --> J[Record F max and Plot Curve]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    J --> K{Within Specification?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Pass and Release Batch]
    K -- No --> M[Hold and Investigate Root Cause]
    M --> N[Adjust Temperature or Pressure or Dwell]
    N --> O[Re-run and Validate]
    O --> G
    L --> P[End]
```

## 🗺️ 4.2 Seal Strength Test Setup (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LoadFrame[Universal Testing Machine UTM]
        A1[Upper Grip] --> A2[Load Cell 100 N Capacity]
        A2 --> A3[Crosshead Drive Servo]
        A3 --> A4[Data Acquisition DAQ]
    end
    subgraph Specimen[Test Specimen]
        B1[Sealed Film Strip 15 mm Wide]
        B1 --> B2[Seal Zone in Middle]
        B2 --> B3[Two Free Tails for Gripping]
    end
    subgraph Environment[Controlled Chamber]
        C1[Temperature 23 plus minus 2 deg C]
        C2[Humidity 50 plus minus 5 percent RH]
    end
    A1 --> B3
    A4 --> D[PC with Software]
    D --> E[Force vs Displacement Plot]
    E --> F[Calculate Seal Strength N per 15 mm]
```

## 🗺️ 4.3 Seal Failure Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Apply Peel Force] --> T{Where does Failure Initiate?}
    T -- AT --> U[Adhesive Failure at Interface]
    T -- PT --> V[Material Tear of Substrate]
    T -- ST --> W[Sealant Tearing Cohesive]
    T -- WB --> X[Welded Briding Bubble Defect]
    U --> U1[Cure Temperature too Low or Sealant Mismatch]
    V --> V1[Film too Weak relative to Seal]
    W --> W1[Optimal Seal Sealant Heals Fully]
    X --> X1[Air Trapped Increase Pressure]
```

## 📊 4.4 Typical Seal Strength Curve (Force vs Displacement)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Curve[Seal Peel Curve Phases]
        P1[Phase 1 Initial Peak F1] --> P2[Phase 2 Plateau Region F avg]
        P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Final Tail F end]
    end
    P1 --> Note1[Used to characterize peel initiation energy]
    P2 --> Note2[Reported as Average Seal Strength N per 15 mm]
    P3 --> Note3[Indicates cohesive vs adhesive fracture mode]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Interpretation of Curve:**
> - A **flat plateau** indicates consistent cohesive failure within the sealant — a *good* seal.
> - A **continuously rising curve** indicates partial welding — *under-sealed*.
> - A **high initial peak followed by drop to zero** indicates *seal burn-through* or *seal thinning* — *over-sealed*.
> - **Oscillating force** suggests *sticking-slip* peeling, characteristic of contaminated seal interfaces.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define seal strength as per ASTM F88 and state its units. Mention the standard specimen width used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Seal strength, as per **ASTM F88**, is defined as the **maximum force required to peel apart a sealed flexible film specimen**, divided by the **width of the specimen**. It is calculated as:

$$
S_p = \frac{F_{max}}{W}
$$

Units: **N/15 mm** (Newtons per 15 mm of seal width). The standard specimen width is **15 mm** (sometimes 25.4 mm is used for wider seals), and the test is conducted at a crosshead speed of **200–300 mm/min** at a peel angle of 90° or 180°.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Distinguish between 'peel strength' and 'hot tack strength'. Why is hot tack critical in form-fill-seal (FFS) lines?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Peel strength (ASTM F88):** The force required to peel a seal *after it has fully cooled and crystallized* at ambient conditions. It represents long-term package integrity.
- **Hot tack strength (ASTM F1921):** The force required to peel a seal *immediately after the jaws open*, while the polymer is still molten/semi-molten.

**Why hot tack matters:** In **Form-Fill-Seal (FFS)** lines, the product (e.g., liquid, granules) is dropped onto the freshly made seal within **0.1–0.5 seconds**. If the hot tack is insufficient, the seal opens under product weight, causing **leakers** on the line. Hot tack is therefore the **process-limiting parameter** on high-speed lines, while peel strength is the **post-process durability parameter**.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEPOT748, Modified Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle, procedure, and interpretation** of the **ASTM F88 seal strength test** with a neat sketch. Discuss the **different seal failure modes** observed during peel testing and how each is interpreted. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flexible laminate pouch is made of PET (12 μm) / Al foil (9 μm) / LLDPE (80 μm). The seal is formed on the LLDPE side. During burst testing (ASTM F1140), the burst occurs at the seal at a pressure of **45 kPa** with effective diameter **80 mm**. If the LLDPE layer yields at **12 MPa** and its thickness is **80 μm**, calculate: **(7 marks)**
- (i) Burst pressure of the base film alone.
- (ii) The **seal shear strength** assuming seal fails first.
- (iii) Comment on whether the seal is the **weakest link** in this design.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Principle:** ASTM F88 measures the force required to separate two sealed films by peeling one from the other at a controlled angle (90° or 180°). The test simulates real-world stresses encountered during opening, transportation, and handling.

**Apparatus:**
- Universal Testing Machine (UTM) with 100 N load cell
- Pneumatic or screw grips with rubber faces
- Specimen cutter (15 mm wide)
- Environmental chamber (optional for hot seal tests)

**Procedure:**
1. Cut sealed specimen to **15 mm × 100 mm**, with seal at the center.
2. Condition at **23 ± 2 °C / 50 ± 5 % RH** for 24 h (per ASTM D618).
3. Mount free tails in upper and lower grips with peel angle maintained at 90° or 180°.
4. Pull at **200 mm/min** until complete separation or 100 mm peel length.
5. Record force vs displacement curve.

**Failure Modes & Interpretation:**

| Mode | Description | Indicates |
|------|-------------|-----------|
| **Adhesion failure (AF)** | Clean separation at the original interface | Under-sealing, contamination, or sealant mismatch |
| **Cohesion failure (CF)** | Tearing within the sealant layer | Optimal seal — full polymer fusion |
| **Substrate failure (SF)** | Tear propagates into the film body | Seal stronger than the film — ideal for hermetic applications |
| **Bridging failure** | Gaps, bubbles, or wrinkles at seal | Trapped air, inconsistent pressure, dirty jaws |

**(b) [7 marks]**

**(i) Film burst pressure:**

$$
P_b^{film} = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot t}{D} = \frac{2 \times 12 \times 10^6 \times 80 \times 10^{-6}}{80 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
P_b^{film} = \frac{2 \times 12 \times 80}{80 \times 10^3}\ \text{kPa} = 24\ \text{kPa}
$$

**(ii) Seal shear strength:**

Given the seal fails first at $P_b = 45$ kPa, and since this is higher than 24 kPa, the **seal does not actually fail first** — the calculation must reconcile this. Re-read: the burst occurred at the seal at 45 kPa. We use:

$$
\tau_s = \frac{P_b \cdot D}{2 \cdot w_s} = \frac{45 \times 10^3 \times 80 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

Assuming seal width $w_s = 3$ mm:

$$
\tau_s = \frac{45{,}000 \times 0.08}{0.006} = \frac{3600}{0.006} = 600{,}000\ \text{Pa} = 0.6\ \text{MPa}
$$

**(iii) Comparison:**

The seal shear strength (**0.6 MPa**) is much **lower** than the film yield strength (**12 MPa**). Therefore, the seal is the **weakest link**. Even though the burst pressure exceeded the film-only theoretical value (due to multilayer laminate reinforcement from PET and Al foil not accounted for in the simple model), the seal rupture at 45 kPa confirms it as the **failure-initiating component**. In production, increasing **seal temperature** or **dwell time** would improve $\tau_s$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the **hot tack test (ASTM F1921)** in detail. Explain why **hot tack strength** and not **peel strength** is the limiting parameter on high-speed FFS lines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **creep test** for flexible package seals. How is it used to predict long-term seal reliability under static load? Provide an example calculation of creep compliance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Hot Tack Test (ASTM F1921) — Detailed Explanation:**

The hot tack test measures the **strength of a freshly made seal immediately after the jaws open**, while the polymer is still in a molten or partially molten state.

**Principle:** Two jaws are heated to the seal temperature, close on the film for a controlled **dwell time**, then open at a preset **delay time** $t_{delay}$ (typically 0.1–1.0 s). One tail of the specimen is immediately gripped and pulled at a defined rate; the force required to peel the molten seal is recorded.

**Apparatus:**
- Heated jaws with PID temperature control
- Programmable delay-time mechanism (ms resolution)
- Load cell (typically 50–100 N)
- Specimen cutter

**Test Variables:**
- Seal Temperature: 90–200 °C
- Seal Pressure: 1.5–4.0 bar
- Dwell Time: 0.25–2.0 s
- Delay Time: 0.1–1.0 s

**Why Hot Tack Limits FFS Performance:**

In **Vertical Form-Fill-Seal (VFFS)** lines running at 60–120 bags/min, the product fill cycle takes ~0.5–1.0 s. During this window, the **vertical seal is supporting the weight of the product**. If hot tack strength at $t = t_{delay}$ is less than the product-induced stress, the seal **opens prematurely**, causing:

- Leakers on the line
- Product loss
- Contamination
- Stoppage for rework

**Comparison Chart:**

| Parameter | Peel Strength (ASTM F88) | Hot Tack Strength (ASTM F1921) |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Test timing | After full cooling | Immediately after jaw opening |
| Critical for | Storage & distribution | In-process handling |
| For LDPE | ~3–5 N/15 mm | ~0.5–2 N/15 mm |
| For Ionomer | ~3–5 N/15 mm | ~2–4 N/15 mm |

**Polymer Selection Insight:** Ionomers (e.g., Surlyn®) are preferred for FFS due to their **rapid crystallization** and **high hot tack** even at low temperatures, while standard LDPE is unsuitable for ultra-high-speed lines.

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Creep Test for Seals:**

**Definition:** Creep is the **time-dependent deformation** of a sealed joint under a **constant static load** at constant temperature. Creep testing simulates long-term storage of heavy products (e.g., detergent pouches, liquid pouches stacked in warehouses).

**Test Procedure:**
