In the context of **plastics packaging**, thermoforming is the dominant process for producing **rigid and semi-rigid disposable/returnable containers**, including blister packs, clamshells, trays, lids, cups, tubs, and protective cushion inserts. It is classified under **IS 14543:1998 / ASTM D 1741** related converting operations and forms a vital part of the *plastics packaging value chain* introduced in Module 4 of PEPOT748.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine making a *modak* or a *kuzhalappam* using hot thermoplastic dough. You take a flat piece of dough, warm it until it becomes stretchy and soft, then press it down onto a shaped wooden/metal mold. A gentle vacuum or hand-pressure pulls the soft sheet into every groove of the mold. Once it cools, the sheet "remembers" the new shape, and you trim off the excess edges. **That is exactly how a thermoformed blister pack or chocolate tray is made — except the "dough" is a real polymer sheet, and the "hand" is a vacuum pump or compressed air.**

The visual mnemonic is simple:
- **Heat** the polymer sheet until it sags.
- **Form** it against a mold using vacuum or pressure.
- **Cool** the formed shape until it becomes dimensionally stable.
- **Trim** the finished container off the web.

This four-step mantra (**H-F-C-T**) is the operational backbone of every commercial thermoforming line — from a 2-station lab former to a 12-station rotary **form-fill-seal (FFS)** machine running at **30–50 cycles/min**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics in Packaging

- **Standard forming temperature window:** **120 °C – 180 °C** for amorphous grades (PS, PVC, PET, PETG); **135 °C – 165 °C** for PP; **100 °C – 130 °C** for LDPE.
- **Vacuum pressure for vacuum forming:** typically **–0.6 bar to –0.95 bar gauge** (i.e., 60–95 kPa below atmospheric).
- **Plug-assist pressure (pressure forming):** **1.0 – 4.0 bar** pneumatic.
- **Cycle time:** **2 – 12 seconds** depending on material thickness, mold conductivity, and machine stations.
- **Material thickness (gauge) range:** **0.05 mm – 6.0 mm**; for packaging **0.15 mm – 1.5 mm** is most common.
- **Mold material cost (relative):** **Aluminum = 1×, Fiber-reinforced epoxy (FRP) = 0.6×, Tooling board = 0.4×**.

## 1.4 Syllabus Anchors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Anchor Map for "Thermoformed"**
> - 4.3 — Thermoforming of plastic packaging webs: principles and equipment.
> - 4.4 — Vacuum forming, pressure forming, plug-assist, twin-sheet forming.
> - 4.5 — Material selection (PVC, PET, PS, PP, PE, EVOH, multilayer barrier structures).
> - 4.6 — Molds, trimming, stacking, and downstream integration with **form-fill-seal (FFS)** lines.
> - 4.7 — Defect analysis: webbing, chill marks, sag, draw-down, orange peel.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Sequential Stages of Thermoforming

1. **Heating (Softening Stage)**
   - The thermoplastic sheet is gripped in a clamping frame and transported to the oven zone.
   - **Heaters:** Calrod, ceramic infrared, quartz, or catalytic infrared emitters.
   - Radiative and convective transfer raises the polymer above its softening point.
   - Sheet sag (vertical drop) must be controlled to prevent uneven thickness.

2. **Forming (Shaping Stage)**
   - The pliable sheet is mechanically, pneumatically, or vacuum-driven into the mold cavity.
   - The polymer chain segments reorient, leading to **biaxial orientation** — this enhances **tensile strength, barrier, and clarity** in oriented grades such as **OPS, BOPS, APET, CPET**.

3. **Cooling (Solidification Stage)**
   - Heat is removed via conduction into the mold, assisted by water channels, fans, or chilled air.
   - The part must cool **below the heat-distortion temperature (HDT)** before ejection; otherwise, **post-ejection warpage** occurs.

4. **Trimming (Finishing Stage)**
   - The formed web is separated from the parent sheet using steel-rule dies, matched-metal dies, or CNC routers.
   - Trim scrap is typically **25% – 50%** of the original web and is reground for in-house recycling (a circular-economy strength of thermoforming).

## 2.2 Classification of Thermoforming Methods

| **Method** | **Driving Force** | **Mold Type** | **Packaging Application** | **Relative Tooling Cost** |
|------------|-------------------|---------------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Vacuum Forming** | Vacuum (~ –0.9 bar) | Female (cavity) | Blister packs, trays, cups | **Low** (1×) |
| **Pressure Forming** | Compressed air (2 – 4 bar) + vacuum | Male (plug) | Detailed clamshells, point-of-sale | **Medium** (3 – 5×) |
| **Drape Forming** | Mechanical drape over male mold | Male | Skylights, thick display packs | **Low** |
| **Plug-Assist Forming** | Mechanical plug + vacuum | Female | Deep-draw cups, margarine tubs | **Medium** (2×) |
| **Twin-Sheet Forming** | Two sheets bonded between molds | Matched | Hollow pallets, large containers | **High** (8 – 12×) |
| **Free-Blowing / Billet** | Air pressure (no mold) | None | Sheet biaxial orientation | N/A |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat-Sheet Box — All the equations a KTU 2024 paper-trainer can expect on "Thermoformed"**

| **#** | **Equation / Relation** | **LaTeX Form** | **Variables & Units** | **Where Used** |
|-------|------------------------|----------------|------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Sheet sag under self-weight | $\delta = \dfrac{\rho g L^{4}}{32 \, E \, t^{2}}$ | $\delta$ = sag (m), $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $g = 9.81$ m/s², $L$ = span (m), $E$ = tensile modulus (Pa), $t$ = sheet thickness (m) | Sag control, oven design |
| 2 | Heat absorbed for softening | $Q = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | $Q$ (J), $m$ (kg), $c_p$ (J/kg·K), $\Delta T$ (K) | Heater load sizing |
| 3 | Radiative heat flux (Stefan-Boltzmann) | $q = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^{4}$ | $q$ (W/m²), $\varepsilon$ = emissivity, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴, $T$ (K) | IR oven design |
| 4 | Thickness distribution (vacuum) | $t_{f} = t_{0} \cdot \dfrac{A_{0}}{A_{f}}$ | $t_{0}$ = original thickness, $A_{0}$ = original area, $t_{f}$ = final thickness, $A_{f}$ = final area | Material distribution prediction |
| 5 | Draw ratio | $DR = \dfrac{h}{t_{0}}$ | $h$ = draw depth (m), $t_{0}$ = original thickness (m) | Deep-draw feasibility check |
| 6 | Biaxial orientation strain (true) | $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln \left( \dfrac{\lambda_{f}}{\lambda_{0}} \right)$ | $\lambda$ = draw ratio | Strength/clarity correlation |
| 7 | Cooling time (1-D conduction) | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{s^{2}}{4 \alpha}$ | $s$ = thickness (m), $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity (m²/s) | Cycle-time estimation |
| 8 | Trim scrap fraction | $S = \dfrac{A_{web} - A_{parts}}{A_{web}} \times 100$ | $A$ = areas (m²) | Recycling/economics |
| 9 | Clamping force | $F_{c} = p \cdot A_{clamp}$ | $F_{c}$ (N), $p$ = air pressure (Pa), $A_{clamp}$ (m²) | Clamp frame sizing |
| 10 | Material throughput | $T_{h} = N_{c} \cdot A_{web} \cdot t \cdot \rho$ | $N_{c}$ = cycles/min, $T_{h}$ (kg/h) | Production capacity |

> **Examination Tip:** The examiner loves questions where you must substitute values into Eq. (1), (4), or (7) to justify a design decision. Always state the units explicitly.

## 2.4 Why This Matters in the Engineering Field

- **Sustainability angle:** Thermoforming trim scrap is *in-house re-cyclable* (closed-loop), unlike injection molding sprue/runner waste. This makes thermoformed packaging favored for **EU SUP Directive (2019/904)** and **India EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility)** compliance.
- **Cost angle:** Tooling is **5 – 10× cheaper** than injection molding for short to medium runs (< 1 million parts), enabling low-MOQ custom packaging.
- **Performance angle:** Biaxial orientation achieved *in situ* during forming gives **30% – 50% improvement** in impact and clarity vs. an extruded sheet of the same base resin.
- **Industry reach:** Used by **Huhtamaki, Pactiv, Sealed Air, Sonoco, Constantia Flexibles, UFlex, Manjushree, and EPL** for dairy cups, blister lidding, punnets, and clamshells.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sheet Sag Equation (cantilever beam self-weight)

**Starting physical model:** A clamped thermoplastic sheet of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, and density $\rho$ sags under gravity. We treat it as a thin plate fixed at both ends with distributed self-weight $q = \rho g t$ per unit area.

**Step 1 — Distributed load per unit length**
$$
q = \rho \cdot g \cdot t \quad \text{[N/m²]}
$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent load on a strip of width $w$**
$$
Q = q \cdot w = \rho \cdot g \cdot t \cdot w \quad \text{[N/m]}
$$

**Step 3 — Treat as a simply supported beam under uniform load. Maximum deflection at mid-span:**
$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \cdot Q \cdot L^{4}}{384 \cdot E \cdot I}
$$

**Step 4 — Moment of inertia of a rectangular cross-section:**
$$
I = \dfrac{w \cdot t^{3}}{12} \quad \text{[m⁴]}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $Q$ and $I$ into the deflection expression:**
$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \cdot (\rho g t w) \cdot L^{4}}{384 \cdot E \cdot (\frac{w t^{3}}{12})}
$$

**Step 6 — Algebraic simplification (cancelling $w$ and $t$):**
$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot L^{4}}{32 \cdot E \cdot t^{2}}
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering approximation (formally known as the **packed-beam factor**):**
$$
\boxed{\delta = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot L^{4}}{32 \cdot E \cdot t^{2}}}
$$

> This is the **K key relation** that justifies the *oven-sag-control* strategy: a thicker sheet, lower span, or stiffer resin reduces the dangerous mid-web sag.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thickness Distribution During Vacuum Forming

**Mass conservation principle:** Incompressible polymer (volume is conserved) means:
$$
A_{0} \cdot t_{0} = A_{f} \cdot t_{f}
$$

**Solving for final thickness $t_{f}$:**
$$
\boxed{t_{f} = t_{0} \cdot \dfrac{A_{0}}{A_{f}}}
$$

**For a hemispherical blister of radius $R$ and original flat sheet area $A_{0} = \pi R^{2}$:**

**Step 1 — Area of hemisphere surface:**
$$
A_{f} = 2 \pi R^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into thickness formula:**
$$
t_{f} = t_{0} \cdot \dfrac{\pi R^{2}}{2 \pi R^{2}} = \dfrac{t_{0}}{2}
$$

**Result:** A hemispherical blister from a 0.30 mm sheet yields a minimum **wall thickness of 0.15 mm** at the pole.

> This shows why deep-draw, thin-walled cups are **inherently thickness-limited**, and why *plug-assist* forming is used to redistribute material toward the bottom corners.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cooling Time Estimate (Transient Conduction)

**Fourier number at centerline of a slab cooled from both sides equals $\sim 0.5$ at full solidification:**
$$
Fo = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot t_{cool}}{s^{2}} = 0.5
$$

**Solving for cooling time:**
$$
\boxed{t_{cool} = \dfrac{s^{2}}{2 \alpha}}
$$

A more conservative and **industry-accepted** estimate uses the **2σ** simplification (skin solidifies first, then bulk follows):
$$
\boxed{t_{cool} = \dfrac{s^{2}}{4 \alpha}}
$$

**Numerical example:** For a 1.0 mm HIPS sheet with $\alpha = 9 \times 10^{-8}$ m²/s:
$$
t_{cool} = \dfrac{(1.0 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{4 \times 9 \times 10^{-8}} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-6}}{3.6 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 2.78 \text{ s}
$$

> This 2.8-second result aligns with empirical cycle times on commercial FFS thermoformers.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Defect, Cause, Cure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favourite Analytical Table — Examiner will award full marks only if you map *Defect → Root Cause → Cure* explicitly.**

| **Defect** | **Visual Symptom** | **Probable Root Cause** | **Engineering Cure** |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| **Webbing** | Excess polymer bridging mold corners | Insufficient vacuum; high sheet temperature; shallow draw | Lower sheet temp, increase vacuum to **–0.95 bar**, sharpen mold corner radii |
| **Chill Mark** | Whitish streaks / surface gloss loss | Premature contact with cold mold | Pre-heat mold to **60 – 80 °C**, lower clamp force |
| **Sag / Draw-down** | Excessive vertical droop in oven | Heater too hot, span too long, low-modulus resin | Reduce heater power, add **sag-control frame**, increase $t$ |
| **Orange Peel** | Matte, dimpled surface | Non-uniform heating, low sheet temp | Verify IR zone balance, raise $T$ by **5 – 10 °C** |
| **Pinholes** | Microscopic perforations | Material contamination, excessive draw ratio | Filter regrind, cap $DR < 1.5$, use EVOH tie-layer |
| **Post-ejection Warp** | Twisted or cupped part | Ejected above HDT; uneven cooling | Cool **15 °C below HDT**, balance coolant channels |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow of Roll-Fed Thermoforming (Inline FFS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rollA[Roll Unwind Station] --> clampA[Sheet Clamp Frame]
    clampA --> ovenA[Infrared Oven Indexing Zone]
    ovenA --> formA[Forming Station Vacuum and Pressure]
    formA --> coolA[Mold Cooling and Solidification]
    coolA --> trimA[Steel Rule Die Trimming]
    trimA --> stackA[Stacking and Discharge Conveyor]
    trimA --> scrapA[Trim Scrap Regrind Granulator]
    scrapA --> extrA[Sheet Extrusion Line]
    extrA --> rollA
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The polymer sheet unwinds, indexes through a four-zone IR oven, forms on a vacuum mold, cools, is trimmed, and the scrap is re-extruded into a fresh sheet — a **closed-loop recycling circuit** valued by KTU 2024 sustainability questions.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pressure-Forming Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ControlSystem
        ctrA[PLC Controller]
        ctrB[HMI Operator Panel]
        ctrC[Temperature PID Loops]
    end
    subgraph HeatingModule
        heatA[IR Heater Bank Top Zone]
        heatB[IR Heater Bank Bottom Zone]
    end
    subgraph FormingModule
        plugA[Plug Assist Cylinder]
        vacA[Vacuum Pump Reservoir]
        presA[Compressed Air Manifold]
        moldA[Aluminum Mold]
    end
    subgraph TrimmingModule
        trimA2[Steel Rule Die Station]
        trimB2[Trim Press and Die Cushion]
    end
    ctrA --> heatA
    ctrA --> heatB
    ctrA --> plugA
    ctrA --> vacA
    ctrA --> presA
    ctrB --> ctrA
    ctrC --> heatA
    ctrC --> heatB
    plugA --> moldA
    vacA --> moldA
    presA --> moldA
    moldA --> trimA2
    trimA2 --> trimB2
```

## 4.3 Thermoform–Mold Cooling Water Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    chillerA[Chiller Unit] --> pumpA[Circulation Pump]
    pumpA --> manA[Manifold Header]
    manA --> moldA3[Mold Cooling Channel A]
    moldA3 --> moldB3[Mold Cooling Channel B]
    moldB3 --> manB[Return Manifold]
    manB --> chillerA
    manA --> tempA[Inlet Thermocouple]
    manB --> tempB[Outlet Thermocouple]
    tempA --> ctrA3[PLC Feedback Loop]
    tempB --> ctrA3
    ctrA3 --> chillerA
```

## 4.4 Material-Selection Decision Tree (KTU exam-favorite)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startA[Choose Thermoformed Packaging Resin] --> q1{Is contact with food?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Need high clarity?}
    q2 -- Yes --> q3{Need heat resistance?}
    q3 -- Yes --> resA[CPET or APET Tray]
    q3 -- No --> resB[PET or PETG Cup]
    q2 -- No --> q4{Need oxygen barrier?}
    q4 -- Yes --> resC[PP EVOH Multilayer]
    q4 -- No --> resD[PP or HIPS Container]
    q1 -- No --> q5{Need flame retardancy?}
    q5 -- Yes --> resE[FR ABS Sheet]
    q5 -- No --> resF[PVC or PS General Purpose]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper PEPOT748]**
**Define the term "Thermoforming" as applied to plastics packaging. List any four common products obtained by this process.**

**Model Answer:**
Thermoforming is a plastics packaging process in which a flat thermoplastic sheet is heated to a pliable state, formed against a mold using vacuum, pressure, or mechanical assist, and cooled to retain the shape. The four common products are:
1. Blister packs (for pharmaceutical tablets).
2. Clamshell containers (for toys and electronics).
3. Thermoformed cups and tubs (for dairy, ice cream).
4. Tray and lidding structures (for ready-to-eat meals, fruits, chocolates).

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark per product, total 3.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the significance of the *draw ratio* in thermoforming? What is the safe upper limit for a single-stage vacuum forming operation?**

**Model Answer:**
The draw ratio is the ratio of draw depth to original sheet thickness, $DR = h / t_{0}$. It predicts wall-thinning and the likelihood of pinholing, webbing, or split corners. The **safe upper limit** for single-stage vacuum forming is approximately **1.5:1** for commodity grades and up to **3:1** with plug-assist for deep-draw packaging such as margarine tubs and yogurt cups.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for limit, 1 mark for example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Most students **lose 2–3 marks** by writing the *concept* of vacuum forming without labeling the **pressurization sequence** (vacuum timing vs. plug timing).
> - Another common 2-mark deduction: failing to give **units** when stating equations like $t_{f} = t_{0} (A_{0}/A_{f})$.
> - Always remember to mention **trim-scrap recycling** — it is a favorite board mark-distinction question.
> - For "compare" questions, use a **two-column table**; bulleted prose earns only 50% of the available marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Vacuum Forming of Food Packaging Trays**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4, Q. PEPOT748-04-B1]**

**(a)** With the help of a clearly labeled schematic, describe the **step-by-step working of a roll-fed vacuum forming machine** for the production of rigid PET trays. Explain the role of each station. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 0.5 mm thick PET sheet is used to produce a rectangular tray of dimensions **140 mm × 100 mm × 25 mm deep**, with a uniform draft angle of 2°. The original web area is 200 mm × 140 mm. Calculate (i) the final average wall thickness, and (ii) the trim-scrap percentage. Comment on the feasibility of the design. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution (a):**

The **six stations** in a roll-fed vacuum forming line are:

1. **Unwind station** — mounts the roll of PET sheet, tension-controlled via a dancer roll.
2. **Indexing / Clamp frame** — pneumatic clamps grip the sheet edges and transport it to the oven.
3. **Heating zone (oven)** — IR heaters raise the sheet to **130 – 150 °C** for PET, just above $T_g \approx 80 °C$ but below the crystallization peak.
4. **Forming station** — the heated sheet drapes over the female mold; vacuum at **–0.9 bar** is applied through microporous vents to pull the polymer onto the cavity walls.
5. **Cooling station** — chilled water at **15 °C** circulates through aluminum mold channels; the formed part cools to **below 60 °C** before release.
6. **Trimming & stacking** — a steel-rule die cuts the tray perimeter; the formed parts are stacked; trim scrap is baled and re-extruded.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $t_{0} = 0.5$ mm; tray dimensions 140 mm × 100 mm × 25 mm; web area 200 mm × 140 mm = **28,000 mm²**.

**(i) Final average wall thickness using mass conservation:**

Inner surface area of the tray (with 2° draft):
$$
A_{f} = (140 \times 100) + 2 \times (140 \times 25) + 2 \times (100 \times 25) = 14{,}000 + 7{,}000 + 5{,}000 = 26{,}000 \text{ mm²}
$$

Apply Eq. (4) from the formula sheet:
$$
t_{f} = t_{0} \cdot \dfrac{A_{0}}{A_{f}} = 0.5 \times \dfrac{28{,}000}{26{,}000} \approx 0.538 \text{ mm}
$$

**Result:** $t_{f} \approx$ **0.54 mm** average wall thickness.

**(ii) Trim-scrap percentage using Eq. (8):**
$$
S = \dfrac{28{,}000 - 14{,}000}{28{,}000} \times 100 = 50.0 \text{ \%}
$$

**Feasibility Comment:** Although wall thickness (0.54 mm) is safe (well above the 0.10 mm pinhole threshold), the **50% trim scrap** is high. The designer should optimize part nesting to drop scrap below **30%**, and consider **inline scrap regrinding** for the extrusion line.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Pressure Forming vs. Plug-Assist Forming**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, Q. PEPOT748-04-B2 — Alternative choice]**

**(a)** Compare **vacuum forming**, **pressure forming**, and **plug-assist forming** under the headings: (i) driving force, (ii) achievable draw ratio, (iii) mold material & cost, (iv) typical packaging application, and (v) clarity & detail. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 0.30 mm HIPS sheet is to be thermoformed into a chocolate tray with a maximum cavity depth of 18 mm. Compute the **draw ratio** and decide which of the three methods above is best suited. Justify your answer with a quantitative thickness-distribution estimate. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution (a):**

| **Parameter** | **Vacuum Forming** | **Pressure Forming** | **Plug-Assist Forming** |
|---------------|--------------------|----------------------|-------------------------|
| Driving force | Vacuum (~ –0.9 bar) | Compressed air (2 – 4 bar) + vacuum | Mechanical plug push + vacuum |
| Achievable DR | 1.0 : 1 to 1.5 : 1 | 1.5 : 1 to 2.5 : 1 | 1.5 : 1 to 3.0 : 1 |
| Mold cost | Lowest (aluminum, FRP) | Higher (must withstand pressure) | Medium (mechanical plug system) |
| Application | Trays, cups, lids | Detailed clamshells | Deep-draw tubs (margarine, yogurt) |
| Clarity / detail | Moderate | High (sharp text & corners) | Good bottom-wall uniformity |

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $t_{0} = 0.30$ mm = $0.30 \times 10^{-3}$ m; depth $h = 18$ mm = $0.018$ m.

**Draw ratio (Eq. 5):**
$$
DR = \dfrac{h}{t_{0}} = \dfrac{18 \text{ mm}}{0.30 \text{ mm}} = 60
$$

**This value is misleading** because the formula above is for *full sheet thickness*, while the *cavity-depth-to-thickness* ratio is more meaningful:
$$
DR_{effective} = \dfrac{18}{0.30} = 60 \quad \text{(raw)} \quad ; \quad DR_{cavity-to-thickness} = \dfrac{18}{0.30} = 60
$$

A more practical figure-of-merit (perimeter-to-thickness) for a 30 mm × 30 mm cavity is:
$$
DR_{cavity} = \dfrac{(2 \times 30) + (2 \times 30) + (4 \times 18)}{0.30} = \dfrac{192}{0.30} \approx 640
$$

> In practice, vacuum forming supports **cavity depths up to ~3× the minor dimension** for HIPS at 0.30 mm; since 18 mm < 30 mm minor dimension, the part is **comfortably within vacuum-forming range**.

**Decision — Use Vacuum Forming with selective plug-assist:**

- Vacuum forming is the **cheapest** and sufficient for this shallow geometry.
- Add a **plug-assist** to redistribute material toward the corners, reducing *thinning* to below 15%.
- Predicted corner thickness using Eq. (4): $t_{f,corner} \approx 0.30 \times (0.85) = 0.255$ mm, still above pinhole threshold.

**Conclusion:** A **plug-assisted vacuum forming** is recommended, balancing tooling cost and quality.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Definition:** Thermoforming = heat + form + cool + trim (the **H-F-C-T mantra**).
- [ ] **Vacuum vs. Pressure vs. Plug-Assist:** vacuum = cheapest, pressure = sharpest detail, plug = deepest draw.
- [ ] **Draw ratio (Eq. 5):** $DR = h / t_{0}$; keep below **1.5** for simple vacuum, up to **3.0** with plug-assist.
- [ ] **Mass-conservation (Eq. 4):** $t_{f} = t_{0} \cdot (A_{0} / A_{f})$ — always state *units* in mm or m.
- [ ] **Sag control (Eq. 1):** $\delta = \rho g L^{4} / (32 E t^{2})$ — thicker sheets, shorter spans, stiffer resins (PS, PP) sag less.
- [ ] **Cooling time (Eq. 7):** $t_{cool} = s^{2} / (4 \alpha)$ — drives cycle time; lower for high-$\alpha$ materials like aluminum-mold-cooled PS.
- [ ] **Trim scrap = 25 – 50%** of web; re-extrude back into the sheet line (**closed-loop**).
- [ ] **Common resins:** PS (cups), PET (trays), PP (microwavable tubs), PVC (blister packs), EVOH multilayer (oxygen-barrier).
- [ ] **Defect-cheat-sheet:** Webbing → vacuum too low; Chill marks → mold too cold; Sag → span too long; Pinholes → DR too high; Warpage → eject above HDT.
- [ ] **Sustainability:** Thermoforming's **trim-scrap recyclability** and **low tool cost** make it favoured under EU SUP and India EPR 2022 rules.
- [ ] **Industry players to remember for KTU viva:** Huhtamaki, Sealed Air, Pactiv, Constantia Flexibles, UFlex, Manjushree, EPL.
- [ ] **Standard form-fill-seal (FFS) cycle speed:** 30 – 50 cycles/min on modern dairy lines.
- [ ] **Quick mnemonic for defect diagnosis:** **"W-C-S-P-O-W"** = Webbing, Chill, Sag, Pinholes, Orange peel, Warp.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moulded and Rigid Packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Moulded and Rigid Packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Moulded and Rigid Packages** refer to plastic packaging containers and components that retain a definite, self-supporting shape under normal handling, stacking, and thermal conditions, and are produced primarily through shaping processes where a molten, softened, or reactive polymer mass is forced into a precisely machined cavity (mould) to solidify into the desired geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rigid Packaging (KTU Definition):** Packaging structures whose flexural modulus exceeds **\(10^3 \text{ MPa}\)** at ambient temperature and whose deformation under a standard load of **\(9.8 \text{ N}\)** is less than **1%** of the original dimension.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, these packages are categorized by the **forming process** (injection, blow, thermoforming, rotational, compression) and by the **end-use geometry** (bottles, tubs, crates, caps, trays, blisters).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Baked Clay Pot" Analogy**
> Imagine you are a potter shaping wet clay. You press the clay into a hollow stone mould (the cavity), the clay holds the shape of the inside of the mould, and once it dries/hardens, you have a rigid vessel. **Moulded plastic packaging is the same concept** — except instead of clay and air drying, we use **molten thermoplastic pellets** and **rapid cooling** in a steel or aluminium mould.
> - **Flexible package** = like a balloon (deflates, returns to shape)
> - **Rigid package** = like a coconut shell (holds shape permanently)
> - **Moulded package** = like an ice cube from a tray (takes the exact shape of its mould cavity)

## 1.3 Classification Overview

The KTU syllabus classifies moulded and rigid packages into **four primary technology families**, each producing a distinct geometry and serving a specific market:

| Technology Family | Typical Product Geometry | Representative Material |
|---|---|---|
| Injection Moulding | Crates, tubs, caps, closures, thin-wall containers | **PP, HDPE, PS** |
| Blow Moulding | Bottles, jerry cans, drums, fuel tanks | **HDPE, PET, PP** |
| Thermoforming | Trays, blister packs, clamshells, lids, cups | **PS, PET, PVC, PP** |
| Rotational Moulding | Large tanks, kayaks, pallets, drums | **LLDPE, HDPE, XLPE** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Rigid" matters in KTU examinations:** Rigid packages provide **mechanical protection**, **dimensional stability for stacking**, **barrier integrity**, and **premium shelf-appeal** — functions that flexible pouches and films cannot perform.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Standard moulding pressure (Injection): **\(70\text{–}140 \text{ MPa}\)**
- Standard moulding temperature: **\(180\text{–}280 \text{ °C}\)**
- Typical wall thickness range: **\(0.3 \text{ mm}\) to \(15 \text{ mm}\)**
- Demoulding shrinkage allowance: **\(0.3\%\text{–}3.0\%\)** (material dependent)
- ASTM D638 tensile modulus threshold for "rigid" classification: **\(>700 \text{ MPa}\)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Injection Moulded Rigid Packages

### 2.1.1 Process Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **Feeding:** Plastic granules (pellets) are fed from the hopper into the heated barrel via a rotating screw.
2. **Plasticization:** The screw rotates, converting mechanical energy into frictional heat while external band heaters raise the polymer above its melting point.
3. **Injection:** The screw acts as a plunger, forcing the melt through the nozzle into a closed mould cavity at high pressure.
4. **Cooling & Solidification:** Heat is extracted through water-cooled mould channels; polymer crystallizes (semi-crystalline) or freezes (amorphous).
5. **Ejection:** The mould opens; ejector pins push the solidified part out.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why injection moulding dominates rigid packaging:** It offers **±0.05 mm dimensional accuracy**, **cycle times under 10 seconds** for thin-wall parts, and the ability to mould **complex 3D features** (threads, snap-fits, hinges) in a single operation.

### 2.1.2 Why & How — Engineering Logic

- **Clamping force** is required to keep the mould closed against injection pressure. Insufficient clamping causes flash (excess material) — a frequent KTU short-answer topic.
- **Shrinkage** is a function of **crystallinity, mould temperature, and holding pressure**. PP shrinks more than PS because of higher crystalline content.

## 2.2 Blow Moulded Rigid Packages

### 2.2.1 Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)

1. A molten **parison** (a hollow tube of polymer) is extruded vertically.
2. The parison is captured between two halves of a mould.
3. Compressed air (typically **\(0.5\text{–}1.0 \text{ MPa}\)**) is injected through a blow pin, forcing the parison to expand against the cooled mould walls.
4. The part cools, the mould opens, and the container is ejected with **flash** (excess material at the mould parting line) which is trimmed.

### 2.2.2 Injection Blow Moulding (IBM) & Stretch Blow Moulding (SBM)

- **IBM** produces a **preform** by injection moulding, then re-heats and blows it. Used for high-precision pharmaceutical and cosmetic bottles.
- **SBM** is the standard for **PET bottles** (water, soft drinks). The preform is stretched axially by a stretch rod AND radially by air, inducing **biaxial orientation** that dramatically improves clarity, strength, and barrier properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Biaxial orientation of PET increases tensile strength by **\(2\times\) to \(4\times\)** and reduces oxygen permeability by **\(30\%\text{–}50\%\)** compared to unoriented PET.

## 2.3 Thermoformed Rigid Packages

### 2.3.1 Process Logic

1. A plastic **sheet** (typically \(0.2\text{–}2.0 \text{ mm}\) thick) is clamped in a frame.
2. The sheet is heated above its **glass transition temperature (Tg)** for amorphous polymers or near the **melting point** for semi-crystalline polymers.
3. **Vacuum**, **pressure**, or **mechanical plug-assist** forces the softened sheet into a mould cavity.
4. The part cools against the mould, is trimmed, and the scrap web is reground.

### 2.3.2 Why & How — Engineering Logic

- Thermoforming produces **thin-wall rigid packages** with **low tooling cost** but **higher per-part cost** than injection moulding for large volumes.
- **Plug-assist** thermoforming controls material distribution in deep-draw parts (e.g., disposable cups, blister packs).
- **Wall thickness distribution** follows the rule: *thicker at the base, thinner at the corners*. This is a frequent KTU numerical/analytical question.

## 2.4 Rotational Moulded Rigid Packages

- A measured charge of **powder or liquid polymer** is placed in a closed mould.
- The mould is heated while being rotated **biaxially** (two perpendicular axes).
- The polymer melts and coats the inside surface uniformly.
- The mould is cooled, opened, and the hollow part is removed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why rotational moulding for large rigid packages:** Zero internal stress, uniform wall thickness, no weld lines, and the ability to mould **parts up to 5 m diameter**. Limitation: long cycle times (20–60 minutes).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Relation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shot Weight** | \(W = V_{\text{cavity}} \times \rho_{\text{melt}} \times \text{loss factor}\) | g | Injection moulding charge calculation |
| **Clamping Force** | \(F_c = P_{\text{inj}} \times A_{\text{projection}}\) | N (or kN) | Machine selection |
| **Injection Pressure** | \(P_{\text{inj}} = \dfrac{4 F_c}{\pi D^2}\) | MPa | Hydraulic pressure check |
| **Volumetric Shrinkage** | \(S_v = \dfrac{V_{\text{mould}} - V_{\text{part}}}{V_{\text{mould}}} \times 100\) | % | Mould compensation |
| **Linear Shrinkage** | \(S_L \approx \dfrac{S_v}{3}\) | % | Dimension allowance |
| **Cooling Time (est.)** | \(t_c = \dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w}\right)\) | s | Mould cycle design |
| **Biaxial Stretch Ratio (PET)** | \(\lambda = \dfrac{L_{\text{stretched}}}{L_{\text{preform}}}\) | dimensionless | SBM process control |
| **Wall Thickness (Blow)** | \(t = \dfrac{W}{\rho \times A_{\text{mould}}}\) | mm | Parison programming |
| **Heat Transfer (Fourier)** | \(Q = \dfrac{k A \Delta T}{s}\) | W | Mould cooling channel design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notational conventions:** \(V_{\text{cavity}}\) = mould cavity volume, \(\rho_{\text{melt}}\) = melt density, \(s\) = wall thickness, \(\alpha\) = thermal diffusivity, \(T_m\) = melt temperature, \(T_w\) = wall (mould) temperature, \(T_e\) = ejection temperature.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food & Beverage:** PET bottles (SBM), PP tubs (injection), HDPE jerry cans (EBM).
- **Pharmaceutical:** Injection moulded caps with tamper-evidence, PVC/Aclar blister packs.
- **Industrial Chemicals:** Blow moulded 20 L jerry cans, rotational moulded 200 L drums.
- **Consumer Durables:** PP crates for bottles, HDPE pallets, PS yoghurt cups (thermoformed).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric to Linear Shrinkage Relationship

This is a frequently tested **7-mark derivation** in KTU ESE Module 4 questions.

### Problem Setup
A polypropylene injection moulded tub has a mould cavity volume of **\(500 \text{ cm}^3\)**. After moulding and 24-hour conditioning at \(23 \text{ °C}\), the part volume is measured as **\(488 \text{ cm}^3\)**. Determine the volumetric shrinkage and estimate the linear shrinkage for mould compensation.

### Step 1: Write the defining equation

$$S_v = \dfrac{V_{\text{mould}} - V_{\text{part}}}{V_{\text{mould}}} \times 100$$

### Step 2: Substitute numerical values

$$S_v = \dfrac{500 - 488}{500} \times 100$$

### Step 3: Compute the numerator

$$S_v = \dfrac{12}{500} \times 100$$

### Step 4: Final evaluation

$$S_v = 0.024 \times 100 = 2.4\%$$

### Step 5: Convert to linear shrinkage (isotropic assumption)

Assuming the polymer shrinks equally in all three Cartesian directions (isotropic), the volumetric strain is the sum of three linear strains:

$$\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

For isotropic shrinkage, \(\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = S_L\):

$$\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = 3 S_L$$

Therefore:

$$S_L = \dfrac{S_v}{3}$$

### Step 6: Numerical substitution

$$S_L = \dfrac{2.4\%}{3} = 0.8\%$$

### Step 7: Mould cavity compensation

The mould must be over-sized by **\(0.8\%\)** in every linear dimension. For a cavity length of \(L_{\text{part}} = 100 \text{ mm}\):

$$L_{\text{mould}} = L_{\text{part}} (1 + S_L) = 100 \times (1 + 0.008)$$

$$L_{\text{mould}} = 100.8 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic check:** A 2.4% volume change on an isotropic solid corresponds to roughly 0.8% per dimension. The cube root approximation \(S_L \approx \sqrt[3]{1+S_v} - 1\) gives \(S_L = 0.795\%\), confirming the linear approximation is valid for small shrinkages.

## 3.2 Derivation: Clamping Force Requirement

### Problem Setup
An injection moulding machine is to produce **\(120 \times 120 \text{ mm}\)** PP tubs in a 4-cavity mould. The required injection pressure is **\(80 \text{ MPa}\)**. Calculate the minimum clamping force required.

### Step 1: Compute the projected area of one cavity

$$A_1 = 120 \times 120 = 14{,}400 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 2: Multiply by number of cavities

$$A_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 14{,}400 = 57{,}600 \text{ mm}^2 = 5.76 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 3: Apply the clamping force equation

$$F_c = P_{\text{inj}} \times A_{\text{projection}}$$

$$F_c = 80 \text{ MPa} \times 5.76 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 4: Unit consistency

$$1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$$

$$F_c = 80 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 \times 5.76 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 5: Final numerical result

$$F_c = 4.608 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 4608 \text{ kN} \approx 470 \text{ tonnes-force}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Process Selection for Rigid Packaging

| Selection Criterion | Injection | Blow (EBM) | Blow (SBM) | Thermoforming | Rotational |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hollow geometry** | ✗ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓✓ |
| **Tight tolerances** | ✓✓✓ | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Tooling cost** | High | Medium | High | Low | Medium |
| **Cycle time** | Fast (5–30 s) | Medium (15–60 s) | Fast (4–8 s) | Fast (3–15 s) | Slow (20–60 min) |
| **Min. wall thickness** | 0.3 mm | 0.5 mm | 0.25 mm | 0.2 mm | 2.0 mm |
| **Max. part volume** | ~10 L | ~2000 L | ~5 L | ~10 L | ~10,000 L |
| **Best material fit** | PP, PS, ABS | HDPE, PP | PET | PS, PET, PVC | LLDPE, XLPE |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legend:** ✓✓✓ = highly suitable, ✓ = suitable, ✗ = not suitable.

## 3.4 Numerical Example: PET Preform Biaxial Stretch Ratio

### Given
A PET preform has length **\(L_0 = 100 \text{ mm}\)** and diameter **\(D_0 = 25 \text{ mm}\)**. After stretch blow moulding, the bottle has length **\(L_f = 250 \text{ mm}\)** and diameter **\(D_f = 70 \text{ mm}\)**.

### Step 1: Axial (longitudinal) stretch ratio

$$\lambda_a = \dfrac{L_f}{L_0} = \dfrac{250}{100} = 2.5$$

### Step 2: Hoop (transverse) stretch ratio

$$\lambda_h = \dfrac{D_f}{D_0} = \dfrac{70}{25} = 2.8$$

### Step 3: Overall areal stretch ratio

$$\lambda_{\text{areal}} = \lambda_a \times \lambda_h = 2.5 \times 2.8 = 7.0$$

### Step 4: Resultant wall-thinning ratio

For a preform with wall thickness \(t_0 = 4 \text{ mm}\), assuming constant volume:

$$t_f = \dfrac{t_0}{\lambda_{\text{areal}}} = \dfrac{4}{7.0} \approx 0.57 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A combined areal stretch of 7× is optimal for PET — giving **maximum strain-induced crystallization** and the best balance of clarity, strength, and barrier. Excessive stretch causes **haze** (stress-whitening), while insufficient stretch leaves the bottle weak and cloudy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: Moulded Rigid Package Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Resin Selection] --> B[Material Drying and Conditioning]
    B --> C{Process Selection}
    C -->|Crates, Caps, Tubs| D1[Injection Moulding]
    C -->|Bottles, Drums, Cans| D2[Blow Moulding]
    C -->|Trays, Blisters, Cups| D3[Thermoforming]
    C -->|Large Tanks, Pallets| D4[Rotational Moulding]
    D1 --> E1[Plasticization and Injection]
    D2 --> E2[Parison Extrusion or Preform]
    D3 --> E3[Sheet Heating and Drape]
    D4 --> E4[Biaxial Rotation and Cure]
    E1 --> F[Mould Cavity Filling]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Cooling and Solidification]
    G --> H[Ejection and Trimming]
    H --> I[Quality Inspection]
    I --> J[Decoration and Labelling]
    J --> K[Finished Rigid Package]
    K --> L[Distribution and End Use]
```

## 4.2 Process Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IM[Injection Moulding]
        IM1[Granules] --> IM2[Heated Barrel]
        IM2 --> IM3[Screw Plasticization]
        IM3 --> IM4[High Pressure Injection]
        IM4 --> IM5[Closed Mould Cavity]
        IM5 --> IM6[Cooling and Ejection]
    end
    subgraph BM[Blow Moulding]
        BM1[Resin] --> BM2[Extruder or Preform]
        BM2 --> BM3[Parison or Preform]
        BM3 --> BM4[Mould Clamp]
        BM4 --> BM5[Air Inflation]
        BM5 --> BM6[Cooling and Deflash]
    end
    subgraph TF[Thermoforming]
        TF1[Sheet Extrusion] --> TF2[Sheet Roll]
        TF2 --> TF3[Heating to Tg]
        TF3 --> TF4[Vacuum or Pressure Forming]
        TF4 --> TF5[Cooling and Trimming]
    end
    subgraph RM[Rotational Moulding]
        RM1[Powder Charge] --> RM2[Closed Mould]
        RM2 --> RM3[Biaxial Rotation]
        RM3 --> RM4[Heated Oven]
        RM4 --> RM5[Cooling Chamber]
        RM5 --> RM6[Demoulding]
    end
    IM6 --> P[Finished Rigid Plastic Package]
    BM6 --> P
    TF5 --> P
    RM6 --> P
```

## 4.3 Wall Thickness Distribution in Blow Moulded Bottles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Blow Moulded Bottle Cross-Section] --> B[Shoulder Region]
    A --> C[Body Region]
    A --> D[Base Region]
    B --> B1[Thinnest area: 0.25 to 0.40 mm]
    C --> C1[Uniform thickness: 0.35 to 0.50 mm]
    D --> D1[Thickest area: 0.60 to 1.00 mm]
    C1 --> E[Parison programming adjusts thickness]
    B1 --> E
    D1 --> E
    E --> F[Engineering Trade-off: Material distribution vs. Mechanical strength]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree for Rigid Packaging Material Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Required Rigid Package] --> B{Need Transparency?}
    B -->|Yes| C{Need High Barrier?}
    B -->|No| D{Need Impact Resistance?}
    C -->|Yes| E[PET or PVC]
    C -->|No| F[PS or SAN]
    D -->|Yes| G[HDPE or PP]
    D -->|No| H{Need Heat Resistance?}
    H -->|Yes| I[PP or PC]
    H -->|No| J[PS or PVC]
    E --> K[Final Material Selection]
    F --> K
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** These diagrams map the *cause-effect relationships* of process variables (temperature, pressure, cooling) to the *final rigid package attributes* (wall thickness, clarity, strength). KTU examiners frequently award marks for correctly labelled flow diagrams in the 14-mark analytical questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define rigid packaging. List four major moulding processes used to produce rigid plastic packages.**

**Model Answer:**
Rigid packaging refers to plastic packaging structures that retain their shape under mechanical and thermal stress during handling, storage, and transport, with a flexural modulus generally exceeding **\(700 \text{ MPa}\)**.

The four major moulding processes are:
1. **Injection Moulding** – for crates, tubs, caps.
2. **Blow Moulding** – for bottles and jerry cans.
3. **Thermoforming** – for trays, blister packs.
4. **Rotational Moulding** – for large hollow containers.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Writing only "injection" and "blow" without thermoforming/rotational moulding loses 1 mark. Examiners expect **at least four** correctly named processes.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the significance of biaxial orientation in PET bottles.**

**Model Answer:**
Biaxial orientation in PET is achieved through **stretch blow moulding (SBM)**, where the preform is simultaneously stretched **axially** (by a stretch rod) and **radially** (by compressed air).

Significance:
- **Improves tensile strength** by \(2\times\) to \(4\times\).
- **Enhances clarity** by strain-induced crystallization.
- **Reduces oxygen and CO₂ permeability** by \(30\%\)–\(50\%\).
- **Enables down-gauging** — thinner walls, less material, lower cost.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **injection moulding process** for producing rigid plastic packaging. Discuss the role of clamping force, holding pressure, and cooling time in determining part quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polypropylene tub is injection moulded with a mould cavity of volume **\(320 \text{ cm}^3\)**. The part volume after 24 hours is **\(312 \text{ cm}^3\)**. Calculate the volumetric and linear shrinkage. If the finished tub length is **\(150 \text{ mm}\)**, what should be the corresponding mould dimension? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**(a) Injection Moulding Process:**

**Schematic Explanation (refer to Section 4.2 diagram IM block):**
The injection moulding machine has two principal units: the **injection unit** and the **clamping unit**.

**Step 1 — Plasticization:** Polymer granules from the hopper enter the heated barrel where the rotating screw melts and homogenizes the polymer through a combination of conduction and shear heating.

**Step 2 — Injection:** The screw acts as a plunger, injecting the melt through the nozzle into the closed mould cavity at pressures of **\(70\text{–}140 \text{ MPa}\)**.

**Step 3 — Packing/Holding:** After the cavity is filled, additional material is forced in at **holding pressure** to compensate for volumetric shrinkage during cooling. This prevents sink marks and voids.

**Step 4 — Cooling:** Water or oil circulating through mould channels extracts heat. Cooling time is governed by the **Fourier heat conduction** equation and depends on wall thickness \(s\) and thermal diffusivity \(\alpha\).

**Step 5 — Ejection:** The mould opens; ejector pins push the solidified part out.

**Role of Critical Parameters:**

| Parameter | Role | Consequence of Error |
|---|---|---|
| **Clamping Force \(F_c\)** | Keeps mould halves closed against melt pressure | Insufficient \(F_c\) → flash; excessive → mould damage |
| **Holding Pressure \(P_h\)** | Compensates shrinkage during solidification | Low \(P_h\) → sink marks, voids |
| **Cooling Time \(t_c\)** | Determines cycle time, crystallinity | Short \(t_c\) → warpage; long → low productivity |

**(b) Shrinkage Calculation:**

**Step 1: Volumetric Shrinkage**

$$S_v = \dfrac{V_{\text{mould}} - V_{\text{part}}}{V_{\text{mould}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{320 - 312}{320} \times 100$$

$$S_v = \dfrac{8}{320} \times 100 = 2.5\%$$

**Step 2: Linear Shrinkage**

$$S_L = \dfrac{S_v}{3} = \dfrac{2.5\%}{3} = 0.833\%$$

**Step 3: Mould Dimension for \(L_{\text{part}} = 150 \text{ mm}\)**

$$L_{\text{mould}} = L_{\text{part}} \times (1 + S_L) = 150 \times 1.00833$$

$$L_{\text{mould}} = 150 + 1.25 = 151.25 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Examiner's Note):** Many students forget to multiply the final answer by 3 in the linear shrinkage conversion, or apply the mould compensation to the volume instead of the linear dimension. **Always apply linear shrinkage to linear dimensions** — mould drafters work in mm, not cm³.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Compare **extrusion blow moulding (EBM)** and **stretch blow moulding (SBM)** with respect to process steps, materials, product properties, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An HDPE jerry can is produced by extrusion blow moulding. The parison has a mass of **\(850 \text{ g}\)** and a melt density of **\(0.74 \text{ g/cm}^3\)**. The finished can has a wall thickness of **\(1.2 \text{ mm}\)** and an internal surface area of **\(4200 \text{ cm}^2\)**. Calculate the **flash fraction** (mass of flash as a percentage of total parison mass) if the actual can mass is **\(780 \text{ g}\)**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM) | Stretch Blow Moulding (SBM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting form** | Continuous extruded parison | Injection-moulded preform |
| **Process steps** | Extrude → Clamp → Blow → Cool → Eject | Inject preform → Reheat → Stretch + Blow → Eject |
| **Stretching** | None (free inflation) | Biaxial (axial rod + radial air) |
| **Materials** | HDPE, PP, PVC | PET, PP, PEN |
| **Molecular orientation** | Minimal | High biaxial orientation |
| **Clarity** | Usually opaque/translucent | Glass-clear (PET) |
| **Strength** | Moderate | High (\(2\times\)–\(4\times\)) |
| **Barrier (O₂, CO₂)** | Moderate | Excellent for PET |
| **Applications** | Detergent bottles, jerry cans, drums | Soft drinks, water, edible oil |
| **Tooling cost** | Lower | Higher (two-stage preform + blow mould) |
| **Cycle time** | 15–60 s | 4–8 s (rotary SBM) |

**(b) Flash Fraction Calculation:**

**Step 1: Theoretical can mass (no flash) from parison volume distributed as wall**

The wall volume equals wall thickness × internal area:

$$V_{\text{wall}} = t \times A = 1.2 \text{ mm} \times 4200 \text{ cm}^2$$

Converting units: \(1.2 \text{ mm} = 0.12 \text{ cm}\)

$$V_{\text{wall}} = 0.12 \times 4200 = 504 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 2: Theoretical mass of can (no flash)**

$$m_{\text{theoretical}} = V_{\text{wall}} \times \rho_{\text{melt}} = 504 \times 0.74$$

$$m_{\text{theoretical}} = 372.96 \text{ g} \approx 373 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3: Flash mass**

$$m_{\text{flash}} = m_{\text{parison}} - m_{\text{actual}} = 850 - 780 = 70 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4: Flash fraction (percentage of parison mass)**

$$\% \text{Flash} = \dfrac{m_{\text{flash}}}{m_{\text{parison}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{70}{850} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{Flash} = 8.235\%$$

**Step 5: Verification**

Theoretical can mass (\(373 \text{ g}\)) is much less than the actual can mass (\(780 \text{ g}\)). This indicates the part is thicker than the minimum design wall, but the difference between **actual** (\(780 \text{ g}\)) and **parison** (\(850 \text{ g}\)) represents the **flash that was trimmed away** — yielding an **8.24%** flash fraction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse flash fraction with over-thickness fraction. Flash fraction is **parison minus actual part**; over-thickness is **actual minus theoretical minimum**. Markers deduct 2 marks if these are interchanged.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Moulded and Rigid Packages**

- **Rigid packaging** is defined by flexural modulus **\(>700 \text{ MPa}\)** and shape retention under load.
- **Four primary moulding processes:** Injection, Blow (EBM/SBM/IBM), Thermoforming, Rotational.
- **Injection moulding** suits **solid, dimensionally critical** parts — caps, crates, tubs, closures.
- **Blow moulding** produces **hollow** parts — bottles, jerry cans, drums.
- **SBM (PET)** imparts **biaxial orientation**, multiplying strength by **\(2\times\)–\(4\times\)** and reducing barrier loss by **30%–50%**.
- **Thermoforming** uses pre-extruded sheets and is best for **thin-wall trays, blisters, cups**.
- **Rotational moulding** produces **large, stress-free hollow** items — tanks, pallets, kayaks.
- **Volumetric shrinkage** = \((V_{\text{mould}} - V_{\text{part}})/V_{\text{mould}}\); **linear shrinkage** ≈ volumetric/3.
- **Mould dimension** = part dimension × \((1 + S_L)\).
- **Clamping force** = injection pressure × projected area.
- **Cooling time** depends on wall thickness squared (Fourier conduction).
- **Wall thickness distribution** in blow moulding: base > body > shoulder; controlled by **parison programming**.
- **PET** is the dominant material for clear rigid bottles; **HDPE** for opaque chemical containers; **PP** for hot-fill and microwavable containers; **PS** for disposable cups.
- **ASTM D638** is the reference standard for tensile properties used in rigid classification.
- **For KTU 14-mark questions:** always (1) draw a labelled flow diagram, (2) tabulate process parameters, (3) show **all** intermediate calculation steps with units.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermoforming Packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thermoforming Packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermoforming** is a secondary plastics processing technique in which a flat thermoplastic sheet (extruded, cast, or calendered) is heated to a pliable, rubbery state above its **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** for amorphous polymers (e.g., PET, PS, PVC) or above its **melting temperature ($T_m$)** for semi-crystalline polymers (e.g., PP, HDPE), forced against a cooled mold by vacuum, pressure, or mechanical means to assume the mold's geometry, and subsequently cooled, ejected, and trimmed to yield a finished packaging article.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the focus of *Thermoforming Packages* is restricted to **packaging applications** such as:
- **Blister packs** (for tablets, capsules, small hardware)
- **Clamshell enclosures** (for consumer goods, electronics)
- **Barrier trays** (for MAP — Modified Atmosphere Packaging of meat, cheese, ready-meals)
- **Insert trays** (for chocolates, biscuits, cosmetics)
- **Disposable cups, tubs, and lids** (dairy, ice-cream, FMCG)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Play-Doh Stamp"**
> Imagine you take a flat, thin slab of warm Play-Doh and press it down over a small plastic toy (the mold). Air is pushed out from under the slab (or sucked away), the slab drapes itself exactly over the toy's shape, and once it cools, you peel it off. You now have a perfect, hollow plastic "shell" of that toy. **That is thermoforming in a nutshell.** The "slab" is the heated plastic sheet, the "toy" is the mold, and the "air being pushed/sucked" is either vacuum or compressed air. The formed shell is your **package**.

A more industry-relevant analogy is **vacuum-cling food wrap over a bowl**: the wrap softens, conforms to the bowl's contour due to pressure differential, and retains the shape once cooled. Industrial thermoforming is simply a controlled, repeatable, and high-precision version of this everyday phenomenon.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics Used in Thermoforming

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Process Windows (Bold Standard Metrics):**
> - **Heater-to-sheet stand-off distance: 50 – 150 mm** (typical industrial range).
> - **Sheet surface temperature during forming: 130 °C – 200 °C** depending on polymer.
> - **Forming pressure: 0.05 MPa – 0.10 MPa** for vacuum; **up to 1.0 – 2.5 MPa** for high-pressure forming.
> - **Mold temperature: 40 °C – 90 °C** (cooled below the polymer's heat-distortion temperature).
> - **Draw ratio (depth : width): typically 0.5 : 1 to 1 : 1** for packaging; >1:1 requires plug-assist.
> - **Cycle time: 2 – 12 seconds** for thin-gauge (<0.75 mm) packaging, 30 – 90 s for heavy-gauge.

## 1.4 Why Thermoforming Dominates Plastics Packaging

> [!NOTE]
> - **Low tooling cost** (aluminum or epoxy molds vs. injection-mold steel) — typically **5–10 % the cost of an injection mold** of equivalent footprint.
> - **Fast turnaround** — prototype tooling in **1–2 weeks** vs. 6–12 weeks for injection.
> - **Material efficiency** for large, thin-walled, shallow-draw packaging (cups, lids, trays).
> - **Multi-layer sheet compatibility** — EVOH/PE barrier sheets can be thermoformed (injection requires co-injection complexity).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Operational Phases of Thermoforming

1. **Clamping & Loading:** A thermoplastic sheet (roll-fed for thin-gauge, cut-sheet for heavy-gauge) is clamped in a frame. The clamp prevents the sheet from shrinking during heating.
2. **Heating:** Radiant (IR ceramic/quartz) or convective heaters raise the sheet uniformly to the forming temperature. Temperature uniformity is critical — typically within **± 5 °C** across the sheet to avoid sag, blistering, or uneven thinning.
3. **Forming (the "active" step):** The softened sheet is forced against the mold by one of four mechanisms:
   - **Vacuum forming (negative pressure, 0.05 – 0.09 MPa)**
   - **Pressure forming (positive compressed air, up to 2.5 MPa)**
   - **Mechanical matched-die forming**
   - **Twin-sheet forming (two sheets, two molds)**
4. **Cooling & Solidification:** The formed article remains on the mold until it cools below the heat-distortion temperature (HDT). Internal mold cooling channels or forced air jets are used.
5. **Trimming:** The formed sheet is separated from the web. Methods: steel-rule die cutting, router (heavy-gauge), or in-line punch & die for inline (roll-fed) systems.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Phase

> [!NOTE]
> - **Why heat above $T_g$ / $T_m$?** Polymer chains must acquire segmental mobility — the *rubbery plateau* on the modulus-temperature curve — to deform without fracture.
> - **Why cool on-mold?** Cooling while in contact with the mold surface freezes in the geometry; removal before cooling causes warpage/springback.
> - **Why trim?** The unformed flange (sheet edge) and any holes (e.g., lid features) must be removed to deliver a finished package.

## 2.3 Classification of Thermoforming Techniques (Packaging-Relevant)

| **Technique** | **Driving Force** | **Typical Pressure** | **Packaging Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Drape forming** | Gravity + vacuum | 0.05 – 0.08 MPa | Display trays, chocolate boxes |
| **Vacuum forming (straight)** | Vacuum only | 0.05 – 0.09 MPa | Blister packs, simple trays |
| **Drape-vacuum (snap-back)** | Vacuum + mechanical plug | 0.05 – 0.09 MPa | Deep-draw tubs (yogurt) |
| **Plug-assist vacuum** | Mechanical plug + vacuum | 0.05 – 0.09 MPa | Cups, dairy containers |
| **Pressure forming** | Compressed air | 0.3 – 2.5 MPa | High-detail clamshells |
| **Twin-sheet forming** | Two molds + air pressure | 0.2 – 0.8 MPa | Hollow pallets, large enclosures |
| **Mechanical matched-die** | Convex punch | Contact pressure | Heavy-gauge dunnage trays |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Thermoforming Calculations (Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute value to preserve markdown table syntax.)**

| **Equation No.** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_f = t_0 \cdot \dfrac{A_0}{A_f}$ | Wall thickness (mass conservation) | mm |
| 2 | $DR = \dfrac{h}{w}$ | Draw ratio (depth ÷ width) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $AR = \dfrac{A_f}{A_0}$ | Area ratio (surface expansion) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $t_f = \dfrac{t_0}{AR}$ | Re-arranged form of Eq. 1 | mm |
| 5 | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{cavity}$ | Pressure differential driving forming | MPa |
| 6 | $F_{form} = \Delta P \cdot A_{sheet}$ | Total forming force | N |
| 7 | $q = k \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{x}$ | Heat flux through sheet (Fourier) | W·m$^{-2}$ |
| 8 | $t_{cycle} = t_{heat} + t_{form} + t_{cool} + t_{trim}$ | Total cycle time | s |
| 9 | $Y_{mat} = \dfrac{m_{tray}}{m_{sheet,total}}$ | Material yield (1 − scrap fraction) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $t_{cool} \propto \dfrac{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot x^2}{k}$ | Approx. cooling time (1-D conduction) | s |

**Boundary Conditions & Valid Ranges:**
- Eq. 1 assumes *incompressible melt, no material loss, uniform deformation.* Valid when $t_0 < 3$ mm and $AR < 2.5$.
- Eq. 2: For packaging, $DR$ rarely exceeds **0.75:1** without plug-assist.
- Eq. 6: $\Delta P$ must exceed the polymer's *melt strength* at forming temperature; otherwise sheet tears.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Wall-thickness control (Eq. 1)** dictates material cost: a chocolate tray with 30 % thinning (AR = 1.43) means the user has already paid for "extra" 0.5 mm of polymer that the customer never sees.
> - **Draw ratio (Eq. 2)** drives mold design — a 1:1 draw ratio requires plug-assist to distribute material into deep corners.
> - **Cooling time (Eq. 10)** is the *rate-limiting step* in 70 % of thermoforming cycles — design of mold cooling channels directly drives factory throughput.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wall-Thickness Distribution (Conservation of Mass)

This is the single most important derivation in thermoforming; examiners almost always ask for it.

### 3.1.1 Governing Assumption

> The polymer sheet behaves as an **incompressible, Newtonian-like fluid** during forming. Volume is conserved (no voids, no thinning to zero).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Let:
- $t_0$ = initial uniform sheet thickness
- $\rho$ = polymer density (assumed constant)
- $A_0$ = surface area of the original flat sheet region that becomes the formed part
- $A_f$ = total surface area of the final formed part (mold cavity walls + bottom)
- $t_f$ = final average wall thickness

The volume of polymer in the original flat region:

$$V_0 = A_0 \cdot t_0$$

The volume of polymer in the formed part (after the sheet drapes over the mold):

$$V_f = A_f \cdot t_f$$

By conservation of mass (incompressibility → $V_0 = V_f$):

$$A_0 \cdot t_0 = A_f \cdot t_f$$

Solving for the final thickness:

$$t_f = t_0 \cdot \frac{A_0}{A_f}$$

In terms of the **Area Ratio** $AR = \dfrac{A_f}{A_0}$:

$$t_f = \frac{t_0}{AR}$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard)

**Problem:** A 1.0 mm thick PS sheet is thermoformed into a hemispherical chocolate-dome tray of diameter 120 mm. Calculate (a) the final average wall thickness, and (b) the thinnest-region thickness.

**Given:**
- $t_0 = 1.0$ mm
- Mold diameter $= 120$ mm → radius $r = 60$ mm

**Step 1 — Initial flat-sheet area (clamped region that will form the dome):**

The original flat region must supply material for the hemisphere. Conservatively, the original region is a flat disc of the same radius $r$:

$$A_0 = \pi r^2 = \pi \cdot (60)^2 = 3600\pi \approx 11309.7 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Final formed surface area (hemisphere, inner + outer):**

A hemisphere has surface area $2\pi r^2$. Treating it as a single-walled shell (one side):

$$A_f = 2\pi r^2 = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot (60)^2 = 7200\pi \approx 22619.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply the wall-thickness formula:**

$$\frac{t_f}{t_0} = \frac{A_0}{A_f} = \frac{3600\pi}{7200\pi} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$t_f = \frac{1.0 \text{ mm}}{2} = 0.50 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Practical interpretation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The chocolate-dome tray has only **50 % of its original thickness** after forming. In real molds, material is non-uniformly distributed — the *bottom* of the dome (apex) is thicker (~0.6 mm) and the *equator* is thinner (~0.4 mm). This is why a 1.0 mm starting sheet is used when a 0.4 mm minimum is required.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Forming Force (Vacuum vs. Pressure)

The total force driving the sheet onto the mold:

$$F_{form} = \Delta P \cdot A_{sheet}$$

For vacuum forming:

$$\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{cavity} \approx 0.10 \text{ MPa (full vacuum)}$$

For pressure forming with compressed air at 1.0 MPa (gauge):

$$\Delta P = P_{air,gauge} = 1.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Comparison — same 0.5 m × 0.5 m sheet:**

$$F_{vac} = 0.10 \times 10^6 \cdot (0.5 \cdot 0.5) = 25{,}000 \text{ N} = 25 \text{ kN}$$

$$F_{press} = 1.0 \times 10^6 \cdot (0.5 \cdot 0.5) = 250{,}000 \text{ N} = 250 \text{ kN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?** Pressure forming (250 kN vs. 25 kN) drives the sheet into sharp mold features, replicating fine textures, logos, and undercuts — which is why premium blister packs and clamshells use pressure forming instead of pure vacuum.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cooling Time on the Mold (Fourier 1-D Conduction)

Heat must flow from the hot sheet through the polymer thickness into the cooled mold. Newton's law of cooling (lumped-capacitance) gives:

$$\frac{T(t) - T_{mold}}{T_{sheet,initial} - T_{mold}} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{h_{eff} \cdot A}{m \cdot c_p} \cdot t\right)$$

For conduction-limited cooling through a polymer slab of thickness $x$, the half-cooling time is approximately:

$$t_{cool} \approx \frac{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot x^2}{4 k}$$

**Worked example — PET sheet, $x = 0.5$ mm, cooling from 150 °C to 60 °C (mold at 40 °C):**

- $\rho_{PET} = 1350$ kg·m$^{-3}$
- $c_{p,PET} = 1200$ J·kg$^{-1}$·K$^{-1}$
- $k_{PET} = 0.29$ W·m$^{-1}$·K$^{-1}$
- $x = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m

$$t_{cool} = \frac{1350 \cdot 1200 \cdot (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4 \cdot 0.29}$$

$$t_{cool} = \frac{1350 \cdot 1200 \cdot 0.25 \times 10^{-6}}{1.16}$$

$$t_{cool} = \frac{0.405}{1.16} \approx 0.349 \text{ s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Theoretical half-cooling is sub-second, but practical cycle time is dominated by **demolding safety margin** — the formed part must cool uniformly to avoid warpage. Real-world PET tray cycle: **2.5 – 4.0 seconds**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Polymer Selection for Packaging

| **Polymer** | **Forming Temp (°C)** | **$T_g$ (°C)** | **Key Packaging Property** | **Typical Package** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PVC (rigid)** | 120 – 150 | 80 | Clarity, low cost | Blister packs, clamshells |
| **PET (APET/CPET)** | 130 – 160 | 70 | Clarity, gas barrier | Trays, drink cups |
| **PS (general purpose)** | 130 – 160 | 100 | Stiffness, brittle | Yogurt cups, lids |
| **HIPS** | 140 – 170 | 100 | Impact resistance | Dairy tubs, egg trays |
| **PP** | 150 – 180 | −10 (crystalline) | Hot-fill, microwavable | Ready-meal trays, margarine tubs |
| **PE (HDPE/LDPE)** | 130 – 170 | −120 (crystalline) | Toughness, sealing | Liner films (not commonly formed) |
| **EVOH-multilayer** | 140 – 170 | 70 (co-polymer) | Oxygen barrier | MAP meat trays |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Roll-Fed Inline Thermoforming Line

This is the **dominant configuration** for thin-gauge packaging thermoforming (cups, lids, blister strips).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Roll Unwind Station] --> B[Sheet Indexing and Clamp Frame]
    B --> C[Top IR Heater Bank]
    C --> D[Bottom IR Heater Bank]
    D --> E[Forming Station Vacuum Pressure or Plug Assist]
    E --> F[Cooling Station Forced Air and Mold Channels]
    F --> G[In-line Trimming Station Steel Rule Die]
    G --> H[Stack and Count Station]
    H --> I[Scroll Re-wind of Skeleton Web]
    I --> J[Finished Package Output]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Thermoforming Process Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph HeatingSubsystem
        H1[IR Heater Array] --> H2[Pyrometer Feedback Loop]
        H2 --> H3[PLC Temperature Controller]
    end
    subgraph FormingSubsystem
        F1[Vacuum Pump] --> F2[Vacuum Reservoir]
        F3[Compressed Air Supply] --> F4[Pressure Regulator]
        F2 --> F5[Vacuum Valve Manifold]
        F4 --> F5
        F5 --> F6[Mold Cavity]
    end
    subgraph CoolingSubsystem
        C1[Chiller Unit] --> C2[Mold Cooling Channels]
        C2 --> F6
    end
    subgraph ControlSubsystem
        S1[Operator HMI] --> S2[PLC Master]
        S2 --> H3
        S2 --> F5
        S2 --> C1
    end
    H3 -.controls.-> H1
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Selecting a Thermoforming Technique for a Package

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Package Design] --> B{Part Depth lt 50 mm?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Detail lt 0.5 mm features?}
    B -- No --> D{Width gt 300 mm?}
    C -- No --> E[Vacuum Forming]
    C -- Yes --> F[Pressure Forming]
    D -- Yes --> G{Sharp Internal Corners?}
    D -- No --> H[Plug Assist Vacuum]
    G -- Yes --> I[Matched Die Mechanical]
    G -- No --> J[Twin Sheet Forming]
    E --> K[End: Choose Mold Material]
    F --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Mold Quantity}
    L -- lt 5000 --> M[Aluminum or Epoxy]
    L -- gt 50000 --> N[Steel Hard Tool]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Wall-Thickness Variation Across a Typical Formed Tray

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TrayCrossSection
        A1[Flange Region t = 1.0 mm original] --> A2[Sidewall Region t = 0.55 mm]
        A2 --> A3[Corner Radius Region t = 0.40 mm]
        A3 --> A4[Bottom Flat Region t = 0.75 mm]
        A4 --> A5[Center Boss Region t = 0.85 mm]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **corner radius** is universally the thinnest zone — this is where most thermoformed packages fail in drop tests, and is the single most-examined feature in KTU 14-mark problems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

### Question A1 (3 marks)
**Define thermoforming. List any four thermoplastic polymers commonly used in thermoformed packaging, with one example package for each.**

**Model Answer:**
Thermoforming is a plastics processing method in which a heated thermoplastic sheet is shaped over a mold using vacuum, pressure, or mechanical force, then cooled and trimmed.

| **Polymer** | **Example Package** |
|---|---|
| PVC | Tablet blister pack |
| PET (APET) | Berry punnet / drink cup |
| PS (GPPS) | Yogurt cup |
| PP | Ready-meal tray (microwavable) |
| HIPS | Dairy tub |

*(Any four correctly paired = 3 marks; only listing = 2 marks; definition missing = −1 mark.)*

### Question A2 (3 marks)
**Explain the "wall-thickness distribution" problem in thermoforming. State the governing equation and identify which region of a typical tray is thinnest.**

**Model Answer:**
Because the polymer sheet's volume is conserved, stretching it over a 3-D mold spreads the material over a larger area, reducing thickness. The governing equation is:

$$t_f = t_0 \cdot \frac{A_0}{A_f}$$

The **corner radius region** is the thinnest because the area expansion (AR) is maximum there due to biaxial stretching from two perpendicular surfaces meeting.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Module 4 Internal Choice Set]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — OR — Question B (14 Marks)**

---

#### **Question A: Vacuum Forming & Pressure Forming for Clamshell Packaging**

**Part (a) [7 marks]** — Compare vacuum forming and pressure forming with respect to: (i) pressure range, (ii) mold detail replication, (iii) cycle time, (iv) equipment cost. State two packaging applications for each.

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

| **Parameter** | **Vacuum Forming** | **Pressure Forming** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Pressure | 0.05 – 0.09 MPa (vacuum) | 0.3 – 2.5 MPa (compressed air) |
| (ii) Detail replication | Limited (rounded features only) | Excellent (sharp edges, fine textures, undercuts) |
| (iii) Cycle time | 3 – 8 s | 4 – 12 s (slightly longer due to sealing) |
| (iv) Equipment cost | Low (single vacuum pump) | High (booster, plenum, pressure vessel) |

**Applications — Vacuum Forming:**
1. Tablet blister packs (PVC/PVDC)
2. Chocolate insert trays (HIPS)

**Applications — Pressure Forming:**
1. Premium clamshell enclosures for consumer electronics
2. Tamper-evident blister cards with embossed logos

*(Each correct comparison row: 0.5 mark × 4 = 2 marks; applications: 1 mark × 4 = 4 marks; neat tabular format: 1 mark. Total = 7.)*

**Part (b) [7 marks]** — A flat HIPS sheet of thickness 1.2 mm is thermoformed over a cylindrical mold (cup-shape) of diameter 80 mm and depth 60 mm. Calculate:
(i) the original flat-sheet area supplying the cup wall + bottom,
(ii) the final formed surface area (open cup, no lid),
(iii) the final average wall thickness,
(iv) the **theoretical minimum thickness** if the cup is a thin-walled cylinder (comment on its feasibility).

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $t_0 = 1.2$ mm
- Diameter $d = 80$ mm → radius $r = 40$ mm
- Depth $h = 60$ mm

**Step 1 — Original flat area ($A_0$):**
The original region is a flat disc of radius $r$:

$$A_0 = \pi r^2 = \pi (40)^2 = 1600\pi \approx 5026.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Final formed area ($A_f$):**
- Bottom: $\pi r^2 = 1600\pi$
- Sidewall: $2\pi r h = 2 \pi \cdot 40 \cdot 60 = 4800\pi$

$$A_f = 1600\pi + 4800\pi = 6400\pi \approx 20106.2 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Final average wall thickness:**

$$t_f = t_0 \cdot \frac{A_0}{A_f} = 1.2 \cdot \frac{1600\pi}{6400\pi} = 1.2 \cdot \frac{1}{4} = 0.30 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical minimum thickness (deepest corner, $AR_{max}$):**
The maximum area ratio occurs at the bottom edge where the corner is formed. Empirically, the corner stretches ~30 % more than the average, so:

$$t_{min} \approx t_f \cdot 0.75 \approx 0.225 \text{ mm}$$

This is feasible for HIPS (typical formed thickness range 0.2 – 1.0 mm) but would not be feasible for brittle PS at this draw.

*(Step 1: 1.5; Step 2: 2; Step 3: 2; Step 4: 1.5. Total = 7 marks.)*

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice): Twin-Sheet Forming & Material Selection**

**Part (a) [7 marks]** — Describe the **twin-sheet thermoforming** process with a labeled schematic. List three packaging-relevant products made by this process and explain one advantage and one limitation versus single-sheet forming.

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

**Twin-sheet thermoforming process steps:**
1. Two thermoplastic sheets are independently heated in two separate heating zones.
2. The two sheets are indexed into a forming station containing **two mold halves** (typically male–female, both convex, that meet in the middle).
3. Compressed air is injected between the sheets, blowing them into their respective mold halves.
4. The mold halves clamp together; a final "blow-and-pin" step fuses the two sheets at the perimeter.
5. The hollow, sealed, double-walled part is cooled, ejected, and trimmed.

**Three packaging/dunnage products:**
1. Hollow plastic pallets (heavy-gauge, PP/HDPE)
2. Twin-sheet dunnage trays (automotive parts shipping)
3. Insulated shipping containers (EPS-free, twin-wall PP)

**Advantage:** Produces a sealed, hollow, structurally rigid part with **double-wall thickness** and **insulation value** — not possible in single-sheet forming.
**Limitation:** Requires two synchronized heating zones and matched mold halves — capital cost is roughly 2× that of single-sheet forming.

*(Process steps with sequence: 3 marks; 3 products: 1.5 marks; advantage + limitation: 2.5 marks. Total = 7.)*

**Part (b) [7 marks]** — A multilayer sheet with the structure **PET / EVOH / PE** (thicknesses 200 µm / 30 µm / 270 µm, total 500 µm) is to be thermoformed into a meat MAP tray. Discuss:
(i) the role of each layer,
(ii) the forming temperature window and why EVOH is challenging,
(iii) the effect of thermoforming on the EVOH barrier layer.

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**(i) Layer roles:**
- **PET (200 µm) —** structural, clarity, rigidity, printable outer skin.
- **EVOH (30 µm) —** oxygen barrier (primary function; OTR < 1 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹).
- **PE (270 µm) —** inner sealing layer, food contact, moisture barrier, hot-tack strength for lidding.

**(ii) Forming window & EVOH challenge:**
- PET softens at 70 – 80 °C ($T_g$); PE melts at 110 – 130 °C; EVOH is sensitive to moisture above 70 °C.
- **Process window:** 130 – 150 °C (must exceed PE melting but not degrade EVOH).
- **Challenge:** EVOH is hygroscopic — pre-drying the sheet at 80 °C for 4 – 6 hours is mandatory to prevent bubbling, voids, and barrier loss.

**(iii) Effect of thermoforming on EVOH:**
- The biaxial stretching during forming **thins the EVOH layer** in proportion to the area ratio (Eq. 1).
- A tray with $AR = 1.4$ reduces EVOH from 30 µm to ~21 µm.
- Thinner EVOH + possible moisture-induced pinholes → OTR may **double**.
- **Mitigation:** Use a thicker initial EVOH (40 – 50 µm) and minimize $AR$ by using a shallow tray design.

*(Part i: 2 marks; Part ii: 2.5 marks; Part iii: 2.5 marks. Total = 7 marks.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in Thermoforming Questions (compiled from KTU valuation keys 2022–2024):**
> 1. **Forgetting units in Eq. 1** — writing $t_f = t_0 \cdot A_0/A_f$ without specifying mm. Examiner deducts 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Confusing draw ratio vs. area ratio** — $DR$ is linear ($h/w$), $AR$ is areal ($A_f/A_0$). Mixing them up = 1-mark penalty.
> 3. **Not specifying polymer** in forming-temperature questions — every temperature window is polymer-specific. "Heat to 150 °C" without naming the polymer is incomplete.
> 4. **Skipping the assumption statement** in Eq. 1 derivations — examiners award 1 mark for stating "assuming incompressible deformation and constant density."
> 5. **In twin-sheet diagrams, drawing only one mold** — full 2-mark deduction if the second (opposing) mold half is missing.
> 6. **Forgetting to comment on feasibility** of thinnest-region thickness — always tie the calculated value to the polymer's processable range (0.2 – 1.0 mm for HIPS, etc.).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Thermoforming Packages**

- [ ] **Definition:** Heated thermoplastic sheet → formed over mold → cooled → trimmed.
- [ ] **Five operational phases:** Clamp → Heat → Form → Cool → Trim.
- [ ] **Four forming mechanisms:** Vacuum, pressure, mechanical matched-die, twin-sheet.
- [ ] **Master equation:** $t_f = t_0 \cdot \dfrac{A_0}{A_f} = \dfrac{t_0}{AR}$ (incompressibility, conservation of mass).
- [ ] **Draw ratio $DR = h / w$** — packaging typical $DR < 0.75:1$.
- [ ] **Area ratio $AR = A_f / A_0 \geq 1$** — drives thinning.
- [ ] **Vacuum pressure:** 0.05 – 0.09 MPa. **Pressure forming:** up to 2.5 MPa.
- [ ] **Forming temperature window:** above $T_g$ (amorphous) or $T_m$ (semi-crystalline), typically **130 – 180 °C**.
- [ ] **Thinnest region:** corner radius (biaxial stretch).
- [ ] **Thickest region:** bottom flat or apex (uniaxial / no stretch).
- [ ] **Dominant polymers for packaging:** PET, PVC, PS, HIPS, PP, PE, EVOH (multilayer).
- [ ] **Plug-assist:** used for deep-draw (DR > 0.5) to redistribute material pre-vacuum.
- [ ] **Twin-sheet:** two sheets + two molds + perimeter fuse = hollow, sealed part.
- [ ] **Cooling time (Fourier 1-D):** $t_{cool} \propto \rho c_p x^2 / k$ — thickness-squared dependence.
- [ ] **Forming force:** $F = \Delta P \cdot A_{sheet}$ — pressure forming gives 10–25× vacuum force.
- [ ] **Tooling cost:** thermoforming molds = 5–10 % of equivalent injection mold cost.
- [ ] **Cycle time:** 2 – 12 s for thin-gauge packaging; cooling-limited.
- [ ] **Material yield:** $Y = 1 - (\text{scrap fraction})$; trim skeleton re-grinds back to extruder.
- [ ] **Clamshell, blister, tray, cup, lid** — five canonical thermoformed package types.
- [ ] **MAP trays** use multilayer PET/EVOH/PE for oxygen barrier.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Position & Thermoforming & Wrap Forming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Position & Thermoforming & Wrap Forming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Position in Thermoforming Context

In plastics packaging thermoforming, **Position** refers to the precise spatial placement and timing of three critical elements relative to each other: (1) the thermoplastic **sheet**, (2) the **heater** assembly, and (3) the **mold** (or **plug-assist** device). Position governs the heating uniformity, sag control, and final wall-thickness distribution of the formed package.

> [!IMPORTANT]
