> **KTU 2024 Definition (Position):** The geometric and temporal placement of the plastic sheet, heater, and mold that determines the sag profile, heat gradient, and pre-forming alignment in a thermoforming line. It is the *first control variable* in achieving consistent packaging quality.

**Real-world Analogy (Position):** Think of **position** like the placement of bread in a toaster. If you push the bread too close to the heating coils, one side burns while the other stays cold. If you centre it properly, both sides brown evenly. In thermoforming, positioning the sheet at the correct distance from the radiant heater and aligning it with the mold cavity determines whether the package forms uniformly or develops thin spots that will fail under load.

---

### 1.2 Thermoforming — Formal Definition

**Thermoforming** is a secondary plastics processing technique in which a flat thermoplastic sheet is heated to a pliable forming temperature (above its glass-transition temperature, $T_g$, for amorphous polymers, or near its melting temperature, $T_m$, for semi-crystalline polymers), forced against a single-sided mold by vacuum, pressure, or mechanical means, cooled below its softening point, and then trimmed to yield a finished packaging component.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Thermoforming Temperature Bands (for packaging):**
> - **Amorphous polymers (e.g., PS, PVC, PET, APET):** $T_g + 30^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $T_g + 70^{\circ}\text{C}$
> - **Semi-crystalline polymers (e.g., PP, HDPE):** $T_m - 10^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $T_m + 20^{\circ}\text{C}$
> - **Typical forming range:** $120^{\circ}\text{C}$ – $200^{\circ}\text{C}$ for most packaging grades.

**Conceptual Analogy (Thermoforming):** Imagine blowing up a **balloon inside a shoebox lid**. You first stretch the balloon (heat the sheet so it sags and softens), then push it into the box (mold) and suck the air out (vacuum). The balloon takes the shape of the box. Thermoforming is the same idea, except the "balloon" is a heated plastic sheet and the "sucking" is industrial vacuum or compressed air.

---

### 1.3 Wrap Forming — Formal Definition

**Wrap forming** (also called **drape forming** or **male-mold forming**) is a variant of thermoforming in which a heated thermoplastic sheet is mechanically draped (wrapped) over a positive (male) mold, after which vacuum applied through the mold body pulls the sheet tightly against the mold contours. The sheet is *not* stretched into a cavity; rather, it is stretched *outward* over a convex profile.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Wrap Forming):** A thermoforming process in which a heated plastic sheet is stretched over a male (positive) mold by mechanical drape and vacuum, producing a part whose outer surface matches the male tool geometry. The sheet's original top surface becomes the part's *outer* (cosmetic) surface.

**Real-world Analogy (Wrap Forming):** Picture laying a thin **plastic food wrap** over a half-sphere (like a melon half). As you press the wrap down by hand, it conforms loosely. Now imagine sucking the air out from underneath through a hole in the sphere — the wrap gets sucked tight and copies every curve of the melon perfectly. Wrap forming is the industrial version of this action, using a heated, slightly stretchy plastic sheet instead of household wrap, and a precisely machined male mold instead of a melon.

---

### 1.4 Why Position, Thermoforming, and Wrap Forming Matter in Packaging

| Element | Role in Packaging Industry |
|---|---|
| **Position** | Determines package wall-thickness consistency, rejects, and cycle time. |
| **Thermoforming** | Produces > **70%** of all rigid plastic packaging (cups, trays, blister packs, lids, clamshells). |
| **Wrap Forming** | Produces large shallow parts (lidding films, decorative covers, insert trays) with excellent *outer-surface* cosmetics. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Metric:** Packaging thermoforming lines typically run at **20 – 60 cycles/minute** with sheet thicknesses from **0.2 mm** to **3.0 mm** and mold areas up to **1.2 m × 1.0 m**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Core Steps of Thermoforming (Logical Sequence)

1. **Sheet Loading & Clamping** — The thermoplastic sheet is gripped between two clamping frames. *Clamp position* must be perfectly horizontal; any tilt > **0.5°** causes asymmetric heating.

2. **Heating** — Radiant (ceramic, quartz, halogen) or contact heaters raise the sheet to the forming temperature. *Heater-to-sheet position* (typically **50 mm – 150 mm** vertical gap) controls heat flux.

3. **Sheet Sag (Position Drift)** — As the sheet softens, it sags downward due to gravity. The sag must be **measured and controlled** (typically **0.5% – 2%** of the clamped span) to avoid contact with lower tooling.

4. **Forming (Mold/Plug Positioning)** — The mold (or plug-assist) rises into position, or the sheet is driven onto the mold. Plug *position* and *speed* (typically **100 mm/s – 500 mm/s**) are critical.

5. **Cooling & Solidification** — The formed sheet is cooled below $T_g$ (for amorphous) or crystallization temperature (for semi-crystalline). Cooling time ≈ **0.5 – 2.0 seconds per 0.1 mm** wall thickness.

6. **Trimming & Stacking** — The formed part is trimmed by steel-rule die, laser, or router. Position of the trim die must align within **±0.1 mm** for clean cuts.

---

### 2.2 Position Variables in Thermoforming — The Engineering View

The position vector of the sheet relative to the heater and mold can be expressed as a function of time:

$$P(t) = P_0 + v_s \cdot t + \Delta_{\text{sag}}(t)$$

Where:
- $P_0$ = initial sheet position (mm)
- $v_s$ = sheet transport speed (mm/s)
- $\Delta_{\text{sag}}(t)$ = time-dependent sag displacement (mm)

The **sag displacement** of a clamped, self-weighted viscoelastic sheet is approximated by a clamped-beam deflection equation:

$$\Delta_{\text{sag}} = \frac{5 \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E_{\text{hot}} \cdot I}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = sheet density (kg/m³) ≈ **950 – 1450 kg/m³** for packaging polymers
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration = **9.81 m/s²**
- $L$ = clamped span (m)
- $E_{\text{hot}}$ = elastic modulus at forming temperature (Pa) — drops to **~1 – 10 MPa** when heated
- $I$ = second moment of area per unit width = $t_s^3 / 12$ (m⁴/m)
- $t_s$ = sheet thickness (m)

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_f = h / d$ | Forming (draw) ratio: depth ÷ diameter | dimensionless | Wall-thickness prediction |
| 2 | $t_f = t_0 / R_f^{\,n}$ | Final thickness (approx); $n \approx 0.5$ – $1.0$ | mm | Plug-assist design |
| 3 | $\Delta_{\text{sag}} = \frac{5\rho g L^4}{384 E_{\text{hot}} I}$ | Sheet sag at forming temperature | mm | Heater positioning |
| 4 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat energy to bring sheet to forming temp | J | Heater wattage sizing |
| 5 | $q = k \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{d}$ | Conduction heat flux (mold contact) | W/m² | Mold cooling design |
| 6 | $t_{\text{cycle}} = t_h + t_f + t_c + t_t$ | Total cycle time (heat + form + cool + trim) | s | Productivity analysis |
| 7 | $\tau = \eta_{\text{hot}} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Viscous stress in sheet at forming temp | Pa | Plug force calculation |
| 8 | $A_{\text{stretch}} = \frac{\text{surface area of part}}{\text{clamped sheet area}}$ | Areal stretch ratio | dimensionless | Material distribution |
| 9 | $P_{\text{vac}} \le 101.3$ kPa | Maximum vacuum (sea level) | kPa | Vacuum-pump sizing |
| 10 | $\theta_{\text{draw}} = \arctan(h/d)$ | Draw angle | degrees | Draft in mold design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** In a final exam, replace $E_{\text{hot}}$ with the value given in the problem (typically **1 – 10 MPa**). The room-temperature $E$ of **2 – 3 GPa** is *wrong* for hot-sheet sag and is the most common student error.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Packaging Industry:** The forming-ratio formula $t_f = t_0 / R_f^{\,n}$ is used every day by packaging designers to choose starting sheet thickness for a new tray or cup. If the part needs to be **0.4 mm** thick everywhere and the draw ratio is **2:1**, the starting sheet is **0.8 mm** (with $n=1$).
- **Sag control** determines how close the heater can be placed to the sheet without the sheet drooping onto the heater or the lower platen.
- **Wrap forming** uses the *male-mold geometry* to set the draw ratio, so the same equations apply — but the cosmetic (top) surface of the sheet becomes the outer package surface.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Wall-Thickness Distribution in Wrap Forming (Polar Geometry)

For a **wrap-formed** part over a hemispherical male mold of radius $R$, the starting sheet thickness $t_0$ becomes the final thickness $t_f$ at the apex of the dome. The original sheet has area $A_0$, and the final formed dome has surface area $A_f = 2\pi R^2$ (hemisphere).

**Step 1 — State the conservation of volume (incompressible sheet):**

$$V_0 = V_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_0 \cdot t_0 = A_f \cdot t_{\text{avg}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute areas** (assume the clamped blank is a circle of radius $R_0$):

$$\pi R_0^2 \cdot t_0 = 2\pi R^2 \cdot t_{\text{avg}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the *average* final thickness:**

$$t_{\text{avg}} = \frac{R_0^2 \cdot t_0}{2R^2}$$

**Step 4 — But the apex of a hemisphere stretches *most*, so the local thickness there is lower than $t_{\text{avg}}$.** Using equal-biaxial stretch at the pole:

$$t_{\text{apex}} = t_0 \cdot \left(\frac{R_0}{R}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2 \cos^2 \phi}$$

where $\phi$ is the polar angle measured from the apex. At the apex, $\phi = 0$, so $\cos \phi = 1$:

$$t_{\text{apex}} = \frac{t_0}{2}\left(\frac{R_0}{R}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 5 — General form for any polar angle $\phi$:**

$$t_f(\phi) = t_{\text{apex}} \cdot \cos^2 \phi = \frac{t_0 \cdot R_0^2}{2 R^2} \cdot \cos^2 \phi$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The wrap-formed hemisphere is *thinnest* at the apex (the pole) and *thickest* at the equator. This is the opposite of female-mold (cavity) forming, where the part is thickest at the apex and thinnest at the rim.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Sheet Sag in a Heated Thermoplastic Sheet

**Step 1 — Treat the clamped, heated sheet as a viscoelastic beam** under self-weight. For a uniform clamped-clamped beam of length $L$, width $b$, thickness $t_s$, the maximum sag at mid-span is:

$$\Delta_{\text{sag}} = \frac{5 w L^4}{384 E_{\text{hot}} I}$$

where $w = \rho g t_s b$ is the weight per unit length.

**Step 2 — Compute $I$ per unit width** (set $b = 1$ m):

$$I = \frac{t_s^3}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the sag equation:**

$$\Delta_{\text{sag}} = \frac{5 \rho g t_s L^4}{384 E_{\text{hot}} \cdot (t_s^3 / 12)} = \frac{5 \rho g L^4 \cdot 12}{384 E_{\text{hot}} t_s^2}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify** ($12 / 384 = 1/32$):

$$\Delta_{\text{sag}} = \frac{5 \rho g L^4}{32 E_{\text{hot}} t_s^2}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example** (typical APET sheet):
- $L = 0.6$ m, $t_s = 1.0$ mm $= 10^{-3}$ m, $\rho = 1350$ kg/m³, $E_{\text{hot}} = 5 \times 10^6$ Pa, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

$$\Delta_{\text{sag}} = \frac{5 \cdot 1350 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (0.6)^4}{32 \cdot (5 \times 10^6) \cdot (10^{-3})^2}$$

Compute numerator: $5 \cdot 1350 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.1296 = 8583.7$

Compute denominator: $32 \cdot 5 \times 10^6 \cdot 10^{-6} = 160$

$$\Delta_{\text{sag}} = \frac{8583.7}{160} \approx 53.6 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This ~50 mm sag** is what the *position* of the lower platen must accommodate. If the lower platen sits closer than 50 mm + safety margin, the hot sheet will touch it and be marked.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison: Female-Mold vs. Wrap (Male-Mold) Forming

| Parameter | Female (Cavity) Forming | Wrap (Male-Mold) Forming |
|---|---|---|
| Mold type | Negative (concave) | Positive (convex) |
| Sheet direction of stretch | *Into* the cavity | *Over* the mold |
| Thickest area | Rim / flange | Equator / base |
| Thinnest area | Apex / corners of cavity | Apex / dome top |
| Outer (cosmetic) surface | Sheet *bottom* (mold side) | Sheet *top* (free side during drape, then mold side after vacuum) |
| Best for | Deep cups, tubs, deep trays | Shallow trays, lids, display covers |
| Wall-thickness uniformity | Harder to control at deep draws | Better on shallow parts |
| Plug-assist use | Very common (deep draw) | Rare |
| Typical forming ratio $R_f$ | 1:1 to 4:1 | 1:1 to 1.5:1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** Students often write that wrap forming "produces parts with uniform thickness." It does *not*. It produces parts whose thickness varies with $\cos^2 \phi$ (Section 3.1). Wrap forming gives *better* cosmetic outer surface, not *uniform* thickness.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Heat-Energy Requirement to Bring a Sheet to Forming Temperature

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the sheet:**

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Mass in terms of sheet area and density:**

$$m = \rho \cdot A_{\text{sheet}} \cdot t_s$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$Q = \rho \cdot A_{\text{sheet}} \cdot t_s \cdot c_p \cdot (T_f - T_{\text{ambient}})$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example** (PS sheet, $A = 1$ m², $t_s = 1$ mm, $T_f = 150°$C, $T_{\text{amb}} = 25°$C, $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1300$ J/(kg·K)):

$$Q = 1050 \cdot 1 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot 1300 \cdot (150 - 25)$$

$$Q = 1050 \cdot 0.001 \cdot 1300 \cdot 125 = 1.706 \times 10^5 \text{ J} \approx 170.6 \text{ kJ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Heater Wattage Sizing:** If the cycle allows **15 s** of heating, the heater must deliver $Q/t = 170{,}625 / 15 \approx 11.4$ kW per square meter of sheet. This is why industrial thermoforming heaters are rated in **kW/m²**, typically **30 – 80 kW/m²**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Thermoforming Line — Position Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Sheet Reel / Stack] --> nodeB[Sheet Feed & Clamp Frame]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Heater Station - Position Adjustable]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Sheet Sag Monitor - Optical / Laser]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Plug-Assist Positioner - Servo XY Z]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Mold Platen - Vacuum + Pressure]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Cooling Station - Air Water Mist]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Trimming Die Station - Position Calibrated]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Stacked Finished Packages]

    nodeC -.heat flux.-> nodeD
    nodeD -.feedback.-> nodeE
    nodeF -.vacuum signal.-> nodeG

    classDef ctrl fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef mech fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef sensor fill:#b8860b,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff

    class nodeB,nodeE,nodeF,nodeH mech
    class nodeC,nodeG ctrl
    class nodeD sensor
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Sheet travels left-to-right. The *Position* of three elements — clamp, heater, and plug/mold — is independently adjustable. The *sag monitor* is the central feedback sensor that triggers plug descent at the correct instant.

---

### 4.2 Schematic: Position Geometry of the Sheet, Heater, and Mold

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HS[Heater Station]
        H1[Top Radiant Heater - Quartz / Ceramic]
        H2[Bottom Radiant Heater - Symmetry Control]
    end

    subgraph SS[Sheet Region]
        S1[Clamped Sheet - Heated Region - Sag delta]
    end

    subgraph MS[Mold Station]
        M1[Upper Clamp Frame]
        M2[Vacuum Box - 101.3 kPa]
        M3[Female Mold Cavity OR Male Mold Boss]
        P1[Plug Assist - Position Servo]
    end

    H1 -- Gap d1 mm --> S1
    H2 -- Gap d2 mm --> S1
    S1 -- Drains into --> M3
    P1 -- Penetration depth hp --> M3
    M1 -- Clamp Force F --> S1
    M2 -- Vacuum Pv --> S1

    classDef heater fill:#c0392b,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    classDef sheet fill:#2980b9,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    classDef mold fill:#16a085,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff

    class H1,H2 heater
    class S1 sheet
    class M1,M2,M3,P1 mold
```

> **Reading the diagram:** $d_1$ and $d_2$ are the heater-to-sheet position gaps (typically **50–150 mm**). $h_p$ is the plug penetration depth, set by the *position servo*. The plug-assist position is the most critical variable in deep-draw thermoforming.

---

### 4.3 Schematic: Wrap Forming — Sequence & Wall-Thickness Profile

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Step 1: Clamp Cold Sheet] --> B[Step 2: Heat Sheet to Tf]
    B --> C[Step 3: Drape Sheet Over Male Mold - Wraps Naturally]
    C --> D[Step 4: Apply Vacuum Through Male Mold - 80 to 95 kPa]
    D --> E[Step 5: Sheet Pulls Tight Against Male Profile]
    E --> F[Step 6: Cool Below Tg]
    F --> G[Step 7: Release Vacuum and Remove Part]
    G --> H[Step 8: Trim Flange - Position Calibrated to plus minus 0.1 mm]

    H --> I[Final Wrap Formed Package - Cosmetics on Outside]

    classDef step fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#ecf0f1,color:#ffffff
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H step
    class I fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Wrap forming uses the *male mold as the vacuum plenum*. Air is sucked *outward* from inside the mold body, pulling the draped sheet *inward* to hug the convex shape.

---

### 4.4 Wall-Thickness Profile Across a Wrap-Formed Hemisphere

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Profile[Wall Thickness Profile - Wrap Formed Hemisphere]
        APEX[At Apex - phi equals 0 - t apex equals t0 times R0 squared divided by 2 R squared]
        MID[At Mid Latitude - phi equals 45 degrees - t equals t apex times cos squared 45]
        EQU[At Equator - phi equals 90 degrees - t equals 0 in theory but flange supports it]
    end

    APEX --> MID --> EQU

    classDef thin fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    classDef med fill:#f39c12,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    classDef thick fill:#27ae60,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff

    class APEX thin
    class MID med
    class EQU thick
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The wrap-formed part is **thinnest at the apex** (pole of the male mold) and **thickest at the equator/base**. This is the exact opposite of female-mold forming.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define thermoforming. List any two polymers commonly used in thermoformed packaging with their typical forming-temperature range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Thermoforming is a plastics manufacturing process in which a flat thermoplastic sheet is heated to its forming temperature, deformed against a single-sided mold by vacuum, pressure, or mechanical force, cooled, and trimmed to produce a finished part.*

*Two common packaging polymers:*
1. *Polystyrene (PS): amorphous, $T_g \approx 100°$C, forming range $130°$C – $160°$C.*
2. *Polypropylene (PP): semi-crystalline, $T_m \approx 165°$C, forming range $155°$C – $175°$C.*

*(Any two polymers with valid forming temperatures — 1 mark each; definition — 1 mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is wrap forming? How does it differ from conventional female-mold vacuum forming in terms of (a) mold type and (b) final wall-thickness distribution?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Wrap forming is a thermoforming technique in which a heated plastic sheet is mechanically draped over a positive (male) mold and pulled against it by vacuum applied through the mold body.*

| Aspect | Wrap Forming | Female-Mold Forming |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Mold type | Male (positive, convex) | Female (negative, concave) |
| (b) Wall-thickness | Thinnest at apex, thickest at base | Thickest at apex, thinnest at corners |

*(Definition — 1 mark; correct mold-type difference — 1 mark; correct thickness distribution — 1 mark.)*

---

### **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)**

---

## **Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Internal Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the step-by-step operation of a **plug-assist vacuum thermoforming** line. Highlight the role of the **plug position** and **sheet sag** in determining the final wall thickness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **0.8 mm** thick APET sheet (density **1350 kg/m³**, $c_p$ **1200 J/(kg·K)**, $E_{\text{hot}}$ **4 × 10⁶ Pa**) is clamped over a span of **0.5 m** and heated to **145 °C** from an ambient of **25 °C**. Calculate:
   (i) The mid-span sag of the sheet at forming temperature. **(4 marks)**
   (ii) The heat energy required to bring **1 m²** of sheet to forming temperature. **(3 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Schematic (2 marks):** A labelled diagram showing the four stations: clamp frame, top + bottom heaters, plug-assist with servo-positioner, mold with vacuum channels, cooling jets, and trim die.

**Step-by-step operation (5 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Clamping:** Sheet is gripped between upper and lower clamp frames. Clamp pressure ≈ **0.3 – 0.5 MPa**. Clamp position must be horizontal within ± 0.5°.

2. **Heating:** Radiant heaters (top and bottom) raise the sheet from ambient to **145 °C** (for APET). Heater position (gap **50 – 150 mm**) is set so that heat flux is uniform across the sheet.

3. **Sheet Sag & Plug Pre-Position:** As the sheet softens, it sags downward under gravity. A sag monitor (laser or ultrasonic) detects the *peak* of the sag. The plug is held in the *stand-off position* (typically **25 – 50 mm** above the sheet) until sag reaches **~1%** of span.

4. **Plug Penetration:** The plug descends at **100 – 500 mm/s** until it reaches its *forming position*, typically **60 – 80%** of the mold cavity depth. The plug pre-stretches the sheet, distributing material into the corners.

5. **Vacuum Application:** Vacuum (≈ **80 – 95 kPa**) pulls the pre-stretched sheet into the female cavity. Final wall thickness depends on the *plug position* and *timing*.

6. **Cooling & Ejection:** Air/water mist cools the part below $T_g$ (≈ **75 °C** for APET**). The mold opens and the part is ejected for trimming.

> **Role of plug position:** The plug must enter *before* the sheet contacts the mold floor. If the plug position is too shallow, the sheet stretches unevenly — corners thin out. If it is too deep, the sheet contacts the plug tip and cools locally, creating a *cold mark* and uneven wall thickness.

> **Role of sheet sag:** Excessive sag causes the sheet to touch the lower platen (marking the cosmetic surface) or to be pre-stretched non-uniformly. Controlled sag (1–2% of span) is actually *used* to pre-distribute material.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $t_s = 0.8$ mm $= 8 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $\rho = 1350$ kg/m³
- $c_p = 1200$ J/(kg·K)
- $E_{\text{hot}} = 4 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $L = 0.5$ m
- $T_f = 145°$C, $T_{\text{amb}} = 25°$C
- $A_{\text{sheet}} = 1$ m²

**Part (b)(i) — Sag calculation (4 marks):**

Use the clamped-beam formula:

$$\Delta_{\text{sag}} = \frac{5 \rho g L^4}{32 E_{\text{hot}} t_s^2}$$

**Step 1 — Compute numerator:**

$$\text{Num} = 5 \cdot 1350 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (0.5)^4 = 5 \cdot 1350 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.0625 = 4139.0$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator:**

$$\text{Den} = 32 \cdot (4 \times 10^6) \cdot (8 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 32 \cdot 4 \times 10^6 \cdot 6.4 \times 10^{-7} = 81.92$$

**Step 3 — Divide:**

$$\Delta_{\text{sag}} = \frac{4139.0}{81.92} \approx 50.5 \text{ mm}$$

> **Result:** The sheet sags about **50 mm** at the forming temperature. The lower platen must be at least **75 – 100 mm** below the sheet.

**Part (b)(ii) — Heat energy (3 marks):**

$$Q = \rho \cdot A \cdot t_s \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 1 — Mass of 1 m² sheet:**

$$m = 1350 \cdot 1 \cdot 8 \times 10^{-4} = 1.08 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = 145 - 25 = 120 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply:**

$$Q = 1.08 \cdot 1200 \cdot 120 = 1.555 \times 10^5 \text{ J} = 155.5 \text{ kJ}$$

> **Result:** About **155.5 kJ** of energy is needed per square meter of sheet.

---

## **Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain **wrap forming** in detail. Compare it with **female-mold vacuum forming** with respect to (i) mold type, (ii) cosmetic surface, (iii) typical applications, and (iv) wall-thickness distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **PS sheet** of original thickness **1.2 mm** is to be wrap-formed over a hemispherical male mold of radius **40 mm**. If the original clamped blank has a radius of **60 mm**, calculate: **(7 marks)**
   (i) The average final thickness of the formed hemisphere. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The thickness at the apex of the dome. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) The thickness at a polar angle of **60°** from the apex. **(2 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

*Definition (1.5 marks):* Wrap forming is a thermoforming technique in which a heated thermoplastic sheet is **mechanically draped** over a male (positive) mold and pulled tight by **vacuum applied through the mold body**.

*Detailed Explanation (1.5 marks):*
- The sheet is clamped, heated, and then *allowed to drape* over the male mold under its own weight and a mechanical pusher.
- Vacuum applied through small holes in the male mold body evacuates the air between the sheet and the mold, creating a pressure differential of up to **95 kPa**.
- The sheet conforms to the convex mold surface, copies its surface finish, and is then cooled.
- The *top* (originally free) surface of the sheet becomes the *inner* surface of the part; the *bottom* (mold-contact) surface becomes the *outer cosmetic* surface.

*Comparison Table (4 marks — 1 mark per row):*

| Parameter | Wrap Forming | Female-Mold Vacuum Forming |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Mold type | Male (positive, convex) | Female (negative, concave) |
| (ii) Cosmetic surface | Outer surface (mold-contact) | Inner surface (mold-contact) |
| (iii) Typical applications | Shallow trays, lids, blisters, displays | Deep cups, tubs, containers, deep trays |
| (iv) Wall-thickness | Thinnest at apex, thickest at flange | Thickest at apex, thinnest at deep corners |

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $t_0 = 1.2$ mm
- $R = 40$ mm
- $R_0 = 60$ mm

**Part (b)(i) — Average thickness (3 marks):**

Conservation of volume: $A_0 \cdot t_0 = A_f \cdot t_{\text{avg}}$

$$\pi R_0^2 \cdot t_0 = 2\pi R^2 \cdot t_{\text{avg}}$$

$$t_{\text{avg}} = \frac{R_0^2 \cdot t_0}{2R^2} = \frac{(60)^2 \cdot 1.2}{2 \cdot (40)^2} = \frac{3600 \cdot 1.2}{2 \cdot 1600} = \frac{4320}{3200} = 1.35 \text{ mm}$$

> **Result:** Average final thickness ≈ **1.35 mm**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Many students mistakenly write $t_{\text{avg}} = 1.2 \cdot (R_0/R)^2 = 1.2 \cdot 2.25 = 2.7$ mm. This is wrong because it ignores the area change of the hemisphere.

**Part (b)(ii) — Apex thickness (2 marks):**

At $\phi = 0$:

$$t_{\text{apex}} = \frac{t_0}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{R_0}{R}\right)^2 = \frac{1.2}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{60}{40}\right)^2 = 0.6 \cdot 2.25 = 1.35 \text{ mm}$$

> **Result:** Apex thickness = **1.35 mm** (coincidentally equal to the average for this geometry).

**Part (b)(iii) — Thickness at $\phi = 60°$ (2 marks):**

$$t_f(\phi) = t_{\text{apex}} \cdot \cos^2 \phi = 1.35 \cdot (\cos 60°)^2 = 1.35 \cdot (0.5)^2 = 1.35 \cdot 0.25$$

$$t_f(60°) = 0.3375 \text{ mm}$$

> **Result:** At 60° from the apex, the wall is only **~0.34 mm** — less than one-third of the original sheet. Designers must verify that this meets the *minimum* packaging-barrier and *mechanical-strength* requirements.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Using room-temperature $E$ in the sag formula** — drops the sag to a few mm and gives a completely wrong answer. Always use $E_{\text{hot}}$ (1 – 10 MPa).
> 2. **Confusing wrap forming with female-mold forming** — wrap forming is *over* a male mold, *not into* a cavity. Examiners deduct 2 marks instantly for this.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2 in $t_{\text{apex}}$** — the apex of a hemisphere is *half* the average thickness. The $\frac{1}{2}$ comes from the geometric area of a hemisphere being twice that of its equatorial disc.
> 4. **Not stating the unit of draw ratio or polar angle** — KTU examiners look for the unit (dimensionless, degrees) in the final answer.
> 5. **Skipping the schematic in the 7-mark question** — a labelled diagram is worth **at least 2 marks** by itself. Always draw one.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

- **Position** = the *geometric and temporal placement* of sheet, heater, plug, and mold. It controls sag, heating uniformity, and wall thickness.
- **Thermoforming** = heat a thermoplastic sheet to $T_g + 30°$C to $T_g + 70°$C (amorphous) or near $T_m$ (semi-crystalline), form against a single mold, cool, trim.
- **Wrap forming** = drape a heated sheet over a *male* mold and pull with vacuum; cosmetic surface is the *outside* of the part.
- **Six steps:** Clamp → Heat → Sag → Form → Cool → Trim.
- **Forming ratio $R_f = h / d$** — directly determines final wall thickness.
- **Sheet sag** = use $E_{\text{hot}}$ (1 – 10 MPa), **not** room-temperature $E$ (2 – 3 GPa).
- **Heat energy** = $Q = m c_p \Delta T$; heater power ≈ **30 – 80 kW/m²** for packaging grades.
- **Wrap-formed hemisphere thickness profile:** $t_f(\phi) = t_0 R_0^2 \cos^2 \phi / (2 R^2)$; *thinnest at apex, thickest at base*.
- **Female-mold thickness profile:** *thinnest at deep corners, thickest at flange*.
- **Plug-assist position** = **60 – 80%** of cavity depth; too shallow → thin corners, too deep → cold marks.
- **Heater-to-sheet position** = **50 – 150 mm**; closer for thin sheets, farther for thick.
- **Cycle time** = $t_h + t_f + t_c + t_t$ ≈ **4 – 12 s** for packaging.
- **Vacuum** = typically **80 – 95 kPa**; max sea-level vacuum = **101.3 kPa**.
- **Industry packaging grades:** APET, RPET, PS, HIPS, PP, PVC, EVOH-multilayer sheets.
- **Always draw a labelled schematic** in 7-mark answers; it carries 2 marks on its own.
- **State the units** in every final numerical answer; KTU examiners deduct half a mark for missing units.
- **Wrap forming applications:** blister packs, lidding trays, insert trays, decorative covers.
- **Female-mold applications:** yogurt cups, margarine tubs, deep trays, clamshells, cups.
- **Plug-assist use** is *common in female-mold* deep-draw forming; *rare* in wrap forming.
- **Sag monitoring** is done by laser, ultrasonic, or mechanical probe; triggers plug descent.
- **Forming-ratio exponent $n$** in $t_f = t_0 / R_f^{\,n}$ is **0.5 – 1.0** depending on polymer rheology.
- **Draft angle** in the mold: typically **1° – 3°** for clean part release.
- **Cooling time** ≈ **0.5 – 2.0 s per 0.1 mm** of wall thickness for chilled-mold operation.
- **Industry rejects** in thermoforming are dominated (≈ **60%**) by *wall-thickness variation*, directly traceable to improper *position* of the sheet, heater, or plug.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid Phase Pressure Forming
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solid Phase Pressure Forming

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Solid Phase Pressure Forming (SPPF)**, also termed *Solid Phase Sheet Forming* or *Cold Forming*, is a thermoforming variant in which a thermoplastic sheet (typically a semi-crystalline polymer such as **Polypropylene (PP)**, **High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE)**, or **Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET)**) is heated to a temperature **below its crystalline melting point ($T_m$)** but **above its glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** — for amorphous grades, just below the softening point — and subsequently deformed into a mold cavity using pneumatic, hydraulic, or mechanical pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Unlike conventional thermoforming (which operates near the melt state), Solid Phase Forming retains the polymer in a **rubbery, semi-ordered state**. The crystalline regions remain intact, providing superior mechanical integrity, dimensional stability, and minimal sag during processing.

The standard operating window is governed by:

$$T_g < T_{form} < T_m$$

where $T_{form}$ is the forming temperature. For amorphous polymers such as **PS** and **PVC**, $T_m$ is replaced by the heat distortion temperature.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Clay Tile vs. Molten Glass" Metaphor**

Imagine you have two slabs of material:
1. A **molten glass pane** (representing *melt-phase thermoforming*) — easily deformable, but sags, flows, and loses shape memory.
2. A **cold clay tile warmed slightly in the sun** (representing *solid phase pressure forming*) — soft enough to bend and mold, but still firm, with its internal grain structure preserved.

Solid Phase Pressure Forming is essentially **"warm bending with intent"**. The polymer is warmed just enough to become pliable (rubbery state), pressed against a mold, and cooled while still under pressure. The crystalline lamellae act like **internal scaffolding** that resists uncontrolled flow, giving the final packaging container its strength, clarity, and tight dimensional tolerances.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Notation | Typical Industrial Range |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------------|
| Forming Temperature | $T_{form}$ | **80 °C – 165 °C** (polymer dependent) |
| Forming Pressure | $P_f$ | **0.3 MPa – 1.5 MPa** |
| Sheet Thickness | $t_0$ | **0.2 mm – 3.0 mm** |
| Draw Ratio | $D_r$ | **0.5 – 2.0** |
| Mold Temperature | $T_{mold}$ | **40 °C – 85 °C** |
| Cycle Time | $t_c$ | **4 s – 20 s** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** In SPPF, the polymer never crosses the melting isotherm. This preserves **~70–90% of its original crystallinity**, which is the principal reason pharmaceutical blister packs and dairy cups made by this process exhibit superior barrier and stiffness properties.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The SPPF process is governed by **five sequential logic steps**, each critical to producing defect-free packaging:

### Step 1 — Sheet Clamping & Pre-Heating
The extruded or calendered polymer sheet is clamped in a **frame** and transported through an infrared (IR) or convection oven. The sheet is heated uniformly from **both sides** to bring it into the rubbery regime.

> **Why?** Uniform heating prevents uneven thinning ("webbing") during forming.

### Step 2 — Temperature Equilibration
The sheet dwells in the heating station long enough to eliminate thermal gradients through the thickness.

> **How?** Bi-axial IR heaters with pyrometric feedback maintain $\pm 2\,^\circ\text{C}$ uniformity.

### Step 3 — Pressure Application (Forming Stroke)
The heated sheet is transferred over the mold cavity. Compressed air, vacuum, or a **plug-assist** pushes the sheet into the female (or onto the male) mold.

> **Why pressure instead of pure vacuum?** Pressures of **0.5–1.5 MPa** allow deeper draws without the sheet tearing — vacuum alone is limited to **~0.1 MPa (atmospheric)**.

### Step 4 — In-Mold Cooling
While maintaining pressure, chilled air or water channels in the mold cool the formed part back below $T_g$ (for amorphous zones) or to a temperature where crystallites lock in.

### Step 5 — Demolding & Trimming
The solidified part is ejected and the flange is trimmed via a steel-rule die.

---

## 2.2 Material Selection Logic

| Polymer | $T_g$ (°C) | $T_m$ (°C) | SPPF Window (°C) | Suitability |
|---------|-----------|-----------|------------------|-------------|
| **PP** | −10 | 165 | 130 – 158 | Excellent |
| **HDPE** | −120 | 130 | 100 – 125 | Excellent |
| **PET (CPET/APET)** | 70 | 245 | 95 – 140 | Good (crystallizable) |
| **PVC (rigid)** | 80 | — | 95 – 130 | Good |
| **PS** | 100 | — | 110 – 135 | Limited |
| **PC** | 145 | — | 155 – 200 | Specialty |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Semi-crystalline polymers are **preferred** for SPPF because their crystalline domains act as physical crosslinks, preventing excessive sag and providing high post-formed stiffness.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma_{form} = \dfrac{P_f \cdot R}{2t}$ | Hoop stress in a formed dome (thin-wall pressure vessel analogy) | Pa |
| 2 | $D_r = \dfrac{h}{R}$ | Draw ratio: depth-to-width of mold cavity | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\dfrac{t_1}{t_0} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + D_r^2}}$ | Thickness distribution in a hemispherical dome (volume conservation) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $t_{heat} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot t_0^2}{4 k}$ | Characteristic heating time (Fourier number $Fo = 1$) | s |
| 5 | $F_{plug} = P_f \cdot A_{plug}$ | Plug-assist force required | N |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_{biax} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{D_f}{D_0}\right)$ | True biaxial strain during forming | dimensionless |
| 7 | $T_{form,opt} = T_g + 0.75(T_m - T_g)$ | Empirical optimum forming temperature (semi-crystals) | °C / K |
| 8 | $\eta_{rubbery} = \eta_0 \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R_g}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{form}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{ref}}\right)\right]$ | Melt viscosity in rubbery regime (Arrhenius) | Pa·s |

> **Note on notation:** All vertical bars (e.g., for absolute value or determinants) are rendered as $\vert$ to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Packaging Engineering

* **Pharmaceutical Blister Packs** — Cold-formed **Alu-Alu** laminates use SPPF principles for deep-drawn cavities with high moisture barrier.
* **Dairy Cups (PP Yogurt Containers)** — Formed near $150\,^\circ\text{C}$ below $T_m$ to retain gloss and impact strength.
* **Microwaveable Trays (CPET)** — Crystallization during SPPF confers heat resistance up to **220 °C** for oven use.
* **Single-serve Coffee Capsules** — HDPE/EVOH barrier sheets are SPPF-formed to lock in aroma.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Wall-Thickness Distribution in a Hemispherical Dome

**Problem Setup:** A flat sheet of initial thickness $t_0$ and radius $R_0$ is pressure-formed into a hemispherical dome of radius $R$. Determine the final thickness $t_1$ at the pole.

**Assumption:** Constant volume (incompressible rubbery polymer), no material loss at the flange.

### Step 1 — Initial Sheet Volume
The disc that becomes the dome has volume:

$$V_0 = \pi R_0^{\,2} \cdot t_0$$

### Step 2 — Final Dome Volume
A hemispherical shell of inner radius $R$ and uniform thickness $t_1$ has volume:

$$V_1 = \frac{2}{3}\pi (R + t_1)^3 - \frac{2}{3}\pi R^3$$

### Step 3 — Thin-Wall Approximation
Since $t_1 \ll R$, we apply the binomial expansion $(R+t_1)^3 \approx R^3 + 3R^2 t_1$, yielding:

$$V_1 \approx 2\pi R^2 t_1$$

### Step 4 — Apply Volume Conservation
Setting $V_0 = V_1$:

$$\pi R_0^{\,2} t_0 = 2\pi R^2 t_1$$

$$t_1 = \frac{R_0^{\,2} t_0}{2R^2}$$

### Step 5 — Express in Draw Ratio
For a hemispherical dome, $R_0 = R$ (the dome radius equals the original disc radius), giving:

$$t_1 = \frac{t_0}{2}$$

### Step 6 — Generalised Form Using Draw Ratio
For a non-hemispherical dome where $D_r = h/R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= \frac{t_0}{\sqrt{1 + D_r^2}} \\
\text{(derived from surface-area conservation: } A_{\text{sheet}} &= A_{\text{dome}} \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A deeper draw ($D_r \uparrow$) **thins** the wall. For $D_r = 1$, $t_1 \approx 0.707\,t_0$; for $D_r = 2$, $t_1 \approx 0.447\,t_0$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Optimum Forming Temperature

**Empirical Derivation from Polymer Rheology:**

For a semi-crystalline polymer, the rubbery modulus $E_r$ drops sharply above $T_g$ but plateaus until $T_m$. The optimum forming temperature is where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_r(T_{form}) &= E_{crit} \\
\text{where } E_{crit} &= 0.1 \cdot E_{glassy}
\end{aligned}
$$

Empirically, this corresponds to:

$$T_{form,opt} = T_g + 0.75(T_m - T_g)$$

### Worked Numerical Example — Polypropylene (PP)
Given: $T_g = -10\,^\circ\text{C} = 263\,\text{K}$ and $T_m = 165\,^\circ\text{C} = 438\,\text{K}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{form,opt} &= 263 + 0.75 \times (438 - 263) \\
&= 263 + 0.75 \times 175 \\
&= 263 + 131.25 \\
&= 394.25\,\text{K} \\
&\approx 121.25\,^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** PP should be SPPF-formed near **121 °C** — well below its 165 °C melting point, preserving crystallinity while ensuring sufficient formability.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Plug-Assist Force Required

To pre-stretch a sheet uniformly before final forming, a plug of area $A_{plug}$ pushes the sheet into the cavity.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{plug} &= P_f \cdot A_{plug} \\
\text{For a circular plug: } F_{plug} &= P_f \cdot \pi r_{plug}^{\,2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Example
A plug of radius **50 mm** under forming pressure **0.8 MPa**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{plug} &= 0.8 \times 10^6 \cdot \pi \times (0.05)^2 \\
&= 0.8 \times 10^6 \cdot 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 6283\,\text{N} \\
&\approx 6.28\,\text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SPPF vs. Conventional Thermoforming

| Attribute | Solid Phase Pressure Forming | Conventional (Melt) Thermoforming |
|-----------|------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Operating temperature | Below $T_m$ | At/near $T_m$ |
| Sag resistance | **High** (crystallites intact) | Low |
| Dimensional tolerance | $\pm 0.05$ mm | $\pm 0.15$ mm |
| Sheet thickness uniformity | Excellent | Variable |
| Cycle time | Slightly longer (cooling) | Shorter |
| Mechanical strength of part | **Superior** | Moderate |
| Energy consumption | Lower (no full melt) | Higher |
| Material crystallinity post-form | **Preserved/enhanced** | Reduced |
| Typical polymers | PP, HDPE, PET, PVC | PS, ABS, PVC |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Process Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Extruded Sheet Reel] --> B[Sheet Feed & Clamp Frame]
    B --> C[IR Heating Station Top Heater]
    C --> D[IR Heating Station Bottom Heater]
    D --> E{Temperature Uniform? Yes}
    E -- No --> C
    E -- Yes --> F[Transfer to Mold Station]
    F --> G[Plug Assist Pre-Stretch]
    G --> H[Pressure Forming 0.5 to 1.5 MPa]
    H --> I[In Mold Cooling Below Tg]
    I --> J[Demolding & Ejection]
    J --> K[Flange Trimming Die Cut]
    K --> L[Stacked Finished Packages]
    L --> M[Quality Inspection Station]
    M --> N[Cartoning & Shipment]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — SPPF Machine Stations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Station 1 Material Feed]
        A1[Sheet Unwind Reel]
        A2[Edge Guide Alignment]
        A3[Sheet Thickness Gauge]
    end

    subgraph S2[Station 2 Heating Zone]
        B1[Top IR Ceramic Heater]
        B2[Bottom IR Quartz Heater]
        B3[Pyrometer Temperature Sensor]
        B4[PLC Temperature Controller]
    end

    subgraph S3[Station 3 Forming Zone]
        C1[Mold Clamp Frame]
        C2[Female Aluminum Mold]
        C3[Plug Assist Pneumatic Cylinder]
        C4[Pressure Regulator 0 to 1.5 MPa]
    end

    subgraph S4[Station 4 Cooling Zone]
        D1[Water Circulating Mold Channels]
        D2[Forced Air Cooling Manifold]
    end

    subgraph S5[Station 5 Trimming Output]
        E1[Steel Rule Die Press]
        E2[Scrap Regrind Granulator]
        E3[Stacker Conveyor]
    end

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B1
    B4 --> B2
    C4 --> C3
    D1 --> D2
```

## 4.3 Process State Diagram — Temperature vs. Polymer Phase

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> GlassyState
    GlassyState: Glassy State T less than Tg
    GlassyState --> RubberyState: Heat above Tg
    RubberyState: Rubbery State Tg less than T less than Tm
    RubberyState --> MeltState: Heat above Tm
    MeltState: Molten State T greater than Tm
    RubberyState --> FormedPart: Apply Pressure P 0.5 to 1.5 MPa
    FormedPart: Cool Under Pressure Below Tg
    FormedPart --> DemoldedPart: Eject
    DemoldedPart --> [*]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define Solid Phase Pressure Forming. Why is it preferred for semi-crystalline polymers?**

**Model Answer:**
Solid Phase Pressure Forming (SPPF) is a thermoforming technique in which the polymer sheet is heated to a temperature **between its glass transition temperature ($T_g$) and melting point ($T_m$)**, and then pressure-formed into a mold.

It is preferred for semi-crystalline polymers because:
1. The crystalline lamellae are retained, providing **dimensional stability** and resistance to sag.
2. Mechanical properties such as **stiffness, impact strength, and barrier performance** are superior compared to melt-formed parts.
3. Crystallinity can be **enhanced post-form** by annealing, increasing heat resistance (e.g., CPET trays).
4. Wall-thickness distribution is **more uniform**, reducing material wastage.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**State the temperature window for SPPF of Polypropylene (PP) and justify it.**

**Model Answer:**
For PP: $T_g = -10\,^\circ\text{C}$ and $T_m = 165\,^\circ\text{C}$.

The SPPF operating window is:

$$T_g < T_{form} < T_m$$

Using the empirical optimum formula:

$$T_{form,opt} = T_g + 0.75(T_m - T_g)$$

Substituting values:

$$T_{form,opt} = -10 + 0.75(165 - (-10)) = -10 + 131.25 = 121.25\,^\circ\text{C}$$

Hence PP is SPPF-formed in the range **120 °C – 155 °C**. Operating in this range keeps crystallites intact, giving high gloss, stiffness, and impact strength in the final container.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students choose ONE of two alternatives per module. Each 14-mark question typically carries **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) Explain the Solid Phase Pressure Forming process with a neat flow diagram. List any four polymers suitable for the process. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Process Steps:**

1. **Sheet Loading:** A flat extruded or calendered sheet of semi-crystalline polymer (PP, HDPE, PET, PVC) is loaded into the clamp frame of the forming machine.
2. **Heating:** The sheet is transported into a heating station equipped with **top and bottom IR heaters**. The sheet temperature is raised to between $T_g$ and $T_m$ (e.g., 120 °C for PP). Heating is bi-axial to ensure uniform temperature throughout the thickness.
3. **Temperature Equilibration:** The sheet dwells for a calculated time (based on Fourier heat conduction) so that the centre-line temperature matches the surface temperature within $\pm 2\,^\circ\text{C}$.
4. **Forming:** The heated sheet is transferred over the mold cavity. Compressed air at **0.5–1.5 MPa** (or a plug-assist followed by air pressure) pushes the sheet into the female mold.
5. **Cooling in Mold:** Chilled water circulating through the mold channels cools the formed part while pressure is maintained. Cooling continues until the part temperature drops below $T_g$, locking in the geometry.
6. **Demolding:** The formed part is ejected from the mold cavity.
7. **Trimming:** A steel-rule die cuts the flange, producing the final packaging container.

**Suitable Polymers (any four):**
* **Polypropylene (PP)** — yogurt cups, margarine tubs
* **High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE)** — rigid containers
* **Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET, APET/CPET)** — ovenable trays
* **Rigid Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)** — blister packs
* **Polycarbonate (PC)** — specialty reusable containers

**(b) Derive the wall-thickness distribution for a hemispherical dome formed from a circular blank. A 1.2 mm PP sheet is formed into a hemispherical dome. Calculate the final thickness at the pole. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Derivation already shown in Section 3.1.*

**Given:** $t_0 = 1.2\,\text{mm}$, hemispherical dome ($D_r = 1$).

**Apply the formula:**

$$t_1 = \frac{t_0}{\sqrt{1 + D_r^2}}$$

**Substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= \frac{1.2}{\sqrt{1 + 1^2}} \\
&= \frac{1.2}{\sqrt{2}} \\
&= \frac{1.2}{1.4142} \\
&= 0.8485\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The pole thickness of the formed dome is **≈ 0.85 mm**, representing a **~29% reduction** from the original sheet.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often forget that **volume conservation** assumes no material flows into the flange. In real SPPF, ~10–15% material remains as flange trim, making actual $t_1$ slightly **lower** than calculated. Always state this assumption.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**

**(a) Compare Solid Phase Pressure Forming with conventional melt-phase thermoforming. Highlight at least six distinguishing features. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | SPPF | Melt-Phase Thermoforming |
|---------|------|--------------------------|
| Operating temperature | Below $T_m$ | At/near $T_m$ |
| Sheet sag | Minimal (crystallites support) | High (sheet droops) |
| Dimensional tolerance | **±0.05 mm** | ±0.15 mm |
| Post-form crystallinity | **Preserved/enhanced** | Reduced |
| Mechanical strength | High | Moderate |
| Cycle time | Slightly longer | Shorter |
| Energy consumption | **Lower** | Higher |
| Suitable polymers | Semi-crystalline (PP, HDPE, PET) | Mainly amorphous (PS, PVC, ABS) |
| Wall thickness uniformity | Excellent | Variable, prone to thinning |
| Application | Pharma, dairy, ovenable trays | Disposable cups, trays, lids |

**(b) A plug-assist system uses a cylindrical plug of diameter 80 mm. If the forming pressure is 1.2 MPa, calculate the plug force. If the sheet is heated from 25 °C to 130 °C, estimate the heating time for a 1.5 mm PP sheet. Assume $\rho = 905\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 1925\,\text{J/kg·K}$, $k = 0.18\,\text{W/m·K}$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Plug Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{plug} &= P_f \cdot A_{plug} \\
&= P_f \cdot \pi \cdot r_{plug}^{\,2} \\
&= 1.2 \times 10^6 \cdot \pi \cdot (0.04)^2 \\
&= 1.2 \times 10^6 \cdot 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 6032\,\text{N} \\
&\approx 6.03\,\text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part 2 — Heating Time:**

Apply the characteristic heating time formula:

$$t_{heat} = \frac{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot t_0^2}{4 k}$$

**Substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{heat} &= \frac{905 \cdot 1925 \cdot (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4 \cdot 0.18} \\
&= \frac{905 \cdot 1925 \cdot 2.25 \times 10^{-6}}{0.72} \\
&= \frac{3.920 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 2.25}{0.72} \quad\text{[Note: regrouping]} \\
&= \frac{1.742 \times 10^{6} \cdot 2.25 \times 10^{-6}}{0.72} \\
&= \frac{3.920}{0.72} \\
&= 5.44\,\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The plug force is **~6.03 kN**, and the heating time required is **~5.4 seconds** to bring a 1.5 mm PP sheet to forming temperature.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently confuse **plate thickness** in the heating formula — the formula uses **half-thickness** implicitly. If your professor uses $t_0$ as half-thickness, the denominator becomes $k$ instead of $4k$. Always state your convention.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** SPPF forms sheets **below $T_m$ but above $T_g$**, preserving the crystalline phase.
- **Polymer preference:** **Semi-crystalline polymers** (PP, HDPE, PET) dominate SPPF applications.
- **Temperature window:** $T_g < T_{form} < T_m$ — never melt the sheet.
- **Optimum forming temperature:** $T_{form,opt} = T_g + 0.75(T_m - T_g)$.
- **Forming pressures:** **0.3–1.5 MPa** — far higher than vacuum-only forming (~0.1 MPa).
- **Plug-assist formula:** $F_{plug} = P_f \cdot A_{plug}$.
- **Wall-thinning formula:** $t_1 = \dfrac{t_0}{\sqrt{1 + D_r^2}}$.
- **Heating time:** $t_{heat} = \dfrac{\rho c_p t_0^2}{4 k}$.
- **Pressure advantages:** Enables **deeper draws**, better material distribution, and reduced webbing.
- **Key applications:** Pharmaceutical **blister packs**, dairy cups, **CPET ovenable trays**, coffee capsules.
- **Cycle efficiency:** Cooling dominates; faster cooling = shorter cycle = higher productivity.
- **Energy saving:** Operating below $T_m$ saves **15–25%** energy compared to melt-phase forming.
- **Mold materials:** Usually **cast aluminum** for SPPF — high thermal conductivity for rapid cooling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Mantra for KTU Exam:**
> *"Below the melt, above the glass — pressure does the rest."* — This single sentence captures the essence of Solid Phase Pressure Forming.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Skin Packaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Skin Packaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Skin Packaging** is a specialized variant of *thermoform-fill-seal* packaging in which a thin, heat-softened plastic film is draped directly over a product resting on a porous substrate (typically a printed paperboard or card). A vacuum is then applied through the porous substrate, evacuating the air trapped between the film and the product, causing atmospheric pressure to force the softened film to conform tightly to the contours of the product. The film bonds to the substrate, creating a secure, transparent, form-fitting "second skin" around the article.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *Skin packaging is a vacuum-forming technique where a heated thermoplastic film is drawn over a product placed on a porous backing card, conforming to the product's shape by evacuation of air through the card, and sealing to the card by means of an adhesive coating or heat-seal layer.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine wrapping a fragile gift (say, a watch) in clear cling film stretched tightly over a piece of cardboard. The cling film takes the exact shape of the watch — every curve, button, and lug is visible. Now imagine that the cling film is sealed to the cardboard around the watch, and the package is so tight that you can see the watch's serial number through the plastic. That is **skin packaging**.

> **Key Distinction from a Cling Film Wrap:** In skin packaging, vacuum (not manual stretching) provides the conforming force, and the bond is permanent to a rigid backing — not just wrapped around.

### 1.3 Industry Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Skin Film Thickness** | **50 µm – 200 µm** | Usually LDPE, Surlyn®, or EVA-based |
| **Backing Card** | **250 – 600 gsm** (porous, coated) | Provides rigidity & display surface |
| **Vacuum Level** | **600 – 760 mm Hg** (≈ 80–100 kPa absolute vacuum) | Drives film conformation |
| **Heater Temperature** | **120 °C – 180 °C** | Film-specific (Surlyn® ≈ 140 °C) |
| **Cycle Time** | **8 – 25 seconds** | Production throughput driver |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** A defining feature of skin packaging is the **direct contact between the plastic film and the product** with **no air gap**, distinguishing it sharply from *blister packaging* where a pre-formed pocket holds the product with deliberate clearance.

### 1.4 Historical Context

Skin packaging was popularized in the **1960s** by the DuPont Company using **Surlyn® ionomer resin** (ethylene-based with sodium/zinc ion crosslinks). The technique revolutionized retail packaging of small hardware, electronics components, and DIY items.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physical Principles

The skin packaging process is governed by **three sequential physical phenomena**:

1. **Thermal Softening of the Film:** The thermoplastic film is heated above its *glass transition temperature (Tg)* or near its *melting point (Tm)*, depending on whether it is amorphous (e.g., PVC) or semi-crystalline (e.g., LDPE, Surlyn®). The modulus drops by **2–3 orders of magnitude**, allowing the film to deform under stress.

2. **Vacuum-Induced Drape:** Once the film is in a softened, rubbery state, the air between the film and the porous card is evacuated. The **pressure differential** pushes the film onto the product and card. The film acts essentially as a **stretched membrane under uniform atmospheric load**.

3. **Adhesive / Heat-Seal Bonding:** The film is bonded to the card either by:
   - A pre-applied **adhesive layer** on the card (water-based or hot-melt), or
   - **Direct heat-seal** when the film itself contains a sealing layer (e.g., Surlyn®).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Process Logic (Bulleted)

**Step 1 — Loading:** The product is placed on a porous backing card (the "skin board"). The card is pre-printed with product information and has a sealing-coat or adhesive layer where bonding to the film is desired.

**Step 2 — Film Indexing:** A roll of skin film is unrolled and clamped into a frame above the platen. The film is typically **1–2 cm larger** than the card on all sides to allow sealing margin.

**Step 3 — Heating:** The film passes over a **radiant heater** (typically a bank of infrared quartz heaters or a flat ceramic heater). The film reaches its forming temperature in **5–10 seconds**.

**Step 4 — Platen Rise & Vacuum Application:** The platen carrying the card (with the product) rises to within **2–5 cm** of the heated film. Vacuum is then applied through the platen. Air rushes out through the porous card, and atmospheric pressure presses the soft film down onto the product and card.

**Step 5 — Cooling & Setting:** Vacuum is maintained while the film cools. Crystallization (in semi-crystalline films) or vitrification (in amorphous films) restores rigidity, locking the film in the conformed shape.

**Step 6 — Die Cutting / Trimming:** The excess film around the card edges is trimmed, yielding a finished skin pack.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Engineering Parameters)

| # | Parameter / Equation | Symbol | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Net Forming Force on Film** | $F = \Delta P \times A$ | $N$ | Where $\Delta P$ is pressure differential and $A$ is film area |
| 2 | **Pressure Differential (vacuum)** | $\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$ | $Pa$ | $P_{atm} \approx 101.325 \, kPa$ |
| 3 | **Heat Required to Soften Film** | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $J$ | $m$ = film mass, $c_p$ = specific heat, $\Delta T$ = temperature rise |
| 4 | **Strain on Conformed Film** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{L_{wrap} - L_{flat}}{L_{flat}}$ | dimensionless | Wrapping strain (must stay below yield) |
| 5 | **Film Area Utilization** | $\eta_A = \dfrac{A_{product}}{A_{card}}$ | % | Material efficiency indicator |
| 6 | **Cycle Time Index** | $t_c = t_h + t_v + t_{cool}$ | $s$ | $t_h$ = heat, $t_v$ = vacuum, $t_{cool}$ = cooling |
| 7 | **Throughput** | $N = \dfrac{3600}{t_c \times n_{up}}$ | packs/hr | $n_{up}$ = products per cycle (usually 1) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Equation 1 ($F = \Delta P \cdot A$) explains why a larger product area requires a tighter vacuum seal. Equation 7 is critical for **production economics** — minimizing $t_{cool}$ (using Surlyn® with rapid crystallization) is the main lever for high-speed skin packaging lines.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Retail Hardware:** Wall hooks, drill bits, fasteners — visible product, low cost.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Unit-dose compliance blister-skin packs for clinical trials.
- **Automotive Aftermarket:** Replacement parts display packaging (gaskets, bearings).
- **Electronics:** Memory modules, cables, accessories.
- **Museum & Archival:** Custom-fit protective enclosures for irregular artifacts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Net Conformational Force on the Skin Film

Consider a rectangular skin film of dimensions $L \times W$ draped over a product on a card. When vacuum is applied below the film, atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$ acts on the top surface while the pressure underneath drops to $P_{vac}$.

**Step 1 — Define the pressure differential:**

$$
\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vac}
$$

> **Explanation:** This is the net pressure pushing the film downward. The greater the vacuum (i.e., the lower $P_{vac}$), the larger the $\Delta P$.

**Step 2 — Express the total force on the film:**

$$
F = \Delta P \times A_{film}
$$

where $A_{film} = L \times W$.

> **Explanation:** Force = Pressure × Area, by Newton's definition. This force is the **driving pressure** that conforms the film to the product geometry.

**Step 3 — Substituting $A_{film}$:**

$$
F = \Delta P \times L \times W
$$

**Step 4 — Energy stored in the film as it wraps:**

The film acts as a stretched membrane. As it conforms, it stores **elastic strain energy** $U$. For a linear elastic membrane of thickness $t$ and modulus $E$:

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sigma \cdot \varepsilon \cdot V
$$

where $V = A_{film} \cdot t$, $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$, and $\varepsilon$ is the local strain.

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot (E \varepsilon) \cdot \varepsilon \cdot (A_{film} \cdot t) \\
  &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot E \cdot \varepsilon^{2} \cdot A_{film} \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** For a given film material, minimizing $\varepsilon$ (e.g., using a thicker film or smaller product) reduces stored energy and thus the risk of film "spring-back" or tearing.

**Step 6 — Equating work done by pressure to strain energy (quasi-static approximation):**

$$
F \cdot \delta = U
$$

$$
(\Delta P \cdot A_{film}) \cdot \delta = \frac{1}{2} E \varepsilon^{2} A_{film} t
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the maximum allowable strain (a useful design check):**

$$
\varepsilon_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P \cdot \delta}{E \cdot t}}
$$

> **Conclusion:** This is a **useful design rule** — the maximum strain the film can experience is determined by the vacuum level, the displacement, the film's Young's modulus, and its thickness.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison with Adjacent Technologies

| Attribute | **Skin Packaging** | **Blister Packaging** | **Shrink Wrapping** | **Stretch Wrapping** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Product Containment** | Form-fit (no clearance) | Pre-formed pocket (with clearance) | Loose sleeve, shrunk | Wrapped under tension, no heat |
| **Backing** | Porous card (essential) | Cardboard or foil lid | None | None |
| **Forming Force** | Vacuum (≈ 80–100 kPa ΔP) | Vacuum + plug assist | Heat shrinkage (no vacuum) | Mechanical stretch |
| **Film Thickness** | 50–200 µm | 200–500 µm | 15–50 µm | 17–35 µm |
| **Production Speed** | 6–15 cycles/min | 10–30 cycles/min | 20–60 packs/min | Up to 100+ pallets/hr |
| **Capital Cost** | Low–Medium | Medium–High | Low | Very Low |
| **Best For** | Irregular 3-D retail items | Tablets, capsules, small components | Boxed consumer goods | Pallet load stabilization |
| **Visible Product** | Full 360° visibility | Limited (through pocket) | Full 360° visibility | Limited (opaque film common) |
| **Anti-Theft** | High (film cannot be resealed) | High | Moderate | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 examiners frequently test the **distinction between skin and blister packaging**. Memorize that **skin packaging uses a porous card and has no air gap**, while **blister uses a thermoformed cavity with deliberate clearance**.

### 3.3 Material Selection Matrix

| Component | Material Options | Key Properties Required |
|---|---|---|
| **Skin Film** | LDPE, Surlyn® (ionomer), EVA, PVC | Optical clarity, drape, tear resistance, sealability |
| **Backing Card** | Clay-coated SBS, corrugated fiberboard | Porosity (vacuum passage), printability, stiffness |
| **Adhesive Layer** | Water-based EVA, hot-melt EVA, PVA | Heat activation temp, bond strength to film & ink |
| **Release Coating** (optional) | Silicone | Prevents film sticking where not desired |

> **Why Surlyn® dominates premium skin packaging:** Surlyn® ionomer has **exceptional optical clarity, deep-draw toughness, and oil/grease resistance**, allowing it to conform around sharp-cornered hardware without puncturing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Place product on porous card] --> B[Load card onto vacuum platen]
    B --> C[Index skin film from roll above platen]
    C --> D[Activate IR radiant heaters]
    D --> E{Heater timer expires 5-10 s}
    E -- No --> D
    E -- Yes --> F[Raise platen to 2-5 cm below film]
    F --> G[Open vacuum valve]
    G --> H[Evacuate air through porous card]
    H --> I[Atmospheric pressure forces film onto product]
    I --> J[Hold vacuum during cooling 3-8 s]
    J --> K[Release vacuum]
    K --> L[Lower platen and remove sealed card]
    L --> M[Trim excess film via die cut]
    M --> N[End: Finished skin pack]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style N fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    style G fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ff9800,color:#000
    style I fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ff9800,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Subsystem]
        A1[Film Roll Unwind]
        A2[Card Magazine Feeder]
        A3[Product Manual Placement or Pick-and-Place]
    end

    subgraph HEATING[Heating Subsystem]
        B1[IR Quartz Heater Bank]
        B2[Pyrometer / Thermocouple Feedback]
        B3[Film Temperature Controller]
    end

    subgraph FORMING[Vacuum Forming Subsystem]
        C1[Vacuum Pump Receiver Tank]
        C2[Vacuum Solenoid Valve]
        C3[Porous Vacuum Platen]
        C4[Rising Platen Actuator]
    end

    subgraph SEAL[Sealing and Cooling Subsystem]
        D1[Sealing Frame Clamps]
        D2[Fan Cooled Plenum]
        D3[Cycle Timer PLC]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Subsystem]
        E1[Die Cutting Station]
        E2[Trim Reel Winder]
        E3[Conveyor Outfeed]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> C3
    A3 --> C3
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B1
    C3 --> C2
    C2 --> C1
    C4 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E1 --> E3

    style HEATING fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57c00,color:#000
    style FORMING fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2,color:#000
    style SEAL fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#7b1fa2,color:#000
    style OUTPUT fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#388e3c,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Schematic of a Skin Packaging Machine (Block Form)

| Stage | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Film Supply** | Unwind stand, tension control, edge guides | Provide flat, tensioned film |
| **Heating Zone** | IR heater, reflector housing, shutters | Soften film to forming temperature |
| **Forming Zone** | Platen, vacuum channels, product holder | Conform film to product |
| **Sealing Zone** | Sealing frame, cooled platen | Bond film to card, set shape |
| **Trimming Zone** | Die cutter, scrap winder | Cut finished pack from web |
| **Control** | PLC, HMI, sensors | Sequence and monitor cycle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Diagrams:** When asked to draw a skin packaging machine, students are expected to label **at least: heater, film, product, porous card, vacuum platen, and vacuum line**. A diagram with these six labels scores full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define skin packaging. Mention any two materials used as skin film.**

**Model Answer:**
Skin packaging is a thermoforming process in which a heated thermoplastic film is draped over a product placed on a porous backing card. A vacuum is applied through the porous card, evacuating the air beneath the film and forcing it to conform tightly to the product's contours. The film bonds to the card, forming a transparent, form-fitting package.

**Two materials used as skin film:**
1. **Surlyn® (Ionomer resin)** – DuPont's ethylene-based ionomer offering exceptional clarity, toughness, and deep-draw capability.
2. **LDPE (Low-Density Polyethylene)** – Cost-effective film widely used for non-demanding retail applications.

*(Acceptable alternatives: EVA copolymer, PVC, Polyethylene blends.)* — **3 marks**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between skin packaging and blister packaging in terms of (i) backing and (ii) product fit.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Skin Packaging | Blister Packaging |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Backing** | Uses a **porous paperboard card** that allows air evacuation through it. | Uses a **pre-formed plastic pocket** sealed to a card or foil lid. The card is not porous. |
| **(ii) Product Fit** | Film conforms **directly to the product** with **no air gap** (true form-fit). | Product sits **inside a pre-formed cavity** with **deliberate clearance** for easy insertion/removal. |

— **3 marks**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Module 4 Internal Choice (As per KTU ESE Pattern):**
> *Answer any ONE full question from this module.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]*

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat labeled diagram, explain the **step-by-step process of skin packaging**. Discuss the role of the porous backing card. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The skin packaging process consists of the following steps:

1. **Loading the Product:** The product is manually placed (or pick-and-place loaded) on a porous backing card positioned on a vacuum platen.

2. **Film Indexing:** A roll of thermoplastic skin film is unwound and held in a clamping frame above the platen.

3. **Heating:** Infrared radiant heaters raise the film temperature to its **forming temperature** (typically 120–180 °C). The film softens and becomes rubbery.

4. **Platen Elevation & Film Drape:** The platen rises, bringing the product within 2–5 cm of the heated film. The soft film sags slightly and drapes over the product.

5. **Vacuum Application:** A vacuum pump evacuates air through the porous platen and the porous card. The pressure differential (≈ 80–100 kPa) forces the soft film tightly against the product and the card.

6. **Sealing & Cooling:** The film bonds to the card (via adhesive or heat-seal layer) and cools, restoring its rigidity. Vacuum is held during cooling to lock in the conformation.

7. **Trimming:** The finished pack is removed and excess film is trimmed off via a die cutter.

**Role of the Porous Backing Card (3 key roles):**
- **Air evacuation pathway** — allows vacuum to reach the underside of the film.
- **Rigid substrate** — provides dimensional stability and product display.
- **Printable surface** — serves as the graphic/label area for branding and product information.

*(Diagram: draw a labeled figure with heater on top, film below heater, product on porous card, vacuum platen, and vacuum line going to pump.)* — **7 marks**

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss the **materials used for skin film and backing card** in detail. Compare **Surlyn®** with **LDPE** as skin film materials. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Skin Film Materials:**
- **Surlyn® (Ionomer):** Ethylene-methacrylic acid copolymer neutralized with sodium or zinc ions. Excellent clarity, high tensile strength, deep-draw toughness, and good sealability. Premium choice.
- **LDPE (Low-Density Polyethylene):** Cost-effective, good clarity, fair drawability. Used for less-demanding applications.
- **EVA (Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate):** Good low-temperature flexibility and seal strength.
- **PVC:** Used historically; offers good drape and clarity but environmental concerns limit its use.

**Backing Card Materials:**
- **Clay-coated Solid Bleached Sulfate (SBS):** Smooth, printable, adequately porous.
- **Corrugated fiberboard:** For heavier products.
- **Specialty porous boards:** With controlled porosity for consistent vacuum.

**Surlyn® vs LDPE Comparison:**

| Property | Surlyn® (Ionomer) | LDPE |
|---|---|---|
| Optical Clarity | Excellent (glassy) | Good (slightly hazy) |
| Tensile Strength | High | Moderate |
| Deep-Draw Toughness | Excellent | Fair |
| Puncture Resistance | Excellent | Moderate |
| Oil/Grease Resistance | Excellent | Poor |
| Cost | High (2–4× LDPE) | Low |
| Typical Applications | Hardware, electronics | Light retail, hobby items |

**Conclusion:** Surlyn® is preferred for premium, sharp-cornered products requiring deep-draw and clarity. LDPE is chosen where cost is paramount and product geometry is mild. — **7 marks**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**Q. (a)** Explain the **principle of skin packaging** with reference to **atmospheric pressure and film conformation**. Derive the expression for the **net forming force** on the skin film. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
Skin packaging is governed by the **pressure differential principle**. When the air between the heated, softened film and the porous card is evacuated by a vacuum pump, the atmospheric pressure acting on the top surface of the film is no longer balanced. This unbalanced atmospheric pressure pushes the film down onto the product and card, causing the film to drape and conform to the product's geometry. As the film cools under vacuum, it sets in the new shape and bonds to the card.

**Derivation of Net Forming Force:**

The net forming force $F$ on the film is the product of the pressure differential $\Delta P$ and the effective film area $A$:

**Step 1:** Atmospheric pressure acts on the upper surface: $P_{atm} \approx 101.325 \, kPa$.

**Step 2:** Pressure below the film drops to $P_{vac}$ (typically 1–20 kPa absolute).

**Step 3:** Pressure differential:

$$
\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vac}
$$

**Step 4:** Net force:

$$
F = \Delta P \cdot A
$$

where $A = L \times W$ is the film area.

**Step 5:** This force drives the film to conform. It must overcome the film's elastic resistance but not exceed its tear strength, giving a usable operating window.

— **7 marks**

---

**Q. (b)** List the **advantages and limitations of skin packaging**. Compare it briefly with **shrink wrapping** in terms of forming mechanism and appearance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages of Skin Packaging:**
1. **Excellent product visibility** — 360° clear display.
2. **Anti-theft / tamper-evident** — film cannot be resealed once cut.
3. **Low material cost** — thin films (50–200 µm) and inexpensive cards.
4. **Conforms to irregular shapes** — no pre-formed mold needed.
5. **Short tooling changeover** — no cavity molds; just die cutters.
6. **Lightweight** — reduces shipping cost vs. rigid blister or box.

**Limitations of Skin Packaging:**
1. **Limited product protection** — film offers minimal cushioning or barrier.
2. **Restricted to lightweight products** — typically < 2 kg.
3. **Card porosity requirements** — limits card material options.
4. **Film is not re-sealable** — once opened, package is destroyed.
5. **Slower than shrink wrapping** for high-volume applications.
6. **Adhesive / seal failures** possible under humidity.

**Comparison with Shrink Wrapping:**

| Aspect | Skin Packaging | Shrink Wrapping |
|---|---|---|
| **Forming Mechanism** | **Vacuum + atmospheric pressure** on porous card | **Heat-induced shrinkage** of pre-stretched film; no vacuum, no card |
| **Backing** | Required (porous card) | None (free-standing sleeve) |
| **Final Appearance** | Form-fit, tight, anchored to card | Loose "second skin" sleeve around product |
| **Display** | Product visible, secured to a card | Product fully enclosed, often loose inside the sleeve |

— **7 marks**

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Skin Packaging Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing skin packaging with blister packaging** — the *porous card* and *no air gap* are the two defining features. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if these are omitted.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of the porous card** — students often describe only the film and skip the substrate, losing **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Not mentioning atmospheric pressure as the conforming force** — it is the *unbalanced atmospheric pressure* (not just "vacuum") that does the work. A subtle but frequently tested point.
> 4. **Skipping the heating step** — skin packaging involves a *thermal softening* stage, unlike cold-form blister or stretch wrapping. Mentioning film heating is essential.
> 5. **Missing units in numerical answers** — Pressure must be in **Pa or kPa**, not just "vacuum".

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Skin packaging** = heated film + vacuum + **porous card** + atmospheric pressure = form-fit transparent package.
- ✅ The **porous backing card** is *not optional* — it is the vacuum pathway and the display surface.
- ✅ **No air gap** between film and product — this is the key distinction from blister packaging.
- ✅ **Surlyn® ionomer** is the premium skin film; **LDPE** is the economical choice.
- ✅ Driving force = **pressure differential** $\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$ acting over the film area.
- ✅ Typical cycle: **8–25 seconds** (heat + vacuum + cool).
- ✅ Three sequential physical phenomena: **thermal softening → vacuum drape → adhesive/seal bonding**.
- ✅ Best suited for **lightweight retail items, hardware, electronics, and pharmaceutical unit doses**.
- ✅ Limitations: minimal cushioning, no re-seal, card porosity constraints.
- ✅ Exam-grade distinction to remember: **Skin = porous card + no air gap**; **Blister = cavity + air gap**; **Shrink = heat + no card**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermoforming Moulds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thermoforming Moulds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermoforming Moulds** are precision-engineered tooling components used in the thermoforming process, where a thermoplastic sheet (pre-heated to its softening/plasticization temperature) is mechanically or pneumatically forced against or into the mould cavity to take its final three-dimensional shape. The mould defines the geometry, surface texture, dimensional accuracy, and structural integrity of the finished packaging component.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEPOT748 - Plastics Packaging Technology (Module 4)**, thermoforming moulds are categorized based on:
- **Gender (Male/Female/Matched)**
- **Construction Material (Aluminium, Epoxy, Wood, Steel, Kirksite)**
- **Production Volume (Prototype vs. Production)**
