- **Function (Forming, Trimming, Stacking)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Unlike injection moulds, thermoforming moulds typically **do not require high tonnage clamping forces** because the plastic pressure in thermoforming is very low (typically **0.3 to 1.0 bar / 30 to 100 kPa**). This allows cheaper mould materials to be used.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **flat, soft slice of mozzarella cheese** (the heated plastic sheet) and you want to drape it over a **convex bowl upside down** versus pressing it **into a concave bowl**:

- **Drape over a convex shape** (like a hill) → this is a **MALE MOULD (Positive mould)**. The plastic wraps around the outside of the mould.
- **Press into a concave cavity** (like a bowl) → this is a **FEMALE MOULD (Negative mould)**. The plastic is sucked into a hollow.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Trick:** *"Male sticks OUT, Female caves IN"*. The plastic in female moulds touches the **outer walls of the cavity**, while in male moulds it touches the **outer surface of the plug**.

In real packaging applications:
- **Female moulds** are used for **thin-walled disposable cups, yogurt containers, and trays** (most common).
- **Male moulds** are used for products requiring **better surface finish on the outside**, like refrigerator liners and vehicle dashboards.
- **Matched moulds (dies)** are used for **high-precision items** such as rigid food packaging lids.

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

The following constants and metrics are repeatedly asked in KTU examinations and should be memorized:

- **Forming Pressure:** **0.3 – 1.0 MPa** (vacuum + compressed air assist)
- **Mould Temperature Range:** **40 °C to 90 °C** for most polyolefins
- **Standard Draft Angle:** **1° to 3°** (minimum), can go up to **5° to 7°** for deep draws
- **Corner Radius (Inside):** Minimum **1.0 × sheet thickness** (for PS, PP)
- **Shrinkage Allowance for Thermoformed Parts:** **0.5% to 1.5%** (linear)
- **Sheet Heating Temperature:** Typically **120 °C to 180 °C** above glass transition (amorphous) or melting point (semi-crystalline)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Thermoforming Moulds (Structured Logic)

### A) Based on Gender / Configuration

**1. Female (Negative / Cavity) Mould**
- Sheet is **dragged INTO** a recessed cavity.
- The **outside surface** of the part gets the best finish (contact with plug assist or untextured air side).
- Used for **cups, tubs, clamshells, blister packs**.
- Sheet thinning is concentrated at the **corners and base**.

**2. Male (Positive / Plug) Mould**
- Sheet is **forced OVER** a raised protrusion.
- The **inside surface** of the part gets the best finish (mould-side contact).
- Used for **large-area items** like refrigerator door liners, pallets, and bath tubs.
- Sheet thinning is on the **side walls** (not the bottom).

**3. Matched Moulds (Die + Counter-die)**
- Two mould halves close like a clamshell on the heated sheet.
- Gives **uniform wall thickness** and **tight tolerances**.
- Used for **precision lids and thin-gauge rigid packaging**.
- Higher tooling cost.

### B) Based on Construction Material

| Mould Material | Cost (Relative) | Thermal Conductivity (W/m·K) | Best For | Life-Cycle |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Aluminium (Cast/Machined)** | High | **~205** | Production runs, food contact | **>1,000,000 cycles** |
| **Epoxy / Fibre-Reinforced Plastic (FRP)** | Low | **~0.3** | Short runs, prototypes | **~10,000 cycles** |
| **Hardwood (Maple/Mahogany)** | Very Low | **~0.16** | Prototypes, low volume | **~1,000 cycles** |
| **Steel (Machined)** | Very High | **~45** | Very high volume + abrasive materials | **>5,000,000 cycles** |
| **Kirksite (Zn-Al Alloy)** | Medium | **~120** | Medium volume, good detail | **~100,000 cycles** |
| **3D-Printed (SLA/FDM)** | Variable | **~0.2** | Rapid prototyping | **<500 cycles** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Aluminium dominates thermoforming tooling:** Its high thermal conductivity (**205 W/m·K**) ensures **rapid heat extraction** from the plastic sheet, which **shortens cycle time** — the most critical economic factor in thermoforming.

### C) Based on Production Volume

- **Prototype / Soft Tooling:** Wood, epoxy, 3D-printed polymers, low-cost cast aluminium.
- **Bridge Tooling:** Machined aluminium with limited features.
- **Production / Hard Tooling:** Fully machined, temperature-controlled aluminium or steel with integrated water lines.

## 2.2 Critical Mould Design Parameters (The 'Why' Behind Each)

1. **Draft Angle (Taper):**
   - **Why:** Allows the cooled, slightly-shrunk plastic part to be **ejected without jamming** or scuffing.
   - **Standard:** 1° to 3° per side for shallow draws; up to **7° for deep draws > 150 mm**.

2. **Corner Radii:**
   - **Why:** Sharp internal corners cause **stress concentration**, **thinning**, and **mould-induced brittleness**.
   - **Rule of Thumb:** Internal radius $\geq$ **1.0 × t** (sheet thickness), External radius $\geq$ **1.5 × t**.

3. **Venting:**
   - **Why:** Trapped air between the sheet and mould cavity prevents full material contact, causing **blowholes, voids, and loss of surface detail**.
   - **Solution:** Micro-channels (**0.05 – 0.10 mm deep**) machined into the cavity surface, or use of **porous aluminium / sintered bronze vents**.

4. **Cooling Channels:**
   - **Why:** Faster cooling = **shorter cycle time** = higher productivity.
   - **Placement:** Ideally within **1.5 × channel diameter** from the mould cavity surface.

5. **Shrinkage Compensation:**
   - **Why:** Thermoplastics contract as they cool (especially semi-crystalline ones like **PP and PET**).
   - **Mould Compensation:** Mould is made **oversize by the shrinkage percentage** of the chosen material.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **must-know formulas** for the KTU examination. These are derived from the *Sophisticated Thermoforming Design Guidelines* and are consistent with the **ASTM D638 / D882** standard testing approaches.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linear Shrinkage** | $S_L = \dfrac{L_m - L_p}{L_m} \times 100$ | $L_m$ = mould dim., $L_p$ = part dim. (mm) | Mould compensation |
| 2 | **Wall Thickness (Female Mould, bottom)** | $t_b = t_0 \cdot \dfrac{A_0}{A_b}$ | $t_0$ = initial thickness, $A_0, A_b$ = areas (m²) | Predict thinning at base |
| 3 | **Wall Thickness (Male Mould, side)** | $t_s = t_0 \cdot \dfrac{A_0}{A_s}$ | $A_s$ = side-wall area (m²) | Predict side-wall thinning |
| 4 | **Draw Ratio (DR)** | $DR = \dfrac{h}{w} \quad \text{or} \quad \dfrac{\text{Depth}}{\text{Width}}$ | $h$ = depth, $w$ = width (mm) | Material selection limit |
| 5 | **Material Distribution Index** | $MDI = \dfrac{t_{min}}{t_{max}}$ | Ratio of min. to max. thickness | Quality check |
| 6 | **Draft Angle (Minimum)** | $\theta_{min} = \arctan\left(\dfrac{\Delta_s}{2h}\right)$ | $\Delta_s$ = total shrinkage, $h$ = depth | Ejectability check |
| 7 | **Cooling Time (Simplified)** | $t_c = \dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\left(\dfrac{T_m - T_{mould}}{T_e - T_{mould}}\right)$ | $s$ = thickness, $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity | Cycle-time estimation |
| 8 | **Forming Pressure (Vacuum)** | $P_v = P_{atm} - P_{cavity}$ | $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa | Pressure drop analysis |
| 9 | **Heat Required (Sheet)** | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $m$ = mass (kg), $c_p$ = heat capacity | Heater design |
| 10 | **Plug Force (Male mould)** | $F_p = P \cdot A_{plug}$ | $P$ = forming pressure, $A$ = area (m²) | Press tonnage check |

> [!TIP]
> **Note on KTU valuation:** When the table uses $\vert x \vert$ in the absolute-value sense, examiners will always accept $\lvert x \rvert$ as correct; never use the vertical pipe character `\|` inside the table cell as it breaks markdown rendering.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Thermoforming moulds are the **backbone of the global rigid plastics packaging industry**, which produces:
- **>500 billion units/year** of disposable plastic cups, trays, and clamshells.
- **Medical blister packaging** (PVC, PET, PP) for pills and devices.
- **Electronics trays** (ESD-safe thermoformed PS/PET).
- **Automotive interior panels** (ABS, TPO sheets).
- **Refrigerator liners** (HIPS, ABS) — male moulds of up to **2 m × 1 m**.

The choice of mould material and design directly impacts **tooling cost amortization, cycle time, part quality, and scrap rate** — making mould design a high-leverage decision in packaging engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are **fully expanded** with every algebraic step shown. No skips, no shortcuts.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linear Shrinkage Compensation for Mould Dimension

**Given:**
- Final desired part dimension: $L_p = 100$ mm
- Material: Polypropylene (PP), known linear shrinkage: $S_L = 1.5\%$

**Find:** Required mould dimension $L_m$

**Starting from the definition of linear shrinkage:**

$$S_L = \frac{L_m - L_p}{L_m} \times 100$$

**Step 1 — Convert percentage to decimal for easier algebra:**

$$s = \frac{S_L}{100} = \frac{1.5}{100} = 0.015$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the original equation:**

$$0.015 = \frac{L_m - L_p}{L_m}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply to eliminate the fraction:**

$$0.015 \cdot L_m = L_m - L_p$$

**Step 4 — Isolate $L_m$ terms on one side:**

$$L_m - 0.015 \cdot L_m = L_p$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $L_m$:**

$$L_m (1 - 0.015) = L_p$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the bracket:**

$$L_m (0.985) = L_p$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $L_m$:**

$$L_m = \frac{L_p}{0.985}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$L_m = \frac{100 \text{ mm}}{0.985}$$

**Step 9 — Final numerical result:**

$$L_m = 101.52 \text{ mm}$$

**Interpretation:** The mould cavity must be **oversize by 1.52 mm** on this 100 mm dimension so that, after PP shrinks by 1.5%, the part measures exactly 100 mm.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Wall Thickness Distribution in a Female Mould

**Given:**
- A circular female cavity of diameter $D = 80$ mm and depth $h = 40$ mm
- Initial sheet thickness: $t_0 = 1.0$ mm
- Forming method: Drape forming into the cavity

**Find:** Approximate wall thickness at the base of the cavity ($t_b$)

**Step 1 — Use the **constant-volume / constant-area** conservation principle (sheet material is incompressible during forming):**

$$A_0 \cdot t_0 = A_b \cdot t_b + A_s \cdot t_s$$

For a quick estimate at the base, assume most material is consumed in covering the side wall, and the base area equals the projected footprint of the cavity.

**Step 2 — Compute initial sheet area covering the cavity (flat disc of diameter D):**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.080)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the cavity base area (same as projected disc):**

$$A_b = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Use the **area-stretch** approximation for base thickness:**

$$t_b = t_0 \cdot \frac{A_0}{A_b}$$

**Step 5 — Plug in the numbers:**

$$t_b = 1.0 \text{ mm} \times \frac{5.027 \times 10^{-3}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 6 — Result for the base:**

$$t_b \approx 1.0 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{base remains near original thickness})$$

**Step 7 — Now compute the side-wall area (cylindrical wall, ignoring corner effects):**

$$A_s = \pi D h = \pi (0.080)(0.040) = 1.005 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 8 — Approximate side-wall thickness using the area-stretch equation:**

$$t_s = t_0 \cdot \frac{A_0}{A_s}$$

**Step 9 — Plug in numbers:**

$$t_s = 1.0 \text{ mm} \times \frac{5.027 \times 10^{-3}}{1.005 \times 10^{-2}}$$

**Step 10 — Final result:**

$$t_s = 0.500 \text{ mm}$$

**Interpretation:** The side walls are thinned to **half the original sheet thickness**, while the base retains its original gauge. This is **typical of female mould parts** and explains why deep-drawn cups have **stiff bottoms and flexible walls**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Draft Angle for Ejectability

**Given:**
- Cavity depth: $h = 60$ mm
- Total linear shrinkage: $\Delta_s = 0.9$ mm (i.e. 0.9 mm contraction along depth)

**Find:** Minimum draft angle $\theta_{min}$ so the part does not jam on ejection

**Step 1 — Visualize the geometry.** A part inside a straight-walled cavity of depth $h$ shrinks by $\Delta_s$ radially. To allow ejection, the side wall must taper by at least $\theta_{min}$ on each side, such that:

$$2 h \tan(\theta_{min}) \geq \Delta_s$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the draft angle:**

$$\theta_{min} = \arctan\left(\frac{\Delta_s}{2 h}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$\theta_{min} = \arctan\left(\frac{0.9}{2 \times 60}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the denominator:**

$$\theta_{min} = \arctan\left(\frac{0.9}{120}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Decimal evaluation:**

$$\theta_{min} = \arctan(0.0075)$$

**Step 6 — For small angles, $\arctan(x) \approx x$ in radians:**

$$\theta_{min} \approx 0.0075 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 7 — Convert radians to degrees (× 180/π):**

$$\theta_{min} = 0.0075 \times \frac{180}{\pi}$$

**Step 8 — Numerical result:**

$$\theta_{min} = 0.43^\circ$$

**Step 9 — Apply the safety factor used in industry (typically 2× to 3×):**

$$\theta_{design} = 2 \times 0.43^\circ \approx 0.86^\circ \quad (\text{round up to } 1^\circ)$$

**Interpretation:** A **1° draft** is the **practical minimum** for thermoformed packaging moulds. Designers often adopt **2° to 3°** for safety, especially for deep-draw packaging.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Mould Type

| Decision Criterion | Female (Cavity) Mould | Male (Plug) Mould | Matched Die |
|---|---|---|---|
| Part has **good outside surface** | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Fair | ✅ Excellent |
| Part has **good inside surface** | ❌ Fair | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Excellent |
| **Material distribution** | Thick base, thin walls | Thick walls, thin top | Uniform |
| **Trimming** | Easy (open top) | Easy (open bottom) | Requires matched trim dies |
| **Tooling cost** | Low | Low | High |
| **Cycle time** | Fast | Fast | Slowest (matched die close) |
| **Best for packaging** | Cups, tubs, trays, blister packs | Large panels, lids | Precision lids, containers |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mould Gender Configuration Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heated Plastic Sheet] --> B{Mould Type?}
    B -- Female Cavity --> C1[Sheet Drapes Into Cavity]
    C1 --> D1[Vacuum Pulls Sheet Down]
    D1 --> E1[Outside Surface Best Finish]
    B -- Male Plug --> C2[Sheet Stretches Over Plug]
    C2 --> D2[Vacuum Pulls Sheet Against Plug]
    D2 --> E2[Inside Surface Best Finish]
    B -- Matched Die --> C3[Two Halves Close on Sheet]
    C3 --> D3[Pressure from Both Sides]
    D3 --> E3[Uniform Wall Thickness]
    E1 --> F[Cool, Eject, Trim]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Finished Packaging Part]
```

```mermaid
    end

    end

```

```mermaid
    Q2 -- Yes --> M3[Cast Aluminium with Cooling]
```
