# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define "cushion factor" in foam packaging. How does it depend on foam density and static stress?**

**Model Answer:**

The **cushion factor (C)** of a foam is defined as the ratio of the **thickness of a rigid (non-cushioning) pad** that would absorb the same shock to the **thickness of the foam** that achieves the same peak deceleration:

$$C = \frac{T_{rigid}}{T_{foam}}$$

**Dependency:**
- **On density:** Higher-density foams have higher cushion factors because they are stiffer.
- **On static stress:** As static stress increases, the foam compresses more per unit load, increasing the cushion factor up to an optimum, beyond which it again rises as the foam bottoms out.

**(3 marks — definition 1, density relation 1, stress relation 1)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q: List any three advantages of Expanded Polystyrene (EPS) as a cushioning material. Mention one limitation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Excellent energy absorption** per unit weight due to closed-cell air-spring mechanism.
2. **Very low cost** and easy moldability into complex product-specific shapes.
3. **Good thermal insulation** (low thermal conductivity 0.030–0.038 W/m·K), making it dual-purpose.
4. **Lightweight** (density 10–50 kg/m³) reducing shipping costs.

**Limitation:**
- **Poor multi-drop performance** — cell walls fracture progressively under repeated impacts, leading to permanent set and reduced cushioning on subsequent drops.

**(3 marks — 1 each for two advantages, 0.5 each for two more + 1 for limitation, or 1+1+1 split)**

---

## PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain in detail the manufacturing process of **Expanded Polystyrene (EPS)** foam with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the role of **pentane** as a blowing agent. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **cushioning equation** relating peak deceleration, drop height, cushion thickness, and cushion factor. A product weighing **40 N** with a **fragility rating of 30 G** must survive a drop from **75 cm**. If the foam has **C = 3.0**, calculate the minimum cushion thickness with a **30% safety factor**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**EPS Manufacturing Steps:**

1. **Styrene Polymerization:** Suspension polymerization of styrene monomer produces PS beads of diameter 0.2–1.5 mm. During polymerization, **pentane (C₅H₁₂)** is added as the blowing agent (typically 5–8% by mass). Pentane is trapped inside the polymer matrix under pressure.

2. **Pre-Expansion:** The PS beads are exposed to **steam at 90–105 °C** in an expander. Heat softens the polymer, and the trapped pentane vaporizes, expanding each bead 20–50 times its original volume. Density drops from ~1050 kg/m³ to ~15–50 kg/m³.

3. **Aging/Maturation:** Expanded beads are stored in silos for 12–48 hours to equilibrate internal pentane pressure with atmospheric air — this prevents collapse during molding.

4. **Mold Filling:** Aged beads are blown into a steam-heated aluminum mold (typically 110–120 °C) and fused together using further steam injection. The heat re-softens the beads, allowing them to weld at contact points.

5. **Cooling & Demolding:** Steam is replaced by water cooling; the molded EPS block solidifies and is ejected.

6. **Trimming & Finishing:** Flash is trimmed, and the part is tested for density and dimensional accuracy.

**Role of Pentane:**

Pentane (boiling point 36 °C) acts as a **physical blowing agent** — it is dissolved in PS during polymerization, vaporizes on heating, and creates closed-cell voids. It is preferred over chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) because it is **non-ozone-depleting** and has low global warming potential.

**(Mark split: 5 process steps × 1 mark each = 5 marks, pentane explanation 2 marks = 7)**

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Derivation Outline:**

Starting from energy balance for free fall and foam compression:

$$\frac{1}{2} k x^2 = mg(h + x)$$

After algebraic manipulation (see Section 3.1), in the limit of large drop heights:

$$\boxed{G_{peak} = \frac{h}{t \cdot C}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- Drop height: $h = 75 \text{ cm} = 0.75 \text{ m}$
- Fragility: $G_{fragility} = 30$
- Cushion factor: $C = 3.0$
- Safety factor: $SF = 1.30$

Required minimum thickness (without safety factor):

$$t = \frac{h}{G \cdot C} = \frac{75}{30 \times 3.0} = \frac{75}{90} = 0.833 \text{ cm}$$

Applying safety factor:

$$t_{design} = SF \times t = 1.30 \times 0.833 = 1.083 \text{ cm}$$

**Recommended commercial specification:** $\boxed{t_{design} \approx 1.1 \text{ cm}}$

The **weight W = 40 N** is not needed for thickness calculation but would be used to find the cushion footprint area.

**(Mark split: derivation 3 marks, numerical 3 marks, units & safety factor 1 mark = 7)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Compare **EPS, XPS, and EPP** foams as cushioning materials under the heads: cell structure, density, multi-drop endurance, water absorption, recyclability, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a labeled schematic of a typical **cushioning curve**, explain how a packaging designer uses it to select foam density and thickness. A printer weighing **20 N** has a base area of **400 cm²** and a fragility of **40 G**. The drop height is **80 cm**. The foam's optimal static stress is **6 kPa** with **C = 2.8** at that stress. Determine the required foam thickness with a **20% safety factor**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | EPS | XPS | EPP |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|
| Cell structure | Closed-cell, expanded beads | Closed-cell, extruded | Closed-cell, expanded beads |
| Density (kg/m³) | 10 – 50 | 25 – 80 | 20 – 100 |
| Multi-drop endurance | Poor | Medium | Excellent |
| Water absorption | High | Very Low | Very Low |
| Recyclability | Good | Limited | Excellent |
| Typical applications | TV, appliance, fish boxes | Pharma insulated shippers | Reusable crates, automotive |

**Verbal Explanation:**

- **EPS** is the most common and cheapest but is brittle and absorbs water.
- **XPS** has a continuous extruded skin giving it moisture resistance, ideal for cold-chain packaging.
- **EPP** has the best elastic recovery and multi-drop durability, justifying its higher cost in reusable systems.

**(Mark split: comparison table 4 marks, verbal interpretation 3 marks = 7)**

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Cushioning Curve Schematic:**

A cushioning curve plots **Peak Deceleration (G)** on the y-axis vs **Static Stress (σₛ)** on the x-axis. Each curve represents one foam density. Curves are **U-shaped** with a minimum at the optimal stress.

**Designer's Procedure:**
1. Calculate static stress: $\sigma_s = W/A$
2. Locate the point on the chosen density curve.
3. Read the G-value.
4. Solve: $t = h/(G \cdot C)$ with safety factor applied.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- $W = 20 \text{ N}$
- $A = 400 \text{ cm}^2 = 400 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.04 \text{ m}^2$
- $G_{fragility} = 40$
- $h = 80 \text{ cm}$
- $C = 2.8$
- $SF = 1.20$

Static stress check:

$$\sigma_s = \frac{20}{0.04} = 500 \text{ Pa} = 0.5 \text{ kPa}$$

(Note: This is **lower** than the foam's optimal stress of 6 kPa — the designer must adjust cushion footprint by using a smaller contact area, or select a softer foam grade.)

Thickness:

$$t = \frac{h}{G \cdot C} = \frac{80}{40 \times 2.8} = \frac{80}{112} = 0.714 \text{ cm}$$

With safety factor:

$$t_{design} = 1.20 \times 0.714 = 0.857 \text{ cm}$$

**Recommended specification:** $\boxed{t_{design} \approx 0.9 \text{ cm (round up to 1.0 cm)}}$

**(Mark split: curve explanation 2 marks, procedure 2 marks, numerical 3 marks = 7)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting units** in the cushioning equation — always check whether the answer is in cm or m, then convert drop height to match thickness units.
> - **Mixing up static stress and dynamic stress** — students often compute the wrong denominator.
> - **Omitting the safety factor** — KTU answers without SF are marked down by 1–2 marks even if the math is right.
> - **Not stating assumptions** — explicitly write "assuming linear foam response" and "ignoring air resistance."
> - **Confusing EPS with XPS** — they have very different manufacturing routes (bead expansion vs extrusion) and properties; this is a frequent short-answer trap.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PEPOT748 Module 4:**

- **Polystyrene (PS)** is an amorphous aromatic thermoplastic with pendant phenyl groups that give it rigidity.
- **EPS** is manufactured by steam-expanding PS beads containing trapped **pentane blowing agent**.
- **XPS** is manufactured by **extrusion with blowing agents** — gives a smoother skin and lower water absorption.
- **EPP** is the **reusable, high-endurance** foam choice for returnable packaging.
- The **cushioning equation** is $G_{peak} = h/(t \cdot C)$.
- The **cushion factor C** depends on foam density and static stress.
- **Cushioning curves** are U-shaped: bottom of U is the optimal stress region.
- Always apply a **safety factor (typically 1.2–1.5)** to the calculated cushion thickness.
- Peak deceleration must be **below the product's fragility G** rating.
- Standards to remember: **ASTM D4168** (dynamic cushioning), **ASTM D1596** (vibration), **ASTM D5276** (drop test).
- Multi-drop performance: **EPS < XPS < EPP**.
- Closed-cell foams cushion via **gas compression + cell wall buckling**; open-cell foams (PU) cushion mainly via **air flow and viscous dissipation**.
- **Cellularity** (foam volume % that is solid) is typically 1–5% for EPS — the rest is air/gas.
- Solid PS density is **1050 kg/m³**; foam densities are 10–200 kg/m³.
- **Anti-static and conductive foams** are required for electronic packaging to prevent ESD damage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Moulded Cushioning Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Moulded Cushioning Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moulded Cushioning System:** A pre-engineered, geometrically contoured protective packaging component — typically fabricated from **expanded polymeric foams** (EPS, EPP, EPE, PU) — that is custom-shaped to envelop, cradle, and isolate a product from the transmission of mechanical shock, vibration, and static load during distribution handling.

In the strict context of **PEPOT748 – Plastics Packaging Technology**, the *design* of such a system is the systematic procedure of selecting a cushion material, geometry, and thickness such that the **peak deceleration (G-force)** experienced by the encapsulated product during an impact event remains below the product's **fragility rating (G)**.

### Key Technical Terminology

- **Cushioning**: The science of attenuating mechanical shock energy transmitted to a packaged product.
- **Fragility (G)**: The maximum number of gravitational accelerations a product can withstand without functional or cosmetic damage.
- **Cushion Curve (Stress–Strain Curve)**: A graphical representation of the static stress versus strain for a given cushion material at a specific drop height.
- **Moulded Foam**: A cellular polymeric structure produced by expanding a polymer (e.g., polystyrene beads) inside a closed mould cavity using steam and pressure.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Egg in the Sponge Cradle":**
> Imagine you are transporting a raw egg across a bumpy road inside a thin cardboard box (no cushioning). Every pothole would transmit the shock directly to the eggshell, causing it to crack. Now imagine placing the egg inside a perfectly contoured sponge cradle shaped exactly to the egg's geometry. When the box hits the ground, the sponge *deforms*, *absorbs*, and *dissipates* the impact energy over a longer time interval, drastically reducing the force transferred to the egg.
>
> The **sponge** = the moulded cushioning system.
> The **egg** = the protected product.
> The **pothole / drop** = the impact event.
> The **cracking threshold** = the product's fragility rating.

The cushion acts as a **mechanical "shock absorber"** analogous to the hydraulic dampers in an automobile suspension. It converts a sudden, high-amplitude, short-duration force pulse into a longer-duration, lower-amplitude pulse — exploiting the principle of *impulse–momentum conservation*.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Moulded Cushioning Materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Cushioning Materials used in KTU Module 4:**

| Material | Full Form | Key Property | Density Range |
|----------|-----------|--------------|---------------|
| **EPS** | Expanded Polystyrene | Rigid, brittle foam | **16–32 kg/m³** |
| **EPP** | Expanded Polypropylene | Flexible, resilient foam | **20–80 kg/m³** |
| **EPE** | Expanded Polyethylene | Soft, chemically stable foam | **20–45 kg/m³** |
| **PU Foam** | Polyurethane Foam | Open/closed cell, varied | **24–80 kg/m³** |

> **Bold Standard Metric:** The standard gravitational acceleration used in all KTU cushion design problems is **g = 9.81 m/s²** (often approximated as **9.8 m/s²** or **10 m/s²** in numerical problems).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Physics of Cushioning — Step-by-Step Logic

When a packaged product falls from height **h** and strikes a rigid surface, the cushioning material must perform three sequential functions:

1. **Energy Absorption (Step 1):** Convert the kinetic energy of the falling package into strain energy within the foam's cellular structure.
2. **Force Attenuation (Step 2):** Spread the impact force over a larger cushion area, reducing the *peak* force transmitted to the product.
3. **Deceleration Control (Step 3):** Limit the deceleration experienced by the product to a value below its fragility rating **G**.

### 2.1.1 The Kinetic Energy of Impact

When a package of mass **m** falls from height **h**, the impact velocity is governed by free-fall mechanics:

$$v = \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot h}$$

The kinetic energy **KE** that the cushion must absorb is therefore:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^{2} = m \cdot g \cdot h$$

> **Plain-English Interpretation:** The cushion must absorb an amount of energy numerically equal to the *potential energy* the package possessed at the drop height. No energy is "destroyed" — it is merely converted into strain energy in the foam.

### 2.1.2 Newton's Impact Law (Impulse–Momentum Equivalence)

The peak impact force **F** experienced by the product is governed by:

$$F \cdot t = m \cdot v$$

where **t** is the duration of the impact event (in seconds). Rearranging:

$$F = \frac{m \cdot v}{t}$$

> **Why this matters:** A longer impact duration **t** (achieved by using a *softer* or *thicker* cushion) produces a *smaller* peak force. This is the foundational principle behind ALL cushioning design.

### 2.1.3 The G-Force (Deceleration) Relationship

The deceleration experienced by the product in units of **g** is:

$$G = \frac{F}{W} = \frac{a}{g}$$

where **W = m·g** is the weight of the product, and **a** is the linear deceleration. Substituting Newton's law:

$$G = \frac{v}{g \cdot t}$$

### 2.1.4 Static Stress and Dynamic Stress

- **Static Stress** (cushion under product's own weight at rest):

$$\sigma_{s} = \frac{W}{A}$$

- **Dynamic Stress** (peak stress during impact, read directly off cushion curve):

$$\sigma_{d} = G \cdot \sigma_{s}$$

where **A** is the bearing contact area between product and cushion (in m² or in²).

---

## 2.2 Cushion Curves — The Engineer's Design Map

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A *cushion curve* plots the dynamic stress **σ_d** (vertical axis) versus the static stress **σ_s** (horizontal axis) for a specific cushion material, density, thickness, and drop height. Each curve is empirically generated by dropping instrumented test packages from controlled heights.

### 2.2.1 How to Use a Cushion Curve (Design Procedure)

1. **Step 1 — Establish Fragility:** Determine the maximum allowable G-force (**G_max**) the product can withstand.
2. **Step 2 — Determine Static Stress:** Calculate **σ_s = W/A** based on the product weight and chosen bearing area.
3. **Step 3 — Compute Required Dynamic Stress:** Set **σ_d = G_max · σ_s**.
4. **Step 4 — Read the Curve:** On the cushion curve for the chosen material/density/thickness, find the intersection point. If this point lies **below** the curve for the prescribed drop height → design is **acceptable**.
5. **Step 5 — Iterate:** If unacceptable, change material, density, thickness, or contact area and re-evaluate.

### 2.2.2 Cushion Thickness Calculation

The minimum cushion thickness **t_c** required is:

$$t_{c} = h \cdot \varepsilon_{max}$$

where **ε_max** is the maximum allowable strain (typically **0.6 to 0.8** for foams). A more rigorous approach uses the cushion curve's strain axis.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations. They appear in nearly every KTU ESE question on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | $v = \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot h}$ | Impact velocity | m/s |
| 2 | $KE = m \cdot g \cdot h$ | Impact kinetic energy | J |
| 3 | $\sigma_{s} = \dfrac{W}{A}$ | Static bearing stress | Pa or N/m² |
| 4 | $\sigma_{d} = G \cdot \sigma_{s}$ | Dynamic peak stress | Pa or N/m² |
| 5 | $F = m \cdot a = G \cdot W$ | Peak impact force | N |
| 6 | $F \cdot t = m \cdot v$ | Impulse–momentum | N·s |
| 7 | $t_{c} = h \cdot \varepsilon_{max}$ | Cushion thickness | m |
| 8 | $G = \dfrac{v}{g \cdot t_{d}}$ | G-force (deceleration) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $W_{cushion} = \rho \cdot V_{cushion}$ | Cushion mass (for shipping cost) | kg |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{absorbed}}{E_{input}}$ | Cushion energy efficiency | dimensionless |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table above, all absolute-value-style notations use the fraction slash `\dfrac{}` to preserve markdown compatibility. **Never use vertical bars `|` inside markdown table rows.**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-commerce Packaging:** Amazon, Flipkart use EPS/EPE moulded inserts for fragile electronics — the design ensures G-forces stay below 25–40 even from drop heights of 1.2 m.
- **Automotive Component Shipment:** EPS moulds protect windscreens, headlamps, and instrument panels.
- **Medical Device Packaging:** Sterile moulded cushioning systems protect Class II/III medical devices during hospital-to-hospital transit.
- **Defense Applications:** EPP moulded cushions protect sensitive avionics in military logistics chains where multiple drops are expected.

> The same equations govern the design of **child car seats, helmet liners, and footwear midsoles** — proving that moulded cushioning design is a *transversal* engineering discipline.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — From Newton's Law to G-Force Equation

We start from Newton's second law applied during the cushioning event:

$$F_{peak} = m \cdot a_{peak}$$

### Step 1: Express acceleration in terms of deceleration time

Since the cushion decelerates the product from velocity **v** to rest in time **t_d**:

$$a_{peak} = \frac{v}{t_{d}}$$

### Step 2: Substitute velocity expression

From free-fall, $v = \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot h}$, so:

$$a_{peak} = \frac{\sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot h}}{t_{d}}$$

### Step 3: Compute the peak force

$$F_{peak} = m \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot h}}{t_{d}}$$

### Step 4: Convert to G-force by dividing by weight **W = m·g**

$$G = \frac{F_{peak}}{W} = \frac{m \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot h}}{t_{d} \cdot m \cdot g}$$

### Step 5: Simplify the expression algebraically

$$G = \frac{\sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot h}}{t_{d} \cdot g}$$

$$\boxed{G = \frac{\sqrt{2 \cdot h / g}}{t_{d}} = \frac{1}{t_{d}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot h}{g}}}$$

This is the **master G-force equation** used in KTU cushioning problems.

> **Plain-English Logic:** The G-force is *directly proportional* to the square root of drop height and *inversely proportional* to cushion-induced impact duration.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Full Step-by-Step Solution

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Numerical Pattern (14-mark question pattern)**

**Problem Statement:**
A product weighing **W = 20 N** has a fragility rating of **G = 40**. The product is to be packaged using an **EPS moulded cushion** and shipped via a logistics chain with a maximum drop height of **h = 1.0 m**. Determine:
- (a) The required static stress if the contact area is **A = 0.01 m²**.
- (b) The corresponding dynamic stress.
- (c) The peak impact force.
- (d) The minimum cushion thickness for a foam strain limit of **ε_max = 0.7**.

**Given Data:**

$$W = 20 \text{ N}, \quad G = 40, \quad h = 1.0 \text{ m}, \quad A = 0.01 \text{ m}^{2}, \quad \varepsilon_{max} = 0.7$$

**Take** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$.

---

### Part (a) — Static Stress Calculation

**Equation:**

$$\sigma_{s} = \frac{W}{A}$$

**Substitution:**

$$\sigma_{s} = \frac{20}{0.01}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{s} = 2000 \text{ Pa} = 2 \text{ kPa}}$$

---

### Part (b) — Dynamic Stress Calculation

**Equation:**

$$\sigma_{d} = G \cdot \sigma_{s}$$

**Substitution:**

$$\sigma_{d} = 40 \cdot 2000$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{d} = 80{,}000 \text{ Pa} = 80 \text{ kPa}}$$

---

### Part (c) — Peak Impact Force Calculation

**Approach 1 (via G-force definition):**

$$F_{peak} = G \cdot W$$

**Substitution:**

$$F_{peak} = 40 \cdot 20$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{F_{peak} = 800 \text{ N}}$$

**Verification (Approach 2 — via Newton's Law):**
First compute mass: $m = W/g = 20/9.81 = 2.039 \text{ kg}$
Impact velocity: $v = \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1.0} = 4.43 \text{ m/s}$
Impact duration: $t_{d} = v/(G \cdot g) = 4.43/(40 \cdot 9.81) = 0.01129 \text{ s}$
Peak force: $F = m \cdot v/t_{d} = 2.039 \cdot 4.43 / 0.01129 \approx 800 \text{ N}$ ✓

---

### Part (d) — Minimum Cushion Thickness

**Equation:**

$$t_{c} = h \cdot \varepsilon_{max}$$

**Substitution:**

$$t_{c} = 1.0 \cdot 0.7$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{t_{c} = 0.7 \text{ m}}$$

> **Engineering Reality Check:** A 0.7 m thick cushion is impractical. Therefore, in practice, the designer must either (i) increase the contact area **A** (use a wider mould), (ii) select a *softer* foam density, or (iii) restrict drop heights using orientation labels and "THIS SIDE UP" markings.

---

## 3.3 The Multiple-Drop Design Consideration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In real distribution chains, packages experience **5–15 drops**, not just one. The cushion must therefore be designed for the **cumulative plastic deformation** that occurs over multiple impacts.

The fatigue-corrected G-force equation is:

$$G_{design} = \frac{G_{single} \cdot f_{fatigue}}{\text{Safety Factor}}$$

Typical values:
- **Single drop:** $f_{fatigue} = 1.0$
- **5 drops:** $f_{fatigue} = 0.85$
- **10 drops:** $f_{fatigue} = 0.75$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Tree

| Problem Symptom | Likely Design Error | Corrective Action |
|-----------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Peak G > Fragility | Cushion too thin | Increase thickness by 25% |
| Peak G > Fragility | Material too stiff | Use lower density foam |
| Cost too high | Over-design | Reduce area, use stiffer foam |
| Cushion bottoms out | Strain limit exceeded | Increase thickness or use multi-layer |
| Product vibrates | No vibration isolation | Add 5–10 mm closed-cell layer |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cushion Design Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the *complete* cushion design procedure as a block-level functional architecture. This is the type of schematic that KTU board examiners expect students to draw in Module-4 ESE answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Product Specification] --> nodeB[Determine Product Weight W]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Identify Fragility Rating Gmax]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Define Drop Height h from Distribution Chain]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Select Cushion Material Family EPS EPP EPE or PU]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Choose Material Density rho kg per cubic meter]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compute Static Stress sigma s equals W divided by A]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Compute Dynamic Stress sigma d equals Gmax times sigma s]
    nodeH --> nodeI{Consult Cushion Curve for Selected Material and Thickness}
    I -->|Point lies below curve| nodeJ[Acceptable Design Proceed]
    I -->|Point lies above curve| nodeK[Iterate: Increase Area or Reduce Density]
    K --> nodeF
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Compute Cushion Thickness tc equals h times epsilon max]
    L --> nodeM[Apply Fatigue Correction Factor]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Apply Safety Factor 1.5 to 2.0]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Final Validated Cushion Design]
    nodeO --> nodeP[Generate Mould Drawing and Tooling Spec]
```

> **Reading Guide:** The flowchart begins with product specifications (top-left), proceeds through a sequence of calculations, and uses a **decision diamond** at the centre to either accept the design or iterate. The double-loop pattern at `nodeK → nodeF` represents the iterative optimisation characteristic of real-world cushion design.

---

## 4.2 Moulded Cushion Cross-Section (Functional Block View)

The following diagram illustrates the layered functional architecture of a typical moulded cushion assembly:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeOuter[Outer Carton Walls] --> nodeAir1[Air Gap 5 to 10 mm]
    nodeAir1 --> nodeMould[Moulded Cushion Geometry]
    nodeMould --> nodeCavity[Product Cavity Custom Contoured]
    nodeCavity --> nodeProd[Encapsulated Product]
    nodeMould --> nodeLiner[Optional Anti Static Liner]
    nodeLiner --> nodeProd
```

> **Reading Guide:** The carton provides **structural rigidity**, the air gap provides **secondary cushioning**, the moulded geometry provides **primary shock absorption**, and the cavity contour ensures the product is **immobilized** during transit.

---

## 4.3 Force–Time Pulse Visualisation (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Without Cushion: Tall Narrow Force Peak] --> B[Force Exceeds Fragility Threshold]
    C[With Moulded Cushion: Short Wide Force Peak] --> D[Force Remains Below Fragility Threshold]
    B --> E[Product Damaged]
    D --> F[Product Safe]
```

> **Reading Guide:** The cushion *trades* a tall, dangerous force spike for a shorter, wider, safer pulse. The total *area* under both curves is approximately equal (energy conservation), but the *peak height* of the cushioned curve is dramatically lower.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**Q: Define "Cushion Curve" and state its significance in the design of moulded cushioning systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **cushion curve** is a graph that plots the **dynamic stress (σ_d)** experienced by a cushion material on the y-axis against the **static stress (σ_s)** on the x-axis for a fixed drop height, material, and thickness.

**Significance:**
1. It allows the designer to **predict the peak G-force** transmitted to the product for any combination of product weight and bearing area.
2. It is the **primary design tool** used to select the correct cushion density and thickness.
3. It is generated **empirically** by dropping instrumented test packages, making it a reliable engineering reference.

> **Marks Distribution (as per KTU valuation):** Definition — 1 mark; Significance point 1 — 1 mark; Significance point 2 or 3 — 1 mark.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**Q: Differentiate between Expanded Polystyrene (EPS) and Expanded Polypropylene (EPP) as cushion materials.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | EPS | EPP |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Flexibility | Rigid, brittle | Flexible, resilient |
| Multi-drop resistance | Poor (crushes) | Excellent (recovers) |
| Typical density | **16–32 kg/m³** | **20–80 kg/m³** |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Recyclability | Limited | Better |

**Conclusion:** EPS is suitable for **single-trip, low-cost** applications; EPP is preferred for **multi-drop, reusable, premium** applications such as automotive and returnable logistics.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question B-A `(a)` — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q (a): Derive the expression relating the G-force experienced by a packaged product to the drop height and cushion-induced impact duration. Clearly state all assumptions.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The package falls freely with no air resistance.
2. The cushion deformation is the *only* deceleration phase.
3. The product does not rebound significantly during the event.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Free-fall velocity at impact:
$$v = \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot h}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Deceleration from Newton's second law:
$$a = \frac{v}{t_{d}}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Peak impact force:
$$F = m \cdot a = m \cdot \frac{v}{t_{d}}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Definition of G-force:
$$G = \frac{F}{W} = \frac{F}{m \cdot g}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Substitution:
$$G = \frac{m \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot h}}{t_{d} \cdot m \cdot g}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Final simplification:
$$\boxed{G = \frac{1}{t_{d}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot h}{g}}}$$

---

### Question B-A `(b)` — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q (b): A fragile instrument weighs 15 N and has a fragility rating of G = 30. It must survive a drop of 1.2 m. Using an EPS moulded cushion with a static stress of 1.5 kPa and a strain limit of 0.65, calculate: (i) the required bearing contact area, (ii) the peak impact force, and (iii) the cushion thickness.**

**Given:**
- $W = 15 \text{ N}$, $G = 30$, $h = 1.2 \text{ m}$, $\sigma_{s} = 1500 \text{ Pa}$, $\varepsilon_{max} = 0.65$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Bearing Contact Area (3 marks):**

From $G = \sigma_{d}/\sigma_{s}$:

$$\sigma_{d} = G \cdot \sigma_{s} = 30 \cdot 1500 = 45{,}000 \text{ Pa}$$

Also, dynamic stress = peak force / area = $G \cdot W / A$, so:

$$A = \frac{G \cdot W}{\sigma_{d}} = \frac{30 \cdot 15}{45{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{A = 0.01 \text{ m}^{2} = 100 \text{ cm}^{2}}$$

**(ii) Peak Impact Force (2 marks):**

$$F_{peak} = G \cdot W = 30 \cdot 15$$

$$\boxed{F_{peak} = 450 \text{ N}}$$

**(iii) Cushion Thickness (2 marks):**

$$t_{c} = h \cdot \varepsilon_{max} = 1.2 \cdot 0.65$$

$$\boxed{t_{c} = 0.78 \text{ m}}$$

> **Practical Recommendation (extra credit):** 0.78 m is excessive; use a *softer* foam (lower density) to reduce $\sigma_d$ and thus reduce $t_c$ to ~0.3 m.

---

### Question B-B `(a)` — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q (a): Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a steam-chest moulding process for expanded polystyrene (EPS) cushion production.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- An EPS mould consists of a **two-part aluminium cavity** with internal steam channels.
- The mould is mounted on a **steam-chest press** with water-cooled jackets.
- Raw **pre-expanded polystyrene beads** (containing pentane blowing agent) are loaded into the cavity.

**Working (4 marks):**
1. **Filling:** Pre-expanded beads are blown into the mould cavity by compressed air.
2. **Steaming:** High-pressure steam (typically 1.0–2.5 bar) is injected through perforations, causing the beads to **expand further and fuse together**.
3. **Cooling:** Cold water is circulated through the mould's cooling channels to solidify the fused bead structure.
4. **Ejection:** The mould opens and the moulded EPS cushion is ejected, typically after a dwell time of 60–180 seconds.

---

### Question B-B `(b)` — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q (b): A 25 N product (G = 25) is to be shipped in a single-drop scenario from 0.8 m. Design a moulded EPS cushion and verify that the design is acceptable. Assume EPS density = 24 kg/m³ and an available standard contact area of 0.015 m².**

**Given:** $W = 25 \text{ N}$, $G = 25$, $h = 0.8 \text{ m}$, $A = 0.015 \text{ m}^{2}$, $\rho_{EPS} = 24 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Static stress:**

$$\sigma_{s} = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{25}{0.015} = 1666.67 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.67 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Dynamic stress:**

$$\sigma_{d} = G \cdot \sigma_{s} = 25 \cdot 1666.67 = 41{,}666.75 \text{ Pa} \approx 41.67 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Consult Cushion Curve:**
For 24 kg/m³ EPS at 1.5-inch thickness and 0.8 m drop, typical allowable $\sigma_d \approx 45 \text{ kPa}$ at $\sigma_s = 1.67 \text{ kPa}$.

Since calculated $\sigma_d = 41.67 \text{ kPa} < 45 \text{ kPa}$ ✓ → **Design is acceptable.**

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Cushion Thickness:**

$$t_{c} = h \cdot \varepsilon_{max} = 0.8 \cdot 0.7 = 0.56 \text{ m}$$

(using standard $\varepsilon_{max} = 0.7$ for EPS)

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Verification:**

Peak force: $F_{peak} = G \cdot W = 25 \cdot 25 = 625 \text{ N}$
Impact velocity: $v = \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.8} = 3.96 \text{ m/s}$
Impact time: $t_d = v / (G \cdot g) = 3.96 / (25 \cdot 9.81) = 0.01615 \text{ s}$

All values are within typical cushion design envelopes → **Design verified.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–2 marks):** Students often mix **N/m²** with **N/cm²** or use **kg** instead of **N** in the static stress equation. Always carry units throughout the solution.
> 2. **Confusing Static and Dynamic Stress (–1 mark):** Dynamic stress is *larger* than static stress by a factor of G. Many students invert the relationship.
> 3. **Ignoring Multi-Drop Fatigue (–1 mark):** Real shipments face 5–15 drops. Always mention the fatigue correction factor in design problems.
> 4. **Forgetting the Safety Factor (–1 mark):** A 1.5× safety factor on cushion thickness is industry-standard and expected in KTU answers.
> 5. **Incomplete Cushion Curve Reading (–2 marks):** When referring to cushion curves, explicitly state the *material, density, thickness, and drop height* conditions. A bare curve reference loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Moulded Cushioning Design**

- ☐ **Cushioning** = attenuating shock transmission through controlled deformation.
- ☐ **Primary equation:** $G = \dfrac{1}{t_{d}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot h}{g}}$.
- ☐ **Static stress:** $\sigma_{s} = W/A$.
- ☐ **Dynamic stress:** $\sigma_{d} = G \cdot \sigma_{s}$.
- ☐ **Peak force:** $F_{peak} = G \cdot W$.
- ☐ **Cushion thickness:** $t_{c} = h \cdot \varepsilon_{max}$.
- ☐ **Standard g:** Use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$.
- ☐ **Common materials:** EPS (rigid), EPP (resilient), EPE (soft), PU (versatile).
- ☐ **Cushion curve** = σ_d vs σ_s graph for a specific drop height and material.
- ☐ **Design procedure:** Find W → find G → compute σ_s → read σ_d from curve → verify σ_d ≥ G·σ_s → iterate if not.
- ☐ **Always** apply a **safety factor of 1.5×** on thickness and **fatigue factor of 0.75–0.85** for multi-drop scenarios.
- ☐ **Never** use raw vertical bars `|` inside KTU markdown table answers — use `\vert` or `\mid`.
- ☐ **Always** state assumptions before deriving any cushioning equation.
- ☐ **Always** carry units and perform a dimensional sanity check.
- ☐ **Practical tip:** If computed thickness > 0.3 m, redesign with a softer foam — it indicates over-rigidity, not a fundamental flaw.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Design of Moulded Cushioning Systems**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plastic Pallets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Plastic Pallets # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)** > A **plastic pallet** is a rigid, flat transport structure manufactured predominantly from thermoplastic polymers (and occasionally thermoset composites) that is used as a base platform for the unitized handling, storage, and transportation of goods in a supply chain. It serves as a **secondary packaging** element that enables mechanical handling via forklifts, pallet jacks, and automated conveyor systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight** > The module classifies plastic pallets as a **rigid industrial packaging component** under the category of *returnable transit packaging (RTP)*, distinguishing them from single-use shipping containers due to their multi-trip durability and engineered polymer construction. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine the **Indian domestic LPG cylinder** delivery system. The metal cylinders are moved from the plant to distributors and finally to homes. Behind the scenes, dozens of these heavy cylinders are stacked on a flat platform, lifted by a forklift, and loaded onto a truck. That flat, strong platform is a **pallet**. Now, replace the traditional **wooden platform** with one made entirely of **molded plastic** (often HDPE or PP), and you have a **plastic pallet**. > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Comparison** > - A **wooden pallet** is like a **hand-stitched jute bag** — natural, sturdy, but inconsistent in quality, prone to splinters, nails, and rot. > - A **plastic pallet** is like a **synthetic polyester travel bag** — uniform, hygienic, weather-resistant, lighter, and designed for repeated use. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Key Specifications | Metric | Standard Value (Global) | KTU Reference | |---|---|---| | Standard Footprint | **1200 mm × 1000 mm** (Euro pallet) / **1219 mm × 1016 mm** (GMA, US) | ISO 6780 | | Static Load Capacity | **1500 – 6000 kg** | ISO 8611 | | Dynamic Load Capacity | **500 – 1500 kg** | ISO 8611 | | Operating Temperature | **−20 °C to +60 °C** (HDPE) | ASTM D4169 | | ISPM-15 Compliance | **Exempt (Plastic)** | IPPC ISPM-15 | | Average Service Life | **50–250 trips** (returnable) | Industry benchmark | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Constant to Remember** > The standard **dynamic load capacity** of a typical HDPE injection-molded pallet is approximately **1000 kg**, while **static load capacity** can exceed **4000 kg** due to uniform stress distribution on a flat warehouse floor. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Plastic Pallets (KTU Taxonomy) Plastic pallets are classified based on **structural design**, **manufacturing process**, and **application profile**. ### A. By Structural Design 1. **Block Pallets** – 9-legged or 3-runner base, suitable for heavy-duty racking systems. 2. **Stringer Pallets** – 2-runner base, lighter and more economical. 3. **Double-Face Pallets** – Both top and bottom decks, used for double-stack loading. 4. **Single-Face Pallets** – Single deck, used for export shipments. ### B. By Functional Geometry 1. **Rackable Pallets** – Designed for warehouse racking with reinforced top deck. 2. **Stackable Pallets** – Feature interlocking feet for vertical stacking of empty pallets. 3. **Nestable Pallets** – Hollow legs allowing nested storage (saves 75% space when empty). 4. **Drum Pallets** – Cylindrical cavities molded for 55-gallon drums. ### C. By Manufacturing Process - **Injection Molded Pallets** (HDPE, PP) - **Structural Foam Molded Pallets** (HDPE, PP) - **Rotationally Molded Pallets** (LDPE, LLDPE) - **Thermoformed Pallets** (HIPS, PP sheets) - **Compression Molded Pallets** (Recycled HDPE/PP) - **Composite Pallets** (PP + glass fiber, phenolic resin) > [!TIP] > **Why This Matters for KTU Exams** > Examiners frequently ask: *"Distinguish between rackable and nestable pallets."* The core difference lies in **load-bearing geometry** vs. **empty-space optimization**. --- ## 2.2 Polymers Used and Their Property Rationale | Polymer | Full Form | Key Property | Why Used | |---|---|---|---| | **HDPE** | High-Density Polyethylene | High impact strength, chemical resistance | Most common for structural foam pallets | | **PP** | Polypropylene | Higher melting point, better rigidity | Preferred for hot-fill or warm environments | | **LDPE/LLDPE** | Low-Density / Linear LDPE | Excellent impact, rotomolding grade | Used in large hollow rotomolded pallets | | **HIPS** | High-Impact Polystyrene | Rigidity, low cost | Thermoformed export pallets | | **PET** | Polyethylene Terephthalate | High strength, food-grade | Cold-chain hygienic pallets | | **PA6/PA66** | Polyamide (Nylon) | High temperature resistance | High-heat industrial applications | | **PBT** | Polybutylene Terephthalate | Glass-fiber reinforced, structural | Composite reinforced pallets | | **PVC** | Polyvinyl Chloride | Flame retardant, rigid | Specialty chemical-resistant pallets | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use `\vert` or `\mid` in tables to avoid markdown conflicts.** | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | Bending Stress on Deck | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | N/m² or MPa | Maximum stress on top deck under load | | Where $M$ is bending moment | $M = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{4}$ for 4-point bending | N·m | Moment on the pallet deck | | Second Moment of Area (Solid Slab) | $I = \dfrac{b \cdot t^3}{12}$ | m⁴ | Deck rigidity parameter | | Deflection of Simply-Supported Deck | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \cdot W \cdot L^3}{384 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | m | Maximum center deflection | | Critical Buckling Load (Cylindrical Leg) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{(K \cdot L)^2}$ | N | Euler buckling for pallet legs | | Stack Height Utilization | $\eta_{stack} = \dfrac{N_{loaded}}{N_{max}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Vertical space efficiency | | Nesting Ratio | $R_{nest} = \dfrac{H_{single}}{H_{nested}}$ | dimensionless | Storage efficiency of empty pallets | | Pallet Density (Weight Efficiency) | $\rho_p = \dfrac{m_{pallet}}{V_{pallet}}$ | kg/m³ | Material compactness indicator | | Cost-per-Trip | $C_{trip} = \dfrac{C_{purchase} + C_{maintenance}}{N_{trips}}$ | ₹ or $/trip | Economic feasibility metric | | Thermal Expansion | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$ | m | Dimensional change with temperature | --- ## 2.4 Step-by-Step Design Logic When a plastic pallet is loaded, the following **load transfer mechanism** occurs: - **Step 1:** Distributed load from goods acts on the top deck. - **Step 2:** The top deck acts as a **plate** in bending, transferring load to the **supporting legs/feet**. - **Step 3:** Legs act as **columns** under compressive load, transferring to warehouse floor. - **Step 4:** Floor absorbs load uniformly; no localized stress concentration. - **Step 5:** Bending moment in deck is **maximum at the center**, so the deck requires **reinforcement ribs** to prevent sag. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering 'Why'** > The choice of **HDPE over PP** for cold-storage pallets is because HDPE retains its impact strength down to **−40 °C**, while PP becomes brittle below **0 °C** due to its glass transition temperature (Tg ≈ −20 °C). --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Plastic pallets are critical in: - **Pharmaceutical industry** (cleanroom compatibility, no wood splinters, no fungal growth). - **Food & beverage** (hygienic, washable, USDA-FDA approved grades). - **Automotive export** (lightweight → reduced air-freight costs). - **Chemical industry** (HDPE resists acids/alkalis; wood would degrade). - **Cold chain logistics** (low-temperature resilience). - **Export shipments** (ISPM-15 exempt, no fumigation required → saves time and cost). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Bending Deflection of a Pallet Top Deck **Problem Setup:** A plastic pallet top deck, treated as a simply-supported rectangular slab of length $L$, width $b$, and uniform thickness $t$, is loaded by a uniformly distributed load $w$ (N/m²). Compute the maximum center deflection. **Given:** - Deck span: $L = 1.2$ m (Euro pallet long edge) - Deck width: $b = 1.0$ m - Deck thickness: $t = 0.012$ m (12 mm) - Distributed load: $w = 5000$ N/m² - Modulus of HDPE: $E = 1.0$ GPa $= 1.0 \times 10^9$ N/m² --- ### Step 1 — Total Equivalent Load on the Deck $$ W_{total} = w \cdot L \cdot b $$ $$ W_{total} = 5000 \times 1.2 \times 1.0 = 6000 \text{ N} $$ This represents the total goods weight pressing down on the top deck. --- ### Step 2 — Treat the Slab as a Beam (Bending Analysis) For deflection, we approximate the slab as a beam of width $b$ and thickness $t$ loaded by a total load $W_{total}$. The total load per unit length along the span is: $$ q = \frac{W_{total}}{L} = w \cdot b $$ $$ q = 5000 \times 1.0 = 5000 \text{ N/m} $$ --- ### Step 3 — Compute Second Moment of Area For a rectangular cross-section, the second moment of area about the neutral axis is: $$ I = \frac{b \cdot t^3}{12} $$ $$ I = \frac{1.0 \times (0.012)^3}{12} = \frac{1.0 \times 1.728 \times 10^{-6}}{12} = 1.44 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4 $$ --- ### Step 4 — Apply the Standard Beam Deflection Formula For a simply-supported beam with uniformly distributed load $q$, the maximum deflection (at center) is: $$ \delta_{max} = \frac{5 \cdot q \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I} $$ Substituting: $$ \delta_{max} = \frac{5 \times 5000 \times (1.2)^4}{384 \times 1.0 \times 10^9 \times 1.44 \times 10^{-7}} $$ Compute numerator: $$ 5 \times 5000 \times 2.0736 = 51{,}840 $$ Compute denominator: $$ 384 \times 1.0 \times 10^9 \times 1.44 \times 10^{-7} = 384 \times 144 = 55{,}296 $$ Therefore: $$ \delta_{max} = \frac{51{,}840}{55{,}296} = 0.9375 \text{ mm} \approx 0.94 \text{ mm} $$ --- ### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation A deflection of $\approx 0.94$ mm under full load is **well within the ISO 8611 allowable limit of 1.5 mm** for a class-A pallet. This confirms that an HDPE deck of 12 mm thickness is structurally adequate for a 6000 N distributed load on a Euro-pallet footprint. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Table for Pallet Materials | Property | HDPE | PP | LDPE | Wood (Pine) | Steel | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Density (kg/m³) | **950** | **905** | **920** | **500–700** | **7850** | | Tensile Strength (MPa) | **22–31** | **30–40** | **10–15** | **40–80** | **250–550** | | Flexural Modulus (GPa) | **0.8–1.2** | **1.3–1.8** | **0.2–0.4** | **8–12** | **200** | | Impact (Izod, J/m) | **130–500** | **80–150** | **No break** | Low | High | | Water Absorption (%) | **<0.01** | **<0.03** | **<0.02** | **40–80** | None | | Chemical Resistance | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Poor | Moderate | | Cost (₹/kg, indicative) | **80–120** | **90–140** | **85–130** | **20–40** | **70–100** | | Recyclability | High | High | High | Medium | High | | ISPM-15 Exempt | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | No (needs treatment) | Yes | | Splinter Risk | None | None | None | High | Sharp edges | --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Cost-per-Trip Analysis (Return on Investment) Suppose a plastic pallet costs **₹1500** to purchase, has an average life of **100 trips**, and maintenance cost is **₹300** over its lifetime. $$ C_{trip} = \frac{C_{purchase} + C_{maintenance}}{N_{trips}} $$ $$ C_{trip} = \frac{1500 + 300}{100} = \frac{1800}{100} = 18 \text{ ₹/trip} $$ Compare with a **wooden pallet** costing **₹250** but lasting only **8 trips**: $$ C_{trip}^{wood} = \frac{250 + 50}{8} = \frac{300}{8} = 37.5 \text{ ₹/trip} $$ Therefore, the **plastic pallet is 2.08× more economical per trip** despite higher initial cost. --- ## 3.4 Mathematical Condition for Safe Buckling of Pallet Legs A pallet leg, modeled as a hollow cylindrical column with outer diameter $D_o$, inner diameter $D_i$, length $L$, fixed at both ends ($K = 0.5$): $$ P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{(K \cdot L)^2} $$ $$ I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( D_o^4 - D_i^4 \right) $$ For a leg of $D_o = 0.06$ m, $D_i = 0.04$ m, $L = 0.12$ m, $E = 1.0 \times 10^9$ N/m², $K = 0.5$: $$ I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( (0.06)^4 - (0.04)^4 \right) = \frac{\pi}{64} (1.296 \times 10^{-5} - 2.56 \times 10^{-6}) $$ $$ I = \frac{\pi}{64} \times 1.04 \times 10^{-5} = 5.105 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4 $$ $$ P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 1.0 \times 10^9 \times 5.105 \times 10^{-7}}{(0.5 \times 0.12)^2} = \frac{5038.5}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.4 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 1400 \text{ kN} $$ This is **far greater than typical load per leg (~750 kg ≈ 7.36 kN)**, confirming structural safety with a **safety factor > 190×**. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Pallet Anatomy — Block-Level Functional Architecture The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture of a typical injection-molded plastic pallet**, illustrating how each component contributes to load-bearing and handling. ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["TOP DECK
HDPE / PP
Load Bearing Surface"] node2["REINFORCEMENT RIBS
Radial + Cross Ribs
Bending Stiffness"] node3["SUPPORT LEGS
9 Block Pattern
Compressive Load Transfer"] node4["BOTTOM DECK
Open or Closed
Forklift Tine Entry"] node5["FOUR WAY ENTRY
Tine Slots 360 Degrees
Forklift Accessibility"] node6["RIM AND EDGE
Anti-Slip Texture
ISPM 15 Exempt"] node7["ANTI SLIP MAT
Optional Rubber Insert
Friction Enhancement"] node8["RFID TAG SLOT
Optional Embedded
Tracking Pallet"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node1 --> node6 node6 --> node7 node3 --> node8 ``` --- ## 4.2 Manufacturing Process Selection Flow This diagram helps a KTU student decide which manufacturing process to use for a given pallet application. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA["Start: Define Pallet Application"] q1{"Load Capacity Required?"} p1["HEAVY DUTY > 1500 kg
Injection Molding HDPE / PP"] p2["MEDIUM DUTY 500 to 1500 kg
Structural Foam Molding"] p3["LIGHT DUTY < 500 kg
Thermoforming HIPS or PP Sheet"] p4["LARGE BULK > 2.5 m length
Rotational Molding LDPE / LLDPE"] p5["ECO RECYCLED CONTENT
Compression Molding HDPE / PP"] startA --> q1 q1 -- "> 1500 kg" --> p1 q1 -- "500 to 1500 kg" --> p2 q1 -- "< 500 kg" --> p3 p1 --> p4 p3 --> p5 ``` --- ## 4.3 Load Transfer Path During Forklift Handling ```mermaid flowchart LR cargoA["GOODS ON TOP DECK"] --> deckA["TOP DECK PLATE
Distributes Load"] deckA --> ribA["RIBS
Bending Resistance"] ribA --> legA["VERTICAL LEGS
Column Compression"] legA --> floorA["WAREHOUSE FLOOR
Uniform Reaction Force"] tineA["FORKLIFT TINES
Bottom Entry"] --> legA ``` --- ## 4.4 Comparison Block: Plastic vs. Wood vs. Metal Pallet | Feature | Plastic Pallet | Wood Pallet | Metal Pallet | |---|---|---|---| | Weight (Euro) | **12–25 kg** | 20–30 kg | 35–60 kg | | Cost | Medium | Low | High | | Hygienic | **Excellent** | Poor (fungal) | Good | | Lifespan | **50–250 trips** | 5–10 trips | 500+ trips | | ISPM-15 | **Exempt** | Required | Exempt | | Recyclable | **Yes** | Limited | Yes | | Impact Damage | **Resilient** | Splinters | Dents | | Water Absorption | **None** | High | None | | Color Coding | **Possible** | Difficult | Difficult | --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a plastic pallet. List any four advantages over wooden pallets. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **Model Answer:** A plastic pallet is a rigid, flat transport structure manufactured from thermoplastic polymers (HDPE, PP) used as a base for unitized handling, storage, and transportation of goods. **Advantages over wooden pallets:** 1. **ISPM-15 Exempt** – No fumigation/heat treatment required for export (saves cost and time). 2. **Hygienic** – Non-porous, washable, no fungal or bacterial growth; suitable for pharma and food. 3. **Consistent Dimensions** – Injection-molded to tight tolerances; no nails, splinters, or warping. 4. **Longer Service Life** – 50–250 trips vs. 5–10 for wood, giving lower cost-per-trip. 5. **Lighter Weight** – Reduces air-freight costs in exports. 6. **Recyclable** – End-of-life pallets can be reprocessed into new products. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall** > Do not confuse the definition with that of a *plastic crate* (has walls) or a *plastic skid* (no bottom deck). The pallet **must have a top deck plus a forklift-tine accessible base**. --- ### Question 2: What is the role of ISPM-15 standard in plastic pallet selection? **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **Model Answer:** **ISPM-15 (International Standards for Phytosanitary Measures No. 15)** is an IPPC regulation governing **wood packaging material** used in international trade to prevent the spread of pests. **Key Points:** - It mandates that all **wooden** pallets and crates be either **heat-treated (HT)** or **methyl-bromide fumigated (MB)** before export. - **Plastic pallets are fully exempt** from ISPM-15 compliance because they are **synthetic and non-organic**, harboring no pests. - This exemption gives plastic pallets a **significant logistical and economic advantage** in cross-border shipments, eliminating fumigation costs and processing delays. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- ### 🔵 QUESTION A (Option 1) #### (a) Explain in detail the various types of plastic pallets based on their design and functional geometry. (7 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** Plastic pallets are classified by design and functional geometry as follows: **1. Based on Design:** - **Block Pallets:** Have multiple blocks (typically 9) supporting the top deck. Suitable for heavy-duty racking systems. They provide high load capacity and four-way forklift entry. - **Stringer Pallets:** Use 2 or 3 stringers (longitudinal runners) between top and bottom decks. Lighter but less rigid. - **Double-Face Pallets:** Feature decks on both sides, useful for double-stack loading without flipping. - **Single-Face Pallets:** Have only one deck, typically used for export or one-trip applications. **2. Based on Functional Geometry:** - **Rackable Pallets:** Reinforced top decks designed to bear load across open warehouse racking beams. - **Stackable Pallets:** Interlocking base-and-deck geometry, allowing empty pallets to be stacked vertically, saving floor space. - **Nestable Pallets:** Hollow legs that nest into the next pallet; **saves up to 75% volume** for return transport. - **Drum Pallets:** Molded with cylindrical cavities for 55-gallon chemical drums. **Comparison Summary:** | Type | Empty Storage | Load Capacity | Best Use | |---|---|---|---| | Rackable | Poor | Very High | Warehousing | | Stackable | Good | High | Distribution | | Nestable | Excellent | Medium | Return logistics | --- #### (b) Discuss the manufacturing processes for plastic pallets. Compare injection molding with rotational molding. (7 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** **Manufacturing Processes:** 1. **Injection Molding:** Molten HDPE/PP is injected under high pressure into a steel mold. Fast cycle times, high precision, suitable for mass production of complex geometries. 2. **Structural Foam Molding:** A blowing agent (e.g., nitrogen) is added to the melt, creating a cellular core with a solid skin. Lighter weight, less sink marks, suitable for large pallets. 3. **Rotational Molding:** Powdered LDPE/LLDPE is placed in a hollow mold and rotated in an oven. Produces seamless, stress-free parts; ideal for large hollow pallets. 4. **Thermoforming:** Plastic sheets (HIPS/PP) are heated and vacuum-formed onto a mold. Economical for light-duty export pallets. 5. **Compression Molding:** Recycled HDPE/PP flakes are heated and pressed in a mold. Used for **eco-friendly recycled-content pallets**. **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Injection Molding | Rotational Molding | |---|---|---| | Polymer Used | HDPE, PP | LDPE, LLDPE | | Mold Cost | High | Moderate | | Cycle Time | **30–90 seconds** | **20–40 minutes** | | Wall Thickness | Uniform | Variable | | Surface Finish | Excellent | Moderate | | Part Size | Small to medium | **Large** (>2 m) | | Stress in Part | Internal molded stress | Stress-free | | Production Volume | High | Low to medium | **Conclusion:** Injection molding is preferred for high-volume, high-strength pallets; rotational molding is suited for large, low-volume specialty pallets. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall** > Students often omit the **cycle-time difference**, which is the *single most important* parameter distinguishing the two processes. Always mention cycle time and part-size capability. --- ### 🟢 QUESTION B (Option 2) #### (a) Compare plastic pallets with wooden and metal pallets in terms of weight, cost, hygiene, and ISPM-15 compliance. (7 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Plastic Pallet | Wooden Pallet | Metal Pallet | |---|---|---|---| | **Weight (Euro)** | **12–25 kg** | 20–30 kg | 35–60 kg | | **Cost per Unit** | ₹1200–2500 | ₹200–400 | ₹2500–5000 | | **Hygiene** | Excellent (washable) | Poor (fungal risk) | Good (rust risk) | | **ISPM-15** | **Exempt** | Required (HT/MB) | Exempt | | **Lifespan** | 50–250 trips | 5–10 trips | 500+ trips | | **Impact Damage** | Resilient | Splinters | Dents | | **Recyclability** | High | Limited | High | | **Color Coding** | Possible | Difficult | Difficult | | **Best Application** | Pharma, food, export | Domestic transport | Heavy industrial | **Key Insights:** - Plastic pallets offer the **best balance** of hygiene, weight, and cost for most modern supply chains. - Wooden pallets are **cheapest initially** but have hidden costs (fumigation, repair, replacement). - Metal pallets dominate in **heavy-engineering industries** with extreme loads but are cost-prohibitive for FMCG. --- #### (b) Describe the step-by-step process of structural foam molding for plastic pallets. Mention its advantages and limitations. (7 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Model Answer:** **Process Steps:** 1. **Material Preparation:** HDPE or PP pellets are blended with a **chemical blowing agent** (e.g., azodicarbonamide, 0.5–1.5% by weight). 2. **Heating:** The blend is heated in the injection barrel to a temperature just below the decomposition point of the blowing agent. 3. **Injection:** The molten mixture is injected into a closed mold at high pressure. The pressure keeps the gas in solution. 4. **Gas Expansion (Foaming):** As the material enters the mold cavity, pressure drops, causing the blowing agent to **decompose and release nitrogen gas**, which forms a **cellular core** within the part. 5. **Skin Formation:** The outer layer of the melt, in contact with the cool mold walls, solidifies rapidly to form a **dense, smooth skin** around the foamed core. 6. **Cooling & Ejection:** The part cools, solidifies, and is ejected. The cellular core reduces weight and material consumption by **20–40%** compared to solid injection molding. **Advantages:** - **Lighter weight** (20–40% reduction) without losing rigidity. - **Lower internal stress** → reduced warping and sink marks. - **Larger part capability** → suitable for one-piece pallet molds. - **Material cost saving** → less polymer per pallet. **Limitations:** - **Coarser surface texture** than solid injection molding. - **Slightly longer cycle time** due to foaming and skin formation. - **Not suitable for thin-walled sections** (< 6 mm). - **Chemical blowing agent residues** may affect food-grade applications unless food-safe grades are used. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake in Answer Scripts** > Students often confuse *structural foam molding* with *gas-assist injection molding*. Remember: structural foam uses a **chemical blowing agent in the melt**, while gas-assist uses **injected nitrogen gas through a separate channel**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam)** - **Definition:** Plastic pallet = rigid, flat, polymer-based unit-load platform for storage/transport. - **Standard Sizes:** 1200×1000 mm (Euro), 1219×1016 mm (US GMA), 1100×1100 mm (Asia). - **Most Common Polymers:** HDPE (most used), PP (high-temp), LDPE (rotomolded), HIPS (thermoformed). - **Manufacturing Processes:** Injection molding (most common), structural foam, rotational molding, thermoforming, compression molding (recycled). - **Pallet Types:** Block, Stringer, Double-face, Single-face; Rackable, Stackable, Nestable, Drum. - **ISPM-15:** Plastic pallets are **exempt** (key exam point!). - **Key Formulas:** - Bending stress: $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M y}{I}$ - Deflection: $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 W L^3}{384 E I}$ - Buckling: $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{(K L)^2}$ - Cost-per-trip: $C_{trip} = \dfrac{C_{purchase} + C_{maintenance}}{N_{trips}}$ - **Static Load:** 1500–6000 kg; **Dynamic Load:** 500–1500 kg. - **Key Standards:** ISO 6780 (size), ISO 8611 (performance), ISPM-15 (export), ASTM D4169 (testing). - **Advantages:** Hygienic, ISPM-15 exempt, long life, light weight, recyclable, no splinters. - **Limitations:** Higher initial cost, lower heat resistance (HDPE), may deform under sustained heavy load. - **Applications:** Pharma, food, chemical, automotive, export, cold-chain. - **Eco-Fact:** A recycled-content plastic pallet can use up to **100% post-consumer HDPE**, supporting circular economy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy** > - Always **draw a labeled diagram** of a pallet with parts named (top deck, ribs, legs, bottom deck, tine slots). > - In 14-mark answers, **use a comparison table** — it instantly boosts presentation and marks. > - Always mention **ISPM-15 exemption** whenever plastic vs. wood is compared; it's a **favorite examiner line of questioning**. ---

topics.title: Drums & Other Shipping Containers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Drums & Other Shipping Containers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shipping Container (Plastics):** A rigid or semi-rigid transport package of capacity typically exceeding 5 litres, manufactured predominantly from thermoplastics (HDPE, PP, LLDPE) or fibre-reinforced composites, engineered to withstand the mechanical, chemical, and environmental stresses encountered during inter-modal distribution (road, rail, sea, air) as governed by **UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods (UN RTDG)** and **ISO 15750 / ASTM D1999** standards.

Among shipping containers, the **drum** is the flagship format. In KTU Module 4 parlance:

> **Plastic Drum:** A cylindrical, free-standing industrial container, generally fabricated by **Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)** or **Injection Blow Moulding (IBM)**, with nominal capacities ranging from **20 L to 220 L**, closed by a re-sealable head fitted with gasketted closures (bungs, latches). The standard nominal capacities recognized internationally are **30 L, 60 L, 120 L, 160 L, 200 L, and 220 L**.

### Related Shipping Container Families

| Container Family | Capacity Range | Head Configuration | Dominant Resin |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pail** | 1 L – 25 L | Open-top with bail handle | HDPE / PP |
| **Jerrican** | 5 L – 30 L | Tight-head, integral handle | HDPE |
| **Drum (Keg)** | 20 L – 220 L | Tight-head or open-head | HDPE |
| **IBC (Tote)** | 1000 L (standard) | Sling/frame-supported bladder | HDPE inner + steel cage |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Think of a plastic drum as the 'suitcase of the chemical industry'."**
>
> Imagine a heavy-duty trekking backpack, but instead of holding your clothes, it carries 50 kg of sulphuric acid across the Pacific Ocean. The drum must (a) not leak from the top (closure integrity), (b) not burst from the bottom when stacked four-high in a warehouse (hoop stress resistance), (c) not shatter when dropped off a forklift (impact absorption), and (d) not contaminate the food/pharma inside (FDA/ECHA compliance). A jerrican is the small backpack, a pail is the lunchbox, and an IBC is the entire shipping crate on wheels.

---

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Standard Density of HDPE:** **ρ = 0.954 g/cm³** (moulding grade)
- **Tensile Yield Strength of HDPE:** **σ_y ≈ 22 – 31 MPa**
- **Nominal 200 L Drum Mass (HDPE):** **m ≈ 9 – 11 kg** (single-piece blow-moulded)
- **Maximum Stacking Load (UN 1H1):** **F_stack ≥ 1.8 × (3 months static load)** → typically **≥ 400 kg per drum**
- **Standard Test Drop Height (Packing Group II):** **h = 1.2 m**
- **Hydrostatic Test Pressure:** **P_test = 100 kPa (gauge) minimum for 30 minutes**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy of a Plastic Drum

A plastic drum is essentially a **thin-walled pressure vessel** (closed-end cylinder) that must survive four simultaneous load categories. KTU examiners expect students to *map each load category to its governing equation*.

### Load Category 1 — Internal Hydrostatic Pressure
When the drum holds liquid, internal hydrostatic pressure acts radially. For a thin-walled cylinder (radius r, wall thickness t):

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{p \cdot r}{t}$$

Hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress and is therefore the **governing failure mode**.

### Load Category 2 — Stacking / Axial Compression
Four drums stacked on the bottom drum transfer axial load F. Compressive stress in the sidewall:

$$\sigma_{axial} = \frac{F}{2 \pi r t}$$

### Load Category 3 — Drop Impact
Converts potential energy (m·g·h) into strain energy of the drum body. Energy balance:

$$\frac{1}{2} k \delta^2 = m g h$$

Where k = effective stiffness of the curved shell.

### Load Category 4 — Creep / Environmental Stress Cracking (ESC)
HDPE under sustained load in presence of surfactants (detergents, oils) fails by **ESC**. Designers mitigate via:
- Higher molecular weight (MFI < 1 g/10 min)
- Bimodal HDPE grades (e.g., **HEURREEDENE by INEOS**)
- Increased wall thickness at corners ("belly band" reinforcement)

---

## 2.2 Manufacturing Route Selection (Why EBM dominates)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why HDPE + EBM?** HDPE has the **best environmental stress cracking resistance** of all commodity thermoplastics, a melt strength ideal for parison sag control, and FDA 21 CFR §177.1520 food-contact compliance. Extrusion Blow Moulding produces a **fully stress-relieved, seamless hollow body** with no weld lines — critical for pressure-vessel integrity.

| Process | Parison / Preform State | Typical Drum Range | Tooling Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)** | Continuous parison | 20 L – 220 L | Low–Medium |
| **Injection Blow Moulding (IBM)** | Injection-moulded preform | Up to 30 L | Medium |
| **Rotational Moulding** | Powder | 50 L – 500 L (lower stiffness) | Low |
| **Injection Moulding (single piece)** | Molten polymer | Up to 60 L (pails) | High |

---

## 2.3 Closure Engineering — The Bung System

> [!NOTE]
> The closure is the **#1 leak-failure point**. A 200 L tight-head drum has either:
> - **Two 2-inch (50.8 mm) NPT bungs** (one for filling, one for venting/dispensing), or
> - A **single Tri-Sure® style flanged closure** with EPDM gasket.

The closure torque must develop a **gasket contact stress σ_gasket ≥ 7 MPa** to seal against internal vapour pressure.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet — High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{h} = \dfrac{p \cdot r}{t}$ | Hoop stress | Pa | Valid only if $t \lt \dfrac{r}{10}$ (thin wall) |
| 2 | $\sigma_{l} = \dfrac{p \cdot r}{2 t}$ | Longitudinal stress | Pa | Thin-wall assumption |
| 3 | $\sigma_{vm} = \dfrac{p \cdot r \sqrt{3}}{2 t}$ | Von-Mises equivalent stress | Pa | Used for HDPE (ductile) |
| 4 | $p_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \sigma_{y} \cdot t}{D}$ | Burst pressure | Pa | $t$ = min wall, $D$ = OD |
| 5 | $\sigma_{stack} = \dfrac{4 W g}{\pi D^2} \cdot \dfrac{D}{t}$ | Stress under stacked drum (axial) | Pa | $W$ = weight per drum |
| 6 | $\delta_{drop} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 m g h}{k}}$ | Deflection at impact | m | $k = \dfrac{E \cdot t}{r}$ for shell |
| 7 | $E_{HDPE} \approx 0.8 \text{ – } 1.2 \text{ GPa}$ | Modulus of HDPE | Pa | Short-term, 23 °C |
| 8 | $SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{vm, max}}$ | Safety Factor | dimensionless | UN spec: $SF \geq 2.5$ |
| 9 | $V_{drum} = \pi r^2 H_{eff}$ | Volumetric capacity | m³ | $H_{eff}$ ignores chimb |
| 10 | $t_{min} = \dfrac{p_{test} \cdot r}{\sigma_{y}/SF}$ | Minimum wall thickness | m | Test pressure $p_{test} = 100$ kPa |

> **Mark Pitfall #1 (HIGH FREQUENCY):** Students forget the **2 multiplier** in the hoop stress. Hoop is always **double** the longitudinal. The KTU examiner will deliberately offer a "trap" option with the longitudinal formula when asking for hoop.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chemical Industry:** Sulfuric acid, sodium hypochlorite, hydrogen peroxide — HDPE is the **only** cost-effective drum material for oxidizers because it does not corrode.
- **Food Industry:** Bulk edible oil, fruit concentrates — drums become pallet-mounted 1000 L IBCs on steel cages.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** API (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient) bulk storage — drums meet **FDA 21 CFR**, **USP <661>**, and **EU 10/2011**.
- **Defence:** UN Type 1H1 drums for fuel and lubricant logistics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hoop Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Drum

We consider an element of the drum wall cut along a longitudinal plane of unit length L. The internal pressure p acts radially outward; equilibrium must be maintained by tensile forces in the two halves of the cylinder.

### Step 1 — Free-Body Cut
Cut the cylinder longitudinally. The internal pressure on the projected area (diameter × unit length) must be balanced by the tangential (hoop) tensile force in the two wall sections.

$$F_{pressure} = p \cdot (2r) \cdot L$$

### Step 2 — Tensile Reaction
The two wall sections each carry a tensile force per unit length equal to σ_h · t. With two such sections:

$$F_{tension} = 2 \cdot (\sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L)$$

### Step 3 — Force Equilibrium
Equating pressure force to tensile reaction:

$$p \cdot (2r) \cdot L = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L$$

### Step 4 — Simplification
Cancel 2L from both sides:

$$p \cdot r = \sigma_h \cdot t$$

### Step 5 — Final Expression
Solving for hoop stress:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot r}{t}$$

This derivation shows hoop stress depends **linearly on radius and pressure** and **inversely on thickness** — explaining why large IBCs (r ≈ 0.6 m) require considerably more design attention than pails.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Wall Thickness from Hydrostatic Test Pressure

The UN hydrostatic test applies **p_test = 100 kPa (gauge)** for 30 minutes. We require that the Von-Mises stress during this test does not exceed σ_y / SF.

### Step 1 — Hoop Stress During Test
Substituting test pressure:

$$\sigma_{h,test} = \frac{p_{test} \cdot r}{t}$$

### Step 2 — Longitudinal Stress During Test
$$\sigma_{l,test} = \frac{p_{test} \cdot r}{2 t}$$

### Step 3 — Von-Mises Equivalent Stress
For a biaxial stress state (no radial stress in thin wall):

$$\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\sigma_h^{\,2} - \sigma_h \sigma_l + \sigma_l^{\,2}}$$

Substituting $\sigma_l = \sigma_h / 2$:

$$\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\sigma_h^{\,2} - \sigma_h \cdot \frac{\sigma_h}{2} + \frac{\sigma_h^{\,2}}{4}}$$

$$\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\frac{3}{4} \, \sigma_h^{\,2}}$$

$$\sigma_{vm} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \, \sigma_h$$

### Step 4 — Failure Criterion
Setting σ_vm equal to allowable:

$$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \frac{p_{test} \cdot r}{t} = \frac{\sigma_y}{SF}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for t_min
Rearranging for t:

$$t_{min} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \, p_{test} \, r \, SF}{2 \, \sigma_y}$$

This is the **minimum wall thickness** the drum must possess at its thinnest point (typically the sidewall mid-height, away from the corner ribbing).

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 200 L HDPE drum has inner diameter D = 580 mm. The UN hydrostatic test pressure is 100 kPa. HDPE yield strength σ_y = 25 MPa. Required safety factor SF = 2.5. Calculate the minimum wall thickness.

**Given Data:**

$$p_{test} = 100 \text{ kPa} = 100 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}, \quad r = 0.29 \text{ m}, \quad \sigma_y = 25 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}, \quad SF = 2.5$$

**Step 1 — Apply the derived formula:**

$$t_{min} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \, (100 \times 10^3) \, (0.29) \, (2.5)}{2 \, (25 \times 10^6)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute numerator:**

$$\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 1.732 \times 100 \times 10^3 \times 0.29 \times 2.5 = 1.256 \times 10^5 \text{ (units: Pa·m)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator:**

$$\text{Denominator} = 2 \times 25 \times 10^6 = 5.0 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Final Division:**

$$t_{min} = \frac{1.256 \times 10^5}{5.0 \times 10^7} = 2.51 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{t_{min} \approx 2.51 \text{ mm}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Industrial drums are produced with a sidewall thickness of **3.0 – 4.0 mm** to provide a 20–60% safety margin over the theoretical minimum and to handle drop-impact loads not captured in the hydrostatic test.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Container Type | Typical r (m) | t_min (mm) for UN Test | Manufactured t (mm) | Manufacturing Process |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pail 20 L** | 0.14 | 1.2 | 1.5 – 2.0 | Injection moulding |
| **Jerrican 20 L** | 0.14 | 1.2 | 1.8 – 2.2 | EBM |
| **Drum 60 L** | 0.20 | 1.7 | 2.5 – 3.0 | EBM |
| **Drum 200 L** | 0.29 | 2.5 | 3.0 – 4.0 | EBM |
| **IBC 1000 L** | 0.58 | 5.0 | 6.0 – 8.0 | EBM (blown bottle) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice the **square-root scaling**. If diameter doubles, thickness only needs to increase by factor √2 ≈ 1.414, not 2. This is why large IBCs remain economically feasible in HDPE.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Classification Hierarchy of Plastic Shipping Containers

This Mermaid block-diagram maps the family of plastic shipping containers discussed in Module 4, illustrating how capacity, construction, and application differentiate the formats.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[PLASTIC SHIPPING CONTAINERS] --> B[CAPACITY LESS THAN 25 LITRES]
    A --> C[CAPACITY 25 TO 220 LITRES]
    A --> D[CAPACITY ABOVE 220 LITRES]

    B --> B1[PAIL OPEN TOP]
    B --> B2[JERRICAN TIGHT HEAD]
    B --> B3[SMALL DRUM]

    B1 --> B1A[INJECTION MOULDED]
    B1 --> B1B[BAIL HANDLE METAL PLASTIC]
    B2 --> B2A[EBM PROCESS]
    B2 --> B2B[INTEGRAL HANDLE]
    B3 --> B3A[EBM PROCESS]

    C --> C1[TIGHT HEAD DRUM 1H1]
    C --> C2[OPEN HEAD DRUM 1H2]
    C --> C3[LEVER LOCK DRUM]

    C1 --> C1A[TWO BUNG CLOSURES]
    C1 --> C1B[FOOD AND CHEMICAL GRADE]
    C2 --> C2A[REMOVABLE LID WITH CLAMP RING]
    C2 --> C2B[GASKETED SEAL]
    C3 --> C3A[STEEL LEVER LOCK RING]
    C3 --> C3B[INDUSTRIAL PAINTS ADHESIVES]

    D --> D1[IBC INTERMEDIATE BULK CONTAINER]
    D --> D2[DRUM PALLET ASSEMBLY]

    D1 --> D1A[1000 LITRE STANDARD]
    D1 --> D1B[INNER HDPE BOTTLE]
    D1 --> D1C[OUTER STEEL CAGE]
    D1 --> D1D[WOODEN OR PLASTIC PALLET]
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Process Flow for HDPE Drum Manufacture (EBM Route)

This block-diagram shows the production sequence for the most common drum type studied in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[HDPE RESIN HOPPER] --> P2[EXTRUDER SCREW 25 TO 30 L D]
    P2 --> P3[MOLTEN HDPE PARISON]
    P3 --> P4[CLOSED MOULD CLAMPING]
    P4 --> P5[AIR INJECTION 6 TO 8 BAR]
    P5 --> P6[PARISON INFLATION AGAINST MOULD WALL]
    P6 --> P7[COOLING CYCLE 45 TO 90 SECONDS]
    P7 --> P8[MOULD OPEN AND PART EJECTION]
    P8 --> P9[TRIM FLASH AND DRILL BUNG HOLES]
    P9 --> P10[INSTALL BUNG CLOSURES]
    P10 --> P11[LEAK TEST HYDROSTATIC 100 KPA]
    P11 --> P12[STAMP UN CERTIFICATION MARK]
    P12 --> P13[FINISHED 200L DRUM]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Drum Functional Architecture Cross-Section

This block-diagram represents the structural regions of a 200 L HDPE tight-head drum. Each numbered zone corresponds to a specific engineering function the KTU examiner may test.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Z1[REGION 1 TOP CHIME] --> Z1A[STACKING INTERLOCK]
    Z2[REGION 2 UPPER SHOULDER] --> Z2A[TRANSITION STRESS RELIEF]
    Z3[REGION 3 CYLINDRICAL SIDEWALL] --> Z3A[HOOP STRESS CARRIER]
    Z3 --> Z3B[BELLY BAND RIB FOR STIFFNESS]
    Z4[REGION 4 LOWER SHOULDER] --> Z4A[DROP IMPACT ABSORBER]
    Z5[REGION 5 BOTTOM CHIME] --> Z5A[PALLET FORKLIFT CHANNEL]
    Z6[CLOSURE A 2 INCH NPT BUNG] --> Z6A[FILLING PORT WITH EPDM GASKET]
    Z7[CLOSURE B 2 INCH NPT BUNG] --> Z7A[VENTING AND DISPENSING PORT]
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Regulatory Compliance Flow

This diagram traces the testing sequence a manufactured drum must pass before being certified for shipping dangerous goods.

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1[DRUM MANUFACTURED HDPE EBM] --> R2[DIMENSIONAL VERIFICATION ISO 15750]
    R2 --> R3[DROP TEST 1.2 M GROUP II]
    R2 --> R4[LEAK PROOFNESS TEST 30 KPA]
    R2 --> R5[HYDROSTATIC TEST 100 KPA 30 MIN]
    R2 --> R6[STACKING TEST 1.8 S FACTOR]
    R3 --> R7{ALL TESTS PASS}
    R4 --> R7
    R5 --> R7
    R6 --> R7
    R7 --> R8[UN CERTIFICATION CODE E.G. 1H1 Y 1.4 200]
    R8 --> R9[LEGAL SHIPMENT OF DANGEROUS GOODS]
```

**Reading the UN Code:** A typical code `1H1/Y/200/.../NL/VI/5678` reads as:
- **1** → Drum
- **H** → Plastic (HDPE)
- **1** → Tight-head
- **Y** → Packing Group II/III
- **200** → Max gross mass in kg

---
