# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]
**List any three types of plastic shipping containers and state one typical application of each.**

**Model Answer:**
The three principal types of plastic shipping containers are:
1. **Pails** — Small open-top containers of 1 L to 25 L capacity, used for **paints, varnishes, and food pastes** (e.g., ice cream bulk packs).
2. **Jerricans** — Tight-head containers of 5 L to 30 L with an integral handle, used for **automotive lubricants and motor oil** by the retail consumer.
3. **Drums** — Cylindrical 20 L to 220 L containers, used for **bulk chemicals, edible oils, and pharmaceutical APIs** in industrial supply chains.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]
**Differentiate between a tight-head drum (UN code 1H1) and an open-head drum (UN code 1H2).**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Tight-Head Drum (1H1) | Open-Head Drum (1H2) |
|---|---|---|
| Head construction | Integral, non-removable top | Removable lid held by clamp ring |
| Closure mechanism | Two threaded NPT bungs with gaskets | Lever-lock steel ring or bolt ring |
| Primary use | Liquids (oils, acids, solvents) | Viscous pastes, solids, powders |
| Pressure resistance | Higher (suitable for pressurised contents) | Lower (relies on lid clamp force) |
| Reopening frequency | Limited | Frequent (designed for repeated access) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Module 4 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the constructional features of a 200 L HDPE blow-moulded drum with a neat functional diagram. Discuss why HDPE is preferred over PP for chemical drum applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 220 L plastic drum has an inner diameter of 585 mm and a sidewall thickness of 3.5 mm. During the UN hydrostatic pressure test, the drum is pressurised to 100 kPa (gauge). Calculate: (i) the hoop stress, (ii) the longitudinal stress, and (iii) the Von-Mises equivalent stress in the sidewall. Given σ_y = 25 MPa, comment on the safety factor achieved. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A 200 L HDPE drum is manufactured as a **single-piece extrusion blow-moulded** hollow body. Its main constructional regions are:

1. **Top chime:** A rolled upper rim that interlocks with the bottom chime of a stacked drum. Provides mechanical stack stability.
2. **Upper shoulder:** A toroidal transition zone that reduces stress concentration between the flat top and the cylindrical wall.
3. **Cylindrical sidewall:** Carries the **hoop stress** under internal pressure. Often features a horizontal **belly band** (an embossed rib) to increase bending stiffness without adding weight.
4. **Lower shoulder:** A geometric region designed to deform during a drop impact, absorbing energy that would otherwise fracture the bottom.
5. **Bottom chime:** A rolled lower rim forming the **forklift channel** and pallet interface.
6. **Closures:** Two 2-inch NPT bungs (filling and venting) sealed by **EPDM or Viton gaskets** depending on chemical compatibility.

**Why HDPE is preferred over PP for chemical drums:**

- **Environmental Stress Cracking (ESC) resistance:** HDPE significantly outperforms PP when in contact with surfactants, oils, and oxidizers.
- **Low-temperature toughness:** HDPE retains ductility down to **-40 °C**, whereas PP becomes brittle below 0 °C.
- **Better melt strength for blow moulding:** HDPE's long-chain branching provides superior parison stability during EBM.
- **Wider chemical compatibility:** HDPE is rated for use with over 90% of the chemicals in the **Chemical Resistance Database** (Curtis Ward), whereas PP has narrower compatibility.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** D = 585 mm → r = 0.2925 m; t = 3.5 mm = 3.5 × 10⁻³ m; p = 100 kPa = 100 × 10³ Pa; σ_y = 25 × 10⁶ Pa.

**(i) Hoop Stress:**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot r}{t} = \frac{(100 \times 10^3)(0.2925)}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\sigma_h = \frac{2.925 \times 10^4}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 8.357 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 8.36 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Longitudinal Stress:**

$$\sigma_l = \frac{p \cdot r}{2 t} = \frac{8.36}{2} = 4.18 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Von-Mises Equivalent Stress:**

$$\sigma_{vm} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \sigma_h = 0.866 \times 8.36 = 7.24 \text{ MPa}$$

**Safety Factor:**

$$SF = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{vm}} = \frac{25}{7.24} = 3.45$$

**Comment:** A safety factor of **3.45** comfortably exceeds the UN-mandated minimum of 2.5. The design is therefore acceptable, with a useful margin against creep, ESC, and temperature-induced strength loss.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various types of closures used in plastic shipping drums. How does the choice of gasket material affect chemical compatibility? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a block diagram, describe the manufacturing process of an HDPE 200 L drum by the extrusion blow moulding (EBM) route. State four advantages and two limitations of this process. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Drum Closure Types:**

1. **Threaded NPT Bung (2-inch):** The most common closure on tight-head drums. Consists of a buttress or NPT-threaded plug with a hex head, tightened to a torque of approximately 25 Nm. Used with a flat EPDM gasket.
2. **Tri-Sure® Flanged Closure:** A three-lug bayonet-style flange with a captive gasket. Provides rapid hand-tightened sealing, popular for chemical drums.
3. **Lever-Lock Ring (open-head drums):** A galvanized or stainless steel ring with an over-centre lever that clamps the lid against the drum body. Standard on UN 1H2 open-head drums.
4. **Screw Cap with Tear-off Band:** Used for tamper-evident pharmaceutical applications.
5. **Buttress Closure:** A coarse-thread closure for viscous products like honey or adhesives.

**Gasket Materials and Chemical Compatibility:**

| Gasket Material | Compatible With | Incompatible With |
|---|---|---|
| **EPDM** | Water, acids, alkalis, ketones | Petroleum oils, hydrocarbons |
| **Viton (FKM)** | Aromatic hydrocarbons, chlorinated solvents, fuels | Ketones, esters, hot water/steam |
| **PTFE (Teflon)** | Almost all chemicals (broad-spectrum) | Molten alkali metals, fluorine |
| **Nitrile (NBR)** | Petroleum oils, fuels | Strong oxidizers, ketones |
| **Silicone** | Food, pharmaceuticals, wide temperature | Concentrated solvents, acids |

The choice of gasket governs whether the drum may legally carry a given chemical. A mismatch causes swelling, leakage, or hazardous reactions.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**EBM Process Flow (Block Diagram in Words):**

HDPE resin is fed into the hopper of a reciprocating-screw extruder (typical L/D ratio 25:1 to 30:1). The screw plasticises and melts the resin at 180–220 °C, conveying it through a die head to form a **tubular parison**. Two mould halves close around the parison, pinching it at top and bottom. A **blow pin** inserts and injects compressed air at 6–8 bar, inflating the parison against the chilled mould cavity. After a **cooling cycle of 45–90 seconds**, the mould opens and the part is ejected. Flash is trimmed, bung holes drilled, closures installed, and the drum is leak-tested.

**Four Advantages of EBM for Drums:**
1. **Seamless, stress-relieved hollow body** — no weld lines, superior pressure integrity.
2. **Low tooling cost** compared to injection moulding of equivalent size.
3. **Fast material changeover** — supports small-batch specialty drums.
4. **Ability to mould complex integrated features** (handles, ribs, chimps) in one shot.

**Two Limitations of EBM:**
1. **Material wastage** from flash and trim — typically 10–25% of input mass.
2. **Wall thickness variation** along the parison due to sag and programming limitations; requires careful die-gap profiling.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in hoop vs longitudinal stress — accounts for ~30% of calculation errors.
> 2. **Mixing up UN codes** — "1H1" is tight-head, "1H2" is open-head, "1H3" is jerrican. Confusion here is a guaranteed -2 mark hit.
> 3. **Using yield strength of PP (≈ 35 MPa)** for an HDPE drum problem, or vice versa. Always **re-read the question** to identify the resin.
> 4. **Skipping units** in the worked numerical. KTU examiners actively deduct for missing units even if the final number is correct.
> 5. **Omitting the safety factor check** at the end of a stress calculation. The question often ends with "comment on safety" — students who only calculate without commenting lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Summary — Drums & Other Shipping Containers**

- Plastic shipping containers are categorised by **capacity**: pails (<25 L), jerricans (5–30 L), drums (20–220 L), IBCs (>220 L, typically 1000 L).
- The **200 L HDPE blow-moulded drum** is the global industrial benchmark, manufactured predominantly by **Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)**.
- **UN packaging codes** identify construction: **1** = drum, **H** = plastic, **1** = tight-head, **2** = open-head, **3** = jerrican.
- **HDPE** dominates over PP for chemical drums due to superior **ESC resistance**, low-temperature toughness, and blow-moulding melt strength.
- **Hoop stress** is the governing failure mode: $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p r}{t}$. It is **double** the longitudinal stress.
- **Von-Mises equivalent stress** for a thin-walled cylinder under internal pressure: $\sigma_{vm} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \sigma_h$.
- UN-mandated **minimum safety factor = 2.5** for HDPE drums.
- **Hydrostatic test pressure = 100 kPa** held for 30 minutes; this is the design basis for wall thickness.
- **Closure system** (bungs, lever-locks) is the most common leak-failure site; gasket material selection is dictated by **chemical compatibility**.
- **IBCs** (Intermediate Bulk Containers) are 1000 L composite units with an HDPE inner bottle, steel wire cage, and pallet base — standard for food-grade and hazardous chemical logistics.
- The four design load categories a drum must survive: **internal pressure, stacking compression, drop impact, creep / ESC**.
- **Drop test height** for Packing Group II is **1.2 m**; for Packing Group I it is **1.8 m**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing Plastic Packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Testing Plastic Packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Testing of Plastic Packages** is the systematic application of standardized mechanical, physical, chemical, barrier, optical, and migration-based evaluation methods performed on plastic packaging materials, components (films, sheets, containers, closures, laminates), and the finished pack to verify compliance with regulatory requirements (FDA 21 CFR, EU Regulation 1935/2004, EU 10/2011, BIS IS standards, ASTM, ISO), functional performance, product–package compatibility, and end-use durability.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT748)**, plastic package testing is positioned as a critical link between **material science**, **food/drug regulatory affairs**, and **supply chain logistics**. It ensures that the plastic package performs its four cardinal functions:

1. **Containment** – holds the product without failure.
2. **Protection** – resists mechanical, chemical, thermal, and biological hazards.
3. **Preservation** – controls transmission of gases, moisture, light, and aromas.
4. **Information/Presentation** – retains print, gloss, and appearance for shelf-life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEPOT748 – Module 4):**
> The module specifically covers testing of *tensile, tear, burst, puncture, impact, seal strength, drop, vibration, compression, permeability (WVTR, OTR, CO2TR), migration, organoleptic, and optical properties* of plastic packages.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Lifeguard & Suit" model:**
> Think of a plastic package as a **wetsuit worn by a swimmer (the product)** crossing a **shark-infested ocean (the supply chain)**. Before the swimmer enters the water, the lifeguard (Quality Control team) puts the suit through a series of drills:
> - **Tensile & tear tests** → "How much can we stretch it before it rips?" (mechanical strength of the fabric)
> - **WVTR / OTR tests** → "How fast does water seep through the seams?" (barrier performance)
> - **Drop / vibration tests** → "What happens if the diver falls off a boat or rides a choppy wave?" (distribution hazards)
> - **Migration tests** → "Does the rubber dye leak into the swimmer's skin?" (food-contact safety)
> - **Optical tests** → "Is the suit still glossy and clear after a swim?" (consumer appeal)
>
> Only when the suit passes **all** these drills is the swimmer allowed into the ocean. Likewise, only when a plastic package passes *every relevant test* is it cleared for real-world use.

---

## 1.3 Why Testing is Indispensable (KTU Board Perspective)

- **Regulatory Compliance:** Plastics in contact with food/pharma cannot be released without migration and overall migration limit (OML / SML) data.
- **Performance Guarantee:** Shelf-life claims (e.g., 12 months) must be substantiated by **accelerated aging and barrier test data**.
- **Failure Prevention:** ~**70 %** of packaging recalls in India (FSSAI database) are linked to inadequate seal strength or barrier failure.
- **Sustainability:** Recyclability and reusability claims require **sortability, density, and residual contaminant testing**.
- **Brand Protection:** Print adhesion, gloss, and color-fastness tests protect brand equity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Bodies Referenced in KTU Syllabus:**
> - **ASTM International** (American Society for Testing and Materials)
> - **ISO** (International Organization for Standardization)
> - **BIS** (Bureau of Indian Standards — IS series)
> - **TAPPI** (Technical Association of the Pulp and Paper Industry — for paper–plastic laminates)
> - **FDA** & **EU Regulations** for food-contact plastics.

---

## 1.4 Broad Classification of Plastic Package Tests

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The seven functional families of plastic package tests are:

1. **Mechanical Tests** — tensile, tear, burst, puncture, impact, seal strength, dart drop.
2. **Barrier / Permeation Tests** — WVTR, OTR, CO2TR, grease resistance.
3. **Migration / Chemical Safety Tests** — overall migration, specific migration, NIAS, residual solvents.
4. **Optical / Aesthetic Tests** — haze, transmittance, gloss, color, opacity.
5. **Thermal Tests** — heat-seal strength, hot-tack, DSC melting, heat distortion.
6. **Distribution / Logistic Tests** — drop, vibration, compression, stacking, ISTA protocol.
7. **Organoleptic / Sensory Tests** — odor transfer, taste transfer, Robinson test.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanical Tests — The "Strength Family"

### 2.1.1 Tensile Test (ASTM D882 for films, D638 for rigid plastics)
- **Principle:** A dumbbell-shaped or strip specimen is pulled at a constant rate until break.
- **Outputs:** Tensile strength (MPa), Young's Modulus (MPa), Elongation at break (%), Yield strength.
- **Equation (engineering stress):**
  $$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}$$
  where $F$ is the load (N) and $A_0$ is the original cross-section (m²).

### 2.1.2 Tear Resistance (ASTM D1922 – Elmendorf, ASTM D1004 – Graves)
- Measures the force required to propagate a pre-cut tear.
- Reported as **tear strength in N/mm or mN**.

### 2.1.3 Burst Strength (Mullen Tester, ASTM D774)
- Hydrostatic pressure applied to a film clamped over a rubber diaphragm until rupture.
- Critical for **sacks, woven sacks, paper–poly laminates**.

### 2.1.4 Puncture Resistance (ASTM F1306)
- Energy required for a probe to puncture a flexible package — vital for **blister packs and vacuum pouches**.

### 2.1.5 Dart Drop Impact (ASTM D1709)
- A free-falling dart with adjustable weight strikes a clamped film.
- **F50 (50 % failure weight)** is reported.

### 2.1.6 Heat Seal Strength (ASTM F88)
- Two films sealed at controlled temperature, pressure, dwell time, then peeled at 180° or 90°.
- **Failure modes:** cohesive (within the seal), adhesive (at interface), or peeling.

### 2.1.7 Hot-Tack Strength (ASTM F1921)
- Seal strength measured **immediately after sealing (≤ 0.5 s)** — critical for **form-fill-seal (FFS)** lines where the seal is stressed while still molten.

---

## 2.2 Barrier / Permeation Tests

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permeation is the steady-state diffusion of a permeant (gas/vapor) through a polymer film driven by a partial-pressure gradient.**

### 2.2.1 Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR) — ASTM F1249, ASTM E96
- Units: **g/m²/day** (or g/100 in²/day for US units).
- For **flexible films** of thickness $L$, permeability coefficient is:
  $$P_{H_2O} = \frac{\text{WVTR} \times L}{\Delta p}$$
  where $\Delta p$ is the partial pressure difference of water vapor across the film (Pa).

### 2.2.2 Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR) — ASTM D3985 (Coulometric), ASTM F1927
- Units: **cc/m²/day** or **cc/100 in²/day** (1 atm, 23 °C, 0 % RH unless stated).
- **Barrier classification (KTU reference):**
  - High barrier: OTR < 5 cc/m²/day
  - Medium barrier: 5–100 cc/m²/day
  - Low barrier: > 100 cc/m²/day

### 2.2.3 CO₂ Transmission Rate (CO2TR) — ASTM F2476
- Critical for **carbonated beverages and MAP (Modified Atmosphere Packaging)**.

### 2.2.4 Grease Resistance (TAPPI T507, Kit Test)
- Numbered Kit solutions (1–12) applied to film; **highest number not causing failure = grease resistance rating**.

### 2.2.5 Fick's First Law (Permeation Foundation)
$$J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

- $J$ = flux (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹)
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient (m²/s)
- $C$ = concentration (kg/m³)
- Under steady state, the integrated form gives **permeability**:
$$P = D \cdot S$$
- $S$ = solubility coefficient (Henry-type).
- Permeability is the **product of diffusivity and solubility** — a central KTU concept.

---

## 2.3 Migration / Chemical Safety Tests

### 2.3.1 Overall Migration Limit (OML)
- **EU 10/2011:** OML = **10 mg/dm²** of food-contact surface (or **60 mg/kg** simulant).
- Test simulants (EU): A (10 % ethanol), B (3 % acetic acid), C (20 % ethanol), D1 (50 % ethanol), D2 (vegetable oil), E (polyphenylene oxide powder — MPPO).

### 2.3.2 Specific Migration Limit (SML)
- Substance-specific, e.g., **Vinyl chloride monomer = 0.01 mg/kg**, **DEHP plasticizer = 1.5 mg/kg**, **Monomer styrene = 60 mg/kg** (these are *illustrative* KTU reference values; always check latest regulation).

### 2.3.3 NIAS (Non-Intentionally Added Substances)
- Reaction by-products, oligomers, impurities — require **screening and risk assessment** under EU 10/2011.

### 2.3.4 Residual Solvents (US Pharmacopeia <467>, ICH Q3C)
- Class 1, 2, 3 solvents with daily exposure limits.

---

## 2.4 Optical / Aesthetic Tests

| Property | Standard | Unit | Principle |
|---|---|---|---|
| Haze | ASTM D1003 | % | Diffuse transmittance / total transmittance |
| Total Transmittance | ASTM D1003 | % | Direct + diffuse |
| Gloss (60°) | ASTM D2457 | GU (Gloss Units) | Specular reflection at 60° |
| Opacity | TAPPI T425 | % | Contrast ratio of black/white backing |
| Yellowness Index (YI) | ASTM E313 | dimensionless | Spectrophotometric; important for clear films |
| Color (CIELab) | ASTM D2244 | L\*, a\*, b\* | Spectrophotometric color measurement |

---

## 2.5 Distribution / Logistic Tests

- **Drop Test (ASTM D5276, ISTA 1A, 2A, 3A):** Free-fall onto corners, edges, faces — simulates handling drops.
- **Vibration Test (ASTM D4728, ISTA):** Sinusoidal and random vibration; **1.5 g RMS, 60 min** typical.
- **Compression / Stacking (ASTM D642, TAPPI T 804):** Top-load or stacking load to failure.
- **Climatic Conditioning (ASTM D4332):** Pre-conditioning samples at 23 °C / 50 % RH for 24–48 h.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are testable in the KTU PEPOT748 ESE (End Semester Examination).**

| Concept | Formula | Units | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engineering Stress | $\sigma = F / A_0$ | Pa (MPa) | Tensile test |
| Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | dimensionless | Tensile / seal elongation |
| Young's Modulus | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | MPa | Modulus test |
| Tensile Energy Absorbed | $\text{TEA} = \int \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | J/m² | Toughness measure |
| WVTR Definition | $\text{WVTR} = \dot{m}_{H_2O} / A$ | g/m²/day | Moisture barrier |
| OTR Definition | $\text{OTR} = \dot{V}_{O_2} / A$ | cc/m²/day | Oxygen barrier |
| Permeability Coefficient | $P = D \cdot S$ | m²·s⁻¹ (× solubility) | Permeation |
| Fick's Law (steady) | $J = D \cdot \Delta C / L$ | kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | Permeation |
| Permeance | $R_p^{-1} = \text{WVTR}/\Delta p$ | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ | Engineering unit |
| Henry's Solubility | $C = S \cdot p$ | kg/m³ | Permeation |
| Activation Energy of Permeation | $P = P_0 \exp(-E_p/RT)$ | J/mol | Temperature effect |
| Migration into Food | $M = M_{P,\infty}\left(1 - \sum \frac{4\alpha_n^2 \exp(-D t q_n^2/L^2)}{...}\right)$ | mg/dm² | Crank's equation |
| Storage Stability Estimate | $\text{SLT} = k / (\text{WVTR})$ | days | Shelf-life prediction |
| Compression Buckling (thin cylinder) | $\sigma_{cr} = \frac{E t}{r \sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}}$ | Pa | Pallet stacking model |
| Gravimetric WVTR | $\text{WVTR} = \Delta w / (A \cdot t)$ | g/m²/day | ASTM E96 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Units & Permeation:** A *very common* mark loss in KTU exams is forgetting that **permeability (P)** and **permeance (P/L)** are different. Always report permeability in **barrer** or **cm³·cm·cm⁻²·s⁻¹·cmHg⁻¹**, and permeance in **cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹**.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharma blister packs:** OTR and WVTR data drive *shelf-life dossier* submissions to CDSCO / USFDA.
- **Carbonated soft drinks (PET):** CO2TR loss rate must be < **17 %** over 16 weeks (industry benchmark).
- **Microwave-ready trays:** Heat-distortion and hot-tack data determine FFS line speed.
- **Milk pouches (LDPE):** Dart drop and seal strength govern BIS IS 14625 compliance.
- **EVOH/EVOH-PET laminates:** OTR < 1 cc/m²/day — enables shelf-stable retort pouches.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Permeation Through a Plastic Film (Fick + Henry)

We derive the **permeability equation** that is the theoretical backbone of barrier testing for plastic packages. This derivation is *frequently* tested in KTU PEPOT748 ESE — both in theory (Part A) and numerical sub-parts (Part B).

### Given:
- Polymer film of uniform thickness $L$ (m).
- Steady-state 1D diffusion along the $x$-axis.
- Upstream partial pressure of permeant = $p_1$ (Pa).
- Downstream partial pressure = $p_2$ (Pa), with $p_2 \approx 0$ in most lab WVTR / OTR cells.

### Step 1 — Write Fick's First Law
Fick's first law states that the flux $J$ (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹) of permeant through a cross-section is proportional to the concentration gradient:

$$J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

**Explanation:** $D$ is the diffusion coefficient of the permeant in the polymer matrix, and $\partial C/\partial x$ is the gradient that drives flow from high to low concentration.

### Step 2 — Apply Steady-State Condition
Under steady state, the flux $J$ does not vary with time or position. Therefore the gradient must be linear:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = \frac{C_2 - C_1}{L} = -\frac{C_1 - C_2}{L}$$

### Step 3 — Use Henry's Law of Sorption
At each film surface, the local concentration is in equilibrium with the partial pressure of the permeant:

$$C = S \cdot p$$

**Explanation:** $S$ is the solubility coefficient (kg·m⁻³·Pa⁻¹). Substituting:

$$J = D \cdot \frac{S(p_1 - p_2)}{L} = \frac{P \cdot (p_1 - p_2)}{L}$$

where we have defined the **permeability coefficient**:

$$P \equiv D \cdot S$$

### Step 4 — Final Permeation Equation
$$\boxed{J = \frac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}}$$

**Explanation:** Permeant flux is directly proportional to the pressure difference $\Delta p = p_1 - p_2$ and inversely proportional to film thickness. The product $D \cdot S$ combines the *kinetic* (diffusion) and *thermodynamic* (solubility) contributions to permeation.

### Step 5 — Express in Test-Useful Units
For **WVTR** (g/m²/day) with $\Delta p$ in Pa and $L$ in meters, conversion factor 86400 s/day must be used:

$$\text{WVTR} = \frac{P_{H_2O} \cdot \Delta p_{H_2O}}{L} \cdot \frac{1\ \text{day}}{86400\ \text{s}} \cdot \text{conversion of kg→g}$$

For OTR, $J$ is converted to **cm³(STP)/m²/day** using the ideal gas law at standard temperature.

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-style)

> **Problem:** A PET bottle wall is **0.30 mm** thick. Permeability of CO₂ through PET is $P = 1.4 \times 10^{-13}$ cm³·cm·cm⁻²·s⁻¹·cmHg⁻¹. Internal CO₂ partial pressure = **4.0 atm (≈ 3040 cmHg)**, external = 0. Calculate CO₂ loss in cm³/m²/day.

**Solution — Step 1: Convert units.**
$1\ \text{m}^2 = 10^4\ \text{cm}^2$, $1\ \text{day} = 86400\ \text{s}$, $L = 0.30\ \text{mm} = 0.030\ \text{cm}$.

**Solution — Step 2: Apply permeation equation.**
$J = (P \cdot \Delta p)/L = (1.4 \times 10^{-13} \times 3040)/0.030\ \text{cm}^3/\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$

$\Rightarrow J = 1.4187 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{cm}^3/\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$

**Solution — Step 3: Convert to cm³/m²/day.**
$J = 1.4187 \times 10^{-8} \times 10^4 \times 86400 = 12.26\ \text{cm}^3/\text{m}^2/\text{day}$

**Answer:** CO₂TR ≈ **12.3 cm³/m²/day** (this is the *steady-state* loss; real bottles show a *non-steady lag phase* of ~10–14 days first).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Temperature Dependence (Arrhenius Form)

The permeability coefficient typically follows an Arrhenius temperature dependence:

$$P = P_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_p}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 1:** Take natural log of both sides:

$$\ln P = \ln P_0 - \frac{E_p}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

**Step 2:** A plot of $\ln P$ vs. $1/T$ is linear; slope = $-E_p / R$.

**Step 3:** For **activation energy of permeation $E_p$**, values are typically:
- PE / PP: 30–45 kJ/mol
- PET: 35–50 kJ/mol
- EVOH (dry): 50–70 kJ/mol

**Engineering use:** A 10 °C rise in temperature roughly **doubles** the OTR of a polyolefin — this is the basis of *accelerated shelf-life testing* at 40 °C / 75 % RH for tropical climates.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shelf-Life Estimation from OTR

For a product with critical oxygen tolerance $Q_{O_2}$ (cm³ of O₂ tolerated per package) and an effective headspace of volume $V_h$:

$$\text{SLT} = \frac{Q_{O_2}}{\text{OTR} \cdot A}$$

- $A$ = permeable surface area (m²)
- SLT = Shelf Life Time (days)

**Example:** Sachet of milk powder, $A = 0.02\ \text{m}^2$, OTR of laminate = 1.0 cc/m²/day, $Q_{O_2} = 5\ \text{cc}$.
$\text{SLT} = 5 / (1.0 \times 0.02) = 250\ \text{days} \approx 8.3\ \text{months}$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Overall Migration Calculation (Crank's Series Solution)

For a plastic layer of thickness $L$ in contact with a single-sided food simulant, the time-dependent migration follows Fick's second law:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

With initial condition $C(x,0) = C_0$ (uniform), and boundary condition $C(0,t) = 0$ (infinite simulant), the analytical solution for the cumulative mass migrated per unit area is:

$$M_t = M_{\infty} \left[ 1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2n+1)^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{D(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 t}{4L^2}\right) \right]$$

**Explanation:** At short times $M_t \propto \sqrt{t}$ (Fickian "√t" behaviour); at long times $M_t \to M_{\infty} = C_0 L / 2$.

**Engineering use:** EU Regulation 10/2011 sets the OML test at **10 days at 40 °C** as the standard condition. Acceleration is permitted only with scientifically validated diffusion models — exactly the equation above.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Two direct-conceptual questions per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, with crisp model answers.**

---

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, 3 Marks]

**Define the following terms with one example each:**
(a) Overall Migration Limit (OML)
(b) Specific Migration Limit (SML)
(c) WVTR

**Model Answer (for 3-mark full credit):**

> **(a) OML** — The maximum permitted total amount of *all* non-volatile substances transferred from a plastic food-contact material into a food simulant, set by **EU Regulation 10/2011 at 10 mg/dm²** of the food-contact surface. *Example:* Total extractives from a PP yogurt cup after 10 days at 40 °C in 3 % acetic acid must be < 10 mg/dm².
>
> **(b) SML** — The maximum permitted amount of a *specific, named* substance (e.g., a monomer, additive, or catalyst residue) that may migrate from plastic into food. Each substance has its own toxicologically derived limit. *Example:* Vinyl chloride monomer SML = **0.01 mg/kg** food.
>
> **(c) WVTR (Water Vapor Transmission Rate)** — The steady-state mass of water vapor transmitted through a unit area of film per unit time under specified temperature and humidity conditions. *Example:* A typical LDPE milk pouch has WVTR ≈ 5–8 g/m²/day at 38 °C / 90 % RH (ASTM F1249).

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, 3 Marks]

**Explain the working principle of the Elmendorf tear test (ASTM D1922). What does the result indicate about a plastic film's suitability for packaging?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Elmendorf test measures the force required to **propagate a pre-cut tear** through a plastic film. A pendulum-mounted clamp holds the specimen, a pre-cut notch is made, the pendulum is released, and the tear propagates through the film. The **energy absorbed** is read directly from the dial as **tear strength in grams-force (gf) or millinewtons (mN)**.
>
> High tear strength is critical for **sacks, pouches, and shrink films** that may experience impact or puncture during transit. LDPE typically has tear strength in the range **50–300 gf** (machine direction) and **80–500 gf** (transverse direction) — values < 50 gf indicate a brittle, easy-tear film unsuitable for heavy-duty packaging.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **Following KTU 2024 ESE pattern, internal choice is mandatory in Part B. We present Question A and Question B (alternates) with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.**

---

### Question A. (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** With neat sketches explain the **Coulometric method for Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR) measurement** as per ASTM D3985. List the key test conditions and the mathematical relationship used to compute permeability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **300 µm thick PET bottle wall** is being evaluated for carbonated beverage (cola) packaging. The permeability of CO₂ through PET is $P = 1.4 \times 10^{-13}$ cm³·cm·cm⁻²·s⁻¹·cmHg⁻¹. The internal CO₂ partial pressure is **4.0 atm** and the external is **0.4 atm**. Calculate:
  (i) The **steady-state CO₂ loss rate (CO₂TR) in cm³/m²/day**.
  (ii) The **shelf life of a 500 mL bottle** if the original CO₂ content is **3.5 volumes** and the threshold of consumer acceptance is **2.5 volumes**. Take the bottle's permeable surface area as **0.045 m²** and density of CO₂ at STP = **1.977 g/L**. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — Coulometric OTR Method (ASTM D3985)

> **Step 1 — Principle:** A test film is clamped between two chambers. **Nitrogen (N₂) carrier gas** flows on one side (the *receiving* side), and **pure O₂** flows on the other (the *test* side). The O₂ permeates through the film into the N₂ stream, which carries it to a **coulometric sensor**.

> **Step 2 — Coulometric Detection:** The sensor contains an electrolyte that reacts quantitatively with O₂. The current generated is **proportional to the rate of O₂ arrival** (Faraday's law of electrolysis).

> **Step 3 — Test Conditions:** ASTM D3985 specifies **23 °C**, **0 % RH on the test side** (or controlled RH), **1 atm O₂ pressure**, with results normalized to **cm³/m²/day at STP**.

> **Step 4 — Calculation:** From Fick + Henry:
> $$\text{OTR} = \frac{P_{O_2} \cdot \Delta p}{L}$$
> Permeability is then back-calculated once OTR is known.

> **Step 5 — Sketches expected:** (i) Cross-section of the test cell showing the two halves and the clamped film; (ii) Block diagram of gas flow — O₂ test side, N₂ carrier, coulometric sensor, readout.

> **Marks distribution (typical KTU):** 2 marks principle, 2 marks sensor + Faraday, 1 mark conditions, 2 marks equation + sketch.

#### Part (b) — Numerical CO₂TR and Shelf Life

**Step 1 — Unit conversions.**
- $L = 300\ \mu\text{m} = 0.030\ \text{cm}$
- $P_{CO_2} = 1.4 \times 10^{-13}$ cm³·cm·cm⁻²·s⁻¹·cmHg⁻¹
- $\Delta p = (4.0 - 0.4)\ \text{atm} = 3.6\ \text{atm}$
- $1\ \text{atm} = 76\ \text{cmHg}$, so $\Delta p = 3.6 \times 76 = 273.6\ \text{cmHg}$

**Step 2 — Compute flux $J$.**
$$J = \frac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L} = \frac{(1.4 \times 10^{-13})(273.6)}{0.030}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation.**
$$J = \frac{3.8304 \times 10^{-11}}{0.030} = 1.2768 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{cm}^3/\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to cm³/m²/day.**
- 1 m² = 10⁴ cm² → multiply by 10⁴
- 1 day = 86 400 s → multiply by 86 400

$$J = 1.2768 \times 10^{-9} \times 10^4 \times 86400 = 1.103\ \text{cm}^3/\text{m}^2/\text{day}$$

**Step 5 — Total CO₂ loss per day from bottle.**
$$\dot{V}_{CO_2} = J \cdot A = 1.103 \times 0.045 = 0.0496\ \text{cm}^3/\text{day (at STP)}$$

**Step 6 — Mass loss per day.**
$$m_{CO_2/day} = 0.0496 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{L} \times 1.977\ \text{g/L} = 9.81 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{g/day}$$

**Step 7 — Initial CO₂ mass in 500 mL bottle at 3.5 volumes.**
"Volumes" = (Volume of CO₂ at STP)/(Volume of beverage).
Volume of CO₂ at STP = 3.5 × 500 mL = 1750 mL = 1.75 L.
Mass = $1.75 \times 1.977 = 3.460\ \text{g}$.

**Step 8 — Threshold CO₂ mass at 2.5 volumes.**
Volume at 2.5 volumes = 2.5 × 500 mL = 1250 mL = 1.25 L.
Mass = $1.25 \times 1.977 = 2.471\ \text{g}$.

**Step 9 — Allowable loss.**
$\Delta m = 3.460 - 2.471 = 0.989\ \text{g}$.

**Step 10 — Shelf life.**
$$\text{SLT} = \frac{\Delta m}{\dot{m}} = \frac{0.989}{9.81 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 10\,080\ \text{days}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reality check:** The number 10 080 days is *physically impossible* because **steady-state permeation has not yet been reached**; the actual bottle shows a *lag time* of 10–14 days during which CO₂ loss is much lower. With lag-time correction and temperature factors (25 °C vs 4 °C storage), the realistic shelf life is **12–16 weeks** — this is the standard benchmark for carbonated soft drinks (CSD) in PET.

**Marks distribution (typical KTU):** 2 marks unit conversion, 2 marks formula application, 1 mark CO2TR computation, 2 marks shelf-life computation, and the lag-time discussion (1 mark) is the *bonus* mark that distinguishes a 7/7 from a 5/7 answer.

---

### Question B. (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE ALTERNATIVE [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Discuss the **overall migration test procedure** as per **EU Regulation 10/2011**, including food simulants, test conditions, and acceptance criteria. How does it differ from the US FDA approach? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **multilayer laminate** (PET 12 µm / Al foil 9 µm / LDPE 50 µm) is used for a UHT milk pouch. WVTR of the laminate is measured as **0.5 g/m²/day** at 38 °C / 90 % RH. The pouch holds **500 mL of milk** with initial water content of **87.5 %** by mass, and a **5 % loss of water** is the rejection threshold. Calculate the **shelf life of the pouch**. Density of milk ≈ 1.03 g/mL. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) — EU 10/2011 Overall Migration

> **Step 1 — Scope:** Applies to *all* plastic food-contact materials and articles, including multilayer constructions. It supersedes the older Directive 2002/72/EC.

> **Step 2 — Food Simulants (six):**
> - **Simulant A** → 10 % ethanol (hydrophilic, simulates water-based foods)
> - **Simulant B** → 3 % acetic acid (acidic foods, e.g., salad dressings)
> - **Simulant C** → 20 % ethanol (alcoholic foods up to 20 % ABV)
> - **Simulant D1** → 50 % ethanol (alcoholic foods > 20 % ABV)
> - **Simulant D2** → vegetable oil (fatty foods, e.g., olive oil, sunflower oil)
> - **Simulant E** → poly(2,6-diphenyl-p-phenylene oxide), MPPO, used as a dry-food substitute (for migration into dry foods only)

> **Step 3 — Standard Test Conditions (OML, default):**
> - **10 days at 40 °C** is the universal condition that *covers* all storage times ≥ 6 months at room temperature and above.
> - Higher-temperature applications: e.g., **2 h at 70 °C** for hot-fill, **1 h at 100 °C** + 2 h at 40 °C for retort.

> **Step 4 — Procedure:** Plastic sample of measured surface area is immersed in (or single-side contacted with) the chosen simulant under the specified T–t conditions. Simulant is then **evaporated**, and the **residue is weighed gravimetrically**. OML = mass / area, with the **limit of 10 mg/dm²**.

> **Step 5 — US FDA Approach (contrast):** The FDA's **21 CFR 174–179** framework uses *extractives testing* with food-simulating solvents (water, 8 % ethanol, 50 % ethanol, n-heptane, corn oil) under specific conditions (e.g., **120 °F / 24 h** for hot-fill). Crucially, the FDA sets *non-specific extractives* limits (e.g., **0.5 mg/in²** in extractables) and *specific* limits via the **Threshold of Regulation** and **Food Contact Substance Notification (FCN)**.

> **Marks distribution (typical KTU):** 2 marks simulants, 2 marks conditions + procedure, 2 marks OML value, 1 mark FDA contrast.

#### Part (b) — Shelf Life from WVTR

**Step 1 — Geometry.**
Pouch holds 500 mL of milk with density 1.03 g/mL.
Initial mass of milk = $500 \times 1.03 = 515\ \text{g}$.
Initial water mass = $515 \times 0.875 = 450.6\ \text{g}$.

**Step 2 — Allowable water loss.**
$\Delta m_{water} = 450.6 \times 0.05 = 22.53\ \text{g}$.

**Step 3 — Pouch permeable area.**
The pouch is a flat sachet; assuming a typical surface area of **$A = 0.024\ \text{m}^2$** (provided in standard problem or assumed from geometry).

**Step 4 — Water loss rate.**
$\dot{m} = \text{WVTR} \times A = 0.5 \times 0.024 = 0.012\ \text{g/day}$.

**Step 5 — Shelf life.**
$$\text{SLT} = \frac{\Delta m_{water}}{\dot{m}} = \frac{22.53}{0.012} = 1877.5\ \text{days} \approx 5.1\ \text{years}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** The figure **5.1 years** is *unrealistic* for UHT milk — the *real* rejection driver is **flavour deterioration** and **vitamin loss** (occurring in 3–6 months), not moisture loss. The calculation above is the *theoretical* shelf life; the **practical shelf life** is governed by **organoleptic and microbiological limits**. KTU students are expected to mention this distinction to fetch full 7/7 marks.

**Marks distribution (typical KTU):** 1.5 marks geometry, 1.5 marks water mass, 1.5 marks loss rate, 1.5 marks SLT computation, 1 mark practical-shelf-life caveat.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in PEPOT748 ESE (Module 4):**
>
> 1. **Units trap:** Reporting OTR in "cc/m²/day" *without* clarifying **at STP, 1 atm, 23 °C, 0 % RH** loses 1 mark. Always specify test conditions.
> 2. **Permeability vs. Permeance confusion:** Permeability $P$ has the thickness *inside* its definition; permeance has it *outside*. Many students write "$P/L$" and call it $P$ — instant -1 mark.
> 3. **Migration limits:** OML = **10 mg/dm²** (food-contact surface), but in food-mass terms it's **60 mg/kg**. Many students quote the wrong denominator.
> 4. **Burst vs. Tensile:** Mullen burst tests *hydrostatic* pressure, not *uniaxial* tensile. The two values are *not* interchangeable.
> 5. **Seal strength units:** Always quote in **N/15 mm** (or N/inch) — quoting in plain "N" loses the "per unit width" specification.
> 6. **Optical tests:** Haze is a *percentage*, not a transmittance value. Mis-reporting haze as %T loses marks.
> 7. **ISTA sequence:** Drop → Vibration → Compression → Climatic. Reversing the order is a common error.
> 8. **Shelf-life derivations:** Forgetting the *lag time* in CO₂ loss from PET is a high-frequency mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU PEPOT748 — Module 4: Testing Plastic Packages**

- [x] **Seven test families** = Mechanical, Barrier, Migration, Optical, Thermal, Distribution, Organoleptic.
- [x] **Tensile test** standard for films = **ASTM D882**; for rigid plastics = **ASTM D638**.
- [x] **Elmendorf tear** = ASTM D1922; result in **mN or gf**.
- [x] **Burst strength (Mullen)** = ASTM D774; result in **kPa or psi**.
- [x] **Dart drop impact** = ASTM D1709; **F50** weight reported.
- [x] **Heat seal strength** = ASTM F88; units **N/15 mm**; failure modes = cohesive / adhesive / peeling.
- [x] **Hot-tack** = ASTM F1921; for FFS lines.
- [x] **WVTR** = ASTM F1249; units **g/m²/day**.
- [x] **OTR** = ASTM D3985 (Coulometric); units **cc/m²/day**.
- [x] **CO₂TR** = ASTM F2476; critical for CSD.
- [x] **Barrier classification** — High OTR < 5; Medium 5–100; Low > 100 cc/m²/day.
- [x] **Fick's first law** = $J = -D \, \partial C / \partial x$.
- [x] **Permeability equation** = $P = D \cdot S$ and $J = P \Delta p / L$.
- [x] **Arrhenius form** = $P = P_0 \exp(-E_p / RT)$; 10 °C rise ≈ doubles OTR.
- [x] **OML = 10 mg/dm²** (EU 10/2011) — *or* **60 mg/kg** simulant.
- [x] **Six food simulants** = A, B, C, D1, D2, E (MPPO).
- [x] **Standard migration test** = 10 days, 40 °C.
- [x] **Haze** = ASTM D1003, reported as **%**.
- [x] **Gloss** = ASTM D2457, reported in **GU** at 60° (or 20°/45°/75°).
- [x] **Yellowness Index (YI)** = ASTM E313.
- [x] **Drop test** = ASTM D5276; ISTA 1A, 2A, 3A.
- [x] **Vibration** = ASTM D4728; **1.5 g RMS, 60 min** typical.
- [x] **Compression / top-load** = ASTM D642.
- [x] **Pre-conditioning** = ASTM D4332; 23 °C / 50 % RH, 24–48 h.
- [x] **Robinson test** for organoleptic evaluation.
- [x] **Shelf-life formula** = $SLT = Q_{O_2} / (\text{OTR} \cdot A)$.
- [x] **CSD PET benchmark** = < 17 % CO₂ loss over 16 weeks.
- [x] **NDT lag time** = 10–14 days for PET bottles.
- [x] **Storage** = $4 P$ values of PET OTR: < 1 cc/m²/day for premium barrier laminates.
- [x] **NIAS** = Non-Intentionally Added Substances; screen via GC-MS / LC-MS.
- [x] **FDA 21 CFR 174–179** = US extractives framework.
- [x] **BIS IS 14625** = Indian standard for plastic milk pouches.
- [x] **Crank's migration equation** (series solution) for time-dependent $M_t$.
- [x] **Fickian "√t"** behavior at short times for migration.
- [x] **EVOH** is the gold standard for high-O₂ barrier when dry; humidity-sensitive.
- [x] **PVDC** is the high-barrier standard for WVTR (e.g., Saran™).
- [x] **Al-foil laminates** reduce WVTR to < 0.01 g/m²/day — *essentially* zero.
- [x] **Always specify test conditions** in any numerical answer: T, RH, pressure, sample area, units.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic (KTU Special):** "**T**esting is **B**asic for **M**aking **P**ackages **S**afe" — TB-MPS = **Tensile → Burst → Migration → Permeation → Seal**. Use this to recall the order in which tests are typically performed on a new plastic pack.

---
