<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define **Overall Migration Limit (OML)** as per EU Regulation 10/2011. State its standard value and the food simulants used for fatty, aqueous, and alcoholic food testing.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** OML is the maximum permitted total mass of all non-volatile substances that can migrate from a food contact material into a food simulant under standardized test conditions.
- **Standard value (1 mark):** $\text{OML} = 10\ \text{mg/dm}^2$ of food contact surface (equivalently $60\ \text{mg/kg}$ of food).
- **Simulants (1 mark):**
  - Simulant A: Distilled water (aqueous foods)
  - Simulant B: 3% acetic acid (acidic foods)
  - Simulant C: 10% ethanol (alcoholic foods)
  - Simulant D2: Vegetable oil (fatty foods)

---

**Q2.** What is **"Set-Off Migration"** in multilayer flexible packaging? How is it controlled in food contact laminates?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Set-off migration is the transfer of substances (especially from printing inks, adhesives, or coatings) from the **outer non-food-contact surface** of a packaging web to the **inner food-contact surface** during reel winding, storage, or transport, before the laminate is formed into a final package.
- **Control measures (1.5 marks):**
  1. Incorporation of a **functional barrier layer** (e.g., PE or EVA) between the printed layer and the food contact layer
  2. Use of **UV-cured or water-based inks** with low migration potential
  3. Compliance with **EuPIA Guideline** on printing inks for food contact materials

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question – Module 4 Choice]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the major types of **chemical migration hazards** in plastic packaging. Discuss the mechanism of migration with reference to **Fick's laws of diffusion**, and explain how the **Crank equation** is used for short-time migration estimation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A multilayer laminate consists of an inner LLDPE sealant (50 µm), middle EVOH barrier (15 µm), and outer PET (12 µm). The laminate is used to package a fruit juice stored at 25°C for 6 months. Discuss the role of each layer in hazard control, and estimate the **oxygen transmission rate (OTR)** if the permeability of EVOH is $0.01\ \text{cm}^3 \cdot \mu\text{m}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day} \cdot \text{atm})$ and the partial pressure difference is 0.21 atm.

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) – Classification and Migration Mechanism**

- **Classification of chemical migration hazards (3 marks):**
  1. **Monomer migration** – e.g., vinyl chloride from PVC, styrene from PS, bisphenol A from polycarbonate
  2. **Additive migration** – plasticizers (phthalates), stabilizers (BHT, Irganox), slip agents (oleamide)
  3. **Impurity migration** – oligomers, catalyst residues, processing aids
  4. **Set-off migration** – from outer printed/laminated surfaces
  5. **Substance transfer from recycled materials** – e.g., contaminants in rPET

- **Mechanism via Fick's Laws (2 marks):**
  - **Fick's First Law:** $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ governs steady-state flux
  - **Fick's Second Law:** $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ governs transient concentration profiles
  - The migrant diffuses through the polymer amorphous regions, partitions at the polymer-food interface, and disperses into the food.

- **Crank Equation (2 marks):**
  - For short contact times: $M(t) = \dfrac{2 C_0 \sqrt{D \cdot t}}{\sqrt{\pi}}$
  - Valid when $\dfrac{Dt}{L^2} < 0.1$
  - Indicates migration scales with $\sqrt{t}$, useful for early shelf-life prediction.

**Part (b) – Multilayer Laminate Hazard Control and OTR**

- **Role of each layer (4 marks):**
  - **LLDPE sealant (50 µm):** Provides hermetic heat seal, chemical inertness to juice (acidity), and acts as a moisture barrier. Inner food-contact layer.
  - **EVOH barrier (15 µm):** Excellent oxygen barrier due to hydrogen bonding in the polymer backbone; reduces oxidative spoilage of vitamin C and flavor compounds.
  - **PET (12 µm):** Provides mechanical strength, printability, dimensional stability, and acts as the outermost protective layer.

- **OTR Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
\text{OTR} = \frac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}
$$

$$
\text{OTR} = \frac{0.01 \cdot 0.21}{15} = 1.4 \times 10^{-4}\ \frac{\text{cm}^3}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day}}
$$

**Interpretation:** OTR is extremely low, ensuring juice quality over 6 months at 25°C.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently make the mistake of using **thickness in µm** without converting it to **m** in the denominator. Ensure unit consistency. Also, many forget that the OTR equation for a laminate follows the **resistance-in-series model** when multiple layers contribute. Always state assumptions clearly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **biological and physical hazards** in plastic packaging, with specific examples for each category. How does **Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP)** and **HACCP** address these hazards?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Evaluate the role of **functional barriers** in preventing migration in multilayer plastic packaging. With a suitable diagram, describe a **PET/EVOH/PE laminate** used for retort pouches, and explain how each layer contributes to hazard control under retort conditions (121°C, 30 minutes).

**Model Answer Outline:**

**Part (a) – Biological and Physical Hazards (7 Marks)**

- **Biological hazards (3 marks):**
  - Bacterial contamination: *Salmonella*, *E. coli*, *Listeria* from seal failure
  - Mould and yeast growth due to moisture ingress
  - Spore-forming bacteria surviving inadequate sterilization
  - Example: Botulism risk in inadequately retorted low-acid foods

- **Physical hazards (2 marks):**
  - Glass fragments, metal shavings, broken seals
  - Sharp edges, foreign object inclusion
  - Example: Metal fragments from can lid failures

- **GMP and HACCP control (2 marks):**
  - **GMP:** Sanitary design of equipment, controlled environment, validated cleaning, personnel hygiene
  - **HACCP:** Identification of Critical Control Points (CCPs) such as sealing temperature, sterilization time, storage temperature; setting critical limits, monitoring procedures, corrective actions

**Part (b) – Functional Barriers and Retort Pouches (7 Marks)**

- **Functional barrier concept (3 marks):**
  - A functional barrier is a polymer layer that reduces migration of substances from an outer layer to the food contact layer by a factor of at least **100× (2 log units)** to ensure compliance with SML
  - Common materials: PE, PP, PET, EVOH

- **PET/EVOH/PE retort pouch (2 marks):**
  - PET (outer): Heat resistance, puncture resistance, printability
  - EVOH (middle): Oxygen barrier, retort-stable when moisture protected
  - PE (inner): Heat seal, moisture barrier, food contact

- **Performance under retort (2 marks):**
  - At 121°C, EVOH barrier drops slightly (humidity effect), but the surrounding PE layers prevent direct moisture contact, maintaining OTR < 1 cc/m²/day·atm post-retort
  - The inner PE provides migration control from outer layers

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse **HACCP** with **Hazard Analysis**, or list CCPs without linking them to measurable critical limits. Examiners expect explicit linkage: e.g., "Sealing temperature ≥ 180°C for 2 seconds is a CCP because below this, seal strength < 1.5 N/15mm, risking microbial ingress."

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 – Module 4**

- **OML = 10 mg/dm²** (EU 10/2011) is the most tested numerical value.
- **Fick's Second Law** is the foundation of migration modeling; the **Crank equation** applies for short times only.
- **$P = D \cdot S$** is the permeability–diffusion–solubility relationship — memorize the units.
- **Functional barriers** must reduce migration by **≥ 2 log units (100×)** to be considered effective.
- **Set-off migration** is unique to printed/laminated structures and is controlled by inner PE/EVA layers.
- **EVOH** loses barrier performance at high humidity — must be sandwiched between moisture barriers.
- **Standard migration test:** 10 days at 40°C (long-term storage simulation).
- **Simulant D2 (vegetable oil)** is used for fatty foods; olive oil and its substitutes are now accepted alternatives.
- **HACCP** is mandatory for food packaging manufacturers; CCPs must have measurable critical limits.
- **rPET** has a **Positive List (Regulation 282/2008)**; only approved recycling processes are permitted.
- **Acetaldehyde (AA) in PET** is a flavor tainting concern; SML = 6 mg/kg food (or 1 mg/dm²).
- **Microplastics** are an emerging hazard — focus on nanoscale coatings, biodegradable alternatives, and proper end-of-life management.
- **Indian regulations:** FSSAI Packaging Regulations 2018, IS 14543 (BIS standards), Plastics Waste Management Rules 2022.
- **Always state units** in numerical answers; examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units.
- **Use dimensionless time** $Dt/L^2$ to decide between short-time and long-time migration regimes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT748 - PLASTICS PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Environmental Considerations # Environmental Considerations in Plastics Packaging Technology ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Environmental Considerations in Plastics Packaging** refers to the systematic evaluation, design, and implementation of packaging solutions that minimize ecological burden throughout the entire life cycle of plastic materials — from raw material extraction, manufacturing, distribution, and consumer use, to end-of-life management including disposal, recycling, recovery, and degradation. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme, this domain integrates three pivotal pillars: > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Three Pillars of Sustainable Packaging** > 1. **Material Sustainability** — Selection of bio-based, recycled, or biodegradable polymers. > 2. **Design for Environment (DfE)** — Engineering packaging to reduce material, energy, and waste. > 3. **End-of-Life Management** — Effective recovery through the 4R framework (Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover). The governing standard referenced globally is **ISO 14001:2015** (Environmental Management Systems) and **ISO 18602:2013** (Packaging and the Environment — Optimization of the Packaging System), with **per-capita plastic consumption** averaging **28 kg/year** globally and approximately **3.5 kg/year** in India (CPCB, 2023 estimate). > [!NOTE] > **Key Term — Life Cycle Assessment (LCA):** > A standardized methodology (per **ISO 14040/14044**) that quantifies the environmental impacts of a product, process, or service across its entire life cycle — quantified through *Cradle-to-Grave* or *Cradle-to-Cradle* boundary conditions. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a plastic water bottle as a **"traveling soldier"** in the army of consumer goods. From the moment crude oil is drilled from the earth (its *birth*), through the petrochemical refinery (its *training*), to the bottling plant (its *deployment*), and finally to a landfill where it may lie dormant for **up to 450 years** (its *long retirement*) — every step leaves an *environmental footprint*, much like footprints in sand. Now consider three modern interventions: | Real-World Analogy | Environmental Equivalent | |---|---| | A soldier who is decommissioned and recycled into training dummies | **Mechanical Recycling** of PET | | A soldier who safely biodegrades into harmless soil after service | **Compostable Bioplastics (PLA, PHA)** | | A soldier whose armor is converted back to raw metal/energy | **Chemical Recycling / Energy Recovery (Pyrolysis)** | > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Takeaway:** The goal of environmental considerations is not to *eliminate* plastic packaging (since no alternative offers the same cost-to-protection ratio for many applications), but to *engineer it to participate in a circular flow* — keeping the material in productive use for as long as possible and recovering it cleanly at end-of-life. --- ### 1.3 Standard Environmental Metrics (Highlighted) The following metrics are universally cited in KTU textbooks and the Indian Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2022 (amended): - **Cradle-to-Grave Carbon Footprint of Virgin PET:** ~ **2.15 kg CO₂-eq per kg PET** - **Cradle-to-Grave Carbon Footprint of r-PET (100% recycled):** ~ **0.94 kg CO₂-eq per kg r-PET** - **Global Warming Potential (GWP₁₀₀)** unit: **kg CO₂-equivalent (CO₂-eq)** - **Cumulative Energy Demand (CED)** unit: **MJ (Megajoule) per functional unit** --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Plastic Waste Hierarchy — Theoretical Foundation The plastic waste management hierarchy is a **tiered decision framework** that ranks interventions by their environmental preference. Every KTU question on this topic tests the student's ability to *prioritize* interventions in the correct order. ### 2.1.1 The 5R / 9R Framework The classical framework is the **4R Hierarchy** (Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover), extended to the **9R Framework** in circular economy literature: ``` 1. R0 — Refuse (Highest priority — refuse unnecessary packaging) 2. R1 — Rethink 3. R2 — Reduce (Lightweighting, source reduction) 4. R3 — Reuse (Reusable packaging systems) 5. R4 — Repair 6. R5 — Refurbish 7. R6 — Remanufacture 8. R7 — Repurpose 9. R8 — Recycle (Mechanical, Chemical) 10. R9 — Recover (Energy recovery via incineration — lowest priority) ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** When asked to rank "the most environmentally preferred option," the answer is **always** the option highest in the 9R hierarchy. Recycling is *preferred* over energy recovery, but *less preferred* than reduction and reuse. --- ### 2.2 Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) — Phases per ISO 14040 LCA is structured into **four mandatory phases**: | Phase | Name | Purpose | |---|---|---| | I | **Goal & Scope Definition** | Define functional unit, system boundaries, allocation rules | | II | **Life Cycle Inventory (LCI)** | Quantify inputs (raw materials, energy) and outputs (emissions, waste) | | III | **Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)** | Translate inventory data into impact categories (GWP, eutrophication, etc.) | | IV | **Interpretation** | Draw conclusions, identify hotspots, perform sensitivity analysis | ### 2.3 Functional Unit & System Boundaries - **Functional Unit (FU):** The quantified performance of a product system for use as a reference unit (e.g., *"packaging of 1 L of milk for 7 days of shelf life"*). - **System Boundary:** Defines which life cycle stages are included. - **Cradle-to-Gate:** Raw material extraction → Factory exit - **Cradle-to-Grave:** Raw material → Disposal - **Cradle-to-Cradle:** Raw material → Recycled feedstock (closed loop) --- ## 2.4 Recycling Pathways for Plastics ### 2.4.1 Mechanical Recycling Mechanical recycling involves **physical processing** — sorting, washing, shredding, melting, and re-extrusion — without altering the polymer's chemical structure. **Process Flow:** ``` Used Plastic → Collection → Sorting (by Resin Code) → Washing → Shredding → Melt Extrusion → Pelletization → r-Resin ``` **Quality Degradation:** Each recycling loop reduces the polymer chain length via **thermo-mechanical degradation**, quantified by the change in **Melt Flow Index (MFI)**: $$MFI = \frac{m \cdot 600}{t \cdot \rho_{melt}}$$ where $m$ = mass of extrudate (g), $t$ = time interval (s), $\rho_{melt}$ = melt density. > **Engineer's Note:** Higher MFI = greater chain scission = lower molecular weight = lower mechanical properties. ### 2.4.2 Chemical Recycling (Feedstock Recycling) Chemical recycling **depolymerizes** the polymer back into monomers or converts it into feedstock for petrochemical processes. Key routes: | Route | Input Polymer | Output | |---|---|---| | **Hydrolysis** | PET | Terephthalic acid + Ethylene glycol | | **Glycolysis** | PET | BHET monomer | | **Methanolysis** | PET | DMT + EG | | **Pyrolysis** | Mixed polyolefins | Pyrolysis oil (C₅–C₃₀ hydrocarbons) | | **Enzymatic Depolymerization** | PET (using PETase enzyme) | Mono(2-hydroxyethyl) terephthalate (MHET) | ### 2.4.3 Energy Recovery (Waste-to-Energy) Incineration with energy capture. **Calorific Value** of common plastics: | Plastic | Calorific Value (MJ/kg) | |---|---| | Polyethylene (PE) | ~ 46 | | Polypropylene (PP) | ~ 46 | | Polystyrene (PS) | ~ 40 | | PET | ~ 32 | | PVC | ~ 20 (low — corrosive HCl issues) | | Paper (for comparison) | ~ 17 | --- ## 2.5 Biodegradable & Compostable Plastics — Theoretical Foundation ### 2.5.1 Classification - **Bio-based** ≠ **Biodegradable**. A plastic can be bio-based (from renewable feedstock) but non-biodegradable (e.g., bio-PE from sugarcane ethanol). Conversely, a plastic can be biodegradable but petrochemical-based (e.g., PBAT). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Definition — Biodegradable Plastic (per IS/EN 13432):** > A plastic that undergoes ≥ **90% conversion to CO₂** within **≤ 6 months** under specific composting conditions (industrial composting: 58 °C ± 2 °C, humidity 50–55%, aerobic conditions). ### 2.5.2 Common Biodegradable Polymers | Polymer | Source | Tg (°C) | Tm (°C) | Key Property | |---|---|---|---|---| | PLA (Polylactic Acid) | Corn starch / sugarcane | 55–60 | 150–170 | Rigid, transparent, compostable | | PHB / PHBV | Microbial fermentation | 5 | 170 | Biodegradable in soil & marine | | PHA (Polyhydroxyalkanoates) | Bacterial fermentation | 0–5 | 160–175 | Fully biodegradable | | PBAT | Petrochemical | -30 | 110–120 | Flexible, blends with PLA | | Starch blends | Plant starch | — | — | Water-sensitive, low strength | ### 2.5.3 Degradation Mechanisms 1. **Aerobic Biodegradation:** Polymer → CO₂ + H₂O + Biomass (via microbial action) 2. **Anaerobic Biodegradation:** Polymer → CH₄ + CO₂ + Biomass 3. **Photodegradation:** UV-triggered chain scission (requires pro-oxidant additives) 4. **Hydrolytic Degradation:** Hydrolysis of ester bonds (relevant for PLA, PET) --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Melt Flow Index | $MFI = \dfrac{m \cdot 600}{t \cdot \rho_{melt}}$ | $m$ in g, $t$ in s, $\rho$ in g/cm³ | | 2 | Carbon Footprint Savings | $\Delta CF = CF_{virgin} - CF_{recycled}$ | kg CO₂-eq / kg polymer | | 3 | Recycling Energy Ratio | $RER = \dfrac{E_{recycled}}{E_{virgin}}$ | Dimensionless (0 < RER < 1) | | 4 | Mass Balance in Recycling Loop | $M_{out} = M_{in} \cdot (1 - L)$ | $L$ = process loss fraction | | 5 | Biodegradation Rate (First-order) | $\ln(C_t) = \ln(C_0) - k \cdot t$ | $k$ = rate constant (day⁻¹) | | 6 | Calorific Energy Recovery | $E_{recovery} = m \cdot CV \cdot \eta_{conv}$ | $m$ in kg, $CV$ in MJ/kg, $\eta$ efficiency | | 7 | LCA Functional Unit Service Life | $FU_{adj} = \dfrac{n \cdot M_{pack}}{N_{uses}}$ | $n$ = units, $N_{uses}$ = reuse cycles | | 8 | EPR Compliance Ratio | $EPR_{compliance} = \dfrac{M_{recovered}}{M_{introduced}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | | 9 | Density-based Yield | $Y_{recycle} = \dfrac{\rho_{polymer}}{\rho_{blend}} \cdot \eta_{sep}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | | 10 | Embodied Energy per kg | $EE = \sum_{i} (m_i \cdot e_i)$ | $e_i$ = energy intensity of input $i$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Conditions & Assumptions:** > - All formulas assume **steady-state** mass balance. > - LCA calculations use **TRACI** (US) or **ReCiPe** (EU) impact assessment methods. > - Recycling efficiency $\eta_{recycle}$ for PET: 0.65–0.85 (mechanical); 0.90–0.95 (chemical). > - For mixed plastic streams, **sorting purity ≥ 95%** is required for high-quality recyclate. --- ## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering | Industry Application | Environmental Strategy Used | |---|---| | Beverage bottles (Coca-Cola) | 100% r-PET target by 2030 | | Food packaging (multilayer films) | Mono-material PE/PP design for recyclability | | Agricultural mulch films | Oxo-biodegradable / starch-blended films | | E-commerce cushioning | Dissolvable PVA or paper-based alternatives | | Marine environments | PHA-based biodegradable fishing nets | | Medical packaging | Sterilizable recycled PP (closed-loop hospital systems) | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Embodied Energy Savings via Recycling **Problem Setup:** Compare the embodied energy of producing 1 kg of virgin PET resin versus 1 kg of recycled PET (r-PET) using mechanical recycling. ### Step 1 — Define the Functional Unit We select **1 kg of PET resin** as the functional unit (FU). ### Step 2 — Identify Energy Inputs (Virgin PET Pathway) From standardized ecoinvent database values: | Process Stage | Energy Input (MJ / kg PET) | |---|---| | Crude oil extraction | $E_1 = 18.5$ | | Naphtha cracking to para-xylene + MEG | $E_2 = 24.7$ | | Polymerization (PTA + EG → PET) | $E_3 = 9.3$ | | Pelletization & packaging | $E_4 = 2.1$ | ### Step 3 — Sum the Virgin Pathway Energy $$\begin{aligned} EE_{virgin} &= E_1 + E_2 + E_3 + E_4 \\ &= 18.5 + 24.7 + 9.3 + 2.1 \\ &= 54.6 \; \text{MJ / kg PET} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 — Identify Energy Inputs (r-PET Mechanical Pathway) | Process Stage | Energy Input (MJ / kg r-PET) | |---|---| | Collection & transport | $E_5 = 1.8$ | | Sorting & washing | $E_6 = 2.4$ | | Shredding | $E_7 = 1.1$ | | Extrusion & pelletization | $E_8 = 5.6$ | ### Step 5 — Sum the Recycled Pathway Energy $$\begin{aligned} EE_{rPET} &= E_5 + E_6 + E_7 + E_8 \\ &= 1.8 + 2.4 + 1.1 + 5.6 \\ &= 10.9 \; \text{MJ / kg r-PET} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 6 — Compute the Energy Savings $$\begin{aligned} \Delta EE &= EE_{virgin} - EE_{rPET} \\ &= 54.6 - 10.9 \\ &= 43.7 \; \text{MJ / kg} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 7 — Express as Percentage Reduction $$\begin{aligned} \% \text{ Energy Saved} &= \frac{\Delta EE}{EE_{virgin}} \times 100\% \\ &= \frac{43.7}{54.6} \times 100\% \\ &= 80.04\% \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** Mechanical recycling of PET saves approximately **80% of embodied energy** compared to virgin production. This makes r-PET one of the most environmentally advantageous packaging materials in industrial practice. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: EPR Compliance Calculation **Scenario:** A beverage company introduced **5,000 tonnes** of PET bottles in 2024. Under EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) rules, the company must recover **70%** of the introduced quantity via recycling/collection channels. ### Step 1 — State the EPR Formula $$\begin{aligned} EPR_{compliance} = \frac{M_{recovered}}{M_{introduced}} \times 100\% \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute Known Values $$\begin{aligned} EPR_{compliance} &= \frac{M_{recovered}}{5000} \times 100\% \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Required Recovery Mass for Compliance $$\begin{aligned} M_{recovered, required} &= 5000 \times 0.70 \\ &= 3500 \; \text{tonnes} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 — Verify with Hypothetical Actual Recovery of 3,800 tonnes $$\begin{aligned} EPR_{compliance} &= \frac{3800}{5000} \times 100\% \\ &= 76\% \end{aligned}$$ Since **76% > 70%**, the company is in **compliance**. ### Step 5 — Compute Excess Recovery Credits (for trading in EPR certificate markets) $$\begin{aligned} \Delta M &= 3800 - 3500 \\ &= 300 \; \text{tonnes (excess)} \\ \Rightarrow \text{Tradable EPR Credits} &= 300 \times (\text{Certificate Price per tonne}) \end{aligned}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: First-Order Biodegradation Kinetics For a biodegradable polymer in compost, mass loss often follows first-order kinetics. ### Step 1 — Set Up the Differential Equation $$\begin{aligned} \frac{dC_t}{dt} = -k \cdot C_t \end{aligned}$$ where $C_t$ is the remaining polymer mass at time $t$, and $k$ is the first-order rate constant (day⁻¹). ### Step 2 — Integrate with Boundary Condition $C(0) = C_0$ $$\begin{aligned} \int_{C_0}^{C_t} \frac{dC}{C} &= -k \int_{0}^{t} dt \\ \ln(C_t) - \ln(C_0) &= -k \cdot t \\ \ln\left(\frac{C_t}{C_0}\right) &= -k \cdot t \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3 — Solve for Time to 90% Degradation (per IS 13432 Standard) $$\begin{aligned} \text{At 90\% degradation: } C_t &= 0.10 \cdot C_0 \\ \ln(0.10) &= -k \cdot t_{90} \\ t_{90} &= \frac{\ln(10)}{k} = \frac{2.303}{k} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 — Apply Numerical Example: PLA at Industrial Composting For PLA, $k \approx 0.061 \; \text{day}^{-1}$ (58 °C, aerobic compost): $$\begin{aligned} t_{90} &= \frac{2.303}{0.061} \\ &= 37.75 \; \text{days} \approx 5.4 \; \text{weeks} \end{aligned}$$ This satisfies the **EN 13432** compostability requirement (≤ 6 months = 180 days). ✔ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Environmental Performance | Property | Virgin PET | r-PET | PLA | PHB | |---|---|---|---|---| | Embodied Energy (MJ/kg) | 54.6 | 10.9 | 27.0 | 22.5 | | GWP (kg CO₂-eq/kg) | 2.15 | 0.94 | 1.30 | 1.10 | | Recyclable? | Yes | Yes | No (industrial compost only) | Limited | | Compostable? | No | No | Yes (industrial) | Yes (soil + industrial) | | Marine Biodegradable? | No | No | No | Yes | | Cost (USD/kg, approx.) | 1.10 | 1.40 | 2.20 | 5.00 | | Tensile Strength (MPa) | 55–75 | 45–65 | 50–70 | 25–40 | > [!TIP] > **Analytical Insight for KTU Examinations:** r-PET has the **lowest** GWP and embodied energy. PLA offers compostability but at higher GWP. PHB offers marine biodegradability but at very high cost. The selection criterion is **application-specific**. --- # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) as per ISO 14040. List its four mandatory phases.** **Model Answer:** Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is a systematic, standardized methodology (defined in **ISO 14040** and **ISO 14044**) for quantifying the environmental impacts of a product, process, or service throughout its entire life span — from raw material extraction ("cradle") to final disposal ("grave") or to a recycled feedstock ("cradle-to-cradle"). The **four mandatory phases** are: 1. **Goal and Scope Definition** — Establishes the functional unit, system boundary, and assumptions. 2. **Life Cycle Inventory (LCI)** — Quantifies all material/energy inputs and environmental releases. 3. **Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)** — Translates inventory data into environmental impact categories (e.g., GWP, eutrophication, ozone depletion). 4. **Interpretation** — Identifies significant issues, conducts sensitivity analysis, and formulates recommendations. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between bio-based plastics and biodegradable plastics with two examples each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Bio-based Plastic | Biodegradable Plastic | |---|---|---| | Definition | Derived from **renewable biomass** feedstock (e.g., corn, sugarcane) | Capable of **microbial decomposition** into CO₂, H₂O, and biomass | | Source dependency | Source-based concept | End-of-life behavior concept | | Examples | **Bio-PE** (sugarcane ethanol), **Bio-PET** (partially bio-based MEG) | **PLA** (corn starch), **PHB/PHA** (microbial fermentation) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction:** Bio-based ≠ Biodegradable. *Bio-PE* is bio-based but NOT biodegradable (identical to fossil PE chemically). *PBAT* is biodegradable but petrochemical-based. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format:** Students answer **either Question A or Question B**. Each question has two sub-parts (a) of 7 marks and (b) of 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]` #### **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the 9R Framework in plastics packaging with reference to circular economy principles. Apply this framework to evaluate two alternative strategies for single-use PET water bottles. **Model Solution:** The **9R Framework** (Refuse, Rethink, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Refurbish, Remanufacture, Repurpose, Recycle, Recover) is a circular economy model that prioritizes interventions to retain material value and minimize resource extraction. **Application to Single-Use PET Water Bottles:** **Strategy 1: Refillable/Returnable Glass Bottle System (Higher 9R Position)** - **R3 Reuse:** Bottle is collected, sanitized (industrial wash at 90 °C, NaOH 1.5%), and reused **20–50 times**. - Functional unit service life: $$FU_{adj} = \frac{n \cdot M_{pack}}{N_{uses}} = \frac{1 \times 0.30 \; \text{kg}}{30} = 0.010 \; \text{kg per use}$$ - Embodied energy per use drops to **~ 2 MJ**, versus **5.5 MJ** for single-use PET. **Strategy 2: 100% r-PET Recycling Loop (Lower 9R Position: R8 Recycle)** - **R8 Recycle:** PET is recovered, reprocessed into food-grade r-PET (IV ≥ 0.74 dL/g after SSP). - Energy saving: **~ 80%** compared to virgin PET (as derived in Section 3.1). **Evaluation:** Per the 9R framework, **Strategy 1 (reuse)** is environmentally superior because reuse retains material form and value multiple times, whereas recycling requires reprocessing (energy + quality loss per cycle). --- #### **(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the embodied energy savings for switching 1000 kg of virgin PET packaging to r-PET packaging. Use the energy values: Virgin PET = 54.6 MJ/kg; r-PET = 10.9 MJ/kg. Also express the saving as a percentage of the virgin energy. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Total Energy for 1000 kg Virgin PET:** $$E_{virgin,total} = 1000 \times 54.6 = 54600 \; \text{MJ}$$ **Step 2 — Total Energy for 1000 kg r-PET:** $$E_{rPET,total} = 1000 \times 10.9 = 10900 \; \text{MJ}$$ **Step 3 — Absolute Energy Saving:** $$\Delta E = 54600 - 10900 = 43700 \; \text{MJ}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):** $$\Delta E_{kWh} = \frac{43700}{3.6} = 12138.9 \; \text{kWh}$$ **Step 5 — Percentage Saving:** $$\% \text{Saving} = \frac{43700}{54600} \times 100\% = 80.04\%$$ **Step 6 — Equivalent CO₂ Avoided (using grid factor ~ 0.71 kg CO₂/kWh for India):** $$CO_{2, avoided} = 12138.9 \times 0.71 = 8618.6 \; \text{kg CO}_2\text{-eq}$$ > **Conclusion:** Switching 1000 kg of virgin PET to r-PET saves **43,700 MJ (~ 12,139 kWh)** of energy and avoids approximately **8.62 tonnes of CO₂** emissions. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]` #### **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe mechanical recycling of PET bottles with a process flow diagram. Discuss two limitations of mechanical recycling. **Model Solution:** **Mechanical Recycling Process Flow for PET:** 1. **Collection:** PET bottles are collected via municipal systems, deposit-return schemes, or buy-back centers. 2. **Sorting:** Manual + automated sorting using **near-infrared (NIR) spectroscopy** to separate Resin Code 1 (PET) from other polymers (HDPE, PP, PVC). 3. **Washing:** Hot caustic soda wash (NaOH 1–2%, 80–90 °C) removes labels, adhesives, and organic contaminants. 4. **Shredding:** Flakes are produced (8–12 mm size) for easier handling. 5. **Separation:** **Float-sink separation** in water (PET density 1.38 g/cm³ — sinks; PP/PE float and are skimmed off). 6. **Drying:** Moisture content reduced to **< 50 ppm** to prevent hydrolysis during extrusion. 7. **Extrusion:** Melted at **220–280 °C**, filtered through screen packs (40–200 μm) to remove contaminants. 8. **Pelletization:** r-PET pellets formed, with **Intrinsic Viscosity (IV)** typically 0.55–0.70 dL/g. 9. **Solid-State Polymerization (SSP):** Optional step to raise IV to ≥ 0.74 dL/g for **food-contact** applications. **Two Limitations:** 1. **Quality Degradation per Cycle:** Each cycle causes thermo-oxidative degradation, reducing molecular weight (MFI increases, IV drops). Mechanical properties (tensile strength, impact resistance) deteriorate, limiting the number of cycles to typically **4–6** for bottle-grade PET. 2. **Contamination Sensitivity:** Multilayer packaging, food residues, PVC contamination, and degradation products (acetaldehyde) make sorted PET stream quality inconsistent. This restricts recycled PET to lower-grade applications unless expensive SSP and super-clean processes are used. --- #### **(b) [7 Marks]** What are biodegradable plastics? Explain PLA and PHB with their sources, key properties, and degradation behavior. State two applications in packaging for each. **Model Solution:** **Definition:** Biodegradable plastics are polymeric materials that undergo **microbial decomposition** (aerobic or anaerobic) into CO₂, H₂O (or CH₄), and biomass under specified environmental conditions, per standards such as **EN 13432**, **ASTM D6400**, or **IS 17088**. **PLA (Polylactic Acid):** | Attribute | Detail | |---|---| | **Source** | Fermentation of dextrose from corn starch / sugarcane → lactic acid → lactide → ring-opening polymerization | | **Tg** | 55–60 °C | | **Tm** | 150–170 °C | | **Degradation** | Hydrolytic chain scission of ester bonds, followed by microbial assimilation. **Industrial composting (58 °C, 50–55% RH)** required; degrades slowly in ambient/soil. | | **Tensile Strength** | 50–70 MPa | | **Packaging Applications** | (1) Rigid transparent clamshells and trays for fresh produce; (2) Cold drink cups and single-use cutlery | **PHB (Poly-3-hydroxybutyrate) / PHA family:** | Attribute | Detail | |---|---| | **Source** | Produced by bacterial fermentation (e.g., *Cupriavidus necator*) using sugars or vegetable oils as carbon substrate | | **Tg** | 0–5 °C | | **Tm** | 160–175 °C | | **Degradation** | Biodegrades in **soil, marine water, and industrial compost** via microbial enzymatic action (PHB depolymerase). Faster degradation than PLA in natural environments. | | **Tensile Strength** | 25–40 MPa | | **Packaging Applications** | (1) Marine-degradable fishing-net packaging and bags; (2) Agricultural mulch films and plant pots | > [!NOTE] > **Comparative Insight:** PLA is **stiffer and more transparent**, suitable for rigid packaging. PHB is **softer and tougher**, suitable for films and marine applications. PHB is more expensive (USD 5/kg vs. PLA USD 2.2/kg). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Environmental Considerations Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing bio-based with biodegradable** — Students often use the terms interchangeably. Examiners deduct **1 mark minimum** for this confusion. *Bio-PE is bio-based but not biodegradable.* > > 2. **Incorrect hierarchy ordering** — When ranking waste management options, students sometimes place "Recycle" above "Reuse." Reuse is **always preferred** over recycling because it retains material form and embodied energy more efficiently. > > 3. **Missing units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners strictly penalize answers like "Energy saved = 43700" without **MJ** or equivalent units. Always state **numerical value AND unit**. > > 4. **Forgetting the IV criterion for food-grade r-PET** — Food-contact r-PET requires IV ≥ 0.74 dL/g after SSP. Generic statements like "r-PET can be used for food packaging" without the IV specification lose **1–2 marks**. > > 5. **Omitting the system boundary in LCA answers** — Always specify **Cradle-to-Gate**, **Cradle-to-Grave**, or **Cradle-to-Cradle** when discussing LCA. A vague "life cycle" answer is incomplete. > > 6. **Ignoring India's Plastic Waste Management Rules (2022)** — KTU examiners expect mention of **EPR**, **mandatory thickness rules** (no < 50 μm carry bags), and **PROs (Producer Responsibility Organizations)** for full marks in current-affairs-integrated questions. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Rapid Revision Checklist - [ ] **LCA has 4 phases** per ISO 14040: Goal & Scope → LCI → LCIA → Interpretation. - [ ] **Functional Unit** quantifies the performance reference; do not confuse with system boundary. - [ ] **Bio-based ≠ Biodegradable** — source-based vs. end-of-life concept. - [ ] **9R Hierarchy order** (high → low preference): Refuse → Rethink → Reduce → Reuse → Repair → Refurbish → Remanufacture → Repurpose → Recycle → Recover. - [ ] **Mechanical recycling** of PET saves ~ **80% embodied energy** and ~ **56% GWP** vs. virgin PET. - [ ] **r-PET for food contact** requires **IV ≥ 0.74 dL/g**, achieved via Solid-State Polymerization (SSP). - [ ] **PLA**: industrial composting only (58 °C), Tg = 55–60 °C, transparent, brittle. - [ ] **PHB/PHA**: marine biodegradable, expensive (USD 5/kg), sourced from microbial fermentation. - [ ] **Calorific values**: PE/PP ~ 46 MJ/kg, PET ~ 32 MJ/kg, PVC ~ 20 MJ/kg (corrosive — avoid incineration). - [ ] **EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility)** — producer must recover a specified % of introduced plastic; India mandates ~ **70% recovery** for major PET brands (Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2022). - [ ] **Carry bag thickness rule in India**: minimum **50 microns** for non-woven, **75 microns** for virgin plastic carry bags (single-use ban effect). - [ ] **Carbon footprint of virgin PET** ≈ 2.15 kg CO₂-eq/kg; r-PET ≈ 0.94 kg CO₂-eq/kg. - [ ] **Degradation kinetics** — first-order: $t_{90} = \frac{\ln(10)}{k}$; PLA achieves 90% degradation in **~ 38 days** under industrial composting. - [ ] **Standards to memorize**: ISO 14001, ISO 14040/14044, ISO 18602, EN 13432, ASTM D6400, IS 17088. - [ ] **Recycling inefficiencies**: PET mechanical recycling → typically **4–6 cycles** before significant property loss; each cycle reduces IV by ~ 0.02–0.04 dL/g. - [ ] **Multilayer films problem** — non-recyclable by design; trend toward **mono-material PE/PP** structures for recyclability. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** Always frame environmental answers in the **3-pillar structure** — *Material*, *Design*, *End-of-Life*. Examiners reward structured, systematic responses that demonstrate awareness of the **circular economy** paradigm. ---
